SA_GK22_ksdarppu.title: Darpadalana
SA_GK22_ksdarppu.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK22_ksdarppu.summary: A satirical and didactic poem in seven chapters (vicāras) totaling some 587 stanzas that attacks human pride (darpa). Each chapter targets a cause of arrogance—noble birth, wealth, learning, beauty, valor, charity, and asceticism—and illustrates through embedded narratives how such pride leads to ruin. The work moralizes against vanity and counsels humility.
SA_GK22_ksdarppu.history: Kṣemendra was an 11th-century Kashmiri polymath, satirist, and disciple of Abhinavagupta who wrote prolifically across genres. The Darpadalana belongs to his moralizing-satirical works (alongside the Kalāvilāsa and Deśopadeśa) and survives in the Kāvyamālā series; it is valued for its sharp social criticism of Kashmiri society.

SA_GK19_bhattiku.title: Rāvaṇavadha [Bhaṭṭikāvya]
SA_GK19_bhattiku.author: Bhaṭṭi
SA_GK19_bhattiku.tradition: Hindu (secular court poetry with grammatical/scientific purpose)
SA_GK19_bhattiku.summary: A mahākāvya retelling the story of Rāma and the slaying of Rāvaṇa, paralleling the Rāmāyaṇa narrative across 22 cantos. Its distinguishing feature is that it systematically illustrates the rules of Pāṇinian grammar, poetic figures (alaṃkāra), and verbal moods, functioning simultaneously as poetry and as a teaching aid for Sanskrit grammar—the earliest exemplar of the 'instructional poem' (śāstra-kāvya).
SA_GK19_bhattiku.history: The author is traditionally identified as Bhaṭṭi (sometimes equated with Bhartṛhari, though this is doubted); a colophon associates its composition with Valabhī under King Śrīdharasena. Its original title was Rāvaṇavadha, with Bhaṭṭikāvya being the later popular name. It became a standard pedagogical text and inspired later grammar-poems.

SA_GSP29_nyayasiddhanjana.title: Nyāyasiddhāñjana
SA_GSP29_nyayasiddhanjana.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_nyayasiddhanjana.summary: A systematic Viśiṣṭādvaita treatise (also called Tarkasiddhāñjana) running to six chapters (paricchedas) that expounds the categories (padārtha) of reality—substance and attribute—within Rāmānuja's qualified non-dualism. It treats epistemology and ontology, presenting the world and all souls as constituting the 'body' of God (Brahman/Nārāyaṇa) of which he is the inner self.
SA_GSP29_nyayasiddhanjana.history: Authored by Vedānta Deśika, the leading systematizer of post-Rāmānuja Śrīvaiṣṇava thought, it functions as a companion/continuation to his Nyāyapariśuddhi and is a foundational text of Viśiṣṭādvaita ontology, engaging and refuting Buddhist, Nyāya, and Advaita positions.

SA_GE07_bhg.title: Bhagavadgītā
SA_GE07_bhg.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa; part of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GE07_bhg.genre: philosophical-religious poem (didactic episode within the epic)
SA_GE07_bhg.summary: A 700-verse dialogue forming part of the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata, in which the warrior Arjuna, despairing on the eve of the Kurukṣetra battle, is counseled by his charioteer Kṛṣṇa, revealed as the supreme deity. It synthesizes paths of disciplined action (karma-yoga), devotion (bhakti), and knowledge (jñāna), and includes Kṛṣṇa's theophany in chapter 11.
SA_GE07_bhg.history: Embedded in the Mahābhārata but widely transmitted as an independent scripture, it became the most commented-upon Hindu text, with classic bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, and Madhva, each reading it through their school. It is central to nearly all later Hindu theology and to the modern reception of Hinduism.

SA_GE07_hv_apppu.title: Harivaṃśa (appendix)
SA_GE07_hv_apppu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_hv_apppu.date_estimate: additions accreting c. 4th-10th c. CE (later than the main Harivaṃśa)
SA_GE07_hv_apppu.summary: The appendix material (appendices/interpolated passages) attached to the Harivaṃśa, the khila or supplement to the Mahābhārata. These passages contain further elaborations of the genealogy of Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa, Kṛṣṇa's exploits, and miscellaneous mythological and cosmological additions that grew around the core text.
SA_GE07_hv_apppu.history: In the Poona critical edition the Harivaṃśa is divided into a constituted (con) text and an apparatus of appendices (app) representing later interpolations of varying date and region. The appendix passages document the ongoing expansion of Kṛṣṇa narrative material in the first millennium CE.

SA_GE07_hv_conpu.title: Harivaṃśa
SA_GE07_hv_conpu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_hv_conpu.genre: epic supplement (khila to the Mahābhārata) / proto-purāṇic narrative
SA_GE07_hv_conpu.date_estimate: c. 1st-4th c. CE (composite; later than the epic core)
SA_GE07_hv_conpu.summary: The 'Genealogy of Hari (Viṣṇu)', a supplement (khila) to the Mahābhārata in three sections that narrates the creation, the lineage of the Yādavas, and especially the life and deeds of Kṛṣṇa—his birth, childhood, slaying of Kaṃsa, and exploits—followed by a future-oriented Bhaviṣyaparvan. This 'con' designation refers to the constituted critical text proper.
SA_GE07_hv_conpu.history: Regarded as the nineteenth book or appendix of the Mahābhārata, it is a key early source for Kṛṣṇa biography that bridges epic and purāṇa and strongly influenced the Bhāgavata and Viṣṇu Purāṇas. The Poona critical edition (V. S. Sukthankar/P. L. Vaidya tradition) distinguishes its constituted text from later appendices.

SA_GE07_mbh_02_u.title: Mahābhārata: Sabhāparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_02_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_02_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 4th c. CE (within the composite epic)
SA_GE07_mbh_02_u.summary: The second book ('Book of the Assembly Hall') of the Mahābhārata. It describes Yudhiṣṭhira's royal consecration (rājasūya), the construction of the Pāṇḍavas' magnificent assembly hall, the slaying of Jarāsandha and Śiśupāla, and culminates in the fateful dice game in which Yudhiṣṭhira loses the kingdom, his brothers, and Draupadī, who is humiliated in the hall—setting the stage for exile and war.
SA_GE07_mbh_02_u.history: Part of the great Sanskrit epic traditionally attributed to Vyāsa but recognized as a composite work redacted over centuries; established in the Poona critical edition. The dice-game episode is among the most dramatically pivotal in the epic.

SA_GE07_mbh_03_u.title: Mahābhārata: Āraṇyakaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_03_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_03_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 4th c. CE (within the composite epic)
SA_GE07_mbh_03_u.summary: The third and one of the longest books ('Book of the Forest', also Vanaparvan), narrating the twelve years of the Pāṇḍavas' forest exile after losing the dice game. It is rich in embedded didactic and mythological episodes, including the Nala–Damayantī story, the tale of Sāvitrī, the Yakṣa's questions to Yudhiṣṭhira, and numerous tīrtha (pilgrimage) and sermon passages.
SA_GE07_mbh_03_u.history: A composite layer of the epic that absorbed many independent legends and moral discourses; attributed to Vyāsa and constituted in the Poona critical edition. Its inset tales (e.g., Nala, Sāvitrī) became famous in their own right.

SA_GE07_mbh_04_u.title: Mahābhārata: Virāṭaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_04_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_04_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 4th c. CE (within the composite epic)
SA_GE07_mbh_04_u.summary: The fourth book ('Book of Virāṭa'), recounting the thirteenth year of exile, which the Pāṇḍavas and Draupadī spend in disguise at the court of King Virāṭa of Matsya. Yudhiṣṭhira poses as a courtier, Bhīma as a cook, Arjuna as the eunuch dance-teacher Bṛhannaḍā, and others in menial roles; the book climaxes with Arjuna's defense of Virāṭa's cattle against the Kauravas, ending their incognito period.
SA_GE07_mbh_04_u.history: Part of the composite epic ascribed to Vyāsa and constituted in the Poona critical edition. The disguise narrative provides comic and dramatic relief before the war books.

SA_GE07_mbh_07_u.title: Mahābhārata: Droṇaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_07_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_07_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 4th c. CE (within the composite epic)
SA_GE07_mbh_07_u.summary: The seventh book ('Book of Droṇa'), describing days eleven through fifteen of the Kurukṣetra war while the preceptor Droṇa serves as Kaurava commander-in-chief. It contains major events such as the death of Abhimanyu in the cakravyūha formation, Arjuna's vow to slay Jayadratha, and ultimately Droṇa's own death after the deceptive announcement of Aśvatthāman's killing.
SA_GE07_mbh_07_u.history: A central war-book of the epic, attributed to Vyāsa and constituted in the Poona critical edition. It explores the moral ambiguities (use of deception against Droṇa) that pervade the epic's depiction of dharma in war.

SA_GE07_mbh_08_u.title: Mahābhārata: Karṇaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_08_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_08_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 4th c. CE (within the composite epic)
SA_GE07_mbh_08_u.summary: The eighth book ('Book of Karṇa') of the Mahābhārata, covering days sixteen and seventeen of the war when Karṇa serves as the third Kaurava commander-in-chief. It narrates the intense fighting, the conflict between Karṇa and Śalya as charioteer, and culminates in the final duel in which Arjuna kills Karṇa as his chariot wheel sinks into the earth. (The batch title field was corrupted to '7'; the work_id _08_ identifies it as the eighth parvan.)
SA_GE07_mbh_08_u.history: A pivotal war-book of the composite epic ascribed to Vyāsa; the critical (Poona) edition has 69 chapters versus the vulgate's ~96. The death of Karṇa, achieved through Kṛṣṇa's urging at a vulnerable moment, is a major node in the epic's reflections on dharma.

SA_GE07_mbh_09_u.title: Mahābhārata: Śalyaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_09_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_09_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 4th c. CE (within the composite epic)
SA_GE07_mbh_09_u.summary: The ninth book ('Book of Śalya'), describing the eighteenth and final day of battle when Śalya becomes the last Kaurava commander and is slain by Yudhiṣṭhira. The Kaurava army collapses; the book includes Balarāma's pilgrimage along the Sarasvatī and culminates in the climactic mace duel between Bhīma and Duryodhana, in which Bhīma fells Duryodhana with a blow below the belt.
SA_GE07_mbh_09_u.history: Part of the composite epic ascribed to Vyāsa and constituted in the Poona critical edition. It effectively ends the great war, leaving Duryodhana mortally wounded and setting up the night-massacre of the Sauptikaparvan.

SA_GE07_mbh_u.title: Mahābhārata
SA_GE07_mbh_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_u.date_estimate: c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (composite; core perhaps earlier)
SA_GE07_mbh_u.summary: The great Sanskrit epic in eighteen books (parvans) of roughly 100,000 verses, narrating the dynastic struggle between the Pāṇḍavas and Kauravas culminating in the Kurukṣetra war. Beyond the central story it is an encyclopedic compendium of myth, genealogy, statecraft, and religious and philosophical teaching (including the Bhagavadgītā and the Śāntiparvan's dharma discourses), declaring 'what is here is found elsewhere; what is not here is nowhere.'
SA_GE07_mbh_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but universally recognized as a composite work that grew by accretion over several centuries; its text was reconstructed in the monumental Poona critical edition (Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute, 1919-1966). It is foundational to Hindu civilization and has generated countless retellings and commentaries.

SA_GE09_ram.title: Rāmāyaṇa
SA_GE09_ram.author: Vālmīki
SA_GE09_ram.date_estimate: core c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE (composite; books 1 and 7 later)
SA_GE09_ram.summary: The epic of Rāma in seven books (kāṇḍas) and roughly 24,000 ślokas, recounting the birth of Rāma, his marriage to Sītā, his exile, the abduction of Sītā by the rākṣasa king Rāvaṇa, the alliance with the monkey-host and the war against Laṅkā to recover her, and Rāma's return and reign. It celebrates ideals of dharma, kingship, and conjugal fidelity.
SA_GE09_ram.history: Attributed to the sage Vālmīki and revered as the ādikāvya (first ornate poem); transmitted in distinct regional recensions and constituted in the Baroda critical edition. The Bālakāṇḍa (book 1) and Uttarakāṇḍa (book 7), which most strongly divinize Rāma as Viṣṇu, are generally regarded as later additions. It shaped a vast tradition of vernacular Rāma-stories across South and Southeast Asia.

SA_GE09_ram_02_u.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 2. Ayodhyākāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_02_u.author: Vālmīki
SA_GE09_ram_02_u.date_estimate: core c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE (one of the oldest books)
SA_GE09_ram_02_u.summary: The second book ('Book of Ayodhyā') of Vālmīki's Rāmāyaṇa. On the eve of Rāma's consecration as crown prince, Queen Kaikeyī, prompted by her servant Mantharā, invokes two boons from King Daśaratha to install her son Bharata and banish Rāma to the forest for fourteen years. Rāma accepts exile out of filial duty, accompanied by Sītā and Lakṣmaṇa; Daśaratha dies of grief, and the righteous Bharata refuses the throne.
SA_GE09_ram_02_u.history: Part of the older core of the epic ascribed to Vālmīki and constituted in the Baroda critical edition. The Ayodhyākāṇḍa is central to the epic's exploration of dharma, duty, and renunciation.

SA_GE09_ram_02su.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 2. Ayodhyākāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_02su.author: Vālmīki
SA_GE09_ram_02su.genre: epic (book/kāṇḍa of the Rāmāyaṇa; recension variant)
SA_GE09_ram_02su.date_estimate: core c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE (one of the oldest books)
SA_GE09_ram_02su.summary: The Ayodhyākāṇḍa (book 2) of Vālmīki's Rāmāyaṇa, here in a distinct recension/electronic version (the 'su' suffix indicating a different textual witness, likely the Southern recension, from the parallel '02_u' record). Its narrative is the same: Kaikeyī's boons, the banishment of Rāma, Daśaratha's death, and Bharata's refusal of the throne.
SA_GE09_ram_02su.history: The Rāmāyaṇa survives in divergent regional recensions (notably Northern and Southern); this entry represents a separate recension or digital edition of the same book, useful for comparing textual transmission. Attributed to Vālmīki.

SA_GK16_dkavy12u.title: Kāvyādarśa
SA_GK16_dkavy12u.author: Daṇḍin
SA_GK16_dkavy12u.summary: A foundational treatise on Sanskrit poetics, the 'Mirror of Poetry', in three pariccheda. This record (suffix '12') covers chapters one and two: chapter 1 classifies kinds of poetry (verse/prose/mixed), the two regional styles (Vaidarbha and Gauḍa) and their ten qualities (guṇa); chapter 2 is a large catalogue and definition of figures of sense (arthālaṃkāra).
SA_GK16_dkavy12u.history: Daṇḍin, who also wrote the prose romance Daśakumāracarita, produced one of the most influential of all poetics manuals; the Kāvyādarśa was translated and adapted across Asia (into Tamil, Sinhala, Tibetan, etc.) and shaped literary theory for centuries.

SA_GK16_dkavy.title: Kāvyādarśa
SA_GK16_dkavy.author: Daṇḍin
SA_GK16_dkavy.date_estimate: c. late 7th-8th c. CE (project hint 500-699 CE; commonly placed late 7th-8th c.)
SA_GK16_dkavy.summary: The complete 'Mirror of Poetry' in three chapters (pariccheda): a typology of poetry and the regional styles with their poetic qualities (guṇa), an extensive treatment of figures of sense (arthālaṃkāra), and a final chapter on figures of sound (śabdālaṃkāra), verbal feats (citra), and faults (doṣa). It defines the nature and ornaments of good poetry.
SA_GK16_dkavy.history: Composed by Daṇḍin (author also of the Daśakumāracarita), it is among the earliest and most widely diffused works of Sanskrit poetics and exerted enormous trans-regional influence, being adapted into Tamil, Sinhala, Kannada, Tibetan, and other traditions; it anchored the 'ornament' (alaṃkāra) school against which later rasa- and dhvani-theorists reacted.

SA_GK16_mamkprku.title: Kāvyaprakāśa
SA_GK16_mamkprku.author: Mammaṭa
SA_GK16_mamkprku.summary: The 'Light on Poetry', a comprehensive manual of Sanskrit poetics organized in ten ullāsa (chapters), combining concise kārikā verses with prose exposition. It systematizes the definition and purpose of poetry, the powers of words (denotation, indication, suggestion), the doctrine of suggested meaning (dhvani) and aesthetic relish (rasa), poetic faults, qualities, styles, and figures of speech.
SA_GK16_mamkprku.history: Mammaṭa, an 11th-12th-century Kashmiri theorist, produced the most authoritative synthesis of the dhvani-rasa tradition descending from Ānandavardhana and Abhinavagupta; the Kāvyaprakāśa became the standard textbook of poetics in India, attracting dozens of commentaries and influencing later works such as the Sāhityadarpaṇa. Its final portion is traditionally said to have been completed by another hand (Allaṭa/Alaka).

SA_GK19_agada.title: Āgamaḍambara
SA_GK19_agada.author: Jayanta Bhaṭṭa
SA_GK19_agada.tradition: Hindu, Nyāya (Kashmir; engages and satirizes rival sects)
SA_GK19_agada.summary: A satirical Sanskrit play in four acts ('Much Ado About Religion') that dramatizes religious and philosophical controversy in Kashmir under King Śaṅkaravarman. A young Vedic graduate campaigns against heterodox and antinomian sects; the drama mixes scathing satire of Buddhists, Jains, Kāpālikas, and others with genuine philosophical debate, ending in a royally brokered religious settlement.
SA_GK19_agada.history: Written by Jayanta Bhaṭṭa (c. 820-900 CE), the great Naiyāyika author of the Nyāyamañjarī and an advisor at Śaṅkaravarman's court, it is a rare example of a philosophically-driven Sanskrit drama and a valuable source for the religious politics of 9th-century Kashmir. It was critically edited and translated by Csaba Dezső for the Clay Sanskrit Library.

SA_GK19_asvbc_1u.title: Buddhacarita
SA_GK19_asvbc_1u.author: Aśvaghoṣa
SA_GK19_asvbc_1u.date_estimate: c. early 2nd c. CE (reign of Kaniṣka I)
SA_GK19_asvbc_1u.summary: A Sanskrit mahakavya narrating the life of the Buddha (Siddhārtha Gautama) from his birth and youth through the Great Departure, enlightenment, first sermon, and (in later cantos) the spread of the dharma and parinirvāṇa. It frames the Buddha's biography in the ornate diction and structure of classical court poetry, blending doctrine with lyrical description.
SA_GK19_asvbc_1u.history: Aśvaghoṣa was a Brahmin convert to Buddhism traditionally associated with the Kushan emperor Kaniṣka; he is regarded as the first named master of Sanskrit kavya and a forerunner of Kālidāsa. Only the first half of the poem (cantos 1-14) survives in Sanskrit; the remainder is preserved through Tibetan and Chinese translations, which have been used to reconstruct the whole.

SA_GK19_asvsaunu.title: Saundarananda
SA_GK19_asvsaunu.author: Aśvaghoṣa
SA_GK19_asvsaunu.summary: A Sanskrit mahakavya in 18 cantos recounting the conversion of Nanda, the Buddha's handsome half-brother, who is torn from his beautiful wife Sundarī and reluctantly led to monastic life. The poem dwells on the conflict between sensual love and renunciation, culminating in Nanda's attainment of arhatship and an extended exposition of Buddhist meditative discipline.
SA_GK19_asvsaunu.history: Composed by the same poet as the Buddhacarita, the Saundarananda survives complete in Sanskrit. Aśvaghoṣa explicitly states he used the sweetness of poetry as a vehicle to convey Buddhist doctrine, making the work a deliberate fusion of kavya artistry and didactic religious aim.

SA_GK19_bahcar.title: Harṣacarita
SA_GK19_bahcar.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar.date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (mid-7th c., reign of Harṣa)
SA_GK19_bahcar.summary: An ornate Sanskrit prose biography of the emperor Harṣavardhana of Kanauj, written by his court poet. After autobiographical introductory chapters describing Bāṇa's own lineage and life, it narrates Harṣa's family, his accession following the murder of his brother-in-law and the abduction of his sister, and his early campaigns, told in a richly descriptive gadya-kavya style.
SA_GK19_bahcar.history: One of the earliest surviving Sanskrit prose works of a quasi-historical character, the Harṣacarita is prized both as a literary masterpiece of the high prose style and as a near-contemporary source for 7th-century North Indian history. Bāṇa also authored the unfinished prose romance Kādambarī.

SA_GK19_bhadipiu.title: Bhāvadīpikā
SA_GK19_bhadipiu.author: Vema Bhūpāla (Vīranārāyaṇa Vema, Reḍḍi dynasty)
SA_GK19_bhadipiu.summary: A Sanskrit commentary by the Reḍḍi king Vema Bhūpāla. The title is borne by more than one of his works; it is best known as his commentary on the Amaruśataka (the famous anthology of erotic muktaka verses), where he analyzes the love-poetry through the categories of rasa, bhāva and rhetorical figures, and a Bhāvadīpikā is also attached to his recension of the Prakrit Saptaśatīsāra.
SA_GK19_bhadipiu.history: Vema Bhūpāla (also called Vemabhūpāla / Komaṭi Vema) was a 14th-15th century king of the Triliṅga (Andhra) Reḍḍi dynasty and a noted scholar-commentator on Sanskrit erotic poetry; he is credited with both the Bhāvadīpikā and the Śṛṅgāradīpikā on the Amaruśataka. The exact text intended by this record is somewhat ambiguous among his commentaries.

SA_GK19_bhall_pu.title: Bhallaṭaśataka
SA_GK19_bhall_pu.author: Bhallaṭa
SA_GK19_bhall_pu.genre: kavya (shataka; anthology of allegorical anyokti verses)
SA_GK19_bhall_pu.summary: A 'century' (shataka) of independent Sanskrit verses in the anyokti (allegory) mode, in which descriptions of animals, plants and natural objects carry a veiled second meaning, often a bitter social or political commentary on patrons, neglected merit, and the injustices of court life. The verses are admired for their concentrated wit and double-edged suggestion (dhvani).
SA_GK19_bhall_pu.history: Bhallaṭa was a Kashmiri poet usually placed in the reign of Avantivarman (9th c.); several of his stanzas are quoted in later anthologies and poetics treatises, attesting to his high reputation. The collection is celebrated as a peak of the allegorical verse tradition.

SA_GK19_dandkc_u.title: Daśakumāracarita
SA_GK19_dandkc_u.author: Daṇḍin
SA_GK19_dandkc_u.summary: A Sanskrit prose romance, 'The Tale of the Ten Princes,' recounting in a framing structure the adventures of ten young princes separated in childhood as they pursue love, wealth and power. The interwoven episodes are notably realistic and earthy, populated by gamblers, courtesans, thieves, ascetics and merchants, offering a vivid panorama of ordinary life.
SA_GK19_dandkc_u.history: Daṇḍin flourished at the Pallava court of Kāñcī around 670-720 CE and is also credited with the influential poetics treatise Kāvyādarśa. The surviving Daśakumāracarita has lost portions at its beginning and end, which were supplemented by later writers (the Pūrvapīṭhikā and Uttarapīṭhikā).

SA_GK19_jaygit1u.title: Gītagovinda-kāvya
SA_GK19_jaygit1u.author: Jayadeva
SA_GK19_jaygit1u.date_estimate: late 12th c. CE (c. 1170-1200); project hint of 11th c. is too early
SA_GK19_jaygit1u.summary: A lyrical Sanskrit poem in twelve cantos celebrating the love of Krishna and the cowherd-maiden Rādhā, charting their passion, estrangement, jealousy, longing in separation, and ecstatic reunion. Structured around twenty-four ashtapadis (eight-line songs) set to specified ragas and talas, it is meant for singing and dance and is famous for its musical, sensuous diction.
SA_GK19_jaygit1u.history: Jayadeva was a poet at the court of King Lakṣmaṇasena of Bengal; he is reckoned among that court's 'five jewels.' The Gītagovinda became enormously influential across India, was integrated into the ritual at the Jagannātha temple in Puri, and decisively shaped later Radha-Krishna bhakti devotion and regional music and painting.

SA_GK19_kakumspu.title: Kumārasambhava
SA_GK19_kakumspu.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK19_kakumspu.summary: A Sanskrit mahakavya, 'The Birth of Kumāra,' narrating the courtship and union of Śiva and Pārvatī leading toward the birth of the war-god Kumāra (Kārttikeya), who is destined to slay the demon Tāraka. It includes the burning of Kāma (the god of love) by Śiva's third eye, Pārvatī's austerities, and the celebrated descriptions of their wedding and love.
SA_GK19_kakumspu.history: Attributed to Kālidāsa, the greatest poet of classical Sanskrit, usually dated to the Gupta era. The poem's authentic portion is generally held to be the first eight cantos; later cantos describing the consummation are often considered spurious or doubtful additions.

SA_GK19_kskala_u.title: Kalāvilāsa
SA_GK19_kskala_u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_kskala_u.summary: A satirical Sanskrit poem in ten cantos (over 500 stanzas) framed as the worldly-wise instruction given by the swindler Muladeva to a naive young man, cataloguing the arts of deceit practiced across society. It exposes the tricks of merchants, physicians, astrologers, courtesans, scribes, ascetics and others, holding a mirror to greed, hypocrisy and folly.
SA_GK19_kskala_u.history: Kṣemendra was a prolific 11th-century Kashmiri polymath and poet who wrote across genres including poetics (Aucityavicāracarcā) and several biting social satires. The Kalāvilāsa is one of his best-known satirical works, alongside the Narmamālā, Deśopadeśa and Samayamātṛkā.

SA_GK19_livasau.title: Līlāvatīsāra
SA_GK19_livasau.author: Jinaratna (Jinaratnasūri)
SA_GK19_livasau.summary: A Sanskrit narrative poem in twenty-one cantos (utsāhas) recounting the successive rebirths of a group of souls on their path toward liberation, framed as edifying tales told to Queen Līlāvatī and King Sinha by the monk Samarasena. The interlinked stories illustrate the karmic consequences of attachment and violence and promote the Jain ethic of nonviolence and renunciation.
SA_GK19_livasau.history: Jinaratna completed the work in 1285 CE at Jābāliputra (Jhalor, Rajasthan) as a Sanskrit abridgement of Jineśvarasūri's much larger Prakrit (Jain Māhārāṣṭrī) Nivvāṇalīlāvaīkahā of 1036 CE. By recasting it in Sanskrit, Jinaratna gave the work a far wider pan-Indian learned readership.

SA_GK19_meghdk_u.title: Meghadūta
SA_GK19_meghdk_u.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK19_meghdk_u.summary: A short lyric Sanskrit poem, 'The Cloud Messenger,' in which a yaksha exiled to central India by his lord Kubera persuades a passing monsoon cloud to carry a message of love to his pining wife in the Himalayan city of Alakā. The first half describes the geography and landscape along the cloud's northward route; the second evokes the city, the bereft wife, and the tender message itself.
SA_GK19_meghdk_u.history: Attributed to Kālidāsa, this poem effectively founded the dūtakavya (messenger-poem) genre and inspired countless imitations across later Sanskrit and vernacular literatures. It is celebrated for its meditative tone and its mastery of the mandākrāntā meter.

SA_GK19_nkalivpu.title: Kaliviḍambana
SA_GK19_nkalivpu.author: Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita
SA_GK19_nkalivpu.summary: A satirical Sanskrit poem of about 102 stanzas, 'A Mockery of the Kali Age,' lampooning the corrupt and absurd types of the contemporary world. With sharp wit it skewers venal poets, quack physicians, greedy priests, fraudulent astrologers and mantra-sorcerers, hypocritical ascetics, and sycophantic courtiers as emblems of the degenerate present age.
SA_GK19_nkalivpu.history: Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita was a major poet-scholar and minister active at the Nāyaka court of Madurai in the early 17th century, and grandnephew of the philosopher Appayya Dīkṣita. He wrote across many genres (mahakavya, devotional stotra, Śrīvidyā works); the Kaliviḍambana is among his most popular pieces for its incisive humor.

SA_GK19_ragh.title: Raghuvaṃśa
SA_GK19_ragh.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK19_ragh.summary: A Sanskrit mahakavya in nineteen cantos chronicling the dynasty of the solar (Raghu) kings of Ayodhyā, from idealized ancestors such as Dilīpa, Raghu and Aja, through the central life of Rāma (a compressed retelling of the Rāmāyaṇa), and on to his lesser successors down to the decadent Agnivarṇa. It presents a model of ideal kingship alongside the eventual decline of the line.
SA_GK19_ragh.history: Attributed to Kālidāsa, the Raghuvaṃśa is regarded as one of the supreme achievements of Sanskrit court epic and a touchstone for later poets and commentators (notably Mallinātha). Its dynastic frame allowed Kālidāsa to range across narrative, description, ethics and statecraft.

SA_GK19_ruphamdu.title: Haṃsadūta
SA_GK19_ruphamdu.author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
SA_GK19_ruphamdu.summary: A Sanskrit messenger-poem in which Lalitā, a confidante of Rādhā, dispatches a swan (haṃsa) to carry a message of love and anguished separation to Krishna, who has departed Vraja for distant Mathurā/Dvārakā. The poem is an intense expression of vipralambha-rasa (the mood of love-in-separation) central to Gauḍīya devotional aesthetics.
SA_GK19_ruphamdu.history: Rūpa Gosvāmī was one of the Six Gosvāmins of Vṛndāvana and the foremost theologian-poet of the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava movement inspired by Caitanya. Modeled on Kālidāsa's Meghadūta, the Haṃsadūta is one of his two dūta-kāvyas (with the Uddhava-sandeśa) and applies the classical messenger-poem form to Caitanyaite devotion. This is distinct from the earlier Haṃsasandeśa of Vedānta Deśika.

SA_GK19_utraca.title: Uttararāmacarita
SA_GK19_utraca.author: Bhavabhūti
SA_GK19_utraca.summary: A seven-act Sanskrit play, 'The Later Deeds of Rāma,' dramatizing the events after Rāma's coronation: his painful banishment of the pregnant, innocent Sītā in response to public rumor, her refuge in Vālmīki's hermitage, the upbringing of their twin sons Lava and Kuśa, and an eventual reconciliation. It is celebrated for its sustained pathos (karuṇa-rasa) and profound exploration of grief and conjugal love.
SA_GK19_utraca.history: Bhavabhūti was a learned poet-dramatist patronized at the court of King Yaśovarman of Kanauj in the early 8th century. The Uttararāmacarita is widely regarded as his masterpiece and one of the most emotionally powerful of all Sanskrit dramas.

SA_GK20_anrghu.title: Anargharāghava
SA_GK20_anrghu.author: Murāri
SA_GK20_anrghu.summary: A seven-act Sanskrit play dramatizing the Rāmāyaṇa story, 'Rāghava (Rāma) Beyond Price,' which recasts the epic narrative as a sequence of political intrigues and battles interwoven with lyrical passages on love, war, honor, myth and the cities and regions of ancient India. It is admired (and sometimes criticized) for its dense, learned, highly ornate poetic style.
SA_GK20_anrghu.history: The Anargharāghava is the only surviving work of Murāri, a Brahmin court poet who lived between the 8th and 10th centuries, probably in Odisha or neighboring South India. Murāri (styled 'bāla-Vālmīki') was much quoted by later poeticians and remained a model of elevated dramatic poetry.

SA_GK20_ksakunpu.title: Abhijñānaśākuntala
SA_GK20_ksakunpu.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK20_ksakunpu.summary: A seven-act Sanskrit play, 'The Recognition of Śakuntalā,' telling how King Duṣyanta falls in love with and secretly weds the hermitage-maiden Śakuntalā; a sage's curse makes him forget her until a signet ring restores his memory, after which the lovers and their son Bharata are reunited. Based on an episode of the Mahābhārata, it is the most celebrated of all Sanskrit dramas.
SA_GK20_ksakunpu.history: Attributed to Kālidāsa, the play won early European fame through Sir William Jones's 1789 translation, which strongly influenced Romantic writers such as Goethe. It survives in several recensions (notably the Devanāgarī and Bengālī), which differ in detail.

SA_GK20_mrtiu.title: Mṛcchakaṭikā
SA_GK20_mrtiu.author: Śūdraka (traditional/legendary attribution)
SA_GK20_mrtiu.date_estimate: disputed; c. 2nd c. BCE-5th c. CE, commonly placed c. 3rd-5th c. CE
SA_GK20_mrtiu.summary: A ten-act Sanskrit prakaraṇa, 'The Little Clay Cart,' depicting the love between the impoverished but noble Brahmin merchant Cārudatta and the wealthy, virtuous courtesan Vasantasenā, set against a backdrop of political revolution, a villainous royal brother-in-law, a false murder charge, and a courtroom drama. Uniquely, it draws its characters not from myth but from everyday urban life—gamblers, thieves, courtesans and rogues.
SA_GK20_mrtiu.history: The play is attributed in its prologue to a king named Śūdraka, whose identity and date remain wholly obscure; much of the plot overlaps with the (incomplete) Cārudatta attributed to Bhāsa, suggesting a shared or borrowed source. It is renowned for its realism, humor and social breadth.

SA_GK20_urmc_u.title: Uttararāmacarita
SA_GK20_urmc_u.author: Bhavabhūti
SA_GK20_urmc_u.summary: A seven-act Sanskrit play, 'The Later Deeds of Rāma,' dramatizing events after Rāma's coronation: his anguished exile of the innocent pregnant Sītā under pressure of public scandal, her shelter in Vālmīki's hermitage, the raising of the twins Lava and Kuśa, and the eventual reunion. It is famed for its deep pathos and meditation on grief and married love. (This record duplicates the same work as GK19utraca, with a variant transliteration of the title.)
SA_GK20_urmc_u.history: Bhavabhūti, patronized at the court of Yaśovarman of Kanauj in the early 8th century, is generally regarded as the foremost Sanskrit dramatist after Kālidāsa, and the Uttararāmacarita is considered his masterpiece for its emotional depth.

SA_GK21_brkas_pu.title: Bṛhatkathāślokasaṃgraha
SA_GK21_brkas_pu.author: Budhasvāmin
SA_GK21_brkas_pu.date_estimate: c. 7th-9th c. CE (uncertain; often placed around 8th-9th c.)
SA_GK21_brkas_pu.summary: A Sanskrit verse abridgement, 'Verse Epitome of the Great Story,' rendering the lost Bṛhatkathā of Guṇāḍhya (originally in the Paiśācī Prakrit) into elegant Sanskrit ślokas. The surviving (incomplete) text recounts the youthful adventures, loves and exploits of Prince Naravāhanadatta, the legendary emperor-to-be of the vidyādharas.
SA_GK21_brkas_pu.history: Budhasvāmin is known only by this work; he is usually thought to belong to Nepal or the northwest and is variously dated from the 7th to the 9th century. His version is regarded as preserving the lost Bṛhatkathā more faithfully than the better-known Kashmiri reworkings, the Bṛhatkathāmañjarī (Kṣemendra) and the Kathāsaritsāgara (Somadeva).

SA_GK21_hitop_u.title: Hitopadeśa
SA_GK21_hitop_u.author: Nārāyaṇa
SA_GK21_hitop_u.genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (kathā with embedded subhāṣita verses)
SA_GK21_hitop_u.date_estimate: core composed c. 9th-10th c. CE (some place it 800-950 CE); oldest surviving manuscripts c. 12th c.; disputed
SA_GK21_hitop_u.summary: A collection of moral and political fables in mixed prose and verse, in which animal characters illustrate principles of practical wisdom (nīti), friendship, statecraft, war and peace. Structured as instruction given by a sage to ignorant princes, it is divided into four books: Mitralābha (acquiring friends), Suhṛdbheda (separating friends), Vigraha (war) and Sandhi (peace). It draws heavily on the Pañcatantra and other gnomic sources.
SA_GK21_hitop_u.history: Attributed to Nārāyaṇa, who in the colophon credits a patron Dhaval(a)candra; it likely originated in eastern India under the Pāla dynasty. It quotes works datable to the 8th c., so must postdate them. It became one of the most widely copied and translated Sanskrit texts, frequently used as a beginner's reader in Sanskrit.

SA_GK21_sokss.title: Kathāsaritsāgara
SA_GK21_sokss.author: Somadeva (Somadeva Bhaṭṭa)
SA_GK21_sokss.summary: An enormous collection of interlinked tales and legends ('Ocean of the Streams of Story'), framed around the adventures of prince Naravāhanadatta. It comprises some 21,000+ verses arranged in 18 books (lambakas) and 124 chapters (taraṅgas), embedding hundreds of stories within stories, including romance, folklore, magic, and didactic tales.
SA_GK21_sokss.history: Composed in Kashmir by the Brahmin poet Somadeva for the entertainment of queen Sūryavatī, wife of King Anantadeva. It is a Sanskrit reworking of the lost Paiśācī-language Bṛhatkathā of Guṇāḍhya, parallel to Kṣemendra's Bṛhatkathāmañjarī. It is a major source for comparative folklore studies (translated by C. H. Tawney).

SA_GK21_suksaptu.title: Śuka-saptati
SA_GK21_suksaptu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GK21_suksaptu.genre: kathā / frame-tale collection (nīti and erotic fables)
SA_GK21_suksaptu.date_estimate: c. 12th c. CE (text in present form); older oral material; disputed
SA_GK21_suksaptu.summary: The 'Seventy Tales of the Parrot' is a frame-story collection in which a pet parrot tells seventy nightly stories to a merchant's wife to dissuade her from going to meet a lover while her husband is away, keeping her engaged until the husband returns. The embedded tales are largely about cunning, infidelity, and clever escapes, told in mixed prose and verse, and exist in textus simplicior and textus ornatior recensions.
SA_GK21_suksaptu.history: Of anonymous authorship, the work circulated widely and was translated into Persian (Ṭūṭī-nāma) and many other languages. It is one of the classic Indian frame-narrative collections alongside the Pañcatantra and Vetālapañcaviṃśati.

SA_GK22_msubhs.title: Mahāsubhāṣitasaṅgraha
SA_GK22_msubhs.author: anonymous/traditional (modern critical compilation ed. by L. Sternbach and others)
SA_GK22_msubhs.date_estimate: verses span roughly 1st millennium BCE - 2nd millennium CE; modern critical edition 20th c.
SA_GK22_msubhs.summary: The 'Great Collection of Fine Sayings' is a vast alphabetically arranged compendium of Sanskrit subhāṣitas—epigrammatic verses on ethics, politics, love, devotion and worldly wisdom—drawn from a huge range of literary, didactic and religious sources. It aims to be the most comprehensive corpus of Sanskrit quotations.
SA_GK22_msubhs.history: It is a scholarly compilation project of the Vishveshvaranand Vedic Research Institute (Hoshiarpur), largely associated with Ludwik Sternbach, continuing the earlier collections of Böhtlingk and Aufrecht. As such it is an editorial anthology of pre-existing verses rather than a single-author classical text.

SA_GK22_vispancu_e.title: Pañcatantra
SA_GK22_vispancu_e.author: traditionally Viṣṇuśarma(n) (anonymous redaction)
SA_GK22_vispancu_e.genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (frame tales in prose and verse)
SA_GK22_vispancu_e.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (commonly placed c. 200 BCE-300 CE); disputed
SA_GK22_vispancu_e.summary: A celebrated collection of interwoven animal fables teaching nīti (practical wisdom and statecraft), framed as instruction composed by the Brahmin Viṣṇuśarma to educate three dull princes. It is organized into five books (tantras): the loss of friends, the gaining of friends, war and peace (crows and owls), loss of gains, and ill-considered action.
SA_GK22_vispancu_e.history: The original is lost and survives in many recensions (e.g., the Tantrākhyāyika, Pūrṇabhadra's recension, the Southern Pañcatantra). It is the most widely diffused work of Indian narrative literature, translated via the Pahlavi/Arabic Kalīla wa-Dimna into dozens of languages, influencing world fable traditions.

SA_GK22_vispancu_e_olivelle.title: Pañcatantra
SA_GK22_vispancu_e_olivelle.author: Viṣṇuśarma(n) (traditional attribution)
SA_GK22_vispancu_e_olivelle.genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (frame tales in prose and verse)
SA_GK22_vispancu_e_olivelle.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (commonly placed c. 200 BCE-300 CE); disputed
SA_GK22_vispancu_e_olivelle.summary: A celebrated collection of interwoven animal fables teaching nīti (practical wisdom and statecraft), framed as instruction composed by the Brahmin Viṣṇuśarma to educate three dull princes. It is organized into five books (tantras): the loss of friends, the gaining of friends, war and peace (crows and owls), loss of gains, and ill-considered action.
SA_GK22_vispancu_e_olivelle.history: The original is lost and survives in many recensions (e.g., the Tantrākhyāyika, Pūrṇabhadra's recension, the Southern Pañcatantra). It is the most widely diffused work of Indian narrative literature, transmitted via the Arabic Kalīla wa-Dimna into world literature. (Duplicate batch entry of GK22vispancu with author hint Viṣṇuśarma.)

SA_GK22_vispancu.title: Pañcatantra
SA_GK22_vispancu.author: Viṣṇuśarma(n) (traditional attribution)
SA_GK22_vispancu.genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (frame tales in prose and verse)
SA_GK22_vispancu.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (commonly placed c. 200 BCE-300 CE); disputed
SA_GK22_vispancu.summary: A celebrated collection of interwoven animal fables teaching nīti (practical wisdom and statecraft), framed as instruction composed by the Brahmin Viṣṇuśarma to educate three dull princes. It is organized into five books (tantras): the loss of friends, the gaining of friends, war and peace, loss of gains, and ill-considered action.
SA_GK22_vispancu.history: The original is lost and survives in many recensions. It is the most widely diffused work of Indian narrative literature, transmitted via the Arabic Kalīla wa-Dimna into world literature. (Second duplicate batch entry of GK22vispancu with author hint Viṣṇuśarma.)

SA_GP10_bhp.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa
SA_GP10_bhp.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp.date_estimate: final redaction c. 9th-10th c. CE; broader range 500-1000 CE; disputed
SA_GP10_bhp.summary: One of the eighteen mahāpurāṇas and the central scripture of Kṛṣṇa-bhakti, comprising twelve books (skandhas) and around 18,000 verses. Its tenth book, narrating Kṛṣṇa's life and the rāsa-līlā with the gopīs, is the most influential, and the text develops a sophisticated theology of loving devotion to Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa alongside cosmology, genealogies and philosophy.
SA_GP10_bhp.history: Traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, modern scholars regard the author as composite; the text shows South Indian (Āḻvār) influence and was known by c. 1030 CE (al-Bīrūnī, Abhinavagupta). It became foundational for later Vaiṣṇava movements, especially the Gauḍīya tradition, and attracted numerous commentaries (e.g., Śrīdhara Svāmin's Bhāvārthadīpikā).

SA_GP11_brahmppu.title: Brahma-Purāṇa
SA_GP11_brahmppu.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP11_brahmppu.date_estimate: composite; core perhaps c. 8th-10th c. CE, with much earlier and later material; later portions to c. 13th c.
SA_GP11_brahmppu.summary: Traditionally listed first among the eighteen mahāpurāṇas, the Brahma-Purāṇa is encyclopedic, covering cosmogony, genealogies, dharma, and especially extensive māhātmyas (glorifications) of sacred sites such as Purūṣottama (Jagannātha Pūrī/Odisha), Konārka, and the Godavarī region. Despite its name it is largely devotional toward Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa and Sūrya as well as Śiva.
SA_GP11_brahmppu.history: Of anonymous, composite authorship, it is a heterogeneous compilation whose tīrtha-māhātmya sections were added over time; scholars treat large parts as borrowed from other purāṇas. It is valued for its detailed accounts of pilgrimage geography, particularly Odishan sites.

SA_GP11_padmpp.title: Padma-Purāṇa
SA_GP11_padmpp.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP11_padmpp.tradition: Hindu (predominantly Vaiṣṇava, with Śaiva and other material)
SA_GP11_padmpp.date_estimate: composite; core c. 4th-8th c. CE with extensive additions to c. 12th-16th c.; disputed
SA_GP11_padmpp.summary: One of the largest mahāpurāṇas (around 55,000 verses), named after the lotus (padma) from which Brahmā emerged. It is encyclopedic, treating cosmogony, geography, dharma, vows, festivals, and numerous tīrtha-māhātmyas, and contains heavily Vaiṣṇava devotional sections glorifying Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa and Rāma.
SA_GP11_padmpp.history: Anonymous and strongly composite, it survives in markedly different North Indian and South Indian recensions, with many sectarian interpolations (including the often-late Uttarakhaṇḍa). It is an important source for later Vaiṣṇava bhakti and ritual literature.

SA_GP12_agp_bi_u.title: Agni-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_agp_bi_u.author: anonymous/traditional (narrated by Agni to Vasiṣṭha)
SA_GP12_agp_bi_u.date_estimate: composite; core perhaps c. 8th-9th c. CE, with material ranging earlier and later; disputed
SA_GP12_agp_bi_u.summary: An encyclopedic mahāpurāṇa framed as Agni's teaching to Vasiṣṭha, remarkable for covering an exceptionally wide range of subjects: cosmology, ritual and tantra, iconography and temple architecture, statecraft, law, grammar, poetics and metrics, medicine, gemmology, and martial arts (dhanurveda). It functions almost as a manual of classical Indian knowledge.
SA_GP12_agp_bi_u.history: Of anonymous, composite authorship, it is one of the most heterogeneous purāṇas, with sections evidently drawn from many specialist treatises. It is prized by scholars as a source for the history of Indian sciences, arts, and ritual practice.

SA_GP12_garup.title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_garup.author: anonymous/traditional (narrated by Viṣṇu to Garuḍa)
SA_GP12_garup.date_estimate: composite; core c. 8th-10th c. CE with later additions; disputed
SA_GP12_garup.summary: A Vaiṣṇava mahāpurāṇa presented as Viṣṇu's discourse to his mount Garuḍa. It is encyclopedic—covering cosmology, dharma, gemmology, medicine and ritual—but is best known for its Preta-kalpa / Uttarakhaṇḍa, which deals extensively with death, the afterlife, funerary rites (śrāddha), the journey of the soul, and rebirth, making it widely used in Hindu mortuary contexts.
SA_GP12_garup.history: Anonymous and composite, it survives in varying recensions and incorporates earlier technical material. Its eschatological sections gave it a distinctive ritual role, recited during the mourning period in many Hindu communities.

SA_GP12_sivap.title: Śiva-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_sivap.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_sivap.date_estimate: composite; redactions largely c. 10th-14th c. CE, with earlier core material; the project hint of 400-600 CE reflects only the oldest layers and is too early for the extant text
SA_GP12_sivap.summary: The principal Śaiva mahāpurāṇa, glorifying Śiva and devotion to him, organized into saṃhitās (such as Vidyeśvara, Rudra, Śatarudra, Koṭirudra, Umā, Kailāsa, Vāyavīya). It narrates Śiva's mythology and forms, the marriage of Śiva and Pārvatī, the origin and worship of the liṅga, the jyotirliṅgas, and Śaiva cosmology, vows, and ritual.
SA_GP12_sivap.history: Anonymous and strongly composite, the Śiva-Purāṇa exists in several recensions and its contents overlap with the Vāyu and Liṅga Purāṇas; its present form is the result of layered redaction. It is a foundational devotional and ritual text for Śaiva traditions across India.

SA_GP12_skp.title: Skanda-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_skp.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GP12_skp.date_estimate: earliest core c. 570-650 CE (Bakker), with continuous accretion over later centuries; the largest mahāpurāṇa
SA_GP12_skp.summary: The longest of the mahāpurāṇas (c. 81,000 verses across recensions), named after Skanda (Kārttikeya), son of Śiva. It is dominated by Śaiva mythology and, above all, by vast collections of tīrtha-māhātmyas sanctifying pilgrimage sites and sacred geography across the subcontinent, alongside ritual, vows, and legends.
SA_GP12_skp.history: Anonymous and highly composite, it survives in an older 'original' Skandapurāṇa (preserved in early Nepalese manuscripts and critically edited by Adriaensen, Bakker, Isaacson and others) and in a much larger vulgate of semi-independent khaṇḍas. Its core has been dated by Bakker to c. AD 570-620; it grew enormously by accretion of regional sthala-purāṇas.

SA_GP12_vipce.title: Viṣṇu-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_vipce.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Parāśara/Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_vipce.date_estimate: core c. 4th-5th c. CE (sometimes extended 1st c. BCE-4th c. CE); regarded as one of the older mahāpurāṇas
SA_GP12_vipce.summary: A model Vaiṣṇava mahāpurāṇa, framed as Parāśara's reply to Maitreya, that conforms closely to the classical pañcalakṣaṇa definition of a purāṇa (creation, dissolution, genealogies of gods and kings, manvantaras, dynastic history). Across its six books it teaches devotion to Viṣṇu and includes the Kṛṣṇa story, cosmology, geography, and an account of the Kali age.
SA_GP12_vipce.history: Of anonymous, composite authorship, it is comparatively well-organized and old, and was influential as a paradigmatic purāṇa; H. H. Wilson's 19th-c. translation made it the first purāṇa widely known in the West. It is foundational for Vaiṣṇava theology and was drawn upon by the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa.

SA_GR14_garsa.title: Garga Saṃhitā
SA_GR14_garsa.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the sage Garga / Gargamuni)
SA_GR14_garsa.date_estimate: uncertain; in present form likely medieval (c. 16th-17th c. CE for the popular Vaiṣṇava recension), traditionally ascribed to ancient Garga
SA_GR14_garsa.summary: A Vaiṣṇava scripture narrating the līlā (pastimes) of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa in seventeen cantos (khaṇḍas), attributed to Garga, the family priest (purohita) of the Yadu/Yadava clan. It elaborates Kṛṣṇa's life with strong emphasis on Rādhā and the gopīs and is noted as an early text linking Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa with festivals such as Holi.
SA_GR14_garsa.history: Though attributed to the ancient sage Garga, the popular text is not among the eighteen mahāpurāṇas and is generally treated as a later Vaiṣṇava (often Gauḍīya-associated) composition with Puranic features. There are also distinct astrological/jyotiṣa works bearing the name Garga-Saṃhitā, which should not be confused with this devotional text.

SA_GR14_parasa.title: (title not given in batch; work_id 'GR14parasa' — most plausibly Parāśara-Saṃhitā / Parāśara-Smṛti)
SA_GR14_parasa.author: attributed to the sage Parāśara (anonymous/traditional)
SA_GR14_parasa.tradition: Hindu (dharmaśāstra; alternatively a Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇava saṃhitā)
SA_GR14_parasa.genre: dharmaśāstra / smṛti (law-code) — or, if a Pāñcarātra text, āgama/saṃhitā
SA_GR14_parasa.date_estimate: uncertain; if Parāśara-Smṛti, core perhaps c. 1st-5th c. CE with later medieval commentary
SA_GR14_parasa.summary: The title is blank in the batch and the identification is uncertain. Given the GR14 grouping (Vaiṣṇava/Gauḍīya texts) and the stem 'parasa', this most likely refers to the Parāśara-Saṃhitā / Parāśara-Smṛti, a dharmaśāstra attributed to the sage Parāśara and traditionally regarded as the law-code (ācāra/prāyaścitta) appropriate for the Kali age. Alternatively it could denote a Pāñcarātra Parāśara-Saṃhitā.
SA_GR14_parasa.history: The Parāśara-Smṛti received a famous medieval commentary, the Parāśara-Mādhavīya, by Mādhavācārya (Sāyaṇa-Mādhava), which greatly expanded its legal authority. Because the batch title is empty, this identification should be treated cautiously.

SA_GR14_vedsy.title: Vedānta-Syāmantaka
SA_GR14_vedsy.author: Rādhā-Dāmodara Gosvāmin (with a gloss/commentary by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa)
SA_GR14_vedsy.date_estimate: c. early 18th c. CE (Baladeva's gloss); the base text by Rādhā-Dāmodara somewhat earlier (late 17th-early 18th c.)
SA_GR14_vedsy.summary: A concise systematic exposition of Gauḍīya Vedānta. After an opening treatment of epistemology (pramāṇas), its chapters expound the five core categories: īśvara (the Supreme Lord), jīva (the living being), prakṛti (material nature), kāla (time) and karma (action).
SA_GR14_vedsy.history: The base work is by Rādhā-Dāmodara Gosvāmin, teacher of Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa; Baladeva—best known for the Govinda-bhāṣya commentary on the Brahmasūtra—added a gloss preserved in only a few rare manuscripts and recently published by the Jiva Institute. It is a compact doctrinal handbook of the Caitanya (Gauḍīya) school.

SA_GR14_vrima.title: Vṛndāvana-Mahimāmṛta
SA_GR14_vrima.author: Prabodhānanda Sarasvatī
SA_GR14_vrima.summary: The 'Nectar of the Glories of Vṛndāvana' is a devotional Sanskrit poem in seventeen śatakas (centuries of verses) exclusively glorifying the sacred land of Vṛndāvana and its inhabitants. It exalts Vṛndāvana as the supreme abode—the eternal stage of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa's pastimes, said to surpass even Vaikuṇṭha—and expresses the poet's intense longing to reside there in pure devotion.
SA_GR14_vrima.history: Composed by Prabodhānanda Sarasvatī, an intimate associate within the circle of Caitanya Mahāprabhu's followers, it became an influential text of Gauḍīya devotional (bhakti/rāgānuga) literature and is widely cited in mañjarī-bhāva meditation traditions.

SA_GS38_kautil.title: Arthaśāstra
SA_GS38_kautil.author: Kauṭilya (Cāṇakya / Viṣṇugupta); composite, multiple hands
SA_GS38_kautil.genre: śāstra — treatise on statecraft, political economy and governance
SA_GS38_kautil.date_estimate: composite, c. 2nd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE for the redaction; earlier arthaśāstra layers from c. 2nd c. BCE; traditional dating places Kauṭilya in the late 4th c. BCE (Mauryan)
SA_GS38_kautil.summary: A comprehensive treatise on rājan, the science of governance, covering administration, law, taxation, economic policy, espionage, diplomacy, warfare, and the conduct of the ideal king and his ministers. Organized into 15 books (adhikaraṇas), it is famed for its hard-headed, pragmatic ('realpolitik') approach to power, the saptanga theory of the state, and the maṇḍala theory of interstate relations.
SA_GS38_kautil.history: Traditionally attributed to Kauṭilya, the minister of Candragupta Maurya, the text was rediscovered in 1905 (manuscript edited by R. Shamasastry). Modern scholarship (McClish, Olivelle) sees it as composite, with at least three overlapping layers reaching its present form between the 1st and 3rd c. CE, building on earlier arthaśāstra traditions; its authorship and date remain debated.

SA_GS39_kamasufu.title: Kāmasūtra
SA_GS39_kamasufu.author: Vātsyāyana (Mallanāga)
SA_GS39_kamasufu.genre: sūtra / śāstra (treatise on erotics and the art of living)
SA_GS39_kamasufu.date_estimate: c. 3rd century CE (range 2nd-4th c. CE; final form 3rd-5th c. CE)
SA_GS39_kamasufu.summary: The foundational Sanskrit treatise on kāma (pleasure, desire, eros), covering courtship, marriage, sexual technique, the conduct of the urbane man-about-town (nāgaraka), the lives of courtesans, and the use of aphrodisiacs and intrigues. Organized into seven books, it presents an analytical, worldly account of erotic and social life rather than a mere manual, situating kāma within the three aims of human life alongside dharma and artha.
SA_GS39_kamasufu.history: Attributed to Vātsyāyana, whose name survives in the colophon though almost nothing else is known of him; he states he is condensing earlier (now lost) works on the subject. Composed probably in northern India (perhaps near Pāṭaliputra), it became the standard authority on kāmaśāstra and attracted commentaries, most famously the Jayamaṅgalā of Yaśodhara (13th c.).

SA_GS40_caraka.title: Carakasaṃhitā
SA_GS40_caraka.author: Agniveśa (original); redacted by Caraka and later by Dṛḍhabala
SA_GS40_caraka.date_estimate: core c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE (Caraka redaction); Dṛḍhabala's final redaction c. 4th c. CE; disputed
SA_GS40_caraka.summary: One of the two foundational classics of Āyurveda, dealing chiefly with internal medicine (kāyacikitsā). It systematizes the theory of the three doṣas, diagnosis, pathology, pharmacology, dietetics, ethics of the physician, and the philosophy of life and health, organized into eight sthānas (sections).
SA_GS40_caraka.history: Traditionally derived from the teachings of Ātreya Punarvasu, compiled by his pupil Agniveśa as the Agniveśa-tantra, then redacted by Caraka and partly lost; the missing portions were supplied by Dṛḍhabala (c. 4th c. CE). Its authoritative medieval commentary is the Āyurvedadīpikā of Cakrapāṇidatta (11th c.). It remains a cornerstone of classical Indian medicine.

SA_GS40_susruta.title: Suśrutasaṃhitā
SA_GS40_susruta.author: Suśruta (with later redactors)
SA_GS40_susruta.date_estimate: core perhaps mid-1st millennium BCE; text as preserved c. 3rd-4th c. CE; disputed
SA_GS40_susruta.summary: The classic Āyurvedic treatise specializing in śalyatantra (surgery), describing surgical instruments, procedures (including plastic and cataract surgery), wound management, anatomy via dissection, toxicology, and obstetrics, alongside general medicine. It transmits the teachings ascribed to the sage Divodāsa Dhanvantari of Kāśī to his pupil Suśruta.
SA_GS40_susruta.history: Attributed to Suśruta, traditionally placed in ancient Vārāṇasī; scholars now hold that multiple authors contributed and that the surviving recension was reworked into the early centuries CE, with the Uttaratantra a later addition often credited to Nāgārjuna. Its principal commentary is the Nibandhasaṃgraha of Ḍalhaṇa (12th c.). It is renowned as one of the earliest systematic surgical texts.

SA_GSD36_manu_o.title: Manusmṛti
SA_GSD36_manu_o.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Manu)
SA_GSD36_manu_o.genre: dharmaśāstra (treatise on law, duty, and social order)
SA_GSD36_manu_o.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (range 2nd c. BCE-3rd c. CE)
SA_GSD36_manu_o.summary: The most influential of the Dharmaśāstras, a versified treatise in twelve chapters laying out the duties (dharma) of the varṇas and stages of life, kingship and statecraft, civil and criminal law, marriage and inheritance, purity, penance, and karma and rebirth. This record (work_id suffix _o) is a variant/version of the same Mānava-Dharmaśāstra.
SA_GSD36_manu_o.history: Ascribed to the primordial lawgiver Manu but composed by an unknown author or redactor; Patrick Olivelle argues for a single organizing author and dates it to the 2nd-3rd c. CE on the basis of vocabulary and the mention of gold coins. It accreted numerous commentaries (Medhātithi, Govindarāja, Kullūka) and became the paradigmatic Hindu legal text, with an enormous and contested modern reception.

SA_GSD36_manu.title: Manusmṛti
SA_GSD36_manu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Manu)
SA_GSD36_manu.genre: dharmaśāstra (treatise on law, duty, and social order)
SA_GSD36_manu.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (range 2nd c. BCE-3rd c. CE)
SA_GSD36_manu.summary: The Mānava-Dharmaśāstra, a versified treatise in twelve chapters on dharma: the duties of the varṇas and life-stages, royal governance and statecraft, the eighteen titles of law, marriage, inheritance, ritual purity, penance, and the workings of karma and rebirth. It is the single most authoritative and most cited work of the Hindu legal tradition.
SA_GSD36_manu.history: Traditionally attributed to Manu but of unknown authorship; Olivelle's critical edition argues for composition by one gifted author or editor around the 2nd-3rd c. CE. It generated a major commentarial literature (Medhātithi, Kullūka, Govindarāja, Rāghavānanda) and remained the touchstone of dharmaśāstra; this entry duplicates work_id GSD36manu_o (a different recension/copy of the same work).

SA_GSD36_manubhau.title: Manubhāṣya
SA_GSD36_manubhau.author: Medhātithi
SA_GSD36_manubhau.summary: The earliest fully surviving and most learned commentary on the Manusmṛti, the Manubhāṣya glosses Manu's verses, expounds disputed topics, raises and answers objections of opponents, and applies Mīmāṃsā interpretive principles to harmonize the legal material. It is prized for its philosophical depth and breadth of citation.
SA_GSD36_manubhau.history: Composed by Medhātithi, very probably a Kashmiri scholar usually dated to the 9th-10th century; the text survives incompletely and was partly restored from later sources. It became the most authoritative voice in the Manu commentarial tradition and was heavily drawn upon by later commentators and digest-writers.

SA_GSD36_visnu.title: Viṣṇusmṛti
SA_GSD36_visnu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Viṣṇu)
SA_GSD36_visnu.date_estimate: c. 7th-10th c. CE (Olivelle: probably 700-1000 CE, Kashmir); older limits 300 BCE-1000 CE
SA_GSD36_visnu.summary: Also called the Vaiṣṇava-Dharmaśāstra or Viṣṇu-sūtra, a legal-religious text largely in prose with framing verses, cast as a dialogue in which the god Viṣṇu instructs the Earth-goddess. It covers the usual dharma topics (varṇa duties, law, penance, śrāddha) but is distinctive for its strong bhakti orientation, its prescription of daily pūjā to Viṣṇu, and its treatment of satī and pilgrimage (tīrtha).
SA_GSD36_visnu.history: Traditionally framed as revelation by Viṣṇu and built on an older Kāṭhaka Dharmasūtra core, but Patrick Olivelle argues the extant text is a late compilation made in Kashmir around 700-1000 CE, evidenced by its bhakti framing, vocabulary, references to written documents, and Vaiṣṇava iconography paralleling post-8th-century Kashmiri images. It is the only Dharmaśāstra that does not directly treat the means of knowing dharma.

SA_GSD37_apastd_u.title: Āpastamba-Dharmasūtra
SA_GSD37_apastd_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Āpastamba school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
SA_GSD37_apastd_u.date_estimate: c. 5th-3rd c. BCE (often regarded as the oldest extant Dharmasūtra)
SA_GSD37_apastd_u.summary: An early aphoristic (sūtra-style) manual of dharma forming part of the Āpastamba Kalpasūtra of the Taittirīya branch of the Black Yajurveda. It treats the conduct of the Vedic student, householder duties, rules of eating and purity, penances, inheritance, and the duties of the king, in a concrete and relatively undeveloped legal idiom.
SA_GSD37_apastd_u.history: Belongs to the ritual corpus of the Āpastamba śākhā; Olivelle regards it as the earliest surviving Dharmasūtra (predating Gautama) because of its archaic terminology and its silence on mixed castes. It survives with the commentary of Haradatta (Ujjvalā) and is a key source for the prehistory of Hindu law.

SA_GSD37_baudhasuu.title: Baudhāyana-Dharmasūtra
SA_GSD37_baudhasuu.author: anonymous/traditional (Baudhāyana school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
SA_GSD37_baudhasuu.date_estimate: c. 500-200 BCE (Kane); composite with later additions
SA_GSD37_baudhasuu.summary: An early Dharmasūtra of the Baudhāyana branch of the Taittirīya Yajurveda, in mixed prose and verse, dealing with the sources of dharma, the four life-stages, rules for students and householders, food and purity, penances, inheritance, the duties of women, and ascetic and ritual matters. It is notable for explicit discussion of regional customs (ācāra) and mixed castes.
SA_GSD37_baudhasuu.history: Part of the large Baudhāyana Kalpasūtra; it is widely held to be composite, with later strata added to an early core, complicating its dating. It survives with the commentary of Govindasvāmin and is one of the four canonical Dharmasūtras edited and translated together by Olivelle.

SA_GSD37_baudhd_u.title: Baudhāyana-Dharmasūtra
SA_GSD37_baudhd_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Baudhāyana school of the Taittirīya Yajurveda)
SA_GSD37_baudhd_u.date_estimate: late centuries BCE (Olivelle: among the four classical Dharmasūtras)
SA_GSD37_baudhd_u.summary: A late-Vedic manual prescribing the solemn (śrauta) sacrifices of the Taittirīya school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, including the agnyādhāna, agnihotra, new- and full-moon offerings, the soma rituals, and the great fire-altar (agnicayana) constructions. It is one of the earliest Śrautasūtras and shows the first movement toward the terse sūtra style. NOTE: the project metadata flags this work_id as mislabeled, but the supplied title is the Baudhāyana Śrautasūtra, which is described here.
SA_GSD37_baudhd_u.history: Belongs to the Baudhāyana Kalpasūtra and is dated to roughly 800-600 BCE, composed in eastern Uttar Pradesh during the late Brāhmaṇa period; it is famous in Indo-Aryan studies for a passage discussed in migration debates. It was critically edited by C. G. Kashikar. It is distinct from the (much later) Baudhāyana-Dharmasūtra of the same school.

SA_GSD37_gautdh_u.title: Gautama-Dharmasūtra
SA_GSD37_gautdh_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Gautama; associated with the Sāmaveda)
SA_GSD37_gautdh_u.date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (often regarded as among the oldest, possibly the oldest, Dharmasūtras)
SA_GSD37_gautdh_u.summary: The shortest and one of the earliest Dharmasūtras, written entirely in prose and traditionally attached to the Sāmaveda. It systematically treats the sources of dharma, initiation and studentship, the duties of the four varṇas and life-stages, rules of purity and food, penances, inheritance, and the law and duties of the king, in a notably orderly arrangement.
SA_GSD37_gautdh_u.history: Ascribed to a sage Gautama; Olivelle (following some predecessors) and Lingat both rank it among the earliest extant Dharmasūtras, with Lingat regarding it as the oldest. It survives with the Mitākṣarā commentary of Haradatta and the Maskarī-bhāṣya, and is one of the four core Dharmasūtras edited by Olivelle.

SA_GSD37_vasist_u.title: Vāsiṣṭha-Dharmasūtra
SA_GSD37_vasist_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Vasiṣṭha school)
SA_GSD37_vasist_u.date_estimate: c. 300-100 BCE (generally the latest of the four canonical Dharmasūtras); composite
SA_GSD37_vasist_u.summary: A Dharmasūtra in mixed prose and verse traditionally linked to the sage Vasiṣṭha and (loosely) to the Ṛgveda, treating the sources and authority of dharma, the four life-stages, the duties of varṇas, marriage and women, purity and food rules, penances, inheritance, and royal duties. It cites earlier authorities and quotes Manu, marking its relatively later position.
SA_GSD37_vasist_u.history: Generally placed last among the four classical Dharmasūtras on the basis of its developed terminology and quotations; it too is composite, with later interpolations. It survives with a commentary attributed to Kṛṣṇapaṇḍita (Vidvanmodinī) and is one of the four Dharmasūtras edited and translated by Olivelle.

SA_GSP27_yogva.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha
SA_GSP27_yogva.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vālmīki; core text = Mokṣopāya, of unknown Kashmiri authorship)
SA_GSP27_yogva.tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta with Yoga and Kashmir Śaiva/Trika influences)
SA_GSP27_yogva.date_estimate: core (Mokṣopāya) c. 10th c. CE Kashmir; expanded/Vedāntized 11th-14th c.; disputed
SA_GSP27_yogva.summary: A vast philosophical work structured as Vasiṣṭha's instruction to the young prince Rāma, teaching liberation through the illusoriness of the world, the mind-made nature of reality, and the path of knowledge, illustrated by hundreds of intricate, often fantastical, frame-stories. It expounds a non-dualism close to Advaita Vedānta and is also known in a shorter recension (Laghu-Yogavāsiṣṭha).
SA_GSP27_yogva.history: Attributed to the poet Vālmīki, but the underlying text is the Kashmiri Mokṣopāya, composed (per Slaje and Hanneder) around the 10th century and subsequently reworked into an orthodox Advaita scripture with Trika overtones between the 11th and 14th centuries; the abridgment is credited to Abhinanda of Kashmir. It became a hugely popular text of practical non-dualist spirituality.

SA_GSP28_tantrava.title: Tantravārttika (on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra / Śābarabhāṣya)
SA_GSP28_tantrava.author: Kumārila Bhaṭṭa
SA_GSP28_tantrava.date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (Kumārila fl. c. 600-660 CE)
SA_GSP28_tantrava.summary: The Tantravārttika is the second of Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's three sub-commentaries on Śabara's Bhāṣya on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra (the root text named in this entry's title), covering adhyāya 1.2 through adhyāya 3. It elaborates Mīmāṃsā theory of Vedic injunction, the authority and self-validity of scripture, rules of textual interpretation, and vigorously defends ritual orthopraxy against Buddhist and other opponents.
SA_GSP28_tantrava.history: Composed by Kumārila Bhaṭṭa, the great 7th-century champion of Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, as part of the trio Ślokavārttika, Tantravārttika, and Ṭupṭīkā. It became a central authority of the Bhāṭṭa school and exerted wide influence on later Mīmāṃsā, Dharmaśāstra exegesis, and the broader debate with Buddhism. (The entry title pairs the root Mīmāṃsāsūtra with Kumārila's vārttika.)

SA_GSP29_nyapra.title: Nyāyaprakāśa (Mīmāṃsānyāyaprakāśa / Āpadevī)
SA_GSP29_nyapra.author: Āpadeva (most likely)
SA_GSP29_nyapra.summary: On the most probable identification, this is the Mīmāṃsānyāyaprakāśa (popularly called the Āpadevī), a concise and lucid manual summarizing the essential interpretive principles (nyāyas) of the Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā system from the Bhāṭṭa standpoint. It became the standard introductory text used by pandits to teach Mīmāṃsā, prized for clarity and brevity over Kumārila's more elaborate works.
SA_GSP29_nyapra.history: Composed by Āpadeva, a scholar who flourished in the early 17th century. The bare title 'Nyāyaprakāśa' is somewhat ambiguous, but it is most plausibly this widely used Mīmāṃsā primer (edited and translated by Franklin Edgerton) rather than a Nyāya-school work; identification is therefore reasonably but not fully certain.

SA_GSP29_nyamanu.title: Nyāyamañjarī
SA_GSP29_nyamanu.author: Jayanta Bhaṭṭa
SA_GSP29_nyamanu.genre: philosophical treatise (independent commentary on the Nyāyasūtra)
SA_GSP29_nyamanu.date_estimate: late 9th c. CE (Jayanta fl. c. 820-900 CE, Kashmir)
SA_GSP29_nyamanu.summary: An encyclopedic and independent-minded exposition of Nyāya philosophy structured loosely around selected Nyāyasūtras, examining the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇas: perception, inference, comparison, testimony), the validity and authorship of the Veda, theory of meaning and the sphoṭa, the existence of God, and the refutation of rival positions (Buddhist, Mīmāṃsā, Cārvāka).
SA_GSP29_nyamanu.history: Composed in Kashmir in the latter ninth century by Jayanta Bhaṭṭa, an adviser to King Śaṅkaravarman (whom he names), giving an unusually secure date. Written in elegant, lively prose, it is a landmark in the historiography of Indian philosophy and a major source for ninth-century intellectual debate; Jayanta also wrote the satirical drama Āgamaḍambara.

SA_GSP30_mabhai.title: Manthānabhairava Tantra
SA_GSP30_mabhai.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GSP30_mabhai.tradition: Hindu (Kaula Tantra; Western Transmission / Paścimāmnāya, Kubjikā cult)
SA_GSP30_mabhai.date_estimate: c. 9th-12th c. CE (associated with the Kubjikā corpus; cult peaked 12th c.)
SA_GSP30_mabhai.summary: A large Kaula tantric scripture of the Western Transmission centered on the goddess Kubjikā ('the hunchbacked/crooked one', identified with Kuṇḍalinī) and the deity Bhairava, expounding the metaphysics, sacred geography, mantra-systems, deity hierarchies, and ritual and yogic practice of this esoteric tradition. The portion translated by Dyczkowski (Kumārikākhaṇḍa) concerns the Virgin Goddess.
SA_GSP30_mabhai.history: Belongs to the Kubjikā/Western-Kaula body of scriptures related to the Kubjikāmatatantra and was transmitted largely in Nepalese manuscripts. Mark S. G. Dyczkowski spent roughly a decade editing and translating it into a 14-volume work, making this previously secretive material accessible. The Kubjikā cult flourished especially around the 12th century.

SA_GSP30_tantralu1.title: Tantrāloka
SA_GSP30_tantralu1.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_tantralu1.tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism; Trika / Anuttara Trika, Pratyabhijñā-informed)
SA_GSP30_tantralu1.date_estimate: c. 1000 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE, Kashmir)
SA_GSP30_tantralu1.summary: Abhinavagupta's monumental synthesis of Trika Śaivism, the 'Light on the Tantras,' comprising over 5,800 verses across 37 chapters (āhnikas). It systematizes non-dual Śaiva metaphysics, the means to liberation (the upāyas), initiation, ritual, mantra, the divine energies, and the sacred calendar, interpreting tantric practice through the philosophical categories of the Pratyabhijñā. This entry is part 1 of the work as split in the project corpus.
SA_GSP30_tantralu1.history: Composed in early-11th-century Kashmir by Abhinavagupta, the greatest master of the Trika; it draws together and ranks the diverse Śaiva scriptural traditions into a coherent system. Its indispensable commentary is the Viveka of Jayaratha (12th-13th c.). It is regarded as the supreme encyclopedic statement of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism.

SA_GSP30_tantralu2.title: Tantrāloka
SA_GSP30_tantralu2.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_tantralu2.tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism; Trika / Anuttara Trika, Pratyabhijñā-informed)
SA_GSP30_tantralu2.date_estimate: c. 1000 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE, Kashmir)
SA_GSP30_tantralu2.summary: A second portion of Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka, the comprehensive 37-chapter verse exposition of non-dual Trika Śaivism covering metaphysics, the means to liberation, initiation, ritual, mantra, and tantric philosophy. This entry corresponds to part 2 of the work as divided in the project corpus and is identical in authorship and character to part 1.
SA_GSP30_tantralu2.history: Same work and author as GSP30tantralu1: written in early-11th-century Kashmir by Abhinavagupta and elucidated by Jayaratha's Viveka commentary. The split into two records is an artifact of the corpus rather than a distinct text. It is the foundational encyclopedic synthesis of Kashmir Śaiva tantra.

SA_GSP30_utipk.title: Īśvarapratyabhijñā-vṛtti
SA_GSP30_utipk.author: Utpaladeva
SA_GSP30_utipk.date_estimate: c. 925 CE (Utpaladeva fl. c. 900-950 CE, Kashmir)
SA_GSP30_utipk.summary: Utpaladeva's own concise commentary (vṛtti) on his Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the verse foundation of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') philosophy. The work argues that liberation comes through the recognition that one's own self is identical with the supreme Lord (Śiva), grounding a monistic, idealist, and theistic system in a sophisticated theory of consciousness, agency, and cognition.
SA_GSP30_utipk.history: Composed by Utpaladeva, pupil of Somānanda (the school's founder), around 925 CE; Abhinavagupta reports that the kārikās and the vṛtti were written together. The kārikās with this vṛtti became the doctrinal core of the Pratyabhijñā, later expounded in Abhinavagupta's two large commentaries (the Vimarśinī and the Vivṛtivimarśinī). It is the pivotal philosophical text of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism.

SA_GSP31_iskamatu.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
SA_GSP31_iskamatu.author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
SA_GSP31_iskamatu.date_estimate: c. 350-450 CE (Gupta era); some scholars argue as early as c. 200 CE
SA_GSP31_iskamatu.summary: The earliest surviving systematic text of the Sāṃkhya school, consisting of roughly 72 verses in the ārya metre (hence the alternative title Sāṃkhyasaptati, 'the seventy [verses] of Sāṃkhya'). It expounds Sāṃkhya's dualist metaphysics: the distinction between unconscious primordial matter (prakṛti) and the plurality of conscious selves (puruṣa), the evolution of the twenty-five principles (tattvas), the three guṇas, the theory of causation (satkāryavāda), and liberation through discriminative knowledge.
SA_GSP31_iskamatu.history: Īśvarakṛṣṇa presents himself as an heir to a teaching lineage descending from the sage Kapila through Āsuri and Pañcaśikha. The work became the foundational text of classical Sāṃkhya and attracted numerous commentaries, including the Yuktidīpikā, Gauḍapāda's bhāṣya, Vācaspati Miśra's Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī, and the Sāṃkhyavṛtti; a Chinese translation by Paramārtha (6th c.) helps fix its date.

SA_GSP31_jaymangu.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
SA_GSP31_jaymangu.author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
SA_GSP31_jaymangu.date_estimate: c. 350-450 CE (Gupta era); possibly as early as c. 200 CE
SA_GSP31_jaymangu.summary: A second e-text instance of the same root work, Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, the earliest extant systematic statement of classical Sāṃkhya philosophy in about 72 ārya verses. It sets out the dualism of prakṛti and puruṣa, the twenty-five tattvas, the three guṇas, satkāryavāda, and liberation by discrimination. This record likely corresponds to a particular commentarial recension (its work_id appears to reference the Jayamaṅgalā commentary).
SA_GSP31_jaymangu.history: The Sāṃkhyakārikā is the locus classicus of Sāṃkhya and survives with several early commentaries; the Jayamaṅgalā is one of the older glosses (sometimes attributed to a Śaṅkara/Śaṅkarārya, distinct from Ādi Śaṅkara). Paramārtha's 6th-century Chinese rendering of the kārikā and a commentary provides a key chronological anchor.

SA_GSP33_brasusau.title: Brahmasūtra (Śaṅkarabhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brasusau.author: Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya); root sūtras attributed to Bādarāyaṇa
SA_GSP33_brasusau.date_estimate: Śaṅkara's commentary c. early 8th c. CE (traditionally c. 788-820 CE); root Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE
SA_GSP33_brasusau.summary: Śaṅkara's commentary (bhāṣya) on the Brahmasūtra (Vedāntasūtra) of Bādarāyaṇa, the foundational systematic treatise of Advaita Vedānta. Across four chapters it interprets the terse aphorisms to argue that Brahman is the sole non-dual reality, that the individual self (ātman) is identical with Brahman, that the empirical world is grounded in māyā/avidyā, and it refutes rival schools (Sāṃkhya, Nyāya, Buddhism, etc.).
SA_GSP33_brasusau.history: The Brahmasūtrabhāṣya is regarded as Śaṅkara's most important authenticated work and the cornerstone of Advaita. It generated a vast subcommentarial literature, most notably Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī and Padmapāda's Pañcapādikā, which gave rise to the two main Advaita exegetical schools (Bhāmatī and Vivaraṇa).
related_works: Bhāṣyas of Rāmānuja and Madhva on the same sūtras

SA_GSP33_jimuvi.title: Jīvanmuktiviveka
SA_GSP33_jimuvi.author: Vidyāraṇya (formerly Mādhava)
SA_GSP33_jimuvi.summary: A treatise on jīvanmukti, 'liberation while still living,' examining the nature, means, and conduct of one who has attained release before bodily death. Drawing on Vedānta together with yogic disciplines, it discusses the dissolution of mind (manonāśa), destruction of latent tendencies (vāsanākṣaya), and the lifestyle of the liberated sage.
SA_GSP33_jimuvi.history: Composed by Vidyāraṇya, the Advaita scholar and Jagadguru of Śṛṅgeri (originally known as Mādhava), after he became a sannyāsin. The work exemplifies a 'yogic Advaita' that supplements Śaṅkara's emphasis on knowledge with disciplines drawn from the Yogasūtra, Bhagavadgītā, Gauḍapāda's Kārikā, and the Laghu-Yogavāsiṣṭha.

SA_GSP33_vacbhamu.title: Bhāmatī
SA_GSP33_vacbhamu.author: Vācaspati Miśra
SA_GSP33_vacbhamu.date_estimate: 9th-10th c. CE (c. 840 CE, or by an alternate calendar reckoning c. 976 CE)
SA_GSP33_vacbhamu.summary: Vācaspati Miśra's sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya. It elucidates and defends Śaṅkara's Advaita reading of the Brahmasūtra and develops distinctive positions, notably locating avidyā (nescience) in the individual jīva rather than in Brahman.
SA_GSP33_vacbhamu.history: The Bhāmatī is so influential that it gave its name to one of the two principal schools of post-Śaṅkara Advaita exegesis (the 'Bhāmatī school,' as opposed to the Vivaraṇa school). It itself attracted further sub-commentaries, including Amalānanda's Kalpataru and Appayya Dīkṣita's Parimala. Vācaspati, a polymath who wrote authoritative works across several darśanas, dated one work to 840 CE (Saṃvat reckoning), the basis for his chronology.

SA_GSP36_sadha.title: Sarva-darśana-saṃgraha
SA_GSP36_sadha.author: Mādhava (commonly identified with Vidyāraṇya); attribution discussed
SA_GSP36_sadha.tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta standpoint, surveying all schools)
SA_GSP36_sadha.summary: An encyclopedic survey ('compendium of all philosophical systems') that expounds the major Indian darśanas one by one, beginning with the materialist Cārvāka and proceeding through Buddhist, Jaina, and various Hindu systems up to Advaita Vedānta.
