The schools are arranged in an ascending hierarchy, each presented in its own terms before being superseded, culminating in the author's own Advaita as the highest truth.
SA_GSP36_sadha.history: Traditionally ascribed to Mādhavācārya, generally identified with Vidyāraṇya of Śṛṅgeri, though the attribution and the exact relation of 'Mādhava' to Vidyāraṇya have been debated by scholars. The work is a major source for our knowledge of medieval Indian philosophy, preserving accounts of schools (and even now-lost works) from a learned 14th-century vantage point.

SA_GV00_avs.title: Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā
SA_GV00_avs.author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers; Śaunaka recension)
SA_GV00_avs.date_estimate: c. 1200-900 BCE (composite; later than the Ṛgveda)
SA_GV00_avs.summary: The fourth Veda, a collection of hymns, charms, spells, and incantations addressing everyday life: healing of diseases, protection from demons and enemies, love and marriage, prosperity, kingship, and expiation, alongside more speculative cosmological and philosophical hymns. This record corresponds to the standard Śaunaka recension (the vulgate of the Atharvaveda).
SA_GV00_avs.history: Distinct in character from the three older 'sacrificial' Vedas, the Atharvaveda was accepted only later into the orthodox canon. It survives chiefly in two recensions, Śaunaka and Paippalāda. Its magical and medical material is a key source for early Indian folk religion and medicine (the roots of Āyurveda).

SA_GV00_avs2.title: Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā
SA_GV00_avs2.author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers)
SA_GV00_avs2.date_estimate: c. 1200-900 BCE (composite; later than the Ṛgveda)
SA_GV00_avs2.summary: A second e-text instance of the Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā, the fourth Veda comprising hymns, charms, and incantations for healing, protection, prosperity, love, and statecraft, together with cosmological and speculative hymns. This duplicate likely represents a different digital edition or recension (Śaunaka or Paippalāda) of the same corpus.
SA_GV00_avs2.history: The Atharvaveda was admitted into the canon later than the Ṛg-, Yajur-, and Sāmaveda and exists in the Śaunaka and Paippalāda recensions. It is the principal Vedic witness to early Indian magic, medicine, and domestic ritual.

SA_GV00_samavedu.title: Sāmaveda-Saṃhitā
SA_GV00_samavedu.author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers)
SA_GV00_samavedu.summary: The 'Veda of melodies/chants,' the third Veda, consisting largely of verses (mostly drawn from the Ṛgveda) arranged and set to melodies (sāman) for liturgical singing by the udgātṛ priests during the soma sacrifice. It comprises an ārcika (verse collection) and gāna (song-books) that indicate how the verses are to be chanted, and is foundational to the Indian musical and chant tradition.
SA_GV00_samavedu.history: Because most of its text is borrowed from the Ṛgveda, the Sāmaveda's importance lies in its musical notation and performance practice rather than new content. It survives in several recensions (Kauthuma, Rāṇāyanīya, Jaiminīya) and is the source from which Indian classical music traces its origins.

SA_GV01_rvh.title: Ṛgveda
SA_GV01_rvh.author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic ṛṣis of various clans)
SA_GV01_rvh.date_estimate: c. 1500-1200 BCE for the core (composite); the oldest Vedic text
SA_GV01_rvh.summary: The oldest of the four Vedas and one of the oldest extant texts in any Indo-European language, comprising 1,028 hymns (sūktas) in ten books (maṇḍalas) addressed mainly to deities such as Indra, Agni, Soma, Varuṇa, and the Aśvins. Alongside ritual invocations it contains cosmogonic, philosophical, and gnomic hymns (e.g. the Puruṣasūkta and the Nāsadīya 'Creation Hymn'). This record ('rvh') corresponds to a metrically restored/Holland-Van Nooten-style edition.
SA_GV01_rvh.history: Composed and transmitted orally with extraordinary fidelity over millennia, the Ṛgveda is the cultural and religious bedrock of the Indian tradition and a primary source for Indo-Aryan history and Proto-Indo-European studies. Its family books (II-VII) are the oldest stratum, with books I and X added later.

SA_GV02_gopbra_u.title: Gopathabrāhmaṇa
SA_GV02_gopbra_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Gopatha)
SA_GV02_gopbra_u.date_estimate: later Vedic period, c. 800-600 BCE; one of the latest Brāhmaṇas (some place it later still)
SA_GV02_gopbra_u.summary: The only Brāhmaṇa associated with the Atharvaveda, a prose text in two parts (pūrva- and uttara-bhāga) that expounds rituals, priestly duties, cosmology, the symbolism of the sacred syllable Oṃ, and the dignity of the Brahman priest and the Atharvaveda itself. It is linked to both the Śaunaka and Paippalāda schools.
SA_GV02_gopbra_u.history: Regarded as one of the latest Brāhmaṇa texts and partly drawing on Vedāṅga material, the Gopathabrāhmaṇa was composed in part to assert the Atharvaveda's parity with the three older Vedas. Its exact date and authorship are debated.

SA_GV03_sb.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa
SA_GV03_sb.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya in parts)
SA_GV03_sb.summary: The 'Brāhmaṇa of a hundred paths,' the great prose commentary attached to the White (Śukla) Yajurveda, describing in detail the major Vedic sacrifices (Agnihotra, new- and full-moon rites, Agnicayana fire-altar construction, Rājasūya, Aśvamedha, etc.). Beyond ritual mechanics it gives myths, etymologies, cosmogony (including a version of the flood/Manu legend), and philosophical reflection, and its final book overlaps with the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad.
SA_GV03_sb.history: Transmitted in two recensions, Mādhyandina and Kāṇva, it is one of the most important and extensive Brāhmaṇas, central to the study of Vedic ritual and early Indian thought; portions are associated with the sage Yājñavalkya. The Agnicayana sections are notable for early geometry/astronomy.

SA_GV05_upanisads.title: 13 Upaniṣads
SA_GV05_upanisads.author: anonymous/traditional (various Vedic seers)
SA_GV05_upanisads.date_estimate: c. 800-200 BCE for the principal Upaniṣads (composite)
SA_GV05_upanisads.summary: A collection of the principal (mukhya) Upaniṣads, the philosophical capstones of the Vedas (Vedānta) concerned with Brahman, ātman, karma, rebirth, and liberation. The set of thirteen is the standard scholarly grouping of the oldest and most authoritative Upaniṣads (e.g. Īśa, Kena, Kaṭha, Praśna, Muṇḍaka, Māṇḍūkya, Taittirīya, Aitareya, Chāndogya, Bṛhadāraṇyaka, Śvetāśvatara, Kauṣītaki, Maitrī).
SA_GV05_upanisads.history: These are the Upaniṣads on which the classical Vedānta tradition (Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, etc.) wrote commentaries and from which the Brahmasūtra draws; they form the bedrock of Indian idealist and non-dualist philosophy. R. E. Hume's well-known translation collected 'The Thirteen Principal Upanishads,' the likely referent here.

SA_GV05_upanisads2.title: 112 Upaniṣads
SA_GV05_upanisads2.author: anonymous/traditional (various)
SA_GV05_upanisads2.tradition: Hindu (Vedānta and sectarian: Yoga, Sannyāsa, Vaiṣṇava, Śaiva, Śākta)
SA_GV05_upanisads2.date_estimate: c. 800 BCE - c. 1500 CE (principal Upaniṣads ancient; many minor ones medieval)
SA_GV05_upanisads2.summary: A large corpus of Upaniṣads, corresponding to the extended Muktikā-style canon (the 108 Upaniṣads of the Muktikopaniṣad list, here numbering 112 in this collection). Beyond the principal Upaniṣads it includes many later, often sectarian texts grouped as Sāmānya (Vedānta), Yoga, Sannyāsa, Vaiṣṇava, Śaiva, and Śākta Upaniṣads, dealing with meditation, renunciation, mantra, and devotion.
SA_GV05_upanisads2.history: Whereas the principal Upaniṣads are late-Vedic, the many minor (gauṇa) Upaniṣads were composed over a long period, some as late as the medieval/early-modern era, and were gathered into canonical lists such as the 108 of the Muktikā Upaniṣad. They document the broadening of 'Upaniṣad' into a genre adopted by yogic and sectarian movements.

SA_GV05_chup.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_chup.author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers)
SA_GV05_chup.date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (one of the oldest Upaniṣads)
SA_GV05_chup.summary: One of the oldest and largest of the principal Upaniṣads, embedded in the Chāndogya-Brāhmaṇa of the Sāmaveda. In eight chapters it teaches through dialogue and parable the nature of Brahman and ātman, the significance of the syllable Oṃ (udgītha), meditation, and the unity of the self with ultimate reality. It contains the celebrated instruction of Uddālaka Āruṇi to his son Śvetaketu culminating in the mahāvākya 'tat tvam asi' ('that thou art').
SA_GV05_chup.history: A foundational source for Vedānta, heavily cited in the Brahmasūtra and commented upon by Śaṅkara and later teachers. Its dialogues (e.g. Satyakāma Jābāla, the doctrine of the five fires and the two paths) are among the most influential passages in Indian philosophy.

SA_GV05_chupsau.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad (Śaṅkarabhāṣya)
SA_GV05_chupsau.author: Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya)
SA_GV05_chupsau.date_estimate: Śaṅkara's commentary c. early 8th c. CE; the root Upaniṣad c. 8th-6th c. BCE
SA_GV05_chupsau.summary: Śaṅkara's commentary on the Chāndogya-Upaniṣad, expounding the text passage by passage from the standpoint of non-dualist (Advaita) Vedānta. It interprets the Upaniṣad's teachings on Oṃ, the meditations, and especially the great identity statement 'tat tvam asi' as affirming the absolute non-difference of ātman and Brahman.
SA_GV05_chupsau.history: One of the major Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas attributed to Śaṅkara, it is a central document of classical Advaita exegesis and shaped the orthodox interpretation of the Chāndogya for the later tradition.

SA_GV05_kathop_u.title: Kaṭha-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_kathop_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Kaṭha school)
SA_GV05_kathop_u.date_estimate: c. 5th-3rd c. BCE (one of the later principal Upaniṣads)
SA_GV05_kathop_u.summary: A principal Upaniṣad of the Kaṭha school of the Black Yajurveda, framed as the dialogue between the boy Naciketas and Yama, the god of death. Through three boons Yama teaches Naciketas about fire-sacrifice, the immortal self (ātman), the contrast between the pleasant (preyas) and the good (śreyas), and the path to liberation, including the famous chariot allegory of the self, body, intellect, and senses.
SA_GV05_kathop_u.history: Among the most poetically admired Upaniṣads, frequently commented upon (including by Śaṅkara) and widely translated. Its apparent acquaintance with ideas resembling Buddhist thought has been used to argue for a relatively late date among the principal Upaniṣads.

SA_GV06_apastgcu.title: Āpastamba-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_apastgcu.author: attributed to Āpastamba
SA_GV06_apastgcu.tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Taittirīya school of the Black Yajurveda)
SA_GV06_apastgcu.date_estimate: c. mid-1st millennium BCE (the Āpastamba corpus dated c. 600-300 BCE)
SA_GV06_apastgcu.summary: The domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) of the Āpastamba school of the Taittirīya (Black Yajurveda) tradition, forming part of the larger Āpastamba Kalpasūtra. In terse sūtra style it prescribes the household rites and rites of passage (saṃskāras): conception and childhood rites, upanayana (initiation), Vedic study, marriage, the daily and seasonal domestic offerings, and funerary observances.
SA_GV06_apastgcu.history: It is closely related to the Hiraṇyakeśi-Gṛhyasūtra and gives only the ritual procedure, the accompanying mantras being collected separately in a Mantrapāṭha. The Āpastamba corpus (Śrauta-, Gṛhya-, and Dharmasūtra) is among the most important sources for the ritual life of South Indian Taittirīya brahmins.

SA_GV06_gogrsuu.title: Gobhila-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_gogrsuu.author: attributed to Gobhila
SA_GV06_gogrsuu.date_estimate: later Vedic / sūtra period, mid-to-late 1st millennium BCE
SA_GV06_gogrsuu.summary: The principal domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) attached to the Sāmaveda, attributed to the sage Gobhila. It sets out the household rites and life-cycle saṃskāras—conception, birth, naming, tonsure, upanayana, marriage, śrāddha (ancestral offerings), and the regular domestic fire offerings—for followers of the Sāmaveda.
SA_GV06_gogrsuu.history: Classed among the Kalpasūtras, it is a key witness to Sāmavedic domestic practice and is often studied together with the Gṛhyasaṃgrahapariśiṣṭa and the Khādira-Gṛhyasūtra (a related, condensed text). It was translated by Hermann Oldenberg in the Sacred Books of the East.

SA_GV06_sankhgsu.title: Śāṅkhāyana-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_sankhgsu.author: attributed to Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki tradition)
SA_GV06_sankhgsu.date_estimate: later Vedic / sūtra period, mid-to-late 1st millennium BCE
SA_GV06_sankhgsu.summary: The domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) of the Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki) school of the Ṛgveda, prescribing the household rites and rites of passage—initiation, Vedic study, marriage, daily and seasonal domestic offerings, and ancestral rites—for Ṛgvedins of this branch.
SA_GV06_sankhgsu.history: It is very closely related to the Kauṣītaki-Gṛhyasūtra, with the first four chapters showing extensive verbal agreement, and stands beside the Āśvalāyana-Gṛhyasūtra as one of the two Ṛgvedic domestic manuals. It belongs to the Kalpasūtra literature of the Kauṣītaki/Śāṅkhāyana lineage attached to the Ṛgveda.

SA_GV_kyajurvedu.title: Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda
SA_GV_kyajurvedu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV_kyajurvedu.date_estimate: c. 1200-900 BCE (oral composition); recensions stabilized later
SA_GV_kyajurvedu.summary: The 'Black' Yajurveda is one of the two main recensions of the Yajurveda, the Veda of sacrificial formulas (yajus) used by the adhvaryu priest in the performance of Vedic ritual. Distinctively, the Kṛṣṇa ('black') recension intermixes mantra (verse formulas) with prose Brahmana-style ritual exegesis directly within the saṃhitā, in contrast to the 'White' (Śukla) Yajurveda which separates them. Its surviving schools include the Taittirīya, Maitrāyaṇī, Kāṭhaka and Kapiṣṭhala saṃhitās.
SA_GV_kyajurvedu.history: Transmitted orally with extraordinary fidelity through priestly schools (śākhā), the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda predates and conditioned the development of the later Vedic ritual and Brahmana literature. The label 'black/unarranged' versus the 'white/well-arranged' Śukla recension reflects a traditional account (Yājñavalkya's separation) of the differing organization of mantra and explanatory prose.

SA_GV_kyatp.title: Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda: Taittirīya-Prātiśākhya
SA_GV_kyatp.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in tradition to Ātreya/Vālmīki schools)
SA_GV_kyatp.date_estimate: c. mid-1st millennium BCE (before Pāṇini in its core)
SA_GV_kyatp.summary: A prātiśākhya is a treatise on the phonetics, sandhi (euphonic combination), accent, and correct recitation peculiar to a particular Vedic school (śākhā). The Taittirīya-Prātiśākhya governs the recitation of the Taittirīya recension of the Kṛṣṇa (Black) Yajurveda, specifying how the continuous (saṃhitā) text relates to its word-by-word (pada) analysis. It is a key early document of Indian phonetic science (śikṣā).
SA_GV_kyatp.history: The prātiśākhyas are among the oldest extant works of systematic Indian linguistic analysis, predating or paralleling the formal grammar of Pāṇini, and they preserve precise descriptions of articulation that helped guarantee accurate oral transmission of the Veda. The Taittirīya-Prātiśākhya is attached specifically to the Taittirīya school.

SA_GV_syajurvedu2.title: Śukla-Yajurveda
SA_GV_syajurvedu2.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with the sage Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV_syajurvedu2.date_estimate: c. 1100-800 BCE (oral composition); slightly later arrangement than Kṛṣṇa recension
SA_GV_syajurvedu2.summary: The 'White' Yajurveda is the recension of the Yajurveda in which the sacrificial mantras (the Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā) are kept separate from their Brahmana prose explanation, the latter relegated to the large Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa. It supplies the adhvaryu priest's formulas for the solemn (śrauta) rituals. Its two surviving schools are the Mādhyandina and the Kāṇva.
SA_GV_syajurvedu2.history: Tradition (e.g., the Viṣṇu Purāṇa) ascribes the 'white/well-arranged' recension to the sage Yājñavalkya, who is said to have received it anew after parting from his teacher Vaiśampāyana of the Black Yajurveda tradition. Its associated Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa and the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad make this branch especially important for ritual and early philosophical thought.

SA_GV_syajurvedu.title: Śukla-Yajurveda
SA_GV_syajurvedu.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with the sage Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV_syajurvedu.summary: A second project record for the 'White' Yajurveda (likely a different recension/encoding, e.g. Mādhyandina vs. Kāṇva, or a separate digital edition). It is the recension of the Yajurveda separating sacrificial mantras (Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā) from their ritual exegesis, which is housed in the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, providing the adhvaryu priest's formulas for śrauta sacrifice.
SA_GV_syajurvedu.history: Identical in substance to the other Śukla-Yajurveda entry; the duplication in the project metadata probably reflects two recensions or two source editions of the same Veda. Traditionally credited to Yājñavalkya, whose lineage is foundational to the ritual and Upaniṣadic literature attached to this branch.

SA_K01_vinv06_u.title: Bhaiṣajyavastu
SA_K01_vinv06_u.author: anonymous/traditional (compiled within the Mūlasarvāstivāda saṅgha)
SA_K01_vinv06_u.date_estimate: c. 1st-5th c. CE (compilation of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv06_u.summary: The Bhaiṣajyavastu, 'Chapter on Medicines,' is the sixth section of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, the monastic code of one of the most influential Indian Buddhist schools. Nominally about monastic regulations governing permitted medicines, it ranges far beyond, containing lists of medicinal substances alongside numerous stories of the Buddha and his disciples, an account of the Buddha quelling an epidemic and converting a nāga, and former-life (jātaka) narratives.
SA_K01_vinv06_u.history: Part of the vast Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, which circulated across the Indian subcontinent and into Southeast Asia by the late seventh century and was preserved in Sanskrit (notably the Gilgit manuscripts), Tibetan, and Chinese. The Sanskrit text has been critically studied and translated in modern scholarship (e.g., by Fumi Yao and the 84000 project).

SA_K06_bsu054_u.title: Prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtra
SA_K06_bsu054_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_bsu054_u.date_estimate: c. 5th-7th c. CE (short text; disputed, possibly redacted in China then back-translated per Jan Nattier)
SA_K06_bsu054_u.summary: The 'Heart Sūtra' is the most concise and most famous of the Perfection of Wisdom (Prajñāpāramitā) texts. In a dialogue framed around Avalokiteśvara addressing Śāriputra, it distills the doctrine of emptiness (śūnyatā): that the five aggregates are empty, encapsulated in the formula 'form is emptiness, emptiness is form,' and culminating in the mantra 'gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā.'
SA_K06_bsu054_u.history: Among the most influential and widely recited Buddhist texts in East Asian and Tibetan traditions, it exists in shorter and longer recensions. Jan Nattier's influential 1992 study argued the short text may have been compiled in China from Kumārajīva's translation of the Large Prajñāpāramitā and then rendered into Sanskrit, a thesis still debated among scholars.

SA_K08_bsu029_u.title: Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā [Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchāsūtra]
SA_K08_bsu029_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K08_bsu029_u.date_estimate: core by c. 1st-2nd c. CE (in Chinese by 270 CE); later strata added
SA_K08_bsu029_u.summary: 'The Questions of Rāṣṭrapāla,' an early Mahāyāna sūtra included in the Ratnakūṭa collection, in which the bodhisattva Rāṣṭrapāla questions the Buddha about the bodhisattva path. It exalts the rigorous forest-dwelling, ascetic bodhisattva ideal and sharply criticizes lax, worldly monks, framing the decline of the Dharma as a result of monastic corruption.
SA_K08_bsu029_u.history: An important witness to the early formation of the Mahāyāna: passages have parallels in Dharmarakṣa's Chinese translation of 270 CE, and at least two textual strata are identifiable across the Sanskrit, Chinese, and Tibetan recensions, with roughly half the later Sanskrit/Tibetan material absent from the earliest Chinese. The earliest complete Sanskrit witness is a 1661 Nepalese manuscript edited by Louis Finot (1901); Daniel Boucher produced a major study and translation.

SA_K10_ayuhparu.title: Āyuḥparyantasūtra
SA_K10_ayuhparu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_ayuhparu.date_estimate: early-mid 1st millennium CE for the Sanskrit recension (doctrinally early Buddhist material)
SA_K10_ayuhparu.summary: The 'Sūtra on the Limits/Span of Life' (āyuḥparyanta) is an early Buddhist scholastic discourse enumerating the lifespans of beings across the various realms and classes of existence in Buddhist cosmology, from humans through the deities of the desire, form, and formless realms. It is essentially a cosmological-Abhidharma list-sūtra detailing the duration of life at each level of rebirth.
SA_K10_ayuhparu.history: Preserved in Sanskrit fragments (from Central Asian finds) and in Tibetan, the text is associated with the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda transmission and reflects the systematic interest in cosmology characteristic of that tradition's Abhidharma. It has been edited from Central Asian manuscript remains.

SA_K10_arthaviniscaya.title: Arthaviniścayasūtra + Nibandhana
SA_K10_arthaviniscaya.author: sūtra anonymous; commentary (Nibandhana) by Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālandā
SA_K10_arthaviniscaya.date_estimate: sūtra c. 1st millennium CE; Nibandhana commentary c. 770 CE
SA_K10_arthaviniscaya.summary: The Arthaviniścayasūtra ('Sūtra on the Ascertainment of Meanings') is a doctrinal compendium presenting and defining the principal numerical categories of Buddhist teaching (the five aggregates, twelve sense-bases, eighteen elements, dependent origination, four noble truths, faculties, etc.) in catechism-like fashion. This record pairs the sūtra with its Nibandhana, the detailed scholastic commentary by Vīryaśrīdatta of Śrī-Nālandāvihāra.
SA_K10_arthaviniscaya.history: The Nibandhana was written by Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālandā, conventionally dated to around 770 CE (in the reign of a King Dharmapāla). The sūtra and commentary were critically edited by N. H. Samtani (Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series, 1971), an edition that remains standard.

SA_K10_bsu005_u.title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
SA_K10_bsu005_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu005_u.date_estimate: c. 1st half of the 1st millennium CE
SA_K10_bsu005_u.summary: The 'Sūtra on the Ascertainment of Meanings,' a systematic doctrinal digest that lists and briefly defines the core enumerated categories of Buddhist teaching: the five skandhas, eighteen dhātus, twelve āyatanas, dependent origination, the four noble truths, the faculties, the limbs of awakening, and the noble eightfold path. It functions as a compact catechism of fundamental Abhidharma-style topics.
SA_K10_bsu005_u.history: Transmitted in Sanskrit and Tibetan, the text became the basis for Vīryaśrīdatta's eighth-century Nibandhana commentary at Nālandā. N. H. Samtani's 1971 critical edition established the standard text of both sūtra and commentary.

SA_K10_bsu022_u.title: Lalitavistara [Lalitavistarasūtra]
SA_K10_bsu022_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu022_u.genre: sūtra / sacred biography (buddhacarita-type), mixed prose and verse
SA_K10_bsu022_u.date_estimate: c. 1st-3rd c. CE (composite; redactions continuing into mid-1st millennium)
SA_K10_bsu022_u.summary: The Lalitavistara, 'The Play in Full' (the 'extensive account of the sport/display' of the Buddha), is an elaborate, semi-legendary biography of Śākyamuni from his decision to descend from Tuṣita heaven through his birth, youth, renunciation, awakening under the Bodhi tree, and first turning of the wheel of the Dharma at Sārnāth. It presents the Buddha's life as the deliberate, miraculous display (lalita) of an already-awakened being.
SA_K10_bsu022_u.history: A composite work showing a Mahāyāna reworking of older Sarvāstivāda biographical traditions, it was translated into Chinese and Tibetan and was hugely influential on Buddhist art, notably the narrative reliefs of Borobudur. Its dating is layered; portions are early while the received Sanskrit text reflects later redaction.

SA_K10_bsu025_u.title: Nairātmyaparipṛcchāmahāyānasūtra
SA_K10_bsu025_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu025_u.summary: 'The Mahāyāna Sūtra of Questions on Selflessness/Non-Self' is a dialogue sūtra centered on the doctrine of nairātmya (anātman), the absence of a permanent, independent self. It expounds, in question-and-answer form, the Mahāyāna understanding that neither the person nor phenomena possess any intrinsic self-nature, linking the teaching to emptiness (śūnyatā).
SA_K10_bsu025_u.history: A relatively short Mahāyāna scripture preserved chiefly in Sanskrit and Tibetan, it belongs to the broad body of 'questions' (paripṛcchā) sūtras that frame doctrinal exposition as inquiry. It is comparatively minor and less studied than the major Prajñāpāramitā or Ratnakūṭa texts.

SA_K10_bsu031.title: Śālistambasūtra
SA_K10_bsu031.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu031.summary: The 'Rice Seedling Sūtra' takes as its image a stalk of rice growing from a seed to illustrate and expound the doctrine of dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda). Through the analogy of seed, sprout, and plant, it teaches both the outer (causal) and inner (psychological, twelvefold) chains of conditioned arising, framed as a teaching prompted by the Buddha's gaze at a rice plant and elaborated by Maitreya to Śāriputra.
SA_K10_bsu031.history: One of the most important Mahāyāna sūtras on causation, it is heavily cited in Madhyamaka literature and in commentaries attributed to figures associated with Nāgārjuna's tradition (e.g., the Śālistamba commentary literature). Preserved in Sanskrit fragments, Tibetan, and Chinese, it served as a key scriptural anchor for the doctrine of dependent origination.

SA_K10_bsu034_u.title: Samādhirājasūtra
SA_K10_bsu034_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu034_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-6th c. CE (composite; core early, expanded over centuries)
SA_K10_bsu034_u.summary: The 'King of Samādhis Sūtra' (also Candrapradīpasūtra) centers on a meditative absorption (samādhi) said to encompass all dharmas, taught by the Buddha to the youth Candraprabha. It expounds emptiness, the equality of all phenomena, and the bodhisattva's conduct, weaving together doctrine, jātaka-like narratives, and praises of the samādhi that 'reveals the sameness of the nature of all phenomena.'
SA_K10_bsu034_u.history: A major Mahāyāna scripture especially influential in Tibetan Buddhism (frequently cited by the Kagyu Mahāmudrā tradition and used by Madhyamaka authors such as Candrakīrti), it grew by accretion over several centuries. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_K10_bsu036_u.title: Saddharmapuṇḍarīkasūtra
SA_K10_bsu036_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu036_u.date_estimate: c. 1st c. BCE-2nd c. CE (composite, in strata)
SA_K10_bsu036_u.summary: The 'Lotus Sūtra' ('White Lotus of the True Dharma') is one of the most influential Mahāyāna scriptures, proclaiming the doctrine of the One Vehicle (ekayāna): that the apparent three vehicles (śrāvaka, pratyekabuddha, bodhisattva) are skillful means (upāya) and that all beings are destined for full buddhahood. Famous for its parables (the burning house, the prodigal son) and for revealing the Buddha's immeasurable lifespan and transcendent nature.
SA_K10_bsu036_u.history: Composed in layers over the first centuries CE, it became foundational for East Asian Buddhism, spawning the Tiantai and Nichiren schools, and was translated into Chinese most famously by Kumārajīva (406 CE). Sanskrit witnesses include the Gilgit and Nepalese manuscript traditions and the Central Asian (Kashgar) recension.

SA_K10_bsu061_u.title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra [Vimalakīrtinirdeśa]
SA_K10_bsu061_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu061_u.summary: 'The Teaching of Vimalakīrti' is a Mahāyāna sūtra whose protagonist is the wealthy lay bodhisattva Vimalakīrti, who feigns illness to draw the Buddha's disciples and bodhisattvas into debate, repeatedly outshining them in wisdom. It celebrates the layman's attainment, the non-duality of phenomena (culminating in Vimalakīrti's famous 'thunderous silence' on non-duality), emptiness, and skillful means.
SA_K10_bsu061_u.history: Long known only through Chinese and Tibetan translations, the Sanskrit text was rediscovered in 1999 in the Potala Palace by a Japanese team and published in 2006. Hugely influential in East Asian Buddhism, especially Chan/Zen, for its valorization of lay practice and its witty deconstruction of dualistic thinking.

SA_K10_jnalokau.title: Jñānālokālaṃkāra sūtra [Sarvabuddhaviṣayāvatārajñānālokālaṃkāra nāma mahāyānasūtra]
SA_K10_jnalokau.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_jnalokau.date_estimate: by c. 5th c. CE (in Chinese by 501 CE); likely composed 3rd-5th c. CE
SA_K10_jnalokau.summary: The 'Ornament of the Light of Wisdom that Enters the Domain of All Buddhas' (Sarvabuddhaviṣayāvatāra-jñānālokālaṃkāra) is a Mahāyāna sūtra expounding the nature of buddha-wisdom (jñāna) and the awakened state, emphasizing the non-origination and luminous nature of mind and reality. It is associated with the tathāgatagarbha (buddha-nature) and Yogācāra-influenced stream of Mahāyāna thought.
SA_K10_jnalokau.history: A Chinese translation existed by 501 CE, establishing a firm terminus ante quem, and the sūtra is cited in influential tathāgatagarbha treatises such as the Ratnagotravibhāga, making it an important scriptural source for buddha-nature doctrine. A modern Sanskrit edition appeared in 2004 alongside studies at the University of Vienna.

SA_K10_mkarmviu.title: Mahākarmavibhaṅga
SA_K10_mkarmviu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_mkarmviu.genre: sūtra (doctrinal discourse on karma) with avadāna illustrations
SA_K10_mkarmviu.date_estimate: early-mid 1st millennium CE (expanding earlier Āgama material)
SA_K10_mkarmviu.summary: The 'Great Exposition of Karma' (Mahākarmavibhaṅga) is a discourse, addressed to the brahmin youth Śuka Taudeyaputra, analyzing in detail how specific actions produce specific karmic results across rebirths. It greatly expands the framework of the Pāli Cūḷakammavibhaṅga-sutta: where the Pāli text treats fourteen deeds and results, the Sanskrit Mahākarmavibhaṅga adds dozens more sections, illustrated with narrative examples.
SA_K10_mkarmviu.history: The Sanskrit manuscript was discovered in Nepal by Sylvain Lévi, who published the editio princeps with French translation in 1932. The text represents a developed, narrative-rich elaboration of canonical teaching on the mechanics of karma and was influential in Buddhist art (its lists are linked to the hidden base reliefs of Borobudur).

SA_K10_n260u.title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
SA_K10_n260u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n260u.date_estimate: c. 1st half of the 1st millennium CE
SA_K10_n260u.summary: A further project record of the 'Sūtra on the Ascertainment of Meanings,' the doctrinal digest enumerating and defining the principal categories of Buddhist teaching: the five aggregates, the eighteen elements, the twelve sense-bases, dependent origination, the four noble truths, the faculties, the factors of awakening, and the eightfold path. It serves as a compact systematic summary of foundational doctrine.
SA_K10_n260u.history: This is a duplicate title in the batch (cf. K10bsu005_u and K10arthaviniscaya), reflecting a different digital edition or recension of the same sūtra. The work became the base text for Vīryaśrīdatta's eighth-century Nibandhana commentary and was critically edited by N. H. Samtani (1971).

SA_K10_n552u.title: Āryābuddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvaṇanirdeśanāma mahāyānasūtra
SA_K10_n552u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n552u.summary: 'The Noble Mahāyāna Sūtra: The Teaching on the Extraordinary Transformation that is the Miracle of Attaining the Buddha's Powers' (Buddhabalādhāna-prātihārya-vikurvaṇa-nirdeśa). In it the Buddha thrice displays supernatural power: transporting his entire assembly to Vārāṇasī; inciting Avalokiteśvara and Vajrapāṇi to gather all beings ripe for awakening and making the world appear as a pure realm like Sukhāvatī; and, with Mañjuśrī's help through a series of dialogues, manifesting innumerable buddhas around him. It teaches that a tathāgata's powers, like a physician's skills, are deployed as skillful means to liberate beings.
SA_K10_n552u.history: A Mahāyāna sūtra preserved in Tibetan (Toh 186) and translated into English by the 84000 project. It belongs to the genre of sūtras showcasing the Buddha's miraculous displays (prātihārya) and pure-land imagery, with prominent roles for the bodhisattvas Avalokiteśvara, Vajrapāṇi, and Mañjuśrī.

SA_K10_vimkn_u.title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśa [Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra]
SA_K10_vimkn_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_vimkn_u.summary: A Mahāyāna sūtra centered on Vimalakīrti, a wealthy lay bodhisattva of Vaiśālī who, while feigning illness, expounds the doctrine of emptiness (śūnyatā), non-duality, and skillful means to a succession of disciples and bodhisattvas. Its dramatic dialogues culminate in Vimalakīrti's famous 'thunderous silence' on the entry into non-duality, and the text repeatedly demonstrates that a layperson can surpass renunciants and arhats in wisdom.
SA_K10_vimkn_u.history: Composed in India in the early centuries CE, it survives in Chinese (notably Kumārajīva's 406 CE translation) and Tibetan, and a complete Sanskrit manuscript was discovered at the Potala Palace in 1999. It became immensely influential in East Asian Buddhism, especially Chan/Zen, for its praise of lay practice and non-dual insight.

SA_K12_bsu003_u.title: Aparimitāyuḥsūtra
SA_K12_bsu003_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu003_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (widely copied from 8th-9th c.)
SA_K12_bsu003_u.summary: A short Mahāyāna dhāraṇī sūtra extolling the buddha Aparimitāyus ('Boundless Life') and the dhāraṇī associated with him, recited to obtain long life, the purification of negative karma, and a good rebirth. The text promises that reciting, copying, hearing, or making offerings to it yields immense merit, and the dhāraṇī is repeated many times within the work.
SA_K12_bsu003_u.history: One of the most widely reproduced Buddhist texts; thousands of copies were made in the late Tibetan Empire, with vast caches surviving among the Dunhuang manuscripts. It is essentially identical to the Aparimitāyurnāmamahāyānasūtra / Aparimitāyurjñāna Sūtra and circulated under several titles.

SA_K12_bsu007_u.title: Śrīmahādevīvyākaraṇa [Śrīmahādevīvyākaraṇasūtra, Dvādaśadaṇḍakanāmāṣṭaśatavimalīkaraṇā]
SA_K12_bsu007_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu007_u.summary: A short Mahāyāna scripture framed as a prophecy (vyākaraṇa) concerning the goddess Śrī Mahādevī (Śrīdevī / Lakṣmī assimilated into the Buddhist pantheon), associated with a dhāraṇī and ritual praise that bestow prosperity, purification, and merit. The subtitle 'purification by the one hundred and eight names in twelve sections' points to a litany of the goddess's names used for cleansing and protection.
SA_K12_bsu007_u.history: A minor devotional dhāraṇī text preserved in Sanskrit manuscript collections and in the Tibetan/Chinese canons; it reflects the absorption of the Hindu goddess Śrī/Lakṣmī into Mahāyāna and proto-tantric goddess veneration. Precise authorship and date are unknown and it is not the better-known Śrīmālādevīsiṃhanāda sūtra.

SA_K12_mamuka.title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa [Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa]
SA_K12_mamuka.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_mamuka.date_estimate: core c. 6th c. CE, redacted through 8th c. CE (composite)
SA_K12_mamuka.summary: A vast composite scripture, the largest single text devoted to the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī, classified both as a Mahāyāna vaipulya sūtra and as a Mantrayāna ritual manual (kalpa). It sets out mantras, maṇḍalas, and esoteric rites for mundane aims (protection, wealth, subjugation of obstacles) and for awakening, and famously includes a verse 'history' (rājavyākaraṇa) chapter used as a source for Indian dynastic chronology.
SA_K12_mamuka.history: Often cited as the earliest extant Indian Buddhist tantra, it grew by accretion over several generations, with a core perhaps in the 6th century and redaction continuing through the 8th century. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese and is an important witness to early esoteric Buddhism and to historical traditions about Indian kings.

SA_K12_manjn1pu.title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti
SA_K12_manjn1pu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_manjn1pu.summary: A celebrated 'Chanting of the Names of Mañjuśrī,' a metrical litany that enumerates the names and epithets of Mañjuśrī understood as the embodiment of non-dual gnosis (jñāna) and identified with the primordial buddha. The recitation is treated as a complete tantric practice, mapping the names onto maṇḍala deities and meditative realization.
SA_K12_manjn1pu.history: Extremely influential in Vajrayāna, it became a foundational text of the Kālacakra and yoga-tantra traditions and generated a large body of Indian and Tibetan commentary. Often transmitted in connection with the Māyājāla cycle, it remains among the most widely recited liturgical texts in Tibetan Buddhism.

SA_K12_mspram_u.title: Mahāsāhasrapramardanī
SA_K12_mspram_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_mspram_u.date_estimate: by c. 8th c. CE (surviving Sanskrit mss from 11th c.)
SA_K12_mspram_u.summary: One of the five protective scriptures of the Pañcarakṣā ('Five Protections') corpus, a dhāraṇī text whose recitation is held to ward off calamities, demons, and disease over a vast (thousandfold) cosmic domain. Like its companion rakṣās it combines invocations, mantras, and lists of protective deities and yakṣas.
SA_K12_mspram_u.history: The Pañcarakṣā collection is attested in Tibetan catalogues by around 800 CE, while the surviving Sanskrit manuscripts (often beautifully illustrated Nepalese/Bengalese palm-leaf codices) date from the 11th century onward. The set became central to Newar Buddhist ritual practice in the Kathmandu Valley.

SA_K12_n259u.title: Aparimitāyunāmamahāyānasūtra
SA_K12_n259u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n259u.date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (widely copied from 8th-9th c.)
SA_K12_n259u.summary: A Mahāyāna dhāraṇī sūtra glorifying the buddha Aparimitāyus ('Boundless/Immeasurable Life') and his dhāraṇī, recited for longevity, the exhaustion of bad karma, and a fortunate rebirth. The work lists the great merits of reciting, writing, hearing, and making offerings to the text, in which the longevity dhāraṇī recurs many times.
SA_K12_n259u.history: This is the full-title form of the same scripture as the Aparimitāyuḥsūtra / Aparimitāyurjñāna Sūtra; it was reproduced in enormous numbers in the late Tibetan Empire and is heavily represented in the Dunhuang manuscript finds, making it one of the most copied of all Buddhist texts.

SA_K12_n283u.title: Bhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājasūtra
SA_K12_n283u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n283u.summary: The 'Sūtra of the Medicine Buddha, the Lapis-Lazuli Radiance King' (Bhaiṣajyaguru), which recounts the twelve great vows the Medicine Buddha made as a bodhisattva to heal physical and spiritual ills, dispel suffering and untimely death, and lead beings to liberation. It describes his eastern pure land Vaiḍūryanirbhāsa and prescribes devotional and ritual practices, including a healing dhāraṇī.
SA_K12_n283u.history: An Indian Mahāyāna scripture that became hugely popular across East and Inner Asia as the canonical basis of the Medicine Buddha cult; it was translated into Chinese several times (from the early 5th century onward) and into Tibetan. It anchors widespread healing rituals in Tibetan, Chinese, Japanese, and Newar Buddhism.

SA_K12_n2967u.title: Śrīguhyasamājatantra
SA_K12_n2967u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n2967u.summary: A foundational scripture of esoteric Buddhism, regarded as the chief of the Anuttarayoga (highest yoga) tantras and the locus classicus of the 'father tantra' Akṣobhya/Guhyasamāja maṇḍala. In seventeen chapters (with an eighteenth Uttaratantra) it sets out the five-buddha maṇḍala, deity yoga, the subtle-body practices of the completion stage, and antinomian ritual language meant to transform the passions into the path.
SA_K12_n2967u.history: Probably composed in India in the late 7th or 8th century, it became the centerpiece of the influential 'Ārya' exegetical school attributed to Nāgārjuna, Āryadeva, and Candrakīrti, and generated an extensive Indo-Tibetan commentarial tradition. It is among the oldest and most important of all Buddhist tantras.

SA_K14_mhvastuu.title: Mahāvastu-avadāna
SA_K14_mhvastuu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_mhvastuu.summary: A large, composite 'Great Story' that narrates the life of the Buddha across many past lives and his final birth, framed within the Vinaya tradition of the Lokottaravāda branch of the Mahāsāṃghika school. It interweaves jātakas, avadānas, and doctrinal passages, and presents an early 'supramundane' (lokottara) view of the Buddha as a transcendent being, foreshadowing Mahāyāna buddhology.
SA_K14_mhvastuu.history: Written in Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit and assembled over several centuries, it is one of the most important sources for early Buddhist legend and for the prehistory of Mahāyāna ideas (including bodhisattva stages). It survives only in Sanskrit/Hybrid Sanskrit, with no Chinese or Tibetan canonical translation.

SA_T02_narsekou.title: Sekoddeśa [Sekoddeśaṭīkā]
SA_T02_narsekou.author: Nāropa (Nāḍapāda)
SA_T02_narsekou.summary: The Sekoddeśa ('Summary of [Tantric] Initiation') is a versified section associated with the Kālacakra mūlatantra dealing with the consecrations/empowerments (abhiṣeka), and the Sekoddeśaṭīkā (also titled Paramārthasaṃgraha) is Nāropa's authoritative commentary on it. The commentary expounds the meaning of the Kālacakra initiations, the subtle-body yogas, and the ultimate (paramārtha) view.
SA_T02_narsekou.history: Attributed to the great Indian siddha Nāropa (Nāḍapāda, d. c. 1040 CE), it is an early and influential exegesis within the rapidly developing Kālacakra tradition of the 11th century. It survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan and remains a key source for the Kālacakra system of consecration.

SA_T04_bsa047.title: Bhāvanākrama
SA_T04_bsa047.author: Kamalaśīla
SA_T04_bsa047.summary: A set of three 'Stages of Meditation' treatises laying out the gradual Mahāyāna path of cultivating compassion (karuṇā), bodhicitta, and the union of calm abiding (śamatha) and insight (vipaśyanā) leading to non-conceptual realization of emptiness. The work defends a graduated, analytical approach to awakening.
SA_T04_bsa047.history: Composed by Kamalaśīla (c. 740-795 CE), student of Śāntarakṣita, the texts are traditionally connected with the Samyé (bSam yas) debate in Tibet, where Kamalaśīla's gradualist position is said to have prevailed over the 'sudden' Chan teaching of Heshang Moheyan. They became foundational for the Tibetan lamrim ('stages of the path') literature.

SA_T04_sanssr.title: Śikṣāsamuccaya
SA_T04_sanssr.author: Śāntideva
SA_T04_sanssr.summary: A 'Compendium of Training' that organizes the bodhisattva path around a set of root verses (the Śikṣāsamuccaya kārikās) and then illustrates each point with extensive quotations from Mahāyāna sūtras. It treats the cultivation, protection, and purification of body, possessions, and merit for the welfare of others, serving as a sourcebook of scriptural authorities.
SA_T04_sanssr.history: Composed by Śāntideva, the 8th-century Nālandā master also famous for the Bodhicaryāvatāra, it is especially valuable for preserving citations from many Mahāyāna sūtras now otherwise lost in Sanskrit. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_T04_tat.title: Tattvasaṃgraha
SA_T04_tat.author: Śāntarakṣita
SA_T04_tat.summary: A monumental verse 'Compendium of Reality' that systematically examines and critiques the major doctrines of Indian philosophy—Sāṃkhya prakṛti, Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika categories, the self (ātman), God (Īśvara), the permanence of sound and the Veda, theories of perception and inference, and more—from a Buddhist epistemological standpoint. It defends momentariness, no-self, and apoha while subjecting rival positions to rigorous logical refutation.
SA_T04_tat.history: Written by Śāntarakṣita (c. 725-788 CE), the influential Madhyamaka philosopher who helped establish Buddhism in Tibet, it is a primary doxographical source for 8th-century Indian thought, preserving the views of many otherwise lost thinkers. It is almost always studied together with Kamalaśīla's Pañjikā.

SA_T04_tatpa.title: Tattvasaṃgrahapañcikā [Tattvasaṃgrahapañjikā]
SA_T04_tatpa.author: Kamalaśīla
SA_T04_tatpa.date_estimate: c. late 8th c. CE (author c. 740-795 CE)
SA_T04_tatpa.summary: Kamalaśīla's word-by-word commentary (pañjikā) on Śāntarakṣita's Tattvasaṃgraha, expanding and clarifying its arguments and supplying the detailed contexts of the rival positions being refuted. The commentary is indispensable for understanding the often terse verses and for reconstructing the opposing views (pūrvapakṣa) of contemporary schools.
SA_T04_tatpa.history: Composed by Kamalaśīla (c. 740-795 CE), Śāntarakṣita's foremost disciple; the standard catalogued title is Tattvasaṃgraha-pañjikā (the 'pañcikā' form in the source metadata appears to be a variant/typo). It is preserved in Sanskrit and Tibetan and edited together with the root text in the Gaekwad's Oriental Series.

SA_T06_asabhs_u.title: Abhidharmasamuccaya
SA_T06_asabhs_u.author: Asaṅga
SA_T06_asabhs_u.summary: A 'Compendium of Abhidharma' that systematizes the categories of Buddhist phenomenology (dharmas, aggregates, elements, sense-fields, the noble truths, the path) from a Yogācāra perspective, integrating Mahāyāna doctrine into the abhidharma framework. It serves as a concise doctrinal summa distinct from the Sarvāstivāda/Sautrāntika abhidharma of Vasubandhu's Kośa.
SA_T06_asabhs_u.history: Attributed to Asaṅga (c. 4th century CE), the founder-systematizer of the Yogācāra school, it became one of the principal abhidharma textbooks of Mahāyāna Buddhism, with a commentary (Bhāṣya) traditionally linked to Sthiramati/Jinaputra. It survives partly in Sanskrit and fully in Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_T06_asmahsuu.title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra
SA_T06_asmahsuu.author: Maitreya(nātha)/Asaṅga (verses); bhāṣya attributed to Vasubandhu
SA_T06_asmahsuu.summary: An 'Ornament of the Mahāyāna Sūtras,' a versified treatise that systematizes core Mahāyāna and Yogācāra doctrine—the bodhisattva path, the perfections, the stages (bhūmis), buddha-bodies, and the nature of mind—as one of the 'Five Treatises of Maitreya.' It argues for the authenticity and superiority of the Mahāyāna and articulates the mind-only (cittamātra) orientation.
SA_T06_asmahsuu.history: Traditionally revealed by the bodhisattva Maitreya to Asaṅga (hence ascribed to Maitreyanātha), with a prose commentary (bhāṣya) attributed to Vasubandhu and later sub-commentaries by Sthiramati and Asvabhāva. The project metadata names Vasubandhu, but the kārikā text is conventionally credited to Maitreya/Asaṅga while the bhāṣya is Vasubandhu's.

SA_T06_bsa018.title: Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā [Triṃśikākārikā]
SA_T06_bsa018.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T06_bsa018.summary: The 'Thirty Verses on Consciousness-Only,' a terse foundational statement of Yogācāra idealism that analyzes experience into the threefold transformation of consciousness—the store-consciousness (ālayavijñāna), the afflicted mind (manas), and the six perceptual consciousnesses—and the three natures (trisvabhāva). It culminates in the realization of mere representation (vijñaptimātra) and the path to non-conceptual wisdom.
SA_T06_bsa018.history: Composed by Vasubandhu (c. 4th-5th century CE), brother of Asaṅga, it is among the most influential Yogācāra texts and the basis of Sthiramati's commentary and of the Chinese Faxiang (Weishi) school via Xuanzang's Cheng weishi lun. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_T06_bsa034.title: Bodhisattvabhūmi
SA_T06_bsa034.author: Asaṅga
SA_T06_bsa034.genre: philosophical treatise (śāstra; section of the Yogācārabhūmi)
SA_T06_bsa034.summary: The 'Bodhisattva Stage,' a comprehensive manual of the Mahāyāna bodhisattva path covering the bodhisattva's lineage (gotra), the arising of bodhicitta, the perfections (pāramitās), the means of attracting beings, the bhūmis, and the attainment of buddhahood. It also contains the influential 'Tattvārtha' chapter on the nature of reality and the inexpressible (nirabhilāpya) ground of phenomena.
SA_T06_bsa034.history: It forms a major part of Asaṅga's encyclopedic Yogācārabhūmi and is a key source for Yogācāra ethics and path theory; its account of gotra and of the 'ineffable' nature of things was widely cited. It survives in Sanskrit as well as Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_T06_bsa065.title: Pañcaskandhaprakaraṇa
SA_T06_bsa065.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T06_bsa065.summary: A concise 'Treatise on the Five Aggregates' that defines and analyzes the five skandhas (form, feeling, perception, formations, consciousness) together with the associated mental factors (caitasikas), the elements, and sense-fields, presented within a Yogācāra framework. It functions as an introductory abhidharma primer that integrates the ālayavijñāna and afflicted mind into the standard list of mental events.
SA_T06_bsa065.history: Written by Vasubandhu, it complements his larger Abhidharmakośa by reframing aggregate-analysis along Yogācāra lines, and it attracted commentaries (e.g., by Sthiramati and Guṇaprabha). It is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese and is widely used as a teaching text.

SA_T06_pskvbhu.title: Pañcaskandhakavibhāṣā
SA_T06_pskvbhu.author: Sthiramati
SA_T06_pskvbhu.summary: An extensive Yogacara commentary on Vasubandhu's Pañcaskandhaka(prakaraṇa), a concise Abhidharma analysis of the five aggregates (skandhas) that constitute the person—form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Sthiramati expands on the technical terminology and classifications of mental factors (caitta), the storehouse consciousness, and the categories of phenomena from a Yogacara standpoint.
SA_T06_pskvbhu.history: Written by the great Yogacara scholiast Sthiramati (c. 510-570 CE), it is one of the few Yogacara Abhidharma works surviving in the original Sanskrit, recovered from manuscripts in the Tibet Autonomous Region and edited critically by Jowita Kramer. It is a key source for early Yogacara technical vocabulary, previously known mainly through its Tibetan translation.

SA_T06_sthtvbh.title: Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya [Triṃśikābhāṣya]
SA_T06_sthtvbh.author: Sthiramati
SA_T06_sthtvbh.summary: Sthiramati's prose commentary on Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā ('Thirty Verses on Consciousness-Only'), the foundational Yogacara verse treatise on cognition-only (vijñaptimātratā). It elucidates the doctrine of the three transformations of consciousness (the store-consciousness ālayavijñāna, the afflicted mind manas, and the six sense-consciousnesses), the three natures (trisvabhāva), and the path to the cessation of conceptual construction.
SA_T06_sthtvbh.history: Composed by Sthiramati (c. 510-570 CE), it became the most influential Indian commentary on the Triṃśikā. The Sanskrit manuscript of the Triṃśikā together with Sthiramati's commentary was discovered by Sylvain Lévi in Nepal in the 1920s and published in 1925, making the Sanskrit text directly available to scholarship.

SA_T06_bsa022_u.title: Viṃśatikā vijñaptimātratāsiddhi [Viṃśikāvṛtti] kārikā
SA_T06_bsa022_u.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T06_bsa022_u.summary: The root verses (kārikā) of Vasubandhu's Viṃśatikā ('Twenty Verses Establishing Consciousness-Only'). This brief work argues for the idealist Yogacara thesis that cognition is 'mere representation' (vijñaptimātra), rebutting realist objections about the apparent externality, spatiotemporal determination, intersubjectivity, and causal efficacy of objects without positing mind-independent external entities.
SA_T06_bsa022_u.history: Attributed to Vasubandhu (c. 4th-5th c. CE), this entry isolates the verses themselves as distinct from the accompanying auto-commentary (vṛtti). The Viṃśatikā together with its vṛtti is one of the classic statements of Yogacara idealism and was transmitted in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_T06_vasvvmsu.title: Viṃśatikā vijñaptimātratāsiddhi [Viṃśikāvṛtti]
SA_T06_vasvvmsu.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T06_vasvvmsu.summary: Vasubandhu's 'Twenty Verses Establishing Consciousness-Only' together with his own prose commentary (vṛtti). The work defends the Yogacara position that the world is cognition-only by answering four standard objections to idealism and offering the famous dream and collective-karma analogies, concluding with reflections on knowledge of other minds.
SA_T06_vasvvmsu.history: Composed by Vasubandhu (c. 4th-5th c. CE), one of the two great founders of the Yogacara school alongside his half-brother Asanga. The Viṃśatikā with its vṛtti is a cornerstone of Buddhist idealist philosophy and was widely commented upon in India, Tibet, and East Asia.

SA_T06_vmvkbh_u.title: Madhyāntavibhāgabhāṣya [Madhyāntavibhāgakārikābhāṣya]
SA_T06_vmvkbh_u.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T06_vmvkbh_u.summary: Vasubandhu's prose commentary on the Madhyāntavibhāga ('Distinguishing the Middle from the Extremes'), a verse treatise traditionally ascribed to Maitreya(natha)/Asanga. It expounds core Yogacara doctrines including the unreal imagination (abhūtaparikalpa), emptiness, the three natures, the obscurations, the constituents of awakening, and the supreme vehicle, framed as the Buddhist 'middle way' between reification and nihilism.
SA_T06_vmvkbh_u.history: Vasubandhu (c. 4th-5th c. CE) supplied the bhāṣya on the kārikās of the Maitreya/Asanga corpus; Sthiramati later wrote a sub-commentary (ṭīkā). The text survives in a single Sanskrit manuscript found in Tibet (edited by G. M. Nagao) as well as in Tibetan and Chinese, and is a central source for the Yogacara treatment of emptiness.

SA_T07_hvsatssu.title: Satyasiddhiśāstra [Tattvasiddhiśāstra]
SA_T07_hvsatssu.author: Harivarman
SA_T07_hvsatssu.tradition: Buddhist (Abhidharma; bridging Sravakayana and Mahayana, associated with Bahusrutiya/Sautrantika tendencies)
SA_T07_hvsatssu.summary: A systematic Abhidharma treatise in some 202 chapters organized around the Four Noble Truths, expounding doctrine with an emphasis on the emptiness (śūnyatā) of both persons and dharmas and the unreality of phenomena at the ultimate level. It critiques Sarvastivada/Vaibhasika positions and is regarded as a philosophical bridge between mainstream and Mahayana Buddhism.
SA_T07_hvsatssu.history: Authored by Harivarman (c. 250-350 CE), a central Indian Brahmin convert. The Indic original is lost; the text survives only in Kumārajīva's Chinese translation (made 411-412 CE), where it became the basis of the East Asian Chengshi (Satyasiddhi/Jojitsu) school.

SA_T06_sthmavtyg.title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā (Yamaguchi)
SA_T06_sthmavtyg.author: Sthiramati
SA_T06_sthmavtyg.summary: Sthiramati's sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on the Madhyāntavibhāga and Vasubandhu's bhāṣya, here in the edition prepared by Susumu Yamaguchi (published 1934). It elaborates the Yogacara analysis of unreal imagination, emptiness, the three natures, and the path, glossing both verses and Vasubandhu's prose in detail.
SA_T06_sthmavtyg.history: This record designates the Yamaguchi recension/edition of Sthiramati's (c. 510-570 CE) ṭīkā, the standard early critical edition of the Sanskrit text. Yamaguchi's edition has long served as the basis for scholarly study of the work alongside the kārikā and bhāṣya.

SA_T06_sthmavt.title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā
SA_T06_sthmavt.author: Sthiramati
SA_T06_sthmavt.summary: Sthiramati's sub-commentary on the Madhyāntavibhāga and its bhāṣya, providing a word-by-word exposition of the Yogacara 'middle way' between extremes. It treats the unreal imagination, the three natures, emptiness in its various aspects, the defilements and their removal, and the stages and factors of the path.
SA_T06_sthmavt.history: Composed by Sthiramati (c. 510-570 CE), the most prolific commentator of the early Yogacara tradition. The ṭīkā survives in Sanskrit (edited by Yamaguchi and later scholars) as well as Tibetan, and is one of the principal witnesses to the Maitreya/Asanga-Vasubandhu textual layer.

SA_T06_sthmavt1.title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā Chapter 1
SA_T06_sthmavt1.author: Sthiramati
SA_T06_sthmavt1.summary: The first chapter (lakṣaṇa-pariccheda, 'Defining Characteristics') of Sthiramati's Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā. This chapter sets out the foundational Yogacara analysis of the unreal imagination (abhūtaparikalpa) and emptiness, establishing that imagination exists but the subject-object duality within it does not, while emptiness exists within the imagination.
SA_T06_sthmavt1.history: An excerpt of Sthiramati's (c. 510-570 CE) sub-commentary corresponding to the opening chapter of the Madhyāntavibhāga. The first chapter is the most philosophically central, articulating the relation between abhūtaparikalpa, duality, and emptiness that defines Yogacara's middle path.

SA_T07_vakobhau1.title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya Chapter 1
SA_T07_vakobhau1.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T07_vakobhau1.tradition: Buddhist (Sarvastivada/Vaibhasika exposition critiqued from a Sautrantika perspective)
SA_T07_vakobhau1.summary: The first chapter (Dhātunirdeśa, 'Exposition of the Elements') of Vasubandhu's prose commentary on his Abhidharmakośa. It introduces the basic Abhidharma categories—the conditioned and unconditioned dharmas, the five aggregates (skandha), twelve sense-bases (āyatana), and eighteen elements (dhātu)—and their classifications and interrelations.
SA_T07_vakobhau1.history: Part of Vasubandhu's (c. 4th-5th c. CE) magnum opus of systematic Abhidharma. The opening chapter lays the taxonomic foundation for the entire work; it survives in the original Sanskrit (recovered by Rāhula Sāṅkṛtyāyana in Tibet) as well as Tibetan and two Chinese translations.

SA_T07_vakobhau.title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya
SA_T07_vakobhau.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T07_vakobhau.tradition: Buddhist (Sarvastivada/Vaibhasika material critiqued from a Sautrantika standpoint)
SA_T07_vakobhau.summary: Vasubandhu's prose auto-commentary on his own Abhidharmakośa verses, a comprehensive synthesis of Sarvastivada-Vaibhasika Abhidharma across eight chapters covering the elements, faculties, cosmology and karma, latent defilements, the path and persons, knowledge, meditative attainments, and (with the appended ninth section) the refutation of the self. Vasubandhu frequently critiques Vaibhasika orthodoxy from a Sautrantika viewpoint.
SA_T07_vakobhau.history: Composed by Vasubandhu (c. 4th-5th c. CE), it is among the most influential Abhidharma works in Buddhist history, foundational for both Tibetan and East Asian scholastic traditions. The Sanskrit text, long thought lost, was rediscovered in Tibet in the 20th century; the Vaibhasika master Saṅghabhadra wrote a rebuttal accusing Vasubandhu of misrepresenting Vaibhasika positions.

SA_T07_vakobhau9.title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya Chapter 9
SA_T07_vakobhau9.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T07_vakobhau9.summary: The ninth section of the Abhidharmakośabhāṣya, the Pudgalanirdeśa ('Refutation of the Person/Self'), an appended prose treatise without root verses. It argues against the existence of a substantial self or person, polemicizing especially against the Buddhist Pudgalavadins (Vatsiputriyas) and against Brahmanical (Nyaya-Vaisesika) theories of the soul.
SA_T07_vakobhau9.history: This chapter is a self-contained polemical essay added by Vasubandhu (c. 4th-5th c. CE) to the Kośa, often studied independently as a classic statement of the Buddhist no-self doctrine. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese and has been separately edited and translated (e.g., by Stcherbatsky and others).

SA_T07_vakobhk.title: Abhidharmakośakārikā
SA_T07_vakobhk.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T07_vakobhk.summary: The root verses (about 600 kārikās in eight chapters) of Vasubandhu's 'Treasury of Abhidharma,' summarizing Sarvastivada-Vaibhasika Abhidharma doctrine: the dharma taxonomy, faculties, cosmology and karma, defilements, the path, knowledge, and meditative absorptions. The verses present the orthodox Vaibhasika system that the accompanying bhāṣya then critiques.
SA_T07_vakobhk.history: Composed by Vasubandhu (c. 4th-5th c. CE), the kārikā became a standard textbook of Abhidharma memorized across Buddhist Asia. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese and attracted numerous commentaries, including those of Yasomitra, Sthiramati, and Purnavardhana.

SA_T09_hjatm.title: Jātakamālā
SA_T09_hjatm.author: Haribhaṭṭa
SA_T09_hjatm.summary: A 'garland' of thirty-four jātaka tales recounting the previous lives of the Buddha as a bodhisattva—born as humans, animals, and even as Indra—each illustrating a perfection or virtue. Composed in elegant champu style (mixed verse and prose) in high kāvya Sanskrit, it follows the model of Aryasura's earlier Jātakamālā.
SA_T09_hjatm.history: Haribhaṭṭa is thought to have lived in or near Kashmir in the early fifth century CE. The complete work survives in Tibetan translation; only part of the original Sanskrit (some fourteen of the stories) is preserved. It is prized as a refined example of Buddhist narrative kāvya.

SA_T17_bsa007_u.title: Dharmasaṃgraha
SA_T17_bsa007_u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (traditional; authorship uncertain)
SA_T17_bsa007_u.date_estimate: uncertain; 1st millennium CE (text as preserved likely later than the historical Nagarjuna)
SA_T17_bsa007_u.summary: A compact Buddhist glossary enumerating sets of technical terms organized by number—lists of categories such as the perfections, powers, kinds of knowledge, marks of a Buddha, and so on. It functions as a reference handbook (a 'numerical dharma' compendium) rather than a discursive treatise.
SA_T17_bsa007_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna, though this attribution is doubtful and the work is essentially a compilation. The Sanskrit original is preserved (edited by Kenjiu Kasawara, Max Müller, and H. Wenzel, 1885); roughly half its terms overlap with the Dharmasarira-sutra fragments found in Central Asia.

SA_XX_bible.title: Sanskrit Bible
SA_XX_bible.author: various Christian missionary translators (e.g., William Carey, John Wenger)
SA_XX_bible.date_estimate: 19th c. CE (New Testament 1808; later revisions to 1886)
SA_XX_bible.summary: A modern Sanskrit translation of the Christian Bible, produced by Protestant missionaries to render the scriptures into the classical learned language of India. It comprises the New Testament and Old Testament rendered into Sanskrit, adapting biblical prose and concepts into Sanskrit idiom.
SA_XX_bible.history: Translation work began under William Carey at the Serampore Mission Press, with the Sanskrit New Testament first published in Calcutta in 1808 and the Old Testament following in volumes from 1818. John Wenger (1811-1880) substantially revised the translation from 1851, his version being the most academically esteemed; this is a colonial-era artifact rather than an indigenous Sanskrit text.

SA_XX_n2890u.title: Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇatantra
SA_XX_n2890u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_XX_n2890u.summary: A Buddhist tantra centered on the wrathful deity Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇa (Acala, the 'Fierce Great Wrathful One') and his consort. It sets out the deity's iconography, mantras, ritual and meditative practices (sādhana), and—notably in its sixth chapter—the sexual yogic practices of the tantric yogi and his consort, characteristic of the Yogini-tantra genre.
SA_XX_n2890u.history: Dated to roughly the tenth-eleventh century CE in the late Mantrayana period, with some scholars proposing a late Nepalese production around 1100 CE. The Sanskrit survives in Nepalese manuscripts; it was translated into Tibetan and attracted commentaries such as Mahasukhavajra's Padmavati. It has been studied (and partly edited/translated) by C. S. George and more recently in the 84000 project.

SA_GE07_bhg4c01u.title: Bhagavadgītā 1
SA_GE07_bhg4c01u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_bhg4c01u.tradition: Hindu (within the Mahabharata; later foundational for Vedanta)
SA_GE07_bhg4c01u.genre: scripture; philosophical-religious poem (part of an epic)
SA_GE07_bhg4c01u.summary: Chapter 1 of the Bhagavadgita, the 'Arjuna-viṣāda-yoga' ('The Yoga of Arjuna's Despair'). It sets the scene on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, describing the assembled armies and Arjuna's collapse into grief and moral paralysis as he refuses to fight against his kinsmen, teachers, and elders, prompting Krishna's teaching.
SA_GE07_bhg4c01u.history: The Bhagavadgita forms part of the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahabharata and is traditionally attributed to the sage Vyāsa. The opening chapter frames the entire dialogue by establishing Arjuna's crisis; the Gita as a whole became the most commented-upon Hindu scripture, with classic bhāṣyas by Shankara, Ramanuja, and Madhva.

SA_GE07_bhg4c02u.title: Bhagavadgītā 2
SA_GE07_bhg4c02u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_bhg4c02u.tradition: Hindu (within the Mahabharata; foundational for Vedanta)
SA_GE07_bhg4c02u.genre: scripture; philosophical-religious poem (part of an epic)
SA_GE07_bhg4c02u.summary: Chapter 2 of the Bhagavadgita, the 'Sāṅkhya-yoga,' often regarded as a summary of the whole work. Krishna begins his teaching by arguing for the immortality and indestructibility of the self (ātman), the duty to act according to one's dharma without attachment to results (karma-yoga), and the marks of the person of steady wisdom (sthitaprajña).
SA_GE07_bhg4c02u.history: The second chapter introduces the Gita's central philosophical and ethical themes and is among its most quoted. Forming part of the Mahabharata's Bhīṣmaparvan and ascribed to Vyāsa, it lays out the synthesis of Samkhya metaphysics with disciplined, detached action that later Vedanta commentators elaborated.

SA_GE07_bhg4c03u.title: Bhagavadgītā 3
SA_GE07_bhg4c03u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_bhg4c03u.tradition: Hindu (within the Mahabharata; foundational for Vedanta)
SA_GE07_bhg4c03u.genre: scripture; philosophical-religious poem (part of an epic)
SA_GE07_bhg4c03u.summary: Chapter 3 of the Bhagavadgita, the 'Karma-yoga' ('The Yoga of Action'). Krishna explains why renunciation of action is impossible and undesirable, urging instead disciplined, selfless action performed as sacrifice (yajña) and without attachment, and addressing the roles of desire and anger as obstacles on the path.
SA_GE07_bhg4c03u.history: The third chapter develops the doctrine of niṣkāma karma (desireless action) that is central to the Gita's ethics, responding to Arjuna's confusion about whether knowledge or action is superior. As part of the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahabharata, it was extensively interpreted by the major Vedanta schools.

SA_GE07_bhg4c04u.title: Bhagavadgītā 4
SA_GE07_bhg4c04u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c04u.tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; theistic synthesis of Sāṃkhya, Yoga and Bhakti)
SA_GE07_bhg4c04u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c04u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c04u.summary: Chapter 4, Jñāna-Karma-Saṃnyāsa Yoga ('Yoga of Knowledge and the Renunciation of Action'). Krishna declares that he repeatedly takes birth (avatāra) in every age to restore dharma, and explains how action performed without attachment, as sacrifice and offered up in knowledge, does not bind. The chapter introduces the parampara (succession) by which this ancient teaching is transmitted and exalts wisdom (jñāna) as the fire that consumes all karma.
SA_GE07_bhg4c04u.history: The Bhagavadgītā is part of the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata (chs. 23-40), traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but in fact an anonymous, possibly composite, insertion into the epic. It became the most commented-upon Hindu text, with classic bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja and Madhva, and was canonized as one of the three foundational texts (prasthānatrayī) of Vedānta.

SA_GE07_bhg4c05u.title: Bhagavadgītā 5
SA_GE07_bhg4c05u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c05u.tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; synthesis of Sāṃkhya, Yoga and Bhakti)
SA_GE07_bhg4c05u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c05u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c05u.summary: Chapter 5, Karma-Saṃnyāsa Yoga ('Yoga of Renunciation of Action'). Arjuna asks whether renunciation of action or the disciplined performance of action is better; Krishna answers that both lead to liberation but that karma-yoga (action without attachment) is superior. The truly wise see that the self acts not at all, attributing action to the senses and gunas, and rest in the equanimity of Brahman.
SA_GE07_bhg4c05u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. The text harmonizes the apparent tension between renunciation and action that animates much of the epic. Extensively commented upon by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva and later teachers.

SA_GE07_bhg4c06u.title: Bhagavadgītā 6
SA_GE07_bhg4c06u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c06u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c06u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c06u.summary: Chapter 6, Dhyāna Yoga or Ātmasaṃyama Yoga ('Yoga of Meditation / Self-Restraint'). Krishna sets out the practice of meditation: posture, moderation in food and sleep, and the steadying of the mind until it rests motionless 'like a lamp in a windless place.' He reassures Arjuna that the yogi who falls short does not perish but is reborn in favorable circumstances to continue, and praises the devotee united with him as the highest yogi.
SA_GE07_bhg4c06u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter is a key locus classicus for classical contemplative practice and was much used by later yoga and Vedānta commentators including Śaṅkara and Rāmānuja.

SA_GE07_bhg4c07u.title: Bhagavadgītā 7
SA_GE07_bhg4c07u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c07u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c07u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c07u.summary: Chapter 7, Jñāna-Vijñāna Yoga ('Yoga of Knowledge and Realization'). Krishna describes his twofold nature: the lower (eightfold material prakṛti) and the higher (the life-principle that sustains the cosmos). He explains how the gunas and divine māyā veil him from beings, distinguishes four kinds of devotees who turn to him, and declares himself the ultimate ground behind all forms of worship.
SA_GE07_bhg4c07u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter opens the middle section (chs. 7-12) emphasizing divine nature and devotion, central to the theistic Vedānta of Rāmānuja and Madhva.

SA_GE07_bhg4c08u.title: Bhagavadgītā 8
SA_GE07_bhg4c08u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c08u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c08u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c08u.summary: Chapter 8, Akṣara-Brahma Yoga ('Yoga of the Imperishable Brahman'). Krishna answers Arjuna's questions about Brahman, the self, action, and the divine, and teaches that whatever state of being one remembers at the moment of death determines one's next condition; hence one should remember him constantly. The chapter also expounds the cosmic cycles (the day and night of Brahmā) and the bright and dark paths by which the dying depart.
SA_GE07_bhg4c08u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. Its teaching on remembrance at death (anta-kāla-smaraṇa) became foundational for Hindu devotional eschatology and was developed by Vaiṣṇava commentators.

SA_GE07_bhg4c09u.title: Bhagavadgītā 9
SA_GE07_bhg4c09u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c09u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c09u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c09u.summary: Chapter 9, Rāja-Vidyā Rāja-Guhya Yoga ('Yoga of the Royal Knowledge and Royal Secret'). Krishna reveals the most confidential knowledge: that all beings abide in him while he remains unattached, pervading the cosmos unseen. He declares that even a simple offering of a leaf, flower, fruit or water given with devotion is accepted, and that even the lowliest devotee who worships him with single-minded love attains him.
SA_GE07_bhg4c09u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter is among the strongest statements of bhakti in the Gītā and was central to the devotional theologies of Rāmānuja and the Vaiṣṇava traditions.

SA_GE07_bhg4c10u.title: Bhagavadgītā 10
SA_GE07_bhg4c10u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c10u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c10u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c10u.summary: Chapter 10, Vibhūti Yoga ('Yoga of the Divine Glories'). Krishna enumerates his manifold manifestations (vibhūtis), declaring himself the supreme principle behind the most excellent of every class of being: among the Ādityas Viṣṇu, among rivers the Ganges, among mountains Meru, and so forth. He teaches that whatever is glorious, beautiful or powerful springs from a fragment of his splendor.
SA_GE07_bhg4c10u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The vibhūti listing furnished later Hindu theology and poetry with a vocabulary for perceiving the divine immanent in the world; it prepares the reader for the theophany of chapter 11.

SA_GE07_bhg4c11u.title: Bhagavadgītā 11
SA_GE07_bhg4c11u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c11u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c11u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c11u.summary: Chapter 11, Viśvarūpa-Darśana Yoga ('Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form'). At Arjuna's request, Krishna grants him divine sight and reveals his cosmic form (viśvarūpa), the entire universe with its gods and worlds blazing within a single body of innumerable mouths and eyes. Terrified at seeing warriors rushing into the devouring jaws of time, Arjuna praises Krishna and begs him to resume his gentle human shape.
SA_GE07_bhg4c11u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The theophany is the dramatic and theological climax of the Gītā, famous for the verse 'I am become Time (kāla), destroyer of worlds,' and has been a touchstone of Hindu art, devotion, and modern reception.

SA_GE07_bhg4c12u.title: Bhagavadgītā 12
SA_GE07_bhg4c12u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c12u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c12u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c12u.summary: Chapter 12, Bhakti Yoga ('Yoga of Devotion'). Arjuna asks which is superior: worship of the personal, manifest God, or of the unmanifest Absolute. Krishna replies that both reach him, but the path of the formless is harder for embodied beings, so loving devotion to his personal form is recommended; he closes by describing the qualities of the devotee who is dear to him.
SA_GE07_bhg4c12u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This short chapter is a central proof-text for the saguna/nirguna debate and for the primacy of bhakti, prized especially by Rāmānuja's school.

SA_GE07_bhg4c13u.title: Bhagavadgītā 13
SA_GE07_bhg4c13u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c13u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c13u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c13u.summary: Chapter 13, Kṣetra-Kṣetrajña-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Distinction between the Field and the Knower of the Field'). Krishna distinguishes the 'field' (kṣetra, the body and material nature) from the 'knower of the field' (kṣetrajña, the conscious self), and identifies himself as the knower in all fields. He expounds prakṛti and puruṣa in Sāṃkhya terms and teaches that liberation comes from discerning the difference between the perishable field and the imperishable knower.
SA_GE07_bhg4c13u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter opens the final group (chs. 13-18), the most overtly Sāṃkhya-Vedānta in idiom, and is heavily drawn on in Vedānta epistemology and metaphysics.

SA_GE07_bhg4c14u.title: Bhagavadgītā 14
SA_GE07_bhg4c14u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c14u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c14u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c14u.summary: Chapter 14, Guṇatraya-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Division of the Three Gunas'). Krishna analyzes the three constituents of material nature, sattva (purity), rajas (passion) and tamas (inertia), describing how each binds the embodied self and conditions rebirth. He teaches that the liberated person transcends the gunas (guṇātīta) and that such transcendence is reached through unwavering devotion to him.
SA_GE07_bhg4c14u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The systematic treatment of the gunas links the Gītā closely to Sāṃkhya psychology while subordinating it to bhakti.

SA_GE07_bhg4c15u.title: Bhagavadgītā 15
SA_GE07_bhg4c15u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c15u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c15u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c15u.summary: Chapter 15, Puruṣottama Yoga ('Yoga of the Supreme Person'). Krishna describes the cosmos as an inverted aśvattha (fig) tree whose roots are above and branches below, to be cut down with the axe of non-attachment. He then distinguishes the perishable (kṣara) and imperishable (akṣara) beings and declares himself the supreme person (puruṣottama) who transcends both, the source of memory, knowledge and the Vedas.
