SA_GE07_bhg4c15u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The cosmic-tree imagery draws on Upaniṣadic precedents (Kaṭha Upaniṣad), and the puruṣottama doctrine is foundational for Vaiṣṇava theology.

SA_GE07_bhg4c16u.title: Bhagavadgītā 16
SA_GE07_bhg4c16u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c16u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c16u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c16u.summary: Chapter 16, Daivāsura-Sampad-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Division between Divine and Demonic Endowments'). Krishna contrasts the qualities of those born to a 'divine' (daivī) destiny, fearlessness, purity, non-violence, truthfulness, with the 'demonic' (āsurī) traits of arrogance, lust, greed and hypocrisy. He warns that those given over to demonic conduct fall into ruin, and commends scripture (śāstra) as the authority for determining right action.
SA_GE07_bhg4c16u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter offers the Gītā's clearest moral typology of human character and its appeal to scriptural authority in conduct.

SA_GE07_bhg4c17u.title: Bhagavadgītā 17
SA_GE07_bhg4c17u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c17u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c17u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c17u.summary: Chapter 17, Śraddhātraya-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Division of the Threefold Faith'). Krishna explains that faith (śraddhā), like food, sacrifice, austerity and giving, takes three forms according to the predominant guna (sattva, rajas, tamas). He also expounds the sacred formula 'oṃ tat sat' as designations of Brahman that consecrate ritual acts.
SA_GE07_bhg4c17u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The chapter integrates Vedic ritual categories into the Gītā's guna-based ethics and supplies the much-cited 'oṃ tat sat' triad.

SA_GE07_bhg4c18u.title: Bhagavadgītā 18
SA_GE07_bhg4c18u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhg4c18u.tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; synthesis of Karma, Jñāna and Bhakti)
SA_GE07_bhg4c18u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
SA_GE07_bhg4c18u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
SA_GE07_bhg4c18u.summary: Chapter 18, Mokṣa-Saṃnyāsa Yoga ('Yoga of Liberation through Renunciation'), the longest and concluding chapter. Krishna distinguishes renunciation (saṃnyāsa) from relinquishment of the fruits of action (tyāga), reviews knowledge, action and agents according to the three gunas, and describes duties suited to one's nature (svadharma). He culminates in his supreme counsel, to abandon all dharmas and take refuge in him alone, after which Arjuna, his doubts dispelled, resolves to fight.
SA_GE07_bhg4c18u.history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. As the summation of the dialogue, this chapter recapitulates the whole teaching; its 'charama śloka' (18.66) is the most debated verse in Vaiṣṇava soteriology, central to Rāmānuja and the Śrīvaiṣṇava prapatti doctrine.

SA_GE07_bhgce__u.title: Bhagavadgītā
SA_GE07_bhgce__u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_bhgce__u.tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; synthesis of Sāṃkhya, Yoga and Bhakti)
SA_GE07_bhgce__u.genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic; foundational Vedānta text (prasthānatrayī)
SA_GE07_bhgce__u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed; often placed 1st c. BCE)
SA_GE07_bhgce__u.summary: The Bhagavadgītā ('Song of the Lord') is a 700-verse dialogue, set on the battlefield of Kurukṣetra, in which the warrior Arjuna, despondent at the prospect of killing his kinsmen, is counseled by his charioteer Krishna, who is revealed as God. Across eighteen chapters Krishna synthesizes the paths of disciplined action (karma-yoga), knowledge (jñāna-yoga) and devotion (bhakti-yoga), teaching the imperishable self, the nature of the divine, and liberation. It culminates in the vision of Krishna's cosmic form and the call to take refuge in him.
SA_GE07_bhgce__u.history: An anonymous, probably composite, work inserted into the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata and traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. It became the most influential and most commented-upon text of Hinduism, canonized with the Upaniṣads and Brahmasūtra as one of the three pillars (prasthānatrayī) of Vedānta and the subject of major bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja and Madhva; in the modern era it shaped figures from Gandhi to global yoga.

SA_GE07_mbh_01_u.title: Mahābhārata: Ādiparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_01_u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_mbh_01_u.date_estimate: c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (composite); core narrative perhaps Mauryan-Gupta period
SA_GE07_mbh_01_u.summary: The Ādiparvan ('Book of the Beginning') is the first of the eighteen books of the Mahābhārata. It frames the epic (Vyāsa's composition, the snake sacrifice of Janamejaya, the bard Ugraśravas) and narrates the origins and youth of the Bhārata dynasty: the births of the Kuru and Pāṇḍava princes, the rivalry of the cousins, episodes such as the burning of the lac house and the contest for Draupadī, and the partition of the kingdom. It also contains famous embedded tales like Śakuntalā and the churning of the ocean.
SA_GE07_mbh_01_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to the sage Vyāsa but in fact the product of centuries of oral and redactional growth. Its critical edition was the first volume of the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute project, edited by V. S. Sukthankar (Adiparvan published 1933), whose prolegomena established the methodology for the whole critical edition (completed 1966).

SA_GE07_mbh_05_u.title: Mahābhārata: Udyogaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_05_u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_mbh_05_u.summary: The Udyogaparvan ('Book of the Effort/Preparation') is the fifth book of the Mahābhārata. After the Pāṇḍavas' exile ends, it recounts the failed diplomacy and mobilization for war: both sides seek allies, and Krishna goes to Hastināpura as peace envoy, but Duryodhana refuses any compromise. It contains the moral counsel given to Dhṛtarāṣṭra by Vidura (the Vidura-nīti) and the teaching of Sanatsujāta, and ends with the armies marshalled for battle.
SA_GE07_mbh_05_u.history: Part of the composite Mahābhārata traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Its critical edition was prepared at the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute by S. K. De. The didactic insertions (Vidura-nīti, Sanatsujātīya) made it a favored source of nīti (ethical-political) wisdom.

SA_GE07_mbh_06_u.title: Mahābhārata: Bhīṣmaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_06_u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_mbh_06_u.summary: The Bhīṣmaparvan ('Book of Bhīṣma') is the sixth book of the Mahābhārata and the first of the war books. It opens the eighteen-day battle of Kurukṣetra under the generalship of the Kuru patriarch Bhīṣma, narrating the first ten days of fighting until Bhīṣma is felled by Arjuna (aided by Śikhaṇḍin) and lies on a bed of arrows. This book contains the Bhagavadgītā (chapters 23-40), Krishna's battlefield discourse to Arjuna.
SA_GE07_mbh_06_u.history: Part of the composite Mahābhārata traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Its critical edition was edited by S. K. Belvalkar at the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute. The book's inclusion of the Bhagavadgītā made it by far the most influential of the parvans in religious and philosophical history.

SA_GE07_mbh_10_u.title: Mahābhārata: Sauptikaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_10_u.author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
SA_GE07_mbh_10_u.summary: The Sauptikaparvan ('Book of the Sleeping/Night Attack') is the tenth and one of the shortest books of the Mahābhārata. After the war has ended in Pāṇḍava victory, the surviving Kaurava warrior Aśvatthāman, with Kṛpa and Kṛtavarman, raids the enemy camp by night and massacres the sleeping Pāṇḍava army, including Draupadī's sons. In the ensuing pursuit Aśvatthāman releases the apocalyptic brahmaśiras weapon, which Vyāsa and Krishna intervene to neutralize, and he is cursed for the atrocity.
SA_GE07_mbh_10_u.history: Part of the composite Mahābhārata traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Its critical edition was prepared at the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute (volume with the Striparvan, ed. H. D. Velankar and V. G. Paranjpe). W. J. Johnson's translation 'The Sauptikaparvan: The Massacre at Night' (Oxford) gave it wide modern currency as a meditation on the ethics of revenge and total war.

SA_GE07_mbh_11_u.title: Mahābhārata: Strīparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_11_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_11_u.date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (whole epic); this book belongs to the older narrative core
SA_GE07_mbh_11_u.summary: The eleventh book of the Mahābhārata, the 'Book of the Women.' Following the catastrophic battle at Kurukṣetra, it depicts the lamentation of the women of both armies as they survey the battlefield strewn with the dead. Queen Gāndhārī, mother of the slain Kauravas, surveys the carnage and curses Kṛṣṇa, and Vidura and Vyāsa offer consolation on grief and the inevitability of death.
SA_GE07_mbh_11_u.history: The Mahābhārata grew over centuries from a martial core into an encyclopedic epic, traditionally ascribed to the sage Vyāsa but in fact a composite, anonymous accretion. The Strīparvan is one of the shorter books and includes the Strīvilāpa (lament) and the Śrāddha/Jalapradānika sections on funeral rites. The Pune Critical Edition (1919-1966) established its standard text.

SA_GE07_mbh_12_u.title: Mahābhārata: Śāntiparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_12_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_12_u.genre: epic (parvan / book of the Mahābhārata); didactic 'wisdom literature'
SA_GE07_mbh_12_u.date_estimate: largely a later didactic stratum, c. 2nd c. BCE - 4th c. CE
SA_GE07_mbh_12_u.summary: The twelfth and longest book of the Mahābhārata, the 'Book of Peace.' After the war, the dying Bhīṣma, lying on a bed of arrows, instructs the grief-stricken king Yudhiṣṭhira at length on rājadharma (the duties of kings and good governance), āpaddharma (conduct in times of distress), and mokṣadharma (the path to liberation). It is an encyclopedic treatise embedding philosophy (Sāṃkhya, Yoga), cosmology, fables, and ethics.
SA_GE07_mbh_12_u.history: The Śāntiparvan, with the following Anuśāsanaparvan, is widely regarded as a large later interpolation in the 'wisdom literature' genre, expanding the epic into a compendium of dharma. The mokṣadharma section in particular preserves important early Vaiṣṇava and philosophical material. The critical edition contains roughly 13,000 verses across some 350 chapters.

SA_GE07_mbh_13_u.title: Mahābhārata: Anuśāsanaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_13_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_13_u.genre: epic (parvan / book of the Mahābhārata); didactic dharmaśāstra material
SA_GE07_mbh_13_u.date_estimate: later didactic stratum, c. 2nd c. BCE - 4th c. CE
SA_GE07_mbh_13_u.summary: The thirteenth book, the 'Book of Instructions,' continues Bhīṣma's discourse to Yudhiṣṭhira begun in the Śāntiparvan. It covers a vast range of dharma: gift-giving (dāna), vows, the duties of the varṇas, the conduct of women, the merits of pilgrimage and ritual, the greatness of brahmins, and the worship of Śiva and Viṣṇu, before narrating Bhīṣma's final passing.
SA_GE07_mbh_13_u.history: Together with the Śāntiparvan, the Anuśāsanaparvan forms the great didactic core of the epic and is treated as a later expansion that absorbed much normative (dharmaśāstric) and devotional material. It contains the Viṣṇusahasranāma (Thousand Names of Viṣṇu), one of the most popular Hindu devotional texts.

SA_GE07_mbh_14_u.title: Mahābhārata: Aśvamedhikaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_14_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_14_u.date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE; contains both narrative and later didactic material
SA_GE07_mbh_14_u.summary: The fourteenth book, the 'Book of the Horse Sacrifice.' Yudhiṣṭhira, to expiate the slaughter of the war, performs the royal aśvamedha (horse sacrifice); the consecrated horse roams the earth followed by Arjuna, who fights numerous kings to assert imperial sovereignty. The book famously embeds the Anugītā, in which Kṛṣṇa restates spiritual teachings to Arjuna, echoing the Bhagavadgītā.
SA_GE07_mbh_14_u.history: The Aśvamedhikaparvan mixes older narrative (Arjuna's campaign, the revival of Parikṣit) with later philosophical insertions such as the Anugītā. The legend of the mongoose at Yudhiṣṭhira's sacrifice, questioning the value of ostentatious ritual, is one of its noted moral episodes.

SA_GE07_mbh_15_u.title: Mahābhārata: Āśramavāsikaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_15_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_15_u.date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
SA_GE07_mbh_15_u.summary: The fifteenth book, the 'Book of the Hermitage.' The aged Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Gāndhārī, Kuntī, and Vidura renounce the kingdom and retire to the forest to live as ascetics. Vyāsa miraculously raises the spirits of the war dead from the Ganges so the bereaved may see them once more; the elders later perish in a forest fire, and Yudhiṣṭhira learns of their deaths.
SA_GE07_mbh_15_u.history: One of the shorter, later books of the epic, it dramatizes the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) stage of life and the theme of renunciation that pervades the post-war books. The 'raising of the dead' episode is among its most striking narrative set-pieces.

SA_GE07_mbh_16_u.title: Mahābhārata: Mausalaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_16_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_16_u.date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
SA_GE07_mbh_16_u.summary: The sixteenth and one of the shortest books, the 'Book of Clubs.' It narrates the self-destruction of the Yādava/Vṛṣṇi clan at Prabhāsa, where, fulfilling Gāndhārī's curse, the Yādavas slaughter one another in a drunken brawl using clubs grown from a cursed iron pestle. Balarāma departs by yoga, and Kṛṣṇa is mortally wounded by the hunter Jarā; the city of Dvārakā is swallowed by the sea.
SA_GE07_mbh_16_u.history: This book brings the divine actors of the epic to their end and sets the stage for the Pāṇḍavas' own renunciation. Its theme of the inexorable fulfillment of curses and the passing of the heroic age marks the onset of the Kali Yuga in epic chronology.

SA_GE07_mbh_17_u.title: Mahābhārata: Mahāprasthānikaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_17_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_17_u.date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
SA_GE07_mbh_17_u.summary: The seventeenth and shortest book, the 'Book of the Great Journey.' The Pāṇḍavas and Draupadī renounce the kingdom and set out on a final pilgrimage toward the Himālayas and Mount Meru, seeking heaven. One by one Draupadī and four of the brothers fall and die along the way, each owing to a moral flaw, leaving only Yudhiṣṭhira, accompanied by a faithful dog, to continue.
SA_GE07_mbh_17_u.history: A brief, deeply moral coda to the epic, it allegorizes the consequences of each character's subtle imperfections. It leads directly into the final book, the Svargārohaṇaparvan.

SA_GE07_mbh_18_u.title: Mahābhārata: Svargārohaṇaparvan
SA_GE07_mbh_18_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GE07_mbh_18_u.date_estimate: composed c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE
SA_GE07_mbh_18_u.summary: The eighteenth and final book, the 'Book of the Ascent to Heaven.' Yudhiṣṭhira, the lone survivor, refuses to abandon the dog accompanying him (revealed as the god Dharma) and is taken to heaven. There he is tested by an illusory vision of his brothers in hell and the Kauravas in heaven, learns the relativity of reward and punishment, and is finally reunited with all in their true celestial forms.
SA_GE07_mbh_18_u.history: This short concluding book resolves the epic's meditation on dharma, loyalty, and the nature of heaven and hell, and ends with the phalaśruti (statement of the merit of hearing the epic). It completes the eighteen-parvan structure of the whole.

SA_GE07_naray__u.title: Nārāyaṇīya
SA_GE07_naray__u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GE07_naray__u.genre: epic episode; theological / devotional treatise embedded in the Mahābhārata
SA_GE07_naray__u.date_estimate: relatively late addition to the epic, c. 3rd-4th c. CE (disputed)
SA_GE07_naray__u.summary: A self-contained theological section (roughly Mahābhārata 12.321-339, the Mokṣadharma sub-parva of the Śāntiparvan) of about 1,000 verses. It expounds the supremacy of Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa, the doctrine of his vyūhas (cosmic emanations), the practice of single-minded devotion (ekāntika-bhakti), and a frame narrative of the sage Nārada's visit to Śvetadvīpa, the 'White Island' of pure devotees.
SA_GE07_naray__u.history: The Nārāyaṇīya is a key witness to early Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇavism and the formation of bhakti theology within the epic; scholars treat it as one of the latest didactic strata of the Śāntiparvan. It has attracted major Indological study (e.g., the Schreiner-led Tübingen project) as a window onto pre-Gupta and Gupta-era Vaiṣṇava religion.

SA_GE09_ram_01_u.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 1. Bālakāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_01_u.author: Vālmīki (traditional); Bālakāṇḍa largely a later addition
SA_GE09_ram_01_u.date_estimate: core epic c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE; Bālakāṇḍa among the latest strata
SA_GE09_ram_01_u.summary: The first book, the 'Book of Childhood.' It narrates the divine plan for Rāma's birth as an avatāra of Viṣṇu to destroy Rāvaṇa, the births of Rāma and his brothers to King Daśaratha, their tutelage under the sage Viśvāmitra and slaying of demons, and Rāma's breaking of Śiva's bow to win Sītā in marriage. It also frames the whole poem with the story of Vālmīki's composition of the first śloka.
SA_GE09_ram_01_u.history: Books 2-6 form the oldest core of the Rāmāyaṇa, while the Bālakāṇḍa (Book 1) and Uttarakāṇḍa (Book 7) are widely regarded by scholars as later additions, on grounds of style, language, and the more pronounced divinization of Rāma; both are absent or attenuated in the oldest manuscript traditions. The work is traditionally ascribed to the ādikavi ('first poet') Vālmīki.

SA_GE09_ram_03_u.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_03_u.author: Vālmīki (traditional)
SA_GE09_ram_03_u.date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
SA_GE09_ram_03_u.summary: The third book, the 'Book of the Forest.' It covers Rāma, Sītā, and Lakṣmaṇa's life of exile in the Daṇḍaka forest, their encounters with sages and demons, the disfiguring of the demoness Śūrpaṇakhā, the slaying of Khara and Dūṣaṇa, and the pivotal abduction of Sītā by the demon-king Rāvaṇa after the ruse of the golden deer, ending with Rāma's grief-stricken search.
SA_GE09_ram_03_u.history: The Araṇyakāṇḍa belongs to the ancient narrative core of the epic (Books 2-6) attributed to Vālmīki. It contains the turning point of the entire story, the abduction of Sītā, which sets in motion the war against Laṅkā.

SA_GE09_ram_03pu.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_03pu.author: Vālmīki (traditional)
SA_GE09_ram_03pu.genre: epic (kāṇḍa / book of the Rāmāyaṇa); recensional/variant text witness
SA_GE09_ram_03pu.date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
SA_GE09_ram_03pu.summary: A second text-witness of the third book of the Rāmāyaṇa, the 'Book of the Forest' (Araṇyakāṇḍa). Its content is the same as GE09ram_03_u: the forest exile, the episode with Śūrpaṇakhā, the slaying of Khara, the golden-deer ruse, and Rāvaṇa's abduction of Sītā. The differing work_id suffix ('pu') indicates a distinct recension or pāda-by-pāda/electronic encoding of the same kāṇḍa.
SA_GE09_ram_03pu.history: The Rāmāyaṇa survives in several regional recensions (notably Northern and Southern), and digital corpora often archive parallel encodings of the same book; this entry appears to be such an alternate version of the Araṇyakāṇḍa. The underlying text remains the Vālmīki Rāmāyaṇa's third book.

SA_GE09_ram_04_u.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 4. Kiṣkindhākāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_04_u.author: Vālmīki (traditional)
SA_GE09_ram_04_u.date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
SA_GE09_ram_04_u.summary: The fourth book, the 'Book of Kiṣkindhā.' Rāma allies with the exiled monkey-prince Sugrīva, kills Sugrīva's brother Vālin to restore him to the throne of Kiṣkindhā, and in return the monkey hosts mobilize search parties to find Sītā. It concludes with Hanumān and the southern party learning from Sampāti that Sītā is held captive in Laṅkā.
SA_GE09_ram_04_u.history: Part of the ancient core of the epic, this book develops the alliance between Rāma and the vānaras and foregrounds Hanumān, whose devotion becomes central to later Rāma bhakti. The Vālin-slaying episode is a noted locus of ethical debate in the commentarial tradition.

SA_GE09_ram_05_u.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 5. Sundarakaṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_05_u.author: Vālmīki (traditional)
SA_GE09_ram_05_u.date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
SA_GE09_ram_05_u.summary: The fifth book, the 'Beautiful Book.' It centers on Hanumān's heroic leap across the ocean to Laṅkā, his search through Rāvaṇa's city, his discovery of the imprisoned Sītā in the Aśoka grove, his delivery of Rāma's signet ring, the burning of Laṅkā with his flaming tail, and his return to report Sītā's whereabouts to Rāma.
SA_GE09_ram_05_u.history: The Sundarakāṇḍa is the most beloved and ritually recited book of the Rāmāyaṇa, prized for its devotional focus on Hanumān and traditionally regarded as especially auspicious. It belongs to the epic's ancient core.

SA_GE09_ram_06_u.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 6. Yuddhakāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_06_u.author: Vālmīki (traditional)
SA_GE09_ram_06_u.date_estimate: part of the older core, c. 5th-4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE
SA_GE09_ram_06_u.summary: The sixth book, the 'Book of War' (also called Laṅkākāṇḍa). It narrates the building of the bridge (setu) to Laṅkā, the great battle between Rāma's monkey army and Rāvaṇa's demons, the deaths of Kumbhakarṇa, Indrajit, and finally Rāvaṇa, the rescue of Sītā and her trial by fire (agniparīkṣā), and the triumphal return to Ayodhyā for Rāma's coronation.
SA_GE09_ram_06_u.history: The climactic and longest action book of the epic's ancient core, it brings the central narrative to its resolution. In most recensions the original story effectively concludes here, with the Uttarakāṇḍa regarded as a later appendix.

SA_GE09_ram_07_u.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 7. Uttarakāṇḍa
SA_GE09_ram_07_u.author: Vālmīki (traditional); widely regarded as a later addition
SA_GE09_ram_07_u.date_estimate: latest stratum of the epic, c. 1st-4th c. CE (later than Books 2-6)
SA_GE09_ram_07_u.summary: The seventh and final book, the 'Later Book.' It recounts events after Rāma's coronation: the history and genealogy of Rāvaṇa and the rākṣasas, Hanumān's origins, and—most famously—Rāma's banishment of the pregnant Sītā in response to public doubt of her chastity. Sītā takes refuge with Vālmīki, bears the twins Lava and Kuśa, and finally returns to the earth, after which Rāma departs the world.
SA_GE09_ram_07_u.history: The Uttarakāṇḍa is, with the Bālakāṇḍa, the part of the Rāmāyaṇa most consistently judged by scholars to be a later interpolation, on grounds of its more explicit divinization of Rāma, narrative inconsistencies, and absence from the oldest manuscripts. Its treatment of Sītā's exile has been a major focus of later literary, devotional, and critical engagement.

SA_GK16_andhvc1u.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 1
SA_GK16_andhvc1u.author: Ānandavardhana
SA_GK16_andhvc1u.date_estimate: c. mid-to-late 9th c. CE (Ānandavardhana fl. c. 820-890 CE, Kashmir)
SA_GK16_andhvc1u.summary: The first chapter (uddyota) of the Dhvanyāloka ('Light on Suggestion'), the foundational work of the dhvani school of Sanskrit poetics. It introduces and defends the thesis that dhvani—suggested or implied meaning—is the very soul (ātman) of poetry, distinguishing the literal/denoted sense from the more powerful suggested sense, against rival theorists who deny suggestion's existence.
SA_GK16_andhvc1u.history: Ānandavardhana, a court scholar under King Avantivarman of Kashmir, established dhvani as the dominant theory of Indian aesthetics. A debate (going back to Abhinavagupta) holds that the kārikās may be by an earlier 'Dhvanikāra' (called Sahṛdaya, hence the alternate title Sahṛdayāloka) while Ānandavardhana wrote the prose vṛtti; others ascribe the whole to him. Abhinavagupta's commentary, the Locana, became its classic exposition.

SA_GK16_andhvc2u.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 2
SA_GK16_andhvc2u.author: Ānandavardhana
SA_GK16_andhvc2u.summary: The second chapter of the Dhvanyāloka. It develops a typology of suggestion, treating in particular the relation of dhvani to rasa (aesthetic emotion), the varieties of suggested meaning, and the way figures of speech (alaṃkāras), qualities (guṇas), and rasa-dhvani operate. It elaborates the famous doctrine that the suggestion of rasa is the highest kind of dhvani.
SA_GK16_andhvc2u.history: Part of Ānandavardhana's four-uddyota treatise that reoriented Sanskrit poetics around suggestion and made rasa-dhvani its central category, profoundly influencing Abhinavagupta, Mammaṭa, and the whole later tradition.

SA_GK16_andhvc3u.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 3
SA_GK16_andhvc3u.author: Ānandavardhana
SA_GK16_andhvc3u.summary: The third and longest chapter of the Dhvanyāloka. It analyzes suggestion in fine grain, distinguishing the suggestion of bare matter, of figures, and of rasa; it discusses how the suggested meaning is conveyed by the power of words and sense, the relation between principal and subordinate suggestion (guṇībhūta-vyaṅgya), and the proper deployment of figures, qualities, and styles in service of rasa.
SA_GK16_andhvc3u.history: This chapter contains much of the technical machinery of dhvani theory and its applications to literary composition, and was extensively commented upon by Abhinavagupta in his Locana.

SA_GK16_andhvc4u.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 4
SA_GK16_andhvc4u.author: Ānandavardhana
SA_GK16_andhvc4u.summary: The fourth and concluding chapter of the Dhvanyāloka. It addresses the inexhaustible creativity of poetry under the theory of suggestion, arguing that despite the antiquity of poetic themes, dhvani allows infinite novelty, and it discusses the poet's genius (pratibhā), originality versus borrowing, and the unlimited scope that suggestion opens for the imaginative writer.
SA_GK16_andhvc4u.history: The final uddyota rounds off Ānandavardhana's argument by celebrating the boundless expressive potential of poetry grounded in suggestion, securing the Dhvanyāloka's status as the cornerstone of classical Indian literary theory.

SA_GK16_andhvy_u.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka]
SA_GK16_andhvy_u.author: Ānandavardhana
SA_GK16_andhvy_u.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with vṛtti)
SA_GK16_andhvy_u.summary: The foundational treatise of the dhvani ('suggestion/resonance') school of Sanskrit literary theory. It argues that the soul of poetry (kāvya) is not its literal sense or its ornaments but the suggested meaning (vyaṅgyārtha), realized especially as suggested aesthetic mood (rasa-dhvani). The work systematically classifies types of suggestion and elevates suggested rasa above figures of speech and qualities.
SA_GK16_andhvy_u.history: Composed by Ānandavardhana, a court poet under King Avantivarman of Kashmir; the title Sahṛdayāloka ('Light for the Connoisseur') is sometimes used. The work transformed Sanskrit poetics by shifting focus to inner meaning, and was given its most influential elaboration in Abhinavagupta's commentary, the Locana. Whether the kārikās and vṛtti are by one or two hands has been debated.

SA_GK16_bhakavpu.title: Kāvyālaṃkāra
SA_GK16_bhakavpu.author: Bhāmaha
SA_GK16_bhakavpu.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (śāstra in verse)
SA_GK16_bhakavpu.date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (disputed; some place late 7th-early 8th c.)
SA_GK16_bhakavpu.summary: One of the earliest extant systematic treatises on Sanskrit poetics, in six chapters (paricchedas) totaling about 398 verses. It treats the nature and aims of poetry, poetic faults (doṣa), figures of speech (alaṃkāra), and correctness of language, and is foundational to the alaṃkāra ('ornament') school. Bhāmaha treats kāvya as a discipline separate from dramaturgy.
SA_GK16_bhakavpu.history: Bhāmaha, probably a Kashmiri, is a key early authority often discussed alongside (and in rivalry with) Daṇḍin; their relative dating is debated. His emphasis on vakrokti (striking/oblique expression) as central to alaṃkāra influenced later theorists such as Udbhaṭa and Kuntaka.

SA_GK16_jaycndpu.title: Candrāloka
SA_GK16_jaycndpu.author: Jayadeva Pīyūṣavarṣa
SA_GK16_jaycndpu.summary: A popular, elementary verse handbook of Sanskrit poetics in ten chapters (mayūkhas), totaling about 350 verses. Written in a lucid, mnemonic style, it covers poetic faults, qualities, and especially figures of speech, often illustrating each device with an example that itself embodies the device. Its arrangement reworks the topic-sequence of Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa.
SA_GK16_jaycndpu.history: By Jayadeva, styled Pīyūṣavarṣa ('rain of nectar'), not to be confused with the Gītagovinda poet Jayadeva. Its popularity is attested by numerous later commentaries (e.g., by Gāgābhaṭṭa, Vaidyanātha). It became a standard teaching text in traditional pāṭhaśālā curricula.

SA_GK16_jgrasg1u.title: Rasagaṅgādhara
SA_GK16_jgrasg1u.author: Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja
SA_GK16_jgrasg1u.summary: A large, sophisticated treatise on poetics that reopens and refines the central questions of rasa, dhvani, and the definition of poetry using the analytical rigor of Navya-Nyāya logic. Jagannātha offers a fresh definition of kāvya as language whose meaning is charming (ramaṇīyārtha) and critically engages the entire prior tradition, especially Mammaṭa and Appayya Dīkṣita. The work is incomplete, breaking off in its treatment of figures.
SA_GK16_jgrasg1u.history: Composed by Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja (c. 1590-1670), a Telugu Brahmin scholar-poet who received the title Paṇḍitarāja at the Mughal court of Shah Jahan. He is traditionally regarded as the last great luminary of classical Sanskrit poetics, and the Rasagaṅgādhara is its culminating analytical work.

SA_GK16_kavsuvr.title: Kāvyālaṃkāra-sūtra-vṛtti
SA_GK16_kavsuvr.author: Vāmana (NOT Bhāmaha; metadata author hint appears incorrect)
SA_GK16_kavsuvr.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra with author's own vṛtti)
SA_GK16_kavsuvr.summary: A treatise composed as aphorisms (sūtras) with the author's own prose gloss (vṛtti), best known for the doctrine that rīti (a particular structured arrangement of words, i.e., 'style/diction') is the soul of poetry. It treats poetic qualities (guṇa), faults, and figures, grounding aesthetic value in the choice and arrangement of words.
SA_GK16_kavsuvr.history: Despite the project metadata attributing this to Bhāmaha, the Kāvyālaṃkārasūtravṛtti is the well-known work of Vāmana, the great exponent of the rīti (style) school and a contemporary of Udbhaṭa, traditionally placed around 800 CE and associated with the court of King Jayāpīḍa of Kashmir. It is regarded as an early systematic philosophy of literary aesthetics.

SA_GK16_kunvjivu.title: Vakroktijīvita
SA_GK16_kunvjivu.author: Kuntaka (Rājānaka Kuntaka)
SA_GK16_kunvjivu.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with author's vṛtti)
SA_GK16_kunvjivu.summary: A major treatise arguing that the essence ('life', jīvita) of all literature is vakrokti, 'oblique/striking expression'—the creative deviation of poetic language from ordinary usage. Kuntaka develops an elaborate six-fold typology of vakratā ('crookedness/strikingness') ranging from the level of phonemes and words up to whole works, presenting it as a unifying principle of poetic beauty.
SA_GK16_kunvjivu.history: Kuntaka, a Kashmiri theorist usually dated between Ānandavardhana (9th c.) and Abhinavagupta (10th-11th c.), reworked Bhāmaha's notion of vakrokti into a comprehensive aesthetic. The text survives incompletely; it was critically edited by S. K. De (1923/1928). It represents an alternative to the dominant dhvani school.

SA_GK16_mamkavpu.title: Kāvyaprakāśa
SA_GK16_mamkavpu.author: Maṃmaṭa (Mammaṭa)
SA_GK16_mamkavpu.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with vṛtti)
SA_GK16_mamkavpu.summary: The single most influential and widely taught compendium of Sanskrit poetics, in ten chapters (ullāsas) of verse kārikās with prose explanation and illustrative examples. It synthesizes the dhvani (suggestion) and alaṃkāra (ornament) traditions, treating the nature and purpose of poetry, word-functions (abhidhā, lakṣaṇā, vyañjanā), faults, qualities, figures, and the classification of poetry by suggestion.
SA_GK16_mamkavpu.history: By Mammaṭa, a Kashmiri scholar of the late 11th-12th c.; the final portion is traditionally said to have been completed by a second hand (Allaṭa/Alaka). It became the standard textbook of poetics, attracting over fifty commentaries—reputedly second only to the Bhagavadgītā among Sanskrit works in number of commentaries.

SA_GK16_mamkpb_u.title: Kāvyaprakāśa
SA_GK16_mamkpb_u.author: Maṃmaṭa (Mammaṭa)
SA_GK16_mamkpb_u.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with vṛtti)
SA_GK16_mamkpb_u.summary: A second digital recension/edition of Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa, the canonical ten-chapter (ullāsa) handbook of Sanskrit poetics. It defines poetry and its aims, analyzes the three word-functions and the mechanism of suggestion (dhvani), and classifies poetic faults, qualities, and figures of speech, synthesizing the dhvani and alaṃkāra schools.
SA_GK16_mamkpb_u.history: Same work as GK16mamkavpu; this work_id likely encodes a different edition or manuscript version (e.g., text-plus-commentary base). Mammaṭa was an 11th-12th c. Kashmiri theorist, and the work spawned the largest commentarial tradition in Sanskrit poetics.

SA_GK16_msabdvvu.title: Śabdavyāpāravicāra
SA_GK16_msabdvvu.author: Maṃmaṭa (Mammaṭa)
SA_GK16_msabdvvu.summary: A short specialized treatise ('Inquiry into the Operations of the Word') by Mammaṭa setting out the semantic functions of language—denotation (abhidhā), indication (lakṣaṇā), and suggestion (vyañjanā)—and defending the independence of vyañjanā as the basis of poetic suggestion. It argues against critics (notably Mukula Bhaṭṭa) who tried to reduce suggestion to the other functions.
SA_GK16_msabdvvu.history: A lesser-known companion piece to Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa, concentrating on the theory of word-power that underlies the dhvani doctrine. It is an important source for the Sanskrit philosophy of language as applied to poetics.

SA_GK16_rajkmimu.title: Kāvyamīmāṃsā
SA_GK16_rajkmimu.author: Rājaśekhara
SA_GK16_rajkmimu.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (practical handbook, partly in sūtra style)
SA_GK16_rajkmimu.summary: A practical handbook (originally planned in eighteen sections, of which the first, the Kavirahasya, survives) on the making of a poet. It discusses the poet's training, talent (pratibhā) and learning, the literary 'assembly', plagiarism, regional styles, and the geography and conventions (kavi-samaya) of poetic composition rather than abstract aesthetics.
SA_GK16_rajkmimu.history: By Rājaśekhara, a poet-dramatist and court scholar of the Pratihāra kings Mahendrapāla and Mahīpāla. The Kāvyamīmāṃsā is unusual in its sociological and pedagogical orientation and is a valuable source on the poetic culture and literary geography of early medieval India.

SA_GK16_rnatc__u.title: Nāṭakacandrikā
SA_GK16_rnatc__u.author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
SA_GK16_rnatc__u.summary: A treatise ('Moonlight of Drama') on the rules of dramaturgy, summarizing classical Sanskrit nāṭya theory (dramatic types, plot structure, sentiments) and adapting it to Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava devotional drama. It provides the technical framework underlying Rūpa's own devotional plays on Kṛṣṇa.
SA_GK16_rnatc__u.history: By Rūpa Gosvāmī (c. 1489-1564), one of the Six Gosvāmins of Vṛndāvana and a leading theologian of the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava movement founded by Caitanya. The work supports his project of recasting classical rasa-dramaturgy in the service of bhakti, as realized in his plays Vidagdha-mādhava and Lalita-mādhava.

SA_GK16_ruvyavivya_pu.title: Vyaktivivekavyākhyā
SA_GK16_ruvyavivya_pu.author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
SA_GK16_ruvyavivya_pu.summary: A commentary by Ruyyaka on Mahima Bhaṭṭa's Vyaktiviveka. The base text (Vyaktiviveka) argues against the dhvani theory, holding that what others call 'suggestion' is really a form of inference (anumāna), so that rasa is inferred rather than directly manifested; Ruyyaka's vyākhyā systematically expounds and evaluates these positions.
SA_GK16_ruvyavivya_pu.history: Ruyyaka, a 12th-century Kashmiri theorist and teacher (reputedly of Maṅkha), was among the first to reflect systematically on Mahima Bhaṭṭa's notion of vyakti ('manifestation'). The commentary is an important witness to the debate between the dhvani school (Ānandavardhana, Abhinavagupta) and its critics.

SA_GK16_ruyalscu.title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
SA_GK16_ruyalscu.author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
SA_GK16_ruyalscu.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra/kārikā with author's vṛtti)
SA_GK16_ruyalscu.summary: A systematic treatise ('The Whole Treasury of Ornament') devoted chiefly to the classification and analysis of figures of speech (alaṃkāra). Ruyyaka treats roughly eighty-two figures—a small number of word-figures (śabdālaṃkāra) and the majority sense-figures (arthālaṃkāra)—defining and exemplifying each within a dhvani-informed framework.
SA_GK16_ruyalscu.history: By Rājānaka Ruyyaka, a 12th-century Kashmiri scholar writing some two and a half centuries after Ānandavardhana. The work became a standard reference on figures and is best known together with the elaborate commentary (Vimarśinī) of Jayaratha. (Authorship has occasionally been linked to Maṅkha, Ruyyaka's pupil.)

SA_GK16_ruyalssu.title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
SA_GK16_ruyalssu.author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
SA_GK16_ruyalssu.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra/kārikā with vṛtti)
SA_GK16_ruyalssu.summary: Another recension/edition of Ruyyaka's Alaṅkārasarvasva, a treatise focused on the systematic classification of figures of speech (alaṃkāras), with definitions and illustrative examples set within a suggestion-oriented theory of poetry. This work_id likely corresponds to a particular edition or accompanying commentary version.
SA_GK16_ruyalssu.history: Same work as GK16ruyalscu; multiple work_ids reflect different editions/manuscripts (notably text with or without Jayaratha's or Samudrabandha's commentary). Ruyyaka is a 12th-century Kashmiri theorist; the text is a key authority on alaṃkāra in the post-Mammaṭa period.

SA_GK16_ruyalsvu.title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
SA_GK16_ruyalsvu.author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
SA_GK16_ruyalsvu.genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra/kārikā with vṛtti)
SA_GK16_ruyalsvu.summary: A further recension/edition of Ruyyaka's Alaṅkārasarvasva, the standard 12th-century treatise on figures of speech. It defines and exemplifies the inventory of śabdālaṃkāras and arthālaṃkāras and situates the analysis of ornament within the dhvani tradition. The 'vsu' suffix in the work_id probably marks a version carrying a particular commentary (e.g., Samudrabandha's vṛtti).
SA_GK16_ruyalsvu.history: Same underlying work as GK16ruyalscu/GK16ruyalssu, transmitted with different commentaries (Jayaratha's Vimarśinī in Kashmir; Samudrabandha's in the South). Ruyyaka's treatise became the principal medieval reference work on alaṃkāra.

SA_GK16_simhrs_u.title: Rasārṇavasudhākara
SA_GK16_simhrs_u.author: Siṃhabhūpāla (Singabhūpāla / Recherla Siṅgama Nāyaka)
SA_GK16_simhrs_u.tradition: Hindu; Sanskrit poetics and dramaturgy (rasa/nāṭya theory)
SA_GK16_simhrs_u.summary: A treatise ('Moon to the Ocean of Aesthetic Sentiment'), arranged in three 'rays' (vilāsas), giving an exhaustive account of rasa theory—the sentiments, their determinants and consequents, the hero and heroine typology (nāyaka-nāyikā-bheda), states of love, and dramatic conventions. It draws heavily on the Nāṭyaśāstra and Daśarūpaka traditions and is widely cited by later writers.
SA_GK16_simhrs_u.history: By Siṃhabhūpāla, a king of the Recherla (Velama) line in Andhra, a learned patron also credited with the music treatise Saṅgītasudhākara, active around 1330. His work on rasa is quoted by the commentator Mallinātha, attesting its early authority. Critically edited by T. Venkatacharya (Adyar Library, 1979).

SA_GK16_simhrsku.title: Rasārṇavasudhākara
SA_GK16_simhrsku.author: Siṃhabhūpāla (Singabhūpāla / Recherla Siṅgama Nāyaka)
SA_GK16_simhrsku.tradition: Hindu; Sanskrit poetics and dramaturgy (rasa/nāṭya theory)
SA_GK16_simhrsku.summary: A second recension/edition of Siṃhabhūpāla's Rasārṇavasudhākara, the three-part (vilāsa) compendium on aesthetic sentiment and dramaturgy. It systematizes rasa, bhāva, the hero-heroine typology, and the conventions of Sanskrit drama, synthesizing the Nāṭyaśāstra and Daśarūpaka traditions.
SA_GK16_simhrsku.history: Same work as GK16simhrs_u; the distinct work_id reflects a separate edition or manuscript version of the text. Composed by the 14th-century Andhra ruler-scholar Siṃhabhūpāla and cited by Mallinātha.

SA_GK16_udkass_u.title: Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha
SA_GK16_udkass_u.author: Udbhaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Udbhaṭa)
SA_GK16_udkass_u.summary: A concise compendium ('Collection of the Essence of the Ornaments of Poetry') treating about forty-one figures of speech (alaṃkāras), word- and sense-based, organized in six groups, each defined and illustrated. It refines and partly responds to Bhāmaha's treatment of figures and adds new ones (e.g., punaruktavadābhāsa, kāvyahetu, kāvyadṛṣṭānta).
SA_GK16_udkass_u.history: By Udbhaṭa, a Kashmiri poetician traditionally said to have been a court scholar (sabhāpati) of King Jayāpīḍa of Kashmir, and a contemporary of Vāmana. The work is a key link between the early (Bhāmaha) and later phases of the alaṃkāra school; it survives with the commentary of Pratīhārendurāja.

SA_GK16_udkassku.title: Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha
SA_GK16_udkassku.author: Udbhaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Udbhaṭa)
SA_GK16_udkassku.summary: Another recension/edition of Udbhaṭa's Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha, a compact treatise on figures of speech arranged in six groups (about 41 alaṃkāras), each defined and exemplified. This work_id likely corresponds to the text accompanied by Pratīhārendurāja's commentary (Laghuvṛtti).
SA_GK16_udkassku.history: Same work as GK16udkass_u; differing work_ids reflect distinct editions or text-plus-commentary versions. Udbhaṭa, a late-8th-century Kashmiri scholar at Jayāpīḍa's court, bridges the early and classical phases of the ornament school.

SA_GK16_ujjvni_u.title: Ujjvalanīlamaṇi
SA_GK16_ujjvni_u.author: Rūpa Gosvāmī (Rūpa Gosvāmin)
SA_GK16_ujjvni_u.summary: A specialized treatise ('The Blazing Blue Gem') that elaborates madhura-rasa, the 'sweet' erotic-devotional sentiment of love between Kṛṣṇa and the gopīs (especially Rādhā), which Rūpa had treated only briefly in his Bhakti-rasāmṛta-sindhu. Applying classical rasa categories (vibhāva, anubhāva, sthāyibhāva) to bhakti, it analyzes the heroines, the divine lovers, their moods, and the structure of sacred amorous emotion.
SA_GK16_ujjvni_u.history: By Rūpa Gosvāmī (c. 1489-1564), a foremost theologian among the Six Gosvāmins of Vṛndāvana in the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition of Caitanya. It is the definitive Gauḍīya text on madhura-rasa and was provided with influential commentaries (e.g., by Jīva Gosvāmī and Viśvanātha Cakravartī).
related_works: Locanarocanī / commentaries by Jīva Gosvāmī and Viśvanātha Cakravartī

SA_GK16_vamkalsu.title: Kāvyālaṅkārasūtra
SA_GK16_vamkalsu.author: Vāmana
SA_GK16_vamkalsu.summary: A foundational treatise of Sanskrit poetics (alaṅkāraśāstra), composed in aphoristic sūtras with the author's own vṛtti (gloss). It is the principal exposition of the rīti (style/diction) school, famously declaring 'rītir ātmā kāvyasya' — that rīti, a particular arrangement of words, is the soul of poetry. It treats the purpose of poetry, its body and soul, the guṇas (excellences) and doṣas (faults), and the alaṅkāras (figures of speech).
SA_GK16_vamkalsu.history: Vāmana, a Kashmiri, is traditionally placed around 800 CE and treated as a contemporary of Udbhaṭa; he systematized Daṇḍin's notion of rīti into a full theory. The work is usually transmitted as the Kāvyālaṅkārasūtravṛtti (sūtras plus auto-commentary) and is a canonical reference point in the history of Indian literary theory.

SA_GK16_vamkalvu.title: Kāvyālaṅkārasūtra
SA_GK16_vamkalvu.author: Vāmana
SA_GK16_vamkalvu.summary: A second digital witness (likely the vṛtti / auto-commentary version) of Vāmana's treatise on Sanskrit poetics. The work, in sūtra-and-vṛtti form, expounds the rīti school, holding that rīti (a special arrangement of words constituting style) is the soul of poetry, and analyzes guṇas, doṣas, and alaṅkāras as the constituents of kāvya.
SA_GK16_vamkalvu.history: Identical in authorship and content to the companion entry (work_id GK16vamkalsu); the two appear to be separate electronic encodings of Vāmana's Kāvyālaṅkārasūtravṛtti. Vāmana, a Kashmiri scholar of c. 800 CE, gave the rīti school its definitive formulation.

SA_GK16_vcamkc_u.title: Camatkāracandrikā
SA_GK16_vcamkc_u.author: Viśveśvara Kavicandra
SA_GK16_vcamkc_u.date_estimate: c. 16th-17th c. CE (disputed; not firmly fixed)
SA_GK16_vcamkc_u.summary: A late treatise on Sanskrit poetics whose title means 'Moonlight of Aesthetic Wonder (camatkāra).' It deals with the elements of literary aesthetics — rasa, the camatkāra (delight/wonder) experienced by the connoisseur, and the figures of speech (alaṅkāras) — in the tradition of the post-classical alaṅkāra manuals.
SA_GK16_vcamkc_u.history: Attributed to Viśveśvara Kavicandra; the work and its author are sparsely documented and should not be confused with the better-known Viśveśvara Paṇḍita (Gāgābhaṭṭa's circle, author of the Alaṅkārakaustubha and Rasacandrikā). The concept of camatkāra it centers on was elaborated by earlier theorists such as Abhinavagupta and Viśvanātha.

SA_GK16_viprud_u.title: Pratāparudrīya [Pratāparudrayaśobhūṣaṇa]
SA_GK16_viprud_u.author: Vidyānātha
SA_GK16_viprud_u.summary: A comprehensive manual of Sanskrit poetics and dramaturgy whose full title, Pratāparudrayaśobhūṣaṇa ('Ornament to the Fame of Pratāparudra'), honors its royal patron. Ingeniously, all of its illustrative verses themselves praise King Pratāparudra, so the work doubles as a panegyric. It systematically treats the hero, rasa, dramaturgy, doṣas, guṇas, śabdālaṅkāra and arthālaṅkāra, broadly within the dhvani-influenced tradition.
SA_GK16_viprud_u.history: Vidyānātha (also called Agastya) was a court poet of Pratāparudra II of the Kākatīya dynasty of Warangal (r. 1295-1323). The text became a standard teaching compendium of alaṅkāraśāstra and attracted commentaries, notably the Ratnāpaṇa of Kumārasvāmin.

SA_GK16_visa01cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 1
SA_GK16_visa01cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa01cu.genre: philosophical treatise (alaṅkāraśāstra / Sanskrit poetics), commentary (sūtra + vṛtti)
SA_GK16_visa01cu.summary: The opening chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa ('Mirror of Composition'). It introduces the work, defends poetry, and gives Viśvanātha's celebrated definition of kāvya as 'vākyaṃ rasātmakaṃ kāvyam' — a sentence whose soul is rasa — explicitly refuting Mammaṭa's definition. It thus lays the rasa-centered, dhvani-based foundation for the whole treatise.
SA_GK16_visa01cu.history: The Sāhityadarpaṇa, composed in sūtra-and-vṛtti form, is one of the most comprehensive works of Indian aesthetics and the first to combine in a single treatise both poetics (śravya) and dramaturgy (dṛśya). Its author Viśvanātha Kavirāja served the Eastern Ganga court in Kalinga (Odisha). P. V. Kane's edition (Pariccheda I-X) remains standard.

SA_GK16_visa02cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 2
SA_GK16_visa02cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa02cu.summary: The second chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, which analyzes the sentence (vākya) and the word (pada), and the three powers of words — denotation (abhidhā), indication (lakṣaṇā), and suggestion (vyañjanā). It also treats the levels of meaning, central to the dhvani theory of suggested sense.
SA_GK16_visa02cu.history: Part of Viśvanātha Kavirāja's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa; this chapter develops the semantic theory underpinning the work's commitment to dhvani (poetic suggestion) inherited from Ānandavardhana and Mammaṭa.

SA_GK16_visa03cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 3
SA_GK16_visa03cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa03cu.summary: The third and one of the most important chapters of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, devoted to rasa (aesthetic emotion). It expounds the doctrine of the eight (or nine) rasas, their underlying sthāyibhāvas, vibhāvas, anubhāvas and vyabhicāribhāvas, and the relishing of rasa as the essence of literature.
SA_GK16_visa03cu.history: Central chapter of Viśvanātha's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa, articulating the rasa theory that the author makes the very soul of poetry, in the lineage of the Nāṭyaśāstra, Abhinavagupta, and the dhvani school.

SA_GK16_visa04cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 4
SA_GK16_visa04cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa04cu.summary: The fourth chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, treating the varieties of poetry classified by the predominance of suggestion: dhvani (where the suggested sense dominates), guṇībhūtavyaṅgya (where it is subordinate), and citra (where it is absent). It thereby ranks kāvya according to the dhvani theory of poetic value.
SA_GK16_visa04cu.history: Part of Viśvanātha Kavirāja's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa, applying the threefold classification of poetry derived from Ānandavardhana's Dhvanyāloka and Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa.

SA_GK16_visa05cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 5
SA_GK16_visa05cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa05cu.summary: The fifth chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, which examines the operation of vyañjanā (suggestion) in detail — how suggested meaning arises from word and sense — and defends the independence of the suggestive function against rival theories that would reduce it to denotation or inference.
SA_GK16_visa05cu.history: A chapter of Viśvanātha's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa, elaborating the dhvani school's account of poetic suggestion and answering objections from the Mīmāṃsā and Naiyāyika analyses of meaning.

SA_GK16_visa06cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 6
SA_GK16_visa06cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa06cu.summary: The sixth and longest chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, devoted to dṛśya kāvya — drama and dramaturgy. It classifies the types of literature into visible (drama) and audible (poetry), then treats the ten major dramatic forms (rūpaka) and minor forms (uparūpaka), plot, hero and heroine types, dramatic junctures (sandhi), and stagecraft.
SA_GK16_visa06cu.history: This chapter makes the Sāhityadarpaṇa the first major poetics treatise to integrate a full dramaturgy alongside poetics; Viśvanātha draws heavily on the Nāṭyaśāstra and Dhanañjaya's Daśarūpaka.

SA_GK16_visa07cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 7
SA_GK16_visa07cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa07cu.summary: The seventh chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, treating the doṣas — the faults or blemishes of poetry. It catalogs defects of word, sentence, meaning, rasa and so on, explaining how each impairs the aesthetic effect of a literary work.
SA_GK16_visa07cu.history: Part of Viśvanātha Kavirāja's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa, following the standard alaṅkāraśāstra sequence (after rasa and drama come the faults, then the excellences and figures).

SA_GK16_visa08cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 8
SA_GK16_visa08cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa08cu.summary: The eighth chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, devoted to the guṇas — the qualities or excellences of poetry. It expounds the three traditional guṇas (mādhurya/sweetness, ojas/vigor, and prasāda/clarity) and their relation to the rasas and to diction.
SA_GK16_visa08cu.history: A chapter of Viśvanātha's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa; in line with the dhvani school it ties the guṇas to rasa as properties of the soul of poetry rather than mere ornaments.

SA_GK16_visa09cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 9
SA_GK16_visa09cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa09cu.summary: The ninth chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, dealing with rīti (style/diction) and related categories such as vṛtti and śabdālaṅkāra (sound figures, e.g. anuprāsa and yamaka). It describes the regional styles of composition and the verbal ornaments that adorn poetic language.
SA_GK16_visa09cu.history: Part of Viśvanātha Kavirāja's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa, incorporating the rīti doctrine of Vāmana into the broader rasa-dhvani framework of the treatise.

SA_GK16_visa10cu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 10
SA_GK16_visa10cu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visa10cu.summary: The tenth and final chapter of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, the largest treatment of arthālaṅkāra — the figures of speech based on meaning (simile, metaphor, and the many varieties such as upamā, rūpaka, utprekṣā, etc.). It defines and illustrates each figure with verse examples, concluding the work.
SA_GK16_visa10cu.history: The closing chapter of Viśvanātha's 14th-century Sāhityadarpaṇa; its systematic catalog of sense-figures with examples made it a standard reference for students of Sanskrit poetics.

SA_GK16_visah1.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa
SA_GK16_visah1.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visah1.summary: A complete-text witness of the Sāhityadarpaṇa ('Mirror of Composition'), a comprehensive ten-chapter (pariccheda) treatise on Sanskrit poetics and dramaturgy in sūtra-and-vṛtti form. It defines poetry as 'a sentence whose soul is rasa,' and covers semantics, rasa, the classification of poetry, drama, faults, qualities, styles, and the verbal and sense figures of speech.
SA_GK16_visah1.history: Composed by Viśvanātha Kavirāja, a minister at the Eastern Ganga court of Kalinga (Odisha) in the 14th century. It is celebrated as the first treatise to unite poetics and dramaturgy in one work and remains a standard textbook of the rasa-dhvani tradition.

SA_GK16_visah.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa (archive bad OCR)
SA_GK16_visah.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visah.summary: A digital witness of Viśvanātha Kavirāja's Sāhityadarpaṇa flagged as containing poor OCR. The underlying text is the standard ten-chapter treatise on Sanskrit poetics and dramaturgy that defines poetry as a sentence whose soul is rasa and surveys rasa, dhvani, drama, doṣas, guṇas, rīti and the alaṅkāras.
SA_GK16_visah.history: Same work and author as the other Sāhityadarpaṇa entries in this batch; this particular file is a lower-quality scanned/OCR version and should be used with caution for textual analysis.

SA_GK16_visvsdpu.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa
SA_GK16_visvsdpu.author: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja)
SA_GK16_visvsdpu.summary: Another complete-text encoding of the Sāhityadarpaṇa, Viśvanātha Kavirāja's comprehensive treatise on Sanskrit literary theory and dramaturgy in ten paricchedas. It defines kāvya as language whose essence is rasa and systematically expounds suggestion (dhvani), aesthetic emotion, drama, poetic faults and excellences, styles, and figures of speech.
SA_GK16_visvsdpu.history: A standard digital edition of the same 14th-century work as the other Sāhityadarpaṇa entries; the multiple work_ids in this batch represent distinct electronic witnesses of one treatise by Viśvanātha Kavirāja of Kalinga.

SA_GK17_chandovu.title: Chandoviciti
SA_GK17_chandovu.author: attributed to Patañjali (uncertain)
SA_GK17_chandovu.summary: A treatise on metre (chandas) titled 'Investigation of Metres,' dealing especially with Vedic metres as part of the Vedāṅga discipline of prosody. It examines the structure and varieties of metres used in sacred and poetic composition.
SA_GK17_chandovu.history: The attribution to Patañjali is uncertain (Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum lists it with a question mark), and the title 'Chandoviciti' is shared by several works — one is quoted by Daṇḍin in the Kāvyādarśa, another is ascribed to Janāśraya, and a related text survives among the Turfan fragments. The identity of this specific witness is therefore not firmly established.

SA_GK17_jvrtmspu.title: Vṛttamālāstuti
SA_GK17_jvrtmspu.author: Jñānaśrīmitra
SA_GK17_jvrtmspu.genre: stotra (hymn) combined with treatise on metrics (chandaśśāstra)
SA_GK17_jvrtmspu.date_estimate: first half of the 11th c. CE (c. 1000-1040)
SA_GK17_jvrtmspu.summary: A 'Garland of Metres' hymn (stuti) composed in about 150 different Sanskrit metres, each verse praising the Bodhisattva Mañjuśrī. With great ingenuity each stanza names its own metre as a natural part of the praise, while also illustrating that metre's syllabic pattern, so the work simultaneously functions as a devotional hymn and a textbook of prosody.
SA_GK17_jvrtmspu.history: Composed by Jñānaśrīmitra (fl. c. 975-1040), a Buddhist epistemologist and dvāra-paṇḍita ('gate-scholar') of Vikramaśīla university who was also an accomplished poet. The text was transmitted with a commentary, the Vṛttamālā(stuti)vivṛti, by Śākyarakṣita, and has been critically edited by Michael Hahn.

SA_GK17_kedvratu.title: Vṛttaratnākara
SA_GK17_kedvratu.author: Kedārabhaṭṭa
SA_GK17_kedvratu.summary: The most popular handbook of classical Sanskrit prosody, the 'Mine of Jewels of Metres.' Arranged in six chapters with about 136 verses, it systematizes the gaṇa (syllabic-foot) notation and defines and illustrates the principal varṇavṛtta (syllabic) and other metres, with each definition typically embodying the metre it describes.
SA_GK17_kedvratu.history: Composed by Kedārabhaṭṭa (c. 950-1050 CE), it summarizes and makes more accessible the older Chandaḥsūtra of Piṅgala. Its great popularity is shown by the more than sixty commentaries written on it across the centuries, making it the standard teaching text of Sanskrit metrics.

SA_GK17_kvrtrsuu.title: Vṛttaratnākara
SA_GK17_kvrtrsuu.author: Kedārabhaṭṭa
SA_GK17_kvrtrsuu.summary: A concise verse manual of Sanskrit prosody (chandaḥśāstra) in six chapters totaling about 136 verses. It systematically defines the gaṇas (metrical feet), the rules for scansion, and catalogues the principal syllabic (varṇavṛtta) and moraic (mātrāvṛtta) meters, with each meter typically illustrated by a self-exemplifying verse.
SA_GK17_kvrtrsuu.history: Composed by Kedārabhaṭṭa, traditionally dated to roughly 950-1050 CE. It became the single most popular handbook of Sanskrit metrics, attracting more than sixty commentaries across the centuries and remaining a standard pedagogical text into the modern era.

SA_GK18_abhidhau.title: Abhinaya-Darpaṇa
SA_GK18_abhidhau.author: Nandikeśvara
SA_GK18_abhidhau.date_estimate: uncertain; commonly placed c. 10th-13th c. CE (some traditions claim much greater antiquity)
SA_GK18_abhidhau.summary: The 'Mirror of Gesture,' a manual of abhinaya (expressive gesture) for classical Indian dance. It codifies hand gestures (hastas/mudrās), movements of the head, eyes, neck, and feet, and the conventions by which a dancer conveys meaning and emotion in performance.
SA_GK18_abhidhau.history: Attributed to a Nandikeśvara whose identity and date are much debated; scholars commonly assign the surviving text to the medieval period (around or after 1000 CE), though some traditional accounts claim great antiquity. It was made widely known in the West through A. K. Coomaraswamy's translation 'The Mirror of Gesture' and remains a foundational reference for Bharatanatyam and other dance forms.

SA_GK18_bharnatu.title: Nāṭyaśāstra
SA_GK18_bharnatu.author: Bharata (Bharatamuni; traditional/composite attribution)
SA_GK18_bharnatu.genre: śāstra (foundational treatise on drama, dance, music, and aesthetics)
SA_GK18_bharnatu.date_estimate: composite; c. 200 BCE-200/400 CE (core layers earlier, expanded over centuries)
SA_GK18_bharnatu.summary: The encyclopedic foundational treatise on the performing arts, traditionally in 36 chapters. It covers the origin of drama, theatre architecture, types of plays (rūpaka), acting and gesture (abhinaya), prosody and language, music and instruments, costume and makeup, and above all the theory of rasa (aesthetic sentiment) and bhāva (emotional states).
SA_GK18_bharnatu.history: Ascribed to the legendary sage Bharata but in fact a composite work accreted over several centuries (roughly 2nd c. BCE to 4th c. CE). It is the fountainhead of Indian aesthetic theory; its rasa doctrine was elaborated by later theorists, most influentially in Abhinavagupta's commentary, the Abhinavabhāratī.

SA_GK18_dhandasu.title: Daśarūpaka
SA_GK18_dhandasu.author: Dhanañjaya
SA_GK18_dhandasu.summary: The 'Ten Forms of Drama,' a concise treatise in four chapters (prakāśas) on Sanskrit dramaturgy. It distills the dramaturgical doctrine of the Nāṭyaśāstra, treating the nature of drama, the ten principal types of play (rūpaka), the characteristics of the hero (nāyaka) and heroine, plot structure, and the theory of rasa.
SA_GK18_dhandasu.history: Written by Dhanañjaya, son of Viṣṇu, a court poet of the Paramāra king Vākpatirāja II (Muñja) of Mālava (r. c. 974-995). It is regarded as the most important systematic work on dramaturgy after the Nāṭyaśāstra and is usually transmitted with the explanatory Avaloka commentary of Dhanika.

SA_GK19_aghatkvu.title: Ghaṭakarparakāvya
SA_GK19_aghatkvu.author: Ghaṭakarpara (traditionally ascribed to Kālidāsa); NOTE: the project hint 'Abhinavagupta' refers to the commentator, not the poet
SA_GK19_aghatkvu.date_estimate: uncertain; poem perhaps c. 4th-7th c. CE; Abhinavagupta's commentary c. 1000 CE
SA_GK19_aghatkvu.summary: A short poem of about twenty-two stanzas in which a husband, separated during the rains, sends a message of love and longing to his recently married wife. Its distinctive feature is the virtuosic use of yamaka (sound-repetition/paronomasia), every verse displaying chiming wordplay.
SA_GK19_aghatkvu.history: Traditionally attributed to Kālidāsa and counted among works of the 'nine gems,' though the attribution is doubtful; the poet's sobriquet 'Ghaṭakarpara' ('broken pot') derives from a legendary challenge over mastery of yamaka. The Kashmir Shaivite polymath Abhinavagupta (fl. c. 1000) wrote a learned commentary on it (the Ghaṭakarpara-kulaka-vivṛti), which is the basis for the project's mistaken author hint.

SA_GK19_akadambu.title: Kādambarīkathāsāra
SA_GK19_akadambu.author: Abhinanda
SA_GK19_akadambu.summary: A metrical verse condensation ('essence of the story of the Kādambarī') that retells in Sanskrit poetry the celebrated prose romance Kādambarī of Bāṇa. It compresses Bāṇa's intricate, ornate narrative of intertwined lovers and rebirths into a more accessible poem.
SA_GK19_akadambu.history: Composed by Abhinanda, son of Jayantabhaṭṭa (the Nyāya philosopher) and a court poet associated with Kashmir, working in the 9th century. The work is studied as an example of the developing Kashmiri poetic style and as evidence for the reception of Bāṇa's prose masterpiece.

SA_GK19_amaru_u.title: Amaruśataka
SA_GK19_amaru_u.author: Amaru (Amaruka)
SA_GK19_amaru_u.date_estimate: c. 7th-8th c. CE (disputed; sometimes placed earlier)
SA_GK19_amaru_u.summary: A celebrated anthology of about one hundred independent stanzas on śṛṅgāra (love), each verse a self-contained vignette capturing a moment of erotic emotion—lovers' quarrels, jealousy, union, separation, and reconciliation. It is prized for psychological subtlety and suggestion (dhvani) achieved in a single verse.
SA_GK19_amaru_u.history: Attributed to the otherwise shadowy poet Amaru; legend even links the collection to Śaṅkara. The text circulated in several regional recensions with differing verses and orderings, and its individual stanzas are frequently cited by later poeticians as model illustrations of erotic rasa.

SA_GK19_anvallcu.title: Vallālacarita
SA_GK19_anvallcu.author: Ānandabhaṭṭa
SA_GK19_anvallcu.date_estimate: c. 16th c. CE (1510 CE per the text's own statement)
SA_GK19_anvallcu.summary: A Sanskrit account of the deeds of Vallālasena (Ballāl Sena), the 12th-century Sena king of Bengal. It narrates his reign and is a key source for the legends of social organization in Bengal, especially the institution of Kulīnism among Brahmins and Kāyasthas.
SA_GK19_anvallcu.history: Composed by Ānandabhaṭṭa, a Bengali Śaivite/Vedānta commentator, reportedly at the request of Buddhimanta Khan, Raja of Nabadwip, in the early 16th century. Written long after the events it describes, it mixes history and legend; it was edited and translated by Haraprasad Sastri (Calcutta, 1901) and is much used in studies of Bengali social history.

SA_GK19_appvairu.title: Vairāgyaśataka
SA_GK19_appvairu.author: Appayya Dīkṣita
SA_GK19_appvairu.genre: stotra / nīti-vairāgya śataka (century of verses on renunciation)
SA_GK19_appvairu.summary: A 'century of verses on dispassion' meditating on the transience of worldly pleasures, the futility of desire and wealth, and the value of detachment and devotion as the path to spiritual peace. It belongs to the devotional-renunciatory poetic tradition exemplified by Bhartṛhari's Vairāgyaśataka.
SA_GK19_appvairu.history: Authored by the prolific South Indian polymath Appayya Dīkṣita (1520-1593), famed for some hundred works across Vedānta, Mīmāṃsā, poetics, and Śaiva theology. The title Vairāgyaśataka is shared with the more famous work of Bhartṛhari and with another by Appayya's grand-nephew Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita, so attribution must be specified by author.

SA_GK19_asvbuc2u.title: Buddhacarita
SA_GK19_asvbuc2u.author: Aśvaghoṣa
SA_GK19_asvbuc2u.summary: An ornate epic poem (mahākāvya) narrating the life of the Buddha, from his birth and youth in luxury, through the encounters that prompt his renunciation, his ascetic struggle and enlightenment, to his teaching and (in the full work) parinirvāṇa. It is the earliest surviving complete Sanskrit kāvya and combines Buddhist doctrine with refined poetic art.
SA_GK19_asvbuc2u.history: Composed by Aśvaghoṣa, a Brahmin convert to Buddhism active around the time of Kaniṣka (1st-2nd c. CE). Only the first thirteen-plus cantos survive in Sanskrit; the complete work is preserved in Tibetan and Chinese translations. It is foundational both for the kāvya tradition and for Buddhist literature.

SA_GK19_asvsau2u.title: Saundarananda
SA_GK19_asvsau2u.author: Aśvaghoṣa
SA_GK19_asvsau2u.summary: An epic poem ('Handsome Nanda') recounting the conversion of the Buddha's half-brother Nanda, who is torn from his beautiful wife Sundarī and reluctantly made a monk. Through teaching and a vision of heavenly nymphs and their impermanence, Nanda overcomes his attachment and attains arhatship; the poem thus dramatizes the path from sensual love to liberation.
SA_GK19_asvsau2u.history: Composed by Aśvaghoṣa (1st-2nd c. CE), the companion piece to his Buddhacarita and, unlike that work, fully preserved in Sanskrit in eighteen cantos. It blends polished kāvya artistry with explicit exposition of Buddhist meditative and doctrinal teaching.

SA_GK19_bahcar2u.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 2
SA_GK19_bahcar2u.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar2u.genre: ākhyāyikā / gadyakāvya (prose biography in ornate prose)
SA_GK19_bahcar2u.summary: The second chapter (ucchvāsa) of Bāṇa's prose biography of Emperor Harṣa. In the opening ucchvāsas Bāṇa narrates his own family background, lineage, and early life, framing the work autobiographically before turning to the emperor; ucchvāsa 2 forms part of this introductory account of the poet's ancestry and personal history.
SA_GK19_bahcar2u.history: The Harṣacarita, written c. 640 CE by Bāṇabhaṭṭa, court poet of Harṣavardhana of Kanauj, is the first historical biography (ākhyāyikā) in Sanskrit and a masterpiece of ornate prose. It survives in eight ucchvāsas plus supplementary matter and is a major source for 7th-century North Indian history.

SA_GK19_bahcar3u.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 3
SA_GK19_bahcar3u.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar3u.genre: ākhyāyikā / gadyakāvya (prose biography in ornate prose)
SA_GK19_bahcar3u.summary: The third chapter of Bāṇa's prose biography of Harṣa. With this ucchvāsa the narrative moves from the poet's own background toward the dynastic story, introducing the Puṣyabhūti (Vardhana) line of Sthāṇvīśvara and the legendary founder-king Puṣyabhūti, thus setting the stage for Harṣa's family history.
SA_GK19_bahcar3u.history: Part of the Harṣacarita, written c. 640 CE by Bāṇabhaṭṭa, court poet of Harṣa, the first historical biography in Sanskrit and a celebrated exemplar of ornate gadya (prose) style, transmitted in eight ucchvāsas.

SA_GK19_bahcar4u.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 4
SA_GK19_bahcar4u.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar4u.genre: ākhyāyikā / gadyakāvya (prose biography in ornate prose)
SA_GK19_bahcar4u.summary: The fourth chapter of Bāṇa's biography of Harṣa, continuing the account of the Puṣyabhūti dynasty. It develops the story of Harṣa's immediate forebears—his father Prabhākaravardhana and the royal household—amid the ornate descriptive prose for which Bāṇa is famed.
SA_GK19_bahcar4u.history: A section of the Harṣacarita, composed c. 640 CE by Bāṇabhaṭṭa for Emperor Harṣa; the work pioneered historical biography in Sanskrit and is a model of the high gadyakāvya style, preserved in eight ucchvāsas.

SA_GK19_bahcar5_pu.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 5
SA_GK19_bahcar5_pu.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar5_pu.genre: ākhyāyikā / gadyakāvya (prose biography in ornate prose)
SA_GK19_bahcar5_pu.summary: The fifth chapter of Bāṇa's biography of Harṣa. It carries the dynastic narrative into the crisis of Harṣa's accession—the illness and death of his father Prabhākaravardhana and the deepening misfortunes of the royal family that lead toward Harṣa's rise to power.
SA_GK19_bahcar5_pu.history: A section of the Harṣacarita, written c. 640 CE by Bāṇabhaṭṭa, court poet of Harṣa of Kanauj; the earliest Sanskrit historical biography and a celebrated exemplar of ornate prose, transmitted in eight ucchvāsas.

SA_GK19_bahcar6u.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 6
SA_GK19_bahcar6u.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar6u.genre: ākhyāyikā / gadyakāvya (prose biography in ornate prose)
SA_GK19_bahcar6u.summary: The sixth chapter of Bāṇa's biography of Harṣa. It treats the tumultuous events surrounding the murder of Harṣa's elder brother Rājyavardhana and the abduction of their sister Rājyaśrī, the calamities that propel the young Harṣa to assume kingship and vow vengeance.
SA_GK19_bahcar6u.history: A section of the Harṣacarita, composed c. 640 CE by Bāṇabhaṭṭa, court poet of Harṣa; the work is the first historical biography in Sanskrit and a high point of ornate prose, preserved in eight ucchvāsas.

SA_GK19_bahcar7u.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 7
SA_GK19_bahcar7u.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar7u.genre: ākhyāyikā / gadyakāvya (prose biography in ornate prose)
SA_GK19_bahcar7u.summary: The seventh chapter of Bāṇa's biography of Harṣa. It describes Harṣa's military campaign and march in search of his lost sister Rājyaśrī, including the famous extended description of his army and camp, as the king pursues his enemies through the forests of the Vindhyas.
SA_GK19_bahcar7u.history: A section of the Harṣacarita, written c. 640 CE by Bāṇabhaṭṭa for Emperor Harṣa; celebrated as the first Sanskrit historical biography and a masterwork of ornate descriptive prose, transmitted in eight ucchvāsas.

SA_GK19_bahcar8u.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 8
SA_GK19_bahcar8u.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_bahcar8u.genre: ākhyāyikā / gadyakāvya (prose biography in ornate prose)
SA_GK19_bahcar8u.summary: The eighth and final extant chapter of Bāṇa's biography of Harṣa. It brings the narrative to its climax with Harṣa's reunion with his sister Rājyaśrī—rescued as she is about to immolate herself in the Vindhya forest—and includes the celebrated description of the ascetic Divākaramitra's woodland hermitage. The work breaks off without a formal conclusion.
SA_GK19_bahcar8u.history: The closing surviving ucchvāsa of the Harṣacarita, composed c. 640 CE by Bāṇabhaṭṭa; the unfinished ending of this first Sanskrit historical biography has long been noted by scholars. It remains a key source for 7th-century North Indian history and society.

SA_GK19_bahcarpu.title: Harṣacarita: Pariśiṣṭa
SA_GK19_bahcarpu.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa); supplementary/appended matter
SA_GK19_bahcarpu.genre: appendix / supplement to a prose biography
SA_GK19_bahcarpu.date_estimate: associated with the Harṣacarita, c. 640 CE; appended material possibly later
SA_GK19_bahcarpu.summary: The 'pariśiṣṭa' (supplement/appendix) attached to the Harṣacarita in the manuscript and editorial tradition. It comprises supplementary or concluding matter associated with Bāṇa's unfinished prose biography of Harṣa rather than a numbered narrative ucchvāsa.
SA_GK19_bahcarpu.history: Bāṇa's Harṣacarita famously ends abruptly in the eighth ucchvāsa; editions sometimes group additional verses or supplementary matter as a pariśiṣṭa. This entry corresponds to that appended portion of the work transmitted alongside the main text.

SA_GK19_bankad1u.title: Kādambarī
SA_GK19_bankad1u.author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa); completed by his son Bhūṣaṇabhaṭṭa (Pulinda)
SA_GK19_bankad1u.summary: One of the supreme prose romances of Sanskrit literature, an elaborate fantastical love story spanning multiple intertwined narratives and successive rebirths, centered on the princess Kādambarī and the prince Candrāpīḍa, with parallel lovers Mahāśvetā and Puṇḍarīka. It is renowned for its immense, intricately ornamented sentences, lush description, and emotional depth.
SA_GK19_bankad1u.history: Begun by Bāṇabhaṭṭa, court poet of Harṣa (7th c.), and left unfinished at his death; it was completed by his son Bhūṣaṇabhaṭṭa. The 'Kādambarī' became a byword for ornate Sanskrit prose (the word kādambarī even came to denote 'a novel' in later Indian languages) and is a cornerstone of the kathā/gadyakāvya tradition.

SA_GK19_bhago.title: Bhaja-Govindam
SA_GK19_bhago.author: Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya), traditionally attributed
SA_GK19_bhago.date_estimate: traditionally c. 8th c. CE (attribution disputed; some verses likely later accretions)
SA_GK19_bhago.summary: A short Sanskrit devotional hymn, also called Mohamudgara ('hammer that destroys delusion'), urging the listener to worship Govinda (Kṛṣṇa/Viṣṇu) and abandon attachment to worldly wealth, pride in scholarship, and sensual desire in light of the inevitability of death. Its refrain 'bhaja govindaṃ' frames a meditation on impermanence and the priority of devotion and self-knowledge over mere grammatical or material pursuits.
SA_GK19_bhago.history: Traditionally ascribed to Ādi Śaṅkara, said to have been improvised in Varanasi on seeing an old man memorizing grammar; the so-called Dvādaśamañjarikā core is attributed to Śaṅkara with additional verses attributed to his disciples (Caturdaśamañjarikā), yielding a transmitted text of around 31-33 verses. It is one of the most popular short devotional poems in the Advaita tradition, frequently set to music and commented upon.

SA_GK19_bhakirpu.title: Kirātārjunīya
SA_GK19_bhakirpu.author: Bhāravi
SA_GK19_bhakirpu.date_estimate: c. 6th c. CE (Bhāravi fl. c. 550-600 CE)
SA_GK19_bhakirpu.summary: A mahākāvya of eighteen cantos elaborating an episode from the Mahabharata's Vanaparvan: the ascetic Arjuna's penance in the Himalayas, his confrontation and combat with Śiva disguised as a kirāta (mountain hunter) over a slain boar, and his eventual recognition of the god, who rewards him with the Pāśupata weapon. The poem is celebrated for its dense, ornate style, structural virtuosity, and emphasis on heroic and political (nīti) themes.
SA_GK19_bhakirpu.history: Bhāravi is one of the canonical Sanskrit mahakavi, often paired with Kalidasa and Magha; his floruit is fixed by the Aihole inscription and other epigraphic references. The Kiratarjuniya attracted more than forty commentaries (notably by Mallinatha) and is frequently quoted in works on poetics and anthologies, with canto 8 verse 37 especially admired.

SA_GK19_bhicau.title: Bhiṣma-Cāritra
SA_GK19_bhicau.author: Dr. Hari Narayan Dikshit (Hari Nārāyaṇa Dīkṣita)
SA_GK19_bhicau.summary: A modern Sanskrit mahākāvya in twenty cantos (roughly 1,118 verses) narrating the life of Devavrata Bhīṣma of the Mahabharata, from his birth as son of Śāntanu and Gaṅgā through his vow of celibacy, his role in the Kuru dynasty, his valor and wisdom, and his death. The poet employs about eleven classical metres in an exhibition of traditional poetic craft applied to a familiar epic hero.
SA_GK19_bhicau.history: A work of contemporary (twentieth-century) Sanskrit composition by Dr. Hari Narayan Dikshit, completed in February 1991; it exemplifies the continuing tradition of original Sanskrit kavya and biographical epic (caritakavya) in modern India and has been the subject of critical study.

SA_GK19_bicauppu.title: Caurapañcāśikā
SA_GK19_bicauppu.author: Bilhaṇa
SA_GK19_bicauppu.summary: A famous Sanskrit lyric poem of about fifty stanzas (also called Caurisuratapañcāśikā), each conventionally beginning 'adyāpi' ('even now'), in which the speaker recalls in passionate, sensuous detail his secret love affair with a princess. Each verse is a vivid erotic-memory tableau of the beloved's beauty and their union.
SA_GK19_bicauppu.history: Attributed to the Kashmiri poet Bilhaṇa (11th c.), author of the Vikramāṅkadevacarita; legend holds he composed the verses while imprisoned and awaiting sentence for a clandestine affair with a king's daughter. The poem circulated in several recensions (notably a Southern/Kashmiri and a Western recension) and became internationally famous through translations such as Barbara Stoler Miller's and E. Powys Mathers's 'Black Marigolds.'

SA_GK19_damkuttu.title: Kuṭṭanīmata
SA_GK19_damkuttu.author: Dāmodaragupta
SA_GK19_damkuttu.date_estimate: c. late 8th c. CE (reign of Jayāpīḍa of Kashmir, 779-813 CE)
SA_GK19_damkuttu.summary: An erotic-comic-didactic poem of over a thousand stanzas, mostly in the Āryā metre, framed as 'the bawd's counsel': an experienced procuress (kuṭṭanī) instructs a young courtesan in the arts and tricks of the trade, embedding a vivid satirical portrait of urban pleasure-culture in early medieval Kashmir. It blends erotic, comic, satiric, and instructional registers.
SA_GK19_damkuttu.history: Composed by Dāmodaragupta, a minister of the Kashmiri king Jayāpīḍa, making it the earliest securely datable Sanskrit work of the erotico-satiric genre; it anticipates and influences later Kashmiri satire such as Kṣemendra's Samayamātṛkā. It is an important social-historical source for the period.

SA_GK19_danddk1u.title: Daśakumāracarita: Ucchvāsas 3, 5 & 6
SA_GK19_danddk1u.author: Daṇḍin
SA_GK19_danddk1u.date_estimate: c. late 7th-8th c. CE (Daṇḍin fl. c. 7th c.)
