SA_GK19_danddk1u.summary: Selected chapters (ucchvāsas) from Daṇḍin's prose romance 'The Tale of the Ten Princes,' which narrates the picaresque adventures, separations, and reunions of ten young princes who recount their experiences of love, intrigue, gambling, theft, and statecraft across the cities of India. These ucchvāsas are individual princes' first-person narratives within the larger frame, showcasing Daṇḍin's lively, worldly storytelling and ornate prose.
SA_GK19_danddk1u.history: Daṇḍin, also author of the poetics treatise Kāvyādarśa, is one of the great masters of Sanskrit prose. The Daśakumāracarita survives in an incomplete and textually complex state, with a Pūrvapīṭhikā and Uttarapīṭhikā of disputed authorship supplying its missing frame; the central ucchvāsas (here 3, 5, 6) form the well-attested core.

SA_GK19_dhoypavu.title: Pavanadūta
SA_GK19_dhoypavu.author: Dhoyī (Dhoyin)
SA_GK19_dhoypavu.date_estimate: c. late 12th c. CE (court of Lakṣmaṇasena of Bengal)
SA_GK19_dhoypavu.summary: A messenger poem of about 104 stanzas in the mandākrāntā metre in which Kuvalayavatī, a gandharva maiden from the south who has fallen in love with King Lakṣmaṇasena during his world-conquest tour, sends the south wind (pavana) as her messenger to carry her longing to him at his capital. Modeled closely on Kalidasa's Meghadūta, it describes the route and landscapes and culminates in the lover's plea.
SA_GK19_dhoypavu.history: Composed by Dhoyī, one of the 'five gems' of poets at the court of the Sena king Lakṣmaṇasena (alongside Jayadeva and Govardhana); it is probably the earliest surviving imitation of the Meghadūta and inaugurated a long line of more than thirty-five later dūtakāvyas. A key witness to the twelfth-century Sanskrit literary 'renaissance' in Bengal.

SA_GK19_gakou.title: Gāhākoṣa
SA_GK19_gakou.author: Hāla (Sātavāhana), traditional compiler
SA_GK19_gakou.date_estimate: core c. 1st-2nd c. CE; expanded/redacted over later centuries (Prakrit, not Sanskrit)
SA_GK19_gakou.summary: The Gāhākoṣa (Sanskrit Gāthākośa/Gāthāsaptaśatī, 'Treasury of Verses' or 'Seven Hundred Verses') is an anthology of roughly seven hundred single-stanza lyric poems (gāthās) in Māhārāṣṭrī Prakrit, mostly in the Āryā metre, traditionally attributed to or compiled by the Sātavāhana king Hāla. The verses, drawn from many poets, vividly evoke rural Deccan life, nature, and erotic love, frequently voiced from a woman's perspective with suggestive, allusive imagery.
SA_GK19_gakou.history: The oldest surviving Prakrit lyric anthology, its core is dated to the early centuries CE, though the collection grew through later recensions and accreted commentaries; the project metadata's 1200-1300 CE hint is incorrect for the work's composition. It deeply influenced later Sanskrit erotic muktaka poetry, including Govardhana's Āryāsaptaśatī, which was modeled on it.

SA_GK19_ghatgu_u.title: Gūḍhārthadīpikā
SA_GK19_ghatgu_u.author: Kuśalamiśra (attribution per project hint; uncertain)
SA_GK19_ghatgu_u.summary: A commentary (the title Gūḍhārthadīpikā means 'lamp on the hidden meaning'). The specific work by Kuśalamiśra could not be reliably identified; the title is shared by several distinct Sanskrit commentaries (e.g., Madhusūdana Sarasvatī's commentary on the Bhagavadgītā, a Vedānta commentary, a dharma text by Vāmadeva, and a commentary on the medical Śārṅgadharasaṃhitā), so the base text being commented on is unclear for this attribution.
SA_GK19_ghatgu_u.history: The author Kuśalamiśra is not prominently attested for a work of this title in standard reference catalogues consulted, and the identification remains uncertain; the title 'Gūḍhārthadīpikā' was a common one applied to commentaries across genres.

SA_GK19_govass_u.title: Āryāsaptaśatī
SA_GK19_govass_u.author: Govardhana (Govardhanācārya)
SA_GK19_govass_u.date_estimate: c. late 12th-early 13th c. CE (court of Lakṣmaṇasena of Bengal)
SA_GK19_govass_u.summary: A collection of about seven hundred single-stanza Sanskrit poems (muktakas) in the Āryā metre, predominantly on erotic (śṛṅgāra) themes, prized for its compressed, suggestive, intellectually dense style. It self-consciously transposes into refined Sanskrit the tradition of Hāla's earlier Prakrit Sattasaī (Gāhākoṣa).
SA_GK19_govass_u.history: Composed by Govardhana, a poet at the court of the Sena king Lakṣmaṇasena and a contemporary of Jayadeva and Dhoyī; it is a flagship of the late twelfth-century Bengali Sanskrit literary efflorescence and was admired as a virtuoso 'antigravity' poetics. The project's 1000-1299 CE hint is loose; a date around c. 1200 CE is more precise.

SA_GK19_gp01-11u.title: Gopālacampū: Pūrvacampū, 1-11
SA_GK19_gp01-11u.author: Jīva Gosvāmin
SA_GK19_gp01-11u.summary: The opening eleven chapters (pūraṇas/sandarbhas) of the Pūrva-campū, the first part of Jīva Gosvāmin's theological campū on the life of Kṛṣṇa. The Pūrvacampū (33 chapters total) narrates Kṛṣṇa's activities in Vraja/Vrindavana, weaving together ornate prose and verse to present his pastimes within a Gaudiya Vaishnava devotional and philosophical framework.
SA_GK19_gp01-11u.history: Jīva Gosvāmin, one of the Six Gosvāmins of Vrindavana and author of the Ṣaṭ Sandarbhas, composed the Gopālacampū in the sixteenth century as a literary-theological masterwork of the Gaudiya tradition founded in the wake of Caitanya. The campū form (alternating prose and verse) is used to dramatize and theologize Kṛṣṇa's līlā.

SA_GK19_gp12-22u.title: Gopālacampū: Pūrvacampū, 12-22
SA_GK19_gp12-22u.author: Jīva Gosvāmin
SA_GK19_gp12-22u.summary: Chapters 12-22 of the Pūrva-campū of Jīva Gosvāmin's Gopālacampū, continuing the elaborate prose-and-verse narration of Kṛṣṇa's childhood and youthful pastimes in Vraja. This middle portion develops his līlā in Vrindavana within the work's Gaudiya Vaishnava devotional theology.
SA_GK19_gp12-22u.history: Part of the same sixteenth-century campū by Jīva Gosvāmin, one of the Six Gosvāmins of Vrindavana; the Pūrvacampū comprises 33 chapters describing Kṛṣṇa in Vraja before the Uttaracampū's account of his later life and return.

SA_GK19_gp23-33u.title: Gopālacampū: Pūrvacampū, 23-33
SA_GK19_gp23-33u.author: Jīva Gosvāmin
SA_GK19_gp23-33u.summary: The concluding chapters (23-33) of the Pūrva-campū of Jīva Gosvāmin's Gopālacampū, completing the first part's account of Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Vraja. These chapters bring the Vrindavana līlā to its close before the transition to the Uttara-campū, all rendered in the campū's characteristic alternation of ornate prose and verse.
SA_GK19_gp23-33u.history: Belongs to the same sixteenth-century Gaudiya Vaishnava campū by Jīva Gosvāmin, completing the 33-chapter Pūrvacampū. The work as a whole is an intensely theological poetic synthesis of Kṛṣṇa-bhakti grounded in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa and Jīva's own Sandarbhas.

SA_GK19_gu01-03u.title: Gopālacampū: Uttaracampū, 1-3
SA_GK19_gu01-03u.author: Jīva Gosvāmin
SA_GK19_gu01-03u.summary: The opening three chapters of the Uttara-campū, the second part (37 chapters total) of Jīva Gosvāmin's Gopālacampū. The Uttaracampū narrates Kṛṣṇa's later pastimes, including his departure from and longed-for return to Vraja, again in alternating prose and verse, with strong devotional and consolatory theological emphasis.
SA_GK19_gu01-03u.history: The second part of Jīva Gosvāmin's sixteenth-century campū; the Uttaracampū is structured to console the residents of Vraja over Kṛṣṇa's absence and affirm his eternal presence, reflecting core Gaudiya Vaishnava theology of līlā and prema.

SA_GK19_gu05-06u.title: Gopālacampū: Uttaracampū, 5-6
SA_GK19_gu05-06u.author: Jīva Gosvāmin
SA_GK19_gu05-06u.summary: Chapters 5-6 of the Uttara-campū of Jīva Gosvāmin's Gopālacampū, continuing the prose-and-verse narration of Kṛṣṇa's later pastimes and the Vraja residents' relationship to him after his departure. Part of the 37-chapter second division of the work.
SA_GK19_gu05-06u.history: A section of Jīva Gosvāmin's sixteenth-century Gopālacampū, the magnum opus campū of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition by one of the Six Gosvāmins of Vrindavana.

SA_GK19_gu29___u.title: Gopālacampū: Uttaracampū, 29
SA_GK19_gu29___u.author: Jīva Gosvāmin
SA_GK19_gu29___u.summary: Chapter 29 of the Uttara-campū of Jīva Gosvāmin's Gopālacampū, a single later chapter within the 37-chapter second part of the work that narrates Kṛṣṇa's mature pastimes in the campū's alternating prose-and-verse style with Gaudiya Vaishnava theological framing.
SA_GK19_gu29___u.history: Part of Jīva Gosvāmin's sixteenth-century Gopālacampū, the culminating literary-theological work of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition founded by the followers of Caitanya.

SA_GK19_jagpranu.title: Prāṇābharaṇa
SA_GK19_jagpranu.author: Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja
SA_GK19_jagpranu.summary: A kāvya (Prāṇābharaṇa) by Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja, the eminent seventeenth-century poet and literary theorist. It is one of his lesser-known poetic compositions; as with several of his occasional works, it is a panegyric/ornamental court poem reflecting his patronage connections and his celebrated mastery of Sanskrit poetic diction.
SA_GK19_jagpranu.history: Jagannātha (c. 1590-1670), titled 'Paṇḍitarāja' reportedly by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan at whose court he was patronized, is best known for the poetics treatise Rasagaṅgādhara and the anthology Bhāminīvilāsa; the Prāṇābharaṇa appears in catalogue lists of his works (Aufrecht) as a kāvya.

SA_GK19_jagsudhu.title: Sudhālaharī
SA_GK19_jagsudhu.author: Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja
SA_GK19_jagsudhu.summary: A short devotional 'laharī' ('wave/flow') hymn by Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja, in the genre of cascading praise-poems for which he is well known (e.g., his Gaṅgālaharī/Pīyūṣalaharī and others). The Sudhālaharī ('Wave of Nectar') is a lyrical stotra blending bhakti sentiment with his characteristic polished poetic style.
SA_GK19_jagsudhu.history: Listed among Jagannātha's works in Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum alongside his other laharī hymns; Jagannātha, the seventeenth-century 'Paṇḍitarāja,' is celebrated both as a poet (Bhāminīvilāsa) and as the author of the poetics treatise Rasagaṅgādhara.

SA_GK19_jalmugpu.title: Mugdhopadeśa
SA_GK19_jalmugpu.author: Jalhaṇa
SA_GK19_jalmugpu.summary: A short Sanskrit kāvya titled 'Instruction of the Naive (or Deluded) One' (Mugdhopadeśa), in the tradition of didactic-satirical poems warning a guileless young man against the wiles of courtesans and the dangers of infatuation. It belongs to the same erotic-cautionary milieu as works like the Kuṭṭanīmata and Samayamātṛkā.
SA_GK19_jalmugpu.history: Attributed to Jalhaṇa and printed in the Kāvyamālā series (part VIII). The author Jalhaṇa is generally associated with the poet of the same name (likely the Kashmiri/medieval Jalhaṇa, distinct from the anthologist Jalhaṇa of the Sūktimuktāvalī); details of date and biography are not firmly established here.

SA_GK19_japa.title: Jānakī Pariṇaya
SA_GK19_japa.author: Rāmabhadra Dīkṣita
SA_GK19_japa.summary: A Sanskrit nāṭaka ('The Marriage of Jānakī/Sītā') dramatizing the Rāmāyaṇa episode of Rāma winning Sītā, including the breaking of Śiva's bow at Janaka's court and the wedding. The play treats the well-loved marriage narrative in classical dramatic form with ornate poetic embellishment.
SA_GK19_japa.history: Composed by Rāmabhadra Dīkṣita (also called Cokkanātha), a Tamil-region Sanskrit scholar of the Kauṇḍinya family in the Tanjore area, associated with the circle of Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita and active in the late seventeenth to early eighteenth century. (Note: other dramatists also wrote plays of this title; this entry follows the project's and catalogues' attribution to Rāmabhadra Dīkṣita.)

SA_GK19_jaravau.title: Jarāsandhavadha
SA_GK19_jaravau.author: Goswami Hariraiji (Harirāi)
SA_GK19_jaravau.summary: A Sanskrit mahākāvya narrating the slaying of Jarāsandha, the powerful king of Magadha, by Bhīma at Kṛṣṇa's instigation. The narrative draws on the Mahābhārata, Harivaṃśa and Bhāgavatapurāṇa, and is structured into cantos that culminate in Jarāsandha's death and the liberation of the kings he had imprisoned. Its moral thrust is the maxim that 'where there is righteousness (dharma), there is victory.'
SA_GK19_jaravau.history: Composed by Goswami Hariraiji, a modern Puṣṭimārgīya Vaiṣṇava scholar and former principal of the Government Sanskrit Pāṭhaśālā in Jamnagar; the poem consciously follows the model of Māgha's Śiśupālavadha. It is a contemporary work of classical Sanskrit poetry rather than a medieval text.

SA_GK19_jaygit2u.title: Gītagovinda
SA_GK19_jaygit2u.author: Jayadeva
SA_GK19_jaygit2u.genre: kavya / lyrical devotional poem (with musical aṣṭapadī songs)
SA_GK19_jaygit2u.date_estimate: late 12th c. CE (c. 1170-1200); project hint of 11th c. is too early
SA_GK19_jaygit2u.summary: A celebrated Sanskrit lyrical poem in twelve cantos dramatizing the love of Kṛṣṇa and the cowherdess Rādhā, moving through their union, estrangement, longing and reconciliation. It is organized around twenty-four aṣṭapadīs (eight-couplet songs) set to specified ragas and talas, blending erotic (śṛṅgāra) sentiment with devotional allegory.
SA_GK19_jaygit2u.history: Composed by Jayadeva, a court poet associated with the Sena king Lakṣmaṇasena; his birthplace is disputed among Odisha, Bengal and Mithila. The earliest commentary appeared in Odisha around 1190 CE. The work became foundational to Vaishnava bhakti, Odissi temple ritual, and later Gauḍīya devotion, generating numerous commentaries and pan-Indian musical traditions.

SA_GK19_jivskd_u.title: Saṅkalpakalpadruma
SA_GK19_jivskd_u.author: Jīva Gosvāmin
SA_GK19_jivskd_u.summary: A devotional Sanskrit work of over 700 verses in which Jīva Gosvāmin addresses his own mind, urging it, as he grows old, to fix itself permanently on Kṛṣṇa's eternal pastimes (nitya-līlā) in Vṛndāvana. It meditates on Kṛṣṇa's līlā from his birth onward and treats the theological relationship between manifest (prakaṭa) and eternal (nitya) pastimes.
SA_GK19_jivskd_u.history: Regarded as one of the last works of Jīva Gosvāmin, the most systematic theologian of the six Gosvāmins of Vṛndāvana in the Caitanya (Gauḍīya) tradition. It functions in part as a recapitulation of the theology of his Gopālacampū, distilling his life's devotional vision.

SA_GK19_kalrs_pu.title: Ṛtusaṃhāra
SA_GK19_kalrs_pu.author: Kālidāsa (attributed)
SA_GK19_kalrs_pu.date_estimate: c. 4th-5th c. CE (Gupta period); authorship sometimes questioned as an early work
SA_GK19_kalrs_pu.summary: A short lyric poem in six cantos describing the six Indian seasons (grīṣma, varṣā, śarad, hemanta, śiśira, vasanta), depicting the effect of each season on nature and on lovers. It is rich in nature imagery and erotic (śṛṅgāra) sentiment, treating the cycle of the year as a backdrop for romantic union.
SA_GK19_kalrs_pu.history: Traditionally ascribed to Kālidāsa and generally regarded as one of his earliest and simplest works, though some scholars have doubted the attribution on stylistic grounds. It is among the earliest extant Sanskrit poems devoted wholly to seasonal description.

SA_GK19_kmeghdpu.title: Meghadūta
SA_GK19_kmeghdpu.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK19_kmeghdpu.summary: A lyric poem in the mandākrāntā metre in which an exiled yakṣa, separated from his beloved, asks a passing rain cloud to carry a message of love to her in the Himalayan city of Alakā. The poem's first half (Pūrvamegha) maps the cloud's route across northern India, while the second half (Uttaramegha) describes Alakā and the lovesick wife. This entry is one of two Meghadūta witnesses in the corpus, likely a distinct recension or part-division.
SA_GK19_kmeghdpu.history: Universally regarded as Kālidāsa's masterpiece of lyric poetry and the model for the entire dūtakāvya (messenger-poem) genre. It attracted dozens of commentaries (notably Mallinātha's Sañjīvanī) and inspired countless imitations across Indian languages.

SA_GK19_kmeghvpu.title: Meghadūta
SA_GK19_kmeghvpu.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK19_kmeghvpu.summary: Kālidāsa's celebrated 'Cloud Messenger,' in which a banished yakṣa entreats a monsoon cloud to convey his words of longing to his distant wife in Alakā. This is a second corpus witness of the Meghadūta (distinct from GK19kmeghdpu), reflecting either a different recension/commentary basis or a separate digital edition of the same poem.
SA_GK19_kmeghvpu.history: The same canonical Kālidāsa poem; multiple recensions and a vast commentarial tradition (e.g., Mallinātha, Vallabhadeva) produced variant texts, which is why more than one witness appears in scholarly corpora.

SA_GK19_kragh_pu.title: Raghuvaṃśa
SA_GK19_kragh_pu.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK19_kragh_pu.summary: A mahākāvya in nineteen cantos chronicling the dynasty of the Raghu (Ikṣvāku/Solar) kings of Ayodhyā, from Dilīpa and Raghu through Aja, Daśaratha, Rāma and his successors down to the decadent Agnivarṇa. It interweaves dynastic history, ideals of kingship and dharma, and a poetic retelling of the Rāma story, showcasing Kālidāsa's mastery of description and metre.
SA_GK19_kragh_pu.history: One of the two great mahākāvyas of Kālidāsa and a model of the classical epic genre, central to the Sanskrit literary canon. It accumulated many commentaries, the most famous being Mallinātha's Sañjīvanī.

SA_GK19_ksbhm06u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 6. Bhīṣmaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm06u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm06u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm06u.date_estimate: 11th c. CE (c. 1037 CE; Kṣemendra fl. c. 1010-1066)
SA_GK19_ksbhm06u.summary: The sixth book of Kṣemendra's verse condensation of the Mahābhārata, corresponding to the epic's Bhīṣmaparvan. It compresses the opening of the Kurukṣetra war and the events surrounding Bhīṣma's generalship, and notably incorporates an abridged version of the Bhagavadgītā as delivered to Arjuna on the battlefield.
SA_GK19_ksbhm06u.history: Kṣemendra (c. 990-1070 CE), a Kashmiri polymath who styled himself Vyāsadāsa, reduced the roughly 100,000-verse Mahābhārata to about 10,692 verses in his Bhāratamañjarī. He freely omitted, abridged and modified portions of the original while preserving its narrative arc in elegant verse.

SA_GK19_ksbhm07u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 7. Droṇaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm07u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm07u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm07u.summary: The seventh book of Kṣemendra's verse Mahābhārata, condensing the Droṇaparvan, in which Droṇa serves as commander of the Kaurava army. It covers the intense central battles, the death of Abhimanyu, and the slaying of Droṇa himself through the stratagem of the false report of Aśvatthāman's death.
SA_GK19_ksbhm07u.history: Part of the integrated Bhāratamañjarī by the Kashmiri poet Kṣemendra (Vyāsadāsa), who summarized the whole epic in roughly 10,692 verses, abridging and selectively reshaping the narrative.

SA_GK19_ksbhm08u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 8. Karṇaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm08u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm08u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm08u.summary: The eighth book of Kṣemendra's verse Mahābhārata, abridging the Karṇaparvan, which narrates the two days during which Karṇa commands the Kaurava forces. It culminates in the climactic duel between Karṇa and Arjuna and Karṇa's death when his chariot wheel sinks into the earth.
SA_GK19_ksbhm08u.history: A section of Kṣemendra's Bhāratamañjarī, the 11th-century Kashmiri verse epitome of the Mahābhārata composed by the poet who called himself Vyāsadāsa.

SA_GK19_ksbhm09u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 9. Śalyaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm09u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm09u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm09u.summary: The ninth book of Kṣemendra's verse Mahābhārata, condensing the Śalyaparvan, in which Śalya becomes the last Kaurava commander. It covers the final collapse of the Kaurava army and leads into the climactic mace duel between Bhīma and Duryodhana.
SA_GK19_ksbhm09u.history: Part of the Bhāratamañjarī, Kṣemendra's 11th-century abridged verse rendering of the Mahābhārata in about 10,692 verses.

SA_GK19_ksbhm10u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 10. Gadāparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm10u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm10u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm10u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse condensation of the 'mace section,' which in the standard Mahābhārata forms the latter part of the Śalyaparvan. It narrates the climactic mace duel between Bhīma and Duryodhana, Bhīma's foul blow to the thigh, and Duryodhana's mortal wounding, effectively ending the great war.
SA_GK19_ksbhm10u.history: A division of Kṣemendra's Bhāratamañjarī; the poet's structural numbering of parvans can differ slightly from the standard 18-book Mahābhārata, here treating the Gadā(yuddha) episode as a separate book.

SA_GK19_ksbhm11u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 11. Sauptikaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm11u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm11u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm11u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse epitome of the Sauptikaparvan, the 'Book of the Night Attack,' in which Aśvatthāman, Kṛpa and Kṛtavarman slaughter the sleeping Pāñcāla warriors and the sons of the Pāṇḍavas by night. It includes the firing of the brahmaśiras weapon and the cursing of Aśvatthāman.
SA_GK19_ksbhm11u.history: Part of the Bhāratamañjarī, Kṣemendra's compact 11th-century verse retelling of the Mahābhārata.

SA_GK19_ksbhm12u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 12. Strīparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm12u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm12u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm12u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse condensation of the Strīparvan, the 'Book of the Women,' depicting the lamentation of the bereaved women of both armies who come to the battlefield to mourn their slain husbands and sons. Gāndhārī's grief and her curse upon Kṛṣṇa are central, as are the funeral rites for the fallen.
SA_GK19_ksbhm12u.history: A section of the Bhāratamañjarī, Kṣemendra's 11th-century Kashmiri abridgement of the Mahābhārata.

SA_GK19_ksbhm13u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 13. Śāntiparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm13u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm13u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm13u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse abridgement of the vast Śāntiparvan, the 'Book of Peace,' in which the dying Bhīṣma, lying on his bed of arrows, instructs Yudhiṣṭhira at length on kingship (rājadharma), duty in calamity (āpaddharma) and the path to liberation (mokṣadharma). Kṣemendra notably has Bhīṣma transmit the thousand names of Śiva and includes a Śivastuti.
SA_GK19_ksbhm13u.history: Part of Kṣemendra's Bhāratamañjarī; the poet greatly compressed the encyclopedic didactic Śāntiparvan while preserving select theological and ethical highlights, reflecting his own Śaiva-inflected interests.

SA_GK19_ksbhm14u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 14. Āśvamedhikaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm14u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm14u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm14u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse epitome of the Āśvamedhikaparvan, the 'Book of the Horse Sacrifice,' in which Yudhiṣṭhira, to expiate the bloodshed of the war, performs the aśvamedha. It follows the wandering sacrificial horse guarded by Arjuna and his ensuing battles, and typically includes the philosophical Anugītā discourse.
SA_GK19_ksbhm14u.history: A division of Kṣemendra's Bhāratamañjarī, his 11th-century condensed verse Mahābhārata.

SA_GK19_ksbhm15u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 15. Āśramavāsikaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm15u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm15u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm15u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse condensation of the Āśramavāsikaparvan, the 'Book of the Hermitage,' in which the aged Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Gāndhārī and Kuntī retire to the forest for ascetic life. It recounts Vidura's death, a vision in which the fallen warriors briefly reappear from the river, and the elders' eventual deaths in a forest fire.
SA_GK19_ksbhm15u.history: Part of the Bhāratamañjarī, Kṣemendra's 11th-century abridged verse Mahābhārata.

SA_GK19_ksbhm16u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 16. Mausalaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm16u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm16u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm16u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse epitome of the Mausalaparvan, the 'Book of the Clubs,' recounting the self-destruction of the Yādava clan in a drunken brawl with iron clubs born of a curse, the deaths of Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa, and the submersion of Dvārakā beneath the sea.
SA_GK19_ksbhm16u.history: A short section of Kṣemendra's Bhāratamañjarī, his 11th-century condensed verse Mahābhārata.

SA_GK19_ksbhm17u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 17. Mahāprasthānikaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm17u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm17u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm17u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse condensation of the Mahāprasthānikaparvan, the 'Book of the Great Departure,' in which the Pāṇḍavas and Draupadī renounce the throne and set out on their final journey toward the Himalayas and Mount Meru, falling one by one along the way, with only Yudhiṣṭhira and a faithful dog continuing.
SA_GK19_ksbhm17u.history: One of the brief closing books of Kṣemendra's Bhāratamañjarī, his 11th-century abridged verse Mahābhārata.

SA_GK19_ksbhm18u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 18. Svargaparvan
SA_GK19_ksbhm18u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm18u.genre: kavya (verse epitome / abridgement of the Mahābhārata)
SA_GK19_ksbhm18u.summary: Kṣemendra's verse epitome of the Svargārohaṇaparvan, the 'Book of the Ascent to Heaven,' the final book, in which Yudhiṣṭhira undergoes a test in which he briefly sees his kin in hell and his foes in heaven, before the illusion is dispelled and all attain their heavenly stations, concluding the epic.
SA_GK19_ksbhm18u.history: The concluding book of Kṣemendra's Bhāratamañjarī, his 11th-century condensed verse rendering of the whole Mahābhārata.

SA_GK19_ksbhm19u.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 19. Harivaṃśa
SA_GK19_ksbhm19u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_GK19_ksbhm19u.summary: The Bhāratamañjarī is Kṣemendra's verse condensation of the entire Mahābhārata, rendering Vyāsa's epic into a compact poetic summary in his own words. This section corresponds to the nineteenth book, the Harivaṃśa, the genealogical and Krishna-centred appendix (khila) of the Mahābhārata that narrates the lineage of Hari (Vishnu/Krishna), the creation, and the deeds of Krishna. Kṣemendra preserves the narrative substance while upholding śānta (serenity) as the work's predominant rasa.
SA_GK19_ksbhm19u.history: Kṣemendra (c. 990–1070 CE) was a prolific Kashmiri polymath who studied under Abhinavagupta and Vaishnava teachers; he was renowned as a master 'abridger' (mañjarī-maker) who also condensed the Rāmāyaṇa (Rāmāyaṇamañjarī) and Guṇāḍhya's Bṛhatkathā. The Bhāratamañjarī is an important witness to the form of the Mahābhārata circulating in 11th-century Kashmir.

SA_GK19_ksyadndu.title: Śyāmalādaṇḍaka
SA_GK19_ksyadndu.author: Kālidāsa (traditionally attributed)
SA_GK19_ksyadndu.date_estimate: traditionally ascribed to Kālidāsa (c. 4th–5th c. CE); attribution uncertain, possibly later
SA_GK19_ksyadndu.summary: The Śyāmalādaṇḍaka is a devotional hymn in praise of the goddess Śyāmalā (also called Mātaṅgī or Rājaśyāmalā), a dark-hued form associated with Sarasvatī and with Lalitā-Tripurasundarī in the Śrīvidyā tradition. It is composed in the rare and demanding daṇḍaka meter, in which long runs of syllables produce an almost prose-like, single continuous sentence praising the goddess's beauty, attributes, and grace.
SA_GK19_ksyadndu.history: Tradition attributes the hymn to Kālidāsa, framing it within the legend of the dull poet transformed by the goddess's grace, though the ascription is doubtful and the work may be considerably later. It remains a popular liturgical text in goddess worship, especially in South India.

SA_GK19_kulmukuu.title: Mukundamālā
SA_GK19_kulmukuu.author: Kulaśekhara (Kulaśekhara Āḻvār)
SA_GK19_kulmukuu.summary: The Mukundamālā ('Garland for Mukunda') is a Sanskrit devotional hymn of roughly 40 verses addressed to Vishnu-Krishna (Mukunda). It expresses single-minded surrender (prapatti) and longing for the deity, expressing the wish to remember Krishna at the moment of death and to find refuge in him rather than in worldly attainments.
SA_GK19_kulmukuu.history: It is attributed to Kulaśekhara, traditionally identified with the Āḻvār poet-saint and Chera king Kulaśekhara, counted among the twelve Āḻvārs of the Tamil Śrī Vaishnava tradition. The hymn is widely recited and has been commented upon within the Vaishnava devotional milieu; the date hint of 900–1099 CE roughly fits the early medieval setting.

SA_GK19_lnksivsu.title: Śivastuti
SA_GK19_lnksivsu.author: attributed to Laṅkeśvara (Rāvaṇa, legendary)
SA_GK19_lnksivsu.date_estimate: uncertain; the Rāvaṇa-attributed Śiva hymn (Śiva Tāṇḍava Stotra) is generally medieval, exact date unknown
SA_GK19_lnksivsu.summary: A hymn of praise to Śiva attributed to Laṅkeśvara, the 'Lord of Lanka' Rāvaṇa, who in Hindu tradition is a supreme devotee of Śiva. The title and author point to the celebrated Rāvaṇa-authored Śiva hymn (best known as the Śiva Tāṇḍava Stotra), which extols Śiva's cosmic dance and his terrifying yet auspicious form—the Ganges in his matted locks, the crescent moon, the third eye, and the sound of the ḍamaru.
SA_GK19_lnksivsu.history: By legend Rāvaṇa composed the hymn while pinned beneath Mount Kailāsa by Śiva's toe, singing praises until released; the Uttarakāṇḍa of the Rāmāyaṇa supplies the narrative frame. The actual date and authorship are unknown and the work is far later than the legendary attribution. Identification of this specific 'Śivastuti' with the Śiva Tāṇḍava Stotra is probable but not certain.

SA_GK19_maghspvu.title: Śiśupālavadha
SA_GK19_maghspvu.author: Māgha
SA_GK19_maghspvu.summary: The Śiśupālavadha ('The Slaying of Śiśupāla') is a mahākāvya in 20 cantos that elaborates an episode from the Mahābhārata: Krishna's killing of the insolent king Śiśupāla of Chedi at Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya sacrifice. The narrative serves as a frame for dazzling poetic ornamentation, learned description, and metrical and verbal virtuosity rather than for plot alone.
SA_GK19_maghspvu.history: Composed by Māgha (son of Dattaka), probably in the late 7th or early 8th century in western India (Śrīmāla/Bhinmal). It is counted among the five canonical mahākāvyas and gave rise to the proverb 'in Māgha are found the three—simile, words, and meaning' (the poet who rivals Kālidāsa and Bhāravi). It is famous for citra (constrained/figured verse) display.

SA_GK19_mala.title: Mālatīmādhava
SA_GK19_mala.author: Bhavabhūti
SA_GK19_mala.genre: drama (prakaraṇa, a 10-act play of invented plot)
SA_GK19_mala.date_estimate: c. early 8th century CE (fl. c. 700–730 CE)
SA_GK19_mala.summary: The Mālatīmādhava is a ten-act prakaraṇa dramatizing the love of Mādhava and Mālatī, whose union is obstructed by her betrothal to another and by intrigue. The play is notable for its dark and macabre elements—a Kāpālika ascetic and his pupil attempt to sacrifice Mālatī to the goddess Cāmuṇḍā—combining romantic sentiment with cremation-ground horror and supernatural danger.
SA_GK19_mala.history: Bhavabhūti (Śrīkaṇṭha Nīlakaṇṭha) was a Maharashtrian Brahmin poet-dramatist patronized at the court of Yaśovarman of Kanauj in the early 8th century. He is ranked just below Kālidāsa among Sanskrit dramatists and is also author of the Mahāvīracarita and Uttararāmacarita.

SA_GK19_naicau.title: Naiṣadhīya-carita
SA_GK19_naicau.author: Śrīharṣa
SA_GK19_naicau.date_estimate: c. second half of the 12th century CE
SA_GK19_naicau.summary: The Naiṣadhīyacarita (Naiṣadhacarita) is a mahākāvya in 22 cantos retelling the romance of King Nala of Niṣadha and Princess Damayantī of Vidarbha, drawn from the Mahābhārata, up to their marriage. It elaborates the swan messenger, Damayantī's svayaṃvara at which the gods disguise themselves as Nala, and Nala's testing—rendered in extraordinarily dense, learned, and ślesa-laden verse.
SA_GK19_naicau.history: Śrīharṣa was a 12th-century philosopher-poet of northern India, also author of the Advaita dialectical treatise Khaṇḍanakhaṇḍakhādya. Counted among the five great mahākāvyas, the poem is famed for its difficulty and is called a 'scholars' tonic' (vidvad-auṣadha), a test of Sanskrit erudition.

SA_GK19_padnscpu.title: Navasāhasāṅkacarita
SA_GK19_padnscpu.author: Padmagupta (Parimala)
SA_GK19_padnscpu.date_estimate: c. 1000 CE (composed during reign of Sindhurāja, r. c. 997–1010)
SA_GK19_padnscpu.summary: The Navasāhasāṅkacarita is a mahākāvya in 18 cantos that romanticizes the Paramāra king Sindhurāja of Malwa (whose epithet was Navasāhasāṅka), centring on his quest and marriage to the princess Śaśiprabhā, narrated in a quasi-fairytale manner with vidyādharas and a Nāga quest. It blends courtly historical eulogy (praśasti) with romantic fantasy in the polished Vaidarbhī style.
SA_GK19_padnscpu.history: Padmagupta, also called Parimala (or Parimala-Kālidāsa), was a court poet of the Paramāra king Sindhurāja and states he composed the poem at the king's command. It is a valued, if poeticized, source for early-11th-century Paramāra dynastic history.

SA_GK19_padyavlu.title: Padyāvalī
SA_GK19_padyavlu.author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
SA_GK19_padyavlu.summary: The Padyāvalī is an anthology of devotional Sanskrit verses (around 380–390 verses) compiled by Rūpa Gosvāmī, gathering compositions by over a hundred poet-devotees. The verses are arranged thematically around aspects of bhakti—the moods and aspirations of devotees and the deeds and pastimes of Krishna and his associates.
SA_GK19_padyavlu.history: Rūpa Gosvāmī (16th c.) was a leading disciple of Caitanya Mahāprabhu and a foundational theologian of the Gauḍīya Vaishnava tradition. The Padyāvalī preserves verses (some otherwise lost) drawn on by later Gauḍīya literature; it was critically edited by S. K. De (Dacca, 1934).

SA_GK19_raggancu.title: Mahāgaṇapatistotra
SA_GK19_raggancu.author: Rāghavacaitanya (attributed)
SA_GK19_raggancu.date_estimate: uncertain; if by the Vaishnava commentator Rāghavacaitanya, c. 16th century CE
SA_GK19_raggancu.summary: A hymn of praise to Mahāgaṇapati, the 'Great Gaṇeśa,' the elephant-headed deity invoked as remover of obstacles and lord of beginnings. As a gaṇapati-stotra it would enumerate the deity's forms, names, and attributes and seek his grace; the specific text under this exact title and authorship is poorly documented.
SA_GK19_raggancu.history: The hymn is ascribed to Rāghavacaitanya, a name borne by a Vaishnava teacher/commentator (associated with commentary on the Gītagovinda, c. 16th c.); the attribution of this particular Gaṇapati hymn to him could not be confirmed from accessible scholarly sources. Identification and dating remain tentative.

SA_GK19_ramodtpu.title: Rāmodanta
SA_GK19_ramodtpu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GK19_ramodtpu.date_estimate: uncertain; likely late medieval / early modern, used as a Sanskrit primer
SA_GK19_ramodtpu.summary: The Rāmodanta (Rāmodantam, 'The Account of Rāma') is a short Sanskrit poem that retells the entire story of the Rāmāyaṇa in simple, accessible verses following Vālmīki's narrative. Because of its simplicity it has long functioned as an introductory text for learning Sanskrit, presenting Rāma's life while exemplifying basic grammar and vocabulary.
SA_GK19_ramodtpu.history: The work is anonymous in the received tradition and is widely used as a beginner's reader in traditional Sanskrit pedagogy, especially in South India. Its compact, didactic character distinguishes it from the great epic it summarizes.

SA_GK19_setu.title: Setubandha
SA_GK19_setu.author: Pravarasena
SA_GK19_setu.date_estimate: c. 5th century CE (disputed; some place it mid-3rd c. CE under the Vākāṭakas)
SA_GK19_setu.summary: The Setubandha ('Building of the Bridge'), also called Rāvaṇavaho ('The Slaying of Rāvaṇa'), is a mahākāvya composed in Mahārāṣṭrī Prakrit that narrates the Rāmāyaṇa episode of Rāma's army building the bridge (setu) to Lanka and the ensuing war against Rāvaṇa. It is celebrated for its elaborate descriptive set-pieces and is the principal surviving early Prakrit court epic.
SA_GK19_setu.history: It is attributed to a king Pravarasena (commonly identified with the Vākāṭaka ruler Pravarasena II); tradition variously connects it to Kālidāsa as well. Dating is disputed between the mid-3rd and the 5th century CE. It is the earliest extant Prakrit mahākāvya and was hugely influential as a model of Prakrit poetic style.

SA_GK19_srngt_pu.title: Śṛṅgāratilaka
SA_GK19_srngt_pu.author: Rudrabhaṭṭa (Rudra Bhaṭṭa)
SA_GK19_srngt_pu.genre: philosophical/literary treatise on poetics (alaṃkāraśāstra), specifically on rasa
SA_GK19_srngt_pu.date_estimate: uncertain; commonly placed c. 10th century CE (variously 10th–13th c.)
SA_GK19_srngt_pu.summary: The Śṛṅgāratilaka ('Ornament of the Erotic') is a short treatise on Sanskrit poetics devoted specifically to rasa (aesthetic emotion), and especially to śṛṅgāra, the erotic sentiment. It is notable as the first independent Indian work to take rasa—and the typology of lovers (nāyaka/nāyikā) and the erotic mood—as its sole subject, illustrating the theory with verses.
SA_GK19_srngt_pu.history: Its author Rudra Bhaṭṭa is poorly identified and sometimes conflated with the poetician Rudraṭa; his date, place, and even name are uncertain. The work is an important early link in the history of rasa theory and of nāyikābheda (classification of heroines). Sheldon Pollock has worked on a critical edition/translation.

SA_GK19_suvasavu.title: Vāsavadattā
SA_GK19_suvasavu.author: Subandhu
SA_GK19_suvasavu.summary: The Vāsavadattā of Subandhu is an ornate prose romance (ākhyāyikā/kathā) recounting how Prince Kandarpaketu, after seeing Princess Vāsavadattā in a dream, journeys to find her; the lovers elope, she is turned to stone by a curse, and is finally restored. The work is famed less for its slender plot than for its dense, pun-laden (śleṣa) ornate prose style.
SA_GK19_suvasavu.history: Subandhu is praised by Bāṇa in the Harṣacarita, placing him before the 7th century; he is generally assigned to the late 6th or early 7th century CE. The Vāsavadattā is one of the earliest specimens of Sanskrit ornate prose romance and a forerunner of Bāṇa's Kādambarī and Harṣacarita.

SA_GK19_uddkokpu.title: Kokilasandeśa
SA_GK19_uddkokpu.author: Uddaṇḍa (Uddaṇḍa Śāstrī)
SA_GK19_uddkokpu.summary: The Kokilasandeśa ('Message of the Koel') is a messenger poem of about 162 verses in which a nameless hero, carried off from his wife by mysterious women, sends a message of love to her by way of a koel (Indian cuckoo). Modelled on Kālidāsa's Meghadūta, it traces the bird's route across Kerala, lavishly describing the towns, rivers, and temples along the way.
SA_GK19_uddkokpu.history: Its author Uddaṇḍa Śāstrī (originally Irugupanātha) was a 15th-century Tamil Brahmin scholar who migrated west and found patronage at the Zamorin's court in Calicut, where he earned the title 'Uddaṇḍa' ('pre-eminent'). It is among the most celebrated of the many Kerala sandeśa kāvyas and a valued source for Kerala's historical geography.

SA_GK20_bhasdutu.title: Dūtavākya
SA_GK20_bhasdutu.author: Bhāsa
SA_GK20_bhasdutu.date_estimate: attributed to Bhāsa; commonly c. 2nd–4th c. CE (Bhāsa's dating disputed)
SA_GK20_bhasdutu.summary: Dūtavākya ('The Envoy's Message') is a one-act play in which Krishna goes as the Pāṇḍavas' peace envoy to Duryodhana's court on the eve of the Mahābhārata war. It dramatizes the verbal confrontation between Krishna and the defiant Duryodhana, culminating in Krishna's display of his cosmic form and his weapons before the assembled kings.
SA_GK20_bhasdutu.history: It is one of the so-called 'Trivandrum plays' recovered in the early 20th century and attributed to Bhāsa, among his cycle of short Mahābhārata-based dramas (with Madhyamavyāyoga, Karṇabhāra, Ūrubhaṅga, Dūtaghaṭotkaca). Bhāsa is cited by Kālidāsa as a predecessor; his exact dates and the authorship of the Trivandrum plays remain debated.

SA_GK20_bhassv_u.title: Svapnavāsavadatta
SA_GK20_bhassv_u.author: Bhāsa
SA_GK20_bhassv_u.date_estimate: attributed to Bhāsa; commonly c. 2nd–4th c. CE (dating disputed)
SA_GK20_bhassv_u.summary: Svapnavāsavadatta ('The Dream of Vāsavadattā') is a six-act play telling how King Udayana of Vatsa, having lost his kingdom, is persuaded by his minister Yaugandharāyaṇa to marry Padmāvatī of Magadha for political alliance; his beloved queen Vāsavadattā, believed dead, lives in disguise. The famous 'dream' scene, in which the sleeping Udayana speaks of his love and Vāsavadattā overhears, is among the most admired in Sanskrit theatre.
SA_GK20_bhassv_u.history: Generally regarded as the finest of the Trivandrum plays attributed to Bhāsa, recovered by T. Gaṇapati Śāstrī in 1909–10. Bhāsa predates Kālidāsa (who names him); his date is uncertain and the attribution of the plays continues to be debated. The drama draws on the Udayana legend cycle (Bṛhatkathā).

SA_GK20_bhveni_u.title: Veṇīsaṃhāra
SA_GK20_bhveni_u.author: Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇa
SA_GK20_bhveni_u.date_estimate: c. late 7th–early 8th century CE (fl. c. 700 CE)
SA_GK20_bhveni_u.summary: The Veṇīsaṃhāra ('The Binding of the Braid') is a six-act play dramatizing the Mahābhārata war, built around Bhīma's vow to avenge the outrage of Draupadī: he will not let her rebind her loosened hair until he has slain Duḥśāsana and Duryodhana and smeared her braid with their blood. The play emphasizes vīra (heroic) and raudra (furious) rasa and culminates in the fulfilment of the vow.
SA_GK20_bhveni_u.history: Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇa flourished around 700 CE; tradition makes him one of the Brahmins invited to Bengal, and he is the earliest source for the dramatic motif of Draupadī's blood-bound braid. The play has attracted commentaries (e.g. Jagaddhara's) and is a standard text of classical Sanskrit dramaturgy.

SA_GK20_dramfrag_pu.title: Dramatic Fragments (buddhist)
SA_GK20_dramfrag_pu.author: Aśvaghoṣa (and unidentified author[s])
SA_GK20_dramfrag_pu.summary: These are the fragmentary remains of early Buddhist Sanskrit plays recovered from the Turfan (Central Asia) palm-leaf finds. They include the colophon-bearing Śāriputraprakaraṇa, a nine-act drama by Aśvaghoṣa dramatizing the conversion of the disciples Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana, together with fragments of one or more allegorical/morality plays featuring personified figures (e.g. Buddhi, Kīrti, Dhṛti).
SA_GK20_dramfrag_pu.history: The fragments were discovered in the Berlin Turfan collection and edited by Heinrich Lüders (Bruchstücke buddhistischer Dramen, Berlin 1911). They are among the oldest surviving specimens of Sanskrit drama, predating Bhāsa and Kālidāsa, and attest the use of theatre for Buddhist religious propagation under Aśvaghoṣa (1st–2nd c. CE, associated with the Kuṣāṇa court of Kaniṣka).

SA_GK20_harnagau.title: Nāgānandanāṭaka
SA_GK20_harnagau.author: Harṣa (Harṣadeva / Śrīharṣadeva)
SA_GK20_harnagau.tradition: Hindu/Buddhist (Buddhist-themed play by a Hindu king)
SA_GK20_harnagau.date_estimate: c. first half of the 7th century CE (r. 606–647 CE)
SA_GK20_harnagau.summary: The Nāgānanda ('Joy of the Nāgas') is a five-act play that dramatizes the legend of the vidyādhara prince Jīmūtavāhana, who, embodying Buddhist compassion, offers his own body to the bird-god Garuḍa to save a young Nāga destined to be devoured, thereby ending Garuḍa's slaughter of the serpents. It blends a romantic first portion (Jīmūtavāhana's love for Malayavatī) with a strongly self-sacrificial, Bodhisattva-like climax.
SA_GK20_harnagau.history: Attributed to the emperor Harṣa of Kanauj (also credited with the plays Ratnāvalī and Priyadarśikā). The Nāgānanda is unusual for its Buddhist ethos and its benedictory verse to the Buddha (and/or Gaurī), reflecting Harṣa's syncretic patronage. The story parallels material later found in the Kathāsaritsāgara.

SA_GK20_hdratn_u.title: Ratnāvalīnāṭikā
SA_GK20_hdratn_u.author: Harṣadeva (Harṣavardhana)
SA_GK20_hdratn_u.date_estimate: early to mid-7th c. CE (during reign of Harṣa, r. c. 606-647 CE)
SA_GK20_hdratn_u.summary: A four-act romantic court comedy (nāṭikā) recounting the love affair between King Udayana of Vatsa and Ratnāvalī (Sāgarikā), a Ceylonese princess who, shipwrecked, enters the court of Udayana's chief queen Vāsavadattā as a maidservant. The plot turns on mistaken identities, the queen's jealousy, palace intrigue, and the eventual revelation of Sāgarikā's royal birth and her union with the king.
SA_GK20_hdratn_u.history: Conventionally attributed to the emperor Harṣa of Kanauj, though some scholars argue a court poet (sometimes named Dhāvaka or Bāṇa) composed the dramas issued under his name. The play is a model of the nāṭikā subgenre and is celebrated for its refined handling of the love-intrigue plot derived ultimately from the Bṛhatkathā tradition.

SA_GK20_hpriydpu.title: Priyadarśikā
SA_GK20_hpriydpu.author: Harṣadeva (Harṣavardhana)
SA_GK20_hpriydpu.date_estimate: early to mid-7th c. CE (reign of Harṣa, r. c. 606-647 CE)
SA_GK20_hpriydpu.summary: A four-act romantic nāṭikā dramatizing the love of King Udayana of Vatsa for Priyadarśikā (Āraṇyakā), daughter of King Dṛḍhavarman, who in disguise serves in the harem of Queen Vāsavadattā. Mistaken identity, a play-within-a-play, and the queen's eventual generosity bring about the recognition of the heroine's royal status and her marriage to the king.
SA_GK20_hpriydpu.history: One of three dramas (with Ratnāvalī and Nāgānanda) traditionally ascribed to Harṣa; the same authorship debate (Harṣa vs. a court poet such as Bāṇa/Dhāvaka) applies. Its plot motif is drawn from the Udayana legend cycle of Guṇāḍhya's Bṛhatkathā and closely parallels Ratnāvalī in structure.

SA_GK20_mmatvipu.title: Mattavilāsaprahasana
SA_GK20_mmatvipu.author: Mahendravarman I (Pallava king)
SA_GK20_mmatvipu.tradition: Hindu (secular satire; lampoons Kāpālika, Pāśupata, Buddhist and Jain ascetics)
SA_GK20_mmatvipu.summary: A one-act Sanskrit farce (prahasana) set in Kāñcīpuram, the Pallava capital. A drunken Kāpālika Śaiva ascetic and his consort lose their begging skull-bowl and accuse a Buddhist monk of stealing it, generating a satirical dispute that mocks the hypocrisy and excesses of various heterodox and ascetic sects.
SA_GK20_mmatvipu.history: Composed by the Pallava king Mahendravarman I (also a patron of rock-cut architecture); the play is referenced in inscriptions and securely dated to the early 7th century. It is one of the earliest and most celebrated examples of the prahasana genre and an important source for the social and religious milieu of 7th-century South India.

SA_GK20_murau.title: Mudrārākṣasa
SA_GK20_murau.author: Viśākhadatta
SA_GK20_murau.date_estimate: disputed; commonly c. 5th-6th c. CE (proposals range late 4th to 8th c. CE); project hint c. 500-600 CE
SA_GK20_murau.summary: A seven-act political drama dramatizing the statecraft of Cāṇakya (Kauṭilya) as he maneuvers to win over Rākṣasa, the loyal minister of the overthrown Nanda dynasty, to the service of the new Mauryan emperor Candragupta. Unusually for Sanskrit drama, it contains no romantic plot and centers entirely on espionage, forged seals (mudrā), and diplomatic intrigue.
SA_GK20_murau.history: By the poet Viśākhadatta, about whose life little is known; his dating is debated over several centuries. The play is prized as a masterwork of plot construction and as a literary window onto ancient Indian political theory, drawing on the Arthaśāstra tradition.

SA_GK20_rmc1-3pu.title: Rāmacaritamahākāvya 1–3
SA_GK20_rmc1-3pu.author: Abhinanda (son of Jayanta/Śatānanda)
SA_GK20_rmc1-3pu.date_estimate: 9th c. CE (Pāla-period; project hint c. 799-999 CE)
SA_GK20_rmc1-3pu.summary: Cantos 1-3 of a Sanskrit court epic (mahākāvya) in some forty cantos retelling the story of Rāma, largely following the narrative of Vālmīki's Rāmāyaṇa but recast in ornate classical kāvya style. The opening cantos cover the early Rāmāyaṇa material (Bāla/Ayodhyā portions).
SA_GK20_rmc1-3pu.history: Composed by Abhinanda, a Sanskrit poet of Gauḍa (Bengal) and court poet associated with the Pāla king Hāravarṣa, in the 9th century. He is distinct from the later Sandhyākara Nandi who wrote a different Rāmacaritam. The Gaekwad's Oriental Series published K. S. Ramaswami Sastri's edition (1930).

SA_GK20_ruysahlu.title: Sahṛdayalīlā
SA_GK20_ruysahlu.author: Ruyyaka (Rājānaka Ruyyaka)
SA_GK20_ruysahlu.date_estimate: first half of the 12th c. CE
SA_GK20_ruysahlu.summary: A short work on Sanskrit literary theory, dealing with the appreciation of poetry by the cultivated connoisseur (sahṛdaya) and matters of figures of speech and aesthetic response. It belongs to the rich Kashmirian tradition of alaṃkāraśāstra.
SA_GK20_ruysahlu.history: Ruyyaka, son of Tilaka, was a leading Kashmirian theorist of the early 12th century, best known for the Alaṃkārasarvasva; the Sahṛdayalīlā is among his minor works. It was edited and published by R. Pischel together with Rudraṭa's Śṛṅgāratilaka. Ruyyaka's oeuvre represents a culmination of Kashmirian aesthetics following Abhinavagupta and Mammaṭa.

SA_GK20_sypadatu.title: Pāḍatāḍitaka
SA_GK20_sypadatu.author: Udīcya Śyāmilaka (Śyāmilaka)
SA_GK20_sypadatu.date_estimate: disputed; Gupta period, commonly c. 5th c. CE (proposals range c. 410 CE to as late as 8th-9th c.)
SA_GK20_sypadatu.summary: The longest of the four bhāṇas collected in the Caturbhāṇī, a one-act monologue play in which a single actor (a viṭa, urbane man-about-town) impersonates and converses with an array of imagined characters drawn from city life, including courtesans, gamblers and rogues. With some 148 verses interspersed with prose, the title ("The Kick") refers to a comic episode of a man kicked in a quarrel.
SA_GK20_sypadatu.history: Attributed to Śyāmilaka, probably a native of Kashmir of the later Gupta era; the precise date is contested (T. Burrow argued c. 410 CE, others later). It is a key source for the social history and urban culture of classical India and for the development of the bhāṇa genre.

SA_GK21_karaka.title: Kathāratnākara
SA_GK21_karaka.author: Hemavijaya (Hemavijayagaṇi)
SA_GK21_karaka.summary: An "Ocean of Story-Jewels," a Sanskrit prose collection of some 258 didactic tales, fables, legends, anecdotes and humorous stories. Hemavijaya drew the material largely from oral tradition and earlier sources, with some of his own composition, aiming both to entertain and to teach moral and religious lessons.
SA_GK21_karaka.history: Composed by the Jain monk Hemavijaya (1565-1631) at Ahmedabad around 1600 CE according to its own colophon. It was translated into German by Johannes Hertel as Das Märchenmeer ("The Sea of Tales") and is a notable late example of the Jain narrative tradition.

SA_GK21_soksvppu.title: Kathāsaritsāgara
SA_GK21_soksvppu.author: Somadeva (Somadeva Bhaṭṭa)
SA_GK21_soksvppu.genre: narrative literature (kathā, story collection in verse)
SA_GK21_soksvppu.date_estimate: c. 1063-1081 CE (11th c.; project hint c. 1100-1350 broadly later)
SA_GK21_soksvppu.summary: The "Ocean of the Streams of Story," a vast Sanskrit verse compendium of tales arranged in 18 books (lambakas) and over 120 chapters (taraṅgas), framed around the adventures of Naravāhanadatta. It embeds hundreds of interlocking fairy tales, fables, romances and didactic stories, including well-known cycles such as the Vetālapañcaviṃśati.
SA_GK21_soksvppu.history: Compiled c. 1070 CE by the Kashmirian Śaiva Brahmin Somadeva for Sūryavatī, queen of King Ananta of Kashmir, as a Sanskrit recasting of the lost Bṛhatkathā of Guṇāḍhya (via its Paiśācī tradition). It is one of the most important repositories of Indian folklore and was famously translated by C. H. Tawney.

SA_GK21_ttrkhy1u.title: Tantrākhyāyika 1
SA_GK21_ttrkhy1u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Viṣṇuśarman)
SA_GK21_ttrkhy1u.date_estimate: earliest recension; text ultimately c. 300 BCE-570 CE, this Kashmirian recension preserving an early form (1st millennium CE)
SA_GK21_ttrkhy1u.summary: Book 1 of the Tantrākhyāyika, the oldest surviving Kashmirian recension of the Pañcatantra. This book is "Mitrabheda" (The Separation of Friends), in which the jackals Damanaka and Karaṭaka engineer the estrangement of the lion-king Piṅgalaka from the bull Saṃjīvaka, illustrating principles of statecraft (nīti) through interwoven animal fables.
SA_GK21_ttrkhy1u.history: The Tantrākhyāyika was identified and edited by Johannes Hertel (Berlin 1910; Harvard 1915) as the oldest extant recension of the Pañcatantra, the classic Indian "mirror for princes." Authorship is traditionally assigned to Viṣṇuśarman; the underlying work spread across Asia via the Middle Persian and Arabic Kalīla wa-Dimna traditions.

SA_GK21_ttrkhy2u.title: Tantrākhyāyika 2
SA_GK21_ttrkhy2u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Viṣṇuśarman)
SA_GK21_ttrkhy2u.date_estimate: earliest recension; text ultimately c. 300 BCE-570 CE, this Kashmirian recension preserving an early form (1st millennium CE)
SA_GK21_ttrkhy2u.summary: Book 2 of the Tantrākhyāyika (oldest Kashmirian recension of the Pañcatantra), corresponding to "Mitrasaṃprāpti" (The Winning of Friends), in which a crow, mouse, tortoise and deer form a mutually protective friendship that allows them to escape hunters and traps. It illustrates the value of alliance and cooperation among the weak.
SA_GK21_ttrkhy2u.history: Part of the same early Kashmirian recension edited by Johannes Hertel as the oldest form of the Pañcatantra, traditionally ascribed to Viṣṇuśarman. The Pañcatantra became one of the most widely translated works in world literature through the Kalīla wa-Dimna line of transmission.

SA_GK22_bharst_u.title: Śatakatraya: 1. Nītiśataka, 2. Śṛṅgāraśataka, 3. Vairāgyaśataka
SA_GK22_bharst_u.author: Bhartṛhari
SA_GK22_bharst_u.tradition: Hindu (secular gnomic poetry; with renunciatory/Vedānta-Yoga colouring)
SA_GK22_bharst_u.date_estimate: c. 5th c. CE (commonly; project hint c. 500-650 CE)
SA_GK22_bharst_u.summary: A triad of "centuries" (śataka) of epigrammatic verses: the Nītiśataka on worldly wisdom, ethics and conduct; the Śṛṅgāraśataka on love, women and sensual pleasure; and the Vairāgyaśataka on dispassion and renunciation. Together they move from engagement with the world toward detachment, and are among the most quoted of all Sanskrit subhāṣita verses.
SA_GK22_bharst_u.history: Attributed to Bhartṛhari, sometimes (problematically) identified with the grammarian-philosopher of the Vākyapadīya. The Chinese pilgrim Yijing reports a Bhartṛhari who oscillated between monastery and worldly life, fitting the mood of the verses. The collection circulated in numerous recensions and was the first Sanskrit work rendered into a European language (Abraham Roger, 17th c.).

SA_GK22_subhashitasataka.title: Subhāṣitaśataka
SA_GK22_subhashitasataka.author: anonymous/traditional (title shared by several compilers)
SA_GK22_subhashitasataka.date_estimate: uncertain; generic title for medieval subhāṣita collections (1st-2nd millennium CE)
SA_GK22_subhashitasataka.summary: A "century of well-spoken verses," i.e., a collection of roughly one hundred polished gnomic and didactic Sanskrit epigrams on ethics, conduct, wisdom and worldly life. The title is generic and has been borne by more than one compilation in the subhāṣita tradition.
SA_GK22_subhashitasataka.history: The exact work intended by this bare title cannot be pinned to a single author with certainty; several śataka-style subhāṣita anthologies circulate under such names (one notable example being attributed to the poet-anthologist tradition). It belongs to the broad medieval Sanskrit practice of compiling memorable, quotable verses.

SA_GK22_valsubhu.title: Subhāṣitāvalī
SA_GK22_valsubhu.author: Vallabhadeva
SA_GK22_valsubhu.date_estimate: c. 15th c. CE (commonly c. 1417-1467; a minority argues for a 10th-c. Vallabhadeva), project hint c. 1400-1699
SA_GK22_valsubhu.summary: A large Sanskrit anthology of about 3,527 verse epigrams grouped under 101 thematic headings (cosmic, erotic, ethical, comic and other verse) and ascribed to some 362 named poets besides many anonymous pieces. It preserves verses spanning roughly fifteen centuries of Sanskrit poetry, with a marked Kashmirian flavor.
SA_GK22_valsubhu.history: Compiled by Vallabhadeva, generally identified as a Kashmirian (kāśmīraka); his date is debated between the 10th and 15th centuries, with most modern opinion favoring the 15th. It is a major source for otherwise-lost poets and was edited by Peterson and Durgāprasāda (1886).

SA_GK22_vidsrgpu.title: Subhāṣitaratnakoṣa
SA_GK22_vidsrgpu.author: Vidyākara
SA_GK22_vidsrgpu.date_estimate: c. late 11th-early 12th c. CE (first recension before 1100, second by c. 1130 CE); project hint c. 900-1199
SA_GK22_vidsrgpu.summary: The "Treasury of Well-Turned Verse," an anthology of refined Sanskrit court poetry (over 1,700 verses in its expanded recension) arranged thematically, ranging from invocations of deities through nature, the seasons, love, character types and gnomic reflection. It is a prime witness to the high classical kāvya of the period c. 700-1050 CE.
SA_GK22_vidsrgpu.history: Compiled by Vidyākara, a Buddhist scholar associated with the Jagaddala monastery in Bengal, in two recensions in the late 11th to early 12th century. Famously edited and translated by D. H. H. Ingalls in the Harvard Oriental Series. This entry corresponds to one volume/part of the modern edition; it preserves many verses by otherwise unknown poets.

SA_GK22_vidsripu.title: Subhāṣitaratnakoṣa
SA_GK22_vidsripu.author: Vidyākara
SA_GK22_vidsripu.date_estimate: c. late 11th-early 12th c. CE (first recension before 1100, second by c. 1130 CE); project hint c. 900-1199
SA_GK22_vidsripu.summary: The "Treasury of Well-Turned Verse" by Vidyākara; this entry corresponds to a second volume/part of the same anthology (the work was published in multiple sections). It gathers thematically ordered classical Sanskrit verses on the gods, nature and seasons, love, types of persons, and worldly wisdom, drawn from major kāvyas, plays, minor poems and stray verses.
SA_GK22_vidsripu.history: By the same Bengali Buddhist compiler Vidyākara (Jagaddala monastery), late 11th-early 12th century, in two recensions. The standard scholarship is Ingalls's edition and translation (Harvard Oriental Series). It is invaluable for transmitting verses by poets whose own works are lost.

SA_GK23_jonart.title: Rājataraṅginī
SA_GK23_jonart.author: Jonarāja (and Pseudo-Jonarāja)
SA_GK23_jonart.date_estimate: mid-15th c. CE (Jonarāja d. 1459); Pseudo-Jonarāja interpolations late 16th c. CE; project hint c. 1100-1200 refers to its narrative span, not composition
SA_GK23_jonart.summary: The "second" Rājataraṅginī, a Sanskrit verse chronicle of the kings of Kashmir continuing Kalhaṇa's original from c. 1148/1149 CE down to the reign of Sultan Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn (mid-15th c.). It documents in detail the transition to the Kashmir Sultanate and the Islamization of the valley, with Jonarāja writing in part as an eyewitness.
SA_GK23_jonart.history: Composed by the Kashmirian poet-historian Jonarāja (died 1459), court historian to Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn. One textual recension incorporates roughly 350 additional verses interpolated in the later 16th century by an anonymous continuator known as "Pseudo-Jonarāja." The chronicle was itself continued by Jonarāja's pupil Śrīvara.

SA_GK23_kaulpradsam_u.title: Pradeśasaṃgraha
SA_GK23_kaulpradsam_u.tradition: Hindu (likely Kashmirian Śaiva / Kaula context, given work-id 'kaul')
SA_GK23_kaulpradsam_u.summary: Could not be confidently identified. The title 'Pradeśasaṃgraha' (literally "a collection/compendium of passages or topics") together with the work-id element 'kaul' suggests a compilation associated with the Kashmirian Kaula/Śaiva textual milieu, but no specific edition or attested work matching this exact title could be located in the consulted sources.
SA_GK23_kaulpradsam_u.history: No reliable information on authorship, date or transmission could be confirmed. The title is generic (saṃgraha = anthology/digest), and it may be a small or recently edited text not well represented in available indological references.

SA_GP10_bhp_01u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 1
SA_GP10_bhp_01u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_01u.date_estimate: composite; core commonly c. 9th-10th c. CE (proposals range 6th-10th c.); project hint c. 900-1000
SA_GP10_bhp_01u.summary: Book 1 (Prathama Skandha) of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, the foremost Vaiṣṇava purāṇa. The first skandha sets the frame: the sages at Naimiṣāraṇya question Sūta; Vyāsa's dissatisfaction and Nārada's counsel; the testing and protection of Parīkṣit, and his being cursed to die in seven days, which sets up the recitation that follows.
SA_GP10_bhp_01u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, the Bhāgavata is a composite work whose final form is generally dated to the early medieval period (often 9th-10th c. CE), possibly with South Indian (Tamil bhakti) influences. It became the central scripture of Kṛṣṇa devotion and the basis of numerous commentaries (e.g., Śrīdhara, Vallabha) and vernacular reworkings.

SA_GP10_bhp_02u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 2
SA_GP10_bhp_02u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_02u.date_estimate: composite; core commonly c. 9th-10th c. CE (proposals range 6th-10th c.)
SA_GP10_bhp_02u.summary: Book 2 (Dvitīya Skandha) of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa. Śuka instructs the dying king Parīkṣit on what one about to die should hear and meditate upon, expounding the cosmic form (virāṭ-rūpa) of the Lord, methods of meditation and yogic departure, the process of creation, and a summary of the purāṇa's themes (the ten characteristic topics).
SA_GP10_bhp_02u.history: Part of the composite Bhāgavata Purāṇa traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, with its received form usually placed in the early medieval period. The second skandha lays out the theological and cosmological framework that the later skandhas (especially the tenth, on Kṛṣṇa) elaborate; it is heavily commented upon in the Vaiṣṇava traditions.

SA_GP10_bhp_03u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 3
SA_GP10_bhp_03u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_03u.tradition: Hindu (Vaishnava; Bhakti, with strong Sāṅkhya influence)
SA_GP10_bhp_03u.date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; some elements as early as 6th c.; disputed)
SA_GP10_bhp_03u.summary: The third book (skandha) of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, framed largely as a dialogue between Vidura and the sage Maitreya. It expounds the cosmogony of the universe out of the un-manifest, the appearance of the Puruṣa, and the secondary creation, culminating in the celebrated discourse of the avatāra Kapila to his mother Devahūti on Sāṅkhya philosophy and devotion (bhakti-yoga).
SA_GP10_bhp_03u.history: The Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, one of the most influential and theologically refined of the Mahāpurāṇas, is traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but is generally regarded by scholars as a South Indian composition of roughly the 9th-10th century CE. Canto 3 is notable for integrating classical Sāṅkhya into a devotional Vaishnava frame and attracted major commentaries such as Śrīdhara Svāmin's Bhāvārtha-dīpikā.

SA_GP10_bhp_04u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 4
SA_GP10_bhp_04u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_04u.date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed, possibly from 6th c.)
SA_GP10_bhp_04u.summary: The fourth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, continuing the theme of secondary creation through genealogies and exemplary narratives. It recounts the story of Dakṣa and his sacrifice, the destruction of the sacrifice after Satī's self-immolation, the devotion of the child Dhruva, the righteous king Pṛthu, and the allegory of King Purañjana illustrating the soul's bondage in the body.
SA_GP10_bhp_04u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE in South India. Canto 4's exemplary tales (Dhruva, Pṛthu) became staples of Vaishnava devotional teaching and were widely commented on, notably by Śrīdhara Svāmin.

SA_GP10_bhp_05u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 5
SA_GP10_bhp_05u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_05u.summary: The fifth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, containing the story of the renunciant king Ṛṣabha and the avadhūta-sage Jaḍa Bharata, followed by the work's detailed cosmography (bhuvana-kośa): the structure of the earth-disc (Jambūdvīpa and the surrounding continents), the heavenly bodies, the subterranean realms (pātāla), and the various hells (naraka).
SA_GP10_bhp_05u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. Canto 5 is the principal repository of the Purāṇa's traditional Hindu cosmology and geography and is frequently cited in later cosmographical literature.

SA_GP10_bhp_06u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 6
SA_GP10_bhp_06u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_06u.summary: The sixth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, emphasizing the redemptive power of devotion and the divine name. It includes the story of the sinful Ajāmila saved by uttering 'Nārāyaṇa' at death, the protective Nārāyaṇa-kavaca hymn, the curse of Dakṣa, the birth of the Maruts, and the cosmic battle in which the gods slay the demon Vṛtra with the aid of the sage Dadhīci's bones.
SA_GP10_bhp_06u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Ajāmila episode and Nārāyaṇa-kavaca became central proof-texts in Vaishnava theology of nāma-bhakti (devotion through the divine name).

SA_GP10_bhp_07u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 7
SA_GP10_bhp_07u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_07u.summary: The seventh book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, centered on the story of the demon-king Hiraṇyakaśipu, his devout son Prahlāda, and Viṣṇu's incarnation as Narasiṃha (the man-lion) who tears the tyrant apart to protect the devotee. The canto also includes extended teaching on bhakti and on the duties of the varṇas and āśramas (varṇāśrama-dharma).
SA_GP10_bhp_07u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Prahlāda-Narasiṃha narrative is among the most celebrated of all bhakti stories and underlies the popular Holi/Holika festival traditions.

SA_GP10_bhp_08u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 8
SA_GP10_bhp_08u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_08u.summary: The eighth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, organized around the successive Manus (manvantaras) and several of Viṣṇu's incarnations. It contains the rescue of the elephant Gajendra, the churning of the ocean (samudra-manthana) yielding the nectar of immortality and the Mohinī episode, and the story of the dwarf avatāra Vāmana who subdues the generous demon-king Bali.
SA_GP10_bhp_08u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Gajendra-mokṣa prayer and the churning-of-the-ocean myth from this canto are among the most widely recited and depicted episodes in Vaishnava tradition.

SA_GP10_bhp_09u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 9
SA_GP10_bhp_09u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_09u.summary: The ninth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, presenting the royal genealogies of the Solar (sūrya-vaṃśa) and Lunar (candra-vaṃśa) dynasties. It narrates kings and sages such as Sudyumna, Saubhari, Sagara, and Rāma (a condensed Rāma-kathā), tracing the lineages down to the Yadu dynasty and thereby setting the stage for the advent of Kṛṣṇa in the following canto.
SA_GP10_bhp_09u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The canto functions as the dynastic-historical bridge (vaṃśānucarita) leading to the Kṛṣṇa narrative of Canto 10.

SA_GP10_bhp_10u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 10
SA_GP10_bhp_10u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_10u.summary: The tenth and longest book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, devoted entirely to the life of Kṛṣṇa: his birth in Mathurā, childhood in Vraja, the subduing of demons, the lifting of Mount Govardhana, the love-play (rāsa-līlā) with the gopīs, the slaying of Kaṃsa, and his later career as prince of Dvārakā. It is the theological and devotional heart of the whole Purāṇa.
SA_GP10_bhp_10u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. Canto 10 is the single most influential source for Kṛṣṇa-bhakti, foundational to Vaishnava movements such as Gauḍīya, Vallabha, and Nimbārka traditions, and the subject of innumerable commentaries and vernacular renderings.

SA_GP10_bhp_11u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 11
SA_GP10_bhp_11u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_11u.summary: The eleventh book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, recounting the destruction of the Yadu dynasty through a brahmin curse and Kṛṣṇa's final teachings before his departure from the world. Its core is the Uddhava-gītā, an extended philosophical and devotional discourse by Kṛṣṇa to his friend Uddhava on jñāna, bhakti, yoga, and renunciation, often regarded as a counterpart to the Bhagavad-gītā.
SA_GP10_bhp_11u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Uddhava-gītā (also called Haṃsa-gītā) of this canto is studied as an independent devotional-philosophical text within Vaishnava traditions.

SA_GP10_bhp_12u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 12
SA_GP10_bhp_12u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhp_12u.summary: The twelfth and final book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, prophesying the degeneration of dharma in the Kali Yuga and describing future dynasties, the periodic dissolutions (pralaya) of the universe, and the death of King Parīkṣit by snakebite. It closes the frame narrative, gives an account of the divisions of the Vedas and the Purāṇas, and offers a concluding summary glorifying the text itself.
SA_GP10_bhp_12u.history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Kali-yuga prophecies of this canto are frequently quoted in discussions of Hindu eschatology.

SA_GP10_bhpc101u.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 10, 29–33
SA_GP10_bhpc101u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP10_bhpc101u.summary: Chapters 29-33 of the tenth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, known as the Rāsa-pañcādhyāyī ('five chapters on the rāsa dance'). They narrate Kṛṣṇa's autumn-night circle-dance of divine love (rāsa-līlā) with the cowherd women (gopīs) of Vraja, including their longing during his disappearance and their lament, the Gopī-gīta. This passage is widely treated as the devotional and theological climax of the entire Purāṇa.
SA_GP10_bhpc101u.history: An excerpt of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa Canto 10, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa and scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Rāsa-pañcādhyāyī has attracted dense allegorical and theological commentary from Vaishnava ācāryas (e.g. Śrīdhara, the Gauḍīya commentators), who read the gopīs' love as the supreme model of devotion.

SA_GP11_nmtppu.title: Nīlamata-Purāṇa
SA_GP11_nmtppu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GP11_nmtppu.tradition: Hindu (regional; Naga and Shaiva-Vaishnava cults of Kashmir)
SA_GP11_nmtppu.date_estimate: c. 6th-8th c. CE (with later additions; not after c. 1200 CE)
SA_GP11_nmtppu.summary: A regional Purāṇa of Kashmir presented as the sage Bṛhadaśva's instruction to King Janamejaya on the origins and sacred order of the Kashmir valley. It recounts the draining of the primeval lake by Viṣṇu and the establishment of the Nāga Nīla's worship, and prescribes the valley's tīrthas (pilgrimage sites), festivals, and ritual calendar, incorporating indigenous Nāga and Piśāca traditions.
SA_GP11_nmtppu.history: The Nīlamata is the principal early source for the religious geography and customs of Kashmir, drawn on heavily by Kalhaṇa in the 12th-century Rājataraṅgiṇī. Scholars (e.g. Ved Kumari Ghai) date its core to roughly the 6th-8th century CE, with later interpolations; it was edited and translated in modern times by Ved Kumari Ghai.

SA_GP12_brndp1_u.title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 1
SA_GP12_brndp1_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_brndp1_u.date_estimate: core c. 4th-6th c. CE; reached present form c. 1000 CE (disputed)
SA_GP12_brndp1_u.summary: The first section (Pūrvabhāga / Prakriyā- and Anuṣaṅga-pāda) of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa, the Purāṇa named for its account of the 'cosmic egg' (brahmāṇḍa). This part sets the frame of the recitation and treats the classic Puranic topics of cosmogony, the measures of time and the kalpas, cosmography, and the manvantaras.
SA_GP12_brndp1_u.history: The Brahmāṇḍa is one of the oldest Mahāpurāṇas, closely related to (and once forming a unit with) the Vāyu-Purāṇa; its core is dated to roughly the 4th-6th century CE while the text is generally thought to have reached its present arrangement around 1000 CE (Rocher). It is famous for transmitting the Lalitā-māhātmya and Lalitā-sahasranāma in its later portion.

SA_GP12_brndp2_u.title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 2
SA_GP12_brndp2_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_brndp2_u.date_estimate: core c. 4th-6th c. CE; reached present form c. 1000 CE (disputed)
SA_GP12_brndp2_u.summary: The middle section (Madhyabhāga / Upodghāta-pāda) of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa. It continues the Puranic exposition with royal genealogies and dynastic accounts (vaṃśa and vaṃśānucarita), sage lineages, geographical lists, and supplementary mythological narratives.
SA_GP12_brndp2_u.history: Part of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa, one of the oldest Mahāpurāṇas, closely linked to the Vāyu-Purāṇa. Core dated to roughly the 4th-6th century CE with the present structure achieved about 1000 CE and continual later revision.

SA_GP12_brndp3_u.title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 3
SA_GP12_brndp3_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_brndp3_u.date_estimate: core c. 4th-6th c. CE; Lalitā material later (post-1000 CE); disputed
SA_GP12_brndp3_u.summary: The latter section (Uttarabhāga / Upasaṃhāra-pāda) of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa. It concludes the work and is celebrated for containing the Lalitopākhyāna (Lalitā-māhātmya), the story of the goddess Lalitā Tripurasundarī's destruction of the demon Bhaṇḍāsura, together with the famous Lalitā-sahasranāma, a centerpiece of Śrīvidyā goddess worship.
SA_GP12_brndp3_u.history: Part of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa; while the Purāṇa's core is early (4th-6th century CE), the Lalitā material in this section reflects later Śākta/Śrīvidyā tradition and is generally considered a comparatively late addition. The Lalitā-sahasranāma transmitted here is among the most widely recited goddess hymns.

SA_GP12_dbhp_dgu.title: Devīgītā [Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa]
SA_GP12_dbhp_dgu.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_dbhp_dgu.date_estimate: Devībhāgavata c. 11th-12th c. CE (range 9th-14th c.); Devīgītā within it
SA_GP12_dbhp_dgu.summary: The Devī-gītā ('Song of the Goddess') comprises the final ten chapters of the seventh skandha of the Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa (7.31-40, c. 507 verses). It is a dialogue in which the Great Goddess (Mahādevī/Bhuvaneśvarī) reveals to the mountain-king Himavān her supreme nature as the source of the cosmos and teaches the path to union with her through knowledge, yoga, devotion, and her ritual worship.
SA_GP12_dbhp_dgu.history: The Devī-gītā is the Śākta counterpart to the Bhagavad-gītā, embedded in the Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa, a goddess-centered Purāṇa generally dated to roughly the 11th-12th century CE (scholarly range 9th-14th c.) that builds on the earlier Devī-māhātmya. It is studied and recited as an independent devotional-philosophical text in Śākta traditions.

SA_GP12_garup2_u.title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa 2
SA_GP12_garup2_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_garup2_u.date_estimate: core (Preta-khaṇḍa) c. 1st millennium CE, by c. 900 CE; whole work continually expanded
SA_GP12_garup2_u.summary: The second book of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, the Preta-khaṇḍa (or Dharma-kāṇḍa), dealing with death, the fate of the soul after death, the journey through the realms of Yama, the various hells, funerary and post-funerary rites (śrāddha), and the means of attaining a good afterlife. It is the section commonly recited in connection with Hindu death rituals.
SA_GP12_garup2_u.history: Part of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, framed as Viṣṇu's discourse to his mount Garuḍa and traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. The Preta-khaṇḍa is regarded as the early core of the Purāṇa; the work as a whole is encyclopedic and was continually expanded through the early second millennium, with the core around the 9th-10th century CE. This eschatological section is the best-known and most-used part of the text in popular practice.

SA_GP12_garup3_u.title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa 3
SA_GP12_garup3_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_garup3_u.date_estimate: Brahma-khaṇḍa relatively late, by c. 10th c. CE; whole work continually expanded
SA_GP12_garup3_u.summary: The third book of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, the Brahma-khaṇḍa (or Mokṣa-kāṇḍa), which treats liberation (mokṣa), the nature of Brahman, devotion, and salvific knowledge. It is the shortest of the three sections and shows the influence of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa.
SA_GP12_garup3_u.history: Part of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Scholars regard the Brahma-khaṇḍa as the latest of the three sections because of its Bhāgavata influence, though still not later than about the 10th century CE; the Purāṇa as a whole is an encyclopedic, continually revised text.

SA_GP12_kurmp1_u.title: Kūrma-Purāṇa 1
SA_GP12_kurmp1_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_kurmp1_u.tradition: Hindu (Shaiva-Vaishnava synthesis; Pāñcarātra and Pāśupata material)
SA_GP12_kurmp1_u.date_estimate: c. 7th-9th c. CE (core; range 6th-10th c., disputed)
SA_GP12_kurmp1_u.summary: The first part (Pūrvabhāga) of the Kūrma-Purāṇa, narrated by Viṣṇu in his tortoise (kūrma) incarnation. It covers cosmogony and the churning of the ocean, cosmography and sacred geography, genealogies and manvantaras, devotional accounts of both Viṣṇu and the Goddess, and extensive tīrtha-māhātmyas (glorifications of pilgrimage sites such as Vārāṇasī and Prayāga).
SA_GP12_kurmp1_u.history: The Kūrma-Purāṇa is notable for blending Vaishnava, Śaiva (Pāśupata), and Śākta material and is thought to have been reworked by Pāśupata redactors; its surviving form is generally dated to roughly the 7th-9th century CE. It survives in a recension significantly different from older quoted versions, indicating considerable revision.

SA_GP12_kurmp2_u.title: Kūrma-Purāṇa 2
SA_GP12_kurmp2_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_kurmp2_u.summary: The second part (Uttarabhāga / Uttaravibhāga) of the Kūrma-Purāṇa. It contains teaching on dharma, the āśramas, and ritual, and is especially noted for embedding the Īśvara-gītā, an eleven-chapter philosophical discourse modeled on the Bhagavad-gītā but with Śiva as the supreme speaker, integrating Yoga and non-dualist Vedānta with Pāśupata theology.
