SA_GP12_kurmp2_u.history: Part of the Kūrma-Purāṇa, generally dated to roughly the 7th-9th century CE and associated with Pāśupata redaction. The Īśvara-gītā of this section is an important independent text for the philosophy of devotional Śaivism and the synthesis of Sāṅkhya-Yoga with Vedānta.

SA_GP12_lip_1_pu.title: Liṅga-Purāṇa 1
SA_GP12_lip_1_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_lip_1_pu.date_estimate: core c. 6th-10th c. CE (composite, with later additions)
SA_GP12_lip_1_pu.summary: The first part (pūrvabhāga) of the Liṅga-Purāṇa, one of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas and a major Śaiva text centered on the worship of Śiva in his aniconic form as the liṅga. It treats cosmogony, the manifestation and glory of Śiva, the origin of the liṅga, sacred geography, yogic and devotional practice, vows, and the standard Puranic topics of creation, genealogies, and pilgrimage.
SA_GP12_lip_1_pu.history: Authorship and exact date are unknown; the text is a layered, composite compilation revised over several centuries between roughly the 6th and 10th centuries CE, with possible medieval interpolations. It exists in multiple recensions and is a key source for the theology of liṅga worship and the Pāśupata-influenced Śaiva tradition.

SA_GP12_lip_2__u.title: Liṅga-Purāṇa 2,1–55
SA_GP12_lip_2__u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_lip_2__u.date_estimate: core c. 6th-10th c. CE (composite, with later additions)
SA_GP12_lip_2__u.summary: Chapters 1–55 of the second part (uttarabhāga) of the Liṅga-Purāṇa, continuing the Śaiva subject matter of the work. This portion expands on the glorification of Śiva, ritual and devotional worship of the liṅga, narratives of Śiva's deeds, sacred sites, and instructions on vows, gifts, and observances.
SA_GP12_lip_2__u.history: Part of the same composite Śaiva Mahāpurāṇa as the first part; the uttarabhāga is generally considered a somewhat later stratum within the text's centuries-long redactional history. Transmitted in multiple recensions and printed editions.

SA_GP12_mkp1-93u.title: Mārkaṇḍeya-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_mkp1-93u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_mkp1-93u.date_estimate: core c. 3rd-6th c. CE; Devīmāhātmya layer c. 5th-6th c. CE (composite)
SA_GP12_mkp1-93u.summary: Chapters 1–93 of the Mārkaṇḍeya-Purāṇa, one of the oldest of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, framed as a dialogue involving the sage Mārkaṇḍeya. It covers cosmogony, dharma, accounts of yoga and rebirth, royal genealogies (the Vaivasvata Manu), and famously embeds the Devīmāhātmya (Durgā-saptaśatī), the foundational text of Goddess worship celebrating the Devī's victory over the demons Madhu-Kaiṭabha, Mahiṣāsura, and Śumbha-Niśumbha.
SA_GP12_mkp1-93u.history: Considered one of the earliest Puranas, with an old narrative core perhaps from the early centuries CE; the Devīmāhātmya section (chapters 81–93) is a later but highly influential interpolation (c. 5th-6th c. CE) central to Śākta tradition. The not_after hint of c. 600 CE accords with the dating of this Goddess-hymn layer.

SA_GP12_mtp176pu.title: Matsya-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_mtp176pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_mtp176pu.date_estimate: core c. 3rd-5th c. CE, with material to c. 6th-10th c. CE (composite)
SA_GP12_mtp176pu.summary: One of the oldest of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, narrated by Viṣṇu in his Matsya (fish) avatāra to King Manu during the cosmic deluge. It is encyclopedic, covering cosmogony and dissolution, royal and divine genealogies, the churning of the ocean, accounts of avatāras, temple and image construction, vows and gifts (dāna), pilgrimage (especially Prayāga and Vārāṇasī), and statecraft.
SA_GP12_mtp176pu.history: Regarded as among the earliest Purāṇas with an old core, though expanded over centuries; it preserves relatively archaic Puranic material and important dynastic king-lists used by historians. The work_id 176 likely denotes a particular chapter range or section within the larger text.

SA_GP12_nardp1_u.title: Nārada-Purāṇa 1 [Nāradīya-Purāṇa 1]
SA_GP12_nardp1_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_nardp1_u.date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (pūrvabhāga; composite, with later additions)
SA_GP12_nardp1_u.summary: The first part (pūrvabhāga) of the Nārada-Purāṇa (also called Nāradīya-Purāṇa), one of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, strongly Vaiṣṇava and bhakti-oriented and presented through the teachings of the sage Nārada. It deals with dharma, devotion to Viṣṇu, vows and festivals, the methods of liberation, and notably contains digests on subjects such as grammar, prosody, astronomy/astrology, and a famous summary of the contents of all eighteen Mahāpurāṇas.
SA_GP12_nardp1_u.history: A relatively late Mahāpurāṇa whose pūrvabhāga is usually dated to the early medieval period (c. 9th-11th c. CE); it is valued for its self-referential descriptions of the Puranic corpus and for its encyclopedic treatment of ancillary sciences. Transmitted in two main bhāgas.

SA_GP12_nardp2_u.title: Nārada-Purāṇa 2 [Nāradīya-Purāṇa 2]
SA_GP12_nardp2_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_nardp2_u.summary: The second part (uttarabhāga) of the Nārada-Purāṇa, continuing the Vaiṣṇava and bhakti themes of the work. It includes the Rukmāṅgada-carita (the story of King Rukmāṅgada and the Ekādaśī vow), extensive treatment of pilgrimage sites (tīrthas), the merits of devotional observances, and instruction on attaining liberation through devotion to Viṣṇu.
SA_GP12_nardp2_u.history: The uttarabhāga is generally regarded as a later stratum of the Nārada-Purāṇa than the pūrvabhāga and is noted for its strong emphasis on the Ekādaśī vow and tīrtha-māhātmya material; some sections show late medieval expansion.

SA_GP12_narsippu.title: Narasiṃha-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_narsippu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GP12_narsippu.date_estimate: c. 5th-8th c. CE (disputed; possibly earlier core)
SA_GP12_narsippu.summary: A Vaiṣṇava Upapurāṇa centered on the worship of Viṣṇu, especially in his man-lion (Narasiṃha) avatāra who slays the demon Hiraṇyakaśipu to protect the devotee Prahlāda. It treats cosmogony, the avatāras of Viṣṇu, dharma and vows, genealogies of solar and lunar dynasties, and devotional and eschatological teaching.
SA_GP12_narsippu.history: Classed among the Upapurāṇas; it is relatively early among them, with some scholars (e.g., R. C. Hazra) dating its core to roughly the 5th c. CE while others place it later. It is an important source for early Vaiṣṇava theology surrounding the Narasiṃha avatāra.

SA_GP12_sivap7_u.title: Śiva-Purāṇa 7 [Vāyavīya-Saṃhitā]
SA_GP12_sivap7_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_sivap7_u.summary: The Vāyavīya-Saṃhitā, the seventh saṃhitā (section) of the Śiva-Purāṇa, one of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas devoted to Śiva. Narrated in the voice of Vāyu, it expounds Śaiva cosmology, the supremacy of Śiva, the nature of the liṅga, devotional and yogic practice, the Pāśupata teachings, ritual worship, and the path to liberation through Śiva.
SA_GP12_sivap7_u.history: The Śiva-Purāṇa as transmitted is a composite of multiple saṃhitās compiled at different periods; the Vāyavīya-Saṃhitā (in two parts, pūrva and uttara) overlaps substantially with material in the Vāyu-Purāṇa and reflects the medieval consolidation of the Śaiva Puranic corpus. Several recensions of the Śiva-Purāṇa circulate.

SA_GP12_skprevku.title: Skanda-Purāṇa: Revākhaṇḍa
SA_GP12_skprevku.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_skprevku.date_estimate: c. 12th-15th c. CE (composite; manuscripts as late as 17th c.)
SA_GP12_skprevku.summary: The Revākhaṇḍa, a section devoted to the glorification (māhātmya) of the Narmadā River (Revā) and its many tīrthas (pilgrimage sites) along its course, here transmitted within the printed Skanda-Purāṇa. It narrates the sanctity, origin myths, and pilgrimage rewards of bathing and worship at the sacred fords of the Narmadā.
SA_GP12_skprevku.history: Scholars note that the lengthy Revā/Reva Khaṇḍa (c. 232 chapters) printed in the Skanda-Purāṇa by the Veṅkaṭeśvara Press (1910) is in fact the Revākhaṇḍa originally belonging to the Vāyu-Purāṇa, mistakenly incorporated into the Skanda; the genuine Skanda recension is shorter (c. 116 chapters). The only surviving manuscript of one Revākhaṇḍa recension dates to 1682.

SA_GP12_vamp__u.title: Vāmana-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_vamp__u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_vamp__u.summary: One of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, nominally centered on Viṣṇu's dwarf (Vāmana) avatāra who subdues the demon-king Bali, though much of its content is in fact strongly Śaiva and Śākta. It covers cosmogony and sacred geography, myths of Śiva and the Goddess, pilgrimage (especially Kurukṣetra and Prayāga), vows, and the standard Puranic topics.
SA_GP12_vamp__u.history: Dated to roughly the early medieval period; the surviving text differs considerably from older descriptions of a 'Vāmana-Purāṇa' and circulates in more than one recension (notably a 'Saromāhātmya' recension). It blends Vaiṣṇava, Śaiva, and Śākta material, reflecting its layered composition.

SA_GP12_vampsm_u.title: Vāmana-Purāṇa [Saromāhātmya recension]
SA_GP12_vampsm_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_vampsm_u.summary: A second transmission of the Vāmana-Purāṇa, distinguished by its 'sm' suffix which most plausibly designates the Saromāhātmya recension/appendix — a glorification of sacred lakes (saras) and tīrthas associated with the Vāmana-Purāṇa. Like the main text it combines the Vāmana avatāra narrative with substantial Śaiva and tīrtha-māhātmya material.
SA_GP12_vampsm_u.history: The Vāmana-Purāṇa survives in distinct recensions; this entry, sharing the same title as the preceding record, evidently captures a separate (likely Saromāhātmya) recension or appended section. Identification of the exact recension is somewhat uncertain.

SA_GP12_vd2127_u.title: Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa: Atharvavidhikathana [Atharvavidhāna]
SA_GP12_vd2127_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GP12_vd2127_u.summary: A section of the Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa (an Upapurāṇa appended to the Viṣṇu-Purāṇa) treating the 'Atharvavidhāna' — the prescription of Atharvavedic rites, mantras, and ritual procedures, here spanning chapters around 2.127 (khaṇḍa 2). It presents the application of Atharvaveda-based ritual, expiation, and apotropaic practice within the Purana's encyclopedic framework.
SA_GP12_vd2127_u.history: The Viṣṇudharmottara is a vast, encyclopedic Upapurāṇa in three khaṇḍas, best known for the Citrasūtra section on painting; its core is generally dated to roughly the 5th-7th c. CE. This Atharvavidhāna portion reflects the text's incorporation of Vedic-ritual and śāstric subject matter.

SA_GP12_vd3343pu.title: Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_vd3343pu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GP12_vd3343pu.summary: A portion of the third khaṇḍa of the Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa (around chapters 33–43), which most famously contains the Citrasūtra — a foundational treatise on the theory and practice of painting (citra), as well as related material on iconography, image-making, dance, music, and the arts. The work is an encyclopedic Upapurāṇa attached to the Viṣṇu-Purāṇa.
SA_GP12_vd3343pu.history: The Viṣṇudharmottara is celebrated by art historians for the Citrasūtra (3.35–43), the earliest surviving Indian text on painting, and for its iconographic prescriptions; the third khaṇḍa is a principal source for classical Indian art theory. The core is usually dated to about the 5th-7th c. CE.

SA_GP12_visnup_u.title: Viṣṇu-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_visnup_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Parāśara)
SA_GP12_visnup_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-5th c. CE (core; disputed, possibly earlier)
SA_GP12_visnup_u.summary: One of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas and a model 'pañcalakṣaṇa' Purana, framed as the teaching of the sage Parāśara to Maitreya. It is the central Vaiṣṇava Purana, treating cosmogony and cosmic dissolution, genealogies of gods and kings, the deeds and avatāras of Viṣṇu (including the life of Kṛṣṇa), geography, dharma, and the four yugas, with a strongly devotional and Vaiṣṇava-theistic orientation.
SA_GP12_visnup_u.history: Often regarded as one of the oldest and most systematically structured Mahāpurāṇas, with its core frequently dated to the Gupta period (c. 4th-5th c. CE), though parts may be earlier or later. It received the influential commentary of Śrīdhara Svāmin and is foundational for the Vaiṣṇava tradition.

SA_GP12_vprevk_u.title: Revākhaṇḍa of the Vāyupurāṇa
SA_GP12_vprevk_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_vprevk_u.summary: The Revākhaṇḍa belonging to the Vāyu-Purāṇa, a large (c. 232-chapter) glorification of the Narmadā (Revā) River and its pilgrimage sites, narrating the river's sanctity, origin myths, and the merits of bathing and worship at its many tīrthas. This is the original Vāyu-Purāṇa Revākhaṇḍa, distinct from (though often conflated with) the shorter one in the Skanda-Purāṇa.
SA_GP12_vprevk_u.history: Modern scholarship holds that the long Revākhaṇḍa printed within the Skanda-Purāṇa (Veṅkaṭeśvara Press, 1910) actually originates in the Vāyu-Purāṇa and was wrongly attached to the Skanda; this record reflects that genuine Vāyu-Purāṇa attribution. It is an important Narmadā tīrtha-māhātmya.

SA_GR12_hsasvrpu.title: Śāstravārtāsamuccaya
SA_GR12_hsasvrpu.author: Haribhadrasūri
SA_GR12_hsasvrpu.summary: A Jain philosophical work of some 700 verses surveying, examining, and critically engaging the doctrines of the major non-Jain schools and sects — including Buddhist, Sāṃkhya, Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, Mīmāṃsā, materialist, and theistic positions — refuting and reconciling them while upholding the Jain (anekānta) standpoint, treated with notable respect toward opponents. Haribhadra furnished it with his own auto-commentary, the Dikpradā (c. 2250 verses).
SA_GR12_hsasvrpu.history: Traditionally Haribhadra was placed c. 459-529 CE, but on the basis of his familiarity with Dharmakīrti, modern scholars (following Jinavijaya, 1919) date him after c. 650, and the Śāstravārtāsamuccaya is commonly regarded as an 8th-century work — consistent with the project's not_before/not_after hints of 700-799. It is a key Jain doxographical and inclusivist text.

SA_GR12_sasapa.title: Satyaśāsanaparīkṣā
SA_GR12_sasapa.author: Vidyānanda (Vidyānandin)
SA_GR12_sasapa.summary: An 'Investigation into the True Teaching,' a Digambara Jain philosophical work that presents and refutes twelve rival Indian philosophical systems — including Puruṣādvaita (Advaita Vedānta), Śabdādvaita (grammarians), Vijñānādvaita (Yogācāra), Citrādvaita, Cārvāka, Sautrāntika Buddhism, theistic and non-theistic Sāṃkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Nyāya, and Bhāṭṭa and Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā — in order to establish Jainism as the sole 'true teaching.'
SA_GR12_sasapa.history: Composed by the Digambara logician Vidyānandin (Vidyānanda), generally placed in the 9th-10th c. CE. Known since around 1920 but long understudied, it was first edited by Gokul Chandra Jain (1964); Jens W. Borgland produced a critical edition and study (Harrassowitz, 2020). It is an important source for late first-millennium inter-school polemic.

SA_GR13_bhnstcpu.title: Stavacintāmaṇi
SA_GR13_bhnstcpu.author: Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa
SA_GR13_bhnstcpu.date_estimate: c. 9th c. CE (between Vasugupta and Somānanda)
SA_GR13_bhnstcpu.summary: The 'Wish-fulfilling Jewel of Praise,' a hymn of about 120 verses addressed to Śiva (Paramaśiva) as the Supreme Reality. Highly devotional yet philosophically profound, its sustained theme is that love and devotion to Śiva are everything, expressing the non-dual (advaita) recognition of individual consciousness as one with universal Śiva-consciousness.
SA_GR13_bhnstcpu.history: Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa is placed in the formative period of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism, between Vasugupta and Somānanda (roughly 9th c. CE). The text was commented upon by Kṣemarāja, and the Sanskrit was first published with his commentary in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies (vol. X, 1918).

SA_GR13_bijnighu.title: Bījanighaṇṭu
SA_GR13_bijnighu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Bhairava)
SA_GR13_bijnighu.summary: A tantric lexicon (nighaṇṭu) that decodes and lists the bīja (seed) syllables used in mantras, giving the conventional code-names by which the individual phonemes/seeds are designated in tantric ritual literature. It functions as a reference key enabling practitioners to reconstruct mantras written in coded form.
SA_GR13_bijnighu.history: Traditionally framed as spoken by Bhairava, it is one of the dictionary-texts collected in the Tantrābhidhāna ('Dictionaries of Tantra Śāstra') edited in the Tantric Texts Series by Arthur Avalon (John Woodroffe), alongside the Mātṛkānighaṇṭu, Mudrānighaṇṭu, and similar works. Its precise date and authorship are unknown.

SA_GR13_cakbh_pu.title: Cakrapāṇinātha: Bhāvopahārastotra
SA_GR13_cakbh_pu.author: Cakrapāṇinātha
SA_GR13_cakbh_pu.summary: The Bhāvopahāra ('The Offering of Inner States/Flowers to Śiva'), a Trika hymn to Śiva in which all of one's mental states, sense-activities, and experiences are offered up as worship, expressing the non-dual recognition that every act of consciousness is itself an oblation to Śiva. It is a concise but philosophically dense devotional work of Kashmir Śaivism.
SA_GR13_cakbh_pu.history: The author names himself only obliquely as 'someone'; the commentator Ramyadeva Bhaṭṭa, in the Bhāvopahāravivaraṇa, identifies him through wordplay as Cakrapāṇinātha (or the Lord who holds the wheel). The hymn is generally dated to the 11th century CE and belongs to the non-dual Trika stream of Kashmir Śaivism.

SA_GR13_cakbhavu.title: Cakrapāṇinātha: Bhāvopahārastotra
SA_GR13_cakbhavu.author: Cakrapāṇinātha
SA_GR13_cakbhavu.summary: A Sanskrit hymn to Śiva in the Trika (non-dual Kashmir Shaiva) idiom, whose title means roughly 'The Offering [Made] with One's Inner States/Being.' Rather than external worship, the poem reinterprets the elements of ritual offering as inner, contemplative acts, presenting the worshipper's own states of consciousness and devotion as the true oblation to Śiva.
SA_GR13_cakbhavu.history: The author conceals his own name in the closing verse, where the commentator Ramyadeva Bhaṭṭa (Rāmyadeva), who wrote the Bhāvopahāravivaraṇa, reads it as either 'Cakrapāṇinātha' or as an epithet of the Lord who holds the discus. The hymn was edited and translated into Italian/English by Enrica Garzilli and is associated with Kashmirian Trika devotional poetry.

SA_GR13_devikapu.title: Devīkālottara-Āgama
SA_GR13_devikapu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_devikapu.date_estimate: c. 7th-9th c. CE (one of several recensions of the Kālottara)
SA_GR13_devikapu.summary: One of the many recensions of the Kālottara, a body of early Śaiva scripture, cast as a dialogue in which Śiva imparts liberating wisdom to the Goddess (Īśvarī/Devī). This particular recension emphasizes jñāna (knowledge) and yogic-meditative practice leading to mokṣa rather than elaborate ritual, and it became influential in later Advaita-flavored Śaiva spirituality (e.g. it was translated/commented upon in Ramana Maharshi's circle).
SA_GR13_devikapu.history: The Kālottara circulated in numerous recensions of differing verse-lengths (e.g. the Śatika/hundred-verse and two-hundred-verse versions); the Devīkālottara is the recension framed as Śiva's teaching to the Goddess. Its terse verses on the nature of consciousness and liberation gave it lasting devotional and philosophical reception in South India.

SA_GR13_goramaru.title: Āmaraughaśāsana
SA_GR13_goramaru.author: attributed to Gorakṣanātha (Gorakhnāth)
SA_GR13_goramaru.summary: A short Sanskrit yoga work in the orbit of the Amaraugha ('Lineage/Flow of the Immortals') textual cluster attributed to Gorakṣanātha, teaching haṭha-yogic techniques (kuṇḍalinī, breath, the union of Śakti and Śiva) as the means to attain rājayoga. It belongs to the formative phase in which earlier tantric/Buddhist physical practices were systematized for a Śaiva Nātha audience.
SA_GR13_goramaru.history: The Amaraugha and its recension the Amaraughaprabodha, recently critically edited by Jason Birch, are among the earliest texts to lay out the fourfold yoga culminating in rājayoga; the closely related Āmaraughaśāsana shares this material. Many of these verses were later absorbed into the influential Haṭhapradīpikā.

SA_GR13_gorst1pu.title: Gorakṣaśataka
SA_GR13_gorst1pu.author: attributed to Gorakṣanātha (Gorakhnāth)
SA_GR13_gorst1pu.summary: A foundational haṭha-yoga manual of the Nātha tradition, the 'Hundred Verses of Gorakṣa,' which outlines yogic limbs including āsana, prāṇāyāma, mudrā, the cakras and the raising of kuṇḍalinī (notably the technique of 'stimulating Sarasvatī'). The title 'Gorakṣaśataka' attaches to several overlapping texts; this is one of the transmitted versions/recensions in the corpus.
SA_GR13_gorst1pu.history: The work now often called Gorakṣaśataka is closely entangled with the Vivekamārtaṇḍa and the Gorakṣasaṃhitā; James Mallinson and others have shown that the original ~200-verse Vivekamārtaṇḍa was abridged/retitled to fit a hundred-verse 'śataka.' Though ascribed to Gorakṣanātha, the surviving texts postdate the historical figure.

SA_GR13_gorst2pu.title: Gorakṣaśataka
SA_GR13_gorst2pu.author: attributed to Gorakṣanātha (Gorakhnāth)
SA_GR13_gorst2pu.summary: A second transmitted recension of the 'Hundred Verses of Gorakṣa,' the Nātha haṭha-yoga manual teaching postures, breath control, mudrās, the subtle body and the awakening of kuṇḍalinī. The two GRETIL entries reflect the well-known fact that the Gorakṣaśataka survives in divergent versions of differing length and verse-order.
SA_GR13_gorst2pu.history: As with the parallel entry, the text is bound up with the Vivekamārtaṇḍa/Gorakṣasaṃhitā complex; more than two hundred manuscripts circulate under variant titles. The hundred-verse format is in part a secondary abridgement to match the name 'śataka.' Authorship is traditionally ascribed to Gorakṣanātha but the texts are later than him.

SA_GR13_jssivppu.title: Jñānaśambhu: Śivapūjāstava
SA_GR13_jssivppu.author: Jñānaśambhu (Jñānaśiva)
SA_GR13_jssivppu.summary: A Śaiva hymn/eulogy concerning the worship (pūjā) of Śiva, attributed to Jñānaśambhu, a Saiddhāntika teacher. It articulates, in praise-poetry form, the devotional and ritual adoration of Śiva characteristic of the Śaiva Siddhānta milieu.
SA_GR13_jssivppu.history: Jñānaśambhu (also Jñānaśiva) was a twelfth-century Saiddhāntika guru from the Cōḻa country who worked in Banaras and is best known for the ritual manual Jñānaratnāvalī; the Śivapūjāstava is a shorter hymnic work associated with him. Documentation of this specific stava is sparse, so the attribution rests on the manuscript/corpus tradition.
related_works: Jñānaratnāvalī (ritual manual by the same author)

SA_GR13_kalhans_u.title: Ardhanārīśvarastotra
SA_GR13_kalhans_u.author: attributed to Kalhaṇa
SA_GR13_kalhans_u.date_estimate: c. 12th c. CE (if by Kalhaṇa, author of the Rājataraṅgiṇī)
SA_GR13_kalhans_u.summary: A devotional hymn praising Śiva in his Ardhanārīśvara form, the androgynous deity who is half Śiva and half the Goddess Pārvatī, symbolizing the inseparable union of consciousness and energy (Śiva-Śakti). The verses dwell on the bicorporate divine image, contrasting and harmonizing the male and female halves.
SA_GR13_kalhans_u.history: The hymn is transmitted in some collections as 'kalhaṇakṛtam' (composed by Kalhaṇa), associating it with the famed twelfth-century Kashmirian chronicler of the Rājataraṅgiṇī, though such attributions of short stotras are not always secure. An Ardhanārīśvarastotra is also widely circulated under the name of Śaṅkarācārya, so the title attaches to more than one poem.

SA_GR13_karatopu.title: Karatoyāmāhātmya
SA_GR13_karatopu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR13_karatopu.summary: A māhātmya glorifying the Karatoyā river (in northern Bengal / present-day Bangladesh and West Bengal) as a sacred tīrtha, extolling the spiritual merit of bathing in and visiting it. It describes the prosperity, fertility and purity of the lands along its course and the religious rewards accruing to pilgrims.
SA_GR13_karatopu.history: The Karatoyā, once a great river now reduced to a stream, was celebrated for sanctity in the Mahābhārata and Purāṇas; the Karatoyāmāhātmya, composed sometime before the latter half of the twelfth century, is an important witness to the river's former greatness and to medieval Bengal's sacred geography.

SA_GR13_kirtc_pu.title: Kiraṇatantra
SA_GR13_kirtc_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_kirtc_pu.date_estimate: c. 6th-8th c. CE (early dualist Śaiva Siddhānta)
SA_GR13_kirtc_pu.summary: An early Śaiva Siddhānta scripture cast as a dialogue between Garuḍa (the 'ray,' kiraṇa) and Śiva, covering the canonical fourfold subject matter of doctrine (jñāna), yoga, ritual (kriyā) and conduct (caryā). It treats the metaphysics of Śiva, the bound soul, bondage (mala, karma, māyā), initiation and the path to liberation in the dualist Saiddhāntika framework. This is the 'c' recension/edition in the corpus.
SA_GR13_kirtc_pu.history: The Kiraṇatantra (Kiraṇāgama) is counted among the earliest revealed scriptures of the dualist Śaiva Siddhānta, alongside the Niśvāsa and Svāyambhuvasūtrasaṃgraha. Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha wrote an important commentary on its doctrinal portion, critically edited and translated by Dominic Goodall (1998).
related_works: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's commentary on the Kiraṇatantra

SA_GR13_kirtm_pu.title: Kiraṇatantra
SA_GR13_kirtm_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_kirtm_pu.date_estimate: c. 6th-8th c. CE (early dualist Śaiva Siddhānta)
SA_GR13_kirtm_pu.summary: A second transmitted recension/edition of the Kiraṇatantra (Kiraṇāgama), an early Saiddhāntika Śaiva scripture structured as Śiva's revelation and covering doctrine, yoga, ritual and conduct. The two GRETIL entries reflect distinct manuscript/edition traditions of the same scripture (e.g. a Kashmirian/Nepalese vs. a South Indian transmission).
SA_GR13_kirtm_pu.history: Like its parallel entry, this is the Kiraṇatantra known from the early dualist Śaiva Siddhānta canon, on whose doctrinal chapters Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha composed his commentary (edited by Goodall, 1998). It is frequently cited as a touchstone scripture by later Saiddhāntika authors.
related_works: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's commentary on the Kiraṇatantra

SA_GR13_kubjt_pu.title: Kubjikāmatatantra
SA_GR13_kubjt_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_kubjt_pu.summary: The root scripture (mata) of the Western transmission (Paścimāmnāya) of Kaula tantrism, centered on the goddess Kubjikā ('the crooked/hunchbacked one') and her consort. It expounds the cult's distinctive cosmology, the subtle body and kuṇḍalinī, mantra and the worship of Kubjikā as the embodiment of kuṇḍalinī energy.
SA_GR13_kubjt_pu.history: The Kubjikāmatatantra is the foundational text of the Kubjikā cult, which flourished especially in Nepal where its manuscripts were preserved. Text-critical work (e.g. by Goudriaan, Schoterman, and others) dates it to the ninth or tenth century; the label 'paścimāmnāya' for the tradition is largely a later development absent from the earliest sources.

SA_GR13_mabhedtu.title: Mātṛkābhedatantra
SA_GR13_mabhedtu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR13_mabhedtu.date_estimate: c. 13th-16th c. CE (commonly placed around the 13th c., probably not earlier)
SA_GR13_mabhedtu.summary: A later Śākta-Śaiva Kaula tantra dealing with the worship of Śiva and the Goddess, including mantra-śāstra and the 'mātṛkā' (the matrix of phonemes/letters). Notably, several of its chapters (the first, fifth and eighth) contain alchemical (rasaśāstra) material, especially on the fabrication of mercurial liṅgas.
SA_GR13_mabhedtu.history: The Mātṛkābhedatantra is generally regarded as a relatively late work, often dated to around the thirteenth century but probably not as old as some traditional claims; it has been edited and discussed in studies of Indian alchemy (e.g. White's work on rasa) and Bengali/eastern Śākta tantra.

SA_GR13_malinivu.title: Mālinīvijayottaratantra
SA_GR13_malinivu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_malinivu.date_estimate: c. 8th-9th c. CE (before Abhinavagupta, c. 975-1025 CE)
SA_GR13_malinivu.summary: A Bhairava-tantra of the Trika and, according to Abhinavagupta, the single most authoritative of the sixty-four non-dualist tantras on which the Trika system rests. It treats the Trika cosmology and pantheon (especially the goddess Mālinī and the arrangement of phonemes), initiation, ritual and an influential exposition of yoga and the means (upāyas) to liberation.
SA_GR13_malinivu.history: The Mālinīvijayottara was foundational for Abhinavagupta, who took it as the scriptural backbone of his monumental Tantrāloka and discussed it throughout his works; its yoga sections were critically edited and translated by Somadeva Vasudeva. It is generally dated to roughly the 8th-9th century, predating the Kashmirian exegetes who systematized the Trika.

SA_GR13_mrgt1mpu.title: Mṛgendrāgama [Mṛgendratantra]: 1. Vidyāpāda
SA_GR13_mrgt1mpu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_mrgt1mpu.summary: The Vidyāpāda (doctrine/knowledge section) of the Mṛgendrāgama, an Upāgama affiliated with the Kāmika scriptural tradition of the Śaiva Siddhānta. This section sets out the system's metaphysics: the nature of Śiva (pati), the bound souls (paśu), the bonds (pāśa), the categories (tattvas), epistemology and the rationale of grace and liberation.
SA_GR13_mrgt1mpu.history: The Mṛgendra is among the pre-twelfth-century Śaiva āgamas and was much used by Saiddhāntika theologians; Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇakaṇṭha wrote a vṛtti and Aghoraśiva a dīpikā on it. The Vidyāpāda and Yogapāda with Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha's commentary were published in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies (1930), and a French translation was produced at the IFP.

SA_GR13_mrgt3mpu.title: Mṛgendrāgama [Mṛgendratantra]: 3. Caryāpāda
SA_GR13_mrgt3mpu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_mrgt3mpu.summary: The Caryāpāda (section on conduct/observance) of the Mṛgendrāgama, prescribing the daily religious discipline, behavioral rules, observances and duties of the initiated Śaiva Siddhānta practitioner. It complements the doctrinal, ritual and yogic sections of the same āgama.
SA_GR13_mrgt3mpu.history: Part of the pre-twelfth-century Mṛgendra (an Upāgama of the Kāmika); Hélène Brunner-Lachaux edited and translated the rites and conduct sections (Mṛgendrāgama: section des rites et section du comportement, IFP 1985), with the commentaries of Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇakaṇṭha and Aghoraśiva.

SA_GR13_mrgt4mpu.title: Mṛgendrāgama [Mṛgendratantra]: 4. Yogapāda
SA_GR13_mrgt4mpu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_mrgt4mpu.summary: The Yogapāda (section on yoga) of the Mṛgendrāgama, expounding the Saiddhāntika system of yoga: the disciplines of breath, concentration and meditation by which the initiated soul progresses toward union with Śiva. It forms one quarter of the āgama's canonical fourfold structure.
SA_GR13_mrgt4mpu.history: The Yogapāda, together with the Vidyāpāda and Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha's commentary, was published in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies (1930) and rendered into French at the IFP (Mṛgendrāgama, sections de la doctrine et du yoga). The Mṛgendra is a pre-twelfth-century āgama much cited in Śaiva Siddhānta scholasticism.

SA_GR13_parakspu.title: Paraśurāma-Kalpasūtra
SA_GR13_parakspu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Paraśurāma)
SA_GR13_parakspu.date_estimate: c. 16th c. CE or somewhat earlier (South India)
SA_GR13_parakspu.summary: A concise aphoristic ritual handbook of the Śrīvidyā Kaula tradition, setting out in sūtra form the worship of the goddess Tripurasundarī (Lalitā) and related deities, the śrīcakra, initiation, mantra and daily and occasional rites. It is one of the most authoritative practical manuals of South Indian Śrīvidyā.
SA_GR13_parakspu.history: Ascribed to the sage Paraśurāma, the text was probably compiled in the sixteenth century or a little earlier in South India; its terse aphorisms became accessible largely through Rāmeśvara's detailed commentary (the Saubhāgyodaya), composed in 1831 CE.

SA_GR13_pasupbhu.title: Pāśupatasūtra
SA_GR13_pasupbhu.author: attributed to Lakulīśa
SA_GR13_pasupbhu.genre: sūtra (foundational scripture; here with Kauṇḍinya's bhāṣya)
SA_GR13_pasupbhu.date_estimate: c. 4th-5th c. CE (sūtra); commentary c. 4th-6th c. CE
SA_GR13_pasupbhu.summary: The foundational aphoristic scripture of the Pāśupata movement, the earliest Śaiva ascetic tradition for which textual evidence survives. It lays out the path of the Pāśupata ascetic: vows and observances (vidhi), peculiar transgressive practices, stages of discipline and the goal of the 'end of suffering' through union with Rudra-Śiva (the pati). This entry corresponds to the version transmitted with Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārthabhāṣya ('bhu').
SA_GR13_pasupbhu.history: Traditionally ascribed to Lakulīśa, the systematizer of the Pāśupatas, the sūtras and Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārthabhāṣya were rediscovered and published from a single manuscript in 1930 (Trivandrum). Kauṇḍinya is placed roughly in the 4th-6th century. The Pāśupatas are the principal Atimārga current and a key precursor to later Śaivism.

SA_GR13_pasupspu.title: Pāśupatasūtra
SA_GR13_pasupspu.author: attributed to Lakulīśa
SA_GR13_pasupspu.summary: A second transmitted version of the Pāśupatasūtra, the root aphoristic text of the Pāśupata ascetic tradition (the 'sūtra-only' or alternate-edition recension in the corpus, distinct from the version printed with Kauṇḍinya's bhāṣya). It prescribes the Pāśupata's vows, observances and graded path toward the cessation of suffering through devotion to Rudra-Śiva.
SA_GR13_pasupspu.history: As with the parallel entry, the sūtras are ascribed to Lakulīśa and were recovered together with Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārthabhāṣya in the 1930 Trivandrum edition. The two corpus entries reflect different presentations/editions of the same scripture; the recovery of these texts revolutionized the modern study of early Śaivism.

SA_GR13_pat.title: Parākhyatantra
SA_GR13_pat.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GR13_pat.summary: A Śaiva Siddhānta scripture (also called Pauṣkara-Pārameśvara / Saurabheya in tradition) presenting the dualist Saiddhāntika doctrine in dialogue form: the nature of Śiva, the soul, the bonds and the means to liberation, with substantial philosophical argumentation. It is valued for its clear scholastic treatment of Śaiva metaphysics and epistemology.
SA_GR13_pat.history: The surviving text was critically edited and translated by Dominic Goodall as 'The Parākhyatantra: A Scripture of the Śaiva Siddhānta' (IFP/EFEO, Collection Indologie 98, 2004). It is one of the better-preserved early Saiddhāntika scriptures and is frequently cited by medieval Śaiva exegetes.

SA_GR13_purekaku.title: Ekākṣarakośa
SA_GR13_purekaku.author: Puruṣottamadeva
SA_GR13_purekaku.summary: A short Sanskrit lexicon (kośa) that catalogues monosyllabic (single-syllable) words and the various meanings attached to each. It belongs to the genre of specialized synonym/homonym dictionaries used by poets and scholars to find compact expressions and decode the dense, syllable-laden language of kāvya and tantra.
SA_GR13_purekaku.history: It is one of several lexicons composed by the Buddhist-leaning Bengali lexicographer Puruṣottamadeva (who also wrote the Trikāṇḍaśeṣa, Hārāvalī and Varṇadeśanā), traditionally counted as his fifth kośa. Such monosyllabic glossaries were prized as aids to poetic composition and the interpretation of bīja (seed) syllables.

SA_GR13_saulahpu.title: Saundaryalaharī
SA_GR13_saulahpu.author: attributed to Śaṃkara (disputed)
SA_GR13_saulahpu.date_estimate: disputed; traditionally attributed to Śaṅkara (8th c.), but many scholars date the text c. 1350-1450 CE
SA_GR13_saulahpu.summary: A celebrated Tantric devotional hymn of c. 100 verses in the śikhariṇī metre praising the Goddess Tripurasundarī (Lalitā). The first portion (the Ānandalaharī, c. 41 verses) expounds the worship of the Śrīcakra and the union of Śiva-Śakti, while the remainder offers an extended head-to-foot (keśādipāda) aesthetic description of the Goddess's beauty.
SA_GR13_saulahpu.history: Almost all commentators ascribe it to Ādi Śaṅkara, but its Tantric Śrīvidyā content and pariṇāmavāda (transformation) metaphysics sit awkwardly with Śaṅkara's Advaita, so the attribution is widely doubted and a much later date is often proposed. It has attracted dozens of commentaries (notably Lakṣmīdhara's) and remains one of the most popular and most-recited Śākta hymns.

SA_GR13_sidr_1-3.title: Śivadṛṣṭi, chapters 1-3
SA_GR13_sidr_1-3.author: Somānanda
SA_GR13_sidr_1-3.summary: Chapters 1-3 of the Śivadṛṣṭi ('Vision of Śiva'), the foundational philosophical work of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of Kashmir Shaivism. The text argues a strict non-dualism in which all beings and things in the universe are fully identical with the active, willful god Śiva, the conscious world being real rather than illusory; the opening chapters set out this Śiva-as-everything pantheism and its theory of will (icchā) and divine power (śakti).
SA_GR13_sidr_1-3.history: Somānanda was the first systematic philosopher of the Trika/Pratyabhijñā lineage and teacher of Utpaladeva, who developed his ideas in the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā. The Śivadṛṣṭi is regarded as the first full scholastic philosophical treatise of the school; it survives with the commentary (vṛtti) of Utpaladeva.

SA_G_r13sidr_4.title: Śivadṛṣṭi, chapter 4
SA_G_r13sidr_4.author: Somānanda
SA_G_r13sidr_4.summary: Chapter 4 of Somānanda's Śivadṛṣṭi, continuing the school's exposition of monistic Śaiva metaphysics in which the manifest world unfolds from Śiva's autonomous power (śakti) through the 36 tattvas while remaining wholly identical with Śiva. As part of the larger treatise it advances the argument that the individual self can 'recognize' (pratyabhijñā) its own identity with the supreme conscious Śiva.
SA_G_r13sidr_4.history: Same work as the preceding entry; this record isolates the fourth chapter. Somānanda's text is the cornerstone of the Pratyabhijñā school and was transmitted with Utpaladeva's vṛtti, which is itself incomplete for the later chapters.

SA_GR13_sivmstpu.title: Śivamahimnastava
SA_GR13_sivmstpu.author: attributed to Puṣpadanta (traditional)
SA_GR13_sivmstpu.summary: A famous Sanskrit hymn of c. 43 verses ('Hymn on the Greatness of Śiva') extolling Śiva's cosmic deeds, attributes and ineffable glory, opening with an admission that even the gods cannot fully praise him. It is one of the most widely recited Śaiva stotras.
SA_GR13_sivmstpu.history: Tradition attributes it to the gandharva Puṣpadanta, whose name appears in verse 38, said to have composed it in repentance after offending Śiva; no firm historical date or author is established. It has been transmitted with several commentaries and remains central to popular Śaiva devotion.

SA_GR13_sivsutru.title: Śivasūtra
SA_GR13_sivsutru.author: Vasugupta (revealer)
SA_GR13_sivsutru.summary: A short collection of aphorisms regarded as the foundational revealed scripture of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism, presenting the path to recognizing the identity of the individual self with Śiva-consciousness. The sūtras are organized around three 'means' (upāyas)—śāmbhava, śākta and āṇava—to liberation.
SA_GR13_sivsutru.history: Tradition holds that the aphorisms were revealed to Vasugupta, who is said to have found them inscribed on a rock or received them in a dream. They generated an influential exegetical tradition, including the Spandakārikā and the commentaries Vimarśinī of Kṣemarāja and the Vārttika of Bhāskara.

SA_GR13_sivup_pu.title: Śiva-Upaniṣad
SA_GR13_sivup_pu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR13_sivup_pu.summary: A minor Śaiva Upaniṣad that extols Śiva as identical with the supreme Brahman and the inner Self (Ātman), and treats Śaiva ritual, worship and meditative practice. It belongs to the class of sectarian Upaniṣads that integrate devotional Śaivism into the Upaniṣadic idiom of self-knowledge.
SA_GR13_sivup_pu.history: It is one of the many minor Upaniṣads (the Muktikā corpus counts roughly fifteen as specifically Śaiva); the date and authorship of most such texts are unknown, and they are generally regarded as comparatively late compositions relative to the principal Upaniṣads.

SA_GR13_somsv_u.title: Śāktavijñāna
SA_GR13_somsv_u.author: Somānanda
SA_GR13_somsv_u.summary: A short work attributed to Somānanda concerned with the 'knowledge of Śakti'—the divine power (śakti) through which the non-dual Śiva manifests and recognizes himself as the world. It treats the role of Śakti in the unfolding of the tattvas within the monistic Śaiva framework of the Pratyabhijñā school.
SA_GR13_somsv_u.history: Ascribed to Somānanda, the founding philosopher of the Pratyabhijñā lineage and author of the Śivadṛṣṭi; this is a minor work and its specific transmission is less documented than his principal treatise. Some details of authorship and content for this short text are not fully confirmed in standard reference sources.

SA_GR13_sratrppu.title: Ratnatrayaparīkṣā
SA_GR13_sratrppu.author: Śrīkaṇṭha
SA_GR13_sratrppu.summary: A doctrinal treatise of the dualist Śaiva Siddhānta examining the 'three jewels' (ratnatraya)—the three fundamental ontological categories of pati (the Lord, Śiva), paśu (the bound soul) and pāśa (the bonds, comprising āṇava, karma and māyā). It systematically expounds the relation of God, souls and the fetters in the soteriology of the school.
SA_GR13_sratrppu.history: Attributed to Śrīkaṇṭha, a Śaiva Siddhānta theologian in the line of pre-twelfth-century Kashmiri/northern Siddhānta authors (the milieu of Sadyojyoti and the later commentator Aghoraśiva). It is transmitted with a commentary by Aghoraśiva, who systematized the dualist Siddhānta in the south.

SA_GR13_stkal_pu.title: Sārdhatriśatikālottarāgama
SA_GR13_stkal_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (Śaiva āgama)
SA_GR13_stkal_pu.summary: An abridged recension of the Kālottara, a Śaiva Siddhānta scripture, in 350 (sārdha-triśati) verses across twenty-seven chapters. It treats ritual, initiation, yoga, the nature of the soul and Śaiva cosmology characteristic of the Siddhānta āgamic tradition.
SA_GR13_stkal_pu.history: The Kālottara circulated in multiple recensions of differing length (the shortest being the Śatika Kālajñāna); this 350-verse version was edited with the authoritative commentary of Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (10th c.), which fixes its terminus ante quem. It is studied as a key witness to the early Saiddhāntika canon (work on these recensions notably by A. Sanderson and the IFP, Pondicherry).

SA_GR13_svact_pu.title: Svacchandatantra [Svacchandabhairavatantra]
SA_GR13_svact_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (Bhairava tantra)
SA_GR13_svact_pu.tradition: Hindu, Śaiva (Bhairava / Mantramārga; non-dual reading in Kashmir)
SA_GR13_svact_pu.date_estimate: by c. 7th c. CE (composite, pre-10th c.)
SA_GR13_svact_pu.summary: A large Bhairava tantra centered on the worship of Svacchandabhairava ('Autonomous Bhairava'), a form of Śiva, together with his consort Aghoreśvarī. It covers mantra, initiation (dīkṣā), cosmology, yoga, ritual practice and soteriology, and was one of the most influential and widely worshipped tantric cults in medieval Kashmir.
SA_GR13_svact_pu.history: Datable to at least the 7th century, it became authoritative in Kashmir and received the important Uddyota commentary of Kṣemarāja (late 10th c.), pupil of Abhinavagupta, who reinterpreted its originally more dualist material in a non-dual Trika key, explicitly aiming to refute earlier Siddhāntin commentators.

SA_GR13_todaltpu.title: Toḍalatantra
SA_GR13_todaltpu.author: anonymous/traditional (Śākta tantra)
SA_GR13_todaltpu.date_estimate: uncertain; comparatively late (later medieval, 2nd millennium CE)
SA_GR13_todaltpu.summary: A Śākta tantra dealing with the worship of the Goddess and the Daśa Mahāvidyā, the ten Tantric wisdom-goddesses. Its tenth chapter notably correlates each of the ten Mahāvidyās with one of Viṣṇu's ten avatāras (e.g. Kālī with Kṛṣṇa, Durgā with Kalki), reflecting an integration of Śākta and Vaiṣṇava schemes.
SA_GR13_todaltpu.history: A relatively late tantra of uncertain authorship and date; its systematic Mahāvidyā-Daśāvatāra correlation marks it as part of a developed Mahāvidyā tradition associated especially with eastern India. No critical scholarly dating is firmly established.

SA_GR13_uddamt_u.title: Uḍḍāmareśvaratantra
SA_GR13_uddamt_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Tantra)
SA_GR13_uddamt_u.summary: A tantra devoted chiefly to magical rites (the ṣaṭkarma and related practices), describing supernatural beings such as the thirty-six Yakṣas and Yakṣiṇīs together with their mantras and ritual procedures for attaining worldly aims. It belongs to the practical/magical stratum of Tantric literature rather than to high speculative theology.
SA_GR13_uddamt_u.history: Of uncertain authorship and date; it was published in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies (ed. Jagad Dhar Zadoo, 1947). Such uḍḍāmara-type texts circulated widely as practical handbooks of mantric magic across both Hindu and Buddhist tantric milieus.
related_works: Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies editions

SA_GR13_utsivsau.title: Śivastotrāvalī
SA_GR13_utsivsau.author: Utpaladeva
SA_GR13_utsivsau.summary: A collection (āvalī, 'garland') of devotional hymns to Śiva by the great Pratyabhijñā philosopher Utpaladeva, expressing intense longing for, and the bliss of, identity with Śiva-consciousness. It is widely regarded as the supreme example of Kashmiri Śaiva devotional poetry, fusing the school's recognition philosophy with ecstatic bhakti.
SA_GR13_utsivsau.history: Composed by Utpaladeva (fl. c. 900-950), disciple of Somānanda and author of the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā; the hymns were compiled and transmitted with a commentary by Kṣemarāja. They remain central to the living Kashmir Śaiva devotional tradition (e.g. taught by Swami Lakshmanjoo).

SA_GR13_vinst_pu.title: Vīṇāśikhatantra
SA_GR13_vinst_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (Śaiva tantra)
SA_GR13_vinst_pu.date_estimate: early stratum of Tantric literature; mid-1st millennium CE
SA_GR13_vinst_pu.summary: A succinct Śaiva tantra and the only surviving text representing the Vāmasrotas ('Left Current') of early Śaiva Tantrism. The deity worshipped is a little-known fourfold-emanating form of Śiva called Tumburu, surrounded by his four Śaktis—Jayā, Vijayā, Ajitā (Jayantī) and Aparājitā—with attendant mantra and ritual material.
SA_GR13_vinst_pu.history: Edited, introduced and translated by Teun Goudriaan (1985). It is valued as a rare witness to the archaic Left-Current tradition that otherwise survives mainly through references in later works, and is dated to an early phase of the tantric corpus.

SA_GR14_astavgpu.title: Aṣṭāvakragītā
SA_GR14_astavgpu.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Aṣṭāvakra)
SA_GR14_astavgpu.date_estimate: disputed; commonly placed c. 8th-14th c. CE (some argue much earlier)
SA_GR14_astavgpu.summary: Also called the Aṣṭāvakra Saṃhitā, a verse dialogue between the sage Aṣṭāvakra and King Janaka expounding radical non-dualism (Advaita): the self is pure, free, witnessing consciousness, and liberation is recognition of one's ever-already-free nature rather than the fruit of any practice. It is admired for its uncompromising, terse statement of jñāna.
SA_GR14_astavgpu.history: Authorship and date are highly uncertain and much disputed: estimates range from c. 500-400 BCE (Mukerjee) to the 8th or 14th century CE (Brockington), the later dating favored because of its developed, systematic Advaita. It is attributed to the legendary sage Aṣṭāvakra known from the Mahābhārata and Purāṇas.

SA_GR14_bhrgus_u.title: Bhṛgu-Saṃhitā
SA_GR14_bhrgus_u.author: attributed to the sage Bhṛgu (traditional)
SA_GR14_bhrgus_u.date_estimate: uncertain; traditionally ancient, extant material later and composite
SA_GR14_bhrgus_u.summary: A jyotiṣa (astrological) treatise attributed to the Vedic sage Bhṛgu, framed as a vast compilation of horoscopes said to contain predictions about a person's present, past and future lives. It is associated with predictive astrology in which an inquirer's chart is matched to one of a large collection of pre-recorded horoscope-readings.
SA_GR14_bhrgus_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Bhṛgu as the first compiler of predictive astrology; no complete manuscript survives, large parts are reportedly lost, and working astrologers use scattered manuscript leaves. (Note: a distinct Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇava āgama also bears a similar 'Bhṛgu' title; this record most plausibly refers to the well-known astrological Bhṛgu-Saṃhitā.) Its dating is highly uncertain.

SA_GR14_brhms5cu.title: Brahma-Saṃhitā: Adhyāya 5
SA_GR14_brhms5cu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Brahmā)
SA_GR14_brhms5cu.date_estimate: c. 1300 CE (Matsubara) for the recovered chapter
SA_GR14_brhms5cu.summary: The fifth chapter of the Brahma-Saṃhitā, a Pāñcarātra text framed as prayers spoken by Brahmā glorifying Kṛṣṇa as the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Its 62 verses describe Goloka Vṛndāvana and the spiritual world, expound the kāma-gāyatrī and kāma-bīja, Mahā-Viṣṇu, the soul and the Supersoul, and open with the celebrated 'govindam ādi-puruṣaṃ tam ahaṃ bhajāmi' verse.
SA_GR14_brhms5cu.history: Chaitanya Mahāprabhu (1486-1534) is said to have rediscovered this chapter, long lost, at the Ādikeśava Perumāl temple at Kanyākumārī, making it a foundational scripture of Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism. M. Matsubara dates the text to c. 1300 CE. The 'Govinda' prayers were popularized worldwide in a 1970 recording produced by George Harrison.

SA_GR14_caitcanu.title: Caitanyacandrāmṛta
SA_GR14_caitcanu.author: Prabodhānanda Sarasvatī
SA_GR14_caitcanu.summary: A devotional Sanskrit poem ('The Moon-nectar of Caitanya') in praise of Caitanya Mahāprabhu, extolling the glories of devotion to him and the supremacy of love of Kṛṣṇa over liberation and other goals. It is a celebrated lyrical expression of Gauḍīya rapture centered on Caitanya as Kṛṣṇa himself.
SA_GR14_caitcanu.history: Composed by Prabodhānanda Sarasvatī, a sannyāsī who, according to tradition, formerly followed Śrī Vaiṣṇavism and was converted by Caitanya during the latter's South-Indian tour (1509-10); he became a major Vrindavan-associated Gauḍīya/Rādhāvallabha author. The work is transmitted with later commentaries and Hindi translations.

SA_GR14_dhyggs_u.title: Gauragovindārcanasmaraṇapaddhati
SA_GR14_dhyggs_u.author: Dhyānacandra Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_dhyggs_u.summary: A Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava manual systematizing the daily practice of arcana (worship) and smaraṇa (contemplative remembrance) of Caitanya Mahāprabhu and Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa. It lays out mantras, gāyatrīs, mental offerings and the meditative cultivation of the practitioner's spiritual identity, and is among the earliest texts to describe the esoteric process of siddha-praṇālī.
SA_GR14_dhyggs_u.history: Authored by Dhyānacandra Gosvāmī, a disciple in the line of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī; it is counted among the foundational paddhati-traya manuals of Gauḍīya esoteric worship and remains in liturgical use. It has been translated into English (e.g. by Bhānu Swami).

SA_GR14_dhyrkk_u.title: Rādhākṛṣṇāṣṭakālīyalīlāsmaraṇakramapaddhati
SA_GR14_dhyrkk_u.author: Dhyānacandra Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_dhyrkk_u.summary: A Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava manual outlining the systematic sequence (krama) for the meditative remembrance (smaraṇa) of the aṣṭakālīya-līlā, the eight daily periods of the eternal pastimes of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa in Vraja. It guides the practitioner through visualizing and mentally serving the divine couple at each part of the day, cultivating mañjarī-bhāva (the mood of intimate female attendants). It forms part of the larger Gaura-Govindārcana-smaraṇa-paddhati tradition of esoteric worship.
SA_GR14_dhyrkk_u.history: Dhyānacandra was a disciple of Gopālaguru Gosvāmī, and composed his paddhati following the format established by his guru; together their two same-titled manuals are the earliest and foundational handbooks of Gauḍīya esoteric (rāgānugā) deity worship and smaraṇa. The text is transmitted within the Caitanya tradition lineage at Purī and Vṛndāvana.

SA_GR14_harinc_u.title: Harināmacintāmaṇi
SA_GR14_harinc_u.author: Kedāranātha Datta (Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura)
SA_GR14_harinc_u.genre: philosophical/devotional treatise on nāma (the holy name)
SA_GR14_harinc_u.summary: A treatise expounding the philosophy and practice of harināma, the chanting of Kṛṣṇa's holy names, cast as a dialogue between Caitanya Mahāprabhu and Haridāsa Ṭhākura. It systematically analyzes the offenses against the holy name (nāmāparādha), the gradations of chanting, and the path from nāmābhāsa (a semblance of the name) to pure nāma, culminating in love of God.
SA_GR14_harinc_u.history: Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura (Kedarnath Datta, 1838–1914) was a Bengali magistrate, philosopher and reformer who revitalized Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism; from 1874 until his death he wrote numerous works in Sanskrit, Bengali and English. The Harināmacintāmaṇi is among his influential expository works, widely studied in the Gauḍīya line that produced the later Gauḍīya Maṭha and ISKCON movements.

SA_GR14_hbhvil_u.title: Haribhaktivilāsa 1–11
SA_GR14_hbhvil_u.author: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī (largely composed/redacted by Sanātana Gosvāmī)
SA_GR14_hbhvil_u.summary: An extensive Sanskrit ritual compendium describing the normative ritual and devotional life of a Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava: initiation (dīkṣā), daily worship, mantras, vows, festivals, deity service, and conduct. The work is largely a digest, with over 90% of its verses cited from earlier Vedic, Tantric, Pāñcarātra and Purāṇic sources. This entry covers the first eleven of the twenty vilāsas (sections).
SA_GR14_hbhvil_u.history: Traditionally attributed to Gopāla Bhaṭṭa, one of the Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana, but the Bhaktiratnākara (1.197–198) records that the idea originated with Gopāla Bhaṭṭa while Sanātana Gosvāmī actually carried out the composition in his name. It became the foundational ritual authority for the entire Gauḍīya tradition and is accompanied by the Dig-darśinī commentary attributed to Sanātana.

SA_GR14_jivrkadu.title: Rādhākṛṣṇārcanadīpikā
SA_GR14_jivrkadu.author: Jīva Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_jivrkadu.summary: A short theological dissertation (c. 150 verses) establishing the rationale for worshipping Rādhā together with Kṛṣṇa. Arguing first that devotees are to be worshipped after the Lord, Jīva grades the devotees and demonstrates with abundant scriptural citation that the gopīs of Vṛndāvana, and Rādhā above all, are the topmost devotees and thus worthy of worship alongside Kṛṣṇa.
SA_GR14_jivrkadu.history: Composed by Jīva Gosvāmī (c. 1513–1598), nephew of Rūpa and Sanātana and the great systematizer of Gauḍīya theology. It articulates the doctrinal basis for the distinctive Gauḍīya worship of the divine couple Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa and remains a standard reference in the tradition's arcana literature.

SA_GR14_jnass01u.title: Jñānāmṛtasāra-Saṃhitā [Nārada-Pañcarātra]
SA_GR14_jnass01u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR14_jnass01u.date_estimate: late saṃhitā stratum, probably c. 12th–16th c. CE
SA_GR14_jnass01u.summary: A Pāñcarātra saṃhitā of just over 3100 ślokas in sixty chapters (in five sections), narrated by Vyāsa as a revelation given by Maheśvara (Śiva) to Nārada. It glorifies the cult of Kṛṣṇa, Gopāla and Rādhā, and expounds five kinds of knowledge—of the Absolute, liberation, devotional service, yoga, and ignorance—with devotion to Kṛṣṇa as its leading theme. It is popularly circulated under the title Nārada-Pañcarātra.
SA_GR14_jnass01u.history: Reckoned among the latest of the Pāñcarātra saṃhitās, it shows the school's evolution toward Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa sectarian devotion: older Pāñcarātra concepts such as the vyūhas recede while śakti as creative power gains prominence. It was popular in Bengal and influenced Gauḍīya circles, where it is frequently cited.

SA_GR14_kbss_1-u.title: Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha
SA_GR14_kbss_1-u.author: Siddha Kṛṣṇadāsa Tātapāda (Govardhana-nivāsī Kṛṣṇadāsa)
SA_GR14_kbss_1-u.genre: compilation / meditation (smaraṇa) manual for rāgānugā bhakti
SA_GR14_kbss_1-u.summary: A compiled handbook on the inner meditative practice (bhāvanā, smaraṇa) of rāgānugā bhakti, gathering material on cultivating the siddha-deha (the meditator's perfected spiritual body) and on mentally serving Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa in Vraja day and night. It belongs to the esoteric Gauḍīya literature on the eightfold daily pastimes and internal devotional absorption.
SA_GR14_kbss_1-u.history: Compiled by Kṛṣṇadāsa Tātapāda, a siddha resident of Govardhana, within the Gauḍīya rāgānugā tradition of meditative worship. It survives in manuscript and printed (Hindi-translated) editions and is part of the practical literature on siddha-praṇālī and mañjarī-bhāva.

SA_GR14_krdipc_u.title: Kramadīpikā
SA_GR14_krdipc_u.author: Keśava Kāśmīrī (Keśava Bhaṭṭa)
SA_GR14_krdipc_u.summary: A work in eight chapters dealing chiefly with the ritualistic side of Vaiṣṇava worship, devoted largely to the various mantras (especially the ten- and eighteen-syllable Gopāla mantras), their nyāsa and meditation, and associated procedures of Kṛṣṇa worship. It was influential in temple ritual, including at the Jagannātha temple in Purī.
SA_GR14_krdipc_u.history: Authored by Keśava Kāśmīrī (Keśava Bhaṭṭācārya), an ācārya of the Nimbārka Sampradāya, who names himself in the colophon. The text was widely respected across Vaiṣṇava lineages and is quoted in the Gauḍīya Haribhaktivilāsa, attesting to its authority in mantra and ritual matters.

SA_GR14_kumukumu.title: Mukundamālā
SA_GR14_kumukumu.author: Kulaśekhara (Kulaśekhara Āḻvār)
SA_GR14_kumukumu.summary: A Sanskrit devotional hymn of about 40 verses addressed to Kṛṣṇa/Viṣṇu as Mukunda, the bestower of liberation. The verses express ardent surrender (prapatti) and longing for the Lord, including the famous prayer to remember Kṛṣṇa at the moment of death, and extol single-minded devotion over even liberation.
SA_GR14_kumukumu.history: Attributed to Kulaśekhara, the poet-saint counted among the twelve Āḻvārs of the Tamil Śrī Vaiṣṇava tradition, identified with a Cera (Kulaśekhara) ruler of Kerala; the hymn is traditionally associated with the Raṅganātha temple at Śrīraṅgam. Its authorship and date are debated, but it is a celebrated and widely commented Vaiṣṇava stotra. The project hint of c. 900–1099 CE is broadly consistent with common datings.

SA_GR14_laghbh_u.title: Laghubhāgavatāmṛta
SA_GR14_laghbh_u.author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_laghbh_u.summary: A theological treatise on Kṛṣṇa-tattva and avatāra-tattva that establishes Kṛṣṇa as the avatārī—the original Supreme source of all incarnations—rather than one incarnation among many. It classifies the forms of Godhead (svayaṃ-rūpa, tadekātma-rūpa with its vilāsa and svāṃśa subdivisions, and āveśa forms) and the categories of devotees, arguing for the supremacy of Kṛṣṇa over Nārāyaṇa and other manifestations.
SA_GR14_laghbh_u.history: Composed by Rūpa Gosvāmī (c. 1489–1564), the foremost theologian among the Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana, as a counterpart to the larger Bṛhad-bhāgavatāmṛta of his brother (here the longer work is by Sanātana). It is a core doctrinal statement of Gauḍīya Kṛṣṇa-centric theology.

SA_GR14_mdhvkmou.title: Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava
SA_GR14_mdhvkmou.author: Madhva (Ānandatīrtha / Pūrṇaprajña)
SA_GR14_mdhvkmou.summary: "The Great Ocean of the Nectar of Kṛṣṇa," a devotional work of about 242–290 verses praising Kṛṣṇa's divine attributes and the fruits of devoted worship—asserting, for instance, that one who worships him with full devotion is never condemned. It compiles scriptural verses and instruction on the merits of Kṛṣṇa-bhakti, including ethical teachings (e.g., the qualities that make a person worthy of respect).
SA_GR14_mdhvkmou.history: One of the thirty-seven works (sarvamūla granthas) of Madhvācārya (Ānandatīrtha), founder of the Dvaita school of Vedānta. It frames Kṛṣṇa-devotion within his realist, dualist theology and is composed, in his words, to give nectar to those poisoned by saṃsāra.

SA_GR14_muktacau.title: Muktācarita
SA_GR14_muktacau.author: Raghunāthadāsa Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_muktacau.summary: A short allegorical narrative ("The Story of the Pearl") set in the divine pastimes of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa in Vraja, in which a playful dispute over pearls becomes a vehicle for devotional and theological themes. It is one of the small esoteric compositions illustrating the intimate līlā of the divine couple.
SA_GR14_muktacau.history: One of three works by Raghunāthadāsa Gosvāmī (c. 1495–1571), one of the Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana renowned for his renunciation and his poetry of separation (vipralambha). His works, including the Dānakelicintāmaṇi and Stavāvalī, are treasured in the Gauḍīya tradition for their depiction of mañjarī-bhāva.

SA_GR14_prakassu.title: Prakāśa-Saṃhitā [Pāñcarātra-Saṃhitā]
SA_GR14_prakassu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR14_prakassu.summary: A Pāñcarātra saṃhitā, one of the numerous Vaiṣṇava āgamic texts of that canon concerned with the four standard divisions of Pāñcarātra subject matter—jñāna (theology/cosmology), yoga, kriyā (temple construction and image-making), and caryā (worship and conduct). Specific contents are poorly documented in accessible scholarship.
SA_GR14_prakassu.history: Named in traditional lists of Pāñcarātra saṃhitās but little studied; it belongs to the broad body of more than two hundred Pāñcarātra texts that shaped Vaiṣṇava temple ritual and theology. Its date and provenance are not securely established.

SA_GR14_ps27-43u.title: Pauṣkara-Saṃhitā
SA_GR14_ps27-43u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR14_ps27-43u.date_estimate: early/ancient Pāñcarātra stratum, c. 5th–8th c. CE (disputed)
SA_GR14_ps27-43u.summary: One of the most ancient and respected Pāñcarātra saṃhitās, a large work (roughly 5900 ślokas in 43 chapters in its published form) framed as a dialogue between the sage Pauṣkara and Bhagavān, covering the full range of Pāñcarātra concern—jñāna, yoga, kriyā and caryā. This entry covers chapters 27–43, dealing with the later ritual portions (temple/image and worship procedures). It is especially esteemed by the temple priests of Śrīraṅgam and Kāñcīpuram.
SA_GR14_ps27-43u.history: Together with the Sātvata and Jayākhya saṃhitās it forms the ratnatraya ("three jewels"), the most authoritative and earliest core of the Pāñcarātra canon. Its antiquity and continued liturgical use give it foundational status in Śrī Vaiṣṇava temple worship.

SA_GR14_ruphbr_u.title: Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu
SA_GR14_ruphbr_u.author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_ruphbr_u.summary: The foundational systematic treatise on bhakti-rasa, adapting the classical rasa theory of Bharata's Nāṭyaśāstra into a theology of devotion. In four divisions (eastern, southern, western, northern "quadrants"), it analyzes the stages of devotional practice (sādhana, bhāva, prema), the constituents of devotional emotion (vibhāva, anubhāva, sāttvika-bhāva, vyabhicāri-bhāva), and the five primary and seven secondary rasas of relationship with Kṛṣṇa.
SA_GR14_ruphbr_u.history: Composed by Rūpa Gosvāmī in 1541 CE (1463 Śaka), it is the central theoretical work of Gauḍīya devotional aesthetics, transforming bhakti into a fully theorized rasa. It is paired with its sequel on the erotic mode, the Ujjvalanīlamaṇi, and was translated and studied extensively by David Haberman and others.

SA_GR14_sadhdipu.title: Sādhanādīpikā
SA_GR14_sadhdipu.author: Rādhākṛṣṇadāsa Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_sadhdipu.summary: A Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava manual on devotional practice (sādhana), treating deity worship (vigraha-sevā / arcana), daily ritual, and the broader regimen of bhakti. It includes discussion of the authorization of deity service in the tradition (e.g., Mahāprabhu's empowering of Gadādhara Paṇḍita for vigraha-sevā) alongside practical worship procedures.
SA_GR14_sadhdipu.history: Authored by Rādhākṛṣṇadāsa Gosvāmī within the Gauḍīya line; the work is cited in discussions of the tradition's standards of deity worship and Gadādhara's role in the sampradāya. Precise biographical and dating details for the author are not well established in accessible scholarship.

SA_GR14_satvtanu.title: Sātvatatantra
SA_GR14_satvtanu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR14_satvtanu.summary: A Pāñcarātra text (sātvata-śāstra) presented as taught by Nārada and propounded by Viśoka, offering a path to liberation (mukti) made accessible even to women, śūdras and the low-born. "Sātvata" refers to an archaic designation of the ancient Bhāgavata/Sātvata bhakti cult, and the text treats Vaiṣṇava theology and devotional means within that framework.
SA_GR14_satvtanu.history: Distinguished in some catalogues from the famous Sātvata Saṃhitā (one of the ratnatraya): wisdomlib treats the Sātvatatantra as a separate text attributed to Viśoka. It is frequently cited in later Vaiṣṇava, including Gauḍīya, literature. Its precise identity, dating and relation to the Sātvata Saṃhitā are not fully settled.

SA_GR14_sdahen_u.title: Daśavidhahetunirūpaṇa
SA_GR14_sdahen_u.author: Śrīnivāsamakhin (Śrīnivāsa Dīkṣita) Vedāntadeśika
SA_GR14_sdahen_u.summary: A doctrinal treatise setting out ten arguments (daśavidha-hetu) for the superiority of the Vaikhānasa school among the Vaiṣṇava sects, serving as an introduction to the larger Vaikhānasasūtratātparyacintāmaṇi. It defends the authority and distinctive practices of the Vaikhānasa tradition of temple worship.
SA_GR14_sdahen_u.history: Despite being grouped with Gauḍīya/Vaiṣṇava e-texts (GRETIL), this is a Vaikhānasa apologetic work by Śrīnivāsamakhin ("Vedāntadeśika" here being an epithet, not the famous Śrī Vaiṣṇava Vedānta Deśika). It is transmitted with Sanskrit and Telugu commentaries and is part of the Vaikhānasa sectarian literature.

SA_GR14_ss1_tatu.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 1. Tattvasandarbha
SA_GR14_ss1_tatu.author: Jīva Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_ss1_tatu.summary: The first of the six Sandarbhas, establishing the epistemological foundation of Gauḍīya theology: it examines the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇa), argues that scripture (śabda) and especially the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is the supreme authority on the Absolute, and identifies the Bhāgavata as both the highest means of knowing and the ultimate object of knowledge.
SA_GR14_ss1_tatu.history: Jīva Gosvāmī (c. 1513–1598) systematized into the Ṣaṭsandarbha ideas inherited from Rūpa, Sanātana, and (per tradition) preliminary notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa. The Tattvasandarbha is the prolegomenon to the whole corpus, providing the methodological basis for the Gauḍīya Acintya-bhedābheda Vedānta; it carries commentaries by Jīva himself and later by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

SA_GR14_ss2_bhgu.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 2. Bhāgavatasandarbha
SA_GR14_ss2_bhgu.author: Jīva Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_ss2_bhgu.summary: The second of the six Sandarbhas (also called Bhagavat-sandarbha), expounding the ontology of the non-dual Absolute Reality and establishing Bhagavān—the personal Lord endowed with all energies—as its fullest and highest expression, above the impersonal Brahman and the inner-controller Paramātmā. It develops the doctrine of the Lord's intrinsic potencies (śaktis).
SA_GR14_ss2_bhgu.history: Part of Jīva Gosvāmī's systematic theological corpus arranging the teachings of the early Gauḍīya masters, addressing the theme of sambandha (the ontological relation between God, his energies, and souls). With the next two Sandarbhas it forms the bhagavat-tattva exposition of the school.

SA_GR14_ss3_paru.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 3. Paramātmasandarbha
SA_GR14_ss3_paru.author: Jīva Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_ss3_paru.summary: The third of the six Sandarbhas, examining the nature of the individual self (jīva), the operation of māyā, and the immanent presence of the Absolute as Paramātmā (the Supersoul) pervading and sustaining the cosmos and all beings. It elaborates the relation between the soul, the material energy, and the indwelling Lord.
SA_GR14_ss3_paru.history: Continuing Jīva Gosvāmī's systematic Bhāgavata Vedānta, it treats the theme of sambandha alongside the Tattva- and Bhāgavata-sandarbhas, working out the metaphysics of jīva and māyā that underpins the school's acintya-bhedābheda (inconceivable difference-and-non-difference) doctrine.

SA_GR14_ss4_krsu.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 4. Kṛṣṇasandarbha
SA_GR14_ss4_krsu.author: Jīva Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_ss4_krsu.genre: philosophical-theological treatise (with commentary on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
SA_GR14_ss4_krsu.summary: The fourth of Jīva Gosvāmī's six Sandarbhas (Ṣaṭsandarbha or Bhāgavata-sandarbha), a systematic exposition of Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava theology drawing heavily on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa. The Kṛṣṇasandarbha argues that Kṛṣṇa, not a mere avatāra, is the original and complete form of Bhagavān (svayaṃ bhagavān), and establishes him as the supreme object of loving devotion, treating his līlā, dhāman (Vraja), and associates.
SA_GR14_ss4_krsu.history: Jīva Gosvāmī (c. 1513-1598), nephew of Rūpa and Sanātana Gosvāmī, systematized the theology of the Caitanya movement at Vrindavan; the Sandarbhas were partly based on notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa. They remain the doctrinal foundation of the Gauḍīya school and were commented upon by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

SA_GR14_ss5_bhku.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 5. Bhaktisandarbha
SA_GR14_ss5_bhku.author: Jīva Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_ss5_bhku.genre: philosophical-theological treatise (with commentary on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
SA_GR14_ss5_bhku.summary: The fifth of the Ṣaṭsandarbhas, devoted to bhakti (devotion). It establishes devotional service to Bhagavān as the sole direct means (abhidheya) to God-realization, analyzing the varieties and stages of bhakti, the qualifications of the devotee, the relation of bhakti to karma and jñāna, and the centrality of the holy name.
SA_GR14_ss5_bhku.history: Composed by Jīva Gosvāmī at Vrindavan as part of his systematization of Gauḍīya theology, drawing primarily on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa. The Bhaktisandarbha is among the most studied of the six and was commented upon by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

SA_GR14_ss6_priu.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 6. Prītisandarbha
SA_GR14_ss6_priu.author: Jīva Gosvāmī
SA_GR14_ss6_priu.genre: philosophical-theological treatise (with commentary on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
SA_GR14_ss6_priu.summary: The sixth and final Sandarbha, treating prīti or prema-bhakti — pure love of God — as the supreme goal (prayojana) of human life. It analyzes the nature of divine love, its relation to liberation (which it surpasses), the rasa-theology of devotional emotion, and the eternal blissful relationship between the soul and Kṛṣṇa.
SA_GR14_ss6_priu.history: The culminating volume of Jīva Gosvāmī's Ṣaṭsandarbha, completing the structure of sambandha (relation), abhidheya (means) and prayojana (goal). It is foundational for Gauḍīya aesthetics of devotion and was commented upon by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

SA_GR14_utajp_pu.title: Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi
SA_GR14_utajp_pu.author: Utpaladeva
SA_GR14_utajp_pu.summary: A short philosophical work ('Proof of a Sentient/Non-inert Knower'), one of the three treatises of Utpaladeva's Siddhitrayī. It argues for the necessity of a conscious, self-luminous knowing subject, against Buddhist and other positions that would reduce cognition to insentient processes, and shows that the limited individual knower is ultimately grounded in the one unlimited Self, establishing the non-dual nature of reality.
SA_GR14_utajp_pu.history: Utpaladeva (c. 900-950 CE) was the chief originator of the Pratyabhijñā ('recognition') system of monistic Kashmir Śaivism, building on his teacher Somānanda and later developed by Abhinavagupta. The Siddhitrayī (Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi, Sambandhasiddhi) accompanies his major work the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā; the text survives with a Vṛtti.

SA_GR14_vaimp__u.title: Vaikhānasamantrapraśna
SA_GR14_vaimp__u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the Vaikhānasa school of Vikhanas)
SA_GR14_vaimp__u.tradition: Hindu (Vaiṣṇava; Vaikhānasa Āgama tradition, rooted in the Taittirīya/Black Yajurveda)
SA_GR14_vaimp__u.date_estimate: early to mid-1st millennium CE (mantra layer archaic; disputed)
SA_GR14_vaimp__u.summary: The mantra-portion ('Mantrapraśna', in praśnas) of the Vaikhānasa corpus, containing the liturgical mantras used in Vaikhānasa Vaiṣṇava temple and domestic worship. It includes numerous accented mantras of clearly Vedic character not found in other Yajurveda Saṃhitās, supplying formulae for the consecration and worship of Viṣṇu and associated deities.
SA_GR14_vaimp__u.history: The Vaikhānasas, followers of the sage Vikhanas, are the hereditary priests (arcakas) of more than half the Viṣṇu temples of South India, including Tirupati. Their tradition claims direct Vedic descent and is regarded as more orthodox/conservative than the Pāñcarātra. The Mantrapraśna is the mantra core complementing the Vaikhānasa Gṛhya/Smārta sūtras and Āgama saṃhitās; a critical edition/translation of Mantrapraśna V-VIII was published by Harvard University Press.

SA_GR14_vdhapadu.title: Viṣṇudharma
SA_GR14_vdhapadu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GR14_vdhapadu.date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (disputed; an early Vaiṣṇava layer)
SA_GR14_vdhapadu.summary: An early Vaiṣṇava 'śāstra'/upapurāṇa (distinct from the larger encyclopedic Viṣṇudharmottara) cast as a dialogue, in the extant version comprising about 105 chapters in which the sage Pulastya (or Śaunaka) instructs Dālbhya on Vaiṣṇava dharma — vows, worship of Viṣṇu, devotional observances and the merit of devotion to Viṣṇu.
SA_GR14_vdhapadu.history: Studied by R. C. Hazra among the Upapurāṇas, the Viṣṇudharma is regarded as one of the older Vaiṣṇava texts, its composition linked to the spread of Buddhism and 'heretical' faiths in the centuries after Aśoka. It is to be distinguished from the encyclopedic Viṣṇudharmottara Purāṇa, of which it is the conceptual predecessor; a critical edition was prepared by R. Grünendahl.

SA_GR14_visvaksu.title: Viṣvaksenasaṃhitā
SA_GR14_visvaksu.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed revelation by Viṣvaksena)
SA_GR14_visvaksu.summary: A Pāñcarātra Āgama scripture of roughly 2800 verses in 39 chapters, dealing with image-worship and temple ritual: the theology and consecration of pratimā icons, iconographic prescriptions, and the construction of the prāsāda (temple structure) to house the images. It is classed as a sāttvika text of the 'Muniprokta' group of Pāñcarātra saṃhitās.
SA_GR14_visvaksu.history: Named for Viṣvaksena, Viṣṇu's commander and gate-guardian, this saṃhitā belongs to the Pāñcarātra Āgama corpus that governs Vaiṣṇava temple liturgy in South India. Its precise date and authorship are unknown, as is typical of the Pāñcarātra revelatory literature; it is cited in later ritual digests.

SA_GR14_yamcatpu.title: Catuḥślokī
SA_GR14_yamcatpu.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya / Āḷavandār)
SA_GR14_yamcatpu.summary: A short devotional hymn of four verses ('Four Stanzas') in praise of Śrī (Lakṣmī), extolling the goddess's supreme role as the consort of Nārāyaṇa and as the mediatrix who grants grace and liberation to individual souls (jīvas). It is foundational for Śrī Vaiṣṇava theology of Lakṣmī's salvific function.
SA_GR14_yamcatpu.history: Yāmunācārya (traditionally c. 918-1038 CE), grandson of Nāthamuni and predecessor of Rāmānuja, was a key systematizer of Viśiṣṭādvaita. The Catuḥślokī is one of his six works and, with his Stotraratna, established the devotional tone of the tradition that Rāmānuja later elaborated philosophically.

SA_GR14_yamsr_pu.title: Stotraratna
SA_GR14_yamsr_pu.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya / Āḷavandār)
SA_GR14_yamsr_pu.summary: The 'Jewel of Hymns', a celebrated devotional poem in praise of Nārāyaṇa (Viṣṇu). It expresses self-surrender (prapatti) and the soul's complete dependence on God's grace, blending intense bhakti with the doctrinal framework of Viśiṣṭādvaita, and opens with homage to the lineage of teachers (Nāthamuni, etc.).
SA_GR14_yamsr_pu.history: Composed by Yāmunācārya (c. 918-1038 CE), the Stotraratna deeply influenced Rāmānuja, who is traditionally said to have been moved to take up the teacher's mission upon Yāmuna's death. It remains a core liturgical and devotional text of the Śrī Vaiṣṇava community.

SA_GS24_jvkasipu.title: Kāśikāvṛtti
SA_GS24_jvkasipu.author: Jayāditya and Vāmana
SA_GS24_jvkasipu.summary: A running commentary (vṛtti) on Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī, the oldest surviving complete commentary covering all of Pāṇini's nearly 4000 sūtras in their original sequence. Noted for lucid explanations, illustrative examples, and awareness of earlier grammatical works (Mahābhāṣya, Cāndravyākaraṇa), it makes Pāṇini's cryptic rules accessible.
SA_GS24_jvkasipu.history: Traditionally co-authored by Jayāditya and Vāmana (c. 7th century CE), reportedly with Jayāditya responsible for the first five adhyāyas and Vāmana the rest. It became the standard reference for Pāṇinian study and was itself the basis of the major sub-commentaries Nyāsa (Jinendrabuddhi) and Padamañjarī (Haradatta).

SA_GS24_panini_u.title: Aṣṭādhyāyī
SA_GS24_panini_u.author: Pāṇini
SA_GS24_panini_u.date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE (commonly c. 350 BCE; disputed)
SA_GS24_panini_u.summary: The foundational grammar of Sanskrit, comprising nearly 4000 terse, algebraically organized sūtras in eight chapters (aṣṭa-adhyāyī) that generate correct Sanskrit forms through an ordered system of rules, metarules, and technical conventions. It is the most comprehensive and rigorous descriptive grammar of antiquity and effectively standardized Classical Sanskrit.
SA_GS24_panini_u.history: Composed by Pāṇini, probably in the northwest (Śalātura, Gandhāra) around the mid-1st millennium BCE, drawing on earlier grammarians it names. Its rule system is often compared to a formal generative grammar. It was elaborated by Kātyāyana's Vārttikas and Patañjali's Mahābhāṣya, and remains the bedrock of the entire Sanskrit grammatical tradition.

SA_GS24_paninipu.title: Aṣṭādhyāyī
SA_GS24_paninipu.author: Pāṇini
SA_GS24_paninipu.date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE (commonly c. 350 BCE; disputed)
SA_GS24_paninipu.summary: The foundational grammar of Sanskrit in nearly 4000 sūtras across eight chapters, generating correct word-forms through an ordered, rule-governed system with sophisticated metalinguistic conventions. (This is a second digital recension/encoding of the same work as GS24panini_u.)
SA_GS24_paninipu.history: By Pāṇini (mid-1st millennium BCE). The work standardized Classical Sanskrit and was elaborated by Kātyāyana and Patañjali; this entry appears to be a separate electronic edition of the same text within the corpus.

SA_GS24_paribhsu.title: Paribhāṣenduśekhara
SA_GS24_paribhsu.author: Nāgeśa Bhaṭṭa (Nāgojibhaṭṭa)
SA_GS24_paribhsu.summary: The authoritative treatise on the paribhāṣās — the interpretive metarules governing the application and ordering of Pāṇini's sūtras. It collects, explains, and critically discusses these governing principles with rigorous argumentation, becoming the standard reference on the subject.
SA_GS24_paribhsu.history: Written by Nāgeśa Bhaṭṭa (Nāgojibhaṭṭa), a Maharashtrian Brahmin grammarian active at Benares/Śṛṅgaverapura in the early 1700s under the patronage of the chieftain Rāma of the Bisena family.
