The most prolific late grammarian, his Paribhāṣenduśekhara attracted more than two dozen commentaries; it was edited and translated by F. Kielhorn.

SA_GS24_pmbhasuu.title: Vyākaraṇamahābhāṣya [Mahābhāṣya]
SA_GS24_pmbhasuu.author: Patañjali
SA_GS24_pmbhasuu.date_estimate: c. 150 BCE (original; project hint 2nd-3rd c. CE may reflect a later recension)
SA_GS24_pmbhasuu.summary: The 'Great Commentary' on Pāṇini's grammar, structured as a discursive commentary on Kātyāyana's Vārttikas and selected Pāṇinian sūtras (extant on 1228 of the 3981 sūtras). Beyond grammar it ranges into philosophy of language, semantics, and methodology, often in lively dialogue form, and is the principal source for Kātyāyana's otherwise lost Vārttikas.
SA_GS24_pmbhasuu.history: Attributed to Patañjali and conventionally dated to c. 150 BCE on the strength of internal references (e.g., to Yavana/Greek campaigns and Puṣyamitra's sacrifice). The third of the 'trimuni' (three sages: Pāṇini, Kātyāyana, Patañjali), it is the supreme authority of the grammatical tradition; the project's not_before/not_after (100-299 CE) likely reflects the dating of a particular transmitted recension rather than original composition.

SA_GS24_samanv_u.title: Samanvayadiś
SA_GS24_samanv_u.author: Kuḍaka
SA_GS24_samanv_u.summary: A Sanskrit work attributed to an author named Kuḍaka; the title ('Samanvayadiś', roughly 'the direction/method of syntactic concord or connection') suggests a treatise on samanvaya — the construal or syntactic-semantic connection of words in a sentence. Could not be confidently identified or its contents verified from available reference sources.
SA_GS24_samanv_u.history: Neither the author Kuḍaka nor the work appears in standard reference catalogues or the consulted databases; it may be a minor or modern grammatical/linguistic composition preserved in a digital corpus (e.g., GRETIL). Identification is uncertain and details could not be corroborated.

SA_GS24_vakyp_pu.title: Vākyapadīya 1.1-3.7
SA_GS24_vakyp_pu.author: Bhartṛhari
SA_GS24_vakyp_pu.summary: A portion (kāṇḍa 1 through chapter 7 of kāṇḍa 3) of Bhartṛhari's seminal treatise on the philosophy of language and grammar. The work expounds Śabdabrahman (the Word as ultimate reality), the sphoṭa theory of meaning-bearing linguistic units, and the relation of sentence and word to meaning; book 3 (Padakāṇḍa) treats semantic categories such as substance, action, time, gender, and number.
SA_GS24_vakyp_pu.history: Bhartṛhari (c. 5th century CE) is the foundational thinker of the grammarian school of Indian philosophy (Vaiyākaraṇa-darśana). The Vākyapadīya (also called Trikāṇḍī) integrates Pāṇinian grammar with metaphysics and deeply influenced later linguistics, philosophy, and aesthetics; this entry covers a defined segment of the text.

SA_GS24_vakyp1pu.title: Vākyapadīya
SA_GS24_vakyp1pu.author: Bhartṛhari
SA_GS24_vakyp1pu.summary: Bhartṛhari's foundational treatise (also Trikāṇḍī) on the philosophy of language in three books (kāṇḍas): the Brahmakāṇḍa on Śabdabrahman and the metaphysics of language, the Vākyakāṇḍa on the sentence and sphoṭa, and the Padakāṇḍa (Prakīrṇakāṇḍa) on individual words and semantic categories. (This entry appears to be the kāṇḍa-1 / full-text encoding of the same work as GS24vakyp_pu.)
SA_GS24_vakyp1pu.history: Bhartṛhari (c. 5th c. CE) founded the grammarian-philosophical school, fusing Pāṇinian linguistics with non-dual metaphysics centered on language. The Vākyapadīya was commented upon (e.g. the Vṛtti, Puṇyarāja, Helārāja) and profoundly shaped Indian theories of meaning and aesthetics.

SA_GS24_varlghku.title: Laghusiddhāntakaumudī [Laghukaumudī]
SA_GS24_varlghku.author: Varadarāja
SA_GS24_varlghku.summary: A widely used introductory manual of Pāṇinian grammar that abridges Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī, rearranging Pāṇini's sūtras topically and presenting only the most essential rules (the laghu 'short' version reduces them dramatically) so that beginners can master core Sanskrit grammar without the full system's complexity.
SA_GS24_varlghku.history: Composed by Varadarāja, a 17th-century pupil of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita, who produced three graded abridgements (madhya, laghu, sāra) of his teacher's Siddhāntakaumudī. The Laghukaumudī remains the standard teaching text in traditional pāṭhaśālās and in modern courses on Pāṇinian grammar.

SA_GS25_amark1pu.title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa, Amarakośa], Kāṇḍa 1
SA_GS25_amark1pu.author: Amarasiṃha
SA_GS25_amark1pu.summary: The first kāṇḍa of the Amarakoṣa ('Immortal Treasury'), the most famous Sanskrit thesaurus, a versified synonym-dictionary arranged thematically. Kāṇḍa 1 (the 'celestial/divine' division) covers vargas on heaven, the directions and sky, time, intellect/sound, drama, the netherworld, hell, and the waters — grouping synonyms by subject with notes on grammatical gender.
SA_GS25_amark1pu.history: Composed by Amarasiṃha, traditionally said to be a Buddhist and a 'jewel' of a royal court (often legendarily linked to Vikramāditya), around the middle of the first millennium CE. Memorized for centuries as the standard Sanskrit lexicon, it set the model for later kośas and attracted more than forty commentaries (e.g. Kṣīrasvāmin's Amarakośodghāṭana).

SA_GS25_amark2_u.title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa, Amarakośa], Kāṇḍa 2
SA_GS25_amark2_u.author: Amarasiṃha
SA_GS25_amark2_u.summary: The second and largest kāṇḍa of the Amarakoṣa, the 'earthly/terrestrial' division (Bhūmivarga, etc.). It groups synonyms by subject across the natural and social world — earth, towns, mountains, plants, animals, humans, the four varṇas and occupational classes, warriors, and the like — serving as a thematic dictionary with gender notation.
SA_GS25_amark2_u.history: Part of Amarasiṃha's Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana (c. mid-1st millennium CE), the standard Sanskrit thesaurus memorized throughout traditional education. Its subject-wise arrangement became the template for nearly all later Sanskrit lexicons and generated an extensive commentarial literature.

SA_GS25_amark3_u.title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa], Kāṇḍa 3
SA_GS25_amark3_u.author: Amarasiṃha
SA_GS25_amark3_u.tradition: secular/scientific (Sanskrit lexicography); traditionally associated with Buddhism
SA_GS25_amark3_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (often c. 5th-6th c. CE; disputed, range 200-500 CE in project hint)
SA_GS25_amark3_u.summary: The Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana, universally known as the Amarakoṣa ('Treasury of Amara'), is the most celebrated Sanskrit synonymic thesaurus, arranged in metrical verse to aid memorization. Its third book, the Sāmānyakāṇḍa (also called Sāmānyādhikāṇḍa or Nānārthavarga section), departs from the thematic arrangement of the first two books and treats general/miscellaneous vocabulary: adjectives, indeclinables, homonyms (nānārtha words), and words grouped by gender, along with verbs and terms of varied application.
SA_GS25_amark3_u.history: Attributed to Amarasiṃha, traditionally counted among the 'nine gems' (navaratna) of a royal court and often said to have Buddhist affiliation, though almost nothing certain is known of him. The Amarakoṣa became the standard reference for Sanskrit poets and pandits across India and Southeast Asia, attracting dozens of commentaries (e.g., by Kṣīrasvāmin, Maheśvara, Liṅgayasūrin) and numerous regional translations.

SA_GS26_jaytuppu.title: Tattvopaplavasiṃha
SA_GS26_jaytuppu.author: Jayarāśi Bhaṭṭa
SA_GS26_jaytuppu.tradition: Indian materialism / radical skepticism, loosely affiliated with the Cārvāka/Lokāyata tradition
SA_GS26_jaytuppu.date_estimate: c. 8th-9th c. CE (most likely c. 770-840 CE); project hint of 500-699 is too early
SA_GS26_jaytuppu.summary: The Tattvopaplavasiṃha ('The Lion that Devours All Categories/Principles') is an extraordinary skeptical treatise that systematically demolishes the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇas)—perception, inference, and testimony—as accepted by every Indian philosophical school. By undermining the very tools of cognition, Jayarāśi argues that no reliable knowledge of reality (tattva) is possible, leaving even Cārvāka's own positive claims unsupported.
SA_GS26_jaytuppu.history: The sole palm-leaf manuscript was discovered in 1926 and first published in 1940 by Sukhlalji Sanghavi and R. C. Parikh. Jayarāśi is a uniquely radical figure: though linked to the materialist Cārvāka/Lokāyata, his thoroughgoing skepticism distinguishes him from typical materialists and makes the work a singular monument of Indian anti-foundationalist philosophy.

SA_GS26_sarvmtsu.title: Sarvamatasaṃgraha
SA_GS26_sarvmtsu.author: anonymous/traditional (attribution uncertain)
SA_GS26_sarvmtsu.tradition: Hindu (doxography surveying multiple schools, both orthodox and heterodox)
SA_GS26_sarvmtsu.summary: The Sarvamatasaṃgraha ('Compendium of All Doctrines') is a doxographical compendium that summarizes the tenets of the various Indian philosophical and religious systems (mata), including both the orthodox darśanas and heterodox schools such as Cārvāka, Buddhism, and Jainism. Such surveys present each school's core positions in brief, often arranged hierarchically.
SA_GS26_sarvmtsu.history: It belongs to the medieval Sanskrit doxographical genre exemplified by Mādhava's far more famous Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha; the Sarvamatasaṃgraha is a smaller, less securely attributed work, and its authorship and precise date remain uncertain. Such compendia are valuable as concise indigenous overviews of the spectrum of Indian thought.

SA_GS26_sbhami_u.title: Āptamīmāṃsā [Devāgamastotra]
SA_GS26_sbhami_u.author: Samantabhadra
SA_GS26_sbhami_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (traditionally placed c. 2nd c. CE; commonly dated c. 5th-6th c. CE)
SA_GS26_sbhami_u.summary: The Āptamīmāṃsā ('Examination/Critique of the Trustworthy Authority'), also called the Devāgamastotra after its opening word, is a concise treatise of 114 verses that investigates the concept of the omniscient, trustworthy teacher (āpta) and the nature of reality. It develops the foundational Jain doctrines of anekāntavāda (non-absolutism / many-sidedness) and syādvāda (conditional, qualified predication).
SA_GS26_sbhami_u.history: Composed by the Digambara Jain ācārya Samantabhadra, it is a seminal work of Jain logic that generated a major commentarial tradition, including Akalaṅka's Aṣṭaśatī and Vidyānanda's Aṣṭasahasrī. It remains one of the most influential statements of Jain epistemology and dialectic.

SA_GS39_jyopancu.title: Pañcasāyaka
SA_GS39_jyopancu.author: Jyotirīśvara Kaviśekhara (Jyotirīśvara Ṭhakkura)
SA_GS39_jyopancu.summary: The Pañcasāyaka ('Five Arrows', named for the five flower-arrows of Kāmadeva) is a Sanskrit manual on the science of erotics (Kāmaśāstra) in five parts. Drawing on Vātsyāyana and later authorities, it classifies types of men and women, treats sexual positions, aphrodisiacs, charms, and the arts of love.
SA_GS39_jyopancu.history: Written by Jyotirīśvara Kaviśekhara (Jyotirīśvara Ṭhakkura), a Maithil court poet also known for the Varṇaratnākara. N. N. Bhattacharyya places it in the first half of the 14th century on the basis of references in Kṣemendra and Śaṅkaramiśra's commentary on the Gītagovinda. It is one of the most widely cited of the post-Kāmasūtra erotic manuals.

SA_GS39_kamasutu.title: Kāmasūtra
SA_GS39_kamasutu.author: Vātsyāyana Mallanāga
SA_GS39_kamasutu.tradition: Hindu (secular erotics / one of the three classical aims of life, kāma)
SA_GS39_kamasutu.date_estimate: c. 3rd c. CE (2nd-4th c. CE; disputed)
SA_GS39_kamasutu.summary: The Kāmasūtra is the foundational Sanskrit treatise on kāma (pleasure, desire, love), one of the three legitimate aims of human life. Composed in aphoristic sūtra style, it covers far more than sexual technique: the conduct of the cultured man-about-town (nāgaraka), courtship and marriage, the duties of wives, courtesans, seduction, and the sixty-four arts, presenting a comprehensive social vision of erotic and domestic life.
SA_GS39_kamasutu.history: Attributed to Vātsyāyana, whose personal name the commentator Yaśodhara gives as Mallanāga; the author states he distilled earlier (now lost) works by Bābhravya and others. It is most often dated to around the 3rd century CE in northern India. Yaśodhara's Jayamaṅgalā is its major commentary, and the text became the canonical reference for all later Kāmaśāstra literature.

SA_GS39_minsmaru.title: Smaradīpikā
SA_GS39_minsmaru.author: Mīnanātha
SA_GS39_minsmaru.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (likely before c. 13th c. CE, as it was drawn upon by later authors)
SA_GS39_minsmaru.summary: The Smaradīpikā ('Lamp of Love/Desire') is a short Sanskrit erotic treatise that classifies men and women into types according to physique and temperament, discusses compatible matches, signs of arousal, and techniques of lovemaking. It belongs to the body of post-Kāmasūtra Kāmaśāstra manuals.
SA_GS39_minsmaru.history: Attributed to Mīnanātha; the work was synthesized and drawn upon by later writers, including material later assembled in the Ratimañjarī tradition associated with the name Jayadeva. Its precise date and the identity of its author are not securely established.

SA_GS39_purukadu.title: Kādambarīsvīkaraṇasūtramañjarī
SA_GS39_purukadu.author: Purūravas (attributed)
SA_GS39_purukadu.tradition: Hindu (secular); apparently a light/satirical śāstric parody on wine-drinking
SA_GS39_purukadu.genre: sūtra-style treatise (mañjarī), likely a humorous/mock-śāstra on liquor (Kādambarī = a kind of wine)
SA_GS39_purukadu.summary: The title Kādambarīsvīkaraṇasūtramañjarī appears to mean a 'cluster of aphorisms on the imbibing (svīkaraṇa) of Kādambarī (wine/liquor)'—evidently a brief mock-śāstric or convivial work, in sūtra form, on the appreciation of wine. The text is very obscure and no detailed scholarly description could be confirmed.
SA_GS39_purukadu.history: Attributed to a 'Purūravas', and the work is little documented in available indological literature; its date, authorship, and exact content could not be verified. (Note: 'Kādambarī' here denotes a type of spirituous liquor, not Bāṇa's famous prose romance.)

SA_GS40_anandk_u.title: Ānandakanda
SA_GS40_anandk_u.author: attributed to Maṇṭhāna Bhairava (anonymous tradition)
SA_GS40_anandk_u.tradition: Hindu (Tantric Śaiva alchemy / Rasaśāstra), with strong Tantric and Haṭhayoga dimensions
SA_GS40_anandk_u.summary: The Ānandakanda ('Root/Bulb of Bliss') is a large medieval Sanskrit alchemical compendium that systematically treats both dhātuvāda (transmutation of base metals into gold) and dehavāda (the preparation of elixirs for bodily immortality and rejuvenation). It is notable for integrating alchemy with Tantra and yogic practice.
SA_GS40_anandk_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to the Siddha Maṇṭhāna Bhairava and assigned to roughly the 13th century, it is prized for its systematically organized subject matter and is described as comparatively independent of earlier rasaśāstra texts; it is also an important source of Tantric material.

SA_GS40_ashr.title: Aṣṭāṅga Hṛdaya
SA_GS40_ashr.author: Vāgbhaṭa (often identified as the 'Laghu' Vāgbhaṭa)
SA_GS40_ashr.tradition: Hindu (Ayurveda); author traditionally said to have Buddhist affiliation
SA_GS40_ashr.summary: The Aṣṭāṅga Hṛdaya ('Heart/Essence of the Eight Branches') is one of the foundational classics of Ayurveda, organizing medical knowledge under the eight limbs of the discipline (internal medicine, surgery, toxicology, pediatrics, etc.). Composed largely in elegant verse, it distills and harmonizes the earlier teachings of Caraka and Suśruta into a concise, systematic handbook.
SA_GS40_ashr.history: Attributed to Vāgbhaṭa, traditionally a Buddhist physician, and usually dated to around the 6th-7th century CE; it is closely related to the companion Aṣṭāṅga Saṃgraha. It is counted among the 'Bṛhattrayī' (great triad) of Ayurvedic classics and remains especially authoritative in Kerala and Tibetan medicine, attracting many commentaries (notably Aruṇadatta's and Hemādri's).

SA_GS40_bhavpr_u.title: Bhāvaprakāśa
SA_GS40_bhavpr_u.author: Bhāvamiśra
SA_GS40_bhavpr_u.summary: The Bhāvaprakāśa is a comprehensive Ayurvedic compendium covering basic principles, anatomy and physiology, diagnosis, and treatment, and it incorporates a celebrated materia-medica section, the Bhāvaprakāśa Nighaṇṭu, which classifies medicinal substances (plant, mineral, and animal) into numerous vargas (groups). It is notable for documenting drugs newly introduced through trade and contact, including substances treated in Unani medicine.
SA_GS40_bhavpr_u.history: Composed by Bhāvamiśra in the 16th century, it is counted among the 'Laghutrayī' (lesser triad) of Ayurvedic classics. Its nighaṇṭu became a standard reference for dravyaguṇa (pharmacology) and reflects the evolving materia medica of early-modern India.

SA_GS40_dhrasciu.title: Rasendracintāmaṇi
SA_GS40_dhrasciu.author: Ḍhuṇḍhukanātha (Dhuṇḍhukanātha)
SA_GS40_dhrasciu.date_estimate: medieval; uncertain (commonly placed c. 13th-16th c. CE)
SA_GS40_dhrasciu.summary: The Rasendracintāmaṇi ('Wish-jewel of the Lord of Essences, i.e., Mercury') is a Sanskrit alchemical/iatrochemical treatise dealing with the purification and processing of mercury and other minerals and metals, and their use in preparing medicinal and rejuvenating preparations (rasa-yogas).
SA_GS40_dhrasciu.history: Attributed to Ḍhuṇḍhukanātha, the work was influential on later rasaśāstra literature; many prescriptions in works such as the Rasendrasārasaṃgraha derive from it. Its precise date is not securely established in the available sources.

SA_GS40_grasht_u.title: Rasahṛdayatantra
SA_GS40_grasht_u.author: Govindabhagavatpāda (Govinda)
SA_GS40_grasht_u.date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (some place it 9th-10th c. CE)
SA_GS40_grasht_u.summary: The Rasahṛdayatantra ('Heart of Mercury') is generally regarded as the earliest systematic work of the Sanskrit alchemical genre rasaśāstra. Across its roughly nineteen chapters it sets out the means of processing mercury (the eighteen saṃskāras), the transmutation of metals, and the use of mercurial elixirs for the body, and it is credited with first documenting the kupīpakva (bottle-cooked) rasāyana method.
SA_GS40_grasht_u.history: Attributed to Govinda, often styled Govindabhagavatpāda (a name also linked, controversially, with the teacher of Śaṅkara), and commonly dated to about the 10th century CE. As the foundational rasaśāstra text it strongly shaped all subsequent Indian alchemical literature.

SA_GS40_nitrrk_u.title: Rasaratnākara
SA_GS40_nitrrk_u.author: Nityanātha Siddha [Nemanātha]
SA_GS40_nitrrk_u.date_estimate: c. 13th-14th c. CE (disputed; some place Nityanātha earlier)
SA_GS40_nitrrk_u.summary: The Rasaratnākara ('Jewel-mine of Mercury') is a large Siddha alchemical compendium divided into five khaṇḍas—on mercury (rasakhaṇḍa), the lord of essences (rasendrakhaṇḍa), doctrine (vādakhaṇḍa), elixirs and tonics (rasāyanakhaṇḍa), and powerful utterances/mantras (mantrakhaṇḍa). It treats mercurial and mineral processing, transmutation, and rejuvenation, and draws on earlier works such as Govinda's Rasahṛdayatantra.
SA_GS40_nitrrk_u.history: Attributed to the Siddha Nityanātha (also called Nemanātha), it is usually placed around the 13th-14th century, though datings vary widely. It is one of the central monuments of the Siddha alchemical tradition. (It should not be confused with the differently authored Rasaratnākara attributed to Nāgārjuna.)

SA_GS40_rajaniu.title: Rasa-jala-nidhi
SA_GS40_rajaniu.author: Bhudeb Mookerji (Kaviraj Bhudeb Mookerjee), compiler/translator
SA_GS40_rajaniu.tradition: Hindu (Rasaśāstra / Ayurvedic alchemy); modern scholarly compilation
SA_GS40_rajaniu.genre: modern compendium of Rasaśāstra (alchemy and iatrochemistry) with translation
SA_GS40_rajaniu.summary: The Rasa-jala-nidhi ('Ocean of Indian Chemistry and Alchemy') is a five-volume compendium of Sanskrit verses on rasaśāstra (Indian alchemy and iatrochemistry) with English translation, covering initiation and laboratory practice, mercurial operations, the purification and medicinal use of minerals and metals, gems, salts, poisons, and chemical medicine (rasa-cikitsā).
SA_GS40_rajaniu.history: Compiled and translated by Rasācārya Kaviraj Bhudeb Mookerji and published in Calcutta in the early 20th century (volumes appearing c. 1926-1938). Though drawing on classical alchemical sources, it is itself a modern editorial compilation that became a widely used reference and English entry point to rasaśāstra.

SA_GS40_rajnighu.title: Rājanighaṇṭu [Nighaṇṭurāja, Abhidhānacūḍāmaṇi]
SA_GS40_rajnighu.author: Narahari Paṇḍita [Nṛsiṃha]
SA_GS40_rajnighu.date_estimate: c. 13th-15th c. CE (commonly c. 13th-14th c.; some place it 15th-17th c.)
SA_GS40_rajnighu.summary: The Rājanighaṇṭu (also Nighaṇṭurāja, Abhidhānacūḍāmaṇi, or Dravyābhidhānaganasaṅgraha) is a major Ayurvedic lexicon of medicinal substances, arranged in some twenty-odd vargas (chapters) that catalogue plants, minerals, animal products, and other materia medica together with their synonyms and properties. It is one of the most comprehensive of the nighaṇṭus.
SA_GS40_rajnighu.history: Composed by Narahari Paṇḍita (also called Nṛsiṃha), son of Īśvarasūri and a Kashmiri scholar conversant with several South Indian languages. It expanded the materia medica beyond earlier nighaṇṭus and remained a standard reference; its precise date is debated, with most scholars placing it in the 13th-15th centuries.

SA_GS40_rasadh_u.title: Rasādhyāya
SA_GS40_rasadh_u.author: anonymous/uncertain
SA_GS40_rasadh_u.summary: The Rasādhyāya ('Chapter/Disquisition on Mercury [rasa]') is a Sanskrit alchemical text giving a systematic account of the processing of mercury (the eighteen saṃskāras / aṣṭādaśa-saṃskāra) for both metallic transmutation and bodily transformation, contributing methods for the preparation of various rasa-yogas (mercurial medicines).
SA_GS40_rasadh_u.history: The title 'Rasādhyāya' is borne by more than one alchemical text/section and its authorship and date are not securely fixed in the available sources; it is treated as an important but textually somewhat fluid work within the rasaśāstra corpus. (Two records in this batch carry this title and may represent distinct editions or recensions.)

SA_GS40_rasadhpu.title: Rasādhyāya
SA_GS40_rasadhpu.author: anonymous/uncertain
SA_GS40_rasadhpu.summary: The Rasādhyāya ('Chapter/Disquisition on Mercury [rasa]') is a Sanskrit alchemical text describing the systematic processing of mercury (the eighteen saṃskāras) for metallic and metabolic transformation and the preparation of mercurial medicines. This record is a second instance of the title in the batch, likely a distinct edition or recension of the same work.
SA_GS40_rasadhpu.history: As with its companion record, the name 'Rasādhyāya' designates more than one alchemical text/section, and authorship and date are not securely established; it belongs to the medieval rasaśāstra corpus.

SA_GS40_rasarnpu.title: Rasārṇava: Paṭalas 1-12
SA_GS40_rasarnpu.author: anonymous/traditional (sometimes attributed to Bhairavānanda; uncertain)
SA_GS40_rasarnpu.date_estimate: c. 12th c. CE (text as a whole)
SA_GS40_rasarnpu.summary: The Rasārṇava ('Ocean of Mercury') is a major Tantric alchemical work in eighteen chapters (paṭalas), cast as a dialogue between Śiva (Bhairava) and the Goddess. This record covers paṭalas 1-12, which set out the religious framing of alchemy, the purification (saṃskāra) of mercury and other minerals, alchemical apparatus, and processes of transmutation, embedding the chemistry in Tantric symbolism (mercury as Śiva's seed, mica/sulphur as the Goddess's emission).
SA_GS40_rasarnpu.history: The complete Rasārṇava is an anonymous Śaiva Tantric text usually assigned to about the 12th century CE; an attribution to 'Bhairavānanda' is sometimes mentioned but remains uncertain. It is one of the most influential Tantric alchemical scriptures and was studied by D. G. White and others for its fusion of Tantra and chemistry.

SA_GS40_rascum_u.title: Rasendracūḍāmaṇi
SA_GS40_rascum_u.author: Somadeva
SA_GS40_rascum_u.summary: The Rasendracūḍāmaṇi ('Crest-jewel of the Lord of Essences, i.e., Mercury') is a Sanskrit alchemical treatise in about thirteen chapters offering a comprehensive treatment of mercury and its processing for medicinal and transmutational purposes, along with minerals, metals, and mercurial preparations.
SA_GS40_rascum_u.history: Composed by Somadeva, it is largely based on earlier chemical treatises and is usually dated to the 12th-13th century CE. The later Rasaratnasamuccaya explicitly draws on Somadeva's descriptions, attesting to the work's authority within the rasaśāstra tradition.

SA_GS40_rassanku.title: Rasasaṃketakalikā
SA_GS40_rassanku.author: Cāmuṇḍa (Kāyastha Cāmuṇḍa, also called Caṇḍa)
SA_GS40_rassanku.genre: rasaśāstra / Ayurvedic alchemical and pharmaceutical treatise
SA_GS40_rassanku.summary: A compact rasaśāstra manual dealing with the processing (śodhana, māraṇa) of mercury, metals, minerals and gems, and their use in medicinal preparations. It belongs to the iatrochemical phase of Indian alchemy in which alchemical knowledge was systematized for therapeutic ends.
SA_GS40_rassanku.history: Authored by the Kāyastha Cāmuṇḍa (Caṇḍa); the text survives in manuscripts and has been edited and translated into English (e.g. by P. Satyanarayana Sastry, Chowkhamba). Its precise date is not firmly established and remains debated among historians of Indian alchemy.

SA_GS40_salrasmu.title: Rasamañjarī
SA_GS40_salrasmu.author: Śālinātha
SA_GS40_salrasmu.summary: A rasaśāstra text describing a wide range of mercurial and herbo-mineral medicinal preparations, their methods of manufacture and therapeutic application. Chapters include treatment of rasāyana (rejuvenation) and ariṣṭa (prognostic/fatal signs), and it discusses pottalī-type formulations.
SA_GS40_salrasmu.history: Attributed to Ācārya Śālinātha; the work is studied in Ayurvedic pharmaceutics and has a modern Hindi commentary by Siddhinandan Mishra. Several alchemical texts bear the title Rasamañjarī (one is ascribed to Śālinātha), and dating is not securely fixed.

SA_GS40_samapviu.title: Āyurvedamahodadhau Annapānavidhi
SA_GS40_samapviu.author: Suṣeṇa (Suṣeṇa Deva)
SA_GS40_samapviu.genre: Ayurvedic treatise (dietetics section of a larger work)
SA_GS40_samapviu.summary: The Annapānavidhi ('rules concerning food and drink') is a section of the larger Ayurvedic work Āyurvedamahodadhi, dealing in detail with dietetics: the classification, qualities and proper consumption of foods and beverages and their effect on health. It belongs to the genre of svasthavṛtta (regimen for health) in Ayurveda.
SA_GS40_samapviu.history: Composed by Suṣeṇa (Suṣeṇa Deva), to whom an Ayurvedic nighaṇṭu (Suṣeṇa Nighaṇṭu) is also attributed. The Annapānavidhi portion was edited and published in the Thanjavur Sarasvati Mahal series; its precise date is not established.

SA_GS40_kalyanakaraka.title: Kalyāṇakāraka
SA_GS40_kalyanakaraka.author: Ugrāditya
SA_GS40_kalyanakaraka.summary: A comprehensive Jain Ayurvedic compendium covering daily and seasonal regimens, anatomy, diagnosis, the eight branches of medicine and treatment procedures, with a notable section advocating strict vegetarianism and rejecting animal/alcoholic substances in medicine in line with Jain ethics. It also contains a special section on prognosis and prediction of a patient's death.
SA_GS40_kalyanakaraka.history: Composed by the Jain monk Ugrāditya, traditionally at Rāmagiri hill in Veṅgī (Andhra). Scholars date it to the first half of the 9th century; its vegetarianism chapter is said to have been delivered at the court of the Rāṣṭrakūṭa king Nṛpatuṅga (Amoghavarṣa) c. 830 CE. It is a key source for the Jain contribution to Indian medicine.

SA_GS40_vagaah_u.title: Aṣṭāṅgahṛdayasūtra (Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya, Sūtrasthāna)
SA_GS40_vagaah_u.author: Vāgbhaṭa
SA_GS40_vagaah_u.genre: Ayurvedic treatise (saṃhitā); this is the Sūtrasthāna (foundational principles section)
SA_GS40_vagaah_u.summary: The Sūtrasthāna is the opening, foundational section of the Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya, one of the three classical 'great' Ayurvedic compendia (bṛhattrayī). It lays out the fundamentals of Ayurveda: the doctrine of the three doṣas, daily and seasonal regimen (dinacaryā, ṛtucaryā), diet, the qualities of substances, and the basics of pañcakarma therapy, in elegant, condensed verse.
SA_GS40_vagaah_u.history: Vāgbhaṭa, who flourished c. 600-700 CE (likely in Sindh and connected to a Buddhist milieu), distilled the teachings of Caraka and Suśruta into the Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya, the most widely used of the bṛhattrayī, attracting many commentaries (notably Aruṇadatta's Sarvāṅgasundarā and Hemādri's Āyurvedarasāyana). The metadata's 700-800 hint is slightly late; most scholars favour the 6th-7th c.

SA_GS40_vanighpu.title: Aṣṭāṅganighaṇṭu
SA_GS40_vanighpu.author: Vāhaṭa (a Vāgbhaṭa)
SA_GS40_vanighpu.summary: An Ayurvedic glossary (nighaṇṭu) that catalogues medicinal substances—chiefly plants—grouping synonyms of each drug together and giving their properties for ease of study. It is one of the simpler, study-oriented lexicons of the nighaṇṭu tradition of Ayurvedic materia medica.
SA_GS40_vanighpu.history: Attributed to Vāhaṭa (a form of the name Vāgbhaṭa); part of the broader nighaṇṭu literature that includes the Dhanvantari, Madanapāla and other lexicons. Its precise authorship and date are uncertain.

SA_GS40_vrasrs_u.title: Rasaratnasamuccaya
SA_GS40_vrasrs_u.author: Vāgbhaṭa (the alchemical Vāgbhaṭa; not the Ayurvedic author of the Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya)
SA_GS40_vrasrs_u.summary: The most authoritative classical compendium of Indian alchemy, a systematic 'collection of the gems of rasaśāstra'. It describes mercury (and the eight saṃskāras of its processing), metals, minerals and gems, their purification, incineration into bhasmas, the apparatus (yantras) and laboratory (rasaśālā) needed, and the use of herbo-mineral preparations to treat numerous diseases.
SA_GS40_vrasrs_u.history: Authored by an alchemical Vāgbhaṭa, distinct from the earlier author of the Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya. It compiles and supersedes earlier rasa texts and became the standard reference of the iatrochemical period, drawing many later commentaries. This record is the root text.

SA_GS40_vrasrscu.title: Rasaratnasamuccaya (with commentary)
SA_GS40_vrasrscu.author: Vāgbhaṭa (mūla); commentary by a later commentator (e.g. Āśubodha/Vinodalāla or others)
SA_GS40_vrasrscu.date_estimate: root text c. 13th-14th c. CE; commentary later
SA_GS40_vrasrscu.summary: The same classical alchemical compendium Rasaratnasamuccaya of Vāgbhaṭa, here in a commented version. The commentary glosses the technical alchemical vocabulary, the processing of mercury and metals into therapeutic bhasmas, the apparatus, and the herbo-mineral formulations of the base text.
SA_GS40_vrasrscu.history: This record parallels the root Rasaratnasamuccaya but represents a version accompanied by a Sanskrit commentary; several commentaries on the text exist. The base work is by the alchemical Vāgbhaṭa, distinct from the author of the Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya.

SA_GS40_yogasatu.title: Yogaśataka
SA_GS40_yogasatu.author: anonymous; traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna (Nāgārjunīya school)
SA_GS40_yogasatu.summary: An Ayurvedic handbook of about one hundred therapeutic 'combinations' (yogas), i.e. compound prescriptions. More a practical collection of recipes than a theoretical treatise, it covers cures for fever, eye disorders, poisoning, demon-caused and childhood ailments, aphrodisiacs, rejuvenatives and purgatives.
SA_GS40_yogasatu.history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna (within the Nāgārjunīya medical tradition); if the report of Yijing is accepted, its compiler lived in the 6th century. Its popularity is shown by its translation into Tibetan and Tocharian (Kuchean), and the text was edited and translated into French (Yogaśataka, IFP, Pondicherry).

SA_GS40_yrasprpu.title: Rasaprakāśasudhākara
SA_GS40_yrasprpu.author: Yaśodhara (Yaśodhara Bhaṭṭa)
SA_GS40_yrasprpu.summary: An important rasaśāstra treatise in thirteen chapters describing mercury, metals, minerals and gems—their varieties, qualities, distinction of good from bad, methods of purification (śodhana) and incineration (māraṇa), and the preparation of medicinal bhasmas. It is a notable source for early Indian knowledge of the metallurgy of zinc and for special preparations such as Somanāthī tāmra-bhasma.
SA_GS40_yrasprpu.history: Composed by Yaśodhara (Yaśodhara Bhaṭṭa) around the 13th century, it is counted among the principal works of the iatrochemical period of Indian alchemy. The metadata hint of 1500-1699 is too late; scholarly consensus places it in the 13th c. CE.

SA_GS41_hayanaratna.title: Hāyanaratna
SA_GS41_hayanaratna.author: Balabhadra (Balabhadra Daivajña)
SA_GS41_hayanaratna.genre: jyotiṣa / astrological treatise (nibandha on annual horoscopy)
SA_GS41_hayanaratna.summary: A major treatise of the Tājika tradition—the Sanskritized adaptation of Perso-Arabic astrology blended with pre-Islamic Indian jyotiṣa and Hellenistic elements. The Hāyanaratna ('Jewel of the Year') is a meta-commentary (nibandha) chiefly concerned with varṣaphala, the casting and interpretation of annual (anniversary/solar-return) horoscopes.
SA_GS41_hayanaratna.history: Written in 1649 CE by Balabhadra Daivajña, an astrologer associated with the Mughal court, drawing on and digesting many earlier Tājika authorities. It has been critically edited and translated into English by Martin Gansten (Brill, 2020).

SA_GS41_aryabh_u.title: Āryabhaṭīya
SA_GS41_aryabh_u.author: Āryabhaṭa (Āryabhaṭa I)
SA_GS41_aryabh_u.summary: A foundational, highly condensed Sanskrit treatise on mathematics and astronomy in 121 verses across four sections: Gītikāpāda (astronomical constants), Gaṇitapāda (arithmetic, algebra, geometry, the sine table, series, an approximation of π ≈ 3.1416), Kālakriyāpāda (reckoning of time and planetary longitudes), and Golapāda (spherical astronomy, eclipses). It famously proposes the Earth's axial rotation.
SA_GS41_aryabh_u.history: Composed by Āryabhaṭa in 499 CE (at age 23), it founded a major school of Indian mathematical astronomy and attracted numerous commentaries (Bhāskara I, Someśvara, Nīlakaṇṭha Somayāji, etc.). It introduced influential algorithms including the kuṭṭaka (pulverizer). This record is the root (mūla) text.

SA_GS41_aryabhcu.title: Āryabhaṭīya (with commentary)
SA_GS41_aryabhcu.author: Āryabhaṭa (mūla); commentary by a later astronomer (e.g. Bhāskara I, 629 CE, or Nīlakaṇṭha Somayāji)
SA_GS41_aryabhcu.date_estimate: root text 499 CE; commentary 7th c. CE or later
SA_GS41_aryabhcu.summary: The same Āryabhaṭīya of Āryabhaṭa, here in a commented version. The commentary expounds the terse aphoristic verses—working out the mathematical procedures (square/cube roots, sine table, kuṭṭaka), the astronomical parameters, time-reckoning and spherical astronomy that the root text only states cryptically.
SA_GS41_aryabhcu.history: The Āryabhaṭīya generated a rich commentarial tradition; the earliest surviving commentary is by Bhāskara I (629 CE), followed by many others including Nīlakaṇṭha Somayāji's. This record corresponds to the text accompanied by such a commentary.

SA_GS41_bijaganu.title: Bījagaṇita
SA_GS41_bijaganu.author: Bhāskara (Bhāskara II / Bhāskarācārya)
SA_GS41_bijaganu.summary: A classic Sanskrit treatise on algebra, forming part of Bhāskara II's larger Siddhāntaśiromaṇi. It treats operations on positive and negative quantities and zero, surds, the kuṭṭaka (indeterminate linear equations), quadratic and higher equations, and the cakravāla method for Pell-type equations (e.g. Nx²+1=y²).
SA_GS41_bijaganu.history: Written by Bhāskara II (1114-1185) c. 1150 CE—not the 7th-century Bhāskara I, so the metadata's 600-799 hint is incorrect. The Bījagaṇita was highly influential, later translated into Persian (1634) and studied in Europe. It is one of the high points of medieval Indian mathematics.

SA_GS41_brhajj_u.title: Bṛhajjātaka (Bṛhat Jātaka)
SA_GS41_brhajj_u.author: Varāhamihira
SA_GS41_brhajj_u.summary: A standard, systematic treatise on horā (natal/horoscopic) astrology, treating the zodiac signs, planets, houses, planetary combinations and yogas, and the prediction of life-events such as longevity, profession and marriage from a birth chart. It became the foundational textbook of Indian predictive astrology.
SA_GS41_brhajj_u.history: Composed by Varāhamihira (b. c. 505 CE, near Ujjain) in the 6th century, it is one of his five principal works (alongside the Pañcasiddhāntikā, Bṛhatsaṃhitā, Laghujātaka and Yogayātrā) and shows assimilation of Hellenistic Greek astrology into the Indian tradition. It drew many commentaries (notably Bhaṭṭotpala's).

SA_GS41_brhats_u.title: Bṛhatsaṃhitā
SA_GS41_brhats_u.author: Varāhamihira
SA_GS41_brhats_u.genre: encyclopaedic treatise (jyotiṣa / omens, natural science, śilpa)
SA_GS41_brhats_u.summary: A wide-ranging encyclopaedia in about 106 chapters covering astral and natural omens, planetary and stellar movements, eclipses, rainfall and clouds (weather prognostication), agriculture, architecture (vāstu) and iconography, gemmology and pearls, perfumery, matrimony, and ritual. It is a key source for the material and scientific culture of Gupta-era India.
SA_GS41_brhats_u.history: Compiled by Varāhamihira in the 6th century, it belongs to the saṃhitā (mundane/omen) branch of jyotiṣa. Its breadth makes it an invaluable witness to 6th-century Indian science, society and art; it too was commented on by Bhaṭṭotpala (10th c.).

SA_GS41_brhatparasarahorasastram.title: Bṛhatpārāśarahorāśāstram
SA_GS41_brhatparasarahorasastram.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Maharṣi Parāśara)
SA_GS41_brhatparasarahorasastram.date_estimate: composite; core c. 7th-8th c. CE, expanded/redacted later
SA_GS41_brhatparasarahorasastram.summary: The most comprehensive extant treatise on Indian natal (horā) astrology, ascribed to the sage Parāśara. It covers cosmogony, descriptions of planets and zodiac signs, vargas (divisional charts), planetary combinations, the daśā systems (especially Viṃśottarī) and remedial measures, and is a primary reference of modern Vedic astrology.
SA_GS41_brhatparasarahorasastram.history: Though attributed to Maharṣi Parāśara, it is a composite work of uncertain origin; the oldest printed core (chs. 1-51) is dated to the 7th-early 8th c. CE and a second part (chs. 52-71) to the later 8th c., with the popular 97-chapter recension being a much later expansion. Authorship and original date remain unknown.

SA_GS41_phaladipika.title: Phaladīpikā
SA_GS41_phaladipika.author: Mantreśvara
SA_GS41_phaladipika.summary: An authoritative and comprehensive treatise on predictive astrology in roughly 865 verses across 28 chapters. It systematically expounds the signs, planets, houses, yogas and daśās and the interpretation of horoscopes to predict events of life, and is widely used alongside the Bṛhatpārāśarahorāśāstra and Bṛhajjātaka.
SA_GS41_phaladipika.history: Written by Mantreśvara, whose dates are uncertain (variously placed from the 13th-14th to the 16th century). It remains one of the most popular practical handbooks of traditional Indian astrology and has many printed editions and translations.

SA_GS41_brsphutu.title: Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta
SA_GS41_brsphutu.author: Brahmagupta
SA_GS41_brsphutu.summary: A major treatise of mathematical astronomy that also contains landmark pure mathematics: the first systematic rules for arithmetic with zero and negative numbers, methods for square roots, solutions of linear and quadratic equations, summation of series, Brahmagupta's identity and his theorem on cyclic quadrilaterals, plus astronomical computations of planetary positions, eclipses, and the length of the year.
SA_GS41_brsphutu.history: Composed by Brahmagupta in 628 CE at Bhillamāla (Bhinmal). It is celebrated for formalizing zero as a number and for rules on negative quantities; it became the chief text of the Brāhmapakṣa and, through its Arabic translation (the Sindhind), transmitted Indian astronomy and the decimal system to the Islamic world.

SA_GS41_lagrvvju.title: Ṛgvedavedāṅgajyotiṣa (Ārca/Ṛk recension of the Vedāṅgajyotiṣa)
SA_GS41_lagrvvju.author: attributed to Lagadha (anonymous/traditional)
SA_GS41_lagrvvju.date_estimate: late Vedic; extant text c. 1st millennium BCE (often c. 1400-1200 BCE traditionally, c. 5th c. BCE on linguistic grounds; disputed)
SA_GS41_lagrvvju.summary: The Ṛgveda recension of the Vedāṅgajyotiṣa, the oldest surviving Indian astronomical text, here in 36 verses. It provides a calendrical and astronomical framework—a five-year yuga, lunar and solar months, tithis, nakṣatras, intercalation and solstice positions—to fix the correct times for Vedic sacrifices and rituals.
SA_GS41_lagrvvju.history: Traditionally ascribed to the sage Lagadha; it is the jyotiṣa Vedāṅga (one of the six limbs of the Veda). Its solstitial data are used to argue for a late-Vedic date, with traditional estimates ranging c. 1400-1200 BCE and conservative linguistic estimates around the mid-1st millennium BCE; the dating is much debated. The text survives in Ṛk (Ārca) and Yajus recensions.

SA_GS41_lilavatu.title: Līlāvatī
SA_GS41_lilavatu.author: Bhāskara II (Bhāskarācārya)
SA_GS41_lilavatu.summary: The Līlāvatī is the arithmetic-and-geometry section of Bhāskara II's larger astronomical compendium Siddhāntaśiromaṇi. In thirteen chapters it treats arithmetic operations, fractions, proportions, arithmetic and geometric progressions, interest, plane and solid mensuration, gnomon/shadow problems, indeterminate equations (kuṭṭaka), and combinatorics, often framed as charming problems addressed to a girl named Līlāvatī. It is one of the most influential pedagogical mathematics texts of premodern India.
SA_GS41_lilavatu.history: Composed by Bhāskara II (b. 1114 CE) of the Ujjain astronomical school, the work circulated for centuries as a standard arithmetic primer and was translated into Persian under Akbar (by Faizi, 1587) and later into European languages. Numerous Sanskrit commentaries (e.g. by Gaṇeśa Daivajña) attest to its enduring authority.

SA_GS41_surysidu.title: Sūryasiddhānta
SA_GS41_surysidu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to a revelation by Sūrya, the Sun-god, to the asura Maya)
SA_GS41_surysidu.date_estimate: extant recension c. 4th-9th c. CE (composite, with earlier and later layers); core often placed c. 400-600 CE
SA_GS41_surysidu.summary: The Sūryasiddhānta is one of the foundational Sanskrit treatises of Indian mathematical astronomy, presented as instruction revealed by the Sun-god. In fourteen chapters it covers planetary mean and true motions, eclipses, heliacal risings and settings, the phases of the moon, planetary conjunctions, cosmography, time-reckoning, and the construction of astronomical instruments, and it embeds early sine-table trigonometry.
SA_GS41_surysidu.history: The text is a 'living document' revised over centuries; the surviving version differs from the older Sūryasiddhānta paraphrased by Varāhamihira in his Pañcasiddhāntikā. It became the most widely cited and commented astronomical authority in India and underlies much of later Hindu calendrical practice; E. Burgess produced an influential English translation in 1860.

SA_GS41_tikaniku.title: Tikanikayātrā
SA_GS41_tikaniku.author: Varāhamihira
SA_GS41_tikaniku.summary: The Tikanikayātrā is one of Varāhamihira's shorter 'yātrā' (military expedition / journey) astrology works, concerned with determining auspicious times and omens for a king setting out on a campaign or journey. It treats planetary and nakṣatra configurations, omens, and ritual precautions governing the success of an expedition, condensing the subject matter handled at greater length in his Bṛhadyātrā and Yogayātrā.
SA_GS41_tikaniku.history: Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE), the celebrated astronomer-astrologer of Ujjain, composed several works on yātrā (catarchic/military astrology); the Tikanikayātrā is the most compact of these and survives mainly through manuscripts and editions of his astrological corpus.

SA_GS41_vivahapu.title: Vivāhapaṭala
SA_GS41_vivahapu.author: Varāhamihira
SA_GS41_vivahapu.summary: The Vivāhapaṭala ('chapter/section on marriage') is Varāhamihira's specialized treatise on the astrology of marriage: determining auspicious times (muhūrta) for weddings, examining the compatibility of bride and groom through nakṣatra and planetary factors, and the omens and configurations bearing on conjugal life. It systematizes the kūṭa-based compatibility analysis that became standard in later Hindu matchmaking.
SA_GS41_vivahapu.history: Ascribed to Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE) of Ujjain, the Vivāhapaṭala is counted among his astrological works and survives as a short specialized tract; some manuscripts treat marriage-electional material that overlaps with chapters of his larger compendia.

SA_GS41_yogay1_u.title: Yogayātrā
SA_GS41_yogay1_u.author: Varāhamihira
SA_GS41_yogay1_u.summary: The Yogayātrā is Varāhamihira's treatise on military and journey astrology, in roughly sixteen chapters (about 485 verses), prescribing how a king should determine auspicious times, omens, planetary yogas, propitiatory rites, and precautions for setting out on a military campaign. It blends astrological calculation with omen-lore (śakuna) and ritual.
SA_GS41_yogay1_u.history: Composed by Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE), the Yogayātrā is one of his several yātrā works alongside the larger Bṛhadyātrā and the brief Tikanikayātrā; this is one digital recension (variant '1') of the text.

SA_GS41_yogay2_u.title: Yogayātrā
SA_GS41_yogay2_u.author: Varāhamihira
SA_GS41_yogay2_u.summary: This is a second digital recension (variant '2') of Varāhamihira's Yogayātrā, his treatise on military and journey astrology. In about sixteen chapters and roughly 485 verses it lays out the determination of auspicious times, omens, planetary yogas, propitiatory rituals, and precautions for a king's expedition.
SA_GS41_yogay2_u.history: Ascribed to Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE) of Ujjain; this entry corresponds to an alternate manuscript/electronic version of the same Yogayātrā, distinct only in its textual recension from the parallel record.

SA_GSD36_angirspu.title: Āṅgirasasmṛti
SA_GSD36_angirspu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Aṅgiras)
SA_GSD36_angirspu.date_estimate: uncertain; 1st millennium CE, likely later layers mid-to-late (disputed)
SA_GSD36_angirspu.summary: The Āṅgirasasmṛti is one of the minor dharmaśāstra texts ascribed to the Vedic sage Aṅgiras. It deals chiefly with penance and expiation (prāyaścitta), ritual purity and impurity, śrāddha (rites for ancestors), and prescriptions varying by caste; some recensions are arranged in two parts (Pūrva- and Uttara-Āṅgiras).
SA_GSD36_angirspu.history: Like other minor smṛtis it survives in varying manuscript forms and is heavily quoted in medieval dharmanibandhas (digests); its attribution to Aṅgiras is traditional rather than historical, and its date is hard to fix because the transmitted material is a layered compilation.

SA_GSD36_brhasp_u.title: Bṛhaspatismṛti
SA_GSD36_brhasp_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Bṛhaspati); modern reconstruction by K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar
SA_GSD36_brhasp_u.summary: The Bṛhaspatismṛti is a lost dharmaśāstra known only through quotations preserved in later commentaries and digests; scholars have reconstructed it from these citations. It is especially noted for its developed treatment of vyavahāra (judicial procedure and substantive law), expanding upon and serving as a kind of vārttika (gloss) on the Manusmṛti, and it ranks among the most juristically sophisticated smṛtis.
SA_GSD36_brhasp_u.history: No complete manuscript survives; Julius Jolly translated some 700 vyavahāra verses (SBE 33) and K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar compiled and edited a fuller reconstruction (~2,400 verses, Gaekwad Oriental Series 85, Baroda 1941). The text closely follows and elaborates Manu, placing it after the Manusmṛti.

SA_GSD36_brhasppu.title: Bṛhaspatismṛti: Vyavahārakāṇḍa
SA_GSD36_brhasppu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Bṛhaspati); reconstructed
SA_GSD36_brhasppu.summary: This is the Vyavahārakāṇḍa (book on legal/judicial procedure) of the reconstructed Bṛhaspatismṛti. It treats the conduct of lawsuits, courts and judges, evidence (witnesses, documents, ordeals/divya), the eighteen titles of law (vyavahārapadas), contracts, debts, inheritance, and related substantive law, representing a high point of classical Hindu juristic thought.
SA_GSD36_brhasppu.history: The Vyavahārakāṇḍa is the best-preserved portion of the otherwise lost Bṛhaspatismṛti, recovered from citations; about 717 vyavahāra verses were collected and translated by Jolly (SBE 33) and the GRETIL plain-text edition reflects this reconstructed legal section.

SA_GSD36_katy2p_u.title: Kātyāyanasmṛti [Kātyāyanasmṛtisāroddhāra]
SA_GSD36_katy2p_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Kātyāyana); reconstruction by P. V. Kane
SA_GSD36_katy2p_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (later than Manu and Yājñavalkya); reconstruction published 1933
SA_GSD36_katy2p_u.summary: The Kātyāyanasmṛti is a lost dharmaśāstra, here in P. V. Kane's reconstructed 'Sāroddhāra' (essence-extraction) form, focused on vyavāhara—judicial procedure and the substantive law of inheritance, contracts, debts, partition, and women's property (strīdhana). Together with Nārada and Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana represents the high-water mark of classical smṛti jurisprudence dealing almost exclusively with law proper.
SA_GSD36_katy2p_u.history: No independent manuscript of Kātyāyana's law text survives; P. V. Kane reconstructed about a thousand verses from quotations in commentaries and digests, publishing 'Kātyāyanasmṛti on Vyavahāra' (Bombay, 1933). The text is dated after the Manusmṛti and Yājñavalkyasmṛti.

SA_GSD36_mvir_sau.title: Vīramitrodaya: Samayaprakāśa
SA_GSD36_mvir_sau.author: Mitramiśra (Mitra Miśra)
SA_GSD36_mvir_sau.genre: nibandha (dharmaśāstra digest), section on calendar/ritual time
SA_GSD36_mvir_sau.summary: The Samayaprakāśa ('illumination of [ritual] times') is one section of Mitramiśra's vast dharmaśāstra digest Vīramitrodaya. It treats the religious calendar—the determination of festivals, vratas (vows), fasts, sacred days, and the auspicious times (samaya) for observances—drawing on the smṛtis and earlier digests to fix proper ritual timing.
SA_GSD36_mvir_sau.history: Composed by Mitramiśra of Gopācala (Gwalior) at the behest of King Vīrasiṃhadeva of Orchhā during the Mughal period, the Vīramitrodaya closely follows the Mitākṣarā and became a leading authority of the Banaras school of Hindu law; the Samayaprakāśa was edited by Vishnu Prasāda Bhandari (Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series, Benares 1935).

SA_GSD36_narads_u.title: Nāradasmṛti
SA_GSD36_narads_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Nārada)
SA_GSD36_narads_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (disputed; late 4th to 8th c. CE)
SA_GSD36_narads_u.summary: The Nāradasmṛti is the dharmaśāstra 'juridical text par excellence,' the only major smṛti devoted purely to vyavahāra (law and legal procedure) and omitting ācāra (conduct) and prāyaścitta (penance). It systematically treats courts, the eighteen titles of law, evidence including ordeals (divya), witnesses, documents, debts, contracts, partition, and the duties of the king as administrator of justice.
SA_GSD36_narads_u.history: Attributed to the divine sage Nārada, the text exists in shorter (minor/Jolly) and longer recensions; its purely procedural focus made it prized by rulers across India and Southeast Asia. Lariviere produced a critical edition and translation; its date is debated within the second half of the first millennium CE.

SA_GSD36_nars2_pu.title: Nāradasmṛti
SA_GSD36_nars2_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Nārada)
SA_GSD36_nars2_pu.date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (disputed; late 4th to 8th c. CE)
SA_GSD36_nars2_pu.summary: This is a second recension/digital version of the Nāradasmṛti, the dharmaśāstra concerned exclusively with vyavāhara (jurisprudence). It covers judicial procedure, the eighteen titles of law, modes of proof and ordeals, debts, contracts, inheritance, and the king's judicial role, omitting personal conduct and penance.
SA_GSD36_nars2_pu.history: Corresponds to a distinct manuscript recension (e.g. the longer/vulgate as opposed to the minor text) of the Nāradasmṛti; the two electronic records reflect different transmitted versions of the same juridical smṛti, which survives in markedly varying lengths.

SA_GSD36_pars1__u.title: Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā [Parāśarasmṛti]
SA_GSD36_pars1__u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Parāśara)
SA_GSD36_pars1__u.genre: smṛti / dharmaśāstra (lawbook for the Kali age)
SA_GSD36_pars1__u.date_estimate: uncertain; latter half 1st millennium CE (often c. 7th-11th c. CE for extant form; disputed)
SA_GSD36_pars1__u.summary: The Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā, or Parāśarasmṛti, is a dharmaśāstra in twelve chapters (about 600 verses) that presents itself as the lawbook appropriate to the Kali age (kaliyuga). It concentrates on ācāra (right conduct), the duties of the four varṇas and āśramas, ritual observances, purification, penance, and the merit of charity (dāna) as the supreme virtue for the present age; it largely omits detailed vyavāhara.
SA_GSD36_pars1__u.history: Traditionally each yuga has its own authoritative lawgiver, with Parāśara assigned to the Kali age. The smṛti became influential precisely as a 'Kali-yuga' code and acquired a major commentary, the Parāśaramādhavīya by Mādhavācārya (Sāyaṇa's brother, 14th c.); the date of the base text is disputed.

SA_GSD36_pars2_pu.title: Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā [Parāśarasmṛti]: Kāṇḍas 1 & 2
SA_GSD36_pars2_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Parāśara)
SA_GSD36_pars2_pu.genre: smṛti / dharmaśāstra (lawbook for the Kali age), opening sections
SA_GSD36_pars2_pu.summary: This record covers the first two kāṇḍas (sections) of the Parāśarasmṛti. The opening portions set out the yuga-dharma framework—why Parāśara's code governs the Kali age—and begin the exposition of the duties of the varṇas and āśramas, daily and ritual conduct (ācāra), and the foundations of purification and penance.
SA_GSD36_pars2_pu.history: A partial (sectional) version of the Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā; the work is the recognized Kali-yuga lawbook and received the celebrated commentary Parāśaramādhavīya by Mādhavācārya in the 14th century.

SA_GSD36_sa_vijJAnezvara-mitAkSarA.title: Mitākṣarā
SA_GSD36_sa_vijJAnezvara-mitAkSarA.author: Vijñāneśvara
SA_GSD36_sa_vijJAnezvara-mitAkSarA.summary: The Mitākṣarā is Vijñāneśvara's authoritative commentary on the Yājñavalkyasmṛti. Best known for its doctrine of 'inheritance by birth' (a son acquires an interest in ancestral property at birth) and its treatment of the joint/coparcenary family, it systematizes Hindu personal law on partition, succession, adoption, women's property, and judicial procedure.
SA_GSD36_sa_vijJAnezvara-mitAkSarA.history: Written by Vijñāneśvara at the Western Cālukya court of Vikramāditya VI at Kalyāṇa, the Mitākṣarā gave rise to the dominant 'Mitākṣarā school' of Hindu law observed across most of India outside Bengal/Assam, and remained central to Anglo-Hindu jurisprudence into the modern era. Its exact date is debated (Kane, Derrett).

SA_GSD36_mitaksara.title: Mitākṣarā, a commentary on the Yājñavalkya-dharmaśāstra
SA_GSD36_mitaksara.author: Vijñāneśvara
SA_GSD36_mitaksara.summary: Same work as the preceding record: Vijñāneśvara's Mitākṣarā commentary on the Yājñavalkyasmṛti (here titled explicitly as a commentary on the Yājñavalkya-dharmaśāstra). It glosses and systematically expounds Yājñavalkya's verses on conduct (ācāra), judicial procedure (vyavāhara), and penance (prāyaścitta), and is foundational for the law of inheritance by birth.
SA_GSD36_mitaksara.history: This is a parallel/alternate digital record of the Mitākṣarā by Vijñāneśvara, composed at the Cālukya court under Vikramāditya VI; it founded the Mitākṣarā school of Hindu law and is among the most influential legal texts of premodern India.

SA_GSD36_yajn2_pu.title: Yājñavalkyasmṛti
SA_GSD36_yajn2_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Yājñavalkya)
SA_GSD36_yajn2_pu.date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (disputed; some place core in 1st-2nd c. CE)
SA_GSD36_yajn2_pu.summary: The Yājñavalkyasmṛti is a major verse dharmaśāstra organized into three sections—ācāra (right conduct), vyavāhara (judicial process), and prāyaścitta (penance/expiation). More concise and systematically arranged than the Manusmṛti, it is especially esteemed for its lucid treatment of legal procedure and had a greater influence on medieval Indian judicial practice than Manu.
SA_GSD36_yajn2_pu.history: Attributed to the Upaniṣadic sage Yājñavalkya, the extant text is a later composition of the dharmaśāstra tradition. It became the base text for the most important medieval commentaries—above all Vijñāneśvara's Mitākṣarā and the Vīramitrodaya—anchoring Hindu personal law.

SA_GSD36_ys-78_pu.title: Yamasmṛti
SA_GSD36_ys-78_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Yama)
SA_GSD36_ys-78_pu.summary: The Yamasmṛti is one of the minor dharmaśāstra texts, ascribed to Yama (named as a lawgiver already in the Yājñavalkyasmṛti's list). It is devoted largely to penance and expiatory atonement (prāyaścitta) for sins and ritual transgressions, purification, and the rules of śrāddha—the propitiatory rites for the ancestors—along with their theoretical justification.
SA_GSD36_ys-78_pu.history: Surviving as a short text quoted extensively in medieval digests, the Yamasmṛti exists in differing recensions; its attribution to Yama is traditional, and its precise date cannot be securely fixed.

SA_GSD36_ys-99_pu.title: Laghuyamasmṛti
SA_GSD36_ys-99_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Yama)
SA_GSD36_ys-99_pu.summary: The Laghuyamasmṛti ('shorter Yama-smṛti') is a brief recension of the dharmaśāstra material ascribed to Yama, concentrating on penance and expiation (prāyaścitta), ritual purification, and śrāddha for the ancestors. It belongs to the family of minor smṛtis dealing chiefly with atonement for sins and impurities.
SA_GSD36_ys-99_pu.history: The Laghuyama text is cited by medieval authorities such as Halāyudha and Mādhavācārya, confirming its currency and the existence of multiple recensions of the Yama material; its date is uncertain.

SA_GSD36_ys182_pu.title: Bṛhadyamasmṛti
SA_GSD36_ys182_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Yama)
SA_GSD36_ys182_pu.date_estimate: later metrical Yama recension, probably c. 5th-10th c. CE (uncertain)
SA_GSD36_ys182_pu.summary: The Bṛhadyamasmṛti ('Greater Yama-smṛti') is one of the metrical law codes attributed to the legendary sage Yama, dealing with topics of dharma such as ritual purity and impurity (āśauca), penances and expiations (prāyaścitta), and rules of conduct and ascetic/religious observance. It is a 'larger' or expanded recension distinct from the shorter Yamasmṛti, and survives partly through quotation in later digests.
SA_GSD36_ys182_pu.history: The Yama corpus is one of the eighteen (or more) smṛtis named in tradition; the verses circulated in multiple recensions and are frequently cited by medieval nibandha authors such as Halāyudha and Vijñāneśvara, indicating their authority in dharma compilations. The original text is not preserved as a single fixed work and is largely reconstructed from such citations.

SA_GSD36_yssoutpu.title: Yamasmṛti
SA_GSD36_yssoutpu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Yama)
SA_GSD36_yssoutpu.date_estimate: c. 1st millennium CE (uncertain; later metrical smṛti)
SA_GSD36_yssoutpu.summary: The Yamasmṛti is a short metrical Hindu law code attributed to the sage Yama, one of the traditional list of smṛti authors. It treats matters of ritual purity and impurity, penances and expiations (prāyaścitta), conduct, and the duties of various social and religious groups.
SA_GSD36_yssoutpu.history: Yama is enumerated among the eighteen authoritative smṛtikāras in tradition, and the text exists in shorter and longer (Bṛhad) recensions. Like other minor smṛtis it survives both in independent manuscript form and through extensive quotation in medieval dharma digests.

SA_GSD37_gautdhcu.title: Gautama-Dharmasūtra
SA_GSD37_gautdhcu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Gautama)
SA_GSD37_gautdhcu.date_estimate: c. 600-400 BCE (often regarded as the oldest or among the oldest extant Dharmasūtras; disputed)
SA_GSD37_gautdhcu.summary: The Gautama Dharmasūtra is a prose aphoristic treatise on dharma covering the sources of law, the four life-stages (āśrama), the duties of the varṇas, rules for students and householders, ritual purity, penances, inheritance, and the duties of the king and judicial procedure. It is one of the foundational texts of the Hindu legal tradition.
SA_GSD37_gautdhcu.history: Traditionally affiliated with the Rāṇāyanīya branch of the Sāmaveda, it was composed by members of that school under the name of the sage Gautama. Scholars including Kane and Lingat regard it as possibly the oldest surviving Dharmasūtra, though Olivelle ranks Āpastamba as likely older; it is preserved with the commentary of Maskarin (Maskari) and is cited as authoritative across later dharmaśāstra literature.

SA_GSD37_vaikhd_u.title: Vaikhānasa Dharmasūtra 1–3 [Vaikhānasa Smārtasūtra 8-10]
SA_GSD37_vaikhd_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Vikhanas)
SA_GSD37_vaikhd_u.tradition: Hindu (Vaikhānasa school, Taittirīya/Black Yajurveda; later Vaiṣṇava)
SA_GSD37_vaikhd_u.date_estimate: c. 4th c. CE (surviving form no older than the 4th century CE; some place its core 3rd c. CE)
SA_GSD37_vaikhd_u.summary: This is the dharma portion (Praśnas 1-3 of the Dharmasūtra, corresponding to chapters 8-10 of the larger Vaikhānasa Smārtasūtra) of the ritual manual of the Vaikhānasa school. It treats the life-stages (āśrama) — with particular and detailed attention to the classes of forest-dwelling ascetics and hermits (vaikhānasas) — along with conduct, purity, and the duties pertaining to each stage of life.
SA_GSD37_vaikhd_u.history: The Vaikhānasa Smārtasūtra, belonging to the Taittirīya recension of the Black Yajurveda, combines a Gṛhyasūtra and a Dharmasūtra and is named after its putative author Vikhanas, later regarded as a manifestation of Viṣṇu. The Sanskrit text was edited by W. Caland (1927); the Vaikhānasa community became prominent as temple-priests in South Indian Vaiṣṇava worship.

SA_GSP27_moksop_u.title: Mokṣopāya
SA_GSP27_moksop_u.author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to Vālmīki)
SA_GSP27_moksop_u.tradition: Hindu (Kashmiri; non-dual idealist philosophy, later assimilated to Advaita/Trika)
SA_GSP27_moksop_u.date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (likely c. 950 CE), Kashmir
SA_GSP27_moksop_u.summary: The Mokṣopāya ('Means to Liberation') is a large Kashmiri verse work that teaches a radically idealist, world-as-mind philosophy through a frame dialogue in which the sage Vasiṣṭha instructs the young prince Rāma, illustrated by numerous embedded parables and stories. It argues that the world is a mental projection and liberation comes through insight, self-effort, and the dissolution of mental constructs.
SA_GSP27_moksop_u.history: It is the original recension underlying the better-known, longer Yogavāsiṣṭha (Vasiṣṭha-Mahārāmāyaṇa). Composed on the Pradyumna hill in Śrīnagar in the 10th century (internal references point to King Yaśaskara, r. 939-948), it was later reworked between the 11th and 14th centuries with Śaiva-Trika and Advaita coloring to become the orthodox Yogavāsiṣṭha. Its critical recovery is associated with the Mokṣopāya Project (J. Hanneder, W. Slaje).

SA_GSP28_dhebta_u.title: Hetubinduṭīkāloka
SA_GSP28_dhebta_u.author: Durveka Miśra
SA_GSP28_dhebta_u.date_estimate: c. 10th-11th c. CE (Durveka Miśra fl. c. 970-1030 CE)
SA_GSP28_dhebta_u.summary: The Hetubinduṭīkāloka ('Light on the Hetubinduṭīkā') is Durveka Miśra's sub-commentary (Āloka) on Arcaṭa's Hetubinduṭīkā, which in turn comments on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu, a treatise on the Buddhist theory of logical reason (hetu) and inference. It elucidates the formal logic, the three characteristics of a valid reason, and the epistemology of inference within the Dharmakīrtian tradition.
SA_GSP28_dhebta_u.history: Durveka Miśra was a notable late Buddhist logician (associated with Vikramaśīla milieu) who wrote sub-commentaries on key works of the pramāṇa tradition. The Hetubinduṭīkāloka was edited by S. Sanghavi and Jinavijaya and published by the Oriental Institute, Baroda (1949). It is an important witness to late Indian Buddhist epistemology before its disappearance from the subcontinent.

SA_GSP28_durdhpru.title: Dharmottarapradīpa
SA_GSP28_durdhpru.author: Durveka Miśra
SA_GSP28_durdhpru.date_estimate: c. 10th-11th c. CE (Durveka Miśra fl. c. 970-1030 CE)
SA_GSP28_durdhpru.summary: The Dharmottarapradīpa ('Lamp on Dharmottara') is Durveka Miśra's sub-commentary on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā, which is itself a commentary on Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu. It expounds and defends the Buddhist epistemology of perception (pratyakṣa) and inference (anumāna), clarifying disputed points in Dharmottara's interpretation.
SA_GSP28_durdhpru.history: It is one of the principal late sub-commentaries in the Nyāyabindu commentarial chain (Dharmakīrti → Dharmottara → Durveka Miśra) and a major source for the views of the late Buddhist logical school. The text was edited by Dalsukhbhai Malvania and published by the K. P. Jayaswal Research Institute, Patna (1955).

SA_GSP28_jaimsutu.title: Mīmāṃsāsūtra
SA_GSP28_jaimsutu.author: Jaimini
SA_GSP28_jaimsutu.genre: sūtra (foundational aphoristic text of a darśana)
SA_GSP28_jaimsutu.date_estimate: c. 3rd-1st c. BCE (final form by c. 200 BCE-200 CE; the project hint of 2nd c. CE is at the late end)
SA_GSP28_jaimsutu.summary: The Mīmāṃsāsūtra (Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā-sūtra or Jaiminisūtra) is the root text of the Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā school, an aphoristic treatise on the interpretation of Vedic injunctions and the principles of ritual exegesis. In about twelve books it systematizes the rules for understanding dharma (defined as that which is enjoined by the Veda), the authority and eternality of the Veda, the structure of sacrificial acts, and hermeneutic principles for resolving textual conflicts.
SA_GSP28_jaimsutu.history: Attributed to the sage Jaimini, it is the largest of the darśana sūtra collections and the foundation of the entire Mīmāṃsā tradition. It was commented upon by Śabara (Śābarabhāṣya), whose commentary was in turn expanded by Kumārila and Prabhākara, founding the two main Mīmāṃsā subschools; its hermeneutics deeply influenced Vedānta, Dharmaśāstra, and Indian legal interpretation.

SA_GSP28_jsbh1-1u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 1, Adhikaraṇa 1
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-1u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-1u.summary: This is the opening section of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā, covering Book 1, first adhikaraṇa — the famous Tarka-pāda discussion that opens with the inquiry into dharma (athāto dharmajijñāsā), establishing the subject-matter, motive, and the definition of dharma as that enjoined by the Veda. It treats the foundational question of the necessity and scope of the Mīmāṃsā inquiry.
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-1u.history: The Bhāṭṭadīpikā is a commentary ('garland of adhikaraṇas') on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra by Khaṇḍadeva Miśra, a leading 17th-century Mīmāṃsaka of Benares and founder of 'New Exegesis' (Navya-Mīmāṃsā), who applied Navya-Nyāya analytical methods to Mīmāṃsā. It remains highly popular as a teaching text, especially in South India.

SA_GSP28_jsbh1-2u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 2
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-2u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-2u.summary: This segment of Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā treats a further adhikaraṇa of Book 1, continuing the Tarka-pāda's analysis of the means of valid knowledge of dharma and the authority of the Veda, including the discussion of the eternal, author-less (apauruṣeya) nature of the Vedic word and the relation of word and meaning.
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-2u.history: Part of Khaṇḍadeva's systematic 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-school commentary on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, the work organizes Jaimini's text adhikaraṇa by adhikaraṇa and is noted for importing Navya-Nyāya precision into Mīmāṃsā exegesis.

SA_GSP28_jsbh1-3u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 1, Adhikaraṇa 3
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-3u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-3u.summary: This portion of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā comments on a further adhikaraṇa in Book 1, advancing the discussion of the validity of the Veda as the source of dharma and addressing objections (e.g., from perception and from the Veda's apparent reference to transient things), as part of the foundational Tarka-pāda.
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-3u.history: Section of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-Mīmāṃsā commentary on Jaimini's sūtras, representative of the Navya-Mīmāṃsā ('New Exegesis') movement he is credited with founding.

SA_GSP28_jsbh1-4u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 1, Adhikaraṇa 4
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-4u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-4u.summary: This section of Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā treats a further adhikaraṇa of Book 1, continuing the foundational discussion of the relation between Vedic word and meaning, the classes of Vedic statements (injunction, hymn, name, prohibition), and the establishment of the Veda's self-validity as the authority for dharma.
SA_GSP28_jsbh1-4u.history: Part of the 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-school commentary by Khaṇḍadeva of Benares; the Bhāṭṭadīpikā was probably his second Mīmāṃsā work, written after the Mīmāṃsākaustubha and before the Bhāṭṭatantrarahasya.

SA_GSP28_jsbh2-1u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 1
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-1u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-1u.summary: This section of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā comments on the opening of Book 2 of the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, which concerns the classification and distinction of ritual acts (karma) — distinguishing primary from subsidiary actions and analyzing the differences of injunctions (codanā) that mark off one rite from another.
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-1u.history: Part of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century systematic Bhāṭṭa-Mīmāṃsā commentary on Jaimini's text, applying Navya-Nyāya analytical rigor to the classical adhikaraṇa structure.

SA_GSP28_jsbh2-2u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 2
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-2u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-2u.summary: This portion of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā treats a further adhikaraṇa of Book 2, continuing the analysis of the differentiation of rites — including the criteria (such as distinct words, repetition, number, name, and context) by which one is to determine whether textual passages enjoin separate actions or qualify a single action.
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-2u.history: A unit of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-school commentary on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, part of the Navya-Mīmāṃsā tradition he founded in Benares.

SA_GSP28_jsbh2-3u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 3
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-3u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-3u.summary: This section of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā comments on a further adhikaraṇa of Book 2, continuing the Mīmāṃsā treatment of the distinction of ritual acts and the application of injunctive force, including the resolution of whether differing scriptural expressions establish the unity or plurality of enjoined rites.
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-3u.history: Component of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-Mīmāṃsā commentary on Jaimini's sūtras, characteristic of his Navya-Mīmāṃsā method.

SA_GSP28_jsbh2-4u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 4
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-4u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-4u.summary: This portion of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā treats a further adhikaraṇa of Book 2, dealing with the differentiation of sacrificial acts and the principles distinguishing principal from subordinate rites, as well as the bearing of mantras and names on the identity of the enjoined action.
SA_GSP28_jsbh2-4u.history: A unit of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-school commentary on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra; the Bhāṭṭadīpikā remains a standard pedagogical work in the Mīmāṃsā curriculum, especially in South India.

SA_GSP28_jsbh3-1u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 3, Adhikaraṇa 1
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-1u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-1u.summary: This section of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā comments on the opening of Book 3 of the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, which treats śeṣa (subsidiariness) — the relations of subservience among the elements of a ritual, i.e., how various textual elements serve the principal act, and the hierarchy of direct statement (śruti), indicatory mark (liṅga), syntactic connection (vākya), and so on, that determine ritual application.
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-1u.history: Part of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-Mīmāṃsā commentary; Book 3 is central to Mīmāṃsā hermeneutics and Khaṇḍadeva treats it with Navya-Nyāya analytical precision.

SA_GSP28_jsbh3-2u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 3, Adhikaraṇa 2
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-2u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-2u.summary: This portion of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā treats a further adhikaraṇa of Book 3, continuing the analysis of subsidiariness (śeṣa) and the means for determining the purpose served by ritual elements, including the ordering and the relative authority of the various indicators of ritual application.
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-2u.history: Component of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-school commentary on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra, a leading product of the Navya-Mīmāṃsā movement.

SA_GSP28_jsbh3-3u.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 3, Adhikaraṇa 3
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-3u.author: Khaṇḍadeva
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-3u.summary: This section of the Bhāṭṭadīpikā comments on a further adhikaraṇa of Book 3, continuing the Mīmāṃsā discussion of subsidiary relations within ritual, including questions of which agent or substance a given subsidiary act serves and how conflicting indications are resolved in determining ritual structure.
SA_GSP28_jsbh3-3u.history: A unit of Khaṇḍadeva's 17th-century Bhāṭṭa-Mīmāṃsā commentary on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, exemplifying his integration of Navya-Nyāya method into classical exegesis.

SA_GSP28_ktv_2-1u.title: Tantravārttika 2.1.1–4
SA_GSP28_ktv_2-1u.author: Kumārila (Kumārilabhaṭṭa)
SA_GSP28_ktv_2-1u.summary: This is a portion of Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's Tantravārttika covering the opening adhikaraṇas of Book 2, pāda 1 of the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, where the discussion concerns the differentiation of ritual acts (karmabheda) and the classification and injunctive force of Vedic statements. The Tantravārttika densely interweaves ritual hermeneutics with broader epistemological, grammatical, and theological argument, including polemics against rival schools.
SA_GSP28_ktv_2-1u.history: The Tantravārttika is the second part of Kumārila's great threefold commentary (Ślokavārttika, Tantravārttika, Ṭupṭīkā) on Śabara's Bhāṣya on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra, covering Śabara from 1.2 through Book 3. Kumārila, the founder of the Bhāṭṭa subschool, was one of the most influential defenders of Vedic orthodoxy and Mīmāṃsā realism; the work was translated into English by Gaṅgānātha Jhā.

SA_GSP28_mimslovu.title: Mīmāṃsāślokavārttika
SA_GSP28_mimslovu.author: Kumārila Bhaṭṭa
SA_GSP28_mimslovu.summary: A metrical (śloka) sub-commentary on the opening section (Tarkapāda, MS 1.1) of Śabara's Bhāṣya on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra. It is primarily a treatise on epistemology and the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇa), defending the intrinsic validity (svataḥ-prāmāṇya) of cognition and the eternality and authorlessness (apauruṣeya) of the Veda, while polemicizing extensively against Buddhist, especially Dignāga-Dharmakīrti, positions on perception, inference, word-meaning (apoha), and the negation of a permanent self.
SA_GSP28_mimslovu.history: The Ślokavārttika is the first and most philosophically influential of Kumārila's three great works on Śabara's Bhāṣya (alongside the Tantravārttika and Ṭupṭīkā/Bṛhaṭṭīkā). It became the foundational text of the Bhāṭṭa branch of Mīmāṃsā and attracted major commentaries including Sucaritamiśra's Kāśikā, Pārthasārathi Miśra's Nyāyaratnākara, and Umbeka Bhaṭṭa's Tātparyaṭīkā.

SA_GSP28_mimsvtku.title: Mīmāṃsāślokavārttikakāśikā
SA_GSP28_mimsvtku.author: Sucaritamiśra
SA_GSP28_mimsvtku.date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (disputed; not_before c. 800 CE)
SA_GSP28_mimsvtku.summary: The Kāśikā is Sucaritamiśra's running commentary on Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's Ślokavārttika. It explicates and defends Kumārila's verse-by-verse arguments in epistemology, the self-validity of knowledge, scriptural authority, and the refutation of Buddhist doctrines (notably the apoha theory of meaning), serving as one of the principal exegetical aids to the Bhāṭṭa-Mīmāṃsā system.
SA_GSP28_mimsvtku.history: Sucaritamiśra is a major Bhāṭṭa commentator whose Kāśikā is valued alongside Pārthasārathi Miśra's Nyāyaratnākara as a key witness to the interpretation of the Ślokavārttika; modern critical editions of its apoha sections have been published. His exact dates are uncertain, generally placed between the 9th and 11th centuries CE.

SA_GSP28_mjnya01u.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 1
SA_GSP28_mjnya01u.author: Mādhava (Mādhavācārya / Vidyāraṇya)
SA_GSP28_mjnya01u.genre: philosophical treatise / metrical compendium with auto-commentary
SA_GSP28_mjnya01u.date_estimate: 14th c. CE (Mādhava Vidyāraṇya, d. 1386); project hint of 400-699 CE is incorrect
SA_GSP28_mjnya01u.summary: Adhyāya 1 of a systematic exposition (nyāyamālā) of the topics (adhikaraṇas) of Jaimini's twelve-chapter Mīmāṃsāsūtra. Each topic is stated in summary verses with Mādhava's own prose 'vistara' commentary laying out doubt, prima-facie view, and settled conclusion. The first adhyāya treats the foundations of dharma: the authority of the Veda, the eternality of the word-Veda relation, and the classification of Vedic injunctions, mantras, and arthavāda.
SA_GSP28_mjnya01u.history: Authored by Mādhava, the celebrated 14th-century scholar-statesman of the early Vijayanagara empire, generally identified with Vidyāraṇya, head of the Śṛṅgeri maṭha and brother/associate of the Vedic commentator Sāyaṇa. The Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara became a standard introductory digest of Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā ritual hermeneutics.

SA_GSP28_mjnya02u.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 2
SA_GSP28_mjnya02u.author: Mādhava (Mādhavācārya / Vidyāraṇya)
SA_GSP28_mjnya02u.genre: philosophical treatise / metrical compendium with auto-commentary
SA_GSP28_mjnya02u.date_estimate: 14th c. CE (Mādhava Vidyāraṇya, d. 1386); project hint of 400-699 CE is incorrect
SA_GSP28_mjnya02u.summary: Adhyāya 2 of Mādhava's metrical digest with prose commentary on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra. This chapter expounds the differentiation of rites (karma-bheda) and the distinctions among Vedic injunctions, classifying primary (apūrva), restrictive (niyama), and exclusionary (parisaṃkhyā) injunctions and analyzing how separate ritual acts are established.
SA_GSP28_mjnya02u.history: Part of the same 14th-century Vijayanagara-era exposition by Mādhava Vidyāraṇya covering all twelve adhyāyas of the Mīmāṃsāsūtra; widely transmitted as a teaching manual of Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā exegesis.

SA_GSP28_mjnya03u.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 3
SA_GSP28_mjnya03u.author: Mādhava (Mādhavācārya / Vidyāraṇya)
SA_GSP28_mjnya03u.genre: philosophical treatise / metrical compendium with auto-commentary
SA_GSP28_mjnya03u.date_estimate: 14th c. CE (Mādhava Vidyāraṇya, d. 1386); project hint of 400-699 CE is incorrect
SA_GSP28_mjnya03u.summary: Adhyāya 3 of Mādhava's Nyāyamālāvistara on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra.
