It treats śeṣa (the subsidiary-principal relation among ritual elements), the means by which auxiliary acts serve the main sacrifice, the authority of direct Vedic statement versus indication and syntactic connection, and the determination of which agent is entitled to perform which acts.
SA_GSP28_mjnya03u.history: Belongs to Mādhava Vidyāraṇya's complete 14th-century exposition of Jaimini's twelve adhyāyas, a foundational digest for the study of Mīmāṃsā ritual hermeneutics in the post-classical period.

SA_GSP28_mjnya04u.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 4
SA_GSP28_mjnya04u.author: Mādhava (Mādhavācārya / Vidyāraṇya)
SA_GSP28_mjnya04u.genre: philosophical treatise / metrical compendium with auto-commentary
SA_GSP28_mjnya04u.date_estimate: 14th c. CE (Mādhava Vidyāraṇya, d. 1386); project hint of 400-699 CE is incorrect
SA_GSP28_mjnya04u.summary: Adhyāya 4 of Mādhava's Nyāyamālāvistara. It addresses prayojana (the purpose or fruit of rites), the distinction between acts performed for a transcendent unseen result (apūrva) and those serving another act, the function of subsidiary rites, and rules concerning the order and motivation of ritual performance.
SA_GSP28_mjnya04u.history: Part of Mādhava Vidyāraṇya's 14th-century Vijayanagara-period digest of the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, transmitted and printed (e.g., in the Ānandāśrama and Bibliotheca Indica series) as a standard reference for Mīmāṃsā adhikaraṇas.

SA_GSP28_msbh1-7u.title: Mīmāṃsāsūtra
SA_GSP28_msbh1-7u.author: Jaimini
SA_GSP28_msbh1-7u.date_estimate: c. 300-100 BCE (composite; sutra text plausibly c. 4th-2nd c. BCE); project hint of 100-199 CE is too late
SA_GSP28_msbh1-7u.summary: The foundational sutra-text of the Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā darśana, attributed to Jaimini, comprising roughly 2,700-3,000 aphorisms in twelve adhyāyas. It develops a systematic hermeneutics (vākyaśāstra) for interpreting the Veda with respect to dharma understood as ritual obligation, establishing the authority and self-sufficiency of Vedic injunction, the eternal word-meaning relation, and rules for resolving apparent textual conflicts. This record covers adhyāyas 1-7, transmitted together with Śabara's Bhāṣya.
SA_GSP28_msbh1-7u.history: The earliest of the six orthodox schools' root texts, it is the basis of all later Mīmāṃsā and deeply shaped Indian exegesis, jurisprudence (dharmaśāstra), and Vedānta. Its sole complete extant ancient commentary is the Śābarabhāṣya, itself sub-commented by Kumārila (Bhāṭṭa) and Prabhākara, founding the two main schools of the tradition.

SA_GSP28_prakp01u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 1
SA_GSP28_prakp01u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp01u.date_estimate: c. 8th-9th c. CE (before late 9th c.; pre-Vācaspati Miśra)
SA_GSP28_prakp01u.summary: The Prakaraṇapañcikā is the principal systematic and independent treatise of the Prābhākara school of Mīmāṃsā, organized into a set of prakaraṇas (topical chapters). Prakaraṇa 1 opens the work, establishing its scope and foundational positions in defense of Prabhākara Miśra's interpretation of Mīmāṃsā epistemology and ritual exegesis.
SA_GSP28_prakp01u.history: Śālikanātha is the leading expositor of Prabhākara's thought; his Prakaraṇapañcikā and his commentary Ṛjuvimalā on Prabhākara's Bṛhatī are the chief sources for the Prābhākara (Guru-mata) system. Vācaspati Miśra (late 9th c.) already refers to him, fixing a terminus ante quem.

SA_GSP28_prakp02u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 2
SA_GSP28_prakp02u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp02u.summary: Prakaraṇa 2 of Śālikanātha's Prakaraṇapañcikā, continuing the systematic exposition of Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā doctrine. The chapters of the work treat in turn the means of knowledge, perception, inference, verbal testimony, the theory of meaning, and ritual-exegetical categories from the Prābhākara standpoint.
SA_GSP28_prakp02u.history: Part of the foundational independent compendium of the Prābhākara school by its chief systematizer, Śālikanāthamiśra; preserved and edited in the Chowkhamba and related Sanskrit series.

SA_GSP28_prakp03u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 3
SA_GSP28_prakp03u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp03u.summary: Prakaraṇa 3 of the Prakaraṇapañcikā, advancing Śālikanātha's defense of Prabhākara's Mīmāṃsā. The work as a whole expounds Prābhākara epistemology and semantics, including the distinctive doctrines of the intrinsic validity of cognition and of anvitābhidhāna (the theory that words denote only as syntactically connected within a sentence).
SA_GSP28_prakp03u.history: One chapter of Śālikanāthamiśra's master treatise, the principal surviving systematic source for Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā alongside his commentary on Prabhākara's Bṛhatī.

SA_GSP28_prakp04u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 4
SA_GSP28_prakp04u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp04u.summary: Prakaraṇa 4 of Śālikanātha's Prakaraṇapañcikā, a topical chapter in the Prābhākara exposition of the sources of valid knowledge (pramāṇas) and the interpretation of Vedic injunction (vidhi) as the core of dharma and the meaning of scripture.
SA_GSP28_prakp04u.history: Part of the leading independent treatise of the Prābhākara branch of Mīmāṃsā, composed by Śālikanāthamiśra, the school's foremost expositor.

SA_GSP28_prakp05u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 5
SA_GSP28_prakp05u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp05u.summary: Prakaraṇa 5 of the Prakaraṇapañcikā, continuing Śālikanātha's systematic treatment of Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā epistemology and the analysis of cognition, error, and the validity of knowledge.
SA_GSP28_prakp05u.history: A chapter of Śālikanāthamiśra's Prakaraṇapañcikā, the chief extant systematic work of the Prābhākara school.

SA_GSP28_prakp06u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 6
SA_GSP28_prakp06u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp06u.summary: Prakaraṇa 6 of Śālikanātha's Prakaraṇapañcikā, a topical section in the Prābhākara exposition dealing with the means of knowledge and the school's distinctive theories of cognition, self-awareness, and the object of valid knowing.
SA_GSP28_prakp06u.history: Part of the foundational Prābhākara treatise by Śālikanāthamiśra, preserved in standard Sanskrit editions of the work.

SA_GSP28_prakp07u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 7
SA_GSP28_prakp07u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp07u.summary: Prakaraṇa 7 of the Prakaraṇapañcikā, advancing Śālikanātha's exposition of Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā, including its analysis of verbal cognition and the relation between word, meaning, and sentence-sense.
SA_GSP28_prakp07u.history: A constituent chapter of Śālikanāthamiśra's Prakaraṇapañcikā, the principal systematic source for the Guru-mata (Prābhākara) school.

SA_GSP28_prakp08u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 8
SA_GSP28_prakp08u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp08u.summary: Prakaraṇa 8 of Śālikanātha's Prakaraṇapañcikā, treating a further topic in Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā semantics and ritual hermeneutics, notably the analysis of the meaning of sentences and injunctions.
SA_GSP28_prakp08u.history: Part of the chief independent treatise of the Prābhākara school by Śālikanāthamiśra.

SA_GSP28_prakp09u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 9
SA_GSP28_prakp09u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp09u.summary: Prakaraṇa 9 of the Prakaraṇapañcikā, including the celebrated Vākyārthamātṛkā in which Śālikanātha develops the Prābhākara doctrine of anvitābhidhāna, the theory that words signify only as already syntactically related within a sentence and that sentence-meaning is grasped through injunctive force.
SA_GSP28_prakp09u.history: One of the most studied portions of Śālikanāthamiśra's treatise, central to modern scholarship on Prābhākara theories of meaning.

SA_GSP28_prakp10u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 10
SA_GSP28_prakp10u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp10u.summary: Prakaraṇa 10 of Śālikanātha's Prakaraṇapañcikā, a topical chapter continuing the Prābhākara exposition of Vedic injunction (vidhi), the nature of dharma, and the exegetical structure of ritual prescription.
SA_GSP28_prakp10u.history: A chapter of the principal systematic Prābhākara treatise composed by Śālikanāthamiśra.

SA_GSP28_prakp11u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 11, Paricchedas 1 & 2
SA_GSP28_prakp11u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp11u.summary: The first two sections (paricchedas) of Prakaraṇa 11 of the Prakaraṇapañcikā, continuing Śālikanātha's detailed treatment of Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā categories, including the analysis of injunction, the agent's entitlement (adhikāra), and the unseen ritual potency (apūrva).
SA_GSP28_prakp11u.history: Part of Śālikanāthamiśra's Prakaraṇapañcikā, the chief surviving compendium of Prābhākara doctrine; the work's later prakaraṇas are subdivided into paricchedas.

SA_GSP28_prakp12u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 12
SA_GSP28_prakp12u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp12u.summary: Prakaraṇa 12 of the Prakaraṇapañcikā, a topical chapter in Śālikanātha's exposition of Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā, dealing with further aspects of ritual exegesis and the interpretation of Vedic commands.
SA_GSP28_prakp12u.history: A chapter of Śālikanāthamiśra's foundational Prābhākara treatise, transmitted in printed Sanskrit editions of the work.

SA_GSP28_prakp13u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 13, Paricchedas 1 & 2
SA_GSP28_prakp13u.author: Śālikanāthamiśra
SA_GSP28_prakp13u.summary: The first two sections (paricchedas) of Prakaraṇa 13 of Śālikanātha's Prakaraṇapañcikā, among the concluding chapters of the work, continuing the Prābhākara treatment of ritual hermeneutics, the structure of subsidiary and principal acts, and the application of Mīmāṃsā interpretive principles.
SA_GSP28_prakp13u.history: Part of the principal independent treatise of the Prābhākara school by Śālikanāthamiśra; the work comprises a series of prakaraṇas (traditionally counted around fourteen), with later chapters divided into paricchedas.

SA_GSP28_prakp14u.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 14
SA_GSP28_prakp14u.author: Śālikanātha Miśra
SA_GSP28_prakp14u.summary: The Prakaraṇapañcikā is the principal independent exposition of the Prābhākara school of Pūrva Mīmāṃsā, systematically presenting its doctrines on epistemology (the validity of cognition, perception, inference, verbal testimony), the theory of meaning, ritual injunction (vidhi), and self. This record is the fourteenth section (prakaraṇa) of that work, treating a specific topic within the larger treatise.
SA_GSP28_prakp14u.history: Śālikanātha was the foremost systematizer of Prabhākara's thought and also wrote the Ṛjuvimalā commentary on Prabhākara's Bṛhatī. The Prakaraṇapañcikā is one of the very few surviving texts that allow reconstruction of the Prābhākara position against the rival Bhāṭṭa (Kumārila) school.

SA_GSP28_sabbha1u.title: Mīmāṃsāsūtrabhāṣya
SA_GSP28_sabbha1u.author: Śabara (Śabarasvāmin)
SA_GSP28_sabbha1u.date_estimate: c. 1st–4th c. CE (commonly placed c. 2nd–4th c. CE)
SA_GSP28_sabbha1u.summary: The Śābarabhāṣya is the foundational and earliest extant complete commentary on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra. It elaborates the principles of Vedic exegesis (ritual interpretation), the eternality and authorlessness of the Veda, the theory of dharma as defined by injunction, and the self-validity of cognition.
SA_GSP28_sabbha1u.history: Śabara's bhāṣya became the authoritative basis for all later Mīmāṃsā, generating the two great sub-schools through Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's Vārttikas and Prabhākara's Bṛhatī. Its precise date is uncertain but it precedes the 7th-century commentators.

SA_GSP28_sastrdiu.title: Śāstradīpikā
SA_GSP28_sastrdiu.author: Pārthasārathi Miśra
SA_GSP28_sastrdiu.date_estimate: c. 11th c. CE (disputed; some place him 10th c.)
SA_GSP28_sastrdiu.summary: The Śāstradīpikā is a comprehensive independent treatise on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra following the Bhāṭṭa (Kumārila) line. It surveys the whole system of Mīmāṃsā, with an extensive epistemological introduction (Tarkapāda) defending the means of valid knowledge and refuting rival schools, before proceeding through the ritual-interpretive topics adhyāya by adhyāya.
SA_GSP28_sastrdiu.history: Pārthasārathi Miśra, a leading defender of Kumārila's Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā, also wrote the Nyāyaratnamālā and Śāstradīpikā became a standard textbook of the school, attracting later commentaries (e.g., the Mayūkhamālikā).

SA_GSP28_shabara.title: Mīmāṃsāsūtra Shabara-Bhāṣya
SA_GSP28_shabara.author: Jaimini (sūtra); Śabara (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP28_shabara.date_estimate: Sūtra c. 3rd–1st c. BCE; bhāṣya c. 1st–4th c. CE
SA_GSP28_shabara.summary: This is the combined text of Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra together with Śabara's foundational commentary. The sūtras lay out the principles of Vedic ritual exegesis and the inquiry into dharma; the bhāṣya supplies the earliest full explanation, establishing Mīmāṃsā doctrines on Vedic authority, injunction, and word-meaning.
SA_GSP28_shabara.history: The pairing of Jaimini's root text with Śabara's bhāṣya constitutes the textual core of the entire Pūrva Mīmāṃsā tradition and the springboard for Kumārila and Prabhākara.

SA_GSP29_antark.title: Tarkasaṃgraha
SA_GSP29_antark.author: Annambhaṭṭa
SA_GSP29_antark.summary: The Tarkasaṃgraha is a concise and immensely popular introductory manual of the combined Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika system. It defines and classifies the seven Vaiśeṣika categories (padārtha) — substance, quality, action, etc. — and expounds the Nyāya theory of the four means of valid knowledge (perception, inference, comparison, testimony).
SA_GSP29_antark.history: Written by Annambhaṭṭa of Andhra, it is traditionally studied with his own auto-commentary the Tarkasaṃgrahadīpikā and has served for centuries as the standard beginner's textbook in Indian logic.

SA_GSP29_antsdi_u.title: Tarkasaṃgraha
SA_GSP29_antsdi_u.author: Annambhaṭṭa
SA_GSP29_antsdi_u.genre: philosophical treatise (primer), likely with Dīpikā commentary
SA_GSP29_antsdi_u.summary: Another witness of Annambhaṭṭa's Tarkasaṃgraha, the standard introductory manual of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika. The work_id suffix 'sdi' suggests this version includes the author's own Dīpikā auto-commentary, which expands on the terse definitions of the categories and the means of valid knowledge.
SA_GSP29_antsdi_u.history: The Tarkasaṃgraha with the Tarkasaṃgrahadīpikā is the most widely used elementary textbook of the Navya-Nyaya-influenced classical logic tradition in India.

SA_GSP29_gangtciu.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi
SA_GSP29_gangtciu.author: Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya
SA_GSP29_gangtciu.date_estimate: c. 12th–14th c. CE (commonly c. 1325; some place him 12th–13th c.)
SA_GSP29_gangtciu.summary: The Tattvacintāmaṇi ('Wish-Fulfilling Jewel of Truth') is the foundational work of Navya-Nyāya ('New Logic'). Organized into four books on the four pramāṇas — perception, inference, comparison, and verbal testimony — it develops a rigorous technical language for analyzing cognition, relations, and conditions, transforming Indian logic and epistemology.
SA_GSP29_gangtciu.history: Gaṅgeśa of Mithilā inaugurated the Navya-Nyāya movement; the Tattvacintāmaṇi spawned a vast commentarial literature (Raghunātha, Mathurānātha, Jagadīśa, Gadādhara) and dominated Sanskrit scholastic method for centuries.

SA_GSP29_nyayakumudachandra.title: Nyāyakumudacandra
SA_GSP29_nyayakumudachandra.author: Prabhācandra
SA_GSP29_nyayakumudachandra.summary: The Nyāyakumudacandra is Prabhācandra's extensive commentary on Akalaṅka's Laghīyastraya, a concise treatise on Jain epistemology and logic. It elaborates the Jain theory of pramāṇa (valid knowledge), the doctrine of many-sidedness (anekāntavāda) and conditional predication (syādvāda), and refutes rival Buddhist, Nyāya, and Mīmāṃsā positions.
SA_GSP29_nyayakumudachandra.history: Prabhācandra was a Digambara Jain logician whose two great commentaries — the Nyāyakumudacandra on the Laghīyastraya and the Prameyakamalamārtaṇḍa on Māṇikyanandin's Parīkṣāmukha — are major monuments of medieval Jain dialectics.

SA_GSP29_gaunys_u.title: Nyāyasūtra
SA_GSP29_gaunys_u.author: Gautama (Akṣapāda Gautama)
SA_GSP29_gaunys_u.date_estimate: core c. 2nd c. BCE–2nd c. CE (composite, redacted to c. 2nd c. CE)
SA_GSP29_gaunys_u.summary: The Nyāyasūtra is the root text of the Nyāya school of logic and epistemology. In five books (adhyāyas) it enumerates sixteen categories of dialectics beginning with the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇa) and objects of knowledge (prameya), and lays out the theory of inference, debate, fallacies, and the path to liberation through correct knowledge.
SA_GSP29_gaunys_u.history: Attributed to Akṣapāda Gautama, the text is composite, reaching its received form around the 2nd century CE; it is the basis of Vātsyāyana's bhāṣya and the entire Nyāya commentarial tradition.

SA_GSP29_muva.title: Muktivāda
SA_GSP29_muva.author: Gadādhara Bhaṭṭācārya
SA_GSP29_muva.summary: The Muktivāda ('Discourse on Liberation') is a Navya-Nyāya monograph examining the concept of mokṣa/mukti — the nature of liberation, whether it consists in the cessation of suffering or in positive bliss, and the conditions and causes of release. It applies the technical analytical apparatus of late Nyāya to the soteriological question.
SA_GSP29_muva.history: Gadādhara of Bengal (Navadvīpa) was one of the greatest late Navya-Nyāya logicians, author of numerous vāda monographs; the Muktivāda continues the school's long inquiry into liberation traceable back to the Nyāyasūtra.

SA_GSP29_nvtp1_1u.title: Nyāyavārttikatātparyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.1
SA_GSP29_nvtp1_1u.author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
SA_GSP29_nvtp1_1u.summary: The Nyāyavārttikatātparyapariśuddhi is Udayana's sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Vācaspati Miśra's Nyāyavārttikatātparyaṭīkā, which itself glosses Uddyotakara's Nyāyavārttika on the Nyāyasūtra. This record covers the first chapter (1.1), treating the foundational topics of the means and objects of valid knowledge and defending Nyāya realism against Buddhist critiques.
SA_GSP29_nvtp1_1u.history: Udayana was a towering figure of late 'old' Nyāya, also famed for the Ātmatattvaviveka and Nyāyakusumāñjali; his Pariśuddhi completes the layered commentarial edifice on Gautama's sūtras.

SA_GSP29_nvtp1_2u.title: Nyāyavārttikatātparyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.2
SA_GSP29_nvtp1_2u.author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
SA_GSP29_nvtp1_2u.summary: This is the second section (adhyāya 1.2) of Udayana's Nyāyavārttikatātparyapariśuddhi, his sub-commentary on Vācaspati Miśra's gloss on the Nyāyavārttika. Book 1.2 of the Nyāyasūtra deals with the categories of debate (vāda, jalpa, vitaṇḍā), fallacious reasons (hetvābhāsa), quibbling, and clinchers; Udayana clarifies and defends these dialectical topics.
SA_GSP29_nvtp1_2u.history: Part of Udayana's authoritative completion of the Nyāya commentarial chain on Gautama's sūtras, integrating the realist refutation of Buddhist epistemology.

SA_GSP29_nyabhu.title: Nyāyabhūṣaṇa
SA_GSP29_nyabhu.author: Bhāsarvajña
SA_GSP29_nyabhu.summary: The Nyāyabhūṣaṇa is Bhāsarvajña's own extensive commentary on his concise treatise the Nyāyasāra. It expounds his distinctive Nyāya epistemology, recognizing perception, inference, and verbal testimony as the means of valid knowledge, and engages critically with Buddhist anti-realism and rival schools on perception, inference, and the existence of God and self.
SA_GSP29_nyabhu.history: Bhāsarvajña (c. 950 CE) represents a partly independent, Śaiva-inflected current within Nyāya; the Nyāyabhūṣaṇa is a rich source for the school's debates with Buddhist logicians of the period.

SA_GSP29_nyaka.title: Nyāyakalikā
SA_GSP29_nyaka.author: Jayanta Bhaṭṭa
SA_GSP29_nyaka.summary: The Nyāyakalikā ('Bud of Nyāya') is a short introductory overview of the basic tenets of the Nyāya system, concisely defining the sixteen categories (padārtha) of the Nyāyasūtra. It is designed as a primer emphasizing clarity and brevity over extensive argument.
SA_GSP29_nyaka.history: Jayanta Bhaṭṭa of Kashmir (c. 820–900 CE), advisor to King Śaṅkaravarman, is best known for his magisterial Nyāyamañjarī; the Nyāyakalikā is his brief beginner's textbook on the same system.

SA_GSP29_nybha.title: Nyāyasūtra + Bhāṣya
SA_GSP29_nybha.author: Gautama (sūtra); Vātsyāyana (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP29_nybha.date_estimate: Sūtra c. 2nd c. CE; Vātsyāyana's bhāṣya c. 4th–5th c. CE
SA_GSP29_nybha.summary: This is the combined text of Gautama's Nyāyasūtra together with Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya, the earliest and authoritative commentary. The bhāṣya explains the sixteen dialectical categories, develops the theory of perception and inference, and defends the realist epistemology and the self against opponents.
SA_GSP29_nybha.history: Vātsyāyana (Pakṣilasvāmin) gave Nyāya its first systematic exposition; his bhāṣya is the base layer for Uddyotakara's vārttika and the whole later tradition.

SA_GSP29_nystik_u.title: Nyāyasūtra
SA_GSP29_nystik_u.author: Gautama (Akṣapāda Gautama)
SA_GSP29_nystik_u.genre: sūtra (here likely with a ṭīkā commentary)
SA_GSP29_nystik_u.date_estimate: core c. 2nd c. BCE–2nd c. CE (composite)
SA_GSP29_nystik_u.summary: A witness of Gautama's Nyāyasūtra, the root text of the Nyāya school, whose work_id suffix 'tik' suggests an accompanying ṭīkā (commentary) layer. The sūtras set out the sixteen categories of logic and epistemology, the four pramāṇas, the theory of inference and debate, and liberation through true knowledge.
SA_GSP29_nystik_u.history: The Nyāyasūtra, attributed to Akṣapāda Gautama and redacted around the 2nd century CE, is the foundation of the Nyāya tradition and its many commentaries.

SA_GSP29_nysu51pu.title: Nyāyasūtra 5.1
SA_GSP29_nysu51pu.author: Gautama (Akṣapāda Gautama)
SA_GSP29_nysu51pu.date_estimate: core c. 2nd c. BCE–2nd c. CE (composite)
SA_GSP29_nysu51pu.summary: This record is the first section of the fifth book (adhyāya 5.1) of Gautama's Nyāyasūtra. Book 5 deals with futile rejoinders (jāti) — the twenty-four types of sophistical counter-arguments — and (in 5.2) the points of defeat (nigrahasthāna) in formal debate; 5.1 catalogues and analyzes the jātis.
SA_GSP29_nysu51pu.history: The fifth book completes the Nyāyasūtra's treatment of debate theory, a subject central to the school's identity as a science of reasoning and disputation.

SA_GSP29_nysvbh.title: Nyāyasūtra
SA_GSP29_nysvbh.author: Gautama (sūtra); Vātsyāyana (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP29_nysvbh.date_estimate: Sūtra c. 2nd c. CE; bhāṣya c. 4th–5th c. CE
SA_GSP29_nysvbh.summary: A witness of the Nyāyasūtra; the work_id element 'svbh' (sūtra-vātsyāyana-bhāṣya) indicates the text is paired with Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya. It presents Gautama's sixteen categories of logic and epistemology together with the earliest authoritative commentary.
SA_GSP29_nysvbh.history: Vātsyāyana's bhāṣya is the foundation of the Nyāya exegetical tradition; this is one of several digitized witnesses of the sūtra-plus-bhāṣya text in the collection.

SA_GSP29_nysvbh1u.title: Nyāyasūtra + Bhāṣya
SA_GSP29_nysvbh1u.author: Gautama (sūtra); Vātsyāyana (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP29_nysvbh1u.date_estimate: Sūtra c. 2nd c. CE; Vātsyāyana's bhāṣya c. 4th–5th c. CE
SA_GSP29_nysvbh1u.summary: Another witness of Gautama's Nyāyasūtra together with Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya. The combined text expounds the sixteen dialectical categories, the four means of valid knowledge, the theory of inference, and the realist defense of the self and the external world.
SA_GSP29_nysvbh1u.history: This is a parallel digitized version of the sūtra-plus-Vātsyāyana-bhāṣya text (cf. work_id GSP29nybha / GSP29nysvbh_u), the cornerstone of classical Nyāya.

SA_GSP29_snyayvpu.title: Nyāyāvatāra
SA_GSP29_snyayvpu.author: Siddhasena Divākara (Siddhasena Mahāmati)
SA_GSP29_snyayvpu.date_estimate: c. 5th–6th c. CE (disputed; sometimes placed after Diṅnāga, post-c. 550 CE)
SA_GSP29_snyayvpu.summary: The Nyāyāvatāra is a short verse work generally regarded as the earliest extant Jain treatise on pure logic and epistemology. In about 32 verses it expounds the Jain theory of valid knowledge (pramāṇa) — direct and indirect cognition, inference, and verbal testimony — and connects it with the Jain doctrines of many-sidedness (syādvāda/anekānta).
SA_GSP29_snyayvpu.history: Traditionally ascribed to Siddhasena Divākara (also author of the Sanmatitarka), its dating and authorship are debated, since elements appear to respond to Diṅnāga's Buddhist logic; it founded the systematic Jain pramāṇa tradition later developed by Akalaṅka and others.

SA_GSP29_udnyku1u.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 1
SA_GSP29_udnyku1u.author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
SA_GSP29_udnyku1u.summary: The Nyāyakusumāñjali ('Offering of a Handful of Flowers of Reasoning') is Udayana's systematic rational defense of theism (īśvaravāda), built in five chapters (stavakas) of kārikās with the author's own prose commentary. Stavaka 1 opens the work by refuting the Mīmāṃsaka and Cārvāka positions that deny the need for a God, defending the reality of adṛṣṭa (unseen moral causation) and the dependence of the moral order on a supreme cause, thereby establishing the necessity of a regulating intelligent agent.
SA_GSP29_udnyku1u.history: Udayana, a Maithila logician at the transition from the older Nyāya to Navya-Nyāya, composed the Nyāyakusumāñjali as the most elaborate Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treatise on the existence of God; it codifies the classic arguments and attracted numerous later commentaries (e.g. by Varadarāja, Vardhamāna, and Haridāsa). It remains the foundational theistic text of the school.

SA_GSP29_udnyku2u.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 2
SA_GSP29_udnyku2u.author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
SA_GSP29_udnyku2u.summary: Second chapter of Udayana's five-chapter theistic treatise. Stavaka 2 argues against those who hold that the world has no need of a cause, defending the principle that effects require an agent-cause and that the cosmos as a contingent product points beyond itself; it counters skeptical and non-theistic objections to causal reasoning that would undermine the inference to God.
SA_GSP29_udnyku2u.history: Part of Udayana's Nyāyakusumāñjali, the school's classic demonstration of īśvara; the work's argumentative architecture culminates only in the final chapter, with the earlier stavakas dismantling rival anti-theistic positions one by one. Widely commented upon in later Nyāya scholasticism.

SA_GSP29_udnyku3u.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 3
SA_GSP29_udnyku3u.author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
SA_GSP29_udnyku3u.summary: Third chapter of the Nyāyakusumāñjali. Stavaka 3 defends the existence of an enduring unseen agency (adṛṣṭa/dharma-adharma) and the soul as moral substrate, refuting the Buddhist doctrine of momentariness and the denial of a permanent self, since the theistic argument requires real agents whose merit and demerit must be supervised by a divine dispenser of results.
SA_GSP29_udnyku3u.history: Within Udayana's anti-Buddhist and pro-theistic program, this chapter overlaps thematically with his Ātmatattvaviveka in rebutting Buddhist denial of permanent entities. The Nyāyakusumāñjali stands as Udayana's mature synthesis and was central to subsequent Navya-Nyāya theism.

SA_GSP29_udnyku4u.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 4
SA_GSP29_udnyku4u.author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
SA_GSP29_udnyku4u.summary: Fourth chapter of the Nyāyakusumāñjali. Stavaka 4 takes up the validity of inference and verbal testimony as instruments by which God can be established, defending the means of knowledge (pramāṇa) against skeptical attacks and preparing the logical ground for the formal proof that follows.
SA_GSP29_udnyku4u.history: This chapter secures the epistemological prerequisites of Udayana's theistic argument, reflecting his broader concern with vindicating the Nyāya pramāṇas. The treatise as a whole is the most fundamental Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika work on the īśvara doctrine.

SA_GSP29_udnyku5u.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 5
SA_GSP29_udnyku5u.author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
SA_GSP29_udnyku5u.summary: The culminating fifth chapter of the Nyāyakusumāñjali, where Udayana presents his positive demonstration of the existence of God, marshalling a famous set of arguments (commonly counted as around nine) including the cosmological argument from effects (kāryāt), from the support of the world (dhṛti), and from the authority of scripture and the necessity of a guarantor of the moral law. Here the entire treatise's argumentative scaffolding is brought to bear on proving a single creator-Lord.
SA_GSP29_udnyku5u.history: Stavaka 5 contains the school's classic proofs of God and is the most cited portion of the work; Udayana's formulation became the standard reference point that later thinkers such as Gaṅgeśa and his critics (e.g. Vyāsatīrtha) engaged with for centuries.

SA_GSP29_vidhi.title: Vidhivāda
SA_GSP29_vidhi.author: Gaṅgeśa (Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya)
SA_GSP29_vidhi.genre: philosophical treatise (section of a larger work)
SA_GSP29_vidhi.date_estimate: first half of the 14th century CE (Gaṅgeśa fl. c. 1325)
SA_GSP29_vidhi.summary: The Vidhivāda ('Discourse on Injunctions') is a section of the Śabdakhaṇḍa (the book on verbal testimony) of Gaṅgeśa's Tattvacintāmaṇi. It treats the philosophy of Vedic and other injunctions (vidhi): what an injunctive verbal form signifies, the nature of the impelling force (bhāvanā/pravartanā) it conveys, and how the listener derives motivation to act, engaging closely with Mīmāṃsā theories of command.
SA_GSP29_vidhi.history: Gaṅgeśa of Mithilā founded the Navya-Nyāya ('New Logic') school, and his Tattvacintāmaṇi became the basic text for all later developments; the Vidhivāda within its Śabda book is a much-studied unit on the semantics of injunctions, treated in modern scholarship (e.g. V. N. Jha's edition with Śaśadhara's gloss).

SA_GSP29_vnyps00u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Bhūmikā
SA_GSP29_vnyps00u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps00u.summary: The Nyāyapariśuddhi ('Purification of Logic') is Vedānta Deśika's systematic treatise on the epistemology (pramāṇa theory) of Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta. The Bhūmikā is its introductory portion, setting out the scope of the work, the enumeration of the valid means of knowledge admitted by the school (perception, inference, and verbal testimony), and the polemical orientation that adapts and modifies Nyāya doctrine to suit Viśiṣṭādvaita.
SA_GSP29_vnyps00u.history: Veṅkaṭanātha, the great Śrīvaiṣṇava poet-philosopher of Kāñcī/Śrīraṅgam, wrote the Nyāyapariśuddhi as the school's counterpart to Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā epistemology; it is regarded as a definitive introduction to Viśiṣṭādvaita pramāṇa theory and was followed by his own Nyāyasiddhāñjana and commentaries by his successors.

SA_GSP29_vnyps11u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.1
SA_GSP29_vnyps11u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps11u.summary: Opening section (chapter 1, part 1) of Vedānta Deśika's Nyāyapariśuddhi. This part establishes the general framework of pramāṇa theory, the definition and number of valid means of knowledge accepted in Viśiṣṭādvaita, and begins the detailed treatment of perception (pratyakṣa) as a source of valid cognition.
SA_GSP29_vnyps11u.history: Part of the school's foundational epistemological treatise, in which Deśika adopts and modifies the Naiyāyika theory of pramāṇa to make it consistent with Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita. Transmitted with traditional Śrīvaiṣṇava commentaries.

SA_GSP29_vnyps12u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.2
SA_GSP29_vnyps12u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps12u.summary: Chapter 1, part 2 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, continuing the analysis of perception (pratyakṣa) and related epistemic issues within the Viśiṣṭādvaita scheme of valid cognition, including the conditions of valid perceptual knowledge and the refutation of rival accounts.
SA_GSP29_vnyps12u.history: Section of Vedānta Deśika's authoritative Viśiṣṭādvaita epistemology, engaging Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā and Buddhist epistemologists while defending the Śrīvaiṣṇava position.

SA_GSP29_vnyps31u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 3.1
SA_GSP29_vnyps31u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps31u.summary: Chapter 3, part 1 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi. The third book turns to inference (anumāna): the structure of valid inferential reasoning, the nature of invariable concomitance (vyāpti), the members of a syllogism, and the fallacies, all reworked to fit Viśiṣṭādvaita commitments.
SA_GSP29_vnyps31u.history: Continuation of Deśika's systematic pramāṇa exposition; his treatment of inference adapts Navya-Nyāya logical apparatus to Śrīvaiṣṇava theology.

SA_GSP29_vnyps32u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 3.2
SA_GSP29_vnyps32u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps32u.summary: Chapter 3, part 2 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, continuing the treatment of inference (anumāna), including further analysis of the grounds of inferential validity, types of inference, and the refutation of opposing logical doctrines.
SA_GSP29_vnyps32u.history: Section of Vedānta Deśika's epistemological treatise on the logic of inference within Viśiṣṭādvaita.

SA_GSP29_vnyps41u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 4.1
SA_GSP29_vnyps41u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps41u.summary: Chapter 4, part 1 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi. The fourth book deals with verbal testimony (śabda/āgama) as a means of valid knowledge: the authority of the Veda, how words convey meaning, sentence-meaning, and the privileged epistemic status of scripture for establishing supersensible truths in Viśiṣṭādvaita.
SA_GSP29_vnyps41u.history: Because śabda is the decisive pramāṇa for Vedāntic theology, this book is central to Deśika's defense of scriptural authority against Mīmāṃsā and others.

SA_GSP29_vnyps42u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 4.2
SA_GSP29_vnyps42u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps42u.summary: Chapter 4, part 2 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, continuing the treatment of verbal testimony (śabda): the apprehension of sentence-meaning, the role of expectancy, proximity and compatibility, and the validity of Vedic and authoritative human speech within the Viśiṣṭādvaita pramāṇa scheme.
SA_GSP29_vnyps42u.history: Section of Deśika's exposition of śabda-pramāṇa, vindicating the epistemic authority of scripture and language for Śrīvaiṣṇava Vedānta.

SA_GSP29_vnyps51u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 5.1
SA_GSP29_vnyps51u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps51u.summary: Chapter 5, part 1 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi. The closing book addresses ancillary epistemological topics and the consolidation of the school's pramāṇa doctrine, including the reduction of disputed alleged means of knowledge (such as comparison, presumption, and non-apprehension) to the three accepted pramāṇas.
SA_GSP29_vnyps51u.history: Concluding portion of Deśika's authoritative Viśiṣṭādvaita epistemology, settling the school's final position against rival enumerations of the pramāṇas.

SA_GSP29_vnyps52u.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 5.2
SA_GSP29_vnyps52u.author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
SA_GSP29_vnyps52u.summary: Chapter 5, part 2, the final section of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, completing the treatment of disputed and reducible means of knowledge and bringing the work's systematic defense of the three-pramāṇa epistemology of Viśiṣṭādvaita to a close.
SA_GSP29_vnyps52u.history: Closing section of Vedānta Deśika's epistemological masterwork, foundational for later Śrīvaiṣṇava logic and apologetics.

SA_GSP29_vyomavati.title: Vyomavatī
SA_GSP29_vyomavati.author: Vyomaśiva (Vyomaśivācārya)
SA_GSP29_vyomavati.date_estimate: c. 9th-10th century CE (commonly c. 900-1000 CE; some propose 8th c.)
SA_GSP29_vyomavati.summary: The Vyomavatī is a major commentary on Praśastapāda's Padārthadharmasaṅgraha, the standard manual of Vaiśeṣika categories (padārtha). It expounds and defends the Vaiśeṣika ontology of substance, quality, action, universal, particularity and inherence, treating at length issues of cognition, the means of knowledge, and the existence of God, and is one of the earliest surviving commentaries on Praśastapāda.
SA_GSP29_vyomavati.history: Vyomaśiva, a Śaiva ascetic-scholar associated with Kashmir, is generally regarded as the earliest of the three classic commentators on the Padārthadharmasaṅgraha (alongside Śrīdhara's Nyāyakandalī and Udayana's Kiraṇāvalī). His date is debated, ranging from the 8th to the 10th century, with most scholars favoring around 900-1000 CE.

SA_GSP29_vytrkt1u.title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 1
SA_GSP29_vytrkt1u.author: Vyāsatīrtha (Vyāsarāja)
SA_GSP29_vytrkt1u.tradition: Hindu (Dvaita / Mādhva Vedānta, polemicizing against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika)
SA_GSP29_vytrkt1u.summary: The Tarkatāṇḍava ('Dance of Reason/Logic') is Vyāsatīrtha's exhaustive Dvaita critique of the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika system, deploying the rigorous techniques of Navya-Nyāya against its own home tradition. Pariccheda 1 corresponds to the examination of perception (pratyakṣa), scrutinizing the Naiyāyika account of perceptual cognition and exposing its alleged inconsistencies from the Mādhva standpoint.
SA_GSP29_vytrkt1u.history: Vyāsatīrtha, the great Mādhva scholar-saint of the Vijayanagara court, composed the Tarkatāṇḍava as one of his three polemical masterworks (the Vyāsatraya, with the Nyāyāmṛta and Tātparyacandrikā). It engages Gaṅgeśa's Tattvacintāmaṇi closely, including the inference of God's existence, and shaped early-modern debates with Navya-Naiyāyikas.

SA_GSP29_vytrkt2u.title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 2
SA_GSP29_vytrkt2u.author: Vyāsatīrtha (Vyāsarāja)
SA_GSP29_vytrkt2u.tradition: Hindu (Dvaita / Mādhva Vedānta, polemicizing against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika)
SA_GSP29_vytrkt2u.summary: Second section of the Tarkatāṇḍava. Following the structure of Nyāya pramāṇa theory, this pariccheda takes up inference (anumāna), critically dissecting the Naiyāyika doctrine of invariable concomitance and inferential reasoning, including Vyāsatīrtha's much-discussed treatment of Gaṅgeśa's inference for the existence of God.
SA_GSP29_vytrkt2u.history: Part of Vyāsatīrtha's encyclopaedic refutation of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika logic; his critique here prompted later responses, notably Appaya Dīkṣita's revision of Gaṅgeśa's īśvarānumāna.

SA_GSP29_vytrkt3u.title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 3
SA_GSP29_vytrkt3u.author: Vyāsatīrtha (Vyāsarāja)
SA_GSP29_vytrkt3u.tradition: Hindu (Dvaita / Mādhva Vedānta, polemicizing against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika)
SA_GSP29_vytrkt3u.summary: Third section of the Tarkatāṇḍava, corresponding to verbal testimony (śabda) and related epistemological topics. Here Vyāsatīrtha critiques the Nyāya theory of the meaning and authority of words and the school's account of valid linguistic cognition, defending the Mādhva position on scripture and language.
SA_GSP29_vytrkt3u.history: Concludes the three-part doxographical critique of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika that, together with the Nyāyāmṛta and Tātparyacandrikā, constitutes Vyāsatīrtha's Vyāsatraya and influenced subsequent Dvaita-Nyāya polemics.

SA_GSP30_abhiprvu.title: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī
SA_GSP30_abhiprvu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_abhiprvu.summary: The Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī is Abhinavagupta's commentary (the shorter 'vimarśinī', as opposed to his longer Vivṛtivimarśinī) on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational verse-text of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of Kashmir Śaivism. It expounds the doctrine that liberation comes through recognizing one's own self as identical with the supreme Lord Śiva, developing a non-dual idealist metaphysics centered on consciousness (cit), reflective awareness (vimarśa), and divine freedom (svātantrya).
SA_GSP30_abhiprvu.history: Abhinavagupta, the towering polymath of Kashmir Śaivism, wrote this commentary to make Utpaladeva's compressed kārikās accessible; it became, with the Vivṛtivimarśinī, the principal vehicle for transmitting Pratyabhijñā philosophy. The work was itself further commented upon (notably by Bhāskarakaṇṭha's Bhāskarī).

SA_GSP30_abhmal1u.title: Mālinīślokavārttika: Kāṇḍa 1
SA_GSP30_abhmal1u.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_abhmal1u.summary: The first kāṇḍa of Abhinavagupta's verse-commentary (ślokavārttika) on the Mālinīvijayottaratantra, the Trika scripture he regarded as supreme. Kāṇḍa 1 is a long exegetical reflection chiefly on the first verse (the maṅgala) of the Mālinīvijaya, unfolding from it the entire metaphysics and soteriology of nondual Trika Shaivism, including the nature of consciousness, the descent of grace (śaktipāta), and the relation of Shiva and Shakti.
SA_GSP30_abhmal1u.history: Composed by the polymath Abhinavagupta (fl. c. 975-1025 CE), the central exegete of nondual Kashmir Shaivism, alongside his Tantrāloka and Tantrasāra. The Mālinīślokavārttika draws not only on the Mālinīvijayottara but also on the Kālī-based Krama tradition, and is among the most important theoretical works of the Trika.

SA_GSP30_abhmal2u.title: Mālinīślokavārttika: Kāṇḍa 2
SA_GSP30_abhmal2u.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_abhmal2u.summary: The second kāṇḍa of Abhinavagupta's verse-commentary on the Mālinīvijayottaratantra. It continues the exposition of Trika doctrine begun in Kāṇḍa 1, treating the means of liberation (the upāyas), the structure of the universe and the tattvas, mantra and the alphabet, and the practical-mystical aspects of nondual Shaiva monism as encoded in the Mālinī scripture.
SA_GSP30_abhmal2u.history: Part of the same work by Abhinavagupta as Kāṇḍa 1, one of his principal exegetical compositions in the Trika tradition. The Mālinīślokavārttika unpacks the esoteric theoretical and practical doctrines of the Mālinīvijayottara.

SA_GSP30_abhtantu.title: Tantrasāra
SA_GSP30_abhtantu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_abhtantu.summary: Abhinavagupta's own prose abridgement ("Essence of Tantra") of his vast verse encyclopedia, the Tantrāloka. In a lucid, mostly prose style it condenses the doctrine, ritual, yoga, and soteriology of the Trika across roughly its chapters, presenting the system of the four means (anupāya, śāmbhava, śākta, āṇava upāyas) of attaining liberation through recognition of one's identity with Shiva.
SA_GSP30_abhtantu.history: Written by Abhinavagupta to spare his disciples the difficulty of the much longer Tantrāloka, the Tantrasāra became a widely studied summary-encyclopedia of Trika tantra. It transmits the synthesis Abhinavagupta forged from the Mālinīvijayottara and the Kaula and Krama currents of Kashmiri Shaivism.

SA_GSP30_adyapppu.title: Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā
SA_GSP30_adyapppu.author: Ādyanātha
SA_GSP30_adyapppu.genre: philosophical/mystical poem (pañcāśikā, fifty verses), stotra-like treatise
SA_GSP30_adyapppu.summary: A short devotional-philosophical poem of about fifty verses on the "light of the Absolute" (anuttara-prakāśa), expounding the nondual Shaiva realization that supreme consciousness (anuttara) is the sole reality shining as the whole universe. It belongs to the genre of compact pañcāśikā works that distil Trika/Kaula recognition doctrine into a meditative, hymn-like form.
SA_GSP30_adyapppu.history: Attributed to one Ādyanātha, an otherwise little-documented author within the nondual Kashmiri Shaiva milieu; the title and idiom place it firmly in the anuttara-centered Trika tradition associated with Abhinavagupta, though its precise date and the author's identity remain obscure.

SA_GSP30_anuttaru.title: Anuttarāṣṭikā
SA_GSP30_anuttaru.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_anuttaru.summary: A brief hymn of eight verses (aṣṭikā) on the Absolute (anuttara) by Abhinavagupta. It is a concentrated expression of nondual realization: there is nothing to attain, abandon, or practice, since one is already the unbounded, ever-free consciousness that is Shiva; the verses dissolve the seeker's effort into spontaneous recognition.
SA_GSP30_anuttaru.history: One of Abhinavagupta's celebrated short stotras, valued in Kashmir Shaivism for distilling the highest (anupāya) teaching of immediate self-recognition. It is regularly anthologized and recited in the living Trika tradition (e.g. by Swami Lakshmanjoo's lineage).

SA_GSP30_aparmyou.title: Paramārthasāra
SA_GSP30_aparmyou.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_aparmyou.summary: The "Essence of the Supreme Truth," a compendium of about 105 ārya verses presenting the nondual Trika metaphysics: the manifestation of the universe as the free self-expression of Shiva-consciousness, the bondage of the soul through contraction, and liberation through recognition (pratyabhijñā) of one's true identity. It is Abhinavagupta's recasting of an older Vaiṣṇava-Sāṃkhya text into Shaiva nondualism.
SA_GSP30_aparmyou.history: Abhinavagupta adapted the title and frame of Ādiśeṣa's (Patañjali-attributed) Paramārthasāra/Ādhārakārikā, transforming it into a Trika manual; Yogarāja later wrote an influential commentary (vivṛti). It is one of the most accessible summaries of Kashmir Shaivism.

SA_GSP30_bhairstu.title: Bhairavastava
SA_GSP30_bhairstu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_bhairstu.summary: A short hymn of praise to Bhairava, the fierce nondual form of Shiva identified with the seeker's own innermost consciousness. The poet, oppressed by fear and the troubles of worldly existence, takes refuge in Bhairava and affirms that remembrance of Bhairava as one's own Self instantly dispels all terror and bondage.
SA_GSP30_bhairstu.history: One of Abhinavagupta's devotional stotras, recited in the Trika tradition (it forms part of the hymns used in Kashmiri Shaiva worship). It expresses in emotional, personal idiom the same anuttara/recognition doctrine systematized in his prose works.

SA_GSP30_bhutisbs_u.title: Svabodhasiddhi
SA_GSP30_bhutisbs_u.author: Bhūti(rāja)
SA_GSP30_bhutisbs_u.summary: A short Shaiva work, the "Establishing of Self-Awareness," arguing for the self-luminous, nondual nature of consciousness as the foundation of liberation. It belongs to the demonstrative (siddhi) genre of Kashmiri Shaiva philosophy that seeks to prove the recognition of the Self as Shiva.
SA_GSP30_bhutisbs_u.history: Identified by Alexis Sanderson as probably the work of the 10th-century Kashmiri Shaiva master Bhūtirāja, who was among Abhinavagupta's principal teachers (instructing him in the dualist/nondualist Siddhānta) and an important guru in the Krama lineage and father of two noted scholars. The text was the subject of an edition/study published in the ZDMG.

SA_GSP30_bodhpanu.title: Bodhapañcadaśikā
SA_GSP30_bodhpanu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_bodhpanu.summary: The "Fifteen Verses on Awakening," a concise didactic poem in which Abhinavagupta sets out the essentials of nondual Shaiva metaphysics for beginners: the single consciousness (caitanya) that is Shiva manifests as Shakti and the whole hierarchy of tattvas, and liberation is the recognition that the perceiver, perceiving, and perceived are all that one light of awareness.
SA_GSP30_bodhpanu.history: A short introductory tract by Abhinavagupta meant to convey the Trika system in compact, memorizable form; it is frequently grouped with his other short philosophical poems and stotras as an entry point to the recognition (pratyabhijñā) doctrine.

SA_GSP30_kramstou.title: Kramastotra
SA_GSP30_kramstou.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_kramstou.summary: A hymn celebrating the Krama ("sequence") goddesses and the cyclic phases of cognition through which the worship of the Kālī-energies of consciousness proceeds. It praises the successive deities/phases of the cognitive cycle (creation, maintenance, dissolution, and the nameless fourth) as forms of the supreme Goddess, leading the worshipper to nondual realization.
SA_GSP30_kramstou.history: Abhinavagupta's Kramastotra reflects the strong Krama (Kālīkula) component he absorbed into his Trika synthesis; the Krama Stotra is among the hymns transmitted and recited within Kashmir Shaivism. An earlier Kramastotra is associated with the Krama master Siddhanātha/Eraka, and Abhinavagupta's hymn stands in that lineage.

SA_GSP30_ksembodhv_u.title: Bodhavilāsa
SA_GSP30_ksembodhv_u.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_GSP30_ksembodhv_u.summary: A short work, the "Play of Awakened Awareness," attributed to Kṣemarāja, expounding the nondual self-recognition doctrine: the world is the joyous self-display (vilāsa) of one luminous consciousness (bodha). It distills, in compact form, the same heart-of-recognition teaching set out in his Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya.
SA_GSP30_ksembodhv_u.history: Ascribed to Kṣemarāja (late 10th-early 11th c.), the foremost disciple of Abhinavagupta and the great systematizer-commentator of Kashmir Shaivism (author of the Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya, Śivasūtravimarśinī, Spandanirṇaya, etc.). The Bodhavilāsa is a minor work and its details and attribution are less securely documented than his major treatises.

SA_GSP30_parmartu.title: Paramārthasāra
SA_GSP30_parmartu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_parmartu.summary: Abhinavagupta's "Essence of the Supreme Truth," a compendium of about 105 ārya verses summarizing nondual Trika metaphysics and soteriology: the free manifestation of the cosmos within Shiva-consciousness, the soul's contraction into bondage, and liberation through recognition of identity with the Absolute. (This is a second catalogue entry for the same work as GSP30aparmyou, here given a narrower date range.)
SA_GSP30_parmartu.history: Adapted by Abhinavagupta from the older Paramārthasāra (Ādhārakārikā) of Ādiśeṣa, recast in Shaiva nondual terms; Yogarāja's commentary (vivṛti) made it a standard teaching text of Kashmir Shaivism.

SA_GSP30_partr_pu.title: Parātriṃśikā
SA_GSP30_partr_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GSP30_partr_pu.date_estimate: before c. 9th c. CE (early tantric layer; date disputed)
SA_GSP30_partr_pu.summary: A short revealed Trika scripture of about 36 verses (also called Parātrīśikā/Parāpraveśikā), traditionally regarded as the concluding/essential portion of the Rudrayāmala Tantra. In dialogue form (Bhairava and the Goddess) it concentrates on the supreme reality and the methods of its realization through mantra, especially a profound metaphysics of the Word (vāc) and the phonematic emanation of the alphabet from consciousness.
SA_GSP30_partr_pu.history: An anonymous tantra held in the highest esteem in Kashmir Shaivism; it attracted commentaries by Somānanda and especially Abhinavagupta (both a short Laghuvṛtti and the extensive Vivaraṇa). Abhinavagupta preferred the title Parātrīśikā ("Supreme Goddess of the Three") over Parātriṃśikā since the verses exceed thirty.

SA_GSP30_partrvpu.title: Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa
SA_GSP30_partrvpu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_partrvpu.summary: Abhinavagupta's extensive commentary (vivaraṇa) on the Parātriṃśikā/Parātrīśikā. Far exceeding the base text in scope, it elaborates a complete metaphysics of consciousness and of language: the stages of the Word (parā, paśyantī, madhyamā, vaikharī), the emanation of the phonemes from supreme awareness, the practice and theory of mantra, and the path of nondual realization in Trika.
SA_GSP30_partrvpu.history: One of Abhinavagupta's deepest works (translated by Jaideva Singh as "The Secret of Tantric Mysticism"); he wrote both this long Vivaraṇa and a shorter Laghuvṛtti on the same scripture, building on Somānanda's earlier commentary. It is a cornerstone of the Trika philosophy of language.

SA_GSP30_parypanu.title: Paryantapañcāśikā
SA_GSP30_parypanu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_parypanu.summary: The "Fifty Verses on the Ultimate Limit," a compact philosophical poem in which Abhinavagupta describes the highest reality and the ultimate term (paryanta) of the spiritual path: the recognition that all that appears is the self-vibration of a single supreme consciousness. It presents the cosmos, the categories, and the means of liberation as resting in and dissolving back into the Absolute.
SA_GSP30_parypanu.history: A late, mature work of Abhinavagupta, sometimes regarded as one of his concluding statements of Trika doctrine; it has been edited and studied (e.g. by V. Raghavan) but is among his less widely circulated compositions.

SA_GSP30_pratyabu.title: Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya
SA_GSP30_pratyabu.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_GSP30_pratyabu.summary: The "Heart of the Doctrine of Recognition," a concise manual of twenty sūtras with the author's own prose commentary, summarizing the Pratyabhijñā philosophy for non-specialists. It teaches that the one absolute consciousness (Citi) freely manifests the universe, contracts itself into limited subjects, and is liberated through the recognition (pratyabhijñā) of its own true nature as Shiva.
SA_GSP30_pratyabu.history: Kṣemarāja, the chief disciple of Abhinavagupta, wrote this popularizing digest to make the dense Īśvarapratyabhijñā system of Utpaladeva and Abhinavagupta accessible. It became, and remains, one of the most widely read introductions to Kashmir Shaivism.

SA_GSP30_sa_avadhUtasiddha-bhagavadbhakt.title: Bhagavadbhaktistotra
SA_GSP30_sa_avadhUtasiddha-bhagavadbhakt.author: Avadhūtasiddha
SA_GSP30_sa_avadhUtasiddha-bhagavadbhakt.summary: A "Hymn of Devotion to the Lord," a Shaiva stotra expressing loving surrender (bhakti) to God conceived as the nondual supreme Self/Shiva. Like other Kashmiri Shaiva hymns in the lineage of Utpaladeva's Śivastotrāvalī, it fuses intense personal devotion with the recognition that the worshipper's own consciousness is identical with the worshipped.
SA_GSP30_sa_avadhUtasiddha-bhagavadbhakt.history: Attributed to one Avadhūtasiddha, a poorly documented figure of the nondual Kashmiri Shaiva milieu; the work survives within the corpus of Kashmiri Shaiva devotional poetry but the author's identity and exact date are not securely established.

SA_GSP30_sa_kSemarAja-bhairavAnukaraNast.title: Bhairavānukaraṇastotra
SA_GSP30_sa_kSemarAja-bhairavAnukaraNast.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_GSP30_sa_kSemarAja-bhairavAnukaraNast.summary: A "Hymn of Imitation of (i.e. assimilation to) Bhairava," in which the devotee identifies with Bhairava, the terrifying nondual form of Shiva who is the worshipper's own essential consciousness. The hymn cultivates the meditative state in which fear and duality dissolve as one becomes Bhairava oneself.
SA_GSP30_sa_kSemarAja-bhairavAnukaraNast.history: Ascribed to Kṣemarāja, the principal disciple of Abhinavagupta; it belongs to the rich stotra literature of the Trika that turns the metaphysics of recognition into experiential, devotional practice. Its details are less fully studied than Kṣemarāja's major prose commentaries.

SA_GSP30_spandkau.title: Spandakārikā
SA_GSP30_spandkau.author: Vasugupta (or his pupil Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa)
SA_GSP30_spandkau.summary: The foundational text of the Spanda ("vibration/pulsation") school of Kashmir Shaivism, about fifty-one verses teaching that the entire cosmos is the pulsating self-expression of one dynamic consciousness, Shiva-Shakti. By attending to the spanda underlying every perception, emotion, and movement of mind, the practitioner recognizes and abides in the ever-active Self.
SA_GSP30_spandkau.history: Traditionally derived from the Śivasūtra revealed to Vasugupta (c. 800-850 CE); authorship of the Kārikā is debated between Vasugupta and his disciple Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa, who at least transmitted and may have composed it. It generated a major commentarial tradition (Kallaṭa's Vṛtti, Kṣemarāja's Spandanirṇaya and Spandasandoha, Rājānaka Rāma's Spandavivṛti).

SA_GSP30_timudghu.title: Timirodghāṭana
SA_GSP30_timudghu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_GSP30_timudghu.summary: An early anonymous Kaula Shaiva tantra, the "Dispelling of Darkness," notable for its account of śaktipāta (the descent of divine power/grace) and samāveśa (immersion or possession by the deity). It is one of the scriptural sources cited in the formation of nondual Trika/Kaula doctrine on initiation and absorption into Shiva-consciousness.
SA_GSP30_timudghu.history: A fragmentary and little-edited early tantra known mainly from citations and from modern scholarship (e.g. Alexis Sanderson) on the early Kaula and Vidyāpīṭha strata of Shaivism; it is valued for its early treatment of grace and immersion that fed into later Trika thought. Its full text, authorship, and precise date remain uncertain.

SA_GSP30_vamjanmu.title: Janmamaraṇavicāra
SA_GSP30_vamjanmu.author: Bhaṭṭa Vāmadeva
SA_GSP30_vamjanmu.summary: A short Śaiva treatise titled 'Reflection on Life and Death' that contemplatively analyzes the nature of birth, death and transmigration. It traces existence from the highest level of consciousness-awareness down through the manifest universe to the embodied, transmigrating individual who undergoes birth and death, offering a non-dual reframing of mortality.
SA_GSP30_vamjanmu.history: Little is known of its author Bhaṭṭa Vāmadeva, who is not known to have written anything else and who was very likely a disciple of the Śaiva exegete Yogarāja (himself in the lineage descending through Kṣemarāja from Abhinavagupta). The editio princeps was prepared from a single manuscript and published by Mukund Rām Shāstrī in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies in 1918; it is a unique work in Śaiva literature.

SA_GSP30_vamsbum_u.title: Svabodhodayamañjarī
SA_GSP30_vamsbum_u.author: Vāmanadatta (Hrasvanātha)
SA_GSP30_vamsbum_u.summary: A short Śaiva meditation manual of about 44 verses whose title means 'The Inflorescence of the Rising of One's Own Awareness'. It offers practical contemplative instruction on the spontaneous arising and recognition of innate consciousness within the non-dual Śaiva framework.
SA_GSP30_vamsbum_u.history: Its author Vāmanadatta, son of Harṣadatta, served as minister of war and peace under King Yaśaskara in mid-tenth-century Kashmir; he also composed the Saṃvitprakāśa. The Svabodhodayamañjarī is quoted by the commentator Utpala in the Spandapradīpikā, anchoring its date and showing its currency within the Spanda/Pratyabhijñā stream of Kashmir Shaivism.

SA_GSP30_vasspacu.title: Spandakārikā
SA_GSP30_vasspacu.author: Vasugupta or his pupil Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (disputed)
SA_GSP30_vasspacu.summary: A foundational set of verses (kārikās) of the Spanda branch of Kashmir Shaivism. It expounds spanda, the subtle 'vibration' or creative pulsation of consciousness (Śiva/Śakti) that underlies all manifestation and perception, teaching recognition of this dynamic ground as the path to liberation.
SA_GSP30_vasspacu.history: Authorship is debated: tradition credits Vasugupta (revealer of the Śiva Sūtras), while others ascribe the verses to his disciple Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa; Śākta-leaning circles favored Kallaṭa. Together with the Śiva Sūtras it represents the earliest non-dual Śaiva systematization opposed to dualist Śaiva Siddhānta, and it generated several major commentaries (Kallaṭa's Vṛtti, Kṣemarāja's Spandanirṇaya/Spandasandoha, Utpala's Spandapradīpikā).

SA_GSP30_vatnsupu.title: Vātūlanāthasūtra
SA_GSP30_vatnsupu.author: attributed to Vātūlanātha
SA_GSP30_vatnsupu.summary: A very short collection of 13 aphoristic sūtras belonging to the esoteric Krama tradition of Kashmir Shaivism, presented as oral teachings of the Yoginīs. The text uses distinctive terminology and centers its highest praxis on the 'Mahāsāhasa' (the Great Audacity), a sudden, overwhelming irruption of expanding, all-consuming pure consciousness.
SA_GSP30_vatnsupu.history: Ascribed to Vātūlanātha and transmitted with a Sanskrit commentary by his disciple Anantaśaktipāda, which alone preserves it. The lineage is traced to the early Krama founder figure Niṣkriyānandanātha. The sūtras with their commentary were published in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies in 1923.

SA_GSP30_vijnbhau.title: Vijñānabhairava
SA_GSP30_vijnbhau.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GSP30_vijnbhau.summary: A Bhairava-tantra of the Trika/Kaula stream, framed as a dialogue in which Bhairava answers the goddess Bhairavī. In some 160 verses it presents 112 dhāraṇās (centering/meditation techniques) for directly recognizing the nature of consciousness, making it a celebrated practical compendium of Śaiva yogic methods.
SA_GSP30_vijnbhau.history: The text is anonymous and traditionally treated as a portion/quintessence of the larger Rudrayāmala Tantra. It became a key practice scripture of Kashmir Shaivism and was commented on by Kṣemarāja (Uddyota) and Śivopādhyāya; it remains widely studied today.

SA_GSP31_bhgank_u.title: Gaṇakārikā 1
SA_GSP31_bhgank_u.author: kārikās attributed to Haradattācārya, with the Ratnaṭīkā of Bhāsarvajña
SA_GSP31_bhgank_u.genre: philosophical verse treatise with commentary (kārikā + ṭīkā)
SA_GSP31_bhgank_u.summary: A short doctrinal text (about eight verses) of the Lākulīśa-Pāśupata Śaiva school, transmitted with Bhāsarvajña's commentary the Ratnaṭīkā. It summarizes the Pāśupata program—the categories of attainment, the stages of practice (yoga), and the means by which the bound soul reaches union with Śiva—organizing the system into mnemonic 'groups' (gaṇa).
SA_GSP31_bhgank_u.history: The verses are commonly attributed to Haradattācārya, and the commentator Bhāsarvajña (the Nyāya logician) lived in the second half of the tenth century. Both the kārikās and the Ratnaṭīkā were recovered and published only in the twentieth century (Gaekwad's Oriental Series), and they are among the principal surviving sources for Pāśupata doctrine alongside the Pāśupatasūtra and Kauṇḍinya's bhāṣya.

SA_GSP31_iskar_u.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
SA_GSP31_iskar_u.author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
SA_GSP31_iskar_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-5th c. CE (Gupta era; some place it c. 350-450, others c. 447-490)
SA_GSP31_iskar_u.summary: The earliest fully extant systematic text of classical Sāṃkhya, in about 72-73 āryā verses (hence also 'Sāṃkhyasaptati', the seventy). It expounds the dualism of puruṣa (pure consciousness) and prakṛti (primordial matter), the three guṇas, the 25 tattvas, satkāryavāda (the pre-existent effect), the means of valid knowledge, and liberation through discriminative knowledge.
SA_GSP31_iskar_u.history: Attributed to Īśvarakṛṣṇa, a successor in the lineage of the legendary founder Kapila. Its terminus ad quem is fixed by Paramārtha's Chinese translation (the Suvarṇasaptati, c. 557-569 CE). It became the root text of the school, drawing the classic commentaries Gauḍapādabhāṣya, Yuktidīpikā, Māṭharavṛtti and Vācaspatimiśra's Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī. This entry is one of several recensions/editions of the same work in the collection.

SA_GSP31_iskgaudu.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
SA_GSP31_iskgaudu.author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa (kārikās), with the bhāṣya of Gauḍapāda
SA_GSP31_iskgaudu.genre: philosophical verse treatise with commentary (kārikā + bhāṣya)
SA_GSP31_iskgaudu.date_estimate: kārikās c. 4th-5th c. CE; Gauḍapāda's bhāṣya c. 6th-8th c. CE
SA_GSP31_iskgaudu.summary: The Sāṃkhyakārikā of Īśvarakṛṣṇa accompanied by the Gauḍapādabhāṣya, an early prose commentary that glosses the verses kārikā by kārikā. It elucidates the core Sāṃkhya doctrine of the duality of puruṣa and prakṛti, the evolution of the tattvas, and liberation through discriminative discernment.
SA_GSP31_iskgaudu.history: This is the recension of the kārikā transmitted with the bhāṣya ascribed to Gauḍapāda (whose identity and relation to the Vedānta Gauḍapāda is disputed). The bhāṣya is one of the standard classical commentaries on the kārikā and helped fix its received reading. The 'gaud' tag in the work_id indicates this Gauḍapāda recension.

SA_GSP31_isvskaru.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
SA_GSP31_isvskaru.author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
SA_GSP31_isvskaru.summary: A further recension/edition of Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, the root text of classical Sāṃkhya in about 72-73 āryā verses ('the seventy'). It sets out the dualism of puruṣa and prakṛti, the doctrine of the three guṇas, the unfolding of the 25 categories, satkāryavāda, and liberation by discriminative knowledge.
SA_GSP31_isvskaru.history: Identical in authorship and content to the other Sāṃkhyakārikā entries; this is a distinct witness/edition (work_id 'isv-skar') in the collection. The kārikā is the basis of the entire later Sāṃkhya commentarial tradition and is securely datable before Paramārtha's mid-6th-century Chinese translation.

SA_GSP31_kavsttvu.title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa
SA_GSP31_kavsttvu.author: Kavirājayati (Kavirājagiri)
SA_GSP31_kavsttvu.summary: A Sāṃkhya expository work titled 'Lamp on the Sāṃkhya Principles', presenting and clarifying the categories (tattvas) and doctrines of the Sāṃkhya system. It belongs to the body of postclassical Sāṃkhya literature that systematized and re-explained the school's metaphysics.
SA_GSP31_kavsttvu.history: Attributed to Kavirājayati (also given as Kavirājagiri), an author about whom little is securely known; the work survives within the broader corpus of late Sāṃkhya treatises and was printed in collections such as the Sāṃkhyasaṅgraha. Its precise date is not well established.

SA_GSP31_kestprau.title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpikā [Sāṃkhyārthatattvapradīpikā]
SA_GSP31_kestprau.author: Keśava (Bhaṭṭa Keśava)
SA_GSP31_kestprau.summary: A brief exposition of the Sāṃkhya system by Bhaṭṭa Keśava, titled 'Illuminator of the Sāṃkhya Principles' (also given as Sāṃkhyārthatattvapradīpikā). It sets forth and clarifies the categories and arguments of Sāṃkhya for instructional purposes.
SA_GSP31_kestprau.history: Authored by Bhaṭṭa Keśava, a figure known mainly through this work and catalogues of Sāṃkhya literature; precise dating is unsettled. It forms part of the late, post-kārikā layer of Sāṃkhya expository texts.

SA_GSP31_samkhsuu.title: Sāṃkhyasūtra
SA_GSP31_samkhsuu.author: traditionally ascribed to Kapila (actually an anonymous late author)
SA_GSP31_samkhsuu.summary: Also called the Sāṃkhyapravacanasūtra, this work in six chapters presents Sāṃkhya doctrine in aphorisms: the first three books expound the core metaphysics (puruṣa, prakṛti, the tattvas, bondage and liberation), the fourth gives illustrative parables, and the fifth and sixth refute rival schools and recapitulate the system.
SA_GSP31_samkhsuu.history: Traditionally attributed to the founder-sage Kapila, but the extant text is the work of an unknown later author of about the 14th-15th century, since it answers doctrines and uses materials of much later date. It renewed medieval interest in Sāṃkhya and drew the major commentaries of Aniruddha (Sāṃkhyasūtravṛtti) and Vijñānabhikṣu (Sāṃkhyapravacanabhāṣya).

SA_GSP31_sparibhu.title: Sāṃkhyaparibhāṣā
SA_GSP31_sparibhu.author: anonymous/uncertain
SA_GSP31_sparibhu.summary: A short Sāṃkhya 'definitional' work that elucidates the technical terminology and key concepts of the Sāṃkhya system. It functions as a concise reference clarifying Sāṃkhya categories and definitions.
SA_GSP31_sparibhu.history: A minor late Sāṃkhya text transmitted within the Sāṃkhyasaṅgraha compilation (printed 1918); its authorship and exact date are not securely established. It belongs to the body of postclassical Sāṃkhya manuals.

SA_GSP31_stk.title: Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī
SA_GSP31_stk.author: Vācaspatimiśra
SA_GSP31_stk.tradition: Hindu; Sāṃkhya (and Vācaspati's broad polymath exegesis)
SA_GSP31_stk.summary: The most celebrated commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, explaining each verse word by word with critical and comparative notes. It treats the Sāṃkhya means of valid knowledge (perception, inference, testimony), satkāryavāda, the doctrine of the three guṇas, the plurality of puruṣas, and related metaphysics.
SA_GSP31_stk.history: Composed by Vācaspatimiśra, the great polymath who wrote authoritative commentaries across Nyāya, Vedānta, Mīmāṃsā, Yoga and Sāṃkhya. The Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī became the standard interpretive lens for the kārikā and itself attracted numerous sub-commentaries (e.g., Tattvārṇava, Tattvacandra, Sāṃkhyatattvavibhākara).

SA_GSP31_tatsuvru.title: Tattvasamāsasūtravṛtti [Kramadīpikā]
SA_GSP31_tatsuvru.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GSP31_tatsuvru.summary: A prose commentary (vṛtti), known from its closing verse as the Kramadīpikā, on the Tattvasamāsa—a terse abstract of Sāṃkhya philosophy in some 23-25 sūtras attributed to Kapila. It expounds the brief sūtras into a connected exposition of the Sāṃkhya categories and stages of knowledge.
SA_GSP31_tatsuvru.history: An anonymous postclassical commentary, conventionally dated around the 14th century along with the Tattvasamāsa it explains. It is one of several surviving commentaries on the Tattvasamāsa and is preserved in Sāṃkhya collections and digitized in GRETIL.

SA_GSP31_tattvmiu.title: Tattvamīmāṃsā
SA_GSP31_tattvmiu.author: uncertain (a Sāṃkhya Tattvamīmāṃsā; not the modern work of Ganapati Muni)
SA_GSP31_tattvmiu.genre: philosophical treatise / commentary on the Tattvasamāsa
SA_GSP31_tattvmiu.summary: A Sāṃkhya work titled 'Investigation of the Principles', associated with the Tattvasamāsa cluster of Sāṃkhya literature and expounding the categories (tattvas) of the system. It is one of the lesser-known expositions transmitted within the late Sāṃkhya corpus.
SA_GSP31_tattvmiu.history: Listed as a Sāṃkhya text in Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum; note that a wholly separate modern Tattvamīmāṃsā (245 ślokas) was composed by Vāsiṣṭha Kāvyakaṇṭha Ganapati Muni (1878-1936), which is not this older Sāṃkhya entry. Authorship and date of the Sāṃkhya Tattvamīmāṃsā are not securely established.

SA_GSP31_tsc_bhau.title: Tattvayāthārthyadīpana
SA_GSP31_tsc_bhau.author: Bhāvāgaṇeśa Dīkṣita (Gaṇeśa Dīkṣita)
SA_GSP31_tsc_bhau.summary: A commentary ('Illumination of the True Nature of the Principles') on the Tattvasamāsa, the brief Sāṃkhya abstract in 23-25 sūtras. It expounds the Sāṃkhya tattvas and doctrine, offering its own division of the sūtra text that differs from other commentaries.
SA_GSP31_tsc_bhau.history: Written by Bhāvāgaṇeśa Dīkṣita, son of Bhāvā Viśvanātha Dīkṣita and associated with the school of Vijñānabhikṣu (c. 16th century). It is one of the principal extant commentaries on the Tattvasamāsa and is preserved in Sāṃkhya collections and GRETIL.

SA_GSP31_tsc_simu.title: Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana
SA_GSP31_tsc_simu.author: Kṣemānanda (variously read as Ṣimānanda/Kṣemendra)
SA_GSP31_tsc_simu.summary: A commentary ('Discernment of the Sāṃkhya Principles') on the Tattvasamāsa, the short Sāṃkhya abstract in 23-25 sūtras. It analyzes and expounds the Sāṃkhya categories enumerated in the sūtras.
SA_GSP31_tsc_simu.history: Attributed to Kṣemānanda; the author's name is read variously in catalogues (Ṣimānanda, Kṣemendra), reflecting manuscript uncertainty. It is one of the several postclassical commentaries on the Tattvasamāsa within the Sāṃkhya corpus; precise dating is unsettled.

SA_GSP31_tsc_vivu.title: Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa
SA_GSP31_tsc_vivu.author: anonymous (ascribed in some sources to a Kṛṣṇa)
SA_GSP31_tsc_vivu.summary: Despite its title 'Exposition of the Sāṃkhya Sūtras', this is a commentary on the Tattvasamāsa, the brief Sāṃkhya abstract in 23-25 sūtras. It glosses and expands the terse sūtras into a connected account of Sāṃkhya doctrine.
SA_GSP31_tsc_vivu.history: Transmitted anonymously (sometimes associated with an author named Kṛṣṇa) and counted among the postclassical commentaries on the Tattvasamāsa. It is preserved in Sāṃkhya collections and digitized in GRETIL; its date and authorship are not securely established.

SA_GSP31_ttssarvu.title: Sarvopakāriṇī
SA_GSP31_ttssarvu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GSP31_ttssarvu.summary: A commentary titled 'The All-Helpful (gloss)' on the Tattvasamāsa, the short Sāṃkhya abstract in 23-25 sūtras. It serves as an explanatory aid expounding the Sāṃkhya categories and doctrines compressed in the sūtras.
SA_GSP31_ttssarvu.history: An anonymous postclassical commentary forming part of the cluster of Tattvasamāsa commentaries (alongside the Kramadīpikā, Tattvayāthārthyadīpana, Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana and Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa). Preserved in Sāṃkhya collections; its date and author are not securely established.

SA_GSP31_tttvsamu.title: Tattvasamāsa
SA_GSP31_tttvsamu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Kapila)
SA_GSP31_tttvsamu.date_estimate: uncertain; the surviving text is generally dated late (c. 14th-16th c. CE), though traditionally ascribed to the founder Kapila
SA_GSP31_tttvsamu.summary: A very brief aphoristic outline (about 23-25 short sūtras/topic-headings) summarizing the categories and principles of classical Sāṃkhya philosophy — the evolutes of prakṛti, the puruṣa, the guṇas, bondage and liberation. It functions as a memorizable skeleton of Sāṃkhya doctrine rather than an argued treatise.
SA_GSP31_tttvsamu.history: Traditionally attributed to the legendary sage Kapila and revered within Sāṃkhya as an ancient summary, but Western scholarship generally regards the extant text as relatively late (often before c. 1600 CE). It became the basis for several later commentaries (e.g. by Kṣemendra, Bhāvāgaṇeśa, Narendra), which is largely why it survived.

SA_GSP31_vijsamsu.title: Sāṅkhyasāra
SA_GSP31_vijsamsu.author: Vijñānabhikṣu
SA_GSP31_vijsamsu.date_estimate: c. 16th c. CE (latter half of the 1500s)
SA_GSP31_vijsamsu.summary: A systematic compendium of Sāṃkhya doctrine by Vijñānabhikṣu, summarizing the metaphysics of prakṛti and puruṣa, the twenty-five tattvas, the guṇas, and the path to liberation. Consistent with his broader project, it reads Sāṃkhya in a theistic and Vedānta-friendly light rather than as strictly atheistic dualism.
SA_GSP31_vijsamsu.history: Vijñānabhikṣu (fl. mid-to-late 16th c.) was a major late synthesizer who sought to harmonize Sāṃkhya, Yoga and Vedānta; he also wrote the Sāṃkhyapravacanabhāṣya and the Yogavārttika. The Sāṅkhyasāra is one of his independent expositions of Sāṃkhya and is important for understanding the late, devotionally inflected revival of the school.

SA_GSP31_yuktdipu.title: Yuktidīpikā
SA_GSP31_yuktdipu.author: anonymous (sometimes conjecturally ascribed to a 'Rājan/Rāja')
SA_GSP31_yuktdipu.summary: The most detailed and philosophically rich surviving commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā. It expounds the kārikās at length, preserves polemics against Buddhist, Nyāya and other opponents, and uniquely transmits information about earlier Sāṃkhya teachers and lost doctrines (e.g. discussions tied to the Ṣaṣṭitantra).
SA_GSP31_yuktdipu.history: Its author is unknown; the work was rediscovered and edited in the 20th century, becoming a key source for reconstructing classical Sāṃkhya beyond the terse Kārikā. The standard critical edition is by Wezler and Motegi (1998). Because of its rich citations it is invaluable for dating and contextualizing rival schools.

SA_GSP32_paddhs_u.title: Padārthadharmasaṃgraha
SA_GSP32_paddhs_u.author: Praśastapāda
SA_GSP32_paddhs_u.genre: philosophical treatise (often termed bhasya, the Praśastapādabhāṣya)
SA_GSP32_paddhs_u.summary: A systematic exposition of Vaiśeṣika categories (padārthas) — substance, quality, motion, universal, particularity, inherence — organized topically rather than as a line-by-line gloss on the sūtras. It develops Vaiśeṣika natural philosophy, atomism, and the theory of qualities far beyond the original aphorisms and effectively reshaped the school.
SA_GSP32_paddhs_u.history: Though traditionally called a 'bhāṣya' on Kaṇāda's Vaiśeṣikasūtra, it is really an independent compendium that became the standard reference for later Vaiśeṣika. It attracted major commentaries, notably Vyomaśiva's Vyomavatī, Śrīdhara's Nyāyakandalī, and Udayana's Kiraṇāvalī.

SA_GSP32_vaisessu.title: Vaiśeṣikasūtra
SA_GSP32_vaisessu.author: Kaṇāda (Kaśyapa/Ulūka)
SA_GSP32_vaisessu.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (composite; foundational layer disputed)
SA_GSP32_vaisessu.summary: The foundational aphoristic text of the Vaiśeṣika school, setting out a pluralistic realist ontology built on categories (padārtha) and an atomic theory of matter. It treats substances, qualities, motion, universals, particularity, and inherence, and grounds dharma and liberation in correct knowledge of reality.
SA_GSP32_vaisessu.history: Attributed to the sage Kaṇāda, the sūtras are early but the text as transmitted is layered and its absolute dating is debated (the project hint of c. 250-300 CE reflects one recension; many scholars place the core earlier). It survives in multiple recensions and is best known through Candrānanda's vṛtti and Praśastapāda's compendium.

SA_GSP32_vaissu1u.title: Vaiśeṣikasūtra
SA_GSP32_vaissu1u.author: Kaṇāda (Kaśyapa/Ulūka)
SA_GSP32_vaissu1u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (composite; disputed). This entry appears to be a second recension/version of the Vaiśeṣikasūtra in the corpus.
SA_GSP32_vaissu1u.summary: The foundational aphoristic text of Vaiśeṣika, presenting a realist categorial ontology (padārthas) and atomism, and deriving knowledge of dharma and liberation from correct understanding of reality. This record is a distinct version (recension) of the same root text as the preceding Vaiśeṣikasūtra entry.
SA_GSP32_vaissu1u.history: The Vaiśeṣikasūtra survives in more than one recension (e.g. the version embedded in Candrānanda's vṛtti versus the older 'vulgate' associated with Śaṅkaramiśra's Upaskāra), which is likely why the corpus carries multiple Vaiśeṣikasūtra files. Attribution to Kaṇāda is traditional; the text is layered.

SA_GSP32_vaissutika.title: Vaiśeṣikasūtraṭīkā
SA_GSP32_vaissutika.author: Candrānanda
SA_GSP32_vaissutika.date_estimate: c. 7th-10th c. CE (commentary; date uncertain, before c. 10th c.)
SA_GSP32_vaissutika.summary: Candrānanda's running commentary (vṛtti, here titled ṭīkā) on Kaṇāda's Vaiśeṣikasūtra. It glosses the aphorisms and preserves what is regarded as the oldest available recension of the sūtra text, making it crucial for reconstructing the earliest form of the Vaiśeṣikasūtra.
SA_GSP32_vaissutika.history: The commentary was first critically edited (with the sūtras) by Muni Jambūvijaya at the University of Baroda (1961). Candrānanda's date is not firmly fixed but he is clearly a relatively early commentator; his version differs notably from the later Upaskāra vulgate.

SA_GSP32_vvmanmhu.title: Mānamanohara
SA_GSP32_vvmanmhu.author: Vādivāgīśvara
SA_GSP32_vvmanmhu.genre: philosophical treatise on pramāṇas (theory of knowledge)
SA_GSP32_vvmanmhu.summary: A treatise on the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇas) in the Nyāya / Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika tradition, attributed to Vādivāgīśvara. As its title ('Delight of the Mind regarding pramāṇa') suggests, it systematically expounds the instruments of cognition such as perception and inference.
SA_GSP32_vvmanmhu.history: The text and its author Vādivāgīśvara are comparatively obscure and not widely documented in mainstream surveys; the work is preserved in manuscript/catalogue traditions of Nyāya epistemological literature. Its precise date and circumstances of composition are not securely established.

SA_GSP33_vedanta-desika.title: Dramidopaniṣad Tātparyaratnāvalī
SA_GSP33_vedanta-desika.author: Vedānta Deśika (Veṅkaṭanātha)
SA_GSP33_vedanta-desika.genre: stotra/expository verse work (commentarial summary in Sanskrit verse)
SA_GSP33_vedanta-desika.date_estimate: late 13th-early 14th c. CE (Vedānta Deśika c. 1268-1369 CE)
SA_GSP33_vedanta-desika.summary: A Sanskrit verse work by Vedānta Deśika that distills the essence of Nammāḻvār's Tamil Tiruvāymoḻi (revered as the 'Drāmiḍa Upaniṣad'/Drāviḍa Veda). It presents, decade by decade, the central import (tātparya) of the Tiruvāymoḻi, treating the Tamil hymn as scripturally equivalent to the Upaniṣads and articulating Śrīvaiṣṇava devotional theology.
SA_GSP33_vedanta-desika.history: Vedānta Deśika was a towering Śrīvaiṣṇava ācārya who composed in both Sanskrit and Tamil and championed the equal authority of the Tamil Āḻvār hymns ('Ubhaya Vedānta'). The work is paired in tradition with his Drāmiḍopaniṣadsāra; a Sanskrit-English edition was published by the Vedanta Desika Research Society (Madras, 1974).

SA_GSP33_bbdip11u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1, Pāda 1
SA_GSP33_bbdip11u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (Vyāsa, traditional)
