SA_GSP33_bbdip11u.genre: sutra (section of the foundational Vedānta aphorisms)
SA_GSP33_bbdip11u.date_estimate: c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (composite; section of a text usually placed in the early centuries CE)
SA_GSP33_bbdip11u.summary: The first quarter (pāda) of the first chapter of the Brahmasūtra. Known as the Samanvaya-adhyāya opening, it establishes Brahman as the source of all and argues that the various Upaniṣadic passages consistently (samanvaya) refer to Brahman, beginning with the famous 'athāto brahmajijñāsā' (now therefore the inquiry into Brahman).
SA_GSP33_bbdip11u.history: This is a subdivision of Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra (Vedāntasūtra), the third pillar of the prasthānatrayī. The corpus segments the work by adhyāya and pāda; pāda 1.1 is where each major commentator (Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, etc.) lays out core hermeneutic principles.

SA_GSP33_bbdip12u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1, Pāda 2
SA_GSP33_bbdip12u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (Vyāsa, traditional)
SA_GSP33_bbdip12u.genre: sutra (section of the foundational Vedānta aphorisms)
SA_GSP33_bbdip12u.date_estimate: c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (composite; section of a text usually placed in the early centuries CE)
SA_GSP33_bbdip12u.summary: The second pāda of Brahmasūtra chapter 1, continuing the samanvaya project of showing that ambiguous Upaniṣadic descriptions (of the inner controller, the all-pervading, etc.) are to be understood as referring to Brahman. It interprets specific scriptural texts as having Brahman as their proper subject.
SA_GSP33_bbdip12u.history: A subdivision of Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra, segmented here by adhyāya/pāda. As with all the pādas, its meaning is heavily shaped by the divergent commentaries of the Vedānta schools.

SA_GSP33_bbdip13u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1, Pāda 3
SA_GSP33_bbdip13u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (Vyāsa, traditional)
SA_GSP33_bbdip13u.genre: sutra (section of the foundational Vedānta aphorisms)
SA_GSP33_bbdip13u.date_estimate: c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (composite; section of a text usually placed in the early centuries CE)
SA_GSP33_bbdip13u.summary: The third pāda of Brahmasūtra chapter 1, again pursuing samanvaya by identifying Brahman as the referent of further Upaniṣadic passages (e.g. the 'abode of heaven and earth', the akṣara, the small space within the heart). It also takes up questions about who is qualified to study Brahman-knowledge.
SA_GSP33_bbdip13u.history: A subdivision of Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra, transmitted and commented upon within the prasthānatrayī framework by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva and others.

SA_GSP33_bhbhg_cu.title: Bhagavadāśayānusaraṇabhāṣya
SA_GSP33_bhbhg_cu.author: Bhāskara (Bhedābheda Vedānta)
SA_GSP33_bhbhg_cu.summary: Bhāskara's commentary on the Bhagavadgītā, whose title ('exposition following the intention of the Blessed Lord') signals an attempt to draw out Kṛṣṇa's own intended meaning. Reading the Gītā through his Bhedābheda lens, Bhāskara emphasizes that the self relates to Brahman as simultaneously different and non-different, and advocates jñāna-karma-samuccaya (the combination of knowledge and action).
SA_GSP33_bhbhg_cu.history: Bhāskara, a probable younger contemporary of Śaṅkara, was a leading exponent of Bhedābheda and a sharp critic of Advaita's doctrine of māyā. He also wrote a commentary on the Brahmasūtra; his Gītā commentary is an important early non-Advaita reading of the text.

SA_GSP33_bhgmbh.title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
SA_GSP33_bhgmbh.author: (unspecified in record; ambiguous — a Bhagavadgītā commentary, possibly Madhva's Gītābhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_bhgmbh.date_estimate: uncertain (depends on author); if Madhva, c. 13th c. CE
SA_GSP33_bhgmbh.summary: A Sanskrit commentary (bhāṣya) on the Bhagavadgītā. The record gives no author, so the specific commentator is uncertain; given the work_id pattern and the 'm' marker it may be Madhva's Gītābhāṣya, a Dvaita reading that interprets the Gītā as teaching devotion to Viṣṇu and the eternal distinction between the soul and God.
SA_GSP33_bhgmbh.history: Numerous Vedānta ācāryas wrote Gītā commentaries titled Bhagavadgītābhāṣya; without an explicit author attribution the exact one here cannot be fixed with certainty. If Madhvācārya's, it is a foundational Dvaita text accompanied by his Gītātātparya.

SA_GSP33_bhgrbh.title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
SA_GSP33_bhgrbh.author: Rāmānuja
SA_GSP33_bhgrbh.date_estimate: c. late 11th-early 12th c. CE (Rāmānuja, trad. 1017-1137 CE)
SA_GSP33_bhgrbh.summary: Rāmānuja's Gītābhāṣya, a Viśiṣṭādvaita interpretation of the Bhagavadgītā. It reads the Gītā as culminating in loving surrender (bhakti/prapatti) to the personal Lord Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa, treats individual souls and matter as the qualified 'body' of Brahman, and harmonizes karma, jñāna and bhakti yoga with bhakti as supreme.
SA_GSP33_bhgrbh.history: Rāmānuja, the principal systematizer of Viśiṣṭādvaita, wrote this alongside his Śrībhāṣya on the Brahmasūtra and the Vedārthasaṃgraha. The Gītābhāṣya became a defining devotional and philosophical statement for the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition.

SA_GSP33_bhgsbh.title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
SA_GSP33_bhgsbh.author: Śaṃkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya)
SA_GSP33_bhgsbh.date_estimate: c. early 8th c. CE (Śaṅkara, commonly c. 700-750 CE)
SA_GSP33_bhgsbh.summary: Śaṅkara's commentary on the Bhagavadgītā, the earliest extant complete Gītā bhāṣya. It reads the Gītā as teaching that liberation comes through knowledge of the non-dual self (jñāna) — with action and devotion as preparatory — and that the highest goal is realization of the identity of ātman and Brahman.
SA_GSP33_bhgsbh.history: Part of Śaṅkara's prasthānatrayī commentaries (Upaniṣads, Brahmasūtra, Gītā), it established the Gītā firmly within the Vedānta canon and set the terms for all later debate. The project's date hint (800-1099) is too late; Śaṅkara is normally placed c. 700-750 CE.

SA_GSP33_brahms2u.title: Brahmasūtra
SA_GSP33_brahms2u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (Vyāsa, traditional)
SA_GSP33_brahms2u.date_estimate: c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (composite; often c. 1st-2nd c. CE in present form)
SA_GSP33_brahms2u.summary: The Brahmasūtra (Vedāntasūtra) systematizes the teachings of the Upaniṣads into 555 terse aphorisms across four chapters, arguing that Brahman is the origin, sustenance and dissolution of the world and the goal of liberation. It coordinates conflicting Upaniṣadic statements, refutes rival systems (Sāṃkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Buddhism, Jainism), and treats meditation and the means to liberation. This record is one recension/copy of the root text.
SA_GSP33_brahms2u.history: Attributed to Bādarāyaṇa, it is one of the prasthānatrayī and the single most commented-upon Vedānta text; its extreme brevity made commentary indispensable, spawning the divergent systems of Śaṅkara, Bhāskara, Rāmānuja, Madhva, Nimbārka, Vallabha and others.

SA_GSP33_brahmsuu.title: Brahmasūtra
SA_GSP33_brahmsuu.author: Bādarāyaṇa (Vyāsa, traditional)
SA_GSP33_brahmsuu.date_estimate: c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (composite; often c. 1st-2nd c. CE in present form)
SA_GSP33_brahmsuu.summary: The foundational aphoristic text of Vedānta, organizing Upaniṣadic doctrine into four adhyāyas on the coordination of scripture (samanvaya), refutation of opposing views (avirodha), the means to realization (sādhana), and the fruit/liberation (phala). This entry is another recension/copy of the same root Brahmasūtra in the corpus.
SA_GSP33_brahmsuu.history: Bādarāyaṇa's sūtras are deliberately cryptic, presupposing oral exposition; the multiple copies in this corpus reflect different recensions or readings inherited from the various commentarial schools. It is the third pillar of the prasthānatrayī.

SA_GSP33_brasi.title: Brahmasiddhi
SA_GSP33_brasi.author: Maṇḍana Miśra
SA_GSP33_brasi.date_estimate: c. 8th c. CE (Maṇḍana Miśra, roughly contemporary with Śaṅkara)
SA_GSP33_brasi.summary: An independent Advaita treatise in four chapters (Brahmakāṇḍa, Tarkakāṇḍa, Niyogakāṇḍa, Siddhikāṇḍa) establishing the reality of non-dual Brahman. It develops a distinctive Advaita influenced by Maṇḍana's Mīmāṃsā background — including views on avidyā, the role of meditative knowledge, and the relation of injunction (niyoga) to liberating knowledge — that differ in emphasis from Śaṅkara's.
SA_GSP33_brasi.history: Maṇḍana Miśra was a major early Advaitin, often associated in later legend with Sureśvara; his Brahmasiddhi represents an alternative strand of early Advaita and was commented upon by Vācaspati Miśra (Brahmatattvasamīkṣā) and others. It is important for tracing the internal diversity of early Advaita.

SA_GSP33_brasunimu.title: Brahmasūtra (Nimbārka) — Vedāntapārijātasaurabha
SA_GSP33_brasunimu.author: Nimbārka (with Śrīnivāsa's Vedāntakaustubha)
SA_GSP33_brasunimu.date_estimate: disputed; Nimbārka variously placed c. 7th c. CE or c. 12th-13th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brasunimu.summary: Nimbārka's terse commentary on the Brahmasūtra, the Vedāntapārijātasaurabha, foundational for his Dvaitādvaita ('dualistic non-dualism' / svābhāvika-bhedābheda) school, which holds that soul and world are simultaneously and naturally both different from and non-different from Brahman (identified with Kṛṣṇa-Rādhā). His disciple Śrīnivāsa expanded it in the Vedāntakaustubha.
SA_GSP33_brasunimu.history: Nimbārka's dating is much disputed — traditional and some scholarly accounts place him as early as the 7th c., while others put him in the 12th-13th c.; the question is unresolved. The Pārijātasaurabha and Kaustubha are the doctrinal core of the Nimbārka Sampradāya, edited and translated into English by Roma Bose.

SA_GSP33_brasurau.title: Brahmasūtra (Rāmānuja)
SA_GSP33_brasurau.author: Rāmānuja
SA_GSP33_brasurau.summary: This is Rāmānuja's Śrībhāṣya, his magisterial commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra. It expounds Viśiṣṭādvaita ('qualified non-dualism'), arguing that Brahman (identified with Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa) is the supreme person whose 'body' is composed of the world of sentient souls and insentient matter, which are real and distinct yet wholly dependent on him. It systematically rebuts Śaṅkara's Advaita interpretation of māyā and the doctrine of an attributeless Brahman.
SA_GSP33_brasurau.history: The Śrībhāṣya is Rāmānuja's most important work and the foundational systematic text of the Śrīvaiṣṇava theological tradition, drawing on the earlier Bodhāyana-vṛtti tradition. It became the authoritative Vedānta reading for the Viśiṣṭādvaita school and generated an extensive sub-commentarial literature (e.g. Sudarśanasūri's Śrutaprakāśikā).

SA_GSP33_brsgor1u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brsgor1u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsgor1u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brsgor1u.summary: This is the first adhyāya (chapter) of the Brahmasūtra accompanied by Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā, a sub-commentary (vyākhyā) on Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya. Adhyāya 1 (samanvaya) establishes that all Upaniṣadic statements consistently refer to Brahman as the sole cause of the world. Govindānanda's gloss clarifies the wording and logic of Śaṅkara's Advaitic exposition.
SA_GSP33_brsgor1u.history: The Ratnaprabhā (Bhāṣyaratnaprabhā) by Govindānanda is one of the standard Advaita glosses on Śaṅkara's commentary, regularly printed alongside Vācaspati's Bhāmatī and Ānandagiri's Nyāyanirṇaya in the Nirṇaya-Sāgara/Bakre editions. This GRETIL e-text (legacy file brsgor1u) was prepared by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brsgor2u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brsgor2u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsgor2u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brsgor2u.summary: The second adhyāya (avirodha) of the Brahmasūtra with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā gloss on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. This chapter answers objections to the Vedānta doctrine, refuting rival systems (Sāṃkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Buddhist, Jain schools) and resolving apparent internal contradictions in the Upaniṣadic teaching about Brahman as cause.
SA_GSP33_brsgor2u.history: Part of the standard Advaita commentarial complex on the Brahmasūtra; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā is a widely used clarificatory sub-commentary on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. GRETIL legacy file brsgor2u, prepared by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brsgor3u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brsgor3u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsgor3u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brsgor3u.summary: The third adhyāya (sādhana) of the Brahmasūtra with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. This chapter treats the means to liberation: the nature of transmigration, meditation on Brahman, the qualifications of the seeker, and the disciplines (sādhanas) that lead to knowledge of Brahman.
SA_GSP33_brsgor3u.history: Part of the standard Advaita commentarial complex on the Brahmasūtra. GRETIL legacy file brsgor3u, prepared by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brsgor4u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brsgor4u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsgor4u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brsgor4u.summary: The fourth and final adhyāya (phala) of the Brahmasūtra with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. This chapter describes the fruit (phala) of Brahman-knowledge: the dawning of liberation, the fate of the knower after death, the path of the gods (devayāna), and final release (mokṣa / videhamukti).
SA_GSP33_brsgor4u.history: Part of the standard Advaita commentarial complex on the Brahmasūtra. GRETIL legacy file brsgor4u, prepared by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brssbh1u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh1u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh1u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE
SA_GSP33_brssbh1u.summary: The first adhyāya (samanvaya) of the Brahmasūtra together with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya, the bare bhāṣya without sub-commentary. The chapter demonstrates that all Upaniṣadic texts harmoniously (samanvaya) teach Brahman as the one cause of the world; Śaṅkara reads this through his non-dualist (Advaita) lens, including the famous adhyāsa-bhāṣya on superimposition that opens the commentary.
SA_GSP33_brssbh1u.history: Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya is the foundational text of the Advaita Vedānta school and the most influential of all Brahmasūtra commentaries, prompting the rival readings of Bhāskara, Rāmānuja, Madhva and others. This GRETIL e-text (legacy file brssbh1u) contains Śaṅkara's bhāṣya alone, converted by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brssbh2u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh2u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh2u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE
SA_GSP33_brssbh2u.summary: The second adhyāya (avirodha) of the Brahmasūtra with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The chapter removes apparent contradictions, refuting the cosmologies of Sāṃkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Buddhist and other rival schools and defending the consistency of the Vedāntic doctrine of Brahman as material and efficient cause.
SA_GSP33_brssbh2u.history: The bare Śaṅkara bhāṣya text; foundational for the Advaita school. GRETIL legacy file brssbh2u, converted by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brssbh3u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh3u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh3u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE
SA_GSP33_brssbh3u.summary: The third adhyāya (sādhana) of the Brahmasūtra with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The chapter treats the means to liberating knowledge: transmigration of the soul, the objects and modes of meditation (vidyās/upāsanās), the qualifications of the aspirant, and the disciplines conducive to realizing Brahman.
SA_GSP33_brssbh3u.history: The bare Śaṅkara bhāṣya text. GRETIL legacy file brssbh3u, converted by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brssbh4u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh4u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brssbh4u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE
SA_GSP33_brssbh4u.summary: The fourth and final adhyāya (phala) of the Brahmasūtra with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The chapter expounds the fruit of Brahman-knowledge: the destruction of karma, the experience of liberation while living (jīvanmukti), the soul's departure by the path of the gods, and final non-return (mokṣa).
SA_GSP33_brssbh4u.history: The bare Śaṅkara bhāṣya text. GRETIL legacy file brssbh4u, converted by the Sansknet project.

SA_GSP33_brssgo1u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo1u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo1u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brssgo1u.summary: Adhyāya 1 (samanvaya) of the Brahmasūtra in the combined form of Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya together with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā gloss — a parallel GRETIL e-text version of the Advaita Brahmasūtra commentarial complex. The chapter shows that all Upaniṣadic statements coherently denote Brahman as the world's sole cause.
SA_GSP33_brssgo1u.history: This GRETIL e-text (legacy file brssgo1u) is a distinct digital recension pairing Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā within the same standard Advaita commentarial tradition. The 'ssgo' code denotes Śaṅkara-bhāṣya plus Govindānanda.

SA_GSP33_brssgo2u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo2u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo2u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brssgo2u.summary: Adhyāya 2 (avirodha) of the Brahmasūtra with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya and Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā. The chapter rebuts rival philosophical systems and resolves apparent contradictions, defending the Vedāntic account of Brahman as the cause of the world.
SA_GSP33_brssgo2u.history: GRETIL e-text (legacy file brssgo2u), a digital recension pairing Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā in the Advaita commentarial tradition.

SA_GSP33_brssgo3u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo3u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo3u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brssgo3u.summary: Adhyāya 3 (sādhana) of the Brahmasūtra with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya and Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā. The chapter treats the means to liberation — transmigration, the meditations (vidyās) on Brahman, and the qualifications and disciplines of the seeker of knowledge.
SA_GSP33_brssgo3u.history: GRETIL e-text (legacy file brssgo3u), a digital recension pairing Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā in the Advaita commentarial tradition.

SA_GSP33_brssgo4u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo4u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya); Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā)
SA_GSP33_brssgo4u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā c. 16th c. CE
SA_GSP33_brssgo4u.summary: Adhyāya 4 (phala) of the Brahmasūtra with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya and Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā. The chapter describes the fruit of Brahman-knowledge: liberation, the soul's post-mortem path, and final release (mokṣa).
SA_GSP33_brssgo4u.history: GRETIL e-text (legacy file brssgo4u), a digital recension pairing Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā in the Advaita commentarial tradition.

SA_GSP33_brsvbh1u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh1u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh1u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Vācaspati's Bhāmatī c. 950-980 CE
SA_GSP33_brsvbh1u.summary: Adhyāya 1 (samanvaya) of the Brahmasūtra with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī, the celebrated sub-commentary on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The chapter establishes the Upaniṣadic harmony in teaching Brahman as cause; the Bhāmatī develops Śaṅkara's Advaita with its distinctive positions (e.g. avidyā located in the individual jīva).
SA_GSP33_brsvbh1u.history: The Bhāmatī, named after Vācaspati Miśra's wife, founded the 'Bhāmatī' sub-school of Advaita Vedānta and stands alongside the Vivaraṇa lineage as a principal stream of post-Śaṅkara Advaita. It spawned further glosses (Amalānanda's Kalpataru, Appayya Dīkṣita's Parimala). GRETIL legacy file brsvbh1u.

SA_GSP33_brsvbh2u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh2u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh2u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Vācaspati's Bhāmatī c. 950-980 CE
SA_GSP33_brsvbh2u.summary: Adhyāya 2 (avirodha) of the Brahmasūtra with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The chapter refutes rival systems and resolves contradictions in the doctrine of Brahman; the Bhāmatī supplies fine logical and exegetical clarification in line with its sub-school's positions.
SA_GSP33_brsvbh2u.history: Part of the Bhāmatī sub-school's foundational commentary on Śaṅkara. GRETIL legacy file brsvbh2u.

SA_GSP33_brsvbh3u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh3u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh3u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Vācaspati's Bhāmatī c. 950-980 CE
SA_GSP33_brsvbh3u.summary: Adhyāya 3 (sādhana) of the Brahmasūtra with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The chapter treats the means to liberation — transmigration, the meditations on Brahman, and the seeker's qualifications and disciplines — glossed in the Bhāmatī.
SA_GSP33_brsvbh3u.history: Part of the Bhāmatī sub-school's foundational commentary on Śaṅkara. GRETIL legacy file brsvbh3u.

SA_GSP33_brsvbh4u.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh4u.author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
SA_GSP33_brsvbh4u.date_estimate: Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya c. 700 CE; Vācaspati's Bhāmatī c. 950-980 CE
SA_GSP33_brsvbh4u.summary: Adhyāya 4 (phala) of the Brahmasūtra with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The chapter expounds the fruit of Brahman-knowledge — liberation, the post-mortem path of the knower, and final release (mokṣa) — with the Bhāmatī's clarifications.
SA_GSP33_brsvbh4u.history: Part of the Bhāmatī sub-school's foundational commentary on Śaṅkara. GRETIL legacy file brsvbh4u.

SA_GSP33_gaukar.title: Āgamaśāstra (Gauḍapādakārikā / Gauḍapādīyakārikā / Māṇḍūkyakārikā)
SA_GSP33_gaukar.author: Gauḍapāda
SA_GSP33_gaukar.tradition: Hindu, Vedānta (Advaita); shows strong Buddhist (Mahāyāna) influence
SA_GSP33_gaukar.date_estimate: c. 6th c. CE (c. 500-600 CE; some place it as early as 5th c.)
SA_GSP33_gaukar.summary: The Āgamaśāstra, better known as the Gauḍapādakārikā or Māṇḍūkyakārikā, is a verse treatise in four chapters (prakaraṇas) traditionally attached to the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad. It expounds an uncompromising non-dualism: the phenomenal world is unreal like a dream, the self (Ātman) alone is real, and ultimate truth is 'non-origination' (ajātivāda). The fourth chapter (Alātaśānti) deploys Mahāyāna Buddhist terminology and dialectic.
SA_GSP33_gaukar.history: It is the earliest surviving systematic text of Advaita Vedānta; Gauḍapāda is regarded as the paramaguru (teacher's teacher) of Śaṅkara, who revered him. Scholars debate the unity of the four chapters and the extent and direction of its engagement with Yogācāra and Madhyamaka Buddhism. Śaṅkara (or a follower) wrote a bhāṣya on it.

SA_GSP33_m_mbhtnu.title: Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇaya
SA_GSP33_m_mbhtnu.author: Madhva (Ānandatīrtha)
SA_GSP33_m_mbhtnu.summary: A wide-ranging work in 32 chapters in which Madhva determines the true purport (tātparya) of the Mahābhārata — together with the Rāmāyaṇa, the birth of Vyāsa, and the Harivaṃśa — reading the epic not as mere narrative but as a doctrinally authoritative (nirṇāyaka) text. It harmonizes apparent contradictions across śruti, smṛti and Purāṇa to present a single consistent Dvaita (dualist, Viṣṇu-supreme) theology, and discusses methods for establishing the authentic text.
SA_GSP33_m_mbhtnu.history: Composed by Madhvācārya, founder of the Dvaita school, it is one of his major works and is included in his collected Sarvamūla. It became central to Mādhva exegesis of epic and Purāṇic literature and attracted later commentaries (tippaṇī).

SA_GSP33_madhanuu.title: Anuvyākhyāna
SA_GSP33_madhanuu.author: Madhva (Ānandatīrtha)
SA_GSP33_madhanuu.genre: verse commentary / supplement (anuvyākhyāna) on the Brahmasūtra
SA_GSP33_madhanuu.summary: The Anuvyākhyāna is Madhva's verse supplement to his own prose Brahmasūtrabhāṣya, restating and defending his Dvaita interpretation of Bādarāyaṇa's sūtras. Composed in a polemical, poetic style comparable to Kumārila's Ślokavārttika, it argues at length against Advaita and rival readings, expounding the real distinction (dvaita) between God, souls and matter.
SA_GSP33_madhanuu.history: Often regarded as Madhva's philosophical masterpiece, the Anuvyākhyāna is the basis of Jayatīrtha's famous sub-commentary the Nyāyasudhā, which became authoritative for the Mādhva tradition. It is part of Madhva's collected Sarvamūla.

SA_GSP33_madhsiddhb_u.title: Siddhāntabindu
SA_GSP33_madhsiddhb_u.author: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī
SA_GSP33_madhsiddhb_u.summary: A commentary on the Daśaślokī (ten verses) attributed to Śaṅkara, expounding the import of the Upaniṣadic mahāvākya 'tat tvam asi' (That thou art). Though formally a gloss, it functions as an independent and exhaustive digest of Advaita Vedānta, systematically presenting and refuting rival views on the nature of the Self and establishing the Self as pure, attributeless consciousness.
SA_GSP33_madhsiddhb_u.history: Written by Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, a renowned Bengali Advaitin of the 16th century, reportedly for the benefit of his pupil Balabhadra. It has been printed repeatedly with subcommentaries, notably by Gauḍabrahmānanda (Nyāyaratnāvalī) and Nārāyaṇatīrtha.

SA_GSP33_mbrahsiu.title: Brahmasiddhi
SA_GSP33_mbrahsiu.author: Maṇḍana Miśra
SA_GSP33_mbrahsiu.summary: Maṇḍana Miśra's largest and most important work, the sole one dedicated wholly to Vedānta. It is a systematic demonstration of Brahman, organized in four chapters (Brahmakāṇḍa, Tarkakāṇḍa, Niyogakāṇḍa, Siddhikāṇḍa), arguing for the nondual reality of Brahman and treating themes such as the nature of ignorance, error, and the relation of injunction (niyoga) to liberating knowledge.
SA_GSP33_mbrahsiu.history: Maṇḍana Miśra, a Mīmāṃsaka influenced by Kumārila who turned to Advaita, was roughly contemporary with Śaṅkara; later tradition sometimes conflated him with Sureśvara. His Advaita differs from Śaṅkara's in several details (e.g. the locus of avidyā). Vācaspati Miśra wrote the commentary Brahmasiddhiṭīkā (Tattvasamīkṣā).

SA_GSP33_mndvivpu.title: Vibhramaviveka
SA_GSP33_mndvivpu.author: Maṇḍana Miśra
SA_GSP33_mndvivpu.summary: A short treatise on the theory of error (khyātivāda) that surveys and compares the major competing accounts of erroneous cognition current in classical Indian epistemology, including the anyathākhyāti of (Pūrva) Mīmāṃsā/Nyāya and the anirvacanīyakhyāti of Advaita. It examines how illusion arises and how understanding error is itself an avenue to truth.
SA_GSP33_mndvivpu.history: One of Maṇḍana Miśra's specialized philosophical works, transmitted alongside his Brahmasiddhi and Vidhiviveka. It is an important early source for the systematic classification of theories of perceptual error.

SA_GSP33_nabodhsu.title: Bodhasāra
SA_GSP33_nabodhsu.author: Narahari
SA_GSP33_nabodhsu.summary: A lengthy 18th-century Advaita Vedānta work that is at once a philosophical treatise and a poetic composition, treating standard Vedāntic topics from a fresh perspective and aiming to convey the experience of jīvanmukti (liberation while living). The author employs varied literary devices, similes, and original examples to communicate Advaita teaching.
SA_GSP33_nabodhsu.history: Composed by the Advaitin Narahari in or shortly before 1789, it is regarded as an example of the renaissance-spirited Sanskrit wisdom literature of pre-colonial India. It was first translated into English by Jennifer and Grahame Cover (Munīndra-Dinācārya / Bodhasāra editions).

SA_GSP33_nysu__1u.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 1
SA_GSP33_nysu__1u.author: Jayatīrtha
SA_GSP33_nysu__1u.summary: The first adhyāya of Jayatīrtha's magnum opus, an exhaustive sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna, which is itself a verse commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra. Adhyāya 1 covers the foundational matters (samanvaya) establishing that Brahman is the purport of all Vedānta, defending Dvaita realism on cognition, error, and the distinctness of God, souls, and world.
SA_GSP33_nysu__1u.history: Jayatīrtha (the 'Ṭīkācārya'), the chief systematizer of Madhva's Dvaita, produced this work as the school's definitive exegetical authority; it is said that the depths of Tattvavāda can only be grasped through the Nyāyasudhā. It attracted numerous later glosses, e.g. by Rāghavendra Tīrtha.

SA_GSP33_nysu__2u.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 2
SA_GSP33_nysu__2u.author: Jayatīrtha
SA_GSP33_nysu__2u.summary: The second adhyāya of Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā, sub-commenting on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna on the Brahmasūtra. This adhyāya (avirodha) is devoted to refuting objections and rival systems—Bauddha, Jaina, Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, Sāṃkhya, Bhāṭṭa and Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā, Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita—demonstrating the non-contradiction of the Dvaita reading of Vedānta.
SA_GSP33_nysu__2u.history: Part of Jayatīrtha's comprehensive defense of Madhva's Tattvavāda; the Nyāyasudhā is the central scholastic text of Dvaita Vedānta and the basis of the school's traditional curriculum and disputation.

SA_GSP33_nysu__3u.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 3
SA_GSP33_nysu__3u.author: Jayatīrtha
SA_GSP33_nysu__3u.summary: The third adhyāya of Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna. Following the Brahmasūtra structure, this adhyāya (sādhana) treats the means to liberation—the disciplines of devotion and knowledge, the soul's dependence on God, and the path of the aspirant—within the Dvaita framework of an eternal hierarchical distinction between God and souls.
SA_GSP33_nysu__3u.history: Continuation of Jayatīrtha's foundational sub-commentary on Madhva's verse exposition of the Brahmasūtra; the work as a whole anchors the Dvaita scholastic tradition.

SA_GSP33_nysu__4u.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 4, Pāda 1
SA_GSP33_nysu__4u.author: Jayatīrtha
SA_GSP33_nysu__4u.summary: The first pāda of the fourth and final adhyāya of Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna. This adhyāya (phala) concerns the fruit of knowledge and meditation; pāda 1 deals with the repeated practice (āvṛtti) of meditation on Brahman and the destruction of accumulated karma, treated according to Dvaita doctrine.
SA_GSP33_nysu__4u.history: Concluding portion of Jayatīrtha's exhaustive sub-commentary, the cornerstone scholastic text of the Mādhva Dvaita Vedānta tradition.

SA_GSP33_pada.title: Pañcadaśī
SA_GSP33_pada.author: Vidyāraṇya (Mādhava); with possible contribution by Bhāratītīrtha
SA_GSP33_pada.summary: A celebrated introductory verse manual of Advaita Vedānta in fifteen chapters, grouped into three pentads: Viveka (discrimination of the real from the unreal), Dīpa (illumination of the nature of consciousness), and Ānanda (the bliss of the Self). It expounds the identity of the individual self and Brahman and the means of realizing it, becoming a standard textbook of the tradition.
SA_GSP33_pada.history: Ascribed to Vidyāraṇya, identified with Mādhava, the Advaita preceptor associated with the founding of Vijayanagara; tradition holds the authorship shifts from the seventh chapter onward, with later portions possibly composed by his associate Bhāratītīrtha. Widely commented upon (e.g. Rāmakṛṣṇa's Vṛtti).

SA_GSP33_ramgbhpu.title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
SA_GSP33_ramgbhpu.author: Rāmānuja
SA_GSP33_ramgbhpu.summary: Rāmānuja's commentary on the Bhagavadgītā, reading the text through Viśiṣṭādvaita (qualified non-dualism). It presents Nārāyaṇa as the supreme personal Brahman, individual selves and matter as His real, dependent modes (śarīra), and bhakti (loving devotion), supported by karma- and jñāna-yoga, as the means to attain Him.
SA_GSP33_ramgbhpu.history: Attributed to Rāmānuja (traditional dates 1017-1137 CE, though the lifespan is debated), it draws structurally on Yāmuna's verse summary Gītārthasaṅgraha. It is one of the three foundational Vedāntic works of the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition and a principal alternative to Śaṅkara's Advaita reading of the Gītā.

SA_GSP33_rams11pu.title: Śrībhāṣya: 1,1.3
SA_GSP33_rams11pu.author: Rāmānuja
SA_GSP33_rams11pu.summary: A section (adhyāya 1, pāda 1, sūtra 3) of Rāmānuja's Śrībhāṣya, his major commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra. The relevant sūtra ('śāstra-yonitvāt') establishes scripture as the source of knowledge of Brahman; this is the locus of Rāmānuja's famous Mahāsiddhānta and his sustained refutation of Advaita illusionism (the Mahāpūrvapakṣa), defending Brahman as endowed with infinite auspicious qualities.
SA_GSP33_rams11pu.history: The Śrībhāṣya is the foundational scriptural commentary of Viśiṣṭādvaita and the definitive Śrīvaiṣṇava response to Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya; tradition holds Rāmānuja composed it after studying the Bodhāyana-vṛtti. Project hints (1017-1137) reflect Rāmānuja's traditional lifespan.

SA_GSP33_ramvaspu.title: Vedārthasaṃgraha
SA_GSP33_ramvaspu.author: Rāmānuja
SA_GSP33_ramvaspu.summary: An independent prose summary of Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita position, expounding the meaning of the Veda (especially the Upaniṣadic mahāvākyas) as teaching a Brahman qualified by real selves and matter. It establishes the body-soul (śarīra-śarīrin) relation between Brahman and the world and the centrality of bhakti, while refuting Advaita and other rival readings.
SA_GSP33_ramvaspu.history: Often regarded as Rāmānuja's earliest major work, it lays out the philosophical framework later developed in the Śrībhāṣya. It is one of his three principal independent compositions and a key text of the Śrīvaiṣṇava school.

SA_GSP33_samkaubh_u.title: Kaṭhopaniṣadbhāṣya
SA_GSP33_samkaubh_u.author: Śaṃkara (Ādi Śaṅkara)
SA_GSP33_samkaubh_u.date_estimate: c. early 8th c. CE (Śaṅkara fl. c. 700-750)
SA_GSP33_samkaubh_u.summary: Śaṅkara's commentary on the Kaṭha Upaniṣad, the dialogue between the boy Naciketas and Yama (Death) on the nature of the Self and immortality. The commentary develops the Advaita reading: the ātman is the imperishable, unborn reality identical with Brahman, knowable not by argument alone but through scriptural revelation and the grace of insight, and liberation consists in this self-knowledge.
SA_GSP33_samkaubh_u.history: One of the genuine Upaniṣad commentaries (bhāṣyas) of Ādi Śaṅkara, the founder of classical Advaita Vedānta. The project's date hint (800-1099) is later than the scholarly consensus, which places Śaṅkara around the early 8th century CE.

SA_GSP33_samkupad_u.title: Upadeśasāhasrī
SA_GSP33_samkupad_u.author: Śaṃkara (Ādi Śaṅkara)
SA_GSP33_samkupad_u.date_estimate: c. early 8th c. CE (Śaṅkara fl. c. 700-750)
SA_GSP33_samkupad_u.summary: The 'Thousand Teachings,' Śaṅkara's principal independent (non-commentarial) treatise on Advaita, comprising a prose part and a metrical part. It instructs on the method of teaching liberating knowledge of the identity of ātman and Brahman, the role of the mahāvākya 'tat tvam asi,' the removal of superimposition (adhyāsa), and the dialogical pedagogy of guiding a qualified pupil to self-realization.
SA_GSP33_samkupad_u.history: Widely accepted by scholars as the most important authentic prakaraṇa-grantha of Ādi Śaṅkara, valuable for reconstructing his views independent of his commentaries. The project's date hint (800-1099) postdates the scholarly consensus of the early 8th century.

SA_GSP33_vesa.title: Vedāntasāra
SA_GSP33_vesa.author: Sadānanda Yogīndra (Yogendra Sarasvatī)
SA_GSP33_vesa.summary: A concise and methodical prose primer of Advaita Vedānta ('Essence of Vedānta'), one of the most popular introductory manuals of the school. It lays out the subject-matter, qualified student, the four prerequisites (sādhana-catuṣṭaya), superimposition and its removal, the cosmology of māyā and the three bodies/states, and the method of scriptural inquiry leading to liberation.
SA_GSP33_vesa.history: Composed by Sadānanda Yogīndra around the mid-15th century, the work synthesizes earlier Advaita sources including Gauḍapāda's Kārikā, Śaṅkara's Upadeśasāhasrī, Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmyasiddhi, and Vidyāraṇya's Pañcadaśī. It remains a standard textbook with several commentaries (e.g. Subodhinī, Vidvanmanorañjinī).

SA_GSP33_vidhivi.title: Vidhiviveka
SA_GSP33_vidhivi.author: Maṇḍana Miśra
SA_GSP33_vidhivi.date_estimate: c. early 8th c. CE (Maṇḍana fl. c. 660-720)
SA_GSP33_vidhivi.summary: An influential Mīmāṃsā treatise on the nature of Vedic injunction (vidhi), in roughly forty-two verses with an extensive auto-commentary. Maṇḍana surveys the rival theories of verbal exhortation current in his time before defending his own view that an injunction conveys to the agent the means to attaining what that agent desires (iṣṭasādhana).
SA_GSP33_vidhivi.history: One of the most important works of Vedic exegesis after Kumārila, by the Mīmāṃsaka-Advaitin Maṇḍana Miśra. The polymath Vācaspati Miśra (10th c.) wrote the major commentary Nyāyakaṇikā on it, in turn glossed by Parameśvara (Juṣadhvaṅkaraṇī and Svaditaṅkaraṇī).

SA_GSP33_vivcud_u.title: Vivekacūḍāmaṇi
SA_GSP33_vivcud_u.author: Traditionally attributed to Śaṃkara (Ādi Śaṅkara); authorship disputed by modern scholarship
SA_GSP33_vivcud_u.date_estimate: traditionally c. 8th c. CE; many scholars regard it as a later (post-Śaṅkara) Advaita compilation
SA_GSP33_vivcud_u.summary: The 'Crest-Jewel of Discrimination,' a popular verse manual of Advaita Vedānta presenting the path to liberation through discrimination (viveka) between the eternal Self and the non-Self. It treats the prerequisites of the aspirant, the structure of the bodies and sheaths (kośas), the nature of bondage and māyā, and the realization of the identity of ātman and Brahman culminating in jīvanmukti.
SA_GSP33_vivcud_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Ādi Śaṅkara and among his most widely read works, but its authenticity is contested by many modern scholars on stylistic and doctrinal grounds, with some assigning it to a later author of the Advaita tradition. The project's date hint (800-1099) reflects this uncertainty.

SA_GSP33_yaatsi_u.title: Siddhitraya: Part 1: Ātmasiddhi
SA_GSP33_yaatsi_u.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya)
SA_GSP33_yaatsi_u.summary: The first of the three 'demonstrations' (siddhi) of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya, establishing the nature of the individual self (ātman). It argues against Advaita and Buddhist no-self/illusion positions that the self is a real, eternal, self-luminous knowing subject (jñātṛ)—distinct from the body, senses, and mind—laying epistemological foundations for Viśiṣṭādvaita.
SA_GSP33_yaatsi_u.history: Yāmunācārya (traditionally c. 918-1038, grand-preceptor of the Śrīvaiṣṇava line) was the seminal thinker whose ideas Rāmānuja later systematized. The Siddhitraya survives incompletely; the Saṃvitsiddhi and Īśvarasiddhi are partly fragmentary.

SA_GSP33_yaissi_u.title: Siddhitraya: Part 2: Īśvarasiddhi
SA_GSP33_yaissi_u.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya)
SA_GSP33_yaissi_u.summary: The second 'demonstration' of the Siddhitraya, the 'establishment of God' (Īśvara). It defends the existence of a supreme personal Lord as the creator and inner controller, countering rival positions, and prepares the theistic ground that distinguishes Viśiṣṭādvaita's qualified Brahman from Advaita's attributeless absolute.
SA_GSP33_yaissi_u.history: Part of Yāmunācārya's chief philosophical work; like the rest of the Siddhitraya it survives in incomplete form. Yāmuna's theism strongly shaped Rāmānuja's later Viśiṣṭādvaita synthesis.

SA_GSP33_yamgaspu.title: Gītārthasaṅgraha
SA_GSP33_yamgaspu.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya)
SA_GSP33_yamgaspu.summary: A concise versified summary of the meaning of the Bhagavadgītā, distilling its teaching into a coherent scheme. Yāmuna shows that the Gītā teaches the attainment of Nārāyaṇa-Parabrahman through the integrated disciplines of karma (action), jñāna (knowledge), and bhakti (devotion), reading the eighteen chapters as three coordinated sets of six.
SA_GSP33_yamgaspu.history: An influential short work of Yāmunācārya that provided the basic interpretive structure for Rāmānuja's later Bhagavadgītābhāṣya. It is foundational for the Śrīvaiṣṇava understanding of the Gītā.

SA_GSP33_yamis_pu.title: Īśvarasiddhi
SA_GSP33_yamis_pu.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya / Ālavandār)
SA_GSP33_yamis_pu.date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (Yāmuna fl. c. 918–1038 CE)
SA_GSP33_yamis_pu.summary: The Īśvarasiddhi ('Demonstration of God') is one of the three component treatises of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya. It argues for the existence and nature of a personal supreme Lord (Īśvara), defending theism against rival positions and laying a foundation for the later Viśiṣṭādvaita doctrine of God as the inner controller of all souls and matter.
SA_GSP33_yamis_pu.history: Yāmuna was the grand-teacher and principal philosophical predecessor of Rāmānuja, and the Siddhitraya provided much of the conceptual groundwork that Rāmānuja systematized. The Īśvarasiddhi survives incompletely and is among the earliest systematic Viśiṣṭādvaita statements on theism.

SA_GSP33_yamss_pu.title: Saṃvitsiddhi
SA_GSP33_yamss_pu.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya / Ālavandār)
SA_GSP33_yamss_pu.date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (Yāmuna fl. c. 918–1038 CE)
SA_GSP33_yamss_pu.summary: The Saṃvitsiddhi ('Demonstration of [the validity of] cognition/empirical reality') is the third treatise of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya. It defends the reality and validity of consciousness and the empirical world (saṃvit) against Advaita illusionism, arguing that knowledge truly reveals real objects and that the manifold world is not a mere appearance.
SA_GSP33_yamss_pu.history: A foundational anti-Advaita polemic of early Viśiṣṭādvaita, the Saṃvitsiddhi anticipates Rāmānuja's critique of māyāvāda. The text is preserved only in part, and its arguments were absorbed and expanded by Rāmānuja and later Śrī Vaiṣṇava authors.

SA_GSP33_yamsvspu.title: Saṃvitsiddhi
SA_GSP33_yamsvspu.author: Yāmunācārya
SA_GSP33_yamsvspu.date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (Yāmuna fl. c. 918–1038 CE)
SA_GSP33_yamsvspu.summary: A copy/recension of Yāmuna's Saṃvitsiddhi, the third part of the Siddhitraya, demonstrating the reality and self-validity of consciousness and the empirical world against Advaita non-dualism. The treatise upholds that cognition discloses genuinely existing objects rather than illusory appearances.
SA_GSP33_yamsvspu.history: This is another textual witness of the same work as GSP33yamss_pu, a cornerstone of early Viśiṣṭādvaita that prepared the philosophical ground for Rāmānuja. It survives in fragmentary form.

SA_GSP33_yasasi_u.title: Siddhitraya: Part 3: Saṃvitsiddhi
SA_GSP33_yasasi_u.author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya / Ālavandār)
SA_GSP33_yasasi_u.date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (Yāmuna fl. c. 918–1038 CE)
SA_GSP33_yasasi_u.summary: Explicitly the third section (Saṃvitsiddhi) of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya, the 'triad of demonstrations.' It establishes the reality of consciousness (saṃvit) and the perceived world, refuting the Advaitin reduction of plurality to illusion and defending the validity of knowledge.
SA_GSP33_yasasi_u.history: The Siddhitraya as a whole comprises Ātmasiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi, and Saṃvitsiddhi, and is reckoned Yāmuna's most important philosophical work and a direct precursor to Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita. The text is transmitted incompletely.

SA_GSP34_ghers_pu.title: Gheraṇḍa-Saṃhitā
SA_GSP34_ghers_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the sage Gheraṇḍa)
SA_GSP34_ghers_pu.summary: The Gheraṇḍa-Saṃhitā is a classical manual of Haṭha Yoga cast as a dialogue in which the sage Gheraṇḍa instructs his pupil Caṇḍakāpāli. It teaches a sevenfold (saptāṅga) path of 'ghaṭastha' yoga covering purification (ṣaṭkarma), āsanas, mudrās, pratyāhāra, prāṇāyāma, dhyāna, and samādhi, with one of the most extensive premodern lists of postures and cleansing techniques.
SA_GSP34_ghers_pu.history: Probably composed in eastern India (Bengal region) in the late 17th century, it is one of the three most influential classical Haṭha Yoga texts alongside the Haṭhayogapradīpikā and Śiva Saṃhitā. Its sevenfold scheme is distinctive among yoga manuals.

SA_GSP34_hathyopu.title: Haṭhayogapradīpikā
SA_GSP34_hathyopu.author: Svātmārāma
SA_GSP34_hathyopu.summary: The Haṭhayogapradīpikā ('Light on Haṭha Yoga') is the most influential classical compendium of Haṭha Yoga, systematizing teachings on āsana, prāṇāyāma (with bandhas and kumbhakas), the ṣaṭkarmas, mudrās, the awakening of kuṇḍalinī, nāda, and samādhi. It frames physical and energetic practices as a ladder leading toward Rāja Yoga.
SA_GSP34_hathyopu.history: Compiled by Svātmārāma, who draws on earlier Nātha and tantric yoga sources, the text became the standard reference work for later Haṭha Yoga and attracted commentaries such as Brahmānanda's Jyotsnā. It strongly shaped modern transmission of postural yoga.

SA_GSP34_patyog_u.title: Yogasūtra
SA_GSP34_patyog_u.author: Patañjali
SA_GSP34_patyog_u.summary: The Yogasūtra of Patañjali is the foundational text of the Yoga school of Indian philosophy, comprising c. 195 terse aphorisms in four pādas (Samādhi, Sādhana, Vibhūti, Kaivalya). It defines yoga as 'the stilling of the fluctuations of the mind' (cittavṛttinirodha) and expounds the eightfold path (aṣṭāṅga yoga) culminating in liberation (kaivalya) within a Sāṃkhya-derived metaphysics.
SA_GSP34_patyog_u.history: Modern scholarship (e.g. Maas) often treats the sūtras and the Bhāṣya as a single 'Pātañjalayogaśāstra' composed/redacted around 400 CE; the text shows awareness of Buddhist (Vasubandhu-era) ideas. It generated a rich commentarial tradition and is the most authoritative classical statement of yoga.

SA_GSP34_patyogbu.title: Yogasūtra with Bhāṣya
SA_GSP34_patyogbu.author: Patañjali (sūtras); Bhāṣya attributed to Vyāsa (or Patañjali himself)
SA_GSP34_patyogbu.date_estimate: c. 350–450 CE (the Pātañjalayogaśāstra, sūtras + Bhāṣya, c. 400 CE)
SA_GSP34_patyogbu.summary: This is the Yogasūtra together with its earliest commentary, the Yogabhāṣya, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. The Bhāṣya explains each aphorism, elaborating the theory of mind, the kleśas, the means of practice, the attainments (vibhūti/siddhi), and kaivalya, and is essential to the classical interpretation of the sūtras.
SA_GSP34_patyogbu.history: Recent scholarship argues the sūtras and Bhāṣya form one original work (the Pātañjalayogaśāstra), the 'Vyāsa' attribution being later; either way the commentary attained near-canonical authority. It anchored the subcommentarial tradition of Vācaspati Miśra and Vijñānabhikṣu.

SA_GSP34_yogasubha.title: Yogasūtrabhāṣya
SA_GSP34_yogasubha.author: attributed to Vyāsa (Vedavyāsa); possibly Patañjali himself
SA_GSP34_yogasubha.summary: The Yogasūtrabhāṣya (Yogabhāṣya) is the foundational prose commentary on Patañjali's Yogasūtra. It systematically expounds the aphorisms, detailing the nature of citta and its modifications, the five kleśas, the eightfold practice, the supernatural attainments, and the doctrine of isolation (kaivalya), embedding the whole in Sāṃkhya-Yoga metaphysics.
SA_GSP34_yogasubha.history: Traditionally credited to the legendary Vyāsa and dated to roughly the 4th–5th century CE, though many modern scholars regard it as integral to the original Pātañjalayogaśāstra. It is the most authoritative classical commentary and the basis for all later Yoga exegesis.

SA_GSP34_yogasutu.title: Yogasūtra
SA_GSP34_yogasutu.author: Patañjali
SA_GSP34_yogasutu.summary: Another witness of Patañjali's Yogasūtra, the c. 195 aphorisms in four pādas that found the Yoga school. It teaches the cessation of mental fluctuations and the eightfold discipline (yama, niyama, āsana, prāṇāyāma, pratyāhāra, dhāraṇā, dhyāna, samādhi) leading to kaivalya.
SA_GSP34_yogasutu.history: The canonical source text of classical Yoga, frequently transmitted alongside the Yogabhāṣya and dated to around 400 CE. It remains the most authoritative philosophical treatment of yoga.

SA_GSP34_hathayogapradipika.title: Haṭhayogapradīpikā
SA_GSP34_hathayogapradipika.author: Svātmārāma
SA_GSP34_hathayogapradipika.summary: A copy of the 'Light on Haṭha Yoga' by Svātmārāma, the standard classical compendium of Haṭha Yoga covering āsana, prāṇāyāma, ṣaṭkarma, mudrā and bandha, kuṇḍalinī awakening, nāda, and samādhi, presented as preparatory to Rāja Yoga.
SA_GSP34_hathayogapradipika.history: This is another textual witness of the same work as GSP34hathyopu. Compiled from earlier Nātha sources, it became the most authoritative premodern Haṭha Yoga text, commented on by Brahmānanda (Jyotsnā).

SA_GSP34_yogsubhu.title: Yogasūtra
SA_GSP34_yogsubhu.author: Patañjali
SA_GSP34_yogsubhu.summary: A further witness of Patañjali's Yogasūtra (the work_id suggests the sūtra with its Bhāṣya). It comprises the four pādas of aphorisms founding the Yoga darśana, defining yoga as cittavṛttinirodha and detailing the eightfold path to kaivalya.
SA_GSP34_yogsubhu.history: The base text of classical Yoga, typically circulated with the Yogabhāṣya and dated to around 400 CE; it remains the school's most authoritative source.

SA_GSP35_sa_bhAskarakaNTha-mokSopAyaTIkA.title: Mokṣopāyaṭīkā
SA_GSP35_sa_bhAskarakaNTha-mokSopAyaTIkA.author: Rājānaka Bhāskarakaṇṭha
SA_GSP35_sa_bhAskarakaNTha-mokSopAyaTIkA.tradition: Hindu, Kashmiri (non-dualist, broadly Kashmir Śaiva milieu)
SA_GSP35_sa_bhAskarakaNTha-mokSopAyaTIkA.summary: The Mokṣopāyaṭīkā is Bhāskarakaṇṭha's vast Sanskrit commentary on the Mokṣopāya (the Kashmiri recension underlying the Yogavāsiṣṭha). Running to some 110,000 verses/units, it elucidates the philosophical narrative of the source text, expounding its idealist doctrine that the world is mind-dependent and that liberation comes through insight.
SA_GSP35_sa_bhAskarakaNTha-mokSopAyaTIkA.history: Bhāskarakaṇṭha, a learned Kashmiri Brahmin, wrote this commentary in the late 17th century; it is a principal witness for reconstructing the original Mokṣopāya and is central to Walter Slaje's critical Mokṣopāya project.

SA_GSP35_sa_mokSopAya.title: Mokṣopāya
SA_GSP35_sa_mokSopAya.author: anonymous (the 'Anonymus Casmiriensis')
SA_GSP35_sa_mokSopAya.tradition: Hindu, Kashmiri non-dualist (idealist; related to Advaita/Kashmir Śaiva milieu)
SA_GSP35_sa_mokSopAya.summary: The Mokṣopāya ('Means to Liberation') is the original Kashmirian work that was later reworked into the popular Yogavāsiṣṭha. Framed as Vasiṣṭha's instruction of Rāma, it teaches through elaborate nested stories an absolute idealism in which the world is a projection of mind/consciousness, and liberation is attained by realizing this while living an active life.
SA_GSP35_sa_mokSopAya.history: Composed by an unknown author (the 'Anonymus Casmiriensis') on the Pradyumna hill in Śrīnagar in the 10th century, it predates and underlies the more theistically reworked vulgate Yogavāsiṣṭha. Its critical reconstruction is the focus of Walter Slaje's Mokṣopāya project.

SA_GV00_maitrs_pu.title: Maitrāyaṇī-Saṃhitā
SA_GV00_maitrs_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (Maitrāyaṇīya school)
SA_GV00_maitrs_pu.summary: The Maitrāyaṇī-Saṃhitā is a saṃhitā of the Black (Kṛṣṇa) Yajurveda belonging to the Maitrāyaṇīya school, intermixing sacrificial mantras with prose discussions (brāhmaṇa-type passages) on the ritual application of the formulas. It is a principal liturgical text for the performance of the śrauta sacrifices.
SA_GV00_maitrs_pu.history: One of the oldest and linguistically most archaic Yajurveda recensions, valued for preserving early Vedic dialectal and ritual features. It is closely related to the Kāṭhaka and Taittirīya saṃhitās and was edited by Leopold von Schroeder.

SA_GV00_sa_paippalAdasaMhitA.title: Paippalāda-Saṃhitā
SA_GV00_sa_paippalAdasaMhitA.author: anonymous/traditional (Paippalāda school)
SA_GV00_sa_paippalAdasaMhitA.date_estimate: c. 1200–900 BCE (composed; surviving recension transmitted later)
SA_GV00_sa_paippalAdasaMhitA.summary: The Paippalāda-Saṃhitā is one of the two surviving recensions of the Atharvaveda (alongside the Śaunaka). It collects hymns, spells, charms, and prayers concerning healing, protection, prosperity, sorcery, royal rites, and speculation, often differing in arrangement and containing material absent from the Śaunaka version.
SA_GV00_sa_paippalAdasaMhitA.history: Long thought largely lost, it was substantially recovered through Kashmiri and especially Odia (Orissa) manuscript traditions, and is the object of major modern critical editions (e.g. Witzel, Zehnder, Lubotsky, Griffiths, Kim). It is essential for Atharvavedic and comparative Vedic studies.

SA_GV01_aibra.title: Aitareyabrāhmaṇa
SA_GV01_aibra.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Mahidāsa Aitareya)
SA_GV01_aibra.summary: The Aitareyabrāhmaṇa is the principal brāhmaṇa of the Ṛgveda, in 40 adhyāyas grouped in eight pañcikās. It expounds the great Soma sacrifices (especially the Agniṣṭoma and other soma rites), the duties of the hotṛ priest, and the royal consecration (rājasūya), explaining the meaning and procedure of the rituals and including legends such as that of Śunaḥśepa.
SA_GV01_aibra.history: Traditionally attributed to the sage Mahidāsa Aitareya, it is a key prose text of the late Vedic period documenting the developed śrauta sacrificial system, and was famously translated by Martin Haug. The associated Aitareya Āraṇyaka and Upaniṣad continue its school.

SA_GV01_rv_01_u.title: Ṛgveda 1
SA_GV01_rv_01_u.author: anonymous/traditional (multiple ṛṣi families)
SA_GV01_rv_01_u.date_estimate: c. 1500–1200 BCE (Maṇḍala 1 partly later within this range)
SA_GV01_rv_01_u.summary: Maṇḍala 1, the first book of the Ṛgveda, contains 191 hymns addressed chiefly to Agni and Indra but also to many other deities (Aśvins, Maruts, Varuṇa, Uṣas, etc.). As one of the later, more heterogeneous 'family' arrangements, it gathers hymns by varied seers and serves partly as an introductory compilation.
SA_GV01_rv_01_u.history: The Ṛgveda is the oldest of the four Vedas and among the oldest surviving texts of any Indo-European language, orally transmitted with extraordinary fidelity. Maṇḍalas 2–7 form the older 'family books,' while Maṇḍalas 1 and 10 are generally considered later additions.

SA_GV01_rv_02_u.title: Ṛgveda 2
SA_GV01_rv_02_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Gṛtsamada family)
SA_GV01_rv_02_u.date_estimate: c. 1500–1200 BCE (early; one of the 'family books')
SA_GV01_rv_02_u.summary: Maṇḍala 2, the second book of the Ṛgveda, comprises 43 hymns mainly to Agni and Indra and is ascribed to the family of the seer Gṛtsamada. As one of the oldest 'family books,' it preserves some of the most archaic strata of Vedic poetry and ritual invocation.
SA_GV01_rv_02_u.history: The family books (Maṇḍalas 2–7) form the core and oldest layer of the Ṛgveda, each associated with a particular ṛṣi lineage. They were orally preserved for millennia with rigorous mnemonic techniques.

SA_GV01_rv_03_u.title: Ṛgveda 3
SA_GV01_rv_03_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Viśvāmitra family)
SA_GV01_rv_03_u.date_estimate: c. 1500–1200 BCE (early; one of the 'family books')
SA_GV01_rv_03_u.summary: Maṇḍala 3, the third book of the Ṛgveda, contains 62 hymns to Agni, Indra, and other deities and is attributed to the family of the seer Viśvāmitra. It famously includes the Gāyatrī mantra (RV 3.62.10), one of the most revered Vedic verses.
SA_GV01_rv_03_u.history: Part of the oldest 'family books' core of the Ṛgveda, associated with the Viśvāmitra lineage. Transmitted orally with great precision, it is foundational to later Vedic ritual and recitation traditions.

SA_GV01_rv_04_u.title: Ṛgveda 4
SA_GV01_rv_04_u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to the Gautama/Vāmadeva family of ṛṣis)
SA_GV01_rv_04_u.date_estimate: c. 1400-1200 BCE (one of the older 'family books')
SA_GV01_rv_04_u.summary: The fourth maṇḍala (book) of the Ṛgveda, comprising 58 hymns (sūktas) addressed mainly to Agni and Indra, with some to the Aśvins, Uṣas, and other deities. As a 'family book' (maṇḍalas 2-7), its hymns are traditionally ascribed to a single lineage of seer-poets, here the family of Vāmadeva Gautama.
SA_GV01_rv_04_u.history: Part of the oldest stratum of the Ṛgveda, the family books were composed orally and transmitted with extraordinary fidelity through the Padapāṭha and Saṃhitāpāṭha recitation systems. The hymns were later arranged and redacted into the canonical collection during the Brahmana period.

SA_GV01_rv_05_u.title: Ṛgveda 5
SA_GV01_rv_05_u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to the Atri family of ṛṣis)
SA_GV01_rv_05_u.summary: The fifth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 87 hymns primarily attributed to the Atri clan of seers, addressed chiefly to Agni and Indra, but also to the Viśvedevas, Maruts, Mitra-Varuṇa, and the Aśvins. As a family book it preserves archaic Indo-Aryan linguistic features.
SA_GV01_rv_05_u.history: One of the six 'family books' forming the core of the Ṛgveda. Absolute dating from philological analysis of its archaic features places composition in the range c. 1400-1200 BCE. Transmitted orally with exact preservation before redaction during the later Vedic period.

SA_GV01_rv_06_u.title: Ṛgveda 6
SA_GV01_rv_06_u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Bharadvāja and the Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas)
SA_GV01_rv_06_u.date_estimate: c. 1500-1200 BCE (family book; argued by some to be the oldest)
SA_GV01_rv_06_u.summary: The sixth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 75 hymns addressed mainly to Agni and Indra. Most of its hymns are attributed to the Bārhaspatya family of the Aṅgirasas, especially to the seer Bharadvāja.
SA_GV01_rv_06_u.history: A family book of the Ṛgveda; some scholars (e.g. Talageri) argue Maṇḍala 6 is the oldest of the family books on internal grounds. Composed and transmitted orally before redaction into the canonical collection.

SA_GV01_rv_07_u.title: Ṛgveda 7
SA_GV01_rv_07_u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Vasiṣṭha and his family)
SA_GV01_rv_07_u.summary: The seventh maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 104 hymns. In the Ṛgvedic Anukramaṇī all its hymns are attributed to the seer Vasiṣṭha. It includes celebrated hymns to Indra (including the Battle of the Ten Kings allusions), Agni, Varuṇa, the Maruts, and the famous frog hymn (maṇḍūka sūkta).
SA_GV01_rv_07_u.history: A family book forming part of the oldest core of the Ṛgveda, associated with the Vasiṣṭha lineage who figure prominently in early Vedic history. Composed orally and transmitted with high fidelity before later redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_08_u.title: Ṛgveda 8
SA_GV01_rv_08_u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns largely attributed to the Kāṇva and Āṅgirasa families)
SA_GV01_rv_08_u.date_estimate: c. 1200-1000 BCE (somewhat later than the family books)
SA_GV01_rv_08_u.summary: The eighth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 103 hymns (including the Vālakhilya hymns 49-59), addressed chiefly to Indra and Agni, with many to the Aśvins. A large portion is attributed to the Kāṇva family of seers.
SA_GV01_rv_08_u.history: Together with Maṇḍala 1, Book 8 is generally regarded as a somewhat later stratum than the family books (2-7), though still within the older Ṛgvedic period. It contains the supplementary Vālakhilya hymns. Transmitted orally before canonical redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_09_u.title: Ṛgveda 9
SA_GV01_rv_09_u.author: anonymous/traditional (composite, drawn from multiple seer families)
SA_GV01_rv_09_u.date_estimate: c. 1500-1000 BCE (composite; hymns of varied age grouped thematically)
SA_GV01_rv_09_u.summary: The ninth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 114 hymns wholly dedicated to Soma Pavamāna ('Soma being purified'), the deified sacred drink and its ritual filtering through the strainer. Unlike the other books, it is organized by deity (Soma) rather than by authoring family.
SA_GV01_rv_09_u.history: Maṇḍala 9 is a thematic compilation gathering Soma hymns extracted from across the seer families, so its constituent hymns span different ages. Its distinctive single-deity arrangement reflects the redactorial organization of the Ṛgveda.

SA_GV01_rv_10_u.title: Ṛgveda 10
SA_GV01_rv_10_u.author: anonymous/traditional (many families; latest stratum)
SA_GV01_rv_10_u.date_estimate: c. 1200-1000 BCE (latest book of the Ṛgveda)
SA_GV01_rv_10_u.summary: The tenth and final maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 191 hymns of diverse content. It contains the most philosophically and cosmologically advanced material, including the Puruṣa Sūkta, the Nāsadīya Sūkta (Creation Hymn), funeral and wedding hymns, and dialogue hymns (e.g. Yama-Yamī, Purūravas-Urvaśī).
SA_GV01_rv_10_u.history: Book 10 is the youngest layer of the Ṛgveda, linguistically and conceptually closer to the Atharvaveda and the later Vedic worldview. It was appended last during the collection's redaction and shows early speculation that prefigures the Upaniṣads.

SA_GV01_rv_hn01u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 1
SA_GV01_rv_hn01u.author: anonymous/traditional (many seer families; later stratum)
SA_GV01_rv_hn01u.date_estimate: c. 1200-1000 BCE (later than the family books)
SA_GV01_rv_hn01u.summary: The first maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 191 hymns by numerous seers addressed to a wide range of deities (Agni, Indra, the Aśvins, Uṣas, the Maruts, etc.). Along with Book 10, it is one of the longest and latest of the ten books. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 1.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn01u.history: Maṇḍala 1 was compiled relatively late, gathering hymns of many families, and serves as an expansive opening to the collection. Like the whole Ṛgveda it was transmitted orally with exact fidelity before redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn02u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 2
SA_GV01_rv_hn02u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Gṛtsamada and his family)
SA_GV01_rv_hn02u.summary: The second maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, the shortest book with 43 hymns, addressed mainly to Agni and Indra. Its hymns are traditionally attributed to the seer Gṛtsamada and his lineage. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 2.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn02u.history: The first of the six 'family books' (2-7) that form the oldest core of the Ṛgveda, arranged by decreasing length of hymns per book. Composed and transmitted orally before canonical redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn03u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 3
SA_GV01_rv_hn03u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Viśvāmitra and his family)
SA_GV01_rv_hn03u.summary: The third maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 62 hymns addressed mainly to Agni and Indra. Its hymns are attributed to the seer Viśvāmitra and his lineage, and it contains the famous Gāyatrī Mantra (RV 3.62.10). (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 3.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn03u.history: A family book forming part of the oldest Ṛgvedic core. Its inclusion of the Gāyatrī, the most sacred Vedic verse, gives it special liturgical prominence. Transmitted orally with high fidelity before redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn04u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 4
SA_GV01_rv_hn04u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Vāmadeva Gautama and family)
SA_GV01_rv_hn04u.summary: The fourth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 58 hymns addressed mainly to Agni and Indra, with hymns to the Aśvins, Uṣas, and Ṛbhus. Its hymns are attributed to the family of Vāmadeva Gautama. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 4, identical content to GV01rv_04_u.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn04u.history: One of the six family books forming the oldest core of the Ṛgveda, composed orally and transmitted through the Padapāṭha and Saṃhitāpāṭha systems before redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn05u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 5
SA_GV01_rv_hn05u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to the Atri family of ṛṣis)
SA_GV01_rv_hn05u.summary: The fifth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 87 hymns primarily attributed to the Atri clan, addressed chiefly to Agni and Indra, with hymns to the Viśvedevas, Maruts, Mitra-Varuṇa, and the Aśvins. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 5, identical content to GV01rv_05_u.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn05u.history: A family book of the Ṛgveda preserving archaic Indo-Aryan features, dated philologically to c. 1400-1200 BCE. Transmitted orally with exact preservation before redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn06u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 6
SA_GV01_rv_hn06u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Bharadvāja and the Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas)
SA_GV01_rv_hn06u.date_estimate: c. 1500-1200 BCE (family book; argued by some as oldest)
SA_GV01_rv_hn06u.summary: The sixth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 75 hymns addressed mainly to Agni and Indra, most attributed to the Bārhaspatya family of the Aṅgirasas, especially Bharadvāja. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 6, identical content to GV01rv_06_u.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn06u.history: A family book of the Ṛgveda, regarded by some scholars as the oldest of the family books. Composed and transmitted orally before canonical redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn07u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 7
SA_GV01_rv_hn07u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Vasiṣṭha and his family)
SA_GV01_rv_hn07u.summary: The seventh maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 104 hymns, all attributed in the Anukramaṇī to the seer Vasiṣṭha. It includes notable hymns to Indra, Varuṇa, Agni, the Maruts, and the celebrated frog hymn. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 7, identical content to GV01rv_07_u.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn07u.history: A family book forming part of the oldest Ṛgvedic core, associated with the historically prominent Vasiṣṭha lineage. Transmitted orally with high fidelity before redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn08u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 8
SA_GV01_rv_hn08u.author: anonymous/traditional (hymns largely attributed to the Kāṇva and Āṅgirasa families)
SA_GV01_rv_hn08u.date_estimate: c. 1200-1000 BCE (later than the family books)
SA_GV01_rv_hn08u.summary: The eighth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 103 hymns (including the Vālakhilya hymns 49-59), addressed chiefly to Indra, Agni, and the Aśvins, with much of the book attributed to the Kāṇva family. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 8.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn08u.history: Generally regarded as a somewhat later stratum than the family books, though still within the older Ṛgvedic period. Contains the supplementary Vālakhilya hymns. Transmitted orally before canonical redaction.

SA_GV01_rv_hn09u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 9
SA_GV01_rv_hn09u.author: anonymous/traditional (composite, drawn from multiple seer families)
SA_GV01_rv_hn09u.summary: The ninth maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 114 hymns wholly devoted to Soma Pavamāna, the deified ritual drink and its purification through the strainer. It is uniquely organized by deity rather than by authoring family. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 9.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn09u.history: A thematic compilation gathering Soma hymns from across the seer families, so its constituent hymns vary in age. Many of its verses are closely paralleled in the Sāmaveda for liturgical chant.

SA_GV01_rv_hn10u.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 10
SA_GV01_rv_hn10u.author: anonymous/traditional (many families; latest stratum)
SA_GV01_rv_hn10u.date_estimate: c. 1200-1000 BCE (latest book of the Ṛgveda)
SA_GV01_rv_hn10u.summary: The tenth and final maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda, comprising 191 hymns of diverse and often philosophical content, including the Puruṣa Sūkta, the Nāsadīya Sūkta (Creation Hymn), and funeral, wedding, and dialogue hymns. (This is the 'hn' edition ID for Maṇḍala 10.)
SA_GV01_rv_hn10u.history: The youngest layer of the Ṛgveda, linguistically and conceptually closer to the Atharvaveda and later Vedic thought, appended last during redaction. Its cosmological speculation prefigures the Upaniṣads.

SA_GV01_rvkhilau.title: The Khila Verses of the Ṛgveda
SA_GV01_rvkhilau.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV01_rvkhilau.date_estimate: c. 1200-1000 BCE (late additions, still within the Mantra period)
SA_GV01_rvkhilau.summary: The Khilāni are a collection of roughly 98 supplementary or 'apocryphal' hymns of the Ṛgveda, preserved in the Bāṣkala recension but absent from the standard Śākala saṃhitā. They include the celebrated Śrī Sūkta and the Kuntāpa hymns used in the Mahāvrata New Year ceremony of the Kuru kingdom.
SA_GV01_rvkhilau.history: Though later than the main Ṛgvedic corpus, the Khila verses still belong to the Mantra period of Vedic Sanskrit, contemporary with the Atharvaveda, Yajurveda, and Sāmaveda. They were edited critically by I. Scheftelowitz (1906) and represent material appended outside the canonical body of the saṃhitā.

SA_GV01_rvpp_01u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 1
SA_GV01_rvpp_01u.author: Śākalya (traditional analyst of the Padapāṭha); hymns anonymous/traditional
SA_GV01_rvpp_01u.date_estimate: hymns c. 1200-1000 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis c. 800-600 BCE (later Brahmana period)
SA_GV01_rvpp_01u.summary: The Padapāṭha ('word-text') version of Maṇḍala 1 of the Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: the hymns of the first book presented word by word with sandhi (euphonic combination) dissolved, separating even the members of compound words. It is the analytic recitation form complementing the continuous Saṃhitāpāṭha.
SA_GV01_rvpp_01u.history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya and represents one of the earliest endeavors in Indian linguistic analysis, fixing the exact word-segmentation of the orally transmitted text. Its codification occurred during the later Brahmana period, several centuries after the hymns were composed, and it remains a key tool for grammatical and textual study.

SA_GV01_rvpp_02u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 2
SA_GV01_rvpp_02u.author: Śākalya (traditional analyst of the Padapāṭha); hymns anonymous/traditional (Gṛtsamada family)
SA_GV01_rvpp_02u.date_estimate: hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis c. 800-600 BCE (later Brahmana period)
SA_GV01_rvpp_02u.summary: The Padapāṭha ('word-text') version of Maṇḍala 2 of the Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: the 43 hymns of the second (Gṛtsamada) family book presented word by word with sandhi dissolved and compounds analyzed into their members. It complements the continuous Saṃhitāpāṭha recitation of the same hymns.
SA_GV01_rvpp_02u.history: Like the Padapāṭha of Maṇḍala 1, this is ascribed to Śākalya and embodies early Vedic linguistic analysis, fixing the word-segmentation of the orally transmitted text during the later Brahmana period. It is foundational for grammatical and metrical study of the Ṛgveda.

SA_GV01_rvpp_03u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 3
SA_GV01_rvpp_03u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 3 attributed to the Viśvāmitra family
SA_GV01_rvpp_03u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
SA_GV01_rvpp_03u.summary: The Padapāṭha is the 'word-for-word' recitational text of the Ṛgveda in which each word is given in its isolated pausal form, dissolving the sandhi (euphonic combinations) of the continuous Saṃhitāpāṭha and resolving compounds. This file covers Maṇḍala 3, the 'family book' of the Viśvāmitra clan, whose hymns are addressed largely to Agni, Indra, and the Viśvedevas, and which contains the famous Gāyatrī mantra (RV 3.62.10). The Padapāṭha was created to preserve exact pronunciation, accent, and word boundaries across oral transmission.
SA_GV01_rvpp_03u.history: The surviving Ṛgveda Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya (the Brahma and Vāyu Purāṇas name Vidagdha/Śākalya), and it forms the analytical basis for the Prātiśākhya and Pāṇinian grammar; the Maṇḍala 3 hymns are among the oldest 'family books' of the collection. The Ṛgveda's oral transmission via Saṃhitā- and Padapāṭha is recognized in UNESCO's Memory of the World register.

SA_GV01_rvpp_04u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 4
SA_GV01_rvpp_04u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 4 attributed to the Vāmadeva family
SA_GV01_rvpp_04u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
SA_GV01_rvpp_04u.summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 4 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Vāmadeva (Gautama) clan. Its hymns are addressed chiefly to Agni and Indra, with notable hymns to the Ṛbhus and to Uṣas; the Padapāṭha presents each word in isolated, sandhi-resolved pausal form for exact preservation of pronunciation and accent.
SA_GV01_rvpp_04u.history: The Ṛgveda Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya and underpins the later grammatical and phonetic (Prātiśākhya) tradition. Maṇḍala 4 belongs to the older core of family books transmitted orally with extraordinary fidelity for over three millennia.

SA_GV01_rvpp_05u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 5
SA_GV01_rvpp_05u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 5 attributed to the Atri family
SA_GV01_rvpp_05u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
SA_GV01_rvpp_05u.summary: The Padapāṭha (word-for-word) recitational text of Maṇḍala 5 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Atri clan. Its hymns honor primarily Agni, Indra, the Maruts, Mitra-Varuṇa, and the Viśvedevas; the Padapāṭha isolates every word in pausal form, dissolving sandhi and analyzing compounds.
SA_GV01_rvpp_05u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Śākalya, the Padapāṭha was devised to safeguard precise word division and accentuation in oral transmission and serves as the foundation for the Ṛkprātiśākhya and Pāṇinian grammatical analysis. Maṇḍala 5 is one of the older Ṛgvedic family books.

SA_GV01_rvpp_06u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 6
SA_GV01_rvpp_06u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 6 attributed to the Bharadvāja family
SA_GV01_rvpp_06u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
SA_GV01_rvpp_06u.summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 6 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Bharadvāja clan. Its hymns are devoted especially to Agni and Indra, with hymns to Pūṣan and other deities; the Padapāṭha gives each word separately in its uncombined pausal form to preserve exact pronunciation and accent.
SA_GV01_rvpp_06u.history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya and is the analytical substrate for later phonetic and grammatical science. Maṇḍala 6 belongs to the archaic core of family books transmitted with remarkable oral fidelity.

SA_GV01_rvpp_07u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 7
SA_GV01_rvpp_07u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 7 attributed to the Vasiṣṭha family
SA_GV01_rvpp_07u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
SA_GV01_rvpp_07u.summary: The Padapāṭha (word-for-word) recitational text of Maṇḍala 7 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Vasiṣṭha clan. It contains celebrated hymns to Agni, Indra, Mitra-Varuṇa, the goddess of dawn Uṣas, and the famous frog hymn and the maṇḍūka, and references to the Battle of the Ten Kings (dāśarājña); the Padapāṭha resolves sandhi and presents each word in isolated form.
SA_GV01_rvpp_07u.history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 7, associated with Vasiṣṭha, is a key family book whose internal allusions are central to reconstructing early Vedic history and chronology; it was transmitted orally with extreme precision.

SA_GV01_rvpp_08u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 8
SA_GV01_rvpp_08u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 8 attributed largely to the Kaṇva and Āṅgirasa families
SA_GV01_rvpp_08u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1000 BCE (partly later than the family books); Padapāṭha analysis c. 7th-5th c. BCE
SA_GV01_rvpp_08u.summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 8 of the Ṛgveda, associated chiefly with the Kaṇva and Āṅgirasa poets. It includes many hymns to Indra and Agni and the distinctive Vālakhilya hymns (apocryphal supplementary hymns), and is noted for its use of the longer strophic metres; the Padapāṭha gives each word in isolated, sandhi-free pausal form.
SA_GV01_rvpp_08u.history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 8, together with Maṇḍala 1, is generally held to be somewhat younger than the core family books (2-7), and the Vālakhilya hymns it contains are regarded as a later interpolated collection.

SA_GV01_rvpp_09u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 9
SA_GV01_rvpp_09u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns by many families
SA_GV01_rvpp_09u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
SA_GV01_rvpp_09u.summary: The Padapāṭha (word-for-word) recitational text of Maṇḍala 9 of the Ṛgveda, which is unique in being entirely dedicated to Soma Pavamāna (Soma 'self-purifying' as it is filtered in the ritual). Its hymns, drawn from poets of many families, accompany the pressing and clarifying of the Soma juice; the Padapāṭha isolates each word in pausal form.
SA_GV01_rvpp_09u.history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 9 was compiled thematically (by deity) rather than by family, gathering Soma hymns from across the other maṇḍalas, and it is central to understanding the Soma cult and the chants later elaborated in the Sāmaveda.

SA_GV01_rvpp_10u.title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 10
SA_GV01_rvpp_10u.author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns by many poets
SA_GV01_rvpp_10u.date_estimate: Hymns c. 1200-1000 BCE (the latest layer of the Ṛgveda); Padapāṭha analysis c. 7th-5th c. BCE
SA_GV01_rvpp_10u.summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 10, the final and generally latest book of the Ṛgveda.
