It contains many of the most philosophically and literarily famous hymns, including the Puruṣasūkta (10.90), the Nāsadīya creation hymn (10.129), the Yama-Yamī and Purūravas-Urvaśī dialogue hymns, marriage and funeral hymns, and Vāc; the Padapāṭha presents each word in isolated pausal form.
SA_GV01_rvpp_10u.history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 10 is widely regarded by scholars as the youngest stratum of the Ṛgveda, linguistically and conceptually closer to the later Vedic period, and its speculative hymns prefigure Upaniṣadic thought.

SA_GV02_gopthbru.title: Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa
SA_GV02_gopthbru.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV02_gopthbru.date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE or later; one of the latest Brāhmaṇas
SA_GV02_gopthbru.summary: The Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa is the only surviving Brāhmaṇa attached to the Atharvaveda (linked to both the Śaunaka and Paippalāda recensions). Divided into a Pūrva-bhāga and an Uttara-bhāga, it discusses the role of the Brahman priest (the priest of the Atharvaveda) in the sacrifice, cosmogonic and mystical speculation (including praise of Om and the gāyatrī), and ritual procedure, partly drawing on and exalting the Atharvaveda's status as the fourth Veda.
SA_GV02_gopthbru.history: Bloomfield argued the Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa is a comparatively late composition, dependent on the Vaitāna-Sūtra and other earlier texts, and produced within Śaunaka circles to give the Atharvaveda a Brāhmaṇa of its own; it was edited by Rajendralala Mitra and Harachandra Vidyabhushana (1872). It is generally counted among the youngest Brāhmaṇas.

SA_GV02_kausibru.title: Kauṣītaki-Brāhmaṇa [Śāṅkhāyana-Brāhmaṇa]
SA_GV02_kausibru.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with the sage Kauṣītaki and his school Śāṅkhāyana)
SA_GV02_kausibru.summary: The Kauṣītaki-Brāhmaṇa, also called the Śāṅkhāyana-Brāhmaṇa, is one of the two Brāhmaṇas of the Ṛgveda (the other being the Aitareya), associated with the Bāṣkala/Kauṣītaki school. In thirty adhyāyas (chapters) and 226 khaṇḍas it treats the fire ritual (Agnyādhāna), the Soma sacrifices (Agniṣṭoma and other Soma rites), and the duties of the Hotṛ priest, presenting its material in a more unified and systematic way than the Aitareya.
SA_GV02_kausibru.history: It is attributed to the sage Kauṣītaki and transmitted by the Śāṅkhāyana school, and Keith dated its codification to roughly 600-400 BCE. It overlaps substantially with the Aitareya-Brāhmaṇa in subject matter but is regarded as somewhat more concise and orderly; its concluding portion connects to the Kauṣītaki Āraṇyaka and Upaniṣad.

SA_GV02_pncvbr1u.title: Pañcaviṃśabrāhmaṇa
SA_GV02_pncvbr1u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV02_pncvbr1u.date_estimate: c. 1st millennium BCE (later than the Taittirīya-Brāhmaṇa); often placed c. 1000-600 BCE
SA_GV02_pncvbr1u.summary: The Pañcaviṃśa-Brāhmaṇa ('Brāhmaṇa of twenty-five [chapters]'), also known as the Tāṇḍya-Mahābrāhmaṇa or Prauḍha-Brāhmaṇa, is the principal Brāhmaṇa of the Sāmaveda, belonging to its Kauthuma and Rāṇāyanīya schools. In twenty-five prapāṭhakas it expounds the duties of the Udgātṛ priests and the various sāman chants, the structure of Soma sacrifices (including the gavāmayana year-long sattra and ekāha, ahīna and sattra rites), and the legendary lore attached to many chants.
SA_GV02_pncvbr1u.history: It is one of the oldest and largest Sāmaveda Brāhmaṇas and is generally placed later than the Taittirīya-Brāhmaṇa but within the older Brāhmaṇa stratum; it was translated into English by W. Caland. It preserves valuable legends (including the famous vrātya hymns) and detailed information on Sāmavedic ritual.

SA_GV03_sb_01_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 1
SA_GV03_sb_01_u.author: anonymous/traditional (the work as a whole is attributed to Yājñavalkya; Kāṇḍas 1-5 closely associated with him)
SA_GV03_sb_01_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 1 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa ('Brāhmaṇa of a hundred paths'), the great prose commentary on the Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā of the Śukla Yajurveda. This first book (the Haviryajña/Ekapādikā kāṇḍa) expounds the New- and Full-Moon sacrifices (darśapūrṇamāsa) and other basic iṣṭi offerings, explaining the meaning, mythology, and rationale of each ritual act.
SA_GV03_sb_01_u.history: The Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa survives in two recensions, Mādhyandina (14 kāṇḍas) and Kāṇva (17 kāṇḍas), and is traditionally ascribed to the sage Yājñavalkya, the patron of the Vājasaneyi school; Julius Eggeling's five-volume translation (SBE) made it famous in the West. It is the largest and most important Brāhmaṇa, a major source for Vedic ritual, cosmology, and early mythology.

SA_GV03_sb_02_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 2
SA_GV03_sb_02_u.author: anonymous/traditional (work attributed to Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV03_sb_02_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 2 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, treating the establishment of the sacred fires (Agnyādheya), the Agnihotra (the obligatory daily fire oblation), the cāturmāsya (seasonal/four-monthly) sacrifices, and the piṇḍapitṛyajña (offerings to the ancestors). It expounds the symbolism and prescribed procedure of these foundational domestic and seasonal rites.
SA_GV03_sb_02_u.history: Part of the Mādhyandina Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa attributed to Yājñavalkya and the Vājasaneyi school of the Śukla Yajurveda; transmitted alongside the Kāṇva recension and translated by Eggeling. It is a primary source for the daily and seasonal Vedic sacrificial cycle.

SA_GV03_sb_04_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 4
SA_GV03_sb_04_u.author: anonymous/traditional (work attributed to Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV03_sb_04_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 4 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, continuing the exposition of the Soma sacrifice. It deals with further details of the Agniṣṭoma and related one-day Soma rites, the various graha (Soma cups) drawn for the gods, the soḍaśin and other chants and recitations, and the concluding rites of the Soma offering.
SA_GV03_sb_04_u.history: Part of the Mādhyandina recension of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa attributed to Yājñavalkya within the Vājasaneyi school of the Śukla Yajurveda; translated by Eggeling in the Sacred Books of the East. Kāṇḍas 1-5 form the first major division of the work and are especially associated with Yājñavalkya.

SA_GV03_sb_05_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 5
SA_GV03_sb_05_u.author: anonymous/traditional (work attributed to Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV03_sb_05_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 5 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, devoted largely to the great royal and complex Soma rites: the Vājapeya ('drink of strength') sacrifice and the Rājasūya (royal consecration). It describes the elaborate procedures, symbolism, and political-cosmological meaning of the rites by which a king is consecrated and asserts sovereignty.
SA_GV03_sb_05_u.history: Part of the Mādhyandina recension attributed to Yājñavalkya and the Vājasaneyi school of the Śukla Yajurveda; translated by Eggeling. These chapters are a key source for understanding early Indian kingship and royal ritual.

SA_GV03_sb_06_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 6
SA_GV03_sb_06_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
SA_GV03_sb_06_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 6 opens the great Agnicayana ('piling of the fire altar') section of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa. It begins the exposition of the construction of the elaborate bird-shaped (falcon) fire altar, including the cosmogonic myth of Prajāpati creating the worlds and identifying himself with the year and the altar, and the symbolic correspondences of the bricks and layers.
SA_GV03_sb_06_u.history: Kāṇḍas 6-10 (the Agnirahasya / Śāṇḍilya kāṇḍas) are attributed in the text to the teacher Śāṇḍilya rather than Yājñavalkya, and are sometimes considered an originally distinct stratum incorporated into the work; translated by Eggeling. The Agnicayana is among the most complex and ancient surviving Vedic rituals.

SA_GV03_sb_07_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 7
SA_GV03_sb_07_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
SA_GV03_sb_07_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 7 continues the Agnicayana (fire-altar) section of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa. It treats the preparation of the altar ground, the laying of the first layer of bricks, the placing of symbolically named bricks (such as the svayamātṛṇṇā, the naturally-perforated brick), and the rich web of cosmic and ritual correspondences associated with building the falcon-shaped altar.
SA_GV03_sb_07_u.history: Part of the Śāṇḍilya (Kāṇḍas 6-10) Agnicayana portion of the Mādhyandina recension, sometimes regarded as a separate compositional layer; translated by Eggeling. The detailed brick symbolism is a major source for Vedic cosmology and proto-geometry (śulba).

SA_GV03_sb_08_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 8
SA_GV03_sb_08_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
SA_GV03_sb_08_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 8 continues the Agnicayana section, describing the laying of the further layers of the fire altar and the placing of additional sets of bricks (including the aśvinī, the ṛtavyā 'seasonal' bricks, and others) with their accompanying formulas. It elaborates the identification of the altar with Prajāpati, the year, the worlds, and the sacrificer himself.
SA_GV03_sb_08_u.history: Part of the Śāṇḍilya Agnicayana portion (Kāṇḍas 6-10) of the Mādhyandina Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa; translated by Eggeling. The intricate brick-and-cosmos symbolism of these chapters is central to the study of Vedic ritual thought.

SA_GV03_sb_09_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 9
SA_GV03_sb_09_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
SA_GV03_sb_09_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 9 completes the building of the fire altar in the Agnicayana and describes the consecration of the finished altar: the Śatarudriya litany to Rudra, the anointing and kindling of the new fire, and the concluding offerings. It explains the symbolism of completing and vivifying the altar as the embodiment of Agni-Prajāpati.
SA_GV03_sb_09_u.history: Concluding portion of the Śāṇḍilya Agnicayana section (Kāṇḍas 6-10) of the Mādhyandina recension; translated by Eggeling. The Śatarudriya here is an important early hymn to Rudra-Śiva.

SA_GV03_sb_10_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 10
SA_GV03_sb_10_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
SA_GV03_sb_10_u.summary: Kāṇḍa 10, the Agnirahasya ('secret/mystery of the fire'), gives the esoteric and speculative interpretation of the Agnicayana. It expounds the hidden symbolic meaning of the fire altar, the identification of Agni with the year, time, and Prajāpati, the building of the altar as the building up of the sacrificer's immortal self, and ideas of death and immortality that anticipate Upaniṣadic thought.
SA_GV03_sb_10_u.history: The final and most mystical book of the Śāṇḍilya Agnicayana section (Kāṇḍas 6-10) of the Mādhyandina recension; translated by Eggeling. Its speculative content forms a bridge from ritual Brāhmaṇa exegesis toward the philosophy of the Āraṇyakas and Upaniṣads.

SA_GV03_sb_11_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 11
SA_GV03_sb_11_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV03_sb_11_u.date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (composite, late Vedic prose)
SA_GV03_sb_11_u.summary: The eleventh kāṇḍa (book) of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa, the great prose commentary on the rituals of the White (Śukla) Yajurveda. This book treats the full or new-moon sacrifices (darśapūrṇamāsa), the establishment of sacred fires (agnyādheya), upanayana (initiation), and includes celebrated cosmogonic and ritual-symbolic narratives. It expounds the meaning, mythology, and procedure behind the sacrificial acts rather than merely prescribing them.
SA_GV03_sb_11_u.history: The Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa ('Brāhmaṇa of a hundred paths') survives in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions and is traditionally associated with Yājñavalkya. Its prose is among the most extensive of the Vedic Brāhmaṇa literature and is a foundational source for early Indian ritual, cosmology, and proto-philosophical speculation that culminates in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad.

SA_GV03_sb_13_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 13
SA_GV03_sb_13_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV03_sb_13_u.date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (composite, late Vedic prose)
SA_GV03_sb_13_u.summary: The thirteenth kāṇḍa of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa, dealing chiefly with the great royal and grand sacrifices: the aśvamedha (horse sacrifice), the puruṣamedha, the sarvamedha, and the pitṛmedha (rites for the ancestors/dead). It gives the procedure and symbolic interpretation of these sovereign and cosmic rituals.
SA_GV03_sb_13_u.history: Part of the most extensive of the Vedic Brāhmaṇas, transmitted in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions of the White Yajurveda and traditionally linked to Yājñavalkya. Its account of the aśvamedha is a key source for the ideology of Vedic kingship.

SA_GV03_sb_14_u.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 14
SA_GV03_sb_14_u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV03_sb_14_u.genre: brahmana / aranyaka / upanishad (Vedic ritual and esoteric exegesis)
SA_GV03_sb_14_u.date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (composite, late Vedic prose)
SA_GV03_sb_14_u.summary: The fourteenth and final kāṇḍa of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa. Its first part expounds the pravargya rite and forms the Āraṇyaka portion, while its concluding adhyāyas constitute the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad, one of the oldest and most important Upaniṣads. Thus this book moves from ritual into the foundational metaphysics of brahman and ātman.
SA_GV03_sb_14_u.history: Because the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad is embedded here, this book is pivotal in the transition from Vedic ritualism to Upaniṣadic philosophy. Transmitted in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions and traditionally connected with the sage Yājñavalkya, who is the central figure of the embedded Upaniṣad.

SA_GV04_sankharu.title: Śāṅkhāyana-Āraṇyaka
SA_GV04_sankharu.author: anonymous/traditional (Śāṅkhāyana/Kauṣītaki school)
SA_GV04_sankharu.summary: The Āraṇyaka of the Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki) school of the Rigveda, comprising fifteen chapters (adhyāyas). It treats the mahāvrata rite, prāṇa (vital-breath) speculation, the symbolism of the uktha, and esoteric ritual doctrine; its central chapters constitute the Kauṣītaki Upaniṣad and its seventh and eighth chapters form the Saṃhitopaniṣad. It bridges ritual practice and meditative-philosophical reflection.
SA_GV04_sankharu.history: Closely parallel to the Aitareya Āraṇyaka of the same Veda, it belongs to the Kauṣītaki/Śāṅkhāyana branch and is a primary witness to Rigvedic Upaniṣadic thought. Edited and translated into English by A. B. Keith.

SA_GV05_agsas_1u.title: Āgamaśāstra 1: Āgamaprakaraṇa
SA_GV05_agsas_1u.author: Gauḍapāda
SA_GV05_agsas_1u.summary: The first chapter (Āgama-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on Tradition', 29 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra, also called the Māṇḍūkya-kārikā or Gauḍapādīya-kārikā. Interwoven with the twelve mantras of the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad, it expounds the four states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep, and turīya) and the syllable OM as the symbol of ātman/brahman, grounding non-dualism in scriptural tradition.
SA_GV05_agsas_1u.history: Gauḍapāda is traditionally regarded as the parama-guru (teacher's teacher) of Śaṅkara and the earliest systematic exponent of Advaita Vedānta. Bhattacharya argued the four prakaraṇas were originally independent treatises later consolidated under the single title Āgamaśāstra; the fourth shows marked Mahāyāna Buddhist influence.

SA_GV05_agsas_2u.title: Āgamaśāstra 2: Vaitathyaprakaraṇa
SA_GV05_agsas_2u.author: Gauḍapāda
SA_GV05_agsas_2u.summary: The second chapter (Vaitathya-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on Unreality/Illusion', 38 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra. Using the analogy of the dream, it argues that the objects of waking experience are as unreal (vaitathya) as dream objects, since both are mental projections, thereby establishing the illusoriness of the phenomenal world.
SA_GV05_agsas_2u.history: One of the four treatises that make up Gauḍapāda's foundational Advaita work. Its dream argument and analysis of unreality parallel and engage Mahāyāna Buddhist (especially Yogācāra/Madhyamaka) reasoning, a much-debated feature of Gauḍapāda's thought.

SA_GV05_agsas_3u.title: Āgamaśāstra 3: Advaitaprakaraṇa
SA_GV05_agsas_3u.author: Gauḍapāda
SA_GV05_agsas_3u.summary: The third chapter (Advaita-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on Non-duality', 48 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra. It establishes the doctrine of non-origination (ajātivāda)—that nothing is ever truly born or destroyed—and presents the absolute non-dual reality of brahman, illustrated by the image of space enclosed in pots.
SA_GV05_agsas_3u.history: Considered the philosophical heart of Gauḍapāda's work, where ajātivāda is most fully developed. This non-origination doctrine became central to later Advaita and shows Gauḍapāda's distinctive synthesis of Upaniṣadic and Buddhist dialectical methods.

SA_GV05_agsas_4u.title: Āgamaśāstra 4: Alātaśāntiprakaraṇa
SA_GV05_agsas_4u.author: Gauḍapāda
SA_GV05_agsas_4u.tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta), with strong Mahāyāna Buddhist coloring
SA_GV05_agsas_4u.summary: The fourth and longest chapter (Alātaśānti-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on the Quenching of the Firebrand', 100 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra. Its title refers to the circle of fire seen when a firebrand (alāta) is whirled—an illusion with no real circle—used to argue that the appearances of consciousness have no real arising. It develops non-origination through dialectical arguments markedly close to Buddhist thought.
SA_GV05_agsas_4u.history: This chapter notably opens with what reads as homage to the Buddha and deploys Madhyamaka- and Yogācāra-style reasoning, making it the focus of scholarly debate over Buddhist influence on early Advaita. Some scholars have even questioned whether it shares authorship with the first three chapters.

SA_GV05_aitup__u.title: Aitareya-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_aitup__u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the seer Aitareya Mahidāsa)
SA_GV05_aitup__u.date_estimate: c. 6th-5th c. BCE (pre-Buddhist, one of the older Upaniṣads)
SA_GV05_aitup__u.summary: One of the principal (mukhya) Upaniṣads, embedded in the Aitareya Āraṇyaka of the Rigveda. It describes the creation of the cosmos and of the human person by the ātman/brahman, the entry of the Self into the body, the three births of a person, and culminates in the mahāvākya 'prajñānam brahma' (consciousness is brahman).
SA_GV05_aitup__u.history: Traditionally ascribed to the sage Mahidāsa Aitareya and counted among the oldest Upaniṣads. It was commented upon by Śaṅkara and other Vedāntic ācāryas and is a standard source-text for the doctrine of brahman as consciousness.

SA_GV05_aitupsbu.title: Aitareya-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_aitupsbu.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the seer Aitareya Mahidāsa)
SA_GV05_aitupsbu.date_estimate: c. 6th-5th c. BCE (text); commentary c. 8th c. CE
SA_GV05_aitupsbu.summary: A second encoded version of the Aitareya Upaniṣad, this work_id (suffix 'sb') likely representing the text as accompanied by Śaṅkara's Bhāṣya. Content is identical to the principal Upaniṣad: cosmic and personal creation from the Self, the three births, and the realization 'prajñānam brahma'.
SA_GV05_aitupsbu.history: Duplicate/recensional entry of the Aitareya Upaniṣad; the variant id signals a distinct edition or the version embedded in a commentary (likely Śaṅkara's). See the parallel entry GV05aitup__u.

SA_GV05_brbiuppu.title: Brahmabindu-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_brbiuppu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_brbiuppu.summary: A short minor Upaniṣad (also called Amṛtabindu Upaniṣad), classed among the Yoga Upaniṣads and attached to the Krishna Yajurveda. In about twenty-two verses it teaches that the mind is the cause of bondage and liberation, distinguishes pure ('śuddha') from impure mind, and advocates meditation on OM and on the silent, attributeless brahman beyond sound.
SA_GV05_brbiuppu.history: One of the corpus of minor Upaniṣads later than the principal Upaniṣads, transmitted within the canon of (commonly) 108 Upaniṣads. Its precise date is unknown; its yogic and nirguṇa-brahman emphasis reflect post-classical Vedānta-Yoga synthesis.

SA_GV05_brup___u.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_brup___u.author: anonymous/traditional (closely associated with the sage Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV05_brup___u.date_estimate: c. 7th-6th c. BCE (one of the oldest Upaniṣads)
SA_GV05_brup___u.summary: The 'Great Forest Teaching', the largest and one of the very oldest of the principal Upaniṣads, forming the concluding part of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa (book 14). It contains the famous dialogues of Yājñavalkya (with King Janaka, with Gārgī, and with his wife Maitreyī), the identification of ātman with brahman, the doctrine 'neti neti', the transmigration of the self, and the prayer 'lead me from the unreal to the real'.
SA_GV05_brup___u.history: A foundational text of Vedānta, repeatedly cited by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva and others, and central to the philosophy of the Self. It survives in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions; this entry represents the whole Upaniṣad (the per-adhyāya splits follow below).

SA_GV05_brupsb.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_brupsb.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV05_brupsb.tradition: Hindu (Vedic / Vedānta; White Yajurveda, Kāṇva recension)
SA_GV05_brupsb.date_estimate: c. 7th-6th c. BCE (one of the oldest Upaniṣads)
SA_GV05_brupsb.summary: The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad as transmitted in/with the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa context (the 'sb' suffix), i.e. the full Upaniṣad embedded in book 14 of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa. Content matches the principal Upaniṣad: the Yājñavalkya dialogues, ātman-brahman identity, 'neti neti', and the doctrine of the self and rebirth.
SA_GV05_brupsb.history: A recensional/encoding variant of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka highlighting its embedding in the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa. The Kāṇva recension is the most widely commented form. See companion per-adhyāya entries.

SA_GV05_brupsb2u.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 2
SA_GV05_brupsb2u.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV05_brupsb2u.summary: The second chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (Śatapatha recension). It includes the dialogue of the proud Gārgya Bālāki with King Ajātaśatru on the nature of brahman, the teaching of the self as the honey (madhu) of all beings, and the first dialogue of Yājñavalkya with his wife Maitreyī on immortality and the love of the Self.
SA_GV05_brupsb2u.history: One of three thematic divisions (the 'Madhu-kāṇḍa') of the Upaniṣad. Heavily commented on by Śaṅkara and subsequent Vedāntins. This is a sectional unit of the larger Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad.

SA_GV05_brupsb3u.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 3
SA_GV05_brupsb3u.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV05_brupsb3u.summary: The third chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad. Set at King Janaka's sacrificial assembly, it presents the famous contest in which Yājñavalkya answers a series of brahmin questioners—including Ārtabhāga, Uṣasta, Kahola, and especially the woman philosopher Gārgī Vācaknavī—on death, the imperishable (akṣara), and the ground of reality.
SA_GV05_brupsb3u.history: Part of the 'Yājñavalkya-kāṇḍa', this chapter is celebrated for its dialectical philosophical debate and for Gārgī as an early female interlocutor. A central source for the apophatic theology of brahman.

SA_GV05_brupsb4u.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 4
SA_GV05_brupsb4u.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV05_brupsb4u.summary: The fourth chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad. It continues Yājñavalkya's instruction of King Janaka on the nature of the self, the states of waking, dream, and deep sleep, the self as 'self-luminous' light, the process of dying and rebirth, and the famous 'neti neti' (not this, not this) characterization of brahman; it closes with the second Maitreyī dialogue.
SA_GV05_brupsb4u.history: Belongs to the 'Yājñavalkya-kāṇḍa'. Its analysis of dream and dreamless sleep and of the transmigrating self deeply shaped later Vedāntic psychology and was extensively interpreted by Śaṅkara.

SA_GV05_brupsb5u.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 5
SA_GV05_brupsb5u.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV05_brupsb5u.summary: The fifth chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad, opening the 'Khila-kāṇḍa' (supplementary book). It comprises a series of shorter teachings and meditations, including the meaning of the syllable OM and the fullness ('pūrṇam adaḥ pūrṇam idam'), the meditation on brahman as the heart, and the celebrated 'da-da-da' parable of Prajāpati teaching self-control (damyata), giving (datta), and compassion (dayadhvam).
SA_GV05_brupsb5u.history: A supplementary, more liturgical and meditative division of the Upaniṣad. The 'da-da-da' passage became famous worldwide through T. S. Eliot's use of it in 'The Waste Land'.

SA_GV05_brupsb6u.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 6
SA_GV05_brupsb6u.author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
SA_GV05_brupsb6u.summary: The sixth and final chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (concluding the Khila-kāṇḍa). It treats the supremacy of the vital breath (prāṇa) among the faculties, the doctrine of the five fires and the two paths of the dead (devayāna and pitṛyāna), rites for conception and the begetting of a learned son, and ends with the lineage (vaṃśa) of teachers transmitting the doctrine.
SA_GV05_brupsb6u.history: Contains the early 'doctrine of the five fires' and rebirth (pañcāgni-vidyā) shared with the Chāndogya Upaniṣad, an important source for theories of transmigration. The closing teacher-lineage documents the chain of oral transmission.

SA_GV05_chupmau.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad (Madhvabhāṣya)
SA_GV05_chupmau.author: Madhva (Madhvācārya / Ānandatīrtha)
SA_GV05_chupmau.summary: Madhvācārya's commentary (bhāṣya) on the Chāndogya Upaniṣad, interpreting this major Sāmavedic Upaniṣad through the lens of Dvaita (dualist) Vedānta. Madhva reads its teachings—including the mahāvākya 'tat tvam asi' and the Sat-Vidyā—as affirming an eternal, real distinction between the supreme Lord (Viṣṇu) and the individual soul, against the Advaitic non-dual reading.
SA_GV05_chupmau.history: Madhva, the 13th-century founder of the Dvaita school, wrote bhāṣyas on the principal Upaniṣads to support his realist, theistic Vedānta; this one was further elaborated by sub-commentators such as Vyāsatīrtha (the Ṭippaṇī). It is a key polemical and exegetical text of the Mādhva tradition.

SA_GV05_garbhupu.title: Garbha-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_garbhupu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_garbhupu.summary: A short minor Upaniṣad attached to the Krishna Yajurveda, distinctive for its embryological and physiological content. It describes the formation and development of the embryo (garbha) month by month in the womb, the composition of the body from the elements and humors, the entry of consciousness into the foetus, and reflections on suffering, rebirth, and liberation that prompt detachment.
SA_GV05_garbhupu.history: Counted among the minor Upaniṣads (and grouped with the few 'physiological' Upaniṣads). It blends Vedāntic and early Āyurvedic-style anatomical and embryological ideas, making it a notable source for ancient Indian conceptions of conception and human development; its precise date is unknown.

SA_GV05_gmandk_u.title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣadkārikā [Gauḍapādakārikā, Māṇḍūkyakārikā, Āgamaśāstra]
SA_GV05_gmandk_u.author: Gauḍapāda
SA_GV05_gmandk_u.date_estimate: c. 6th-7th c. CE (disputed; must predate Bhāvaviveka, before c. 630 CE)
SA_GV05_gmandk_u.summary: A foundational Advaita Vedānta text of 215 verses (kārikās) organized into four chapters (prakaraṇas): Āgama, Vaitathya, Advaita, and Alātaśānti. Expounding the non-duality of Ātman/Brahman, it analyzes the four states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep, and turīya) keyed to the syllable Oṃ, and argues for ajātivāda, the doctrine of non-origination, denying that the world is ever truly created.
SA_GV05_gmandk_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Gauḍapāda, the paramaguru (teacher's teacher) of Śaṅkara, though his identity and exact date are uncertain. The work, also called the Āgamaśāstra, is celebrated as the earliest systematic exposition of Advaita and is noted for its conspicuous engagement with and absorption of Buddhist (especially Madhyamaka/Yogācāra) terminology and argument; the first chapter circulates intertwined with the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad and Śaṅkara wrote a commentary on it.

SA_GV05_isup___u.title: Īśa-Upaniṣad [Īśāvāsyopaniṣad]
SA_GV05_isup___u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_isup___u.date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE or later; among the principal Upaniṣads but dating disputed
SA_GV05_isup___u.summary: One of the shortest principal Upaniṣads, comprising 17-18 verses, forming the closing (fortieth) chapter of the Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā of the Śukla (White) Yajurveda. It teaches that the whole world is pervaded by and to be enveloped in the Lord (īśa), urging renunciation-in-action, the harmonizing of knowledge and ritual works, and a vision of the unity of the Self with all beings.
SA_GV05_isup___u.history: Anonymous and traditionally regarded as revealed (śruti); its placement within the Saṃhitā itself (rather than a Brāhmaṇa or Āraṇyaka) is unusual. It is a much-commented text, with bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara and later Vedāntins, and famous opening invocations; two recensions (Kāṇva and Mādhyandina) survive with minor differences.

SA_GV05_isupsb_u.title: Īśa-Upaniṣad [Īśāvāsyopaniṣad]
SA_GV05_isupsb_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_isupsb_u.date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE or later; dating disputed
SA_GV05_isupsb_u.summary: The Īśā (Īśāvāsya) Upaniṣad, the brief closing chapter of the Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā of the Śukla Yajurveda, teaching that all is pervaded by the Lord and counseling the reconciliation of action and knowledge for liberation. This record appears to be a second copy or a recension/edition (the 'sb' suffix may indicate an associated commentary, e.g. a bhāṣya, but this is uncertain).
SA_GV05_isupsb_u.history: Same text as GV05isup___u; this is a distinct catalogue entry, plausibly a different recension (Kāṇva vs. Mādhyandina) or a version accompanied by a commentary such as Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Heavily commented in the Vedānta tradition.

SA_GV05_isvupu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_isvupu.date_estimate: unknown (likely an Upaniṣad; cannot identify precisely)
SA_GV05_isvupu.summary: No title is supplied in the source metadata. The work_id pattern (GV05isv...) parallels the neighbouring Īśa-Upaniṣad entries (GV05isup...), so this is most plausibly another copy or recension of the Īśā(vāsya) Upaniṣad, but the identification cannot be confirmed from the available data.
SA_GV05_isvupu.history: Identity uncertain owing to the missing title; if it is the Īśā Upaniṣad it shares that text's anonymous śruti status and Vedānta commentarial tradition.

SA_GV05_kaivup_u.title: Kaivalya-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_kaivup_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_kaivup_u.summary: A minor Upaniṣad of 24-26 verses teaching the path to kaivalya (isolation, liberation) through renunciation, faith, devotion and meditation. It identifies the meditator's Self with Śiva/Maheśvara, presenting Rudra-Śiva as the supreme Brahman and Ātman, and famously declares 'I am Brahman' while affirming the unity of the individual self with the divine.
SA_GV05_kaivup_u.history: Anonymous; classified as a Śaiva Upaniṣad and survives in two recensions, one attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda and one to the Atharvaveda. It is dated relatively late among the early Upaniṣads, composed close in spirit to the Śvetāśvatara, Muṇḍaka and Mahānārāyaṇa Upaniṣads.

SA_GV05_mand_upu.title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_mand_upu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_mand_upu.date_estimate: disputed; c. 1st-2nd c. CE or earlier (some place it in the early common era / late Upaniṣadic period)
SA_GV05_mand_upu.summary: The shortest of the principal Upaniṣads, only twelve prose verses, associated with the Atharvaveda. It analyzes the sacred syllable Oṃ (A-U-M) and correlates its parts with the four states of the Self: waking (vaiśvānara), dreaming (taijasa), deep sleep (prājña), and the transcendent fourth (turīya), which is pure non-dual consciousness.
SA_GV05_mand_upu.history: Anonymous śruti; despite its brevity it became immensely influential through Gauḍapāda's Kārikā and Śaṅkara's commentary, and the Muktikā canon ranks it as alone sufficient for liberation. Its terse formulation of the four states is foundational for Advaita Vedānta.

SA_GV05_mandup_u.title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_mandup_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_mandup_u.date_estimate: disputed; c. 1st-2nd c. CE or earlier
SA_GV05_mandup_u.summary: A second catalogue copy/recension of the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad, the twelve-verse Atharvaveda Upaniṣad on Oṃ and the four states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep, turīya). Content is identical to GV05mand_upu.
SA_GV05_mandup_u.history: Anonymous śruti; a duplicate or variant edition of the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad. Famed through Gauḍapāda's Kārikā and Śaṅkara's bhāṣya and central to Advaita Vedānta.

SA_GV05_ndbiuppu.title: Nādabindu-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_ndbiuppu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_ndbiuppu.date_estimate: disputed; c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (Flood) to early 1st millennium CE (Feuerstein)
SA_GV05_ndbiuppu.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad and an early manual of nāda-yoga (the yoga of inner mystic sound). It treats meditation on the praṇava Oṃ, the symbolism of the haṃsa (swan) and its body-parts, and the audition of the unstruck inner sound (anāhata nāda), through which the yogin's mind is absorbed and liberation (mokṣa) attained.
SA_GV05_ndbiuppu.history: Anonymous; attached in traditional listings to the Ṛgveda (some lists to the Atharvaveda) and grouped among the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. It is among the earliest documents on the yoga of sacred sound, influential on later haṭha- and nāda-yoga traditions.

SA_GV05_prasup_u.title: Praśna-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_prasup_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_prasup_u.date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE or later; among the principal/mukhya Upaniṣads
SA_GV05_prasup_u.summary: A principal prose Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda structured as six questions (praśnas) put by six pupils to the sage Pippalāda. It addresses the origin of creatures from prāṇa and rayi (life and matter), the supremacy of prāṇa (breath/vital force), the sixteen parts (kalās) of the person, the states of sleep and dream, and meditation on Oṃ as the way to the supreme Puruṣa.
SA_GV05_prasup_u.history: Anonymous śruti, attached to the Atharvaveda and associated with the Pippalāda school. One of the principal Upaniṣads commented on by Śaṅkara; its question-and-answer format makes it a systematic complement to the closely related Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad.

SA_GV05_prasupbu.title: Praśna-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_prasupbu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_prasupbu.summary: A second catalogue copy of the Praśna Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda, the six-question dialogue with the sage Pippalāda on prāṇa, creation, the sixteen kalās, sleep, and meditation on Oṃ. The 'b' suffix likely indicates an edition accompanied by a bhāṣya (commentary, e.g. Śaṅkara's).
SA_GV05_prasupbu.history: Anonymous śruti; identical text to GV05prasup_u, distinct catalogue entry plausibly bundled with a commentary. A principal Upaniṣad of the Pippalāda Atharvaveda tradition.

SA_GV05_sirup_u.title: Śira-Upaniṣad [Atharvaśira-Upaniṣad]
SA_GV05_sirup_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV05_sirup_u.date_estimate: disputed; some scholars c. 5th c. BCE (predating the Dharmasūtras), others later 1st millennium BCE/CE
SA_GV05_sirup_u.summary: The Atharvaśiras ('head of the Atharva') Upaniṣad, a minor Śaiva Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda centered on the god Rudra. It proclaims that all gods, all beings and all reality are Rudra, who is the innermost essence and highest goal of everything; it also expounds the Paśupata-vrata and the significance of the syllable Oṃ.
SA_GV05_sirup_u.history: Anonymous; one of the cluster of five 'Śiva = Ātman' Upaniṣads and an important early Śaiva/Pāśupata scripture, cited in later ritual and Paśupata literature. Its dating is debated, with some scholars arguing it predates the Dharmasūtras (placing it early), others assigning it a later date.

SA_GV05_svetu_pu.title: Śvetāśvatara-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_svetu_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Śvetāśvatara)
SA_GV05_svetu_pu.date_estimate: c. 4th-2nd c. BCE (last centuries BCE); among the later principal Upaniṣads
SA_GV05_svetu_pu.summary: A principal verse Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, notable as the most theistic of the early Upaniṣads. It develops a personal theology of Rudra-Śiva as the one supreme Lord (Īśa, Deva, Hara), integrating concepts of brahman, sāṃkhya categories, māyā, devotion (bhakti) and yoga, and meditating on the cause of the cosmos.
SA_GV05_svetu_pu.history: Traditionally ascribed to the sage Śvetāśvatara but effectively anonymous. It is a pivotal bridge between the older Vedāntic Upaniṣads and later devotional, Sāṃkhya-Yoga and Śaiva thought, and contains one of the earliest clear statements of monotheistic bhakti; much commented, including by Śaṅkara's school.

SA_GV05_svetugpu.title: Śvetāśvatara-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_svetugpu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Śvetāśvatara)
SA_GV05_svetugpu.summary: A second catalogue copy/recension of the Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad, the theistic Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda Upaniṣad presenting Rudra-Śiva as the supreme Lord and weaving together brahman, sāṃkhya, māyā, yoga and bhakti. Content matches GV05svetu_pu.
SA_GV05_svetugpu.history: Anonymous, ascribed to the sage Śvetāśvatara; a distinct catalogue entry (possibly a different recension or commentary-bearing edition) of this seminal theistic Upaniṣad.

SA_GV06_.title: Pāraskara-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_.author: Pāraskara (traditional)
SA_GV06_.date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period)
SA_GV06_.summary: The principal domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) of the Śukla (White) Yajurveda tradition. In aphoristic sūtra style it prescribes the household sacrifices and life-cycle rites (saṃskāras): the daily fire, marriage, conception, birth, name-giving, upanayana (initiation), Vedic study, the new- and full-moon offerings, and funerary observances.
SA_GV06_.history: Attributed to Pāraskara and forming an appendix to Kātyāyana's Śrautasūtra of the Vājasaneyi (Mādhyandina) school. It is one of the most important and widely used gṛhyasūtras, drawing its mantras from the Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā, and was edited and translated early by Western Indologists (e.g. Stenzler, Oldenberg).

SA_GV06_apsulb_u.title: Āpastamba-Śulbasūtra
SA_GV06_apsulb_u.author: Āpastamba (traditional)
SA_GV06_apsulb_u.tradition: Hindu (Vedic ritual / mathematics), Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda (Taittirīya school)
SA_GV06_apsulb_u.summary: A śulba ('cord/measurement') sūtra belonging to the Āpastamba Kalpasūtra of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, giving the geometric rules for laying out and constructing Vedic fire-altars (vedi, citi) of prescribed shapes and areas. It contains early geometry including statements equivalent to the Pythagorean theorem and a remarkably accurate rational approximation of the square root of 2.
SA_GV06_apsulb_u.history: Part of the Āpastamba school's ritual corpus of the Taittirīya (Black Yajurveda). Together with the Baudhāyana, Mānava and Kātyāyana Śulbasūtras it constitutes the oldest extant geometric literature of India, prized in the history of mathematics for its altar geometry and irrational-number approximations.

SA_GV06_asvgs_u.title: Āśvalāyana-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_asvgs_u.author: Āśvalāyana (traditional)
SA_GV06_asvgs_u.date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period; Oldenberg)
SA_GV06_asvgs_u.summary: The domestic-ritual manual of the Ṛgvedic Āśvalāyana school, prescribing in sūtra form the household sacrifices and life-cycle rites (saṃskāras): marriage, the daily and seasonal offerings, upanayana and Vedic study, conception-to-funeral observances, and rites for prosperity. Its mantras are drawn primarily from the Ṛgveda.
SA_GV06_asvgs_u.history: Attributed to Āśvalāyana, a pupil of Śaunaka, and belonging to the Āśvalāyana Kalpasūtra of the Ṛgveda; it pairs with the Āśvalāyana Śrautasūtra. It is one of the two principal Ṛgvedic gṛhyasūtras (alongside Śāṅkhāyana) and was a key text in early Western Vedic scholarship (the 'not_before' hint 'Oldenberg' refers to Hermann Oldenberg, its translator).

SA_GV06_asvss_u.title: Āśvalāyana-Śrautasūtra
SA_GV06_asvss_u.author: Āśvalāyana (traditional)
SA_GV06_asvss_u.date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period)
SA_GV06_asvss_u.summary: The solemn-sacrifice manual of the Ṛgvedic Āśvalāyana school, codifying in sūtra form the procedures of the great śrauta rituals performed with three fires: the agnihotra, new- and full-moon sacrifices, soma sacrifices, animal offerings and the duties of the hotṛ priest who recites the Ṛgveda.
SA_GV06_asvss_u.history: Attributed to Āśvalāyana (pupil of Śaunaka) and forming, with the Āśvalāyana Gṛhyasūtra, the Kalpasūtra of the Śākala Ṛgveda. It is closely related to the Aitareya Brāhmaṇa and serves as a primary practical guide to Ṛgvedic priestly performance.

SA_GV06_avpari_u.title: Atharvavedapariśiṣṭas
SA_GV06_avpari_u.author: anonymous/traditional (composite)
SA_GV06_avpari_u.date_estimate: composite; tracts ranging across the later Vedic and post-Vedic periods (mid-1st millennium BCE into the early common era)
SA_GV06_avpari_u.summary: A large collection of about 70-74 supplementary tracts (pariśiṣṭas) appended to the Atharvaveda, treating details not covered in the core texts. They cover royal and priestly rituals, the duties of the purohita, omens and portents, astrology and astronomy, propitiatory and magical rites, and other applied liturgical matter.
SA_GV06_avpari_u.history: Anonymous and composite, the Atharvaveda's pariśiṣṭa literature being by far the most extensive among the Vedas. Belonging to different chronological strata, the texts are difficult to date precisely; they are valuable witnesses to popular religion, statecraft and early jyotiṣa associated with the Atharvan tradition.

SA_GV06_avpray_u.title: Atharvaprāyaścittāni
SA_GV06_avpray_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV06_avpray_u.date_estimate: composite, later Vedic to post-Vedic period (mid-1st millennium BCE onward)
SA_GV06_avpray_u.summary: An Atharvavedic text on prāyaścittas, expiatory and atonement rites used to remove sin, ritual error, defilement and inauspiciousness. It prescribes the spells and procedures for expiating faults in sacrifice and in daily life, in keeping with the Atharvaveda's strong concern with apotropaic and corrective magic.
SA_GV06_avpray_u.history: Anonymous; one of the ancillary works (counted among or alongside the Atharvaveda pariśiṣṭas) cited within the Atharvan ancillary literature. Like the broader pariśiṣṭa corpus its precise date is hard to fix, reflecting accreted ritual practice of the Atharvan schools.

SA_GV06_bhasrasuu.title: Bhāradvāja-Śrautasūtra
SA_GV06_bhasrasuu.author: Bhāradvāja (traditional)
SA_GV06_bhasrasuu.date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period)
SA_GV06_bhasrasuu.summary: A solemn-sacrifice (śrauta) manual of the Taittirīya branch of the Kṛṣṇa (Black) Yajurveda, codifying in sūtra form the great fire-rituals: the establishment of the sacred fires, agnihotra, new- and full-moon offerings, soma sacrifices and animal oblations, along with the priestly procedures of the adhvaryu.
SA_GV06_bhasrasuu.history: Attributed to the Bhāradvāja school, one of the seven Śrautasūtra traditions of the Taittirīya Śākhā and reckoned among the earliest sūtrakāras (close to Baudhāyana and preceding/parallel to Āpastamba). It belongs to the Bhāradvāja Kalpasūtra corpus of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda. (The metadata 'not_after: 0' merely flags a BCE composition.)

SA_GV06_dantyovu.title: Dantyoṣṭhyavidhi
SA_GV06_dantyovu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_GV06_dantyovu.summary: A short technical treatise (a lakṣaṇagrantha of the Atharvaveda's ancillary literature) laying down rules for the dental versus labial articulation of sounds, in particular governing where the semivowel 'v' (and related letters) should be pronounced and written as dental (dantya) versus labial (oṣṭhya). It belongs to the body of orthoepic/phonetic aids that fix the correct recitation and spelling of the Vedic text.
SA_GV06_dantyovu.history: It is counted among the lakṣaṇa (phonetic) texts attached to the Atharvaveda alongside the Atharvaveda-Prātiśākhya and related parisiṣṭas; authorship is anonymous and traditional. Such texts were composed to preserve precise oral and written transmission of the Veda and are difficult to date individually.

SA_GV06_higrsuu.title: Hiraṇyakeśi-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_higrsuu.author: attributed to Hiraṇyakeśin (Satyāṣāḍha)
SA_GV06_higrsuu.tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Taittirīya school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
SA_GV06_higrsuu.summary: The domestic-ritual (gṛhya) section of the Hiraṇyakeśi (Satyāṣāḍha) Kalpasūtra of the Taittirīya branch of the Black Yajurveda. It prescribes the household rites of passage and life-cycle ceremonies (saṃskāras such as marriage, birth, initiation, funerary offerings) and recurring domestic observances for a householder of this school.
SA_GV06_higrsuu.history: Forms part of the large Hiraṇyakeśi Kalpasūtra, closely related to the Āpastamba sūtras (Hiraṇyakeśin is traditionally regarded as a reviser within the Āpastamba tradition). It was edited and studied in the Indological tradition (e.g. by J. Kirste; translated in part by Oldenberg in the Sacred Books of the East gṛhyasūtra volumes).

SA_GV06_jaimigsu.title: Jaimini-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_jaimigsu.author: attributed to the Jaiminīya school (trad. Jaimini)
SA_GV06_jaimigsu.tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Jaiminīya school of the Sāmaveda)
SA_GV06_jaimigsu.date_estimate: c. 4th c. BCE (mid first millennium BCE)
SA_GV06_jaimigsu.summary: The domestic-ritual manual of the Jaiminīya (Talavakāra) branch of the Sāmaveda, giving rules for the household rites of passage of Jaiminīya Brahmins. The text is in two halves (about 24 and 9 khaṇḍas), covering saṃskāras and domestic ceremonies.
SA_GV06_jaimigsu.history: Belongs to the Jaiminīya śākhā of the Sāmaveda and was edited and translated by W. Caland (The Jaiminigṛhyasūtra, 1922); a critical edition with Bhavatrāta's Vṛtti and the Gṛhyakārikā was prepared by Asko Parpola. It is one of the principal sources for Sāmavedic domestic ritual.

SA_GV06_kaussu_u.title: Kauśika-Sūtra
SA_GV06_kaussu_u.author: attributed to Kauśika
SA_GV06_kaussu_u.genre: sūtra (gṛhya/ritual manual of magical and domestic rites)
SA_GV06_kaussu_u.date_estimate: c. 5th-3rd c. BCE (oldest of the Atharvavedic ritual sūtras)
SA_GV06_kaussu_u.summary: The principal ritual sūtra of the Atharvaveda, combining domestic (gṛhya) rites with a unique body of magical, medical, and apotropaic practices (sorcery, healing, pacification of omens, royal rites). It supplies the ritual application (viniyoga) of the hymns of the Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā, with about 3008 sūtras across 14 adhyāyas and 141 kaṇḍikās.
SA_GV06_kaussu_u.history: Regarded as the oldest of the Atharvavedic ancillary ritual works; it is foundational for understanding ancient Indian magic and medicine and was edited by Bloomfield (1890) and translated/re-edited in modern scholarship. Commentaries and the Kauśikapaddhati of Keśava explicate its often-elliptical rules.

SA_GV06_kauthgsu.title: Kauthuma-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_kauthgsu.author: anonymous/traditional (Kauthuma school)
SA_GV06_kauthgsu.tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Kauthuma branch of the Sāmaveda)
SA_GV06_kauthgsu.summary: A domestic-ritual manual associated with the Kauthuma śākhā, the principal surviving branch of the Sāmaveda. It prescribes the household saṃskāras and domestic ceremonies for adherents of this school; in practice the Kauthuma tradition's gṛhya rules are closely bound up with the Gobhila-Gṛhyasūtra (and its derivative Khādira-Gṛhyasūtra), the standard domestic sūtras of the Kauthuma/Rāṇāyanīya Sāmavedins.
SA_GV06_kauthgsu.history: The Kauthuma is the main school of the Sāmaveda, alongside the Rāṇāyanīya and Jaiminīya branches, and is still maintained in Gujarat and Tamil Nadu. The domestic ritual of this tradition is represented chiefly by the Gobhila-Gṛhyasūtra; the title 'Kauthuma-Gṛhyasūtra' designates this Kauthuma-school gṛhya material.

SA_GV06_keskaupu.title: Kauśikapaddhati
SA_GV06_keskaupu.author: Keśava
SA_GV06_keskaupu.summary: A practical ritual handbook (paddhati) by Keśava systematizing and explaining the procedures of the Kauśika-Sūtra of the Atharvaveda. It arranges the often-cryptic sūtra rules into usable step-by-step ritual prescriptions for Atharvavedic priests, and is a major aid to interpreting the Kauśika-Sūtra.
SA_GV06_keskaupu.history: Keśava's paddhati (together with the commentary tradition on the Kauśika-Sūtra) was extensively used by Bloomfield and later editors to reconstruct Atharvavedic ritual practice; it is a key medieval link in the transmission of the Atharvaveda's ritual system.

SA_GV06_khagrsuu.title: Khādira-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_khagrsuu.author: attributed to Khādira (a redaction of Gobhila)
SA_GV06_khagrsuu.tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Drāhyāyaṇa/Khādira branch of the Sāmaveda)
SA_GV06_khagrsuu.summary: A concise domestic-ritual manual of the Khādira (Drāhyāyaṇa) school of the Sāmaveda, essentially a condensed and rearranged recension of the Gobhila-Gṛhyasūtra. It sets out the household saṃskāras and domestic ceremonies in a tighter sūtra form.
SA_GV06_khagrsuu.history: Widely recognized as a more compact reworking of Gobhila's gṛhya material for the Khādira/Drāhyāyaṇa branch; it was translated by H. Oldenberg in the Sacred Books of the East series of Gṛhyasūtras. Its close dependence on Gobhila is a standard point in Vedic ritual scholarship.

SA_GV06_manavgsu.title: Mānava-Gṛhyasūtra [Maitrāyaṇīyamānava-Gṛhyasūtra]
SA_GV06_manavgsu.author: anonymous/traditional (Mānava school)
SA_GV06_manavgsu.tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Maitrāyaṇīya/Mānava branch of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
SA_GV06_manavgsu.summary: The domestic-ritual manual of the Mānava (Maitrāyaṇīya) school of the Black Yajurveda, prescribing the household saṃskāras and domestic ceremonies of householders in that tradition. It is notable for some archaic ritual details and is closely associated with the Mānava-Śrautasūtra and the Mānava Dharmaśāstra tradition.
SA_GV06_manavgsu.history: Edited by Friedrich Knauer (1897); it belongs to the Maitrāyaṇīya branch and is studied for its relationship to the broader Mānava sūtra corpus and to early dharma literature. Authorship is anonymous within the school.

SA_GV06_rgvidh_u.title: Ṛgvidhāna
SA_GV06_rgvidh_u.author: attributed (traditionally) to Śaunaka
SA_GV06_rgvidh_u.genre: vidhāna text (manual on ritual/magical application of hymns)
SA_GV06_rgvidh_u.date_estimate: c. last centuries BCE - early centuries CE
SA_GV06_rgvidh_u.summary: A versified manual describing the magical, apotropaic, and ritual uses (vidhāna) of the hymns and verses of the Ṛgveda: which ṛcs to recite, in what manner, and with what accompanying rites to obtain particular ends (healing, protection, prosperity, expiation). It treats the Veda as a repository of power-laden formulas applicable outside the solemn śrauta ritual.
SA_GV06_rgvidh_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Śaunaka and counted among the Ṛgvedic ancillary literature; it parallels the Sāmavidhāna and Atharvavedic vidhāna texts and was edited and translated by M. S. Bhat and earlier by R. Meyer. It is an important witness to popular and magical Vedic religion.

SA_GV06_sankhssu.title: Śāṅkhāyana-Śrautasūtra
SA_GV06_sankhssu.author: attributed to Śāṅkhāyana (Sāṅkhāyana)
SA_GV06_sankhssu.summary: The solemn-sacrifice (śrauta) manual of the Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki) branch of the Ṛgveda, prescribing the great public Vedic sacrifices and the duties of the hotṛ priest and his assistants. It covers the agnihotra, soma sacrifices, and the cycle of śrauta rites with their Ṛgvedic recitations.
SA_GV06_sankhssu.history: Belongs to the Kauṣītaki/Śāṅkhāyana school and is associated with the Kauṣītaki (Śāṅkhāyana) Brāhmaṇa and Āraṇyaka; it was edited by Alfred Hillebrandt and translated by W. Caland. It is one of the two principal Ṛgvedic śrautasūtras (with the Āśvalāyana).

SA_GV06_vaitss_u.title: Vaitāna-Śrautasūtra [Vaitānasūtra]
SA_GV06_vaitss_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Atharvaveda, Śaunaka school)
SA_GV06_vaitss_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-2nd c. BCE (later than the Kauśika-Sūtra)
SA_GV06_vaitss_u.summary: The solemn-sacrifice (śrauta) manual of the Atharvaveda, describing the role of the brahman priest and the Atharvavedic contribution to the great public sacrifices. It supplements the predominantly domestic/magical Kauśika-Sūtra by covering the śrauta ritual side of the Atharvaveda tradition.
SA_GV06_vaitss_u.history: Generally regarded as later than and dependent on the Kauśika-Sūtra; it was edited by R. Garbe and translated into German by W. Caland. It is the principal śrauta text attaching the Atharvaveda to the system of solemn Vedic sacrifice.

SA_GV06_vargs_u.title: Vārāha-Gṛhyasūtra
SA_GV06_vargs_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Vārāha/Maitrāyaṇīya school)
SA_GV06_vargs_u.tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Maitrāyaṇīya branch of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
SA_GV06_vargs_u.summary: The domestic-ritual manual of the Vārāha school of the Maitrāyaṇīya branch of the Black Yajurveda, prescribing the household saṃskāras and domestic ceremonies for that tradition. It is closely related to the Mānava and Kāṭhaka gṛhya material of the same Yajurvedic milieu.
SA_GV06_vargs_u.history: Belongs to the Vārāha recension within the Maitrāyaṇīya tradition and was edited by R. Shamasastry (and studied in relation to the Mānava-Gṛhyasūtra). Authorship is anonymous within the school.

SA_K01_abhisdhu.title: Abhisamācārikadharma
SA_K01_abhisdhu.author: anonymous/traditional (Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda)
SA_K01_abhisdhu.genre: vinaya text (rules of proper conduct / monastic etiquette)
SA_K01_abhisdhu.date_estimate: c. 1st-3rd c. CE (text), reflecting earlier traditions
SA_K01_abhisdhu.summary: A monastic-discipline text (Abhisamācārikā Dharmāḥ, 'rules of proper conduct') of the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda school, laying down the everyday etiquette and behavioral norms for Buddhist monks — how to dress, eat, move, use requisites, and conduct daily monastic life. It complements the Prātimokṣa and Skandhaka by covering customary good conduct rather than formal offenses.
SA_K01_abhisdhu.history: Preserved in a single, very corrupt Sanskrit (Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit) manuscript; first edited in 1969 and re-edited with major reinterpretation by Seishi Karashima in collaboration with Oskar von Hinüber (2012). Its vocabulary is valuable for Indian cultural history.

SA_K01_bhikavau.title: Bhikṣuṇī-Karmavācanā
SA_K01_bhikavau.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_bhikavau.genre: vinaya text (karmavācanā: formulary of monastic legal acts)
SA_K01_bhikavau.date_estimate: early-mid first millennium CE (formulary, reflecting earlier practice)
SA_K01_bhikavau.summary: A formulary (karmavācanā) giving the precise wording of the ecclesiastical legal acts for nuns, above all the procedure for the higher ordination (upasampadā) of bhikṣuṇīs, as practiced in the Mahāsāṃghika(-Lokottaravāda) tradition. It supplies the motions and proclamations a monastic chapter must recite to validly carry out acts of the saṅgha.
SA_K01_bhikavau.history: Such karmavācanā texts were edited from Central Asian and Nepalese Sanskrit manuscripts (e.g. work by Ridding, La Vallée Poussin, and Michael Schmidt); the bhikṣuṇī ordination of the first nuns is described as based on a Mahāsāṃghika karmavācanā and prātimokṣa. It is a key source for the history of nuns' ordination.

SA_K01_bhivin_u.title: Bhikṣuṇīvinaya
SA_K01_bhivin_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda)
SA_K01_bhivin_u.genre: vinaya text (monastic disciplinary code for nuns)
SA_K01_bhivin_u.date_estimate: composed in the early centuries CE, transmitting older material
SA_K01_bhivin_u.summary: The disciplinary code for Buddhist nuns (bhikṣuṇīs) of the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda school, setting out the Prātimokṣa rules specific to nuns together with the narrative occasions for their promulgation. It regulates ordination, the eight gurudharmas, and the offenses and procedures governing the order of nuns.
SA_K01_bhivin_u.history: The Sanskrit Mahāsāṃghika Bhikṣuṇī-Vinaya was edited by Gustav Roth (1970) and translated into English by Akira Hirakawa (1982); it is one of the few extensively preserved Indic-language nuns' Vinayas and a major source for early Buddhist monasticism and women's ordination.

SA_K01_bsa068_u.title: Bhikṣukarmavākya
SA_K01_bsa068_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_bsa068_u.genre: vinaya text (karmavācanā: formulary of monastic legal acts)
SA_K01_bsa068_u.date_estimate: first millennium CE (formulary, transmitting earlier procedure)
SA_K01_bsa068_u.summary: A short formulary giving the wording (karmavākya/karmavācanā) for the ecclesiastical legal acts pertaining to monks (bhikṣus), centrally the procedure for the going-forth (pravrajyā) and higher ordination and other formal acts of the monastic community. It provides the fixed verbal formulas a chapter of monks recites to perform valid saṅgha-acts.
SA_K01_bsa068_u.history: The work_id's title was anonymized in the project metadata; the Sanskrit text (GRETIL/DSBC) is the Bhikṣukarmavākya, edited by A. C. Banerjee in the Indian Historical Quarterly 25 (1949), pp. 19-30 (2nd ed. 1977). It belongs to the karmavācanā genre of ordination/legal-act formularies.

SA_K01_bsu011_u.title: Bodhisattvaprātimokṣasūtra
SA_K01_bsu011_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mahāyāna)
SA_K01_bsu011_u.genre: sūtra (Mahāyāna disciplinary text on bodhisattva ethics)
SA_K01_bsu011_u.summary: A Mahāyāna text that adapts the prātimokṣa (rule-recitation) framework to the bodhisattva path, setting out the ethical discipline (śīla), vows, and precepts that govern a bodhisattva's conduct. It is concerned with the moral training (bodhisattva-saṃvara) that advances a practitioner toward buddhahood rather than with the offenses of ordinary monastic life.
SA_K01_bsu011_u.history: Known from a Sanskrit manuscript discovered in Nepal and edited by Nalinaksa Dutt (1931); it is an important early source for the formulation of bodhisattva precepts and is related to the śīla material of texts such as the Bodhisattvabhūmi.

SA_K01_bsu046_u.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
SA_K01_bsu046_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda)
SA_K01_bsu046_u.genre: sūtra (prātimokṣa: code of monastic rules for recitation)
SA_K01_bsu046_u.date_estimate: core rules early (last centuries BCE); this recension first millennium CE
SA_K01_bsu046_u.summary: The Prātimokṣasūtra is the code of disciplinary rules recited at the fortnightly (uposatha/poṣadha) confession ceremony, enumerating the categories of offenses for fully ordained monks — pārājika, saṃghāvaśeṣa, and the rest — that define monastic discipline. This Sanskrit recension represents the Mūlasarvāstivāda Bhikṣu-Prātimokṣasūtra (preserved partly in Gilgit manuscripts).
SA_K01_bsu046_u.history: The Mūlasarvāstivāda Bhikṣu-Prātimokṣasūtra survives in Sanskrit from Gilgit and Tibetan manuscripts and was critically edited (e.g. by Banerjee, and 'Das Bhikṣu-Prātimokṣasūtra der Mūlasarvāstivādins'). The Prātimokṣa is the disciplinary heart of every Vinaya school and exists in several recensions.

SA_K01_bsu058_u.title: Vinayasūtra [1: Pravrajyāvastu]
SA_K01_bsu058_u.author: Guṇaprabha
SA_K01_bsu058_u.genre: sūtra / śāstra (systematic digest of the Vinaya)
SA_K01_bsu058_u.summary: The opening section, the Pravrajyāvastu ('chapter on going forth'), of Guṇaprabha's Vinayasūtra — a terse, systematic sūtra-style digest condensing the vast Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya. The Pravrajyāvastu treats the rules and procedures for renunciation, novice (śrāmaṇera) and full ordination, and entry into the monastic community.
SA_K01_bsu058_u.history: Guṇaprabha's Vinayasūtra, in seventeen chapters corresponding to the seventeen vastus/topics of the Skandhaka, became the standard scholastic epitome of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya and was hugely influential in India and Tibet (with auto-commentary and later commentaries). Its rediscovery and study mark the revival of Vinaya scholarship ('Guṇaprabha revived the Vinayapiṭaka').

SA_K01_n2853u.title: Śayanāsanavastu
SA_K01_n2853u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda)
SA_K01_n2853u.genre: vinaya text (vastu/skandhaka section on dwellings and furniture)
SA_K01_n2853u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE (Gilgit manuscript c. 6th-7th c. CE)
SA_K01_n2853u.summary: The Śayanāsanavastu ('chapter on bedding and seating'), the 15th section of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, regulating monastic lodgings, dwellings, beds, seats, and furniture — their allotment, use, and the construction and donation of monasteries (vihāras). It includes narrative material on the founding of monastic residences.
SA_K01_n2853u.history: Preserved in the Gilgit Sanskrit manuscripts and edited by Raniero Gnoli (with the Adhikaraṇavastu) as 'The Gilgit Manuscript of the Śayanāsanavastu and the Adhikaraṇavastu' (IsMEO, Serie Orientale Roma, 1978). It is part of the Vinayavastu (Skandhaka) of the Mūlasarvāstivādins and survives also in Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_K01_n2892u.title: Vajrasatvapārājikā [Vajrasattvapārājikā]
SA_K01_n2892u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_n2892u.genre: tantra / vow text (samaya-pārājika, on grave transgressions of tantric vows)
SA_K01_n2892u.date_estimate: later 1st millennium CE; redaction and surviving Nepalese manuscripts c. 11th-17th c. CE (uncertain)
SA_K01_n2892u.summary: A short Vajrayāna scriptural text on the 'pārājika' (gravest, vow-destroying transgressions) of the tantric/bodhisattva commitments (samaya), here framed as the teaching associated with Vajrasattva, the archetypal Buddha and ideal guru of Vajrayāna ritual. Such pārājikā texts enumerate the cardinal downfalls that nullify the practitioner's tantric vows and the means of guarding or restoring them.
SA_K01_n2892u.history: Preserved in the Sanskrit Buddhist manuscript tradition of Nepal (and digitized in the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon). It belongs to the same conceptual sphere as the well-known Vajrayāna mūlāpatti (root-downfall) literature (e.g. Aśvaghoṣa's Vajrayānamūlāpattisaṅgraha listing fourteen root downfalls). Its precise authorship and date are not securely established.

SA_K01_n2894u.title: Saddharmapārājikā
SA_K01_n2894u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_n2894u.genre: tantra / vow text (on grave transgressions, pārājika)
SA_K01_n2894u.date_estimate: later 1st millennium CE; surviving Nepalese manuscripts medieval (uncertain)
SA_K01_n2894u.summary: A short Vajrayāna text on the gravest, vow-destroying transgressions (pārājika) within the Mahāyāna-Tantric framework, here keyed to the 'Saddharma' (the True Dharma). Like its companion pārājikā texts it warns against and enumerates the cardinal downfalls that destroy a practitioner's bodhisattva and tantric commitments.
SA_K01_n2894u.history: Transmitted in the Sanskrit Buddhist manuscript collections of Nepal and included in the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon. It is part of a small cluster of related 'pārājikā' scriptures (Vajrasattva-, Mañjuśrī-, Lokeśvara-, Śrīlokeśvarabhāṣita-) of uncertain individual authorship and date.

SA_K01_n2896u.title: Śrīlokeśvarabhāṣitaparājikā
SA_K01_n2896u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in title to the teaching of Śrī Lokeśvara/Avalokiteśvara)
SA_K01_n2896u.date_estimate: later 1st millennium CE; surviving Nepalese manuscripts medieval (uncertain)
SA_K01_n2896u.summary: A short Vajrayāna scripture, framed as 'spoken by the glorious Lokeśvara' (Avalokiteśvara), on the pārājika — the grave, vow-destroying transgressions of the tantric/bodhisattva path. It belongs to the genre that enumerates cardinal downfalls and the duty to guard one's samaya vows.
SA_K01_n2896u.history: Avalokiteśvara/Lokeśvara is the central cultic deity of Newar Buddhism in the Kathmandu Valley, which makes a Lokeśvara-attributed scripture characteristic of that milieu. The text survives in Nepalese Sanskrit manuscripts (Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon). Its authorship and date are not securely established and it is closely related to the other pārājikā texts in this group.

SA_K01_n2900u.title: Maṃjuśrīpārājikā [Mañjuśrīpārājikā]
SA_K01_n2900u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in title to Mañjuśrī)
SA_K01_n2900u.date_estimate: later 1st millennium CE; surviving Nepalese manuscripts medieval (uncertain)
SA_K01_n2900u.summary: A short Vajrayāna text, associated with the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī, on the pārājika — the gravest transgressions that destroy a practitioner's tantric and bodhisattva vows. It enumerates and warns against the cardinal downfalls of the path.
SA_K01_n2900u.history: Preserved in Nepalese Sanskrit manuscript tradition and digitized in the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon. Part of a closely related cluster of pārājikā scriptures, each framed around a major Vajrayāna deity; individual authorship and date are not established.

SA_K01_n2904u.title: Lokeśvarapārājikā
SA_K01_n2904u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in title to Lokeśvara/Avalokiteśvara)
SA_K01_n2904u.date_estimate: later 1st millennium CE; surviving Nepalese manuscripts medieval (uncertain)
SA_K01_n2904u.summary: A short Vajrayāna scripture associated with Lokeśvara (Avalokiteśvara) on the pārājika — the grave, vow-destroying transgressions of the tantric/bodhisattva commitments. It belongs to the genre enumerating the cardinal downfalls that nullify samaya.
SA_K01_n2904u.history: Closely related to the Śrīlokeśvarabhāṣitaparājikā (the two may represent variant or paired recensions of a Lokeśvara-centered pārājika text). Avalokiteśvara is the principal deity of Newar Buddhism, the milieu in which these Sanskrit manuscripts were transmitted (Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon). Authorship and date are unsettled.

SA_K01_n2954u.title: Upasampadājñapti
SA_K01_n2954u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya tradition; edited by B. Jinananda)
SA_K01_n2954u.genre: vinaya / monastic legal formulary (karmavācanā for ordination)
SA_K01_n2954u.date_estimate: Vinaya material c. 1st-4th c. CE (formative); manuscript transmission later 1st millennium CE
SA_K01_n2954u.summary: A monastic legal text giving the formal motion (jñapti) and legal acts (karmavācanā) for the higher ordination (upasampadā) of a Buddhist monk in the Mūlasarvāstivāda tradition. It sets out the precisely prescribed wording the saṅgha must recite to confer full monastic status.
SA_K01_n2954u.history: An extract from the procedural (karmavācanā/karmavastu) layer of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya. The Sanskrit Upasampadājñaptiḥ was edited by B. Jinananda (Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series, Patna, 1961) from Gilgit/Tibetan-area material. Such formularies were copied independently across the Buddhist world as working ritual texts.

SA_K01_n742u.title: Bhikṣuṇīvinaya
SA_K01_n742u.author: anonymous/traditional (Ārya-Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin recension; ed. Gustav Roth)
SA_K01_n742u.date_estimate: code formed in the early centuries CE; the recension's wording often judged comparatively archaic; surviving manuscript later
SA_K01_n742u.summary: The disciplinary code (Vinaya) governing Buddhist nuns (bhikṣuṇī) of the Ārya-Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin school, including the eight 'weighty rules' (gurudharma), the Bhikṣuṇī-prātimokṣa-vibhaṅga (rule-by-rule analysis), and the Bhikṣuṇī-prakīrṇaka (miscellaneous regulations). It regulates ordination, conduct, communal procedure and the relations between nuns and the monks' order.
SA_K01_n742u.history: Edited for the first time by Gustav Roth as 'Bhikṣuṇī-Vinaya' (K.P. Jayaswal Research Institute, Patna, 1970) from a Tibetan-transmitted Sanskrit manuscript, together with a summary of the Bhikṣu-prakīrṇaka. It is a major source for the comparative study of nuns' Vinaya, its formulations sometimes preserving an older, less streamlined wording than parallel recensions.

SA_K01_prmosu_u.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
SA_K01_prmosu_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda recension)
SA_K01_prmosu_u.genre: vinaya / prātimokṣa (code of monastic precepts for fortnightly recitation)
SA_K01_prmosu_u.date_estimate: core c. 3rd c. BCE onward, redacted in this recension by early centuries CE; Sanskrit manuscript transmission mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_prmosu_u.summary: The Prātimokṣasūtra is the core list of monastic precepts recited communally at the fortnightly poṣadha ceremony. The Mūlasarvāstivāda bhikṣu version comprises 4 pārājika (defeats), 13 saṃghāvaśeṣa (suspensions), 2 aniyata, 30 naiḥsargika-prāyaścittika, 90 prāyaścittika, 4 pratideśanīya, numerous śaikṣa (training) rules and 7 adhikaraṇaśamatha, recited so monks may confess and purify transgressions.
SA_K01_prmosu_u.history: This variant (file marker '_') is one of several Prātimokṣasūtra recensions in the corpus. The Mūlasarvāstivāda Prātimokṣa survives in Sanskrit (notably the Gilgit manuscripts), Tibetan and Chinese; it is the basis of the still-living ordination lineages of Tibetan and East Asian Buddhism.

SA_K01_prmosubu.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
SA_K01_prmosubu.author: anonymous/traditional (Buddhist Vinaya recension)
SA_K01_prmosubu.tradition: Buddhist (Vinaya; likely Mūlasarvāstivāda, marker 'b' possibly bhikṣu/variant)
SA_K01_prmosubu.date_estimate: core c. 3rd c. BCE onward, recension redacted by early centuries CE; manuscript transmission mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_prmosubu.summary: A recension/variant of the Prātimokṣasūtra, the list of monastic precepts recited at the fortnightly poṣadha. It enumerates the categories of offence (pārājika, saṃghāvaśeṣa, aniyata, naiḥsargika-prāyaścittika, prāyaścittika, pratideśanīya, śaikṣa, adhikaraṇaśamatha) by which monastics review and confess their conduct.
SA_K01_prmosubu.history: One of several near-identical Prātimokṣasūtra files in this corpus (markers '_', 'b', 'f', 'l') that likely represent different editions, recensions, or manuscript witnesses. The marker 'bu' may indicate a bhikṣu (monk) version. The Prātimokṣa is among the oldest strata of Buddhist canonical literature.

SA_K01_prmosufu.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
SA_K01_prmosufu.author: anonymous/traditional (Buddhist Vinaya recension)
SA_K01_prmosufu.date_estimate: core c. 3rd c. BCE onward, recension redacted by early centuries CE; manuscript transmission mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_prmosufu.summary: A recension/variant of the Prātimokṣasūtra, the catalogue of monastic precepts recited fortnightly at the poṣadha ceremony, organized by descending gravity of offence. It functions as the confessional checklist by which the saṅgha maintains and purifies its discipline.
SA_K01_prmosufu.history: One of several Prātimokṣasūtra witnesses in this corpus (markers '_', 'b', 'f', 'l'), probably distinct editions or manuscript copies of the same Vinaya code. The text is foundational to all Buddhist monastic ordination traditions.

SA_K01_prmosulu.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
SA_K01_prmosulu.author: anonymous/traditional (Buddhist Vinaya recension)
SA_K01_prmosulu.date_estimate: core c. 3rd c. BCE onward, recension redacted by early centuries CE; manuscript transmission mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_prmosulu.summary: A recension/variant of the Prātimokṣasūtra, the list of monastic precepts recited at the fortnightly poṣadha, arranged by classes of offence from the four pārājika downward. It is recited so that monastics may confess and restore their discipline.
SA_K01_prmosulu.history: One of several Prātimokṣasūtra files in this corpus (markers '_', 'b', 'f', 'l') that likely correspond to different editions, recensions, or manuscript witnesses of the code. The Prātimokṣa belongs to the earliest layer of Buddhist monastic literature.

SA_K01_upsmpjnu.title: Upasaṃpadājñapti
SA_K01_upsmpjnu.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya; ed. B. Jinananda)
SA_K01_upsmpjnu.genre: vinaya / monastic legal formulary (karmavācanā for higher ordination)
SA_K01_upsmpjnu.date_estimate: Vinaya material c. 1st-4th c. CE (formative); manuscript transmission later 1st millennium CE
SA_K01_upsmpjnu.summary: The formulary of the motion (jñapti) and the legal acts (karmavācanā) required to confer the higher ordination (upasampadā) of a monk in the Mūlasarvāstivāda tradition. It records the exact wording the assembled saṅgha must recite to admit a candidate to full monastic status.
SA_K01_upsmpjnu.history: Drawn from the procedural layer of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya; the Sanskrit Upasampadājñaptiḥ was edited by B. Jinananda (Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series, Patna, 1961). This is a duplicate/parallel entry to the Upasampadājñapti (K01n2954u) in the same corpus.

SA_K01_vinsutru.title: Vinayasūtra
SA_K01_vinsutru.author: Guṇaprabha
SA_K01_vinsutru.genre: vinaya / sūtra-style digest (mūla / root aphoristic treatise)
SA_K01_vinsutru.date_estimate: c. 5th-7th c. CE (Guṇaprabha; dating disputed, sometimes placed as late as the early medieval period)
SA_K01_vinsutru.summary: A highly condensed, aphoristic (sūtra-style) digest of the entire Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, covering the Prātimokṣa, the Vibhaṅga (rule analysis), and the Skandhaka/Vastu sections. Its terse statements were designed as mnemonic summaries of monastic law, with chapters corresponding to the topics of the Vinayavastu.
SA_K01_vinsutru.history: Composed by the Indian scholar-monk Guṇaprabha and transmitted with an auto-commentary (Svavyākhyāna) and commentaries such as Dharmamitra's Ṭīkā; translated into Tibetan by Jinamitra and Cog-ro Klu'i rgyal-mtshan and central to the monastic curriculum of Tibetan Buddhism. The Sanskrit text has been recovered in part from Tibetan-area manuscripts.

SA_K01_vinv01_u.title: Pravrajyāvastu
SA_K01_vinv01_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv01_u.genre: vinaya (Vinayavastu chapter on going-forth and ordination)
SA_K01_vinv01_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE; Sanskrit (Gilgit) manuscripts mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_vinv01_u.summary: The first chapter (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu, dealing with 'going forth' (pravrajyā) into the monastic life and the procedures of admission and ordination. It sets out who may be ordained, the disqualifications, and the legal acts for novice (pravrajyā) and full ordination, interwoven with narrative.
SA_K01_vinv01_u.history: Part of the Vinayavastu (Skandhaka), one of the four divisions of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, transmitted in Sanskrit (Gilgit manuscripts), Tibetan (Toh 1, 'The Chapter on Going Forth') and Chinese. The Sanskrit Pravrajyāvastu has been critically edited and is widely studied.

SA_K01_vinv02_u.title: Poṣadhavastu
SA_K01_vinv02_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv02_u.genre: vinaya (Vinayavastu chapter on the poṣadha observance)
SA_K01_vinv02_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE; Sanskrit (Gilgit) manuscripts mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_vinv02_u.summary: The Vinayavastu chapter governing the poṣadha (uposatha), the fortnightly communal observance at which monastics assemble, recite the Prātimokṣa, and confess transgressions. It regulates the boundary (sīmā), the quorum, and the procedure of the ceremony that maintains the unity and purity of the saṅgha.
SA_K01_vinv02_u.history: One of the topics (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu (Skandhaka), preserved in Sanskrit (Gilgit), Tibetan and Chinese. It corresponds to the Uposathakkhandhaka of the Pali Vinaya and is a key source for monastic communal law.

SA_K01_vinv03_u.title: Pravāraṇāvastu
SA_K01_vinv03_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv03_u.genre: vinaya (Vinayavastu chapter on the pravāraṇā ceremony)
SA_K01_vinv03_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE; Sanskrit (Gilgit) manuscripts mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_vinv03_u.summary: The Vinayavastu chapter on the pravāraṇā, the 'invitation' ceremony held at the close of the rains-retreat at which each monk invites the community to point out any faults he has committed. It regulates the procedure by which the saṅgha collectively reviews conduct before dispersing.
SA_K01_vinv03_u.history: A topic (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu (Skandhaka), preserved in Sanskrit (Gilgit), Tibetan and Chinese, corresponding to the Pavāraṇākkhandhaka of the Pali Vinaya. It is closely paired with the Varṣāvastu on the rains-retreat.

SA_K01_vinv04_u.title: Varṣāvastu
SA_K01_vinv04_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv04_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE; Sanskrit (Gilgit) manuscripts mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_vinv04_u.summary: The Vinayavastu chapter (also called Vārṣikavastu) regulating the varṣā, the obligatory three-month rains-retreat during which monastics remain settled in one residence. It sets out the rules for entering and observing the retreat and the permitted reasons for temporary absence.
SA_K01_vinv04_u.history: A topic (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu (Skandhaka), preserved in Sanskrit (Gilgit), Tibetan and Chinese, corresponding to the Vassūpanāyikakkhandhaka of the Pali Vinaya. The rains-retreat structures the monastic year and is closely linked to the following Pravāraṇā and Kaṭhina observances.

SA_K01_vinv05_u.title: Carmavastu
SA_K01_vinv05_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv05_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE; Sanskrit (Gilgit) manuscripts mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_vinv05_u.summary: The Vinayavastu chapter on leather and hides (carman), regulating monastics' use of skins, footwear and leather articles — what is permitted, what is prohibited, and under what conditions. The chapter is famously prefaced by the narrative of the monk Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, which frames the relaxation of certain rules in border regions.
SA_K01_vinv05_u.history: A topic (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu (Skandhaka), preserved in Sanskrit (Gilgit), Tibetan and Chinese, corresponding to the Cammakkhandhaka of the Pali Vinaya. In some enumerations of the vastus the order of Carma- and Cīvara-vastu varies.

SA_K01_vinv07_u.title: Cīvaravastu
SA_K01_vinv07_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv07_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE; Sanskrit (Gilgit) manuscripts mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K01_vinv07_u.summary: The Vinayavastu chapter on monastic robes (cīvara), regulating the acquisition, allotment, making, and ownership of robes, the handling of the robes of deceased monks, and related material matters. It contains substantial narrative material alongside its legal prescriptions.
SA_K01_vinv07_u.history: A topic (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu (Skandhaka), preserved in Sanskrit (Gilgit), Tibetan and Chinese, corresponding to the Cīvarakkhandhaka of the Pali Vinaya. Bilingual Sanskrit–Tocharian fragments containing extracts from the Cīvaravastu are attested, indicating its wide circulation in Central Asia.

SA_K01_vinv08_u.title: Kaṭhinavastu
SA_K01_vinv08_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
SA_K01_vinv08_u.genre: vinaya (Vinayavastu chapter on the kaṭhina robe ceremony)
SA_K01_vinv08_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE; Sanskrit (Gilgit) manuscripts mid-1st millennium CE
