SA_K01_vinv08_u.summary: The Vinayavastu chapter on the kaṭhina, the ceremony following the rains-retreat in which the laity offer cloth that the community formally makes up and 'spreads' as a robe, thereby earning certain temporary relaxations of the robe rules. It regulates the conditions, performance, and lapsing of the kaṭhina privileges.
SA_K01_vinv08_u.history: A topic (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu (Skandhaka), preserved in Sanskrit (Gilgit), Tibetan and Chinese, corresponding to the Kaṭhinakkhandhaka of the Pali Vinaya. It links the rains-retreat cycle (Varṣā, Pravāraṇā) with the robe rules of the Cīvaravastu.

SA_K01_vinv09_u.title: Kośāmbakavastu
SA_K01_vinv09_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv09_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv09_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv09_u.summary: The Kośāmbaka(vastu) is the ninth of the seventeen vastus (topical chapters) of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya. Set against the famous quarrel of the monks of Kauśāmbī (Kosambi), it deals with schism and dissension within the saṅgha and the disciplinary procedures and reconciliation pertaining to monks who have split into factions. Like other vastus it embeds narrative frame-stories around the relevant ecclesiastical rules.
SA_K01_vinv09_u.history: The Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya is preserved in full in Tibetan and Chinese translation and partially in Sanskrit through the Gilgit manuscripts discovered in 1931. Gregory Schopen and others place the school's literary activity roughly in the early centuries CE; the vinaya is a major source for the social and institutional history of north Indian Buddhist monasticism.

SA_K01_vinv10_u.title: Karmavastu
SA_K01_vinv10_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv10_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv10_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv10_u.summary: The Karmavastu is the tenth of the seventeen vastus of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. It deals exclusively with ecclesiastical legal acts (saṅghakarman): the formal procedures, quorums, motions (jñapti) and proclamations (karmavācanā) by which the monastic community conducts its formal business. It is largely technical, full of the formulaic repetitions and definitions of vinaya terminology characteristic of the genre.
SA_K01_vinv10_u.history: Part of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, surviving in Tibetan and Chinese and partly in the Gilgit Sanskrit manuscripts. The school's vinaya is the most important corpus for reconstructing the practice and legal procedure of north Indian Buddhist monasteries.

SA_K01_vinv11_u.title: Pāṇḍulohitakavastu
SA_K01_vinv11_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv11_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv11_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv11_u.summary: The Pāṇḍulohitakavastu is the eleventh of the seventeen vastus of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. Named after the monks Pāṇḍuka and Lohitaka (troublesome monks of the canonical tradition), it treats the disciplinary acts (karmans) imposed on quarrelsome or misbehaving monks, including acts of censure, guidance, banishment, reconciliation and suspension.
SA_K01_vinv11_u.history: A section of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, transmitted in Tibetan and Chinese and partly in Gilgit Sanskrit. It complements the Karmavastu by giving the disciplinary applications of saṅgha legal procedure.

SA_K01_vinv12_u.title: Pudgalavastu
SA_K01_vinv12_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv12_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv12_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv12_u.summary: The Pudgalavastu is the twelfth of the seventeen vastus of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. It concerns the treatment of particular categories of individuals (pudgala) within the disciplinary system, especially the handling of an offending monk depending on his attitude and the probationary or rehabilitative measures applied to him.
SA_K01_vinv12_u.history: A section of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, preserved in Tibetan and Chinese and partly in the Gilgit Sanskrit manuscripts. It forms part of the cluster of vastus (Karma, Pāṇḍulohitaka, Pudgala, Pārivāsika) dealing with monastic discipline and penance.

SA_K01_vinv13_u.title: Pārivāsikavastu
SA_K01_vinv13_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv13_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv13_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv13_u.summary: The Pārivāsikavastu is the thirteenth of the seventeen vastus of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. It deals with the parivāsa, the period of probation imposed on a monk who has committed a saṅghāvaśeṣa offence, together with the related penance (mānatva) and rehabilitation (abhyarhaṇa/āvarhaṇa) by which he is restored to full standing in the community.
SA_K01_vinv13_u.history: A section of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, surviving in Tibetan and Chinese and partly in Gilgit Sanskrit. It details one of the central penitential mechanisms of monastic law.

SA_K01_vinv14_u.title: Poṣadhasthāpanavastu
SA_K01_vinv14_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv14_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv14_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv14_u.summary: The Poṣadhasthāpanavastu is the fourteenth of the seventeen vastus of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. It concerns the suspension or stopping (sthāpana) of the fortnightly poṣadha (uposatha) confession ceremony when a monk known to be impure or guilty of an offence is present, and the procedures for excluding such a monk so that the recitation of the Prātimokṣa may proceed.
SA_K01_vinv14_u.history: A section of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, preserved in Tibetan and Chinese and partly in Gilgit Sanskrit. It complements the Poṣadhavastu (the second vastu) which establishes the poṣadha observance itself.

SA_K01_vinv15_u.title: Śayanāsanavastu
SA_K01_vinv15_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv15_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv15_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv15_u.summary: The Śayanāsanavastu is the fifteenth of the seventeen vastus of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. It concerns 'beds and seats' (śayanāsana), i.e. the monastery's furniture, lodgings and the allocation and administration of dwelling places, including the duties of the monk-officer in charge of dwellings and the management of monastic property and construction.
SA_K01_vinv15_u.history: Edited from the Gilgit manuscript together with the Adhikaraṇavastu by Raniero Gnoli (Serie Orientale Roma L, 1978). The vastu is an important source for the economic and material life of north Indian monasteries.

SA_K01_vinv16_u.title: Adhikaraṇavastu
SA_K01_vinv16_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv16_u.genre: vinaya (monastic law), section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv16_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv16_u.summary: The Adhikaraṇavastu is the sixteenth of the seventeen vastus of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. It treats adhikaraṇa, formal disputes and litigation within the saṅgha, classifying the types of monastic disputes and setting out the seven methods (adhikaraṇa-śamatha) for settling them.
SA_K01_vinv16_u.history: Edited from the Gilgit Sanskrit manuscript together with the Śayanāsanavastu by Raniero Gnoli (Serie Orientale Roma L, 1978); also preserved in Tibetan and Chinese. It is a key text for understanding Buddhist legal conflict resolution.

SA_K01_vinv171u.title: Saṅghabhedavastu 1
SA_K01_vinv171u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv171u.genre: vinaya (monastic law) / Buddha-biography, section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv171u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv171u.summary: The Saṅghabhedavastu is the seventeenth and final vastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu; this is the first of its two parts (as divided in the editions/the Gilgit manuscript). Although its nominal topic is 'schism of the community' (saṅghabheda), the vastu is famous for embedding a vast biography of the Buddha and the early saṅgha: this first portion contains the genealogy of the Śākyas and the life of the Buddha up to his teaching career, providing the narrative setting that culminates in Devadatta's schism.
SA_K01_vinv171u.history: Edited from the Gilgit manuscript in two volumes by Raniero Gnoli and T. Venkatacharya (Serie Orientale Roma XLIX, 1977-1978). The Saṅghabhedavastu is one of the most important sources for the Mūlasarvāstivāda recension of the Buddha's life story.

SA_K01_vinv172u.title: Saṅghabhedavastu 2
SA_K01_vinv172u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K01_vinv172u.genre: vinaya (monastic law) / Buddha-biography, section of the Vinayavastu
SA_K01_vinv172u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (redaction); Sanskrit Gilgit manuscript 5th-6th c. CE
SA_K01_vinv172u.summary: This is the second part of the Saṅghabhedavastu, the seventeenth and last vastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu. It continues the narrative of the Buddha's career and the early community, leading up to and treating in detail the schism (saṅghabheda) provoked by Devadatta, his rivalry with the Buddha, his attempts to take over the saṅgha, and the rules concerning the grave offence of splitting the community.
SA_K01_vinv172u.history: Second volume of Gnoli and Venkatacharya's edition of the Gilgit manuscript (Serie Orientale Roma XLIX, 1977-1978). Together with part 1 it forms the longest single vastu and a major Mūlasarvāstivāda life-of-the-Buddha narrative.

SA_K02_bsu057_u.title: Advayaśatikā Prajñāpāramitāsūtra
SA_K02_bsu057_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K02_bsu057_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-7th c. CE (one of the shorter, later Prajñāpāramitā texts); disputed
SA_K02_bsu057_u.summary: The Advayaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā ('Perfection of Wisdom in 150 [verses], the Non-Dual') is one of the shorter Prajñāpāramitā sūtras. It presents, in compact form, the Buddha teaching Subhūti the perfection of wisdom understood through non-duality (advaya): emptiness, the non-arising of dharmas, and the realization that wisdom is inseparable from all phenomena. Like other short Prajñāpāramitā texts it distils the central teaching of the larger sutras into a brief, recitable scripture.
SA_K02_bsu057_u.history: One of the condensed Prajñāpāramitā scriptures that proliferated after the foundational Aṣṭasāhasrikā; it was translated into Chinese by Dānapāla around 980-1000 CE, which gives a terminus ante quem for the Sanskrit text. It belongs to the broad family of short Perfection of Wisdom sūtras that includes the Heart and Diamond Sutras.

SA_K02_sspp2_1u.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-1 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K02_sspp2_1u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K02_sspp2_1u.date_estimate: c. 1st-4th c. CE (large-sutra expansion of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā); disputed
SA_K02_sspp2_1u.summary: The Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā ('Perfection of Wisdom in 100,000 Lines') is the largest of the Mahāyāna Perfection of Wisdom sutras. This record (II-1) is the first part of the second major division (parivarta) of the text. The work expounds at vast length the bodhisattva's practice of the six perfections, the emptiness (śūnyatā) and non-substantiality of all dharmas, and the path to omniscient buddhahood, through long dialogues between the Buddha, Subhūti, Śāradvatīputra and Maitreya.
SA_K02_sspp2_1u.history: A later, greatly expanded elaboration of the foundational Aṣṭasāhasrikā (8,000-line) Prajñāpāramitā, developed roughly between the 1st and 4th centuries CE, probably in Gandhāra and northwestern India. It survives in Nepalese Sanskrit manuscripts and in Tibetan (translated by Yeshe De, c. 8th-9th c.) and Chinese; the threefold division into 100,000/25,000/18,000-line versions is attested from the time of Bodhiruci (6th c.).

SA_K02_sspp2_2u.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-2 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K02_sspp2_2u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K02_sspp2_2u.summary: Second part of the second major division (II-2) of the Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, the 100,000-line Perfection of Wisdom sutra. It continues the exhaustive exposition of emptiness and the bodhisattva path, treating the non-attachment, non-apprehension (anupalabdhi) and sameness of all dharmas, and the qualities and trainings of the bodhisattva-mahāsattva, in the highly repetitive cumulative style characteristic of the largest Prajñāpāramitā.
SA_K02_sspp2_2u.history: Part of the same 100,000-line text; a sectional division used in the edition/manuscript. See Śatasāhasrikā II-1 for the work's overall composition history.

SA_K02_sspp2_3u.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-3 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K02_sspp2_3u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K02_sspp2_3u.summary: Third part of the second major division (II-3) of the Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, the 100,000-line Perfection of Wisdom sutra. It continues the vast catechetical treatment of the perfection of wisdom, elaborating the emptiness of the aggregates, elements and all categories of dharmas, and the bodhisattva's training in non-apprehension and skillful means.
SA_K02_sspp2_3u.history: A sectional division of the 100,000-line text; see Śatasāhasrikā II-1 for overall history and dating.

SA_K02_sspp2_4u.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-4 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K02_sspp2_4u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K02_sspp2_4u.summary: Fourth part of the second major division (II-4) of the Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, the 100,000-line Perfection of Wisdom sutra. It concludes the long second division, continuing the exposition of the bodhisattva's perfection of wisdom, the emptiness and equality of all dharmas, and the attainment of unsurpassed perfect awakening (anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhi).
SA_K02_sspp2_4u.history: A sectional division of the 100,000-line text; see Śatasāhasrikā II-1 for overall history and dating.

SA_K03_psp_1u.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā I [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K03_psp_1u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K03_psp_1u.date_estimate: c. 1st-4th c. CE (large-sutra expansion of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā); disputed
SA_K03_psp_1u.summary: The Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā ('Perfection of Wisdom in 25,000 Lines') is the 'middle-length' Large Perfection of Wisdom sutra, second longest of the three. This record (Part I) is its first division. It expounds the bodhisattva's six perfections, the emptiness and non-apprehension of all dharmas, and the path to omniscience, through extended dialogues with Subhūti and Śāriputra.
SA_K03_psp_1u.history: A later expansion of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā, sharing a common source with the 18,000-line Aṣṭadaśasāhasrikā (per Shogo Watanabe). It became the basis of Asaṅga/Maitreya's Abhisamayālaṃkāra exegesis and was 're-written' into chapters matching that treatise. It survives in Sanskrit (Nepal, Kashmir) and in Tibetan and Chinese translation.

SA_K03_psp_2-3u.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-III [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K03_psp_2-3u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K03_psp_2-3u.summary: Second and third divisions (II-III) of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, the 25,000-line Perfection of Wisdom sutra. These divisions continue the exposition of the perfection of wisdom: the non-substantiality and sameness of all dharmas, the bodhisattva's non-apprehending practice, skillful means, and the stages of the path culminating in buddhahood.
SA_K03_psp_2-3u.history: Sectional divisions of the 25,000-line text, corresponding to the structure used in the edition/manuscript; see Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā I for overall history and dating.

SA_K03_psp_4u.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā IV [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K03_psp_4u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K03_psp_4u.summary: Fourth division (Part IV) of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, the 25,000-line Perfection of Wisdom sutra. It continues the systematic exposition of the bodhisattva's training in the perfection of wisdom, the emptiness of all phenomena, and the realization of non-apprehension and equality.
SA_K03_psp_4u.history: Sectional division of the 25,000-line text; see Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā I for overall history and dating.

SA_K03_psp_5u.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā V [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K03_psp_5u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K03_psp_5u.summary: Fifth division (Part V) of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, the 25,000-line Perfection of Wisdom sutra. It continues the exposition of the perfection of wisdom, treating the qualities of the advanced bodhisattva, the non-apprehension of all dharmas, and the progress toward omniscient buddhahood.
SA_K03_psp_5u.history: Sectional division of the 25,000-line text; see Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā I for overall history and dating.

SA_K03_psp_6-8u.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā VI-VIII [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K03_psp_6-8u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K03_psp_6-8u.summary: Sixth through eighth divisions (VI-VIII) of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, the 25,000-line Perfection of Wisdom sutra. These concluding divisions complete the exposition of the bodhisattva path and the perfection of wisdom, including its non-apprehending realization, the dedication and rejoicing of merit, skillful means, and the final attainment of unsurpassed awakening.
SA_K03_psp_6-8u.history: Concluding sectional divisions of the 25,000-line text; see Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā I for overall history and dating.

SA_K03_pspdut2u.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K03_pspdut2u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K03_pspdut2u.date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core (Prajñāpāramitā class c. 100 BCE-600 CE)
SA_K03_pspdut2u.summary: One of the 'Large' Perfection of Wisdom sutras, the 'Prajñāpāramitā in 25,000 Lines.' It expounds the bodhisattva path and the perfection of wisdom centered on the emptiness (śūnyatā) of all dharmas, the non-apprehension of any fixed nature in phenomena, and the practice of skillful means. The text systematically treats the meditative attainments, the stages of the bodhisattva, and the qualities of a buddha.
SA_K03_pspdut2u.history: The 25,000-line recension is an expansion of the shorter Aṣṭasāhasrikā, produced as the Prajñāpāramitā corpus grew during the early centuries CE. A revised version was later arranged to match the chapter structure of the Abhisamayālaṃkāra. Kumārajīva translated a commentary on it (the Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra) into Chinese, the earliest known commentary on a Large PP sutra.

SA_K03_pvspl_u.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K03_pvspl_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K03_pvspl_u.date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core
SA_K03_pvspl_u.summary: A version/witness of the 'Perfection of Wisdom in 25,000 Lines,' a Large Prajñāpāramitā sutra teaching the emptiness of all phenomena and the bodhisattva's practice of non-attachment and skillful means. It elaborates the meditative and ethical perfections culminating in unsurpassed awakening.
SA_K03_pvspl_u.history: This is a separate manuscript witness or recension of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā, one of the most influential Large PP sutras in India and East Asia. The text was repeatedly revised and re-structured, including a recension keyed to the Abhisamayālaṃkāra commentarial framework.

SA_K05_adsp55-u.title: Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K05_adsp55-u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K05_adsp55-u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (expansion within the Large PP corpus)
SA_K05_adsp55-u.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 18,000 Lines,' one of the three principal Large Prajñāpāramitā sutras (alongside the 25,000- and 100,000-line versions). Its content largely overlaps with the other Large PP sutras: the emptiness of all dharmas, the non-apprehension of phenomena, the bodhisattva path, and the attributes of buddhahood.
SA_K05_adsp55-u.history: The Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā is one of the parallel expansions of the Prajñāpāramitā material; the three Large sutras share most of their text and differ mainly in length and amount of repetition. It survives in Sanskrit fragments, Tibetan, and Chinese, and was central to the Indian and Tibetan scholastic study of the perfection of wisdom.

SA_K05_adsp70-u.title: Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K05_adsp70-u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K05_adsp70-u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (expansion within the Large PP corpus)
SA_K05_adsp70-u.summary: A further manuscript witness or portion of the 'Perfection of Wisdom in 18,000 Lines,' a Large Prajñāpāramitā sutra teaching the emptiness of all phenomena and the comprehensive bodhisattva path to buddhahood.
SA_K05_adsp70-u.history: This is a separate witness of the Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā, one of the three Large PP sutras that share most of their content. The Large sutras were studied via the Abhisamayālaṃkāra and its commentaries in the Indo-Tibetan scholastic tradition.

SA_K05_bsu049_u.title: Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K05_bsu049_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K05_bsu049_u.date_estimate: core put in writing c. 1st c. BCE, developing c. 100 BCE-100 CE
SA_K05_bsu049_u.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 8,000 Lines,' traditionally regarded as the oldest and foundational Prajñāpāramitā sutra. It presents the bodhisattva ideal, the perfection of wisdom as non-attachment to and non-apprehension of all dharmas, the emptiness of the aggregates, and praise of the bodhisattva path over that of the arhat.
SA_K05_bsu049_u.history: Western scholarship has long held the Aṣṭasāhasrikā to be the earliest PP text; a Gāndhārī manuscript from the Split Collection has been carbon-dated to c. 74 CE (2-sigma 47-147 CE), among the oldest Buddhist manuscripts known. The longer Large PP sutras (25,000 and 100,000 lines) are expansions of this core text.

SA_K05_dsp1-2_u.title: Daśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā
SA_K05_dsp1-2_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K05_dsp1-2_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (within the PP corpus)
SA_K05_dsp1-2_u.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 10,000 Lines,' an intermediate-length recension within the Prajñāpāramitā corpus, presenting the same teaching of the emptiness of all dharmas and the bodhisattva path as the other PP sutras but at a length between the Aṣṭasāhasrikā (8,000) and the Large sutras.
SA_K05_dsp1-2_u.history: The Daśasāhasrikā is one of several length-defined recensions generated by abridging or expanding PP material; it is preserved especially in the Tibetan canon. It is comparatively less studied than the 8,000-, 18,000-, 25,000- and 100,000-line versions.

SA_K06_bsu028pu.title: Ratnaguṇasaṃcayagāthā
SA_K06_bsu028pu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_bsu028pu.date_estimate: earliest verses possibly c. 100 BCE; among the oldest PP material
SA_K06_bsu028pu.summary: The 'Verses on the Accumulation of the Precious Qualities of the Perfection of Wisdom,' a metrical (gāthā) summary of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā in 302 verses. It epitomizes early Mahāyāna thought, stressing the emptiness (śūnyatā) of the aggregates and praising the bodhisattva path over that of the arhat.
SA_K06_bsu028pu.history: Regarded with the Aṣṭasāhasrikā as among the earliest PP texts; its early verses may date as far back as c. 100 BCE and preserve archaic (Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit) language. In the Tibetan canon it appears as the 84th chapter of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā; in Chinese it circulates as a separate verse summary. Edited and translated by Edward Conze.

SA_K06_bsu030_u.title: Suvikrāntavikrāmiparipṛcchā [Suvikrāntavikrāmiparipṛcchāprajñāpāramitāsūtra, Sārdhadvisāhasrikā prajñāpāramitā]
SA_K06_bsu030_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_bsu030_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (later, shorter PP sutra)
SA_K06_bsu030_u.summary: The 'Questions of Suvikrāntavikrāmin,' also called the 'Perfection of Wisdom in 2,500 Lines' (Sārdhadvisāhasrikā). Framed as a dialogue prompted by the bodhisattva Suvikrāntavikrāmin's questions, it teaches the perfection of wisdom, the emptiness of dharmas, and the non-apprehension of any fixed object.
SA_K06_bsu030_u.history: One of the shorter, somewhat later Prajñāpāramitā sutras, preserved in Sanskrit and translated into Tibetan and Chinese. It belongs to the cluster of medium/short PP texts that condense the doctrine into a question-and-answer setting.

SA_K06_bsu051_u.title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K06_bsu051_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_bsu051_u.summary: The 'Diamond Cutter' sutra, a short and immensely influential Perfection of Wisdom text. Cast as a dialogue between the Buddha and the elder Subhūti, it teaches non-attachment to concepts and signs, the emptiness of all dharmas, and the bodhisattva's practice of giving without abiding in notions of self, being, or dharma.
SA_K06_bsu051_u.history: One of the most celebrated Mahāyāna sutras, especially in East Asian Chan/Zen; the Dunhuang Chinese printed edition of 868 CE is the world's earliest dated printed book. It attracted important Indian commentaries, including verse commentaries attributed to Asaṅga and Vasubandhu.

SA_K06_bsu052_u.title: Saptaśatikā prajñāpāramitā [Saptaśatikaprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K06_bsu052_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_bsu052_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (shorter, later PP sutra)
SA_K06_bsu052_u.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 700 Lines,' a short PP sutra also known as the Mañjuśrīparipṛcchā, framed around the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī. It teaches the perfection of wisdom through the non-duality and emptiness of all dharmas, including the famous 'one-practice samādhi' (ekavyūha-samādhi).
SA_K06_bsu052_u.history: A short Prajñāpāramitā text preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese, important in East Asian Buddhism (and Pure Land thought) for its account of the ekavyūha-samādhi. It is one of the cluster of named-interlocutor PP sutras.

SA_K06_bsu053_u.title: Prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtra
SA_K06_bsu053_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_bsu053_u.date_estimate: c. 4th-7th c. CE (debated; existed by 7th c. CE)
SA_K06_bsu053_u.summary: The 'Heart Sutra,' the shortest and most famous condensation of the Perfection of Wisdom. The bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara declares to Śāriputra that form is emptiness and emptiness is form, that all dharmas are empty of intrinsic nature, ending with the celebrated mantra 'gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā.'
SA_K06_bsu053_u.history: Exists in shorter and longer recensions; some scholars (notably Jan Nattier) argue the short version is a Chinese-to-Sanskrit back-translation drawing on the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā, a thesis that remains debated. It is the most chanted Mahāyāna scripture in East Asian and Tibetan Buddhism and attracted numerous Indian commentaries.

SA_K06_n2882u.title: Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitā [Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K06_n2882u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_n2882u.date_estimate: c. 4th-7th c. CE (short, later PP sutra)
SA_K06_n2882u.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 500 Lines,' a short PP sutra condensing the doctrine of the emptiness of all dharmas and the bodhisattva's non-attached practice of wisdom into a brief compass.
SA_K06_n2882u.history: One of the shorter, later texts in the Prajñāpāramitā corpus, preserved in Tibetan and Chinese translation. It belongs to the group of abbreviated PP sutras (alongside the 700-, 300- [Diamond], and 150-line texts) that distill the longer works.

SA_K06_vchedpgu.title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K06_vchedpgu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_vchedpgu.summary: A manuscript witness of the 'Diamond Cutter' Perfection of Wisdom sutra. In dialogue with the elder Subhūti, the Buddha teaches non-attachment to signs and concepts, the emptiness of all dharmas, and the bodhisattva's generosity without abiding in notions of self or dharma.
SA_K06_vchedpgu.history: Another textual witness of the Vajracchedikā, one of the most influential and most-copied Mahāyāna sutras, central to East Asian Chan/Zen and the basis of the famous 868 CE Dunhuang printed scroll. It received verse commentaries attributed to Asaṅga and Vasubandhu.

SA_K06_vchedppu.title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K06_vchedppu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K06_vchedppu.summary: A further manuscript witness of the 'Diamond Cutter' sutra, a short Perfection of Wisdom text teaching non-attachment to concepts and signs, the emptiness of all dharmas, and selfless bodhisattva practice, framed as a dialogue between the Buddha and Subhūti.
SA_K06_vchedppu.history: A distinct witness/edition of the widely transmitted Vajracchedikā. The sutra was enormously influential in East Asia, the subject of major Indian commentaries (Asaṅga, Vasubandhu) and East Asian exegesis, and underlies the world's earliest dated printed book (868 CE).

SA_K07_atrisatu.title: Triśatikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ kārikāsaptatiḥ [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtraśāstrakārikā]
SA_K07_atrisatu.author: Asaṅga
SA_K07_atrisatu.genre: śāstra / kārikā (verse commentary on a sutra)
SA_K07_atrisatu.date_estimate: c. 4th-5th c. CE (Asaṅga, fl. c. 330-405 CE)
SA_K07_atrisatu.summary: The 'Seventy Verses on the Perfection of Wisdom in 300 Lines,' a set of mnemonic kārikās by Asaṅga summarizing and structuring the meaning of the Vajracchedikā (Diamond Sutra). It organizes the sutra's teaching of non-apprehension and emptiness into a scholastic, easily memorized verse outline.
SA_K07_atrisatu.history: Attributed to the Yogācāra master Asaṅga; a prose commentary on these verses is attributed to his brother Vasubandhu, and the pair was translated into Chinese (Taishō no. 1511). The work exemplifies the Yogācāra scholastic appropriation and systematization of Prajñāpāramitā literature.

SA_K07_bsu033_u.title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
SA_K07_bsu033_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K07_bsu033_u.date_estimate: composed in NW India by c. 1st-2nd c. CE (Kushan era)
SA_K07_bsu033_u.summary: The 'Array of the Blissful Land,' a foundational Pure Land sutra describing the buddha Amitābha and his western paradise Sukhāvatī. The Larger version narrates the bodhisattva Dharmākara's vows leading to his buddhahood as Amitābha and the establishment of his Pure Land, where beings may be reborn through devotion and merit.
SA_K07_bsu033_u.history: One of the central scriptures of Pure Land Buddhism, likely composed in Gandhāra or northwest India and possibly originally in a Prakrit. It exists in Larger and Smaller recensions; this entry corresponds to a Sukhāvatīvyūha witness. The sutras became immensely influential in Chinese, Japanese, and Tibetan devotional Buddhism.

SA_K07_ppfrag_u.title: Fragments of Prajñāpāramitā texts
SA_K07_ppfrag_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K07_ppfrag_u.date_estimate: various; PP corpus c. 100 BCE-600 CE
SA_K07_ppfrag_u.summary: A collection of fragmentary remains of Perfection of Wisdom texts rather than a single complete work. Such fragments preserve portions of various PP sutras (e.g., the Aṣṭasāhasrikā or Large PP sutras), bearing on the early textual history of the corpus and its teaching of emptiness and the bodhisattva path.
SA_K07_ppfrag_u.history: Prajñāpāramitā fragments survive in Gāndhārī (e.g., the Split Collection, carbon-dated to the 1st c. CE) and in later Sanskrit and Central Asian manuscript finds; they are crucial witnesses for reconstructing the development and recensional history of the PP literature.

SA_K07_pplg1__u.title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K07_pplg1__u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K07_pplg1__u.date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core
SA_K07_pplg1__u.summary: A witness of the 'Larger Prajñāpāramitā,' here identified with the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā (25,000 Lines). It expounds the bodhisattva path and the perfection of wisdom centered on the emptiness of all dharmas and non-apprehension of phenomena, in expanded form relative to the shorter PP sutras.
SA_K07_pplg1__u.history: 'Larger Prajñāpāramitā' is a cover term for the Large PP sutras (8,000-line core expanded to 18,000/25,000/100,000 lines). This witness is associated with the 25,000-line recension, central to Indo-Tibetan scholastic study via the Abhisamayālaṃkāra.

SA_K07_pplg202u.title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
SA_K07_pplg202u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K07_pplg202u.date_estimate: expanded c. 1st-2nd c. CE from earlier Aṣṭasāhasrikā core
SA_K07_pplg202u.summary: Another witness of the 'Larger Prajñāpāramitā' (Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā, 25,000 Lines), a Large Perfection of Wisdom sutra teaching the emptiness of all phenomena, the non-apprehension of any fixed nature, and the comprehensive bodhisattva path to buddhahood.
SA_K07_pplg202u.history: A distinct manuscript witness of the Large PP material identified with the 25,000-line recension. The Large sutras were repeatedly revised, including a version arranged to match the Abhisamayālaṃkāra, the standard scholastic commentary on the PP corpus.

SA_K07_sukhvylu.title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
SA_K07_sukhvylu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K07_sukhvylu.date_estimate: composed in NW India by c. 1st-2nd c. CE (Kushan era)
SA_K07_sukhvylu.summary: The 'Array of the Blissful Land,' a Pure Land sutra centered on the buddha Amitābha and his western paradise Sukhāvatī. The Larger version recounts the bodhisattva Dharmākara's vows and his attainment of buddhahood as Amitābha, describing the Pure Land and the means of rebirth there; the Smaller version emphasizes faith and recollection of the buddha's name.
SA_K07_sukhvylu.history: A second Sukhāvatīvyūha witness in this batch and a cornerstone of Pure Land Buddhism, likely composed in northwest India, possibly in a Prakrit. It exists in Larger and Smaller recensions and became foundational to East Asian devotional traditions (Chinese Jingtu, Japanese Jōdo and Jōdo Shinshū).

SA_K08_bsu020_u.title: Kāśyapaparivartasūtra [Kāśyapaparivarta]
SA_K08_bsu020_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K08_bsu020_u.date_estimate: c. 1st-2nd c. CE (one of the earliest Mahāyāna sūtras)
SA_K08_bsu020_u.summary: An early Mahāyāna sūtra cast as a dialogue between the Buddha and his disciple Mahākāśyapa, expounding the conduct, qualities, and pitfalls of the bodhisattva path. It treats the middle way between extremes, the dangers of false bodhisattvas and corrupt monks, and emptiness, framing the bodhisattva ideal in concrete ethical terms.
SA_K08_bsu020_u.history: Among the oldest datable Mahāyāna texts, it was one of the first translated into Chinese (by Lokakṣema, late 2nd c. CE). A Sanskrit manuscript was recovered from Khotan in the 1890s and edited by Baron A. von Staël-Holstein (1926); it was later absorbed into the Ratnakūṭa (Mahāratnakūṭa) collection. An early Indian commentary survives in Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_K08_bsu040_u.title: Vinayaviniścaya Upāliparipṛcchā [Vinayaviniścaya upāliparipṛcchā sūtra]
SA_K08_bsu040_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K08_bsu040_u.summary: A Mahāyāna sūtra ('Determining the Vinaya: Upāli's Questions') in which the Buddha answers questions posed by his disciple Upāli concerning monastic discipline. It contrasts the prātimokṣa vows of the śrāvaka with the conduct of the bodhisattva, arguing that bodhisattva ethics are governed by intention and skillful means rather than by the letter of the rules, and it treats confession and the purification of transgressions.
SA_K08_bsu040_u.history: Transmitted as part of the Ratnakūṭa (Mahāratnakūṭa) collection of Mahāyāna sūtras. It is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese and was influential in Mahāyāna discussions of bodhisattva precepts and the relative weight of monastic offenses.

SA_K08_bsu062_u.title: Guṇakāraṇḍavyūhasūtra
SA_K08_bsu062_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K08_bsu062_u.date_estimate: c. 15th c. CE (verse recasting of a c. 4th-5th c. prose original)
SA_K08_bsu062_u.summary: A versified Mahāyāna sūtra glorifying the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara, recounting his compassionate deeds and salvific power and propagating the mantra Oṃ maṇi padme hūṃ. It is a metrical reworking of the prose Kāraṇḍavyūhasūtra and incorporates verses drawn from Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra.
SA_K08_bsu062_u.history: One of the last Sanskrit Buddhist sūtras composed, this longer, polished all-verse form dates to roughly the fifteenth century and is especially important in Nepalese Newar Buddhism. It is based on the older Kāraṇḍavyūhasūtra and remains in active ritual and devotional use.

SA_K08_kasyparu.title: Kāśyapaparivartasūtra [Kāśyapaparivarta]
SA_K08_kasyparu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K08_kasyparu.date_estimate: c. 1st-2nd c. CE (one of the earliest Mahāyāna sūtras)
SA_K08_kasyparu.summary: An early Mahāyāna sūtra in dialogue between the Buddha and Mahākāśyapa expounding the qualities and conduct of the bodhisattva, the middle way, emptiness, and warnings against false bodhisattvas and corrupt monastics. (Duplicate entry of the Kāśyapaparivarta; another recension/copy in the collection.)
SA_K08_kasyparu.history: One of the oldest datable Mahāyāna texts, first rendered into Chinese by Lokakṣema in the late 2nd c. CE, with a Sanskrit manuscript recovered from Khotan and edited by Staël-Holstein (1926). Later incorporated into the Ratnakūṭa collection.

SA_K09_bsu014_u.title: Daśabhūmikasūtra
SA_K09_bsu014_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K09_bsu014_u.summary: An influential Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Ten Stages') describing the ten bhūmis or stages through which a bodhisattva progresses toward complete awakening, each correlated with a perfection (pāramitā). The bodhisattva Vajragarbha, before an assembly led by Maitreya, expounds the qualities, vows, and attainments of each stage.
SA_K09_bsu014_u.history: An early and important text, it circulates both independently and as a chapter of the Avataṃsaka Sūtra. Its oldest Chinese translation is by Dharmarakṣa (c. 297 CE). It became the doctrinal basis of the Daśabhūmika school in China and was the subject of Vasubandhu's commentary (Daśabhūmikavyākhyāna).

SA_K09_bsu016_u.title: Gaṇḍavyūhasūtra [Gaṇḍavyūha]
SA_K09_bsu016_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K09_bsu016_u.summary: A major Mahāyāna sūtra narrating the spiritual pilgrimage of the youth Sudhana, who at the urging of Mañjuśrī visits fifty-three spiritual teachers (kalyāṇamitra) in quest of supreme awakening, culminating in the teaching of Samantabhadra. It elaborates the interpenetration of all phenomena and the bodhisattva's vast vow.
SA_K09_bsu016_u.history: Composed in India c. 200-300 CE, it circulated independently and forms the climactic final book of the Avataṃsaka Sūtra. Richly illustrated in Asian art, it profoundly shaped the Chinese Huayan and Japanese Kegon schools; Sudhana's journey is a celebrated theme in Buddhist narrative and visual culture.

SA_K10_adbhutdu.title: Adbhutadharmaparyāya [Adbhutadharmaparyāyasūtra]
SA_K10_adbhutdu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_adbhutdu.date_estimate: uncertain; before late 8th c. CE (manuscripts in the Gilgit collection)
SA_K10_adbhutdu.summary: A short Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Marvelous Dharma Discourse') on the immense merit generated by building stūpas and caityas for the veneration of a buddha. The Buddha declares that even a tiny stūpa erected for a perfect buddha yields merit far surpassing offerings made to all stream-enterers, once-returners, non-returners, arhats, pratyekabuddhas, and the saṅgha.
SA_K10_adbhutdu.history: Three Sanskrit manuscripts have been identified in the Gilgit collection. The Tibetan translation (Toh 319) was made by Bandé Yeshé Dé with the Indian preceptors Jinamitra and Surendrabodhi, who worked in the late 8th-early 9th c., placing the text's circulation before that period.

SA_K10_ahovcs_u.title: Ahorātravratacaityasevānuśaṃsāvadāna
SA_K10_ahovcs_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_ahovcs_u.date_estimate: uncertain; 1st millennium CE or later (Nepalese avadāna milieu)
SA_K10_ahovcs_u.summary: An avadāna recounting the benefits (anuśaṃsā) accruing from a day-and-night vow (ahorātravrata) devoted to the service and worship (sevā) of a caitya/stūpa. As is typical of the genre, it illustrates karmic reward by narrating the meritorious past and resulting good fortune of devotees who undertook such caitya worship.
SA_K10_ahovcs_u.history: Belongs to the corpus of Sanskrit Buddhist avadāna literature preserved and ritually performed in the Newar Buddhist tradition of the Kathmandu Valley, where caitya worship and vow observances are central devotional practices. It is an obscure text known chiefly from Nepalese manuscript collections; its precise authorship and date are not established.

SA_K10_anitysuu.title: Anityatāsūtra [Āryānityatāsūtra]
SA_K10_anitysuu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_anitysuu.date_estimate: uncertain; early/mainstream Buddhist material, 1st millennium CE
SA_K10_anitysuu.summary: A short discourse ('Sūtra on Impermanence') in which the Buddha reminds his followers that good health, youth, prosperity, and life itself are all impermanent, and that all beings—whatever their status, even arhats, pratyekabuddhas, and buddhas—must die. It closes with verses urging the audience to grasp that true happiness lies in bringing conditioned states to rest.
SA_K10_anitysuu.history: Two short sūtras of this title circulate in the Tibetan Kangyur's General Sūtra section (Toh 309 and 310); the teaching on impermanence (anityatā) is a foundational Buddhist topic shared across traditions. The brevity and content mark it as a concise meditative reminder rather than a developed doctrinal treatise.

SA_K10_avsata_u.title: Avadānaśataka
SA_K10_avsata_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_avsata_u.summary: An anthology of one hundred Buddhist avadāna tales in Sanskrit, organized into ten decades (books), illustrating the workings of karma and the power of devotion, faith, and giving (dāna). The stories typically link a present event to a past deed and a future prophecy, showing how meritorious or harmful acts ripen.
SA_K10_avsata_u.history: Associated with the Mūlasarvāstivāda school and probably composed in the northwest Indian subcontinent; its mention of the dīnāra coin and early Chinese translation (1st half of 3rd c.) support a 2nd c. CE dating. It survives in Sanskrit, was translated into Chinese and Tibetan, and fragments have been found in the Schøyen Collection (possibly Bāmiyān).

SA_K10_bsu001_u.title: Āryānityatāsūtra [Anityatāsūtra]
SA_K10_bsu001_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu001_u.date_estimate: uncertain; early/mainstream Buddhist material, 1st millennium CE
SA_K10_bsu001_u.summary: The 'Noble Sūtra on Impermanence,' a brief discourse in which the Buddha teaches that health, youth, wealth, and life are impermanent and that all that lives must die, concluding with verses on anityatā and on the peace found in stilling conditioned states. (A recension of the Anityatāsūtra; cf. K10anitysuu.)
SA_K10_bsu001_u.history: Circulates in the Tibetan Kangyur (Toh 309/310) and represents the widely shared Buddhist contemplation of impermanence. It functions as a concise devotional and meditative reminder rather than a systematic treatise.

SA_K10_bsu006_u.title: Ajitasenavyākaraṇa
SA_K10_bsu006_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu006_u.date_estimate: uncertain; manuscript c. 6th-7th c. CE, composition likely earlier
SA_K10_bsu006_u.summary: A 'proto-Mahāyāna' sūtra ('The Prophecy concerning King Ajitasena') blending Mahāyāna and Sthaviravāda (mainstream) ideas. It teaches that recitation of the Buddha's name saves from suffering and hell, and ends with the classic Mahāyāna promise that those who teach or even hear a verse of it gain good rebirth and bodhisattvahood, while detractors fall to hell. It has the present-past-future narrative structure typical of avadānas.
SA_K10_bsu006_u.history: Known from manuscripts found near Gilgit (1931) and in Central Asia, with the surviving codices dated to roughly the 6th-7th c. CE; it is studied as evidence for the transition from mainstream Buddhism to the Mahāyāna. Sanskrit manuscript material is held in collections including the IOM RAS.

SA_K10_bsu008_u.title: Āryabuddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvāṇanirdeśanāma Mahāyānasūtra
SA_K10_bsu008_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu008_u.summary: A Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Teaching on the Extraordinary Transformation that is the Miracle of Attaining the Buddha's Powers') in which the Buddha thrice displays supernatural powers: transporting his assembly to Varanasi, prompting Avalokiteśvara and Vajrapāṇi to gather all beings ripe for awakening and transforming the world into a pure realm like Sukhāvatī, and manifesting many buddhas around himself. Through dialogues, partly with Mañjuśrī, it explores miracles (prātihārya), supernatural power (ṛddhi), and extraordinary transformation (vikurvaṇa).
SA_K10_bsu008_u.history: Preserved in Tibetan translation (Toh 186) and Chinese; it belongs to the body of Mahāyāna sūtras exploring the inconceivable powers and skillful means of buddhas and bodhisattvas. Its compositional date is not precisely fixed.

SA_K10_bsu009_u.title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa [Maitreyavyākaraṇasūtra]
SA_K10_bsu009_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu009_u.summary: A sūtra ('The Prophecy of Maitreya') describing the future advent of the bodhisattva Maitreya as the next buddha. It depicts the conditions of his appearance in Jambudvīpa when human lifespan and circumstances have transformed, his awakening, and the means by which devotees may be reborn in his presence.
SA_K10_bsu009_u.history: A widely transmitted text on the future buddha, preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and several Chinese versions, central to the broad Buddhist cult of Maitreya across India, Central Asia, and East Asia. It exists in multiple recensions and was an important vehicle for messianic and devotional expectations.

SA_K10_bsu010_u.title: Bhavasaṅkrāntisūtra
SA_K10_bsu010_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu010_u.date_estimate: uncertain; early Mahāyāna, c. 1st-3rd c. CE
SA_K10_bsu010_u.summary: A short Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Sūtra on the Transition of Existences') explaining how rebirth occurs without any dharma actually transmigrating, in accordance with not-self (anātman). Set at the Veṇuvana near Rājagṛha, King Bimbisāra asks how a long-ceased action can appear at the moment of death given that formations are empty; the Buddha answers with the analogy of a beautiful woman seen in a dream.
SA_K10_bsu010_u.history: Closely linked to the Madhyamaka: a commentarial śāstra of the same name is attributed to Nāgarjuna, and the pair (sūtra and śāstra) was edited together by N. Aiyaswami Sastri. It is preserved in Tibetan and Chinese and is valued for harmonizing emptiness with the continuity of karma and rebirth.

SA_K10_bsu013_u.title: Āryacaturdharmanirdeśasūtra
SA_K10_bsu013_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu013_u.summary: A very short Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Noble Sūtra Teaching the Four Factors') that enumerates four factors enabling a bodhisattva to overcome and purify negative actions: the power of repentance/remorse, the antidotal action (engaging in virtue), the power of restraint (resolving not to repeat the deed), and the power of support (reliance on the Three Jewels and bodhicitta).
SA_K10_bsu013_u.history: Preserved in the Tibetan Kangyur (Toh 251), it is the last of a set of three short sūtras with similar titles on sets of four dharmas; in several Kangyurs these are grouped together. It has been translated into English by the 84000 project. It is brief but doctrinally important for the Mahāyāna practice of confession and purification.

SA_K10_bsu018_u.title: Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī [Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka(sūtra)]
SA_K10_bsu018_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu018_u.summary: A dhāraṇī text invoking the 'mode/aspect of omniscience' (sarvajñatākāra), the comprehensive knowledge that is the goal of the bodhisattva and the unique attainment of a buddha. The bracketed reference indicates it is transmitted in association with the Karuṇāpuṇḍarīkasūtra ('White Lotus of Compassion'), which narrates the origins of buddhas and bodhisattvas and the supremacy of Śākyamuni.
SA_K10_bsu018_u.history: Dhāraṇīs (mnemonic incantations encoding doctrine and conferring protection/merit) were frequently embedded in or appended to Mahāyāna sūtras. The precise identity, scope, and independent transmission of this Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī are not well established; the Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka with which it is grouped is an ancient Mahāyāna scripture possibly datable to as early as the 3rd c. CE.

SA_K10_bsu019.title: Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra [Avalokiteśvaraguṇa-Kāraṇḍavyūha(sūtra)]
SA_K10_bsu019.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu019.summary: A Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Basket's Display' / 'Array of the Casket of Avalokiteśvara's Qualities') extolling the cosmic compassion and salvific deeds of the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara, who rescues beings from every realm including the hells. It is famous as the earliest text to teach the six-syllable mantra Oṃ maṇi padme hūṃ, presenting it as a means to liberation and buddhahood.
SA_K10_bsu019.history: Composed in India around the late 4th-early 5th c. CE; scholarship (e.g. work on the Śivadharmaśāstra) has shown Buddhist-Śaiva interaction shaping its imagery. Hugely influential on the cult of Avalokiteśvara, especially in Tibet, it exists in prose and later verse (Guṇakāraṇḍavyūha) recensions and survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_K10_bsu021_u.title: Saddharmalaṅkāvatārasūtra [Laṅkāvatārasūtra]
SA_K10_bsu021_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu021_u.summary: A major Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Descent of the True Dharma into Laṅkā'), framed as the Buddha's teaching to the rākṣasa king Rāvaṇa on Mount Malaya in Laṅkā. It expounds mind-only (cittamātra), the storehouse consciousness (ālayavijñāna), the three natures, buddha-nature (tathāgatagarbha), the luminous mind, emptiness, and vegetarianism, in a discursive, dialogic form.
SA_K10_bsu021_u.history: A composite text formed between roughly the 3rd and 5th centuries CE, blending Yogācāra and tathāgatagarbha currents; notably it is not cited by the Yogācāra founders Asaṅga or Vasubandhu. It became foundational for the Chan/Zen tradition (linked to Bodhidharma) and survives in Sanskrit and several Chinese and Tibetan versions.

SA_K10_bsu023_u.title: Madhyamakaśālistambasūtra
SA_K10_bsu023_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu023_u.date_estimate: very early Mahāyāna; c. 1st c. BCE - 2nd c. CE
SA_K10_bsu023_u.summary: A Mahāyāna sūtra ('The Rice Seedling') giving one of the most detailed canonical expositions of dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda). Prompted by a brief remark the Buddha made while gazing at a rice seedling, the bodhisattva Maitreya expounds the outer (seed-to-sprout) and inner (twelve-link) causal sequences and how 'whoever sees dependent origination sees the Dharma.'
SA_K10_bsu023_u.history: Among the oldest Mahāyāna texts; no complete Sanskrit original survives, but it is quoted extensively in later treatises. It deeply influenced the Madhyamaka, inspiring Candrakīrti (e.g. the Madhyamakāvatāra); Indian commentaries attributed to Nāgarjuna and to Kamalaśīla are preserved in Tibetan and Mongolian. The 'Madhyamaka-' prefix here marks its association with that school.

SA_K10_bsu024_u.title: Mahāsannipātaratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra [Ratnaketuparivarta, Ratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra]
SA_K10_bsu024_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu024_u.date_estimate: c. 1st-3rd c. CE (formative Mahāyāna period); extant Sanskrit incomplete
SA_K10_bsu024_u.summary: A Mahāyāna sūtra that forms one of the core constituent texts of the great Mahāsannipāta ('Great Collection') corpus, also circulating independently as the Ratnaketu Dhāraṇī. It centers on the bodhisattva/buddha Ratnaketu and presents narratives of the Buddha subduing Māra and his hosts together with protective dhāraṇī formulas. The text dramatizes cosmic struggle between the Dharma and demonic forces and the power of the buddhas to safeguard practitioners.
SA_K10_bsu024_u.history: It is counted among the earliest stratum of Mahāyāna literature, datable to the first centuries CE. The Sanskrit survives only incompletely (notably in Gilgit and Central Asian fragments), with the complete text preserved in the Tibetan Kangyur (Toh 138) and in Chinese translation; it was incorporated as the Ratnaketuparivarta within the larger Mahāsannipāta collection.

SA_K10_bsu026_u.title: Pratītyasamutpādamahāyānasūtra
SA_K10_bsu026_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu026_u.summary: A short Mahāyāna sūtra on dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda). In its frame the Buddha, residing in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, is asked by Avalokiteśvara about the merit gained from building a stūpa; the Buddha responds by reciting the Buddhist creed (ye dharmā hetuprabhavā…) on dependent arising and declares that this dependent arising is itself the dharmakāya. It thus links the doctrine of conditioned causation to the practice of enshrining relics and the verse-formula placed inside stūpas and images.
SA_K10_bsu026_u.history: The 'ye dharmā' creed at its heart is one of the most widely deposited dhāraṇī-like formulas in Buddhist Asia, inscribed in countless stūpas and clay tablets. The sūtra is preserved in Sanskrit and in the Tibetan Kangyur (Sūtra on Dependent Arising, Toh 212) and corresponding Chinese versions.

SA_K10_bsu032_u.title: Sukhāvatyamṛtavyūha [Sukhāvatī(amṛta)vyūhasūtra], the Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha
SA_K10_bsu032_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu032_u.date_estimate: c. 1st-2nd c. CE (probably before c. 100 CE)
SA_K10_bsu032_u.summary: The shorter of the two Sukhāvatīvyūha sūtras (the 'Amitābha Sūtra'), a foundational Pure Land scripture. It describes Sukhāvatī, the western Pure Land of the Buddha Amitābha, in glowing detail and teaches that rebirth there can be attained by single-minded faith and recollection of Amitābha's name at the moment of death. Unlike the larger Sukhāvatīvyūha, it emphasizes faith and devotion over the accumulation of good works.
SA_K10_bsu032_u.history: Scholars such as Luis Gómez and Julian Pas place its written composition in the 1st-2nd century CE in greater Gandhāra/northwest India, with the smaller version generally regarded as earlier. It became one of the three principal Pure Land sūtras of East Asian Buddhism (Chinese, Japanese Jōdo and Shin schools) and survives in Sanskrit, Chinese, and Tibetan.

SA_K10_bsu037_u.title: Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhānavyūhasūtra
SA_K10_bsu037_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu037_u.summary: A Mahāyāna sūtra whose title means 'The Array of the Blessing/Empowerment (adhiṣṭhāna) of All the Tathāgatas.' It concerns the consecrating power and grace bestowed by all the buddhas, in the genre of 'vyūha' (display/array) scriptures, and is associated in fuller titles with the vision of buddha-fields and with dhāraṇī. Its subject is the salvific blessing-power of the tathāgatas extended to beings and to sacred objects.
SA_K10_bsu037_u.history: The text survives chiefly in Sanskrit manuscript and in canonical translation; it belongs to the broad current of vyūha and dhāraṇī sūtras that mediated between Mahāyāna and emerging tantric practice. Precise dating and recension are not securely established, and the title appears in fuller forms (e.g. Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhāna-sattvāvalokana-buddhakṣetra-saṃdarśana-vyūha).

SA_K10_bsu038_u.title: Sāgaranāgarājaparipṛcchā Nāma Mahāyānasūtra [Sāgaranāgarājaparipṛcchāsūtra]
SA_K10_bsu038_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu038_u.summary: A 'Questions of the Nāga King Sāgara' Mahāyāna sūtra, set in the underwater court of the nāga (serpent) king Sāgara. The Buddha discourses to the nāga king and an assembly on karma and its effects, explaining that the variety of forms and indeed all phenomena arise from virtuous and nonvirtuous actions, and (in the shorter recension) that reciting the four seals of the Dharma equals reciting all 84,000 articles of the teaching.
SA_K10_bsu038_u.history: Three distinct sūtras of this title (long, medium, short) are preserved in the Tibetan Kangyur (Toh 153-155), reflecting a popular cycle of nāga-king teachings in Mahāyāna; the texts survive in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese. Sāgara is a prominent nāgarāja throughout Mahāyāna scripture (notably the Lotus Sūtra and Prajñāpāramitā literature).

SA_K10_bsu039_u.title: Triskandhasūtra
SA_K10_bsu039_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu039_u.summary: The 'Sūtra of the Three Heaps' (Triskandha[dharma]sūtra), a Mahāyāna confessional liturgy. Its title reflects three sections—(1) homage/prostration to the Thirty-five Confession Buddhas, (2) confession of negative actions, and (3) dedication of merit—the 'three heaps' of practice. It is widely used to purify transgressions of the bodhisattva vows.
SA_K10_bsu039_u.history: Traditionally drawn from the Ratnakūṭa (Ratnarāśi) collection, it became a central purification rite in Indo-Tibetan Buddhism, with distinct ritual traditions developed in the Madhyamaka (Nāgārjuna) and Yogācāra (Asaṅga/Vasubandhu) lineages for conferring bodhisattva vows. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_K10_bsu045_u.title: Saṃghāṭa Sūtra
SA_K10_bsu045_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_bsu045_u.summary: The Saṃghāṭasūtra ('Saṃghāṭa Dharmaparyāya') is a Mahāyāna sūtra cast as a dialogue in which the Buddha, prompted by the bodhisattva Sarvaśūra, teaches a 'treasury of Dharma' beneficial to both young and old. It promises vast merit, protection, and good rebirth to those who hear, recite, or copy it, presenting itself as an object of intense devotion.
SA_K10_bsu045_u.history: It was enormously popular in northwest India and Central Asia and is one of the texts recovered among the Gilgit manuscripts (1931 and 1938), surviving also in Khotanese, Sanskrit, Chinese, and Tibetan, with translations spanning the 5th-10th centuries. Gregory Schopen and others cite it as a prime example of the Mahāyāna 'cult of the book.'

SA_K10_dbsu1-4u.title: Daśabalasūtra
SA_K10_dbsu1-4u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_dbsu1-4u.date_estimate: early; redacted in early centuries CE; Sanskrit fragments c. 5th-8th c. CE
SA_K10_dbsu1-4u.summary: A canonical discourse on the daśabala, the 'ten powers' unique to a buddha—forms of perfect knowledge such as discerning the possible from the impossible, the ripening of karma, the dispositions and faculties of beings, the paths leading everywhere, the defilements and purification of meditative states, recollection of past lives, the divine eye, and the destruction of the contaminants. The recital of these powers grounds the Buddha's authority to 'roar the lion's roar.'
SA_K10_dbsu1-4u.history: This Sanskrit Daśabalasūtra (here in sections 1-4) is a mainstream (non-Mahāyāna) āgama sūtra of the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda transmission, known from Central Asian (Turfan/Gilgit) Sanskrit fragments and parallel to Pali Dasabala suttas and Chinese āgama versions; the doctrine is elaborated in Abhidharma and in Nāgārjuna's Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra.

SA_K10_disu.title: Dīpasūtra
SA_K10_disu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_disu.summary: A short Buddhist sūtra titled 'Dīpa' ('Lamp'). The title corresponds to the well-known lamp simile in which feeling/consciousness is likened to a flame that burns dependent on oil and wick and is extinguished when its fuel is exhausted, illustrating impermanence and conditioned arising (cf. the Pali Dīpa-sutta, SN 54.8). Identification of this specific Sanskrit text is not certain.
SA_K10_disu.history: The lamp simile is a stock Buddhist image; a discrete Sanskrit Dīpasūtra is not firmly attested in standard reference literature, and this entry may correspond to a short sūtra or fragment in a digital Sanskrit Buddhist corpus rather than a major independent work.

SA_K10_divav29u.title: Aśokāvadāna
SA_K10_divav29u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_divav29u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. CE (oral roots from c. 2nd c. BCE); within Divyāvadāna by c. 4th-5th c. CE
SA_K10_divav29u.summary: The 'Legend of King Aśoka,' the avadāna narrative (story 29 of the Divyāvadāna) recounting the legendary career of the Mauryan emperor Aśoka from a Buddhist standpoint. It tells of his prior incarnation, his violent early reign and conversion, his building of 84,000 stūpas, his patronage of the saṅgha, and his relationship with monks such as Upagupta. It blends purported history with miraculous and karmic motifs.
SA_K10_divav29u.history: Composed in Sanskrit around the 2nd century CE (with earlier oral antecedents), it was redacted by Mathurā-region monks of the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda tradition and later incorporated into the Divyāvadāna anthology. It is a key source for the Buddhist image of Aśoka, distinct from his own edicts. John S. Strong produced the standard study and translation.

SA_K10_divav33u.title: Śārdūlakarṇāvadāna
SA_K10_divav33u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_divav33u.date_estimate: by c. 2nd c. CE (known to An Shigao); within Divyāvadāna by c. 4th-5th c. CE
SA_K10_divav33u.summary: Story 33 of the Divyāvadāna, the tale of Śārdūlakarṇa, concerning Prakṛti, a low-caste Mātaṅga (caṇḍāla) woman, and the Buddha's disciple Ānanda. The narrative mounts a pointed anti-Brahmanical, anti-caste polemic—treating the four varṇas as mere conventional designations—and embeds an extensive encyclopedic exposition of pre-Hellenistic Indian nakṣatra (lunar-mansion) astrology and astronomy.
SA_K10_divav33u.history: It is an unusually early and influential avadāna: summarized in Chinese by the Parthian An Shigao in the 2nd century CE and fully translated twice in the 3rd century, it was widely transmitted across Central Asia and Iran. It is valued by historians of science for preserving an early Indian astronomical/astrological system prior to Greek influence.

SA_K10_divyav_u.title: Divyāvadāna
SA_K10_divyav_u.author: anonymous/traditional (compiled)
SA_K10_divyav_u.date_estimate: compiled c. 2nd-5th c. CE (oral roots from c. 2nd c. BCE); anthology arrangement late
SA_K10_divyav_u.summary: The 'Divine Narratives,' a Sanskrit anthology of 38 avadāna tales illustrating the workings of karma through stories of the Buddha, his disciples, kings, and laypeople and their past and present lives. Many of its narratives derive from the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya; famous components include the Aśokāvadāna, Śārdūlakarṇāvadāna, Pūrṇāvadāna, and Sahasodgatāvadāna.
SA_K10_divyav_u.history: Its tales circulated from as early as the 2nd century BCE and reached largely their present form between the 2nd and 5th centuries CE in northwest India or the western Deccan, though the anthology as a fixed collection is attested chiefly in later Nepalese Sanskrit manuscripts. The standard Western edition was published by Cowell and Neil (1886); Andy Rotman's 'Divine Stories' is the modern translation.

SA_K10_dvavims_tu.title: Dvāviṃśatyavadānakathā
SA_K10_dvavims_tu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_dvavims_tu.date_estimate: later, Nepalese (mid/late 1st-early 2nd millennium CE; manuscripts down to c. 1800 CE)
SA_K10_dvavims_tu.summary: A collection of twenty-two avadāna tales ('Stories of the Twenty-two Avadānas') illustrating the merits of devotion to the Buddha and the duties enjoined by the Dharma. It belongs to the genre of Nepalese Sanskrit avadānamālā literature, narratives that frame moral and devotional teaching through stories of karma and merit.
SA_K10_dvavims_tu.history: It is transmitted in Nepalese Sanskrit multi-text manuscripts (e.g. a Cambridge manuscript dated 1803 CE pairs it with the Ratnamālāvadāna and Upoṣadhāvadāna) and has been studied for textual reuse alongside the Avadānaśataka and Divyāvadāna, illuminating the scribal creation of Nepalese avadāna anthologies.

SA_K10_dvtasutu.title: Devatāsūtra
SA_K10_dvtasutu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_dvtasutu.date_estimate: early discourse; Gilgit Sanskrit manuscript c. 6th-8th c. CE
SA_K10_dvtasutu.summary: A short sūtra in which a divine being (devatā) appears before the Buddha and poses a series of riddle-like questions about how best to live a good life. The Buddha's verse answers form a compact teaching on core Buddhist values—overcoming the three poisons, virtue of body, speech, and mind, and practical wisdom for daily conduct.
SA_K10_dvtasutu.history: It is preserved in numerous first-millennium manuscripts in Sanskrit, Chinese, and Tibetan; a full Sanskrit version closely matching the Tibetan (Toh 329) survives among the Gilgit manuscripts in proto-Śāradā script (c. 6th-8th c. CE). It often travels with the Daśabalasūtra and Alpadevatāsūtra in the manuscript tradition.

SA_K10_ekottaru.title: Ekottarāgama [Ekottarāgamasūtra]
SA_K10_ekottaru.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_ekottaru.date_estimate: early discourses; Sanskrit Gilgit fragments mid-1st millennium CE (Chinese trans. 384-385 CE)
SA_K10_ekottaru.summary: The 'Increasing-by-One Collection,' an āgama (the counterpart of the Pali Aṅguttara Nikāya) in which the Buddha's discourses are arranged numerically, by topics grouped in ascending numbers. Here it denotes the Sanskrit Ekottarāgama, known from manuscript fragments rather than a complete text.
SA_K10_ekottaru.history: A substantial portion of a Sanskrit Ekottarāgama was found at Gilgit and edited/reconstructed by Chandrabhal Tripāṭhi; school affiliation is uncertain, with many scholars pointing to the Mahāsāṃghika or a sub-school. The complete extant version is the Chinese translation by Dharmanandī, edited by Gautama Saṃghadeva and Dao'an (384-385 CE), which underwent later redaction; Gāndhārī Ekottarikāgama-type fragments also survive.

SA_K10_kathinau.title: Kaṭhināvadāna
SA_K10_kathinau.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_kathinau.summary: An avadāna concerning the kaṭhina, the wooden frame used to cut and the seasonal ceremony for offering monastic robes at the close of the rains retreat (varṣā). The narrative illustrates, through karmic story, the great merit accruing to lay donors who offer robes and requisites to the saṅgha and the proper monastic observance surrounding the kaṭhina robe.
SA_K10_kathinau.history: It belongs to the broad Sanskrit avadāna literature rooted in Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya material concerning the kaṭhina ceremony; the kaṭhina robe-offering remains a major rite in Buddhist monasticism. Precise dating and the relation of this specific recension to the Avadānaśataka/Divyāvadāna corpora are not firmly established.

SA_K10_kbavk10u.title: Garbhāvakrāntyavadāna
SA_K10_kbavk10u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_K10_kbavk10u.tradition: Buddhist (Mahāyāna); composed by a Kashmiri Śaiva-background poet
SA_K10_kbavk10u.summary: A pallava (chapter) of Kṣemendra's Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā, the 'Garbhāvakrānti' ('Descent into the Womb') avadāna. It versifies a teaching on the process of conception, gestation, and entry of consciousness into the womb, framed as a Buddhist narrative on impermanence, the sufferings of embodied existence, and karma.
SA_K10_kbavk10u.history: Kṣemendra (c. 990-1070 CE), a prolific Kashmiri polymath who studied under Abhinavagupta, composed the Avadānakalpalatā (Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā), a Sanskrit anthology of 107+ avadānas in elegant kāvya verse, completed c. 1037 and later supplemented by his son Somendra. It became canonical in Tibet, where it was translated into the Tengyur.

SA_K10_kbavk64u.title: Sudhanakinnaryavadāna
SA_K10_kbavk64u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_K10_kbavk64u.summary: A pallava of Kṣemendra's Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā retelling the popular love-and-devotion legend of Prince Sudhana and the kinnarī (celestial half-bird maiden) Manoharā. The tale narrates their union, separation, and Sudhana's arduous quest to recover Manoharā, framed as a former-life story of the Bodhisattva illustrating perseverance and loyalty.
SA_K10_kbavk64u.history: The Sudhana-Kinnarī story is among the most widely diffused Buddhist narratives across India and Southeast Asia (depicted at Borobudur and in Pali/Thai versions). Kṣemendra incorporated it as a verse avadāna in his eleventh-century Kashmiri anthology, later transmitted in the Tibetan Tengyur.

SA_K10_kbavk75u.title: Pratītyasamutpādāvadāna
SA_K10_kbavk75u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_K10_kbavk75u.summary: A pallava of Kṣemendra's Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā, the 'Pratītyasamutpāda' (Dependent Origination) avadāna. It frames the doctrine of conditioned arising and the twelve nidānas within a narrative illustrating how beings are bound in saṃsāra by karma and ignorance and liberated through insight into dependent origination.
SA_K10_kbavk75u.history: Part of Kṣemendra's c. 1037 verse anthology of bodhisattva avadānas, composed in Kashmir and later translated into Tibetan. The Avadānakalpalatā was prized as a model of devotional Buddhist kāvya and circulated widely in Nepal and Tibet.

SA_K10_kbavk93u.title: Sumāgadhāvadāna [Sumāgadhāvadānasūtra]
SA_K10_kbavk93u.author: Kṣemendra
SA_K10_kbavk93u.date_estimate: c. 1037 CE (Kṣemendra fl. c. 990-1070 CE); underlying story by c. 2nd c. CE
SA_K10_kbavk93u.summary: A pallava of Kṣemendra's Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā retelling the story of Sumāgadhā, daughter of the merchant Anāthapiṇḍada, who is married into a Jain (nirgrantha) household in distant Puṇḍravardhana. Disgusted by the Jain ascetics, she invites the Buddha, who arrives miraculously and converts the city; the Buddha then relates her meritorious past lives.
SA_K10_kbavk93u.history: The Sumāgadhā narrative is an old and popular Buddhist tale circulating in Gandhāra by the 2nd century CE and existing in independent Sanskrit avadāna form and Tibetan/Chinese versions (cf. Toh 346); Kṣemendra reworked it as a verse avadāna in his eleventh-century Kashmiri anthology. The version here is the Avadānakalpalatā pallava attributed to Kṣemendra.

SA_K10_klpdrapu.title: Kalpadrumāvadānamālā
SA_K10_klpdrapu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_klpdrapu.genre: avadāna (narrative legend collection), composed in verse
SA_K10_klpdrapu.date_estimate: later than the Avadānaśataka; broadly mid-to-late 1st millennium CE or later medieval (disputed; known only from late Nepalese manuscripts)
SA_K10_klpdrapu.summary: A 'garland of avadānas' (avadānamālā), the 'Wish-Tree Garland of Avadānas' is a poetic verse elaboration of Buddhist legends illustrating karma and moral cause-and-effect. It begins by elaborating the last story of the Avadānaśataka and frames its legends as conversations between Emperor Aśoka and the elder Upagupta.
SA_K10_klpdrapu.history: One of several Sanskrit avadānamālās drawing partly on the older prose Avadānaśataka and partly on other sources, recast entirely in epic ślokas in a style reminiscent of the Purāṇas. It survives only in manuscript and remains largely unedited and untranslated; scholars have called for a critical edition.

SA_K10_mahama_u.title: Mahāmantrānusāriṇī
SA_K10_mahama_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_mahama_u.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (text grouped into the Pañcarakṣā by c. 800 CE; Sanskrit mss from 11th c. onward)
SA_K10_mahama_u.summary: A protective dhāraṇī scripture, the 'Great Mantra-Follower,' which is one of the five texts of the Pañcarakṣā ('Five Protections') collection. It contains spells, lists of benefits, and ritual instructions aimed at worldly welfare—curing snakebite, averting disease and disaster, and overcoming fear.
SA_K10_mahama_u.history: The Pañcarakṣā group (Mahāpratisarā, Mahāmāyūrī, Mahāsāhasrapramardanī, Mahāmantrānusāriṇī, Mahāśītavatī) is first attested as a set in Tibetan catalogues around 800 CE; the surviving Sanskrit collection is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts from the 11th century onward. The deity is described as blue, three-faced and twelve-armed in the Niṣpannayogāvalī.

SA_K10_maivy_pu.title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa [Maitreyavyākaraṇasūtra]
SA_K10_maivy_pu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_maivy_pu.date_estimate: uncertain; c. 3rd-5th c. CE (Gilgit-era fragments c. 400-600 CE)
SA_K10_maivy_pu.summary: The 'Prophecy of Maitreya' foretells the future advent of Maitreya, the next Buddha, and the ideal world of his time. It describes how gods, humans, and other beings will have their doubts dispelled and their cravings cut off through Maitreya's teaching, enabling them to lead the holy life.
SA_K10_maivy_pu.history: A noncanonical Sanskrit prophetic text of uncertain origin and date, paralleling the Pali Anāgatavaṃsa. Early fragments have been found at Mes Aynak (Afghanistan) and Gilgit, predating more complete Nepalese, Tibetan, and Chinese witnesses; the Maitreya cult developed roughly contemporaneously with that of Amitābha around the 3rd century CE.

SA_K10_n206u.title: Avikalpapraveśadhāraṇī [Avikalpapraveśadhāraṇīsūtra]
SA_K10_n206u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n206u.date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (influential in Yogācāra by the 5th-6th c.)
SA_K10_n206u.summary: A short Mahāyāna sūtra, 'Entering into Nonconceptuality,' teaching the attainment of the nonconceptual realm (nirvikalpa) through progressively relinquishing all conceptual signs by not directing the mind toward them. It maps a graded sequence of abandoning conceptual marks corresponding to stages of the bodhisattva path.
SA_K10_n206u.history: From an early period it was closely associated with the Five Treatises of Maitreya and is regarded as a source text for the Dharmadharmatāvibhāga. Indian commentaries include Kamalaśīla's Avikalpapraveśadhāraṇīṭīkā and a lost work by Śākyamitra; it was cited by Yogācārins such as Sthiramati and Ratnākaraśānti.

SA_K10_n262u.title: Āryānityatāsūtra
SA_K10_n262u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n262u.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (Chinese translation by Fatian, 984 CE)
SA_K10_n262u.summary: The 'Noble Sūtra on Impermanence' is a short discourse on the impermanence of all conditioned states (anitya). The Buddha teaches that regardless of social status, rebirth in a heaven, or even the attainment of arhatship, pratyekabuddhahood, or buddhahood, all that lives must die, concluding with verses urging the audience to bring conditioned states to rest.
SA_K10_n262u.history: A brief canonical sūtra preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and a Chinese translation by Fatian (984 CE) whose content broadly agrees across versions. Note that two distinct works share the title Anityatāsūtra in the Tibetan collection.

SA_K10_n266u.title: Āryasāgaranāgarājaparipṛcchānāmamahāyānasūtra
SA_K10_n266u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n266u.summary: 'The Questions of the Nāga King Sāgara' is set in the undersea palace of the nāga king Sāgara, where the Buddha teaches and acts as a peacemaker amid conflicts between gods and asuras and between nāgas and garuḍas. Several distinct recensions exist; their teachings range from the ten nonvirtuous deeds to the merit of reciting the four aphorisms (dharmoddāna) of the Dharma.
SA_K10_n266u.history: Known in three substantially different versions extant in both the Tibetan Kangyur and the Taishō canon (T0598, T0599, T0601; Toh 153-155). The nāga king Sāgara also figures prominently in the Avataṃsaka and Lotus sūtras.

SA_K10_n267u.title: Āryasaṃghāṭasūtra
SA_K10_n267u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n267u.date_estimate: by c. 5th c. CE (translated into Khotanese before mid-5th c.; Gilgit mss 5th c.)
SA_K10_n267u.summary: The 'Saṅghāṭa Sūtra,' a 'dharma-paryāya' widely circulated in northwest India and Central Asia, emphasizes the immense merit, purification of karma, and protection gained from hearing, reciting, or copying the text. It frames its teaching through dialogues with the bodhisattva Sarvaśūra and recounts past-life and afterlife narratives stressing impermanence and the path to liberation.
SA_K10_n267u.history: Among the most popular Mahāyāna scriptures in the region: more copies were found in the Gilgit cache than of the Lotus, Diamond, or Prajñāpāramitā sūtras. It was translated into Khotanese, Chinese, Sogdian, and Tibetan between the 5th and 10th centuries.

SA_K10_n269u.title: Āryatriratnānusmṛtisūtra
SA_K10_n269u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n269u.summary: The 'Sūtra of the Recollection of the Noble Three Jewels' is a short text (in some recensions a set of three short texts) presenting formulas for the recollection (anusmṛti) of the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Saṅgha. It enumerates the qualities of each of the Three Jewels and is commonly recited as a devotional practice to purify karma and generate merit.
SA_K10_n269u.history: A widely recited liturgical scripture across Buddhist traditions, transmitted in Sanskrit and Tibetan and forming a core part of daily chants; it has been commented upon and incorporated into ritual practice in Tibetan and other communities.

SA_K10_n2942u.title: Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra
SA_K10_n2942u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n2942u.date_estimate: first half of the 1st millennium CE (composite)
SA_K10_n2942u.summary: A very large sūtra ('Application of Mindfulness of the True Dharma') treating karma, the realms of rebirth, Buddhist cosmology, and meditation. Its famous chapter 7 offers an unusually detailed, anatomical and osteological meditation on the body, including descriptions of internal worms and the winds that destroy them at death, presented as a dispassionate 'scientific' observation rather than as a generator of disgust.
SA_K10_n2942u.history: An extensive Indian Buddhist sūtra dated to the first half of the first millennium, surviving in Sanskrit fragments and in Chinese and Tibetan translations; it is a major source for Buddhist cosmology and karma doctrine and has attracted recent scholarly study of its distinctive body-meditation.

SA_K10_n443u.title: Mahāmeghasūtra
SA_K10_n443u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n443u.genre: Mahāyāna sūtra (with dhāraṇī and rain ritual)
SA_K10_n443u.date_estimate: by early 5th c. CE (Chinese translation by Dharmakṣema, 385-433)
SA_K10_n443u.summary: The 'Great Cloud Sūtra' is a tathāgatagarbha-type Mahāyāna sūtra teaching that the Buddha is eternal and does not truly enter parinirvāṇa. Asked by the king of the nāgas (dragons) for a way to bring rain, the Buddha teaches the 'single dharma' of loving-kindness and provides a dhāraṇī together with a ritual manual for a rain-making rite involving a mandala, a blue canopy, water vases, and images of four dragon kings.
SA_K10_n443u.history: Translated into Chinese by Dharmakṣema (early 5th c.) and into Tibetan by Surendrabodhi and Yeshe Dé in the 9th century. It became central to imperial state ritual under Empress Wu (683-690) and to court rain-making rituals in Japan for centuries.

SA_K10_n446u.title: Meghasūtra
SA_K10_n446u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n446u.summary: The 'Cloud Sūtra' is the earliest surviving Sanskrit Buddhist rain-making text.
