It contains a long series of invocations to the nāgas (snake-kings) for rain, prescribing a rite called the 'great-cloud-circle' (mahāmeghamaṇḍala) performed in an open space, in which various nāgarājas—Triśīrṣaka, Pañcaśīrṣaka, Saptaśīrṣaka, Navaśīrṣaka and others—are depicted in the four quarters.
SA_K10_n446u.history: Partially edited and translated by Cecil Bendall (1880) from a Nepalese manuscript now in Cambridge University Library (Or.135). It is distinct from, though thematically related to, the larger tathāgatagarbha Mahāmeghasūtra.

SA_K10_n526u.title: Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhānavyūhasūtra
SA_K10_n526u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_n526u.summary: A dhāraṇī sūtra whose full title is roughly 'King of Arrayed Empowerments of All Tathāgatas, by which the Buddha-fields and Beings are Beheld.' It concerns the empowerment (adhiṣṭhāna) of all the Tathāgatas and offers a dhāraṇī through which one beholds the Buddha-fields and surveys all beings, with associated merit and ritual benefits.
SA_K10_n526u.history: A relatively obscure Mahāyāna dhāraṇī scripture preserved in Sanskrit and in Chinese (Zhuangyan wang tuoluoni zhou jing) and Tibetan translations, transmitted within the broader Indian dhāraṇī-sūtra literature; it has received little modern scholarly study.

SA_K10_nagasu.title: nagarasūtra
SA_K10_nagasu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_nagasu.date_estimate: early Buddhist material; Sanskrit recension transmitted within the Sarvāstivāda canon (text material BCE-early CE; mss later)
SA_K10_nagasu.summary: The 'City Sūtra' (Nagarasūtra), preserved in Sanskrit within the Nidānasaṃyukta, recounts the Buddha's discovery of dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda) through the simile of a man who finds an ancient overgrown city and reports it to a king who then restores it. It is the discourse usually known as the Nagaropama ('Simile of the City') sūtra.
SA_K10_nagasu.history: Part of the Sarvāstivāda Saṃyuktāgama / Nidānasaṃyukta, recovered among Sanskrit fragments from Central Asia (Turfan) and edited by Ch. Tripāṭhī in 'Fünfundzwanzig Sūtras des Nidānasaṃyukta'; it has counterparts in the Pali canon and the Chinese Saṃyuktāgama.

SA_K10_nagsu_pu.title: Nagaropamasūtra
SA_K10_nagsu_pu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_nagsu_pu.date_estimate: early Buddhist material; Sanskrit recension transmitted within the Sarvāstivāda canon (text BCE-early CE; mss later)
SA_K10_nagsu_pu.summary: 'The Discourse on the Simile of the City' relates how the Buddha discovered dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda) before his awakening, comparing his rediscovery of the ancient path to a traveler who chances upon a long-forgotten city. It expounds the chain of conditioned arising and its cessation.
SA_K10_nagsu_pu.history: Preserved in Sanskrit within the Nidānasaṃyukta of the Sarvāstivāda Saṃyuktāgama, with fragments from Turfan edited by Ch. Tripāṭhī; it parallels the Pali Nagara Sutta (SN 12.65) and the Chinese Saṃyuktāgama.

SA_K10_nidansyu.title: Nidānasaṃyukta
SA_K10_nidansyu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_nidansyu.date_estimate: early Buddhist material; Sanskrit recension transmitted in the Sarvāstivāda canon (text BCE-early CE; Turfan mss mid-1st millennium CE)
SA_K10_nidansyu.summary: The 'Connected Discourses on Causation' is the section of the Sarvāstivāda Saṃyuktāgama gathering sūtras devoted to dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda) and related causal doctrine. It comprises a set of short discourses—classically a group of twenty-five—including the Nagaropamasūtra and Pratītyasamutpādasūtra.
SA_K10_nidansyu.history: Reconstructed from Sanskrit manuscript fragments recovered from Turfan in Central Asia and edited by Chandrabhāl Tripāṭhī in 'Fünfundzwanzig Sūtras des Nidānasaṃyukta' (1962); it corresponds to the Nidāna section of the Pali Saṃyutta Nikāya and the Chinese Saṃyuktāgama.

SA_K10_ratnakeu.title: Mahāsannipātaratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra [Ratnaketuparivarta, Ratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra]
SA_K10_ratnakeu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_ratnakeu.genre: dhāraṇī sūtra (part of a great-assembly anthology)
SA_K10_ratnakeu.date_estimate: by early 5th c. CE (Chinese translation by Dharmakṣema, from 414)
SA_K10_ratnakeu.summary: The 'Ratnaketu Dhāraṇī' is one of the constituent sūtras of the Mahāsaṃnipāta ('Great Assembly') collection. It narrates the Buddha's subjugation of Māra and hostile forces and provides protective dhāraṇīs, set within the cosmic 'great assembly' framework typical of the anthology.
SA_K10_ratnakeu.history: Belongs to the Mahāvaipulya Mahāsaṃnipāta Sūtra, a Mahāyāna anthology of seventeen sūtras whose complete form survives only in Chinese (begun by Dharmakṣema in 414); individual sūtras like the Ratnaketu survive in Sanskrit and in the Tibetan canon (Toh 138) and has been translated by the 84000 project.

SA_K10_samghscu.title: Saṃghāṭasūtra [Saṅghāṭasūtra]
SA_K10_samghscu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_samghscu.date_estimate: by c. 5th c. CE (Gilgit mss 5th c.; Khotanese translation before mid-5th c.)
SA_K10_samghscu.summary: The 'Saṅghāṭa Sūtra' is a popular Mahāyāna 'dharma-paryāya' promising vast merit, purification of negative karma, and protection to those who hear, recite, or copy it. Through dialogues (notably with the bodhisattva Sarvaśūra) it teaches on impermanence, the experiences of beings after death, and the path to liberation. This is a second catalogue entry for the same work as Āryasaṃghāṭasūtra (K10n267u).
SA_K10_samghscu.history: One of the most widely copied scriptures of northwest India and Central Asia—found in greater numbers at Gilgit than the Lotus or Diamond sūtras—and translated into Khotanese, Chinese, Sogdian, and Tibetan between the 5th and 10th centuries.

SA_K10_samyu_wu.title: Saṃyuktāgama
SA_K10_samyu_wu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_samyu_wu.date_estimate: early Buddhist material (canonical core BCE-early CE); Sanskrit fragments and Chinese translation mid-1st millennium CE
SA_K10_samyu_wu.summary: The 'Connected Discourses,' one of the four principal āgamas, is a large collection of (mostly short) sūtras grouped thematically by subject (e.g., the aggregates, the sense-bases, dependent origination, the path). It corresponds to the Pali Saṃyutta Nikāya and is associated with the Sarvāstivāda school.
SA_K10_samyu_wu.history: The main Chinese version (Za ahan jing, T99) was translated by Guṇabhadra and Baoyun in 435-436 CE; portions of a Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda recension survive as fragments from Central Asia (e.g., the Nidānasaṃyukta), and several sūtras survive in Tibetan.

SA_K10_sjdh_u.title: Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī [Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka(sūtra)]
SA_K10_sjdh_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_sjdh_u.genre: dhāraṇī passage within a Mahāyāna sūtra
SA_K10_sjdh_u.date_estimate: c. 4th c. CE (the parent Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka formed around the 4th c.; Sanskrit text dated c. 414-421)
SA_K10_sjdh_u.summary: The Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī ('Dhāraṇī Embodying the Mode of Omniscience') is a dhāraṇī doctrine taught in the second chapter (Dhāraṇīmukhaparivarta) of the Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka ('White Lotus of Compassion'). By attaining this dhāraṇī one is said to grasp omniscience (sarvajñajñāna) and all the Buddha's teachings; it notably contains a 'Dravidian' mantra phrase (Dramiḍa-mantrapada).
SA_K10_sjdh_u.history: The parent Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka is a Mahāyāna sūtra recounting the origins of many buddhas and bodhisattvas (especially Śākyamuni), centered on the cakravartin Araṇemin and his priest Samudrareṇu; it was formed around the 4th century, with the Sanskrit text dated c. 414-421 CE.

SA_K10_srajsgiu.title: Samādhirājasūtra
SA_K10_srajsgiu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_srajsgiu.summary: The 'King of Samādhis Sūtra' (also Candrapradīpasūtra, 'Moon-Lamp Sūtra') centers on a particular samādhi described as 'the samādhi manifested as the sameness of the essential nature of all dharmas,' presented as the key to the whole path and to the virtues of the buddhas. It expounds emptiness, bodhisattva conduct, mendicancy, past-life tales, and future prophecies.
SA_K10_srajsgiu.history: Its redaction is variously dated from the 2nd-3rd century to the 6th century (the date of the earliest manuscript), and some scholars hold the date cannot be fixed. It is a major scriptural source for the Madhyamaka school, cited by Candrakīrti, Śāntideva, and others, though its doctrinal stance is not purely Mādhyamika.

SA_K10_srnsu.title: Śroṇasūtra
SA_K10_srnsu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_srnsu.date_estimate: core material likely 1st millennium BCE oral tradition; redacted within the early Buddhist canon, exact date uncertain
SA_K10_srnsu.summary: A canonical Buddhist discourse (sūtra) connected with the disciple Śroṇa (Pali Soṇa, often Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa), an important early follower of the Buddha. Such texts typically narrate the conversion, ordination, or instruction of the named disciple and present a point of doctrine or monastic discipline through that episode.
SA_K10_srnsu.history: Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa is a well-attested figure in early Buddhist literature (e.g. the Divyāvadāna and the Vinaya traditions), famed for requesting modifications of monastic rules for border regions. The precise sūtra intended by this short title is not firmly identifiable, so its identity is held with reservation.

SA_K10_sukhvysu.title: Sukhāvatyamṛtavyūha (Sukhāvatī[amṛta]vyūhasūtra), the Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha
SA_K10_sukhvysu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_sukhvysu.summary: The shorter of the two principal Pure Land sūtras describing the buddha-field (Sukhāvatī) of the Buddha Amitābha. The Buddha praises the splendor and bliss of that western paradise and teaches that beings who hear and keep Amitābha's name with single-minded faith will be reborn there. It is one of the foundational scriptures of Pure Land Buddhism.
SA_K10_sukhvysu.history: Composed in Sanskrit, probably in northwest India (Gandhāra region), and translated into Chinese repeatedly between c. 240 and 400 CE, most famously by Kumārajīva in 402; one Tibetan translation exists from the 8th-9th c. by Dānaśīla and Yeshé Dé. The Sanskrit text survives alongside Chinese and Tibetan versions.

SA_K10_tbimbk_u.title: Tathāgatabimbakārāpaṇasūtra
SA_K10_tbimbk_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_tbimbk_u.summary: A short Mahayana sūtra on the merit of commissioning and establishing images of the Buddha (tathāgata-bimba). The Buddha enumerates the benefits accruing to a person who sponsors the making of a Buddha image, such as prosperity, beauty, wealth, royal status and favorable rebirths.
SA_K10_tbimbk_u.history: Preserved in the Tibetan Kangyur (sūtra section) under titles meaning 'The Benefits of Establishing Images of the Noble Tathāgata.' It belongs to a genre of merit-extolling sūtras that supported the cult of image-making and donation in Buddhist communities.

SA_k10vrksu.title: Vṛkṣasūtra
SA_k10vrksu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_k10vrksu.tradition: uncertain; possibly Buddhist or secular/scientific (Vṛkṣāyurveda tradition)
SA_k10vrksu.summary: A short text whose title means 'aphorisms on trees.' If it belongs to the Indian science of plant life (vṛkṣāyurveda), it would treat tree anatomy, planting, soil, watering, propagation and the treatment of plant diseases. The exact work intended here cannot be confidently determined.
SA_k10vrksu.history: A 'Vṛkṣasūtra' / Vṛkṣāyurveda tradition is associated with figures such as Sūrapāla (c. 10th-11th c.) and is excerpted in works like the Bṛhatsaṃhitā of Varāhamihira and the Agni Purāṇa. Because this entry is catalogued among Buddhist (K10) sūtras, its identification is ambiguous and held with low confidence.

SA_K12_adhsatpu.title: Adhyardhaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā (Adhyardhaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra)
SA_K12_adhsatpu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_adhsatpu.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 150 Lines,' a short esoteric Prajñāpāramitā scripture. It presents the perfection of wisdom through a tantric framework, with chapters spoken by various buddhas, each expounding a method of prajñāpāramitā summarized by a mystic syllable. It is a key text of esoteric Buddhism and remains widely recited in Japanese Shingon.
SA_K12_adhsatpu.history: Belonging to the late, tantric phase of Prajñāpāramitā literature, it bridges sūtra and tantra. It was transmitted to East Asia and became central to Shingon practice; Tibetan and Chinese versions also exist.

SA_K12_bsu004_u.title: Sitātapatrā
SA_K12_bsu004_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu004_u.summary: The Sitātapatrā ('White Parasol') Uṣṇīṣa dhāraṇī, a protective scripture centered on the goddess Sitātapatrā, said to have emerged from the uṣṇīṣa (crown protuberance) of the Buddha while in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three. The dhāraṇī is apotropaic, guarding reciters against supernatural dangers, evil spirits and harm.
SA_K12_bsu004_u.history: Sitātapatrā became a fully developed protective deity venerated in both Mahayana and Vajrayana traditions, with multi-armed iconographic forms. Related dhāraṇī material is embedded in the Śūraṅgama Sūtra; the text is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_K12_bsu012_u.title: Bhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājasūtra
SA_K12_bsu012_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu012_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-7th c. CE; Chinese translation by 317 CE
SA_K12_bsu012_u.summary: The sūtra of Bhaiṣajyaguru, the 'Medicine Master and Lapis Lazuli Light King' (the Medicine Buddha). It recounts his twelve great vows made as a bodhisattva to heal illness, relieve suffering and guide beings to enlightenment, and prescribes devotional and ritual practices for healing and protection.
SA_K12_bsu012_u.history: Sanskrit manuscripts survive from Gilgit and Bāmiyān (no later than the 7th c.); the earliest Chinese translation was by Śrīmitra in 317 CE, and the cult spread along the Silk Road into China, Tibet and East Asia. It is a foundational text for the widespread Medicine Buddha devotion.

SA_K12_bsu035_u.title: Suvarṇaprabhāsasūtra (Suvarṇaprabhāsottamasūtrendrarāja)
SA_K12_bsu035_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu035_u.date_estimate: reached current form by c. 4th-5th c. CE; oldest strata 1st-2nd c. CE
SA_K12_bsu035_u.summary: The 'Golden Light Sūtra,' a highly influential Mahayana scripture. It combines confession and purification practices, teachings on emptiness and the Buddha's lifespan, and chapters in which protective deities (e.g. Sarasvatī, Śrī, the Four Great Kings) vow to guard kings and realms who honor the sūtra, making it a key text of Buddhist statecraft and protection.
SA_K12_bsu035_u.history: Originally Sanskrit, translated into Chinese by Dharmakṣema (early 5th c.) and most completely by Yijing in 703 CE, and later into Tibetan and other languages. It became one of the most popular and politically important Mahayana sūtras across Central and East Asia.

SA_K12_bsu041_u.title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa (Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa)
SA_K12_bsu041_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu041_u.genre: tantra / ritual manual (kalpa) with sutra framing
SA_K12_bsu041_u.date_estimate: core c. 6th c. CE; compiled and expanded through the 8th c. CE and after
SA_K12_bsu041_u.summary: The 'Root Manual of the Rites of Mañjuśrī,' a large ritual compendium affiliated with the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī. Across fifty-five chapters the Buddha sets out mantras, maṇḍalas, consecrations (including vase consecration), iconography, astrology and rites for worldly and soteriological ends. It also famously contains a verse 'prophecy' section used as a quasi-historical source for early medieval Indian dynasties.
SA_K12_bsu041_u.history: Often cited as the earliest extant Indian Buddhist tantra. Scholars date a core verse text to around the 6th c. with the bulk compiled through the 8th c. (Matsunaga, Sanderson) and accretions thereafter; in Tibetan classification it is a Kriyā-tantra.

SA_K12_bsu050_u.title: Svalpākṣarā Prajñāpāramitā
SA_K12_bsu050_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu050_u.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in a Few Syllables/Words,' a short esoteric Prajñāpāramitā scripture in which Avalokiteśvara is a chief speaker. It condenses the perfection of wisdom into a brief form combined with mantra/dhāraṇī, intended for recitation by those unable to study the larger Prajñāpāramitā corpus.
SA_K12_bsu050_u.history: One of about forty Prajñāpāramitā texts produced in India, belonging to the later, condensed and tantric-flavored group of such works. It is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_K12_bsu055_u.title: Kauśikaprajñāpāramitāsūtra
SA_K12_bsu055_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu055_u.date_estimate: extant Sanskrit/Chinese recension late 10th-early 11th c. CE; earlier Indian core
SA_K12_bsu055_u.summary: A condensed Prajñāpāramitā sūtra named for Kauśika (an epithet of Indra), to whom the Buddha addresses it. The Buddha equates the characteristics of the perfection of wisdom with those of all phenomena, the five aggregates and elements, stressing the nonduality of conventional phenomena and emptiness; it incorporates mantra and is classed among esoteric Prajñāpāramitā texts.
SA_K12_bsu055_u.history: Brief (about four Tibetan folios), it blends sūtra and tantra and is variously placed in both the Prajñāpāramitā and Tantra sections of the Tibetan Kangyur. A longer Central Asian version with additional mantras also exists; the extant Sanskrit largely matches the late Chinese translation.

SA_K12_bsu056_u.title: Adhyardhaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā (Adhyardhaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra)
SA_K12_bsu056_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_bsu056_u.summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 150 Lines,' a short esoteric Prajñāpāramitā scripture presenting the perfection of wisdom through a tantric framework, with chapters spoken by various buddhas and summarized by mystic syllables. (This entry duplicates the same work as K12adhsatpu in this batch.)
SA_K12_bsu056_u.history: A late, tantric Prajñāpāramitā text bridging sūtra and tantra; it became central to Japanese Shingon practice and is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_K12_dhvajkdu.title: Dhvajāgrakeyūrā-dhāraṇī
SA_K12_dhvajkdu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_dhvajkdu.summary: A protective (apotropaic) dhāraṇī set in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, where Śakra, defeated by the asuras, asks the Buddha for help and is given this spell. Reciters become victorious in conflict, and those who fix the formula atop banners or tie it on the body are guarded by the goddess Dhvajāgrakeyūrā, who removes fear and grants protection and prosperity.
SA_K12_dhvajkdu.history: A dhāraṇī of the state-protection genre whose formula was personified as a goddess; the text instructs followers to inscribe the long dhāraṇī on banner-flags. (This is one of two identical entries in this batch.)

SA_K12_dhvajkeu.title: Dhvajāgrakeyūrā-dhāraṇī
SA_K12_dhvajkeu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_dhvajkeu.summary: A protective dhāraṇī in which Śakra, lord of the gods, defeated by the asuras, receives this spell from the Buddha. The goddess Dhvajāgrakeyūrā grants victory in conflict, removes fear and bestows protection and abundance on those who recite the formula or fasten it to banners or the body.
SA_K12_dhvajkeu.history: Belongs to the Buddhist apotropaic/state-protection dhāraṇī tradition, with the protective formula personified as a goddess inscribed on banner-flags. (This entry is a duplicate of K12dhvajkdu in this batch.)

SA_K12_ekmuhr_u.title: Ekādaśamukhahṛdaya
SA_K12_ekmuhr_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_ekmuhr_u.date_estimate: by c. 6th c. CE (Chinese translation c. 570 CE)
SA_K12_ekmuhr_u.summary: The 'Heart-dhāraṇī of the Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara' (Avalokiteśvara-ekādaśamukha-hṛdaya-dhāraṇī-sūtra), centered on a potent dhāraṇī of the eleven-faced form of the compassionate bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara. It promises protection, healing and spiritual advancement; the eleven faces symbolize the bodhisattva's simultaneous observation of the ten directions and beyond.
SA_K12_ekmuhr_u.history: An early esoteric scripture: a Chinese translation by Yaśogupta appeared around 570 CE and another by Xuanzang in 656 CE. The chant remains popular throughout East Asia and is preserved in Sanskrit, Chinese and Tibetan.

SA_K12_ganphrdu.title: Gaṇapatihṛdaya-Dhāraṇī
SA_K12_ganphrdu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_ganphrdu.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (uncertain); transmitted widely by the medieval period
SA_K12_ganphrdu.summary: The 'Exalted Heart-dhāraṇī of Gaṇapati,' spoken by the Buddha on Vulture Peak to Ānanda. It teaches a heart-mantra of Gaṇapati (the Buddhist form of Gaṇeśa, lord of the gaṇas): whoever upholds and recites it daily will succeed in all endeavors, gain wealth and prosperity, and avoid poverty and suffering.
SA_K12_ganphrdu.history: Preserved in both the Tantra Collection and the Compendium of Dhāraṇīs of the Tibetan Kangyur. In Nepalese practice it is one of the saptavāra ('seven days') dhāraṇīs, associated with a particular weekday; in Buddhist tradition Gaṇapati is treated as a worldly protector or, in some Mahayana accounts, an emanation of Avalokiteśvara.

SA_K12_grahmdhu.title: Grahamātṛkānāmadhāraṇī
SA_K12_grahmdhu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_grahmdhu.date_estimate: materialized in current form in the second half of the 1st millennium CE
SA_K12_grahmdhu.summary: The 'Dhāraṇī of the Mother of Planets,' a short ritual text for protection against malign planetary (graha) influences. It sets out a ritual framework with visualizations and offerings to the nine planets in anthropomorphic form, each with its own mantras, creating a protective astral schema for the practitioner.
SA_K12_grahmdhu.history: Very popular across North India, Central Asia and Tibet in the later first millennium, it reflects the assimilation of Brahmanical planetary worship into Buddhist ritual. Surviving manuscripts are late (no earlier than 1492 CE), but the text itself is considerably older; the Tibetan translation belongs to the early medieval period.

SA_K12_madadh_u.title: Mahādaṇḍadhāraṇīśītavatī
SA_K12_madadh_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_madadh_u.date_estimate: Tibetan translation c. 800 CE; Indian composition earlier in the 1st millennium CE
SA_K12_madadh_u.summary: An apotropaic Buddhist scripture, the 'Great Staff Dhāraṇī, the Cool One' (Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī-Śītavatī). It provides protective dhāraṇīs against dangers such as snakebite, illness and malign forces, with recitation practices including the use of knotted protective threads.
SA_K12_madadh_u.history: Its title evolved in transmission from Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī toward Mahāśītavatī, the name under which it was incorporated as one member of the Pañcarakṣā ('Five Protections') collection. Over 300 Pañcarakṣā manuscripts survive, chiefly from Nepal and eastern India; the Tibetan translation dates to around 800 CE.

SA_K12_mahpratu.title: Mahāpratisarāmahāvidyārājñī
SA_K12_mahpratu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_mahpratu.date_estimate: first half of the 7th c. CE
SA_K12_mahpratu.summary: The 'Great Amulet, Great Queen of Spells,' one of the most popular Buddhist protective dhāraṇī texts. It contains two dhāraṇīs and several mantras with detailed instructions for their recitation and for making amulets, promising protection from dangers, disease and misfortune to those who wear or recite them.
SA_K12_mahpratu.history: Datable to the first half of the 7th c., with five fragmentary Gilgit birch-bark manuscripts as its earliest witnesses. It is the first of the five goddesses of the Pañcarakṣā collection and was enormously widespread, illustrating the evolution of dhāraṇīs in tantric Buddhism from the 6th-8th centuries; it was used as a worn talisman across Asia.

SA_K12_manivdhu.title: Mahāmaṇivipulavimānaviśvasupratiṣṭhitaguhyaparamarahasyakalparājadhāraṇī
SA_K12_manivdhu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_manivdhu.summary: A tantric dhāraṇī-scripture (kalparāja) associated with a great jewel-pavilion (mahāmaṇivipulavimāna), presenting a secret, supreme dhāraṇī together with rites and benefits. Such 'secret essence king of manuals' texts give the dhāraṇī plus instructions for its ritual use, including consecration, stūpa deposit and protection.
SA_K12_manivdhu.history: Known in Chinese as the 大寶廣博樓閣善住祕密陀羅尼經 (the 'Secret Dhāraṇī of the Well-Established Great-Jewel Vast Pavilion'). It circulated in Sanskrit, Chinese and Tibetan and belongs to the esoteric dhāraṇī/kalpa literature used in consecration and stūpa rites.

SA_K12_manjnspu.title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti
SA_K12_manjnspu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_manjnspu.summary: The 'Chanting of the Names of Mañjuśrī' is a celebrated tantric litany of roughly 160 verses (plus prose frame) in which the buddha-bodhisattva of wisdom, Mañjuśrī, is praised through long sequences of his names and epithets. The names equate Mañjuśrī with the ultimate reality (the gnosis-being Ādibuddha) and encode core doctrines of non-dual wisdom and the structure of the maṇḍala.
SA_K12_manjnspu.history: An influential text foundational to later Indo-Tibetan tantra, traditionally drawn from the Māyājāla cycle and connected to the Guhyasamāja, Hevajra, Guhyagarbha and (especially) Kālacakra traditions; an early and authoritative commentary is attributed to Vilāsavajra (8th c.). It became one of the most recited and commented-upon texts of Tibetan Buddhism.

SA_K12_maricdhu.title: Mārīcīnāmadhāraṇī
SA_K12_maricdhu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_maricdhu.summary: A dhāraṇī text dedicated to the goddess Mārīcī ('Ray of Light'), a deity of the dawn associated with light, invisibility and protection. The text supplies her protective incantation and the benefits of its recitation, used to shield the practitioner from enemies, dangers and obstacles.
SA_K12_maricdhu.history: Mārīcī's cult is attested early: the earliest Chinese translations of her dhāraṇī date to the sixth century (Liang dynasty), and Śāntideva quotes her spell in the eighth-century Śikṣāsamuccaya; Amoghavajra (Bukong) produced further versions in the late 8th c. The dhāraṇī circulated widely and entered the Tibetan canon, where it is preserved among the works of many later authors.

SA_K12_mekhdh_u.title: Mekhalā-dhāraṇī
SA_K12_mekhdh_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_mekhdh_u.summary: A short Buddhist dhāraṇī text bearing the name Mekhalā ('girdle/belt'). Like other texts of its class, it presents a protective incantation together with a brief narrative frame and a statement of the merits and protective benefits gained by reciting it.
SA_K12_mekhdh_u.history: A minor dhāraṇī preserved in the dhāraṇī/tantra sections of the Tibetan Kangyur and in Nepalese Sanskrit dhāraṇī compendia; its specific authorship, date and the precise identity of the 'Mekhalā' invoked are not securely established in scholarship.

SA_K12_mpratpru.title: Mahāpratisarā-vidyārājñī [Mahāpratisarādhāraṇī(sūtra)]
SA_K12_mpratpru.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_mpratpru.date_estimate: c. 6th-7th c. CE (one of the Pañcarakṣā)
SA_K12_mpratpru.summary: The 'Great Amulet, Queen of Spells' is the first and most prominent of the five protective dhāraṇīs of the Pañcarakṣā corpus. It comprises the dhāraṇī itself, enumerations of its benefits, and ritual instructions; the spell, often worn as an amulet, protects against sin, disease, evil influences, fire, weapons and calamity.
SA_K12_mpratpru.history: Mahāpratisarā became deified as one of the five Pañcarakṣā goddesses. The earliest evidence for the 'Five Great Dhāraṇīs' grouping comes from Tibetan catalogues c. 800 CE, though the surviving Sanskrit Pañcarakṣā collection is known from manuscripts of the eleventh century onward; Chinese translations of Mahāpratisarā appear from the seventh-eighth centuries.

SA_K12_mprsvdhu.title: Mahāpratisarāvidyādhāraṇī
SA_K12_mprsvdhu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_mprsvdhu.summary: A version/recension of the Mahāpratisarā ('Great Amulet') incantation, here transmitted under the title 'vidyā-dhāraṇī.' Like the related Mahāpratisarā texts it presents the protective spell of the goddess Mahāpratisarā together with its benefits, worn or recited as an apotropaic amulet against disease, evil and calamity.
SA_K12_mprsvdhu.history: This is a parallel/variant transmission of the Mahāpratisarā material that belongs to the Pañcarakṣā ('Five Protections') group; the Tibetan Kangyur preserves multiple closely related recensions of Mahāpratisarā across its sūtra, tantra and dhāraṇī sections. The grouping is attested from c. 800 CE, with surviving Sanskrit witnesses from the eleventh century onward.

SA_K12_n220u.title: Nārāyaṇaparipṛcchā āryamahāmāyāvijayavāhinī nāma dhāraṇī
SA_K12_n220u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n220u.summary: A dhāraṇī titled 'The Noble Mahāmāyā-Vijayavāhinī (Victory-Bearing) Incantation,' framed as a 'request/question by Nārāyaṇa' (Nārāyaṇaparipṛcchā). The Buddha, in response to Nārāyaṇa's request, teaches a protective and victory-granting spell along with its powers and benefits.
SA_K12_n220u.history: A short dhāraṇī preserved in the Tibetan Kangyur (paripṛcchā-framed dhāraṇī genre, in which a deity asks the Buddha for a protective formula). Its precise date and Indian provenance are not securely established; the 'Nārāyaṇa' frame reflects the absorption of Brahmanical deities as interlocutors in Buddhist spell-literature.

SA_K12_n221u.title: Ṣaṇmukhīdhāraṇī
SA_K12_n221u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n221u.summary: The 'Dhāraṇī of the Six Gates/Faces' (ṣaṇmukha, 'six-faced'). In it the Buddha teaches a dhāraṇī expressing six aspirations that counteract the causes of saṃsāric existence; practitioners are instructed to recite it three times by day and three times by night for purification and protection.
SA_K12_n221u.history: A short canonical dhāraṇī (Tōh. 526) transmitted in several almost identical recensions distributed across the sūtra, tantra and dhāraṇī sections of the Tibetan Kangyurs of the Tshalpa group; in the Thempangma Kangyurs it appears only in the tantra section. Translated and studied by the 84000 project.

SA_K12_n2920u.title: Amoghapāśakalparāja Part VI
SA_K12_n2920u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n2920u.date_estimate: c. 7th-8th c. CE (composite; this portion within that range)
SA_K12_n2920u.summary: Part VI of the Amoghapāśakalparāja, the 'Sovereign Ritual Manual of the Unfailing Lasso,' a large tantric ritual compendium centered on Amoghapāśa, a lasso-bearing form of Avalokiteśvara. This section continues the work's extensive ritual program: mantras, maṇḍala construction, image-worship, fire-offerings (homa) and sādhana procedures for mundane and soteriological aims.
SA_K12_n2920u.history: The Amoghapāśakalparāja is regarded by modern scholars as a collection of originally independent Amoghapāśa ritual manuals later compiled into one large work; its components were translated into Chinese over a long span (e.g., 587, 659, 693, 707-709 CE), indicating a composite text of the 7th-8th centuries. The shorter Amoghapāśahṛdaya forms its opening core.

SA_K12_n2922u.title: Amoghapāśakalparāja Part VII
SA_K12_n2922u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n2922u.date_estimate: c. 7th-8th c. CE (composite; this portion within that range)
SA_K12_n2922u.summary: Part VII of the Amoghapāśakalparāja, continuing the great ritual cycle of Amoghapāśa Avalokiteśvara. Like the preceding sections it sets out detailed liturgical instructions—mantra recitation, maṇḍala and altar construction, offerings, mudrās and rites for protection, healing, prosperity and liberation.
SA_K12_n2922u.history: One division of the composite Amoghapāśakalparāja, assembled from independently circulating Amoghapāśa ritual manuals and transmitted via the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon and the Tibetan/Chinese canons. The whole reflects the developed Avalokiteśvara spell-and-ritual literature of the 7th-8th centuries CE.

SA_K12_n2949u.title: Dhāraṇīsaṃgraha
SA_K12_n2949u.author: anonymous/traditional (compilation)
SA_K12_n2949u.summary: A 'Collection of Dhāraṇīs'—an anthology gathering numerous protective and ritual incantations associated with various buddhas, bodhisattvas and deities into a single compendium. Such collections served as practical liturgical handbooks supplying spells for protection, healing, longevity, merit and ritual use.
SA_K12_n2949u.history: Dhāraṇīsaṃgraha is a generic title for compiled spell-collections that circulated widely in Indian, Nepalese and Tibetan Buddhism (and in Chinese/Korean parallels such as the Odae chinŏn). The contents and ordering vary by manuscript and tradition; individual dhāraṇīs within it span the whole 1st millennium CE, while the assembled collections are generally attested in later manuscripts.

SA_K12_n2990u.title: Āryaśrīgrahamātṛkā nāma dhāraṇī
SA_K12_n2990u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_n2990u.summary: The 'Noble Glorious Grahamātṛkā Dhāraṇī'—an incantation invoking the 'Mother of the Planets/Astral Deities' (Grahamātṛkā). It provides a ritual framework with mantras, visualizations and offerings to the nine planets (navagraha) and astral powers, recited for longevity, safety and protection against malefic astral influences.
SA_K12_n2990u.history: The Grahamātṛkādhāraṇī was popular in North India, Central Asia, Nepal and Tibet in the latter half of the first millennium and represents the integration of Brahmanical planetary worship into Buddhism. The dhāraṇī was itself deified as the goddess Grahamātṛkā (by the early 14th c. at the latest), with related maṇḍalas appearing in China in the 12th-13th centuries; the practice survives among Newar Buddhists.

SA_K12_namekvsu.title: Namaskāraikaviṃśatistotra [Namastāre-ekaviṃśatistotraguṇasahita / Tārā-ekaviṃśatistotra]
SA_K12_namekvsu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed; ascribed to the Buddha/Tārā cycle)
SA_K12_namekvsu.genre: stotra (hymn of praise) with embedded dhāraṇī
SA_K12_namekvsu.summary: The famous 'Twenty-One Homages to Tārā' (Praise in Twenty-One Verses of Homage), a liturgy of twenty-one verses, each beginning with namo, praising twenty-one forms/aspects of the savioress goddess Tārā by describing her attributes and protective powers, framed by an invocation and a concluding statement of the benefits of recitation. The mantra oṃ tāre tuttāre ture svāhā and seed-syllables are embedded in it.
SA_K12_namekvsu.history: One of the most widely recited Buddhist devotions, traditionally drawn from Tārā's tantric corpus and transmitted in Sanskrit and Tibetan (Tōh. 438). It is foundational to the cult of the Twenty-One Tārās across Indo-Tibetan and Newar Buddhism and has generated extensive commentarial and ritual literature.

SA_K12_prajnhsu.title: Prajñāpāramitā-hṛdaya-sūtra [Prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtra]
SA_K12_prajnhsu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_prajnhsu.date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (Sanskrit; disputed — Conze argued c. 350 CE, Nattier argues a Chinese origin)
SA_K12_prajnhsu.summary: The 'Heart of the Perfection of Wisdom Sūtra,' the famous short distillation of Prajñāpāramitā teaching. Avalokiteśvara expounds to Śāriputra the emptiness (śūnyatā) of the five aggregates—'form is emptiness, emptiness is form'—negating the categories of the Abhidharma and culminating in the celebrated mantra gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā.
SA_K12_prajnhsu.history: The most popular and widely recited Mahāyāna scripture. Edward Conze dated the Sanskrit original to c. 350 CE, but Jan Nattier's influential 1992 study argues the short text was first composed in China (largely from Kumārajīva's translation of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā/Mahāprajñāpāramitā-upadeśa) and later back-translated into Sanskrit; the theory remains debated. The Hōryū-ji manuscript (609 CE) and Xuanzang's version anchor the 7th-century horizon.

SA_K12_sarvttsu.title: Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṅgraha [Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṃgraha]
SA_K12_sarvttsu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_sarvttsu.summary: The 'Compendium of the Reality (tattva) of All Tathāgatas,' the root scripture of the Yogatantra class. It narrates the awakening of Sarvārthasiddhi/Vajradhātu and sets out the Vajradhātu maṇḍala of the five Buddha families, with their deities, mudrās, mantras and consecration rites, systematizing mature tantric ritual and the path to buddhahood in this life.
SA_K12_sarvttsu.history: Composed in the late decades of the 7th century, the Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṃgraha (also called the Tattvasaṃgraha Tantra) marks the emergence of fully developed Indian Buddhist tantra and became central to the Yogatantra traditions of India, Tibet, China, Japan (Shingon) and Sumatra, spawning a large body of commentarial and ritual literature. It is distinct from Śāntarakṣita's philosophical Tattvasaṃgraha.

SA_K12_sdurst_u.title: Sarvadurgatipariśodhana Tantra
SA_K12_sdurst_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_sdurst_u.summary: The 'Tantra for the Purification of All Evil/Negative Destinies,' a Yogatantra presented as discourses between an assembly of gods and Śākyamuni. Centered on the deity Sarvavid Vairocana and his maṇḍala, it teaches initiation, sādhana and ritual—especially funerary and apotropaic rites—to purify karma and rescue beings from rebirth in the lower realms (durgati).
SA_K12_sdurst_u.history: A widely used yogatantra long associated with funerary practice across Tibetan, Newar and East Asian Buddhism; edited and translated by Tadeusz Skorupski (1983). It belongs to the same generative phase of Yogatantra as the Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṃgraha (late 7th-8th c.) and exists in multiple recensions reflecting transformations of its funerary ritual manuals.

SA_K12_tarb108u.title: Tārābhaṭṭārikānāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotra
SA_K12_tarb108u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_tarb108u.summary: The 'Hymn of the One Hundred and Eight Names of the Noble Lady Tārā' (Āryatārābhaṭṭārikā), a litany praising the savioress goddess Tārā through 108 names/epithets, recited for protection, blessing and the removal of fears and obstacles. (Within the Buddhist Kangyur context; distinct from the separate Hindu/Śākta 108-names of the Mahāvidyā Tārā found in the Tantras.)
SA_K12_tarb108u.history: A canonical Buddhist Tārā praise transmitted in Sanskrit and Tibetan within the Tārā devotional corpus that flourished from the latter half of the first millennium. It accompanies the broader cult of Tārā alongside the 'Twenty-One Homages,' and belongs to the popular nāmāṣṭottaraśataka ('108 names') genre of stotra literature.

SA_K12_vajvidhu.title: Vajravidāraṇī-dhāraṇī
SA_K12_vajvidhu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_vajvidhu.summary: The dhāraṇī of Vajravidāraṇa ('the Vajra that demolishes/conquers'), a purificatory and protective incantation. It opens with homage to all buddhas and bodhisattvas and employs forceful imperative syllables (hara hara 'remove,' chinda chinda 'cut') to destroy grahas, subdue hostile spells and forces, purify obscurations of body, speech and mind, and protect beings.
SA_K12_vajvidhu.history: A widely used purification dhāraṇī (associated with the deity Vajravidāraṇa / Dorje Namjom) preserved in the Tibetan canon and Nepalese dhāraṇī collections (it appears among the spells anthologized in Pañcarakṣā/Saptavāra manuscripts). It became the basis of an independent sādhana practice for swift purification and clearing of obstacles.

SA_K12_vjviddhu.title: Vajravidāraṇā-dhāraṇī
SA_K12_vjviddhu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12_vjviddhu.summary: A recension/spelling-variant of the Vajravidāraṇa purification dhāraṇī (the title varies between Vajravidāraṇī and Vajravidāraṇā). It conveys the same protective and purificatory incantation of the wrathful 'Vajra Conqueror,' using imperative mantra-commands to cut and remove obstacles, demonic forces and karmic obscurations.
SA_K12_vjviddhu.history: This is essentially a parallel transmission of the same text as Vajravidāraṇī-dhāraṇī; multiple closely related recensions of the Vajravidāraṇa spell circulate in the Tibetan canon and in Nepalese Sanskrit dhāraṇī compendia (Pañcarakṣā/Saptavāra manuscripts, etc.).

SA_K12X_bsu017_u.title: Hayagrīvavidyā
SA_K12X_bsu017_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K12X_bsu017_u.summary: A vidyā ('spell') text of Hayagrīva, the horse-necked wrathful deity (a fierce emanation of Avalokiteśvara/Amitābha). The text supplies his protective and subjugating incantation, used to subdue demonic obstacles, hostile forces and karmic hindrances, and to safeguard the practitioner.
SA_K12X_bsu017_u.history: Hayagrīva is a prominent wrathful Vidyārāja of the Lotus (Padma) family who appears across all tantric classes (Kriyā, Caryā, Yoga, Anuttarayoga) as both yidam and dharmapāla. His spell-texts circulated within the broader Avalokiteśvara cult and entered the Tibetan and East Asian canons; the precise date of this particular vidyā is not securely fixed.

SA_K14_ahovk1_u.title: Ahorātravratakathā 1
SA_K14_ahovk1_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_ahovk1_u.tradition: Indic (Hindu or Jain; vrata/devotional literature) — uncertain
SA_K14_ahovk1_u.summary: A 'vow-story' (vratakathā) concerning the ahorātra ('day-and-night') observance — i.e., a one-day-and-night vow/fast. Texts of this genre narrate the origin, procedure, and rewards of a particular religious vow, typically through an illustrative legend that motivates the observance and details its ritual and its merit.
SA_K14_ahovk1_u.history: Vratakathā literature is a popular devotional genre (prominent in both Hindu and Jain traditions) prescribing vows with accompanying explanatory tales. The specific text, its sectarian affiliation, author and date are not securely identifiable from available evidence; the 'K14' grouping and '1' suggest it is one item in a set of such vrata-narratives. Identification is tentative.

SA_K14_ahovk2_u.title: Ahorātravratakathā 2
SA_K14_ahovk2_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_ahovk2_u.genre: vratakathā (narrative attached to a ritual vow/observance)
SA_K14_ahovk2_u.date_estimate: late medieval, c. 15th-18th c. CE (Nepalese transmission)
SA_K14_ahovk2_u.summary: A vratakathā ('story of a vow') prescribing and narrating the merit of the ahorātra ('day-and-night') vrata, a fasting and ritual observance kept over a full day and night. Such texts frame a devotional observance with a legendary tale illustrating the rewards of performing it. The '2' marks this as a second recension or version of the text.
SA_K14_ahovk2_u.history: Vratakathās are a characteristic genre of Newar (Kathmandu Valley) Buddhism, in which pan-Indic vow observances were Buddhicized and embedded in avadāna-like narrative. They survive largely in Nepalese Sanskrit manuscripts of the later medieval and early modern period and remain in living ritual use.

SA_K14_ajitsvyu.title: Ajitasenavyākaraṇa
SA_K14_ajitsvyu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_ajitsvyu.date_estimate: manuscript c. 5th-7th c. CE; composition likely earlier
SA_K14_ajitsvyu.summary: Full title Ajitasenavyākaraṇanirdeśa-nāma-mahāyānasūtra, 'The Mahayana Sutra called the Explanation of the Prophecy [for King] Ajitasena.' It is structured like an avadāna (past, present and future framing) and centers on the Buddha sending Nandimitra to advise King Ajitasena on the importance of almsgiving, also teaching that recitation of the Buddha's name saves from suffering and hell.
SA_K14_ajitsvyu.history: Known from a Gilgit manuscript discovered in 1931, written in upright Brahmi on brown paper and dated paleographically to roughly the 5th-7th centuries CE. Scholars treat it as a 'proto-Mahayana' work blending Mahayana and Sthaviravada/mainstream ideas. (Despite 'Ajitasena' being a common Jain name, this is a Buddhist text.)

SA_K14_amoghapu.title: Āmoghapāśanāmahṛdayasūtra [Āryāmoghapāśanāmahṛdayasūtra]
SA_K14_amoghapu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_amoghapu.date_estimate: c. 6th-7th c. CE (translated to Chinese by 587 and 693 CE)
SA_K14_amoghapu.summary: A dhāraṇī scripture centered on Amoghapāśa ('infallible noose') Lokeśvara, a multi-armed esoteric form of Avalokiteśvara who draws suffering beings to liberation with his noose. The text presents the dhāraṇī's mythic origin (Avalokiteśvara receiving it from the Tathagata Lokeśvararāja aeons ago) and the worldly and spiritual benefits, protections, and ritual procedures associated with its recitation.
SA_K14_amoghapu.history: An influential Kriyā Tantra text; an Amoghapāśa-dhāraṇī was rendered into Chinese by Jñānagupta in 587 CE and the Amoghapāśahṛdaya by Bodhiruci in 693 CE, with later expanded recensions. Amoghapāśa Lokeśvara became especially popular in Nepal. This batch contains two closely related witnesses of the text (cf. K14bsu002_u).

SA_K14_bsa052_u.title: Dharmasamuccaya [Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānakārikā]
SA_K14_bsa052_u.author: Avalokitasiṃha (compiler of the verse extracts)
SA_K14_bsa052_u.genre: verse anthology / doctrinal compendium (kārikā) extracted from a sutra
SA_K14_bsa052_u.date_estimate: verse compendium 1st millennium CE; source sutra c. 2nd-4th c. CE
SA_K14_bsa052_u.summary: The Dharmasamuccaya ('Compendium of the Law') is a metrical anthology of doctrinal stanzas extracted from the large Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra ('Sutra of the Application of Mindfulness of the True Law'), a vast text on karma, the realms of rebirth, cosmology, and meditative observation of body and mind. The verses distill the sutra's ethical and cosmological teaching into memorizable kārikās.
SA_K14_bsa052_u.history: Edited, with Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese versions and a French translation, by Lin Li-kouang (Paris, 1946-1973), who attributed the verse-selection to one Avalokitasiṃha. Lin's accompanying study 'Introduction au Compendium de la loi' (1949) remains the principal scholarship on the parent sutra.

SA_K14_bsu002_u.title: Āmoghapāśanāmahṛdaya sūtra [Ārya amoghapāśahṛdaya nāma mahāyānasūtra]
SA_K14_bsu002_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_bsu002_u.date_estimate: c. 6th-7th c. CE (Chinese translations 587 and 693 CE)
SA_K14_bsu002_u.summary: A recension of the Amoghapāśahṛdaya ('Essence/Heart of the Infallible Noose') sutra, the core dhāraṇī scripture of Amoghapāśa Lokeśvara, an esoteric multi-armed form of Avalokiteśvara. It conveys the dhāraṇī, its origin myth, its ritual employment, and the protections and merits its recitation bestows.
SA_K14_bsu002_u.history: Belongs to the same textual family as K14amoghapu; the Amoghapāśa cult and its dhāraṇī were extremely popular in Nepal and East Asia, and the text exists in short 'heart' and longer expanded forms in Sanskrit, Chinese and Tibetan. Translated into Chinese by Jñānagupta (587) and Bodhiruci (693).

SA_K14_bsu015_u.title: Ekādaśamukha
SA_K14_bsu015_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_bsu015_u.date_estimate: c. 6th-7th c. CE (Chinese translation by Xuanzang, 656 CE)
SA_K14_bsu015_u.summary: The Ekādaśamukha ('Eleven-Faced') Avalokiteśvara dhāraṇī sutra, centered on a potent dhāraṇī of the eleven-faced form of the bodhisattva of compassion. It promises protection, healing and spiritual advancement, structured as an opening homage to the Triple Gem, a central body of invocatory syllables, and a closing dedication sealed with svāhā.
SA_K14_bsu015_u.history: First translated into Chinese in 656 CE by Xuanzang; the Sanskrit title is recovered chiefly from the Tibetan translation, the original Sanskrit having been largely lost so that the text survives mainly via Chinese and Tibetan with varying Sanskrit reconstructions. The eleven-faced Avalokiteśvara became a major esoteric cult figure across Asia.

SA_K14_dhppat_u.title: Patna Dharmapada
SA_K14_dhppat_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_dhppat_u.date_estimate: verses early (pre-Common Era core); manuscript c. 11th-12th c. CE
SA_K14_dhppat_u.summary: A Dharmapada recension — a collection of pithy gnomic verses on ethics, discipline, mindfulness and liberation — paralleling the Pali Dhammapada and the Gandhari and Udanavarga versions. It preserves an independent arrangement and partly distinct verses of this widely circulated Buddhist verse corpus.
SA_K14_dhppat_u.history: Named 'Patna' because the manuscript photographs are held at the K.P. Jayaswal Research Institute, Patna (photographed during Rahula Sankrtyayana's Tibet expeditions). The 21-palm-leaf manuscript, in proto-Bengali script, dates to roughly the 11th-12th c. CE; its language is a Middle Indo-Aryan idiom variously termed Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit or Buddhist Prakrit. It was edited by Margaret Cone (1989).

SA_K14_mavadsuu.title: Mahāvadānasūtra
SA_K14_mavadsuu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_mavadsuu.date_estimate: core early (BCE); Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda recension c. 1st-3rd c. CE
SA_K14_mavadsuu.summary: The 'Sutra of the Great Legend,' the Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda counterpart of the Pali Mahāpadāna-sutta (DN 14). It recounts the lives of seven Buddhas — from Vipaśyin down to the historical Śākyamuni — all following an almost identical pattern of events, narrated by Śākyamuni, and elaborates the standard schema of a Buddha's career.
SA_K14_mavadsuu.history: One of the six fundamental sutras of the Dīrghāgama (the Ṣaṭsūtrakanipāta), abundantly attested among the Central Asian / Turfan Sanskrit manuscripts. The editio princeps was published by Ernst Waldschmidt (1953-1956); a new edition based on the Turfan finds appeared more recently. This batch includes a second witness (K14n445u).

SA_K14_mpsu_w_u.title: Mahāparinirvāṇasūtra
SA_K14_mpsu_w_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_mpsu_w_u.date_estimate: core early (BCE); Sanskrit recension c. 1st-4th c. CE
SA_K14_mpsu_w_u.summary: The Sanskrit (non-Mahayana) Mahāparinirvāṇasūtra, the Sarvāstivāda parallel of the Pali Mahāparinibbāna-sutta (DN 16), narrating the Buddha's last journey, final teachings, death (parinirvāṇa), cremation, and the distribution of his relics. It is a biographical-canonical account of the founder's death, distinct from the later doctrinal Mahayana Mahāparinirvāṇa-sūtra.
SA_K14_mpsu_w_u.history: Another of the six core Dīrghāgama sutras, reconstructed by Ernst Waldschmidt (1950-1951) from Central Asian Sanskrit fragments, collated with the Tibetan, Pali and the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya. (If instead the Mahayana Mahāparinirvāṇa-sūtra is intended, that is a c. 2nd-4th c. CE 'Buddha-nature' scripture; the grouping with other Dīrghāgama sutras in this batch favors the mainstream identification.)

SA_K14_msudarsu.title: Mahāsudarśanāvadāna
SA_K14_msudarsu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_msudarsu.date_estimate: Sanskrit recension c. 1st-4th c. CE (story-core older)
SA_K14_msudarsu.summary: The legend of King Mahāsudarśana (Pali Mahāsudassana), a universal monarch (cakravartin) ruling over the splendid city of Kuśāvatī with its eighty-four thousand cities, palaces and treasures. At the approach of death he contemplates the impermanence of all this glory and dies serenely, reborn in the Brahma world — the narrative serving as a meditation on impermanence and renunciation, identified as a past life of the Buddha.
SA_K14_msudarsu.history: A Sanskrit avadāna recasting of the material of the Pali Mahāsudassana-sutta (DN 17); such avadānas elaborated jātaka/sutta material to teach merit, impermanence and the bodhisattva ideal. The story is also drawn into the Mahāparinirvāṇa narrative complex.

SA_K14_n191u.title: Kaṭhināvadāna
SA_K14_n191u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_n191u.date_estimate: Sanskrit avadāna milieu, c. 2nd-6th c. CE
SA_K14_n191u.summary: An avadāna concerning the kaṭhina — the cotton cloth offered by lay donors to the monastic community at the close of the rains-retreat (varṣā) for making robes. The tale illustrates the great merit accruing from the kaṭhina robe-offering, framed in the typical avadāna manner linking a present situation to deeds in past lives.
SA_K14_n191u.history: The kaṭhina robe-offering is rooted in the Vinaya, where the Buddha permitted monks with worn robes to accept it; the avadāna develops this into a merit narrative. It circulates within the broader Sanskrit avadāna literature (cf. Divyāvadāna), with manuscripts notably preserved in Nepal.

SA_K14_n192u.title: Mahāsattvamaṇicūḍamahārājabodhisattvāvadāna
SA_K14_n192u.author: anonymous/traditional (later versified by Kṣemendra in the Avadānakalpalatā tradition)
SA_K14_n192u.date_estimate: Sanskrit avadāna milieu, c. 2nd-6th c. CE
SA_K14_n192u.summary: The Maṇicūḍāvadāna ('Legend of [King] Maṇicūḍa, the jewel-crested'), a story of the bodhisattva-king Maṇicūḍa whose perfect generosity (dāna-pāramitā) leads him to give away even parts of his own body, including the wish-granting jewel set in his crown. It exemplifies the bodhisattva's boundless self-sacrifice for the welfare of beings.
SA_K14_n192u.history: A popular Sanskrit avadāna preserved especially in Nepalese manuscripts (e.g. Cambridge MS Add.1398). The episode also figures in Kṣemendra's 11th-century verse Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā. The long compound title reflects a Nepalese manuscript heading naming the great-being (mahāsattva) jewel-crested great king bodhisattva.

SA_K14_n285u.title: Catuṣpariṣatsūtra
SA_K14_n285u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_n285u.date_estimate: core early (BCE); Sanskrit recension c. 1st-3rd c. CE
SA_K14_n285u.summary: The 'Sutra of the Four Assemblies/Groups,' a canonical narrative of the founding of the Buddhist community: the awakening, the first turning of the wheel of Dharma at Sarnath, the conversion of the first disciples, and the formation of the fourfold assembly. It belongs to the biographical Vinaya-related material on the establishment of the sangha.
SA_K14_n285u.history: One of the six fundamental Dīrghāgama sutras attested in the Central Asian Sanskrit manuscripts; critically edited by Ernst Waldschmidt as 'Das Catuṣpariṣatsūtra' (Berlin, 1952-1962). It overlaps with the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya account of the community's beginnings.

SA_K14_n2947u.title: Vajrasattvaniṣpādanasūtra [Vajrasattvasādhana]
SA_K14_n2947u.author: Candrakīrti (the tantric author of the Guhyasamāja Ārya school)
SA_K14_n2947u.summary: A sādhana ('means of realization') for accomplishing the state of the primordial Buddha Vajrasattva, expounding the utpattikrama (Generation Stage) of the Ārya school of Guhyasamāja exegesis. It presents the symbolic process of the birth of deities through the yogic union of practitioner and consort, leading to identification with Vajrasattva.
SA_K14_n2947u.history: Ascribed to the tantric Candrakīrti (distinct from the earlier Madhyamaka philosopher of the same name), a commentator within the Ārya tradition of the Guhyasamāja. Hong Luo and Toru Tomabechi produced the first critical edition (Vienna, STTAR) from three manuscripts (12th-14th c.), including one photographed by Rahula Sankrtyayana at Nor monastery.

SA_K14_n445u.title: Mahāvadānasūtra
SA_K14_n445u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_n445u.date_estimate: core early (BCE); Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda recension c. 1st-3rd c. CE
SA_K14_n445u.summary: A witness of the 'Sutra of the Great Legend,' the Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda parallel to the Pali Mahāpadāna-sutta (DN 14), narrating the lives of the seven past Buddhas (Vipaśyin through Śākyamuni), all unfolding along an identical pattern, as told by Śākyamuni. (Duplicate work in this batch; see K14mavadsuu.)
SA_K14_n445u.history: Part of the Ṣaṭsūtrakanipāta of the Dīrghāgama, well attested among Central Asian/Turfan Sanskrit manuscripts and edited by Ernst Waldschmidt (1953-1956). This entry is a second cataloged witness of the same work as K14mavadsuu.

SA_K14_pamsuavu.title: Pāṃśupradāna-avadāna
SA_K14_pamsuavu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_pamsuavu.tradition: Buddhist (mainstream; Sanskrit avadāna tradition, part of the Divyāvadāna)
SA_K14_pamsuavu.summary: The 'Gift of Dust' avadāna, the opening section of the Aśokāvadāna: in a former life the boy Jaya offers a handful of dust (imagined as food) into the Buddha's begging bowl and makes a vow (praṇidhāna), whereby he is later reborn as the emperor Aśoka. The Buddha predicts that a century after his parinirvāṇa this boy will become a cakravartin at Pāṭaliputra who erects 84,000 stupas.
SA_K14_pamsuavu.history: Forms chapter 26 of the Divyāvadāna and the beginning of the Aśokāvadāna cycle, the principal Buddhist legendary biography of Aśoka. It is a key text in the Buddhist construction of Aśoka as the ideal dharma-king and great patron of stupa-building.

SA_K14_ratnml_u.title: Ratnamālāvadāna
SA_K14_ratnml_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_ratnml_u.date_estimate: compilation likely mid-1st millennium CE or later; Nepalese transmission
SA_K14_ratnml_u.summary: A 'Garland of Jewels' — a collection of some 38 avadāna tales told in verse, many revolving around King Aśoka and the monk Upagupta, illustrating the workings of karma and the merit of devotion and offering. Each tale typically links a present circumstance to deeds performed in earlier lives.
SA_K14_ratnml_u.history: Belonging to the same broad milieu as the Avadānaśataka, Aśokāvadāna and Divyāvadāna, it is preserved chiefly in Nepalese Sanskrit manuscripts. The Sanskrit text was edited by Kanga Takahata (Ratnamālāvadāna, 1954). Its precise date of composition is uncertain.

SA_K14_sumagavu.title: Sumāgadhāvadāna
SA_K14_sumagavu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_sumagavu.date_estimate: Sanskrit avadāna milieu, c. 2nd-5th c. CE
SA_K14_sumagavu.summary: The legend of Sumāgadhā, daughter of the great lay donor Anāthapiṇḍada of Śrāvastī. Married into a Jain (nirgrantha) merchant family in distant Puṇḍravardhana, she instills aversion to the Jains in her husband and, by inviting the Buddha and a miracle, converts the entire city to Buddhism. The tale extols faith in the Buddha and the merit of inviting the sangha.
SA_K14_sumagavu.history: Widely transmitted across Buddhist traditions, with a Sanskrit recension, a Tibetan version in the Kangyur, and Chinese translations (including one by Dānapāla). The Sanskrit Sumāgadhāvadāna was edited by Yutaka Iwamoto (Studien zur buddhistischen Erzählungsliteratur 2, Kyoto, 1968). It is also a notable witness to early Buddhism in Bengal/Puṇḍravardhana.

SA_K14_svabhupu.title: Svayambhū Purāṇa
SA_K14_svabhupu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_svabhupu.date_estimate: compiled in present forms c. 14th-18th c. CE (with older legendary strata)
SA_K14_svabhupu.summary: One of the foundational Buddhist purāṇas of Nepal, recounting the mythic origin of the Kathmandu (Nepal) Valley — drained from a primordial lake by Mañjuśrī — and the appearance and sanctity of the self-arisen (svayambhū) light/flame enshrined at the Svayambhū Dharmadhātu Chaitya (Swayambhunath). It narrates the visits of Buddhas to the valley and the holiness of its sacred sites.
SA_K14_svabhupu.history: Exists in multiple Sanskrit (and Newari) recensions differing in chapter and verse count; one common version has 10 chapters and ~1750 verses and is dated to the 18th century, though earlier strata and manuscripts (back to the 14th century) underlie it. It is the central charter-myth and pilgrimage text of Newar Buddhism.
related_works: Newar Buddhist purāṇa and vaṃśāvalī literature

SA_K14_visvantu.title: Viśvantarāvadāna
SA_K14_visvantu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K14_visvantu.date_estimate: Sanskrit recension c. 2nd-5th c. CE (story-core older)
SA_K14_visvantu.summary: The Sanskrit avadāna of Prince Viśvantara (Pali Vessantara), the bodhisattva's celebrated past life as the embodiment of perfect generosity (dāna-pāramitā). He gives away the kingdom's rain-bringing white elephant, then in exile bestows even his wife and children on those who ask, before all is ultimately restored — the supreme exemplar of selfless giving.
SA_K14_visvantu.history: A Sanskrit form of the most famous of all giving-narratives, corresponding to the Pali Vessantara-Jātaka (no. 547) and treated in the Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra and elsewhere; in the avadāna format the jātaka material is adapted to teach the bodhisattva's perfection of generosity. Hugely popular across the Buddhist world in literature and art.

SA_T01_busto1_u.title: Anaparāddhastotra
SA_T01_busto1_u.author: Mātṛceṭa
SA_T01_busto1_u.date_estimate: c. 1st-2nd c. CE (Mātṛceṭa's traditional floruit; some scholars place him 2nd-4th c. CE)
SA_T01_busto1_u.summary: A devotional hymn in praise of the Buddha attributed to the poet Mātṛceṭa, celebrating the Buddha's flawless (an-aparāddha, 'faultless/never-erring') qualities and perfections. It belongs to the same devotional milieu as Mātṛceṭa's celebrated hymns (the Śatapañcāśatka and the Varṇārhavarṇastotra/Catuḥśataka), expressing deep faith and admiration for the Buddha's attainments in a restrained, lucid kavya style.
SA_T01_busto1_u.history: Mātṛceṭa was one of the earliest and most admired Buddhist Sanskrit hymnodists; his hymns were sung across Buddhist schools and were studied by novices as a standard part of monastic training (as Yijing reports). The attribution and exact corpus of his shorter stotras is debated, and this title is preserved chiefly through the Tibetan canon and Sanskrit stotra collections.

SA_T01_n2888u.title: Dvādaśakāranayastotra
SA_T01_n2888u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (traditional)
SA_T01_n2888u.date_estimate: traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna (c. 2nd-3rd c. CE); the hymn as transmitted is likely later (mid-1st millennium CE)
SA_T01_n2888u.summary: A hymn praising the Twelve Deeds (dvādaśa-kāra/dvādaśa-karman) of a Buddha — the canonical sequence of the great acts of a buddha's life, from descent from Tuṣita heaven, entering the womb, birth, mastery of arts, life in the palace, renunciation, austerities, approaching the bodhi tree, defeating Māra, awakening, turning the wheel of Dharma, and passing into parinirvāṇa. Each verse extols one deed of the Buddha's enlightened career.
SA_T01_n2888u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna, the 'In Praise of the Twelve Deeds' (Tib. mdzad pa bcu gnyis) is widely recited in Tibetan monasteries; the attribution is conventional rather than securely established. It survives in Sanskrit stotra collections and the Tibetan canon.
related_works: Twelve Deeds of a Buddha (doctrinal schema)

SA_T01_n2925u.title: Dharmadhātustava
SA_T01_n2925u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (disputed)
SA_T01_n2925u.date_estimate: traditionally Nāgārjuna (c. 2nd-3rd c. CE); many scholars regard it as later, c. 4th-7th c. CE
SA_T01_n2925u.summary: A poem 'In Praise of the Dharmadhātu' (the expanse/element of reality), describing the dharmadhātu as the ever-present, luminous ground that makes liberation possible but is temporarily obscured by adventitious afflictions. Using a series of analogies, it explains how wisdom realizing emptiness removes obscurations to reveal the radiant nature — a text central to later buddha-nature (tathāgatagarbha) interpretation.
SA_T01_n2925u.history: Traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna, though its devotional/buddha-nature content has led many scholars to question the ascription and place it later than his Madhyamaka treatises. It became especially important in Tibet, where the Third Karmapa Rangjung Dorje (1284-1339) wrote its most influential commentary; Karl Brunnhölzl produced a major modern study and translation.

SA_T01_n2971u.title: Bhadracaryādeśanā
SA_T01_n2971u.author: anonymous/traditional (canonical Mahayana)
SA_T01_n2971u.date_estimate: core text c. 3rd-4th c. CE; embedded in the Gaṇḍavyūha layer of the Avataṃsaka
SA_T01_n2971u.summary: An aspiration text on the 'conduct of the Good One' (Bhadracarī), essentially the Bhadracaryāpraṇidhāna — the famous concluding verses of the Gaṇḍavyūha (final section of the Avataṃsaka Sūtra) presenting the bodhisattva Samantabhadra's great vows. The 'deśanā' framing emphasizes the confession/declaration of resolve: paying homage to all buddhas, confessing faults, rejoicing in merit, requesting teaching, and dedicating merit to awakening for all beings.
SA_T01_n2971u.history: One of the most widely recited Mahayana texts in East Asian and Tibetan/Nepalese Buddhism, transmitted both within the Gaṇḍavyūha/Avataṃsaka and as an independent liturgy. Numerous Sanskrit recensions and commentaries exist; it summarizes the bodhicitta and interpenetration (Huayan) teachings.

SA_T01_n802u.title: Ādibuddhadvādaśakastotra
SA_T01_n802u.author: attributed to Mañjuśrī/Mañjughoṣa (traditional)
SA_T01_n802u.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE / early 2nd millennium CE (Vajrayana stratum); uncertain
SA_T01_n802u.summary: A hymn of twelve verses (dvādaśaka) in praise of the Ādi-Buddha, the primordial Buddha of Vajrayana cosmology regarded as the timeless source of all buddhas and reality. It extols the Ādi-Buddha's beginningless, all-pervading nature in devotional verse.
SA_T01_n802u.history: Preserved among the Sanskrit Buddhist stotras of Nepal (in the Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha tradition) and traditionally ascribed to Mañjuśrī; the attribution is conventional and the dating uncertain. Ādi-Buddha doctrine is especially developed in the Kālacakra and later Vajrayana traditions.

SA_T01_n804u.title: Vikramāraccivaibhava
SA_T01_n804u.summary: An obscure Sanskrit Buddhist title that could not be securely identified; the form 'vaibhava' ('majesty, glory') suggests a hymn extolling the glory/majesty of a deity or figure (possibly a corrupted or rare title). The text appears within a digital corpus of Sanskrit Buddhist stotras but no reliable scholarly description was found.
SA_T01_n804u.history: No scholarly identification, author, or dating could be confirmed from available sources. The title may be corrupt or a very rare entry in the Nepalese stotra collections.

SA_T01_n805u.title: Ākāśagarbhanāmāṣṭottaraśatastotra
SA_T01_n805u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n805u.summary: A devotional hymn enumerating the 108 names (aṣṭottaraśata-nāma) of the bodhisattva Ākāśagarbha ('Essence/Womb of Space'), one of the eight great bodhisattvas. Such 108-name stotras list epithets capturing the bodhisattva's qualities and are recited for merit and protection.
SA_T01_n805u.history: Ākāśagarbha is associated with space, purification of transgressions, and wish-granting; his cult is prominent in East Asian esoteric Buddhism. This 108-names hymn belongs to the wide genre of aṣṭottaraśatanāma-stotras preserved in Sanskrit Buddhist and Tibetan collections; specific authorship and dating are not established.

SA_T01_n806u.title: Tārānamaskāraikaviṃśatistotra [Āryatārānamaskāraikaviṃśatistotra]
SA_T01_n806u.author: anonymous/traditional (presented as words of the Buddha; Vairocana/Kriyātantra)
SA_T01_n806u.date_estimate: c. 6th-8th c. CE (origin of the Tārā tantra cycle)
SA_T01_n806u.summary: The famous 'Homage to Tārā in Twenty-One Verses' (Praise to the Twenty-One Tārās), a liturgy of homage and reverence to the goddess Tārā. The first twenty-one verses are simultaneously salutations to twenty-one forms of Tārā and a poetic description of her physical features, postures, colors, and protective powers; the closing verses describe the benefits of recitation.
SA_T01_n806u.history: Drawn from the root Tārā tantra (Sarvatathāgatamātṛtārāviśvakarmabhava-tantra), it is by far the most popular prayer to Tārā in Tibetan Buddhism, with commentaries in all four Tibetan schools tracing to Indian oral lineages. The Sanskrit original underlies the Tibetan and is recited daily across the Vajrayana world.

SA_T01_n809u.title: Tārāstuti [Āryatārāstuti]
SA_T01_n809u.author: anonymous/traditional (several Tārā hymns; cf. Sarvajñamitra's Sragdharāstotra)
SA_T01_n809u.summary: A hymn in praise of the goddess Tārā, the great female bodhisattva and savioress invoked for protection from the eight (or more) great fears and for swift compassionate aid. Tārā-stutis enumerate her epithets, her green and white forms, and her power to rescue devotees from danger.
SA_T01_n809u.history: Tārā-praise hymns form a large devotional genre in Sanskrit and Tibetan Buddhism. Among the most celebrated is the Sragdharāstotra of the Kashmiri poet Sarvajñamitra (first half of the 8th c.), traditionally said to have miraculously rescued sacrificial victims when sung. This particular Āryatārāstuti is one of several such hymns preserved in the Sanskrit Buddhist stotra corpus; its specific authorship is not securely fixed.

SA_T01_n810u.title: Maṅgalāṣṭaka
SA_T01_n810u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n810u.summary: An 'eight auspicious verses' benediction (maṅgala-aṣṭaka) invoking blessings and good fortune by paying homage to the Three Jewels and to auspicious buddhas, bodhisattvas, and protective figures. Such verses are recited at the start of rituals, ceremonies, and new undertakings to remove obstacles and secure favorable conditions.
SA_T01_n810u.history: Maṅgalāṣṭaka benedictions are a standard liturgical form in both Hindu and Buddhist traditions; in Newar Buddhism the Vajrācārya priests recite them at the opening of rituals. The specific text under this title is part of the Nepalese Sanskrit Buddhist stotra corpus; authorship and date are not established.
related_works: Tibetan 'Verses of the Eight Auspicious Ones' (parallel genre)

SA_T01_n811u.title: Aṣṭamātṛkāstotra
SA_T01_n811u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n811u.tradition: Buddhist (Vajrayana; Nepalese/Newar, with shared Tantric goddess milieu)
SA_T01_n811u.date_estimate: uncertain; second half of 1st millennium CE or later
SA_T01_n811u.summary: A hymn in praise of the Eight Mothers (aṣṭa-mātṛkā), a set of goddesses adapted into Tantric Buddhist (and shared with Hindu) ritual as protective and matrix deities surrounding a maṇḍala. The verses salute each of the mother-goddesses and invoke their power.
SA_T01_n811u.history: The aṣṭamātṛkā cult is prominent in Newar religion, where the eight (or more) Mother goddesses guard the city/maṇḍala; this stotra reflects the Buddhist-Tantric appropriation of the Mātṛkā goddesses. Specific authorship and dating are not established; it is preserved in Nepalese Sanskrit stotra collections.

SA_T01_n812u.title: Avadhānastotra [Vandanāstava]
SA_T01_n812u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n812u.summary: A hymn of homage and reverence (vandanā) to the Buddha/buddhas and bodhisattvas; the alternate title Vandanāstava ('hymn of salutation') indicates its devotional, prostration-oriented character, while 'avadhāna' suggests attentive mindfulness directed toward the objects of worship. It belongs to the standard devotional repertoire of confession, homage, and praise.
SA_T01_n812u.history: Part of the Sanskrit Buddhist stotra corpus preserved in Nepal; the double title reflects manuscript variation. No secure author or date is established.

SA_T01_n813u.title: Avalokiteśvarahayagrīvadhāraṇī
SA_T01_n813u.author: anonymous/traditional (canonical dhāraṇī)
SA_T01_n813u.summary: A dhāraṇī (ritual incantation) associated with Hayagrīva ('Horse-necked'), the wrathful, horse-headed manifestation of the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara. Recited for protection, removal of obstacles, subjugation of negative forces, and the power of awakened speech, the text consists of efficacious mantric syllables framed by invocation.
SA_T01_n813u.history: Hayagrīva originated as a yakṣa attendant of Avalokiteśvara in India and developed into a major wrathful protector across Tibetan, Chinese, and Japanese esoteric Buddhism, regarded as the Heruka of speech. Dhāraṇīs of this type circulated in Sanskrit and were transmitted into the East Asian and Tibetan canons; specific authorship is not applicable.

SA_T01_n814u.title: Avalokiteśvarāṣṭakastotra
SA_T01_n814u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n814u.genre: stotra (hymn of praise in eight verses)
SA_T01_n814u.summary: An 'eight-verse hymn' (aṣṭaka) in praise of Avalokiteśvara, the bodhisattva of compassion (Karuṇāmaya), invoking his protective grace and enumerating his compassionate qualities. The aṣṭaka form (eight stanzas plus a phalaśruti) is a common devotional unit.
SA_T01_n814u.history: One of numerous Avalokiteśvara hymns in the Sanskrit Buddhist stotra corpus of Nepal; authorship and precise date are unestablished. Avalokiteśvara (Lokeśvara/Karuṇāmaya) is the central object of Newar Buddhist devotion.

SA_T01_n815u.title: Avalokiteśvarastava
SA_T01_n815u.author: Candrakāntā (bhikṣuṇī)
SA_T01_n815u.date_estimate: uncertain (medieval; date of the nun Candrakāntā not securely fixed)
SA_T01_n815u.summary: A hymn in praise of Avalokiteśvara, the bodhisattva of compassion, attributed to the Buddhist nun (bhikṣuṇī) Candrakāntā. It extols the bodhisattva's compassion and saving power in devotional verse.
SA_T01_n815u.history: Notable as a stotra ascribed to a female monastic author (Candrakāntābhikṣuṇī), it is preserved in the Sanskrit Buddhist stotra collections; little is established about the author's date or identity.

SA_T01_n816u.title: Avalokiteśvarastotra
SA_T01_n816u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n816u.summary: A hymn of praise to Avalokiteśvara, the bodhisattva of infinite compassion, celebrating his vow to relieve the suffering of all beings and his many emanations. One of several Avalokiteśvara stotras in the Sanskrit Buddhist devotional corpus.
SA_T01_n816u.history: Avalokiteśvara is the pre-eminent bodhisattva of compassion across Mahayana and Vajrayana Buddhism and the focus of Newar devotion as Karuṇāmaya/Lokeśvara. This anonymous stotra is one of many such hymns transmitted in Sanskrit collections; authorship and date are not established.

SA_T01_n817u.title: Avalokiteśvarastotra
SA_T01_n817u.author: Carapatipāda (Carpaṭi/Carpaṭipāda; Mahāsiddha)
SA_T01_n817u.date_estimate: c. 8th-10th c. CE (floruit of the siddha Carpaṭi)
SA_T01_n817u.summary: A popular hymn in praise of Ārya Avalokiteśvara attributed to the mahāsiddha Carapatipāda (Carpaṭi). In Nepalese tradition Carpaṭi is credited with bringing an emanation of Avalokiteśvara (the red Matsyendranāth/Karuṇāmaya) to the Kathmandu Valley to end a twelve-year drought, and this stotra is connected to that compassionate intervention.
SA_T01_n817u.history: Carapatipāda/Carpaṭi figures among the Buddhist mahāsiddhas (and overlaps with Nāth-tradition lore), conventionally placed c. 8th-10th c. CE; the precise dating is uncertain. The hymn is well known in Newar Buddhist devotional practice.

SA_T01_n818u.title: Avalokiteśvarāṣṭottaraśatanāmastotra
SA_T01_n818u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n818u.date_estimate: uncertain; mid/late 1st millennium CE or later
SA_T01_n818u.summary: A hymn enumerating the 108 names (aṣṭottaraśata-nāma) of Avalokiteśvara/Lokeśvara, the bodhisattva of compassion. Reciting the 108 epithets is held to confer great merit and protection — traditionally, hearers will not fall into lower realms for ages — and corresponds to the 108 forms of Lokeśvara venerated especially in Newar Buddhism.
SA_T01_n818u.history: The 108 names/forms of Avalokiteśvara are a major feature of Nepalese Buddhist iconography and liturgy (the 108 Lokeśvara), possibly connected to a Kangyur tantra of the same name. This stotra circulates in Sanskrit and is recited in Tibetan and Newar practice; specific authorship is not established.

SA_T01_n819u.title: Lokanāthastotra
SA_T01_n819u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n819u.date_estimate: uncertain; second half of 1st millennium CE or later
SA_T01_n819u.summary: A hymn in praise of Lokanātha ('Lord/Protector of the World'), one of the principal names and forms of Avalokiteśvara. Lokanātha (white-bodied, holding a lotus, displaying the boon-granting mudrā) is described in the Sādhanamālā; the stotra extols his compassion and protective sovereignty over the world.
SA_T01_n819u.history: Lokanātha is among the common Vajrayana names for Avalokiteśvara, with sādhanas preserved in the Sādhanamālā (which draws on materials from c. the 5th c. onward). This stotra belongs to the Avalokiteśvara devotional corpus in Sanskrit; specific authorship and date are not established.

SA_T01_n820u.title: Lokeśvaraśataka
SA_T01_n820u.author: Vajradatta
SA_T01_n820u.genre: stotra / kavya (hymn of a hundred verses)
SA_T01_n820u.date_estimate: 9th c. CE (reign of Devapāla of the Pāla dynasty)
SA_T01_n820u.summary: A 'hymn to the Lord of the World in a hundred verses' (śataka) praising Avalokiteśvara/Lokeśvara, the bodhisattva of compassion, composed in ornate kavya style. Its hundred stanzas celebrate the bodhisattva's compassionate majesty and saving power.
SA_T01_n820u.history: The poet Vajradatta flourished at the court of the Pāla king Devapāla of Bengal in the 9th century, making this one of the more securely datable Buddhist Sanskrit stotras. It was esteemed as accomplished court poetry and survives in Sanskrit collections and Tibetan translation.

SA_T01_n821u.title: Lokeśvarastotra
SA_T01_n821u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n821u.date_estimate: uncertain; likely 1st-2nd millennium CE, transmitted in Nepalese Buddhist liturgy (collection compiled by the medieval/early-modern period)
SA_T01_n821u.summary: A devotional hymn praising Lokeśvara, the 'Lord of the World,' an epithet of the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara, the embodiment of compassion. It extols his salvific power and is recited for protection and blessing.
SA_T01_n821u.history: Preserved among the Buddhist Sanskrit stotras of the Kathmandu Valley and printed in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994), where it appears anonymously. Such hymns remain in living recitation use in Newar Buddhism.

SA_T01_n822u.title: Narakoddhārastotra
SA_T01_n822u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n822u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern transmission within Nepalese Buddhist hymn corpus
SA_T01_n822u.summary: A hymn on 'rescue from the hells' (naraka-uddhāra), invoking a Buddha or bodhisattva (typically Avalokiteśvara) as a savior who lifts beings out of hellish rebirths and the suffering of the lower realms.
SA_T01_n822u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha collection of 108 Buddhist stotras edited by J. S. Pandey (Varanasi, 1994); part of the living Newar Buddhist devotional repertoire of the Kathmandu Valley.

SA_T01_n823u.title: Potalakāṣṭaka
SA_T01_n823u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n823u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n823u.summary: An eight-verse hymn (aṣṭaka) associated with Potalaka, the mountain abode of the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara, praising the deity of that sacred site. It celebrates Avalokiteśvara/Lokeśvara in his Potala aspect.
SA_T01_n823u.history: Anonymous within the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Belongs to the Avalokiteśvara-centred devotional literature preserved in Nepalese Buddhism.

SA_T01_n824u.title: Rakṣākāla(kara)stava
SA_T01_n824u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n824u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n824u.summary: A hymn of praise to a protective deity (rakṣā, 'protection'), invoked for warding off danger and harm. The title variant Rakṣākāra/Rakṣākāla refers to one who effects protection.
SA_T01_n824u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha of 108 Buddhist stotras (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Such apotropaic hymns are part of the protective (rakṣā) ritual literature of Newar Buddhism.

SA_T01_n825u.title: Ratnamālāstotra
SA_T01_n825u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n825u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n825u.summary: A 'Jewel-Garland' hymn praising a Buddha or bodhisattva, the verses likened to a string of jewels offered in devotion. It is a praise-poem in the Mahayana devotional mode.
SA_T01_n825u.history: Anonymous within the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994), preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist hymn tradition.

SA_T01_n826u.title: Rūpastava
SA_T01_n826u.author: anonymous/traditional
