SA_T01_n826u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n826u.summary: A hymn praising the bodily form (rūpa) of a Buddha or bodhisattva, dwelling on the perfected, auspicious physical appearance and major and minor marks of the divine figure as an object of devotion.
SA_T01_n826u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha collection of 108 Buddhist stotras (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994); part of the Newar Buddhist devotional corpus.

SA_T01_n827u.title: Ṣaḍgatistotra
SA_T01_n827u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n827u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n827u.summary: A short hymn on the 'six destinies' (ṣaḍgati) of Buddhist cosmology — the realms of gods, demigods, humans, animals, hungry ghosts, and hell-beings — likely invoking a savior bodhisattva who liberates beings from cyclic rebirth across these six realms.
SA_T01_n827u.history: A six-verse anonymous hymn in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994), reflecting the doctrine of the six realms of saṃsāra in Mahayana devotional practice.

SA_T01_n828u.title: Saptākṣarastotra
SA_T01_n828u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n828u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n828u.summary: A hymn built around the 'seven syllables' (saptākṣara), most likely the seven-syllable mantra/form associated with a tantric deity (such as Heruka/Saṃvara in the saptākṣara form). It praises the deity through the power of the mantric syllables.
SA_T01_n828u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The saptākṣara theme links it to the mantra-based hymnody of Newar Vajrayana.

SA_T01_n829u.title: Saptavidhānuttarastotra
SA_T01_n829u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n829u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n829u.summary: A twelve-verse hymn on the 'sevenfold supreme worship' (saptavidha anuttarā pūjā) — the standard sevenfold devotional service (homage, offering, confession of faults, rejoicing in merit, requesting teaching, requesting the buddhas to remain, and dedication of merit) central to Mahayana liturgy.
SA_T01_n829u.history: Anonymous hymn (12 verses) in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The sevenfold worship (saptāṅga/saptavidhā pūjā) derives from Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra and pervades later devotional practice.

SA_T01_n830u.title: Śāradāṣṭakastotra
SA_T01_n830u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n830u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n830u.summary: An eight-verse hymn (aṣṭaka) to Śāradā, an aspect of the goddess of learning and eloquence (Sarasvatī/Prajñā), praising her as the bestower of wisdom and speech.
SA_T01_n830u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Sarasvatī/Śāradā is venerated in Buddhist as well as Hindu traditions; this hymn belongs to the Newar Buddhist devotional corpus.

SA_T01_n831u.title: Vāgvāṇīstotra
SA_T01_n831u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n831u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n831u.summary: A hymn to Vāgvāṇī ('the Voice/Word'), an epithet of Sarasvatī, the goddess of speech, wisdom, and learning. It praises her as the source of eloquence and knowledge.
SA_T01_n831u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994); part of the Sarasvatī devotional hymnody shared between Hindu and Newar Buddhist worship.

SA_T01_n832u.title: Daśabhūmīśvaro nāma mahāyānasūtraratnarājastotra
SA_T01_n832u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n832u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n832u.summary: A hymn titled 'Lord of the Ten Stages, the Jewel-King among Mahayana sutras,' praising the bodhisattva path through the ten bhūmis (stages) and likely eulogizing the Daśabhūmika-sūtra itself as the 'jewel-king' of Mahayana scriptures.
SA_T01_n832u.history: Anonymous hymn in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Its theme draws on the Daśabhūmika-sūtra, the foundational text of the ten bodhisattva stages later incorporated into the Avataṃsaka.

SA_T01_n833u.title: Caityavandanāstotra
SA_T01_n833u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n833u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n833u.summary: A hymn of homage (vandanā) to the caitya/stūpa, the reliquary shrine that symbolizes the Buddha. It expresses veneration of the stupa as an object of worship and a focus of merit-making.
SA_T01_n833u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Caitya/stūpa veneration is a central practice of Newar Buddhism, where the hymn would accompany circumambulation and offering.

SA_T01_n834u.title: Vidyākṣarastotra
SA_T01_n834u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n834u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern Nepalese Buddhist transmission
SA_T01_n834u.summary: A hymn structured on the 'syllables of knowledge/spell' (vidyākṣara), praising a deity through the letters of a vidyā (mantra/spell). Each syllable is correlated with an aspect of the deity or a soteriological power.
SA_T01_n834u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994); belongs to the akṣara-based mantric hymnody of Newar Vajrayana.

SA_T01_n835u.title: Cakrasaṃvarastuti
SA_T01_n835u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n835u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult (tantra itself c. 8th-9th c. CE; this hymn later)
SA_T01_n835u.summary: A hymn of praise to Cakrasaṃvara (Heruka), the principal yidam of the Cakrasaṃvara/Saṃvara tantric system, depicted in union with his consort Vajravārāhī. It extols his fierce iconography and the maṇḍala over which he presides.
SA_T01_n835u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The Cakrasaṃvara tradition is centrally maintained by the Newar Buddhists of the Kathmandu Valley, where such stutis form part of sādhana practice.

SA_T01_n836u.title: Catuḥṣaṣṭisaṃvarastotra
SA_T01_n836u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n836u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
SA_T01_n836u.summary: A hymn praising the sixty-four (catuḥṣaṣṭi) deities of the Saṃvara maṇḍala, enumerating and venerating the retinue of yoginīs, ḍākas, and ḍākinīs surrounding Cakrasaṃvara and Vajravārāhī.
SA_T01_n836u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The number 64 reflects the goddesses/deities of the Saṃvara maṇḍala system in the yoginītantra literature.

SA_T01_n837u.title: Pīṭhastava
SA_T01_n837u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n837u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
SA_T01_n837u.summary: A hymn to the sacred seats or power-places (pīṭha) of the tantric geography — the network of pīṭhas, upapīṭhas and other sites associated with the Cakrasaṃvara/Vajravārāhī maṇḍala and the yoginī cult. It venerates these sites and their presiding deities.
SA_T01_n837u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The pīṭha scheme is a defining feature of the Cakrasaṃvara/yoginītantra system, mapped onto both external geography and the practitioner's body.

SA_T01_n838u.title: Ṣaṭtriṃśatsaṃvarastuti
SA_T01_n838u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n838u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
SA_T01_n838u.summary: A hymn praising the thirty-six (ṣaṭtriṃśat) deities of a Saṃvara maṇḍala, eulogizing the central couple Cakrasaṃvara-Vajravārāhī together with their thirty-six-fold retinue of yoginīs and guardians.
SA_T01_n838u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Paired thematically with the Catuḥṣaṣṭisaṃvarastotra, it reflects the variant maṇḍala enumerations of the Saṃvara cycle.

SA_T01_n839u.title: Vasantatilakāstuti
SA_T01_n839u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n839u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted within the Cakrasaṃvara cult, medieval to early-modern
SA_T01_n839u.summary: A hymn whose title puns on the Vasantatilakā metre and on Vasantatilakā as a name associated with the Heruka/Saṃvara cycle (the Abhidhānottara/Vasantatilaka tradition). It is a tantric eulogy connected to the Cakrasaṃvara-Vajravārāhī literature.
SA_T01_n839u.history: Anonymous entry in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). 'Vasantatilaka' figures as a title within Heruka/Saṃvara tantric literature as well as being a classical Sanskrit metre.

SA_T01_n840u.title: Dharmadhātunāmastava
SA_T01_n840u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (attribution disputed by some modern scholars)
SA_T01_n840u.date_estimate: if Nāgārjuna, c. 2nd-3rd c. CE; attribution and dating disputed
SA_T01_n840u.summary: A hymn 'in praise of the Dharmadhātu' (the basic realm/element of all phenomena), in roughly 100 verses, describing how the luminous dharmadhātu (equated with buddha-nature) is obscured by adventitious defilements in ordinary beings, progressively uncovered along the bodhisattva path, and fully revealed as buddhahood.
SA_T01_n840u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna (the Dharmadhātustava/Dharmadhātustotra) and unquestioned in India and Tibet, though some modern scholars doubt the attribution given its tathāgatagarbha content. Long known only in Tibetan and Chinese, a Sanskrit manuscript surfaced and was published in 2015; here it appears within the Newar Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha.

SA_T01_n841u.title: Pañcākṣarastotra
SA_T01_n841u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n841u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n841u.summary: A short Buddhist hymn celebrating the five-syllable mantra (pañcākṣara), praising the sacred syllables and the deity (typically Avalokiteśvara or Mañjuśrī, or the five Tathāgatas) they invoke. It belongs to the Nepalese devotional repertoire in which the recitation of mantric syllables is treated as a form of praise and meditative practice.
SA_T01_n841u.history: It is one of the anonymous hymns gathered in the Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. Janardan Shastri Pandey, 1994) drawn from Buddhist manuscripts preserved in Nepal, where such stotras are still chanted by Newar Buddhist practitioners. Its author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n842u.title: Buddhagaṇḍīstava
SA_T01_n842u.author: attributed (traditionally) to Aśvaghoṣa or Āryadeva; uncertain
SA_T01_n842u.date_estimate: disputed; possibly c. 2nd–5th c. CE if Aśvaghoṣa/Āryadeva, otherwise medieval
SA_T01_n842u.summary: A hymn of praise to the Buddha framed around the gaṇḍī, the wooden gong/sounding-board struck to summon the monastic community. The poem uses the sound of the gaṇḍī as a metaphor for the proclamation of the Dharma and the awakening call of the Buddha.
SA_T01_n842u.history: It is closely related to (or a recension of) the Gaṇḍīstotra/Gaṇḍīstava that A. von Staël-Holstein reconstructed from Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese sources; that work has been traditionally ascribed to Aśvaghoṣa and in other sources to Āryadeva, an attribution scholars regard as uncertain. It survives in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha and in the Tibetan Tanjur hymn collection.

SA_T01_n843u.title: Gaṇḍīstava
SA_T01_n843u.author: traditionally attributed to Aśvaghoṣa (also to Āryadeva); uncertain
SA_T01_n843u.date_estimate: disputed; c. 2nd–5th c. CE on the traditional attribution, otherwise medieval
SA_T01_n843u.summary: A Sanskrit hymn (about 34 verses) built on the imagery of the gaṇḍī, the wooden gong used in monasteries to assemble the saṅgha; the striking of the gong becomes a figure for awakening beings to the Dharma. It is a kāvya-style devotional poem praising the Buddha.
SA_T01_n843u.history: The text was largely restored to Sanskrit by Baron A. von Staël-Holstein from Tibetan and Chinese versions (the Chinese catalogues render it as a Ghaṇṭi-stotra/Ghaṇṭi-sūtra). It is preserved in the Tibetan Tanjur collection of hymns and in Nepalese manuscript collections such as the Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha; the ascription to Aśvaghoṣa (or Āryadeva) is traditional and contested.

SA_T01_n844u.title: Gaṇeśastotra
SA_T01_n844u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n844u.tradition: Buddhist (Newar Buddhism of Nepal); deity shared with Hinduism
SA_T01_n844u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n844u.summary: A short hymn in praise of Gaṇeśa, the elephant-headed remover of obstacles, here invoked within the Buddhist (Vajrayana) ritual milieu of Nepal where Gaṇeśa is venerated as a guardian. It seeks his favor for the removal of impediments at the start of religious undertakings.
SA_T01_n844u.history: It is an anonymous hymn from the Nepalese Buddhist stotra repertoire collected in the Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Gaṇeśa is one of several pan-Indian deities absorbed into Newar Buddhist worship; the hymn's author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n845u.title: Hāratīstotra
SA_T01_n845u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n845u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n845u.summary: A short hymn (roughly nine verses) in praise of Hārītī (Hāratī), the goddess who, once a child-devouring yakṣiṇī, was converted by the Buddha and became a protectress of children and a bestower of fertility and well-being. The hymn invokes her protective and benevolent aspects.
SA_T01_n845u.history: Hārītī is a popular protective deity in Nepalese Buddhism (identified with Ajimā/Hāratī at Swayambhu). This stotra is one of the anonymous Nepalese hymns gathered in the Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha and is still recited devotionally; its author and date are unknown.

SA_T01_n850u.title: Mahācakravartināmāṣṭottaraśatastotra
SA_T01_n850u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n850u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n850u.summary: A litany-hymn enumerating the 108 names (aṣṭottaraśata-nāma) of a 'Mahācakravartin' (Great Wheel-Turning) deity or Buddha-form, each name serving as an object of praise and invocation. Hymns of this 108-name type are recited to accumulate merit and secure the deity's protection.
SA_T01_n850u.history: It belongs to the Nepalese Buddhist devotional corpus collected in the Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The 108-name format is a widespread Indian devotional genre; this particular hymn's authorship and date are unrecorded and its precise dedicatee is not securely identified.

SA_T01_n851u.title: Mahākālastotra
SA_T01_n851u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n851u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n851u.summary: A hymn in praise of Mahākāla, the wrathful dharmapāla ('protector of the Dharma') of Vajrayana Buddhism, describing his fierce iconography and invoking his power to destroy obstacles and protect practitioners. Mahākāla is a major guardian deity in Nepalese and Tibetan Buddhism.
SA_T01_n851u.history: It is one of the protector-hymns in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Mahākāla is a deity shared across Hindu and Buddhist traditions; in the Buddhist context he is a wrathful manifestation associated with the Buddha-families. The hymn's author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n852u.title: Vajramahākālastotra
SA_T01_n852u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n852u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n852u.summary: A hymn to Vajra-Mahākāla, the vajra (adamantine/tantric) form of the wrathful protector Mahākāla, praising his ferocious attributes and invoking his protective and obstacle-destroying power within Vajrayana practice. It is a companion piece to the Mahākālastotra emphasizing the deity's tantric, vajra aspect.
SA_T01_n852u.history: It belongs to the Nepalese Buddhist protector-hymn repertoire collected in the Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Mahākāla in his various forms is a central dharmapāla in Newar and Tibetan Vajrayana; this hymn's author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n853u.title: Mañjuśrīnāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotra [Āryamañjuśrīnāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotra]
SA_T01_n853u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n853u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n853u.summary: A devotional litany enumerating the 108 names of Ārya-Mañjuśrī, the bodhisattva of wisdom, each epithet praising an aspect of his nature and serving as an object of recitation. It belongs to the 108-name (aṣṭottaraśata-nāma) hymn genre and is distinct from the longer tantric Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti.
SA_T01_n853u.history: It is one of the Mañjuśrī hymns preserved in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The 108-name litany is a common devotional form; this hymn is closely associated with the broader Mañjuśrī cult expressed in the famous Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti. Its author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n854u.title: Mañjuśrīstotra
SA_T01_n854u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n854u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n854u.summary: A hymn in praise of Mañjuśrī, the bodhisattva embodying transcendent wisdom (prajñā), invoking his sword that cuts ignorance and his book of perfect knowledge, and seeking his blessing of insight and eloquence. Mañjuśrī is especially venerated in Nepal, where he is linked to the legendary draining of the Kathmandu valley.
SA_T01_n854u.history: It is one of several Mañjuśrī hymns in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). Such stotras are still recited by Newar Buddhists; this particular hymn's author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n855u.title: Guhyeśvarīstotra
SA_T01_n855u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n855u.tradition: Buddhist (Vajrayana; Newar Buddhism of Nepal); deity shared with Hindu Śākta tradition
SA_T01_n855u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n855u.summary: A hymn to Guhyeśvarī, the 'Secret Goddess' worshipped at the celebrated Guhyeśvarī shrine in the Kathmandu valley, who is venerated by both Buddhists (as a form of the tantric goddess/ḍākinī Nairātmyā or Prajñā) and Hindus (as a Śākta pīṭha goddess). The hymn praises her hidden, esoteric nature and invokes her protection.
SA_T01_n855u.history: It is among the goddess-hymns preserved in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994) and listed in modern Nepalese stotra collections. Guhyeśvarī is a key site of shared Hindu–Buddhist tantric devotion in Nepal; the hymn's author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n856u.title: Nairātmāṣṭakastotra
SA_T01_n856u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n856u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (late 1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n856u.summary: An eight-verse hymn (aṣṭaka) in praise of Nairātmyā, the principal consort/yoginī of Hevajra and the deification of the doctrine of non-self (nairātmya), the central insight that there is no abiding self in persons or phenomena. The hymn praises her tantric form while pointing to the wisdom of selflessness she embodies.
SA_T01_n856u.history: It belongs to the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994) and the Hevajra-related devotional tradition. Nairātmyā is closely associated with Guhyeśvarī in Nepalese Buddhism. The eight-verse aṣṭaka is a standard hymn form; this work's author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n857u.title: Pañcatathāgatastotra
SA_T01_n857u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n857u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n857u.summary: A hymn in praise of the five Tathāgatas (Vairocana, Akṣobhya, Ratnasambhava, Amitābha, Amoghasiddhi), the five 'wisdom Buddhas' who preside over the maṇḍala of the Buddha-families. The hymn typically praises each Buddha's color, direction, mudra and wisdom, mapping the structure of the Vajrayana maṇḍala.
SA_T01_n857u.history: It is one of the hymns in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The five-Tathāgata scheme derives from the Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṃgraha and related tantras; this devotional hymn's author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n858u.title: Pañcatathāgatastutigāthā
SA_T01_n858u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n858u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n858u.summary: A set of praise-verses (stuti-gāthā) addressed to the five Tathāgatas, the five wisdom Buddhas of the Vajrayana maṇḍala. Like the related Pañcatathāgatastotra, it lauds each of the five Buddhas and their associated wisdoms and Buddha-families.
SA_T01_n858u.history: It is a companion hymn to the Pañcatathāgatastotra in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994). The 'gāthā' designation marks its verse-of-praise form. Its author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n859u.title: Sragdharāpañcakastotra
SA_T01_n859u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n859u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n859u.summary: A hymn of five (pañcaka) stanzas composed in the elaborate sragdharā metre, praising a Buddhist deity—most likely the goddess Tārā or a related figure—in ornate kāvya style. The sragdharā metre is favored in Buddhist Tārā hymnody, the most famous example being Sarvajñamitra's Sragdharāstotra.
SA_T01_n859u.history: It belongs to the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994) and reflects the same metrical-devotional tradition as Sarvajñamitra's celebrated Sragdharāstotra to Tārā (c. 8th c. Kashmir), though it is a distinct, shorter five-verse composition. Its author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n861u.title: Pañcarakṣādevīstotrāṇi
SA_T01_n861u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n861u.tradition: Buddhist (Mahayana/Vajrayana; especially Newar Buddhism of Nepal)
SA_T01_n861u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (the Pañcarakṣā corpus is attested by c. 800 CE; these hymns preserved in Nepalese manuscripts)
SA_T01_n861u.summary: A set of hymns to the five protective goddesses (Pañcarakṣā): Mahāpratisarā, Mahāsāhasrapramardanī, Mahāmāyūrī, Mahāśītavatī, and Mahāmantrānusāriṇī, each the personification of a protective dhāraṇī. The goddesses are invoked to avert disease, snakebite, danger and natural calamity and to secure worldly welfare.
SA_T01_n861u.history: These hymns derive from the immensely popular Pañcarakṣā tradition, a dhāraṇī corpus attested in Tibetan records by about 800 CE, whose manuscripts are found in nearly every traditional Newar Buddhist household. The stotra-hymns to the five goddesses are collected in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha; their authorship is anonymous.

SA_T01_n862u.title: Pratisarāstotra
SA_T01_n862u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n862u.genre: stotra (devotional hymn to a protective goddess)
SA_T01_n862u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (the goddess is attested from the late 8th c. CE; hymn preserved in Nepalese manuscripts)
SA_T01_n862u.summary: A hymn in praise of Mahāpratisarā ('the Great Amulet'), the chief of the five Pañcarakṣā goddesses, who personifies the protective Mahāpratisarā-dhāraṇī worn as an amulet against danger, disease and misfortune. The hymn praises her many-armed form and invokes her protection.
SA_T01_n862u.history: Mahāpratisarā arose from the deification of the Mahāpratisarā-dhāraṇī, which circulated as a protective amulet across Asia between the 8th and 12th centuries. This devotional hymn belongs to the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha and the wider Pañcarakṣā cult; its author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n867u.title: Bhaktiśataka
SA_T01_n867u.author: Rāmacandra Kavibhāratī
SA_T01_n867u.date_estimate: c. 12th–14th c. CE (medieval; commonly placed c. 12th–13th c.)
SA_T01_n867u.summary: A devotional poem of one hundred verses extolling the supremacy of the Buddha as the only being free from ignorance (avidyā), illusion (māyā) and attachment, contrasting him favorably with Brahmā, Viṣṇu and Śiva. It is a rare and influential expression of Buddhist bhakti (devotion), asserting the Buddha's unique spiritual perfection.
SA_T01_n867u.history: It was composed by the Bengali Buddhist poet Rāmacandra Kavibhāratī, who later carried Buddhist learning to Sri Lanka; the work became well known there and survives in Sanskrit with Sinhala and Tibetan transmission. It represents the Buddhist response to Hindu Puranic attempts to absorb the Buddha as an avatāra of Viṣṇu.

SA_T01_n868u.title: Buddhabhaṭṭārakastotra
SA_T01_n868u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n868u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n868u.summary: A hymn of praise to the Buddha addressed with the honorific 'Bhaṭṭāraka' ('Lord, Venerable One'), extolling his enlightened qualities, compassion and teaching. It belongs to the broad class of Buddha-stotras that celebrate the supreme excellences of the Awakened One.
SA_T01_n868u.history: It is one of the anonymous Buddha-hymns gathered in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994) from manuscripts preserved in Nepal. Its author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n869u.title: Buddhagadyaya [Buddhagajjaya]
SA_T01_n869u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n869u.genre: stotra in gadya (prose hymn of praise)
SA_T01_n869u.date_estimate: undated; medieval (1st–2nd millennium CE), preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n869u.summary: A hymn in praise of the Buddha composed in gadya (ornate prose) rather than verse, lauding the Buddha's qualities, deeds and transcendent nature in rhythmic prose. The 'gadya' form places it among the prose-poem panegyrics of the Buddhist devotional tradition.
SA_T01_n869u.history: It is one of the prose hymns preserved in the Nepalese Bauddha-stotra-saṃgraha (ed. J. S. Pandey, 1994); the variant title 'Buddhagajjaya' reflects manuscript/Newari spelling variation. Its author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n870u.title: Buddhastotra
SA_T01_n870u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n870u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts, likely mid- to late 1st / early 2nd millennium CE (composition date unknown)
SA_T01_n870u.summary: A short Sanskrit hymn of praise (stotra) to the Buddha, of the generic 'Buddhastotra' type found throughout Buddhist devotional literature. Such hymns extol the Buddha's perfections, his enlightenment, compassion, and the merit of venerating him, and are recited liturgically. The exact text is one of many anonymous Buddhastotras preserved in the Nepalese stotra corpus rather than a single famous named work.
SA_T01_n870u.history: It belongs to the large body of anonymous Buddhist praise-poetry transmitted in Newar Buddhist liturgical compilations such as the Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha and digitized in the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon (DSBC). The genre descends from classical models such as Mātṛceṭa's Śatapañcaśatika and the Catuḥśataka, but this particular title cannot be confidently tied to a single named author.

SA_T01_n871u.title: Nāmāṣṭaśataka
SA_T01_n871u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n871u.date_estimate: uncertain; 1st-2nd millennium CE, preserved in Nepalese manuscripts
SA_T01_n871u.summary: A 'hymn of one hundred and eight names' (aṣṭaśataka = 108) addressed to a Buddhist deity, most plausibly the Buddha himself. Such nāmāṣṭaśataka texts recite 108 epithets of the venerated figure as an act of devotion and merit-making, combining the litany (nāmastotra) and praise (stotra) genres. It belongs to the well-attested Buddhist subgenre of '108 names' praises (e.g. of Avalokiteśvara, Mañjuśrī, Jambhala).
SA_T01_n871u.history: The 108-names format is a standard Buddhist devotional template; numerous such texts survive in Sanskrit and in Tibetan translation. This particular item is part of the anonymous Nepalese stotra corpus and cannot be securely attributed to a named author or precisely dated.

SA_T01_n872u.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: brahmaṇā kṛtam
SA_T01_n872u.author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by the god Brahmā)
SA_T01_n872u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts (medieval to early modern)
SA_T01_n872u.summary: A hymn of praise to Śākyasiṃha ('the Lion of the Śākyas', i.e. the Buddha Śākyamuni), framed within the collection as having been 'composed/uttered by Brahmā' (brahmaṇā kṛtam). It is one of a set of Śākyasiṃhastotras in the Nepalese tradition, each pseudepigraphically attributed to a different mythological figure (Brahmā, Viṣṇu, Indra/Surapati, Śaṅkara, the Navagrahas, Yaśodharā). The Brahmā version has the Hindu creator-god glorifying the Buddha, a common Buddhist motif of subordinating Brahmanical gods to the Buddha.
SA_T01_n872u.history: These Śākyasiṃhastotras circulate together in Newar Buddhist liturgical manuscripts (e.g. Cambridge MS Add.1614, a Nepalese collection of stotras and songs) and are partly drawn from avadāna literature such as the Bhadrakalpāvadāna. The divine 'authors' are devotional framing devices rather than historical attributions.
related_works: Śākyasiṃhastotra (other recensions: viṣṇukṛtam, navagrahakṛtam, śaṅkarakṛtam, surapatikṛtam, yaśodharākṛtam, chando'mṛtoddhṛtam)

SA_T01_n873u.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: chando'mṛtoddhṛtam
SA_T01_n873u.author: Amṛtānanda (attributed; extracted from his Chandomṛtalatā)
SA_T01_n873u.date_estimate: early 19th c. CE (if from Amṛtānanda's Chandomṛtalatā)
SA_T01_n873u.summary: A hymn to Śākyasiṃha (the Buddha Śākyamuni) described as 'extracted from the Chando-amṛta' (chando'mṛtoddhṛtam) — i.e. drawn from the Chandomṛtalatā, the treatise on Sanskrit prosody by the Nepalese pandit Amṛtānanda. The verses serve simultaneously as devotional praise and as illustrations of metrical forms.
SA_T01_n873u.history: Amṛtānanda was the learned Newar Śākya pandit who worked with Brian Houghton Hodgson from the 1830s and composed several Sanskrit works, including the Chandomṛtalatā on metrics. This Śākyasiṃhastotra is one of the set of similarly titled hymns in the Nepalese stotra corpus; its 'chando'mṛtoddhṛtam' label points to extraction from Amṛtānanda's prosody work.

SA_T01_n875u.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: navagrahakṛtam
SA_T01_n875u.author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by the Nine Planets)
SA_T01_n875u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts (medieval to early modern)
SA_T01_n875u.summary: A praise-hymn to Śākyasiṃha (Buddha Śākyamuni) framed as 'composed by the Navagraha', the nine astral deities (the planets). Like the companion Śākyasiṃhastotras, it presents Brahmanical/astral divinities extolling the Buddha. The Cambridge Nepalese manuscript catalogues a '(Bhadrakalpāvadānoddhṛta)śākyasiṃhastotra by Navagraha', indicating this version is drawn from the Bhadrakalpāvadāna.
SA_T01_n875u.history: Part of the set of Śākyasiṃhastotras transmitted together in Newar Buddhist stotra-and-song manuscripts. The attribution to the Navagraha is a devotional framing; the verses circulate as avadāna-derived liturgical praise.

SA_T01_n876u.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: śaṅkarakṛtam
SA_T01_n876u.author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by Śiva/Śaṅkara)
SA_T01_n876u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts (medieval to early modern)
SA_T01_n876u.summary: A hymn to Śākyasiṃha (Buddha Śākyamuni) framed as 'composed by Śaṅkara', i.e. the god Śiva, who is depicted praising the Buddha. It belongs to the cluster of Śākyasiṃhastotras attributed to various deities, expressing the Newar Buddhist motif of the Hindu gods venerating the Buddha.
SA_T01_n876u.history: Transmitted with its companion Śākyasiṃhastotras in Nepalese liturgical manuscripts and partly derived from avadāna material; the 'authorship' is a pious framing device.

SA_T01_n877u.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: surapatikṛtam
SA_T01_n877u.author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by Indra, lord of the gods)
SA_T01_n877u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts (medieval to early modern)
SA_T01_n877u.summary: A hymn to Śākyasiṃha (Buddha Śākyamuni) framed as 'composed by the Surapati' — the lord of the gods, i.e. Indra/Śakra — who praises the Buddha. One of the set of Śākyasiṃhastotras placed in the mouths of different deities in the Newar Buddhist tradition.
SA_T01_n877u.history: Transmitted alongside the other Śākyasiṃhastotras in Nepalese stotra manuscripts; the divine attribution is a devotional convention reflecting the Buddhist subordination of Brahmanical gods to the Buddha.

SA_T01_n878u.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: viṣṇukṛtam
SA_T01_n878u.author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by Viṣṇu)
SA_T01_n878u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts (medieval to early modern)
SA_T01_n878u.summary: A hymn to Śākyasiṃha (Buddha Śākyamuni) framed as 'composed by Viṣṇu', who is depicted glorifying the Buddha. It is one of the cluster of Śākyasiṃhastotras attributed to various Hindu deities in the Newar Buddhist liturgical tradition.
SA_T01_n878u.history: Part of the set transmitted together in Nepalese stotra-and-song manuscripts and partly drawn from avadāna literature; the attribution to Viṣṇu is a pious framing rather than a historical authorship claim.

SA_T01_n879u.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: yaśodharākṛtam
SA_T01_n879u.author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by Yaśodharā)
SA_T01_n879u.date_estimate: uncertain; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts (medieval to early modern)
SA_T01_n879u.summary: A hymn to Śākyasiṃha (Buddha Śākyamuni) framed as 'composed by Yaśodharā', the Buddha's wife before his renunciation, who praises her former husband as the Awakened One. It is the one Śākyasiṃhastotra in the set attributed to a human (rather than divine) figure, lending it a personal, devotional character.
SA_T01_n879u.history: Catalogued in the Cambridge Nepalese stotra manuscript (MS Add.1614) as 'Śākyasiṃhastotra by Yaśodharā', it circulates with the companion Śākyasiṃhastotras in Newar Buddhist liturgy. The attribution is a literary/devotional framing.

SA_T01_n880u.title: Sattvārādhanagāthā
SA_T01_n880u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (āryanāgārjunakṛtā), almost certainly pseudepigraphic
SA_T01_n880u.date_estimate: uncertain; attribution to Nāgārjuna would imply c. 2nd c. CE, but the work is likely later and the attribution is traditional
SA_T01_n880u.summary: A short devotional poem in eleven verses (gāthās) on the 'propitiation/serving of sentient beings' (sattva-ārādhana) — the bodhisattva's commitment to please and benefit all living beings as the highest worship of the Buddha. The colophon presents it as 'spoken by the Perfectly Awakened One' (samyaksambuddhabhāṣitā) and the heading ascribes it to Ārya Nāgārjuna.
SA_T01_n880u.history: Preserved in the Nepalese tradition and digitized in the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon (proofread by Miroj Shakya). The attribution to Nāgārjuna, founder of Madhyamaka, is traditional and most likely pseudepigraphic, a common practice for short devotional verse collections.

SA_T01_n881u.title: Suprabhātastotra
SA_T01_n881u.author: Harṣadeva / Śrīharṣadeva (King Harṣa of Kanauj), attributed
SA_T01_n881u.date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (if by Harṣa, r. c. 606-647 CE); attribution debated
SA_T01_n881u.summary: Also known as the Suprabhātastava, a 'dawn' or 'good-morning' hymn comprising a selection of about forty-nine litanies (refrain-poetry) addressed to Śākyamuni and other Buddhas and Bodhisattvas. Each verse closes with an auspicious 'good morning' refrain, making it a liturgical morning-praise recited at the start of the day.
SA_T01_n881u.history: Traditionally ascribed to King Śrīharṣadeva of Kanauj (the emperor Harṣa, c. 590-647 CE), to whom Buddhist hymns are credited. The text survives in multiple Sanskrit manuscripts (including newly examined Drepung manuscripts) and a Tibetan translation, and has been edited several times; the Harṣa attribution, though traditional, remains discussed by scholars.

SA_T01_n882u.title: Saptabuddhastotra
SA_T01_n882u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n882u.date_estimate: uncertain; preserved in Nepalese manuscripts; translated by H. H. Wilson in the 19th c.
SA_T01_n882u.summary: A 'Hymn to the Seven Buddhas' — a praise-poem venerating the seven Buddhas of the past (the saptatathāgata / saptabuddha: Vipaśyin, Śikhin, Viśvabhū, Krakucchanda, Kanakamuni, Kāśyapa, and Śākyamuni). Each of the seven is glorified in turn, a standard Buddhist devotional and protective theme.
SA_T01_n882u.history: Listed among the Nepalese Buddhist stotras surviving in manuscript and noted as having been translated by H. H. Wilson. The seven-Buddhas formula is ancient and pan-Buddhist; this particular hymn is anonymous and of uncertain date.

SA_T01_n883u.title: Saptajinastava
SA_T01_n883u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n883u.summary: A 'Praise of the Seven Jinas (Conquerors)' — a hymn to the seven Buddhas of the past, 'Jina' being an epithet of the Buddha. Like the Saptabuddhastotra it eulogizes the lineage of seven Tathāgatas culminating in Śākyamuni, and is essentially a parallel/variant of that seven-Buddhas devotional theme.
SA_T01_n883u.history: Belongs to the anonymous Nepalese Buddhist stotra corpus; closely related in subject to the Saptabuddhastotra. Author and precise date are unknown.

SA_T01_n885u.title: Kamalākarasarvatathāgatastotra
SA_T01_n885u.author: anonymous/traditional (possibly composed/transmitted by a 'Kamalākara')
SA_T01_n885u.summary: A 'Hymn to All the Tathāgatas' (sarvatathāgatastotra) associated with the name Kamalākara — praising all the Buddhas collectively, a common Mahayana/Vajrayana devotional act invoking the full assembly of Tathāgatas. The 'Kamalākara' element may name the composer or the framing context (or, conceivably, a deity/locale).
SA_T01_n885u.history: Part of the Nepalese Buddhist stotra corpus digitized in collections such as the DSBC and the Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha. The text is obscure and its authorship and date are not securely established.

SA_T01_n886u.title: Ṣaḍabhijñastotra
SA_T01_n886u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n886u.summary: A hymn praising the Buddha as possessor of the 'six higher knowledges' (ṣaḍabhijñā) — the six supernormal powers/cognitions: magical power, the divine ear, knowledge of others' minds, recollection of past lives, the divine eye, and knowledge of the destruction of the defilements (āsravakṣaya). The hymn celebrates the Buddha's perfected wisdom and spiritual mastery through this classic sixfold scheme.
SA_T01_n886u.history: Belongs to the anonymous Nepalese Buddhist stotra collection. The doctrine of the six abhijñās is ancient and pan-Buddhist, but this particular praise-hymn is obscure and of uncertain date and authorship.

SA_T01_n887u.title: Kalyāṇapañcaviṃśatistotra
SA_T01_n887u.author: Amṛtānanda
SA_T01_n887u.summary: The 'Twenty-five Blessings Hymn' (Kalyāṇapañcaviṃśatika), a benedictory praise-poem of twenty-five verses composed in the elaborate Sragdharā metre, invoking auspiciousness and blessings in honour of the Buddha. It is a polished example of late Nepalese Buddhist kavya-style devotional verse.
SA_T01_n887u.history: Composed by the Newar Śākya pandit Amṛtānanda, the learned associate of Brian Houghton Hodgson in early 19th-century Nepal, who wrote several Sanskrit works including the Chandomṛtalatā on prosody. It is among the latest datable items in the Nepalese stotra corpus.

SA_T01_n888u.title: Maṅgalaṣoḍaśastuti
SA_T01_n888u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n888u.summary: A 'Sixteen-fold Auspicious Praise' (maṅgala-ṣoḍaśa-stuti) — a benedictory hymn in sixteen units invoking auspiciousness (maṅgala) and blessing, recited to open or sanctify ritual occasions. Such maṅgala-stutis are standard in Newar Buddhist liturgy for generating merit and good fortune.
SA_T01_n888u.history: Part of the anonymous Nepalese Buddhist stotra corpus (Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha / DSBC). Author and precise date are not established.

SA_T01_n889u.title: Mañjuvajrastotra
SA_T01_n889u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n889u.summary: A hymn of praise to Mañjuvajra, the tantric (Vajrayana) form of the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī who serves as the central deity (in place of Vairocana) in certain Guhyasamāja maṇḍalas. The stotra eulogizes Mañjuvajra's wisdom-embodiment and his role as a presiding tantric Buddha, and would be used in esoteric ritual practice.
SA_T01_n889u.history: Belongs to the Vajrayana stratum of the Nepalese Buddhist stotra corpus, reflecting the importance of Mañjuśrī/Mañjuvajra in the Guhyasamāja tradition and in the religious culture of the Kathmandu Valley (where Mañjuśrī is the legendary opener of the valley). The specific hymn is anonymous and of uncertain date.

SA_T01_n890u.title: Svayaṃbhūstava
SA_T01_n890u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n890u.summary: A hymn of praise to Svayambhū — the self-arisen primordial Buddha (Ādibuddha) embodied in the Svayambhū Mahācaitya, the great self-existent stupa shrine on the hill above the Kathmandu Valley. The hymn glorifies this sacred shrine and the Ādibuddha it manifests, central to Newar Buddhist devotion and pilgrimage.
SA_T01_n890u.history: Connected with the cult of the Svayambhū caitya, whose mythology is elaborated in the Svayambhū Purāṇa, the foundational sacred text of Nepalese Buddhism. This praise-hymn belongs to the local Newar liturgical stotra corpus and is anonymous and of uncertain date; it is essentially a shorter companion to the Svayambhūstotra.

SA_T01_n891u.title: Svayambhūstotra
SA_T01_n891u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n891u.summary: A hymn of praise to Svayambhū, the self-existent Ādibuddha worshipped in the form of the Svayambhū Mahācaitya in the Kathmandu Valley. The stotra extols the primordial Buddha-as-shrine and the merit of his veneration, a cornerstone of Newar Buddhist devotional practice.
SA_T01_n891u.history: Part of the local Newar Buddhist stotra corpus tied to the Svayambhū pilgrimage cult, whose legends are recounted in the Svayambhū Purāṇa — one of the most sacred Buddhist texts of Nepal. The hymn is anonymous, transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts, and of uncertain date; it stands as the fuller counterpart to the Svayaṃbhūstava.

SA_T01_n892u.title: Gururatnatrayastotra
SA_T01_n892u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n892u.date_estimate: 1st-2nd millennium CE (undatable precisely; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts)
SA_T01_n892u.summary: A devotional hymn praising the Guru together with the Three Jewels (ratnatraya): the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha. It typically extols the qualities of the spiritual teacher and the three refuges that form the foundation of Buddhist practice, often combining sub-hymns to each Jewel (buddharatna-, dharmaratna-, sangharatna-stotra).
SA_T01_n892u.history: An anonymous Buddhist Sanskrit stotra preserved in the manuscript traditions of the Kathmandu Valley and digitized by the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon (DSBC) project and Sanskrit Documents. Such guru- and ratnatraya-stotras reflect the centrality of guru-devotion and refuge in Vajrayana liturgy; precise authorship and date are not established.

SA_T01_n893u.title: Caṇḍikādaṇḍakastotra
SA_T01_n893u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n893u.tradition: Buddhist (Vajrayana; Newar Buddhism), with the goddess shared with Hindu Shakta imagery
SA_T01_n893u.summary: A hymn in the long, galloping daṇḍaka metre addressed to the fierce goddess Caṇḍikā. It praises her terrifying and protective aspects, invoking her power to destroy obstacles and demons. The daṇḍaka form, with its very long lines, lends the praise a torrential, incantatory quality suited to a wrathful deity.
SA_T01_n893u.history: A Sanskrit stotra transmitted in Buddhist (and overlapping Hindu Shakta) circles; the Buddhist recension was transliterated for the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon. Caṇḍikā/Caṇḍī is a fierce form of the Great Goddess venerated across traditions, and daṇḍaka-stotras to her circulated widely; authorship of this piece is anonymous.

SA_T01_n894u.title: Mahogratārāṣṭakastotra
SA_T01_n894u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n894u.summary: An eight-verse hymn (aṣṭaka) in praise of Mahogratārā ('Great Fierce Tārā'), a wrathful, dark-blue form of the savioress goddess Tārā closely associated with Ekajaṭā/Ugratārā. It extols her power to rescue devotees from fear, danger, and obstacles.
SA_T01_n894u.history: An anonymous Vajrayana stotra to a fierce Tārā form, preserved in Nepalese Buddhist manuscript collections and digitized by the DSBC. Ugra/Mahogra Tārā is a prominent wrathful deity in Buddhist Tantra; many short stotras and aṣṭakas to her circulated. It should not be confused with Sarvajñamitra's famous Sragdharā-stotra to Ārya-Tārā.

SA_T01_n895u.title: Mahogratārāstuti
SA_T01_n895u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n895u.summary: A hymn of praise (stuti) to Mahogratārā, the 'Great Fierce Tārā,' a wrathful blue form of the goddess Tārā. It glorifies her terrifying iconography and her saving, obstacle-removing power, functioning as a companion devotional piece to the corresponding aṣṭaka.
SA_T01_n895u.history: An anonymous Buddhist Tantric praise of Ugra/Mahogra Tārā preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and digitized in the DSBC corpus. It belongs to the cluster of short Tārā stotras current in Newar Vajrayana liturgy; author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n896u.title: Dharmadhātuvāgīśvaramaṇḍalastotra
SA_T01_n896u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n896u.date_estimate: 1st-2nd millennium CE (undatable precisely; deity form known from the Sādhanamālā era, c. 5th-11th c.)
SA_T01_n896u.summary: A hymn praising the maṇḍala of Dharmadhātuvāgīśvara, an esoteric, multi-armed and multi-faced form of the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī ('Lord of Speech of the Dharma-realm'). The text venerates the central deity and the assembly of the maṇḍala, a major cultic focus in Newar Buddhism of the Kathmandu Valley.
SA_T01_n896u.history: Dharmadhātuvāgīśvara is a form of Mañjuśrī whose sādhana is described in the Sādhanamālā; his maṇḍala remained an important object of worship and art in Nepal. This anonymous maṇḍala-stotra is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and digitized in the DSBC/Sanskrit Documents corpus.

SA_T01_n897u.title: Vāgīśvaravarṇanāstotra
SA_T01_n897u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n897u.summary: A descriptive hymn (varṇanā-stotra) praising Vāgīśvara, the 'Lord of Speech,' i.e. the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī in his form as patron of eloquence and learning. It describes his iconography and qualities and celebrates him as the tutelary deity revered throughout Nepal.
SA_T01_n897u.history: Vāgīśvara-Mañjuśrī is the tutelary deity of Nepalese Buddhists, his mantra 'oṃ vāgīśvara mūḥ' inscribed widely in the Kathmandu Valley. This anonymous praise-poem is preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist manuscript tradition and digitized in the DSBC corpus.

SA_T01_n898u.title: Vajrapāṇināmāṣṭottaraśatastotra
SA_T01_n898u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n898u.summary: A litany-hymn enumerating the 108 names (aṣṭottaraśata-nāma) of Vajrapāṇi, the bodhisattva who holds the vajra and embodies the Buddha's power and protective energy. Each name extols an aspect of the deity, and recitation of the full set functions as both praise and protective invocation.
SA_T01_n898u.history: Lists of 108 names form a standard devotional genre across Hindu and Buddhist Sanskrit literature. This anonymous Vajrapāṇi name-hymn belongs to the Newar Vajrayana liturgical repertoire and is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts digitized by the DSBC.

SA_T01_n899u.title: Mahābodhibhaṭṭārakastotra
SA_T01_n899u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n899u.summary: A hymn honouring the Mahābodhi Bhaṭṭāraka, the 'Venerable Great Enlightenment' — the Buddha as enshrined at the Mahābodhi temple in Bodhgayā, the site of his awakening, often as a venerated image. It praises the Buddha's enlightenment and the sacred seat (vajrāsana) of his victory.
SA_T01_n899u.history: Bodhgayā remained a pilgrimage focus for Nepalese and other Buddhists, and replicas of the Mahābodhi temple/image were venerated in Nepal. This anonymous praise-poem is listed among Buddhist Sanskrit stotras and preserved through the Nepalese manuscript tradition and DSBC digitization.

SA_T01_n900u.title: Mahābodhivandanāṣṭaka
SA_T01_n900u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n900u.genre: stotra (vandanā-aṣṭaka, salutation hymn of eight verses)
SA_T01_n900u.summary: An eight-verse hymn of salutation (vandanā) to the Mahābodhi — the Great Enlightenment of the Buddha and its sacred site at Bodhgayā. Each verse offers homage to the awakened Buddha and the place of his enlightenment.
SA_T01_n900u.history: An anonymous Buddhist Sanskrit aṣṭaka of homage to Bodhgayā/the Mahābodhi, part of the devotional literature surrounding the cult of the enlightenment site. Preserved in Nepalese Buddhist manuscripts and listed in the DSBC/Buddhist stotra corpus.

SA_T01_n901u.title: Māravijayastotra
SA_T01_n901u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n901u.summary: A hymn celebrating the Buddha's 'Victory over Māra' (māravijaya) — his defeat of the tempter-demon Māra and his hosts beneath the Bodhi tree on the night of his enlightenment. The text praises the Buddha's serene conquest of fear, desire, and death.
SA_T01_n901u.history: The conquest of Māra is among the most celebrated episodes of the Buddha's life and a recurrent subject of Sanskrit Buddhist poetry. This anonymous stotra was transliterated and proofread for the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon project and is preserved through the Nepalese manuscript tradition.

SA_T01_n902u.title: Vajrasattvastotra
SA_T01_n902u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n902u.summary: A hymn in praise of Vajrasattva, the 'Diamond Being,' the primordial bodhisattva-buddha who embodies the purity of awakened mind and is central to Vajrayana purification practice. It extols his luminous, indestructible nature and his role in cleansing defilements.
SA_T01_n902u.history: Vajrasattva is a key figure of Vajrayana liturgy, especially purification rites. This anonymous praise-poem belongs to the Newar Buddhist stotra repertoire and is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and the DSBC corpus.

SA_T01_n903u.title: Vajrasattvastuti
SA_T01_n903u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n903u.summary: A praise-hymn (stuti) to Vajrasattva, the embodiment of pure awakened mind in Vajrayana Buddhism. A companion piece to the corresponding stotra, it glorifies the deity's purity and his power to remove transgressions and obscurations.
SA_T01_n903u.history: An anonymous Buddhist Tantric praise of Vajrasattva preserved in the Nepalese manuscript tradition and digitized within the DSBC/Sanskrit stotra corpus; author and date are unrecorded.

SA_T01_n904u.title: Kalyāṇatriṃśatikāstotra
SA_T01_n904u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n904u.summary: A hymn of thirty verses (triṃśatikā) of auspicious praise (kalyāṇa) addressed to Trikāyanivāsinī Vajradevī, the 'Vajra Goddess who dwells in the three bodies' (trikāya) of the Buddha. It exalts the goddess as embodiment of the Buddha's body, speech, and mind.
SA_T01_n904u.history: Catalogued in the Sanskrit stotra corpus as 'kalyāṇatriṃśatikāstotram trikāyanivāsinīvajradevyāḥ,' linking it to the Vajradevī cult of Newar Vajrayana. It is an anonymous hymn preserved in Nepalese manuscripts with scans and text digitized by the DSBC and Sanskrit Documents.

SA_T01_n905u.title: Vajradevīstotra
SA_T01_n905u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n905u.summary: A hymn praising Vajradevī, the 'Vajra Goddess' (associated with Trikāyanivāsinī Vajradevī), a Tantric female deity venerated in Newar Buddhism. The text extols her divine attributes and her embodiment of awakened power.
SA_T01_n905u.history: An anonymous Vajrayana stotra to Vajradevī, closely connected with the Kalyāṇatriṃśatikā hymn to Trikāyanivāsinī Vajradevī. Preserved in Nepalese Buddhist manuscripts and digitized within the DSBC/Sanskrit stotra corpus.

SA_T01_n906u.title: Vajravilāsinīsādhanāstava
SA_T01_n906u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n906u.genre: stava/stotra embedded in or accompanying a sādhana
SA_T01_n906u.summary: A hymn of praise (stava) connected with the sādhana (meditative evocation ritual) of Vajravilāsinī, a Tantric form of the female buddha Vajrayoginī/Vajravārāhī in her 'playful, voluptuous' (vilāsinī) aspect. The text accompanies visualization practice, praising the deity as she is to be evoked.
SA_T01_n906u.history: Vajravilāsinī is a form of Vajrayoginī within the Anuttarayoga/Mother-Tantra cycle (related to Cakrasaṃvara and Hevajra). Such sādhana-linked stavas circulated in Newar Buddhist ritual; this anonymous text is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and the DSBC corpus.

SA_T01_n907u.title: Vajravilāsinīstotra
SA_T01_n907u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n907u.summary: A hymn in praise of Vajravilāsinī, a graceful, voluptuous form of the female buddha Vajrayoginī. It glorifies her iconography and her embodiment of blissful awakened wisdom in the Tantric tradition.
SA_T01_n907u.history: An anonymous Vajrayana praise of Vajravilāsinī (a Vajrayoginī/Vajravārāhī form), companion to the associated sādhana-stava. Preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist manuscript tradition and digitized within the DSBC/Sanskrit stotra corpus.

SA_T01_n908u.title: Vajrayoginīpraṇāmaikaviṃśikā
SA_T01_n908u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n908u.genre: stotra (praṇāma, hymn of homage in twenty-one verses)
SA_T01_n908u.summary: A hymn of homage (praṇāma) in twenty-one verses (ekaviṃśatikā) to Vajrayoginī, the female buddha and ḍākinī of the Mother-Tantra class. Each verse offers prostration to an aspect or form of the deity, on the model of the well-known twenty-one praises to Tārā.
SA_T01_n908u.history: Vajrayoginī's cult developed in India between the 10th and 12th centuries out of the Cakrasaṃvara tradition and became central in Newar and Tibetan Vajrayana. This anonymous twenty-one-verse homage is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and digitized in the DSBC corpus. (The original title is given as 'Vajrayoginīpraṇāmaikaviśikā,' read here as ...ekaviṃśikā.)

SA_T01_n909u.title: Vajrayoginyāḥ piṇḍārthastutiḥ
SA_T01_n909u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n909u.summary: A 'praise of the essential meaning' (piṇḍārtha-stuti) of Vajrayoginī, condensing the core significance of the deity and her practice into a hymn of praise. It glorifies Vajrayoginī as the female buddha embodying the union of bliss and emptiness.
SA_T01_n909u.history: An anonymous Vajrayana hymn distilling the essence (piṇḍārtha) of the Vajrayoginī cult, part of the Newar Buddhist devotional repertoire and preserved in Nepalese manuscripts digitized by the DSBC.

SA_T01_n910u.title: Vajrayoginyāḥ stutipraṇidhānam
SA_T01_n910u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n910u.genre: stotra combined with praṇidhāna (praise and aspiration prayer)
SA_T01_n910u.summary: A combined 'praise-and-aspiration' (stuti-praṇidhāna) directed to Vajrayoginī, joining a hymn of praise with a formal aspirational prayer (praṇidhāna) in which the devotee dedicates merit and vows to attain the deity's awakened state.
SA_T01_n910u.history: An anonymous Vajrayana liturgical text uniting praise with aspiration, characteristic of Mahayana/Vajrayana devotional structure. Preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist manuscript tradition and digitized in the DSBC corpus.

SA_T01_n911u.title: Vasudhārānāmadhāraṇīstotra
SA_T01_n911u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n911u.date_estimate: transmitted in Nepal since at least the 11th c. CE; underlying Vasudhārā dhāraṇī is earlier (1st millennium CE)
SA_T01_n911u.summary: A hymn-cum-dhāraṇī devoted to Vasudhārā ('Stream of Gems'), the bodhisattva-goddess of wealth, prosperity, and abundance. Combining praise with the efficacious dhāraṇī formula, it is recited to secure material and spiritual prosperity, drawing on the Vasudhārā Dhāraṇī and the legend of the layman Sucandra.
SA_T01_n911u.history: Vasudhārā has been especially venerated in Newar Buddhism of the Kathmandu Valley since at least the 11th century (early Nepalese sculptures from 1082 CE), with her cult rooted in the Vasudhārā Dhāraṇī. This anonymous dhāraṇī-stotra belongs to that devotional tradition and is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts digitized by the DSBC.

SA_T01_n912u.title: Vasudhārāstotra
SA_T01_n912u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T01_n912u.tradition: Buddhist (Mahayana/Vajrayana; especially Newar Buddhism of Nepal)
SA_T01_n912u.date_estimate: c. mid-1st millennium CE or later; precise date uncertain
SA_T01_n912u.summary: A devotional hymn (stotra) praising Vasudhara ('Stream of Gems'), the Buddhist goddess of wealth, prosperity and abundance, consort of the wealth-deity Jambhala. The text extols her qualities and invokes her grace to bestow material fortune, grain and gems on the devotee. It belongs to the broader cult of Vasudhara, whose worship (centered on the Vasudhara Dharani and her mantra Oṃ Vasudhārāyai Svāhā) is especially strong among the Buddhist Newars of the Kathmandu Valley.
SA_T01_n912u.history: Vasudhara's cult derives from the Vasudhara Dharani and the legend of the layman Sucandra, who sought wealth from the Buddha; the stotra is one of several short hymnic compositions in her honor transmitted especially in Nepalese Buddhist manuscript culture. Its specific authorship is unattested and it survives anonymously within collections of Buddhist stotras.
related_works: Sucandra-paripṛcchā (The Inquiry of the Layman Sucandra)

SA_T01_nagast_u.title: Acintyastava
SA_T01_nagast_u.author: Nāgārjuna (attributed; authorship debated)
SA_T01_nagast_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE if by Nāgārjuna; attribution disputed
SA_T01_nagast_u.summary: The 'Hymn to the Inconceivable [Buddha]' is one of the four hymns making up the Catuḥstava traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna. It praises the Buddha and his awakening while articulating core Madhyamaka doctrines, notably emptiness of own-being (svabhāvaśūnyatā) and dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda), presenting ultimate reality as inconceivable and beyond conceptual elaboration.
SA_T01_nagast_u.history: The Catuḥstava (Lokātīta-, Niraupamya-, Acintya-, Paramārtha-stava) was widely popular in Indian Mahayana circles; Sanskrit versions survive, but it is uncertain which four hymns were the original set and whether Nāgārjuna actually authored all of them. The Acintyastava is generally accepted as among the more securely Nāgārjunian of the group on doctrinal grounds.

SA_T01_naglok_u.title: Lokātītastava
SA_T01_naglok_u.author: Nāgārjuna (attributed; authorship debated)
SA_T01_naglok_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE if by Nāgārjuna; attribution disputed
SA_T01_naglok_u.summary: The 'Hymn to the One Who Transcends the World' is the opening hymn of the Catuḥstava ascribed to Nāgārjuna. It praises the Buddha who has gone beyond worldly categories and uses this praise to expound Madhyamaka teachings—analyzing the aggregates, elements and causal relations to show that all phenomena lack intrinsic existence and arise dependently.
SA_T01_naglok_u.history: Part of the four-hymn Catuḥstava set traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna and esteemed in Indian Mahayana; the Lokātītastava is often regarded, along with the Acintyastava, as among the most genuinely Nāgārjunian of the collection. Sanskrit and Tibetan versions survive, with the exact original membership of the set remaining uncertain.

SA_T01_rahppspu.title: Prajñāpāramitāstotra
SA_T01_rahppspu.author: Rāhulabhadra
SA_T01_rahppspu.summary: A short hymn in praise of the Perfection of Wisdom (Prajñāpāramitā) personified and venerated as 'Mother of the Buddhas.' Using vivid poetic imagery, it describes Prajñāpāramitā as luminous, stainless, and the dispeller of darkness throughout the triple world, expressing devotion to transcendent insight as the source of awakening. The hymn is frequently transmitted as a preface to Prajñāpāramitā sutra manuscripts.
SA_T01_rahppspu.history: Attributed to Rāhulabhadra (sometimes identified with the mahāsiddha Saraha), an early Madhyamaka figure whom Tibetan tradition makes Nāgārjuna's teacher and Chinese sources his disciple; his exact dates are uncertain. The hymn often appears prefixed to manuscripts of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā and has been edited and translated by Edward Conze.

SA_T01_tarasdhu.title: Tārāsragdharāstotra
SA_T01_tarasdhu.author: Sarvajñamitra (of Kashmir)
SA_T01_tarasdhu.summary: Also known simply as the Sragdharā-stotra, this is a celebrated hymn to the goddess Ārya-Tārā composed entirely in the sragdharā meter (the title puns on Tārā as 'garland-bearer' and on the meter). It praises Tārā's compassion and saving power and is recited devotionally to invoke her swift intervention against danger and suffering.
SA_T01_tarasdhu.history: Composed by Sarvajñamitra, a Buddhist bhikṣu of Kashmir who according to the Rājataraṅgiṇī flourished in the 8th century; legend holds he composed the hymn to escape being sacrificed by a king, whereupon Tārā miraculously delivered him and the other intended victims. It is preserved with a Sanskrit commentary (ṭīkā) by Jinarakṣita and in two Tibetan versions, and was edited by Satis Chandra Vidyabhusana.

SA_T02_abhnispu.title: Niṣpannayogāvali
SA_T02_abhnispu.author: Abhayākaragupta
SA_T02_abhnispu.summary: The 'Garland of Perfected Yogas' is a tantric compendium that systematically describes 26 maṇḍalas, giving detailed instructions on the deities, their iconography and arrangement, and the rituals of their visualization. It draws together maṇḍala systems and rituals from across diverse tantric traditions, balancing the yoginī-tantras and the yoga-tantra works.
SA_T02_abhnispu.history: Composed in India (c. 1101-08) by Abhayākaragupta, a leading scholar-monk and abbot of Vikramaśīla monastery. Together with his Vajrāvalī and Jyotirmañjarī it formed the esteemed 'triple garland cycle' of ritual works; first translated into Tibetan in 1204, these liturgies and maṇḍalas became enormously influential in Tibet.

SA_T02_bsa005_u.title: Caryāsaṅgrahapradīpa
SA_T02_bsa005_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T02_bsa005_u.date_estimate: first half of the 11th c. CE (Atiśa fl. 982-1054)
SA_T02_bsa005_u.summary: A short 'Lamp that is a Compendium of Conduct/Practice,' one of the numerous minor doctrinal and practice texts attributed to Atiśa. It compiles and summarizes points of religious conduct (caryā) for the practitioner, in the synoptic, path-oriented style characteristic of Atiśa's didactic works.
SA_T02_bsa005_u.history: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (982-1054), the renowned Bengali master and abbot associated with Vikramaśīla who reformed Buddhism in Tibet, composed many concise practice manuals (the 'lamp/pradīpa' genre, most famously the Bodhipathapradīpa). This minor work survives within his collected writings, primarily in Tibetan translation; details of its independent transmission are limited.

SA_T02_bsa006_u.title: Cittaviśuddhiprakaraṇa
SA_T02_bsa006_u.author: Āryadeva (tantric Āryadeva; traditionally identified with the Madhyamaka Āryadeva)
SA_T02_bsa006_u.date_estimate: probably late 7th-8th c. CE (the project hint of c. 170-270 CE reflects the traditional but problematic identification with the 3rd-c. Āryadeva)
SA_T02_bsa006_u.summary: The 'Treatise on the Purification of the Mind' expounds the principles and philosophy of Buddhist Tantra, treating mind as the foundation of both cognition and spiritual progress. It links purification of mind to emptiness (śūnyatā) and selflessness (nairātmya), arguing that a correct grasp of emptiness requires deconstructing belief in an enduring self and recognizing the insubstantiality of phenomena; it shows a clear Yogācāra tendency.
SA_T02_bsa006_u.history: Attributed to Āryadeva and traditionally conflated with the 3rd-century disciple of Nāgārjuna, but on doctrinal and tantric grounds modern scholarship dates this 'tantric Āryadeva' work much later, probably to the end of the 7th century or the 8th century. It has been studied and translated (e.g. by Mathew Varghese as 'Principles of Buddhist Tantra').

SA_T02_garbhasamgraha.title: Garbhasaṃgraha
SA_T02_garbhasamgraha.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (attributed)
SA_T02_garbhasamgraha.date_estimate: first half of the 11th c. CE (Atiśa fl. 982-1054)
SA_T02_garbhasamgraha.summary: A short 'Compendium on the Womb/Embryo' (garbha) attributed to Atiśa, one of his many brief compendia. By its title it appears to compile teachings concerning gestation/conception and the nature of (re)birth in the womb—a theme treated in Buddhist embryological literature that links the suffering of gestation to renunciation—though the work may instead concern garbha in the sense of the buddha-embryo/tathāgatagarbha; the precise content is not securely established here.
SA_T02_garbhasamgraha.history: Counted among the minor works of Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna and transmitted chiefly in his Tibetan collected writings. Buddhist embryological discourse drew on texts such as the Garbhāvakrāntisūtra, whose original Sanskrit survives only in fragments; this short saṃgraha stands in that broader tradition. Identification of its exact content is tentative.
related_works: Garbhāvakrāntisūtra (Sūtra on Entry into the Womb)

SA_T02_bsa043_u.title: Samādhisaṃbhāraparivarta
SA_T02_bsa043_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T02_bsa043_u.date_estimate: first half of the 11th c. CE (Atiśa fl. 982-1054)
SA_T02_bsa043_u.summary: The 'Chapter on the Accumulations/Prerequisites for Samādhi,' a short doctrinal work attributed to Atiśa that sets out the conditions, supports and prerequisites (saṃbhāra) needed to cultivate meditative concentration (samādhi). It belongs to Atiśa's didactic literature on the graded path of practice.
SA_T02_bsa043_u.history: One of the many minor compositions of Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (982-1054) preserved primarily in his Tibetan collected works; it reflects his characteristic systematic, stages-of-the-path approach to meditative cultivation that fed into the Tibetan lam-rim tradition.

SA_T02_bsa045_u.title: Gurupañcāśikā
SA_T02_bsa045_u.author: Aśvaghoṣa (traditional attribution; the tantric content postdates the famous 1st-2nd c. poet, so authorship is pseudepigraphic/uncertain)
SA_T02_bsa045_u.date_estimate: traditionally credited to Aśvaghoṣa (c. 1st-2nd c. CE), but the tantric subject matter implies a much later author (mid-1st millennium CE or later)
SA_T02_bsa045_u.summary: The 'Fifty Verses on the Guru' is the classic Vajrayana manual on guru devotion, prescribing in detail how a disciple should regard, honor and serve the spiritual teacher. It draws on a wide range of tantras (Guhyasamāja, Kālacakra, Cakrasaṃvara and others) and is customarily taught before conferring tantric initiation, since reliance on the guru is held to be essential to tantric practice.
SA_T02_bsa045_u.history: Attributed by tradition to Aśvaghoṣa, but because it cites tantras that postdate the famous Buddhist poet, the attribution is regarded as pseudepigraphic and the real author unknown. It became foundational in Tibetan Buddhism, with an important commentary by Tsongkhapa (Fulfillment of All Hopes) giving the tantric source for each verse.

SA_T02_bsa048_u.title: Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa
SA_T02_bsa048_u.author: Āryadeva (tantric Āryadeva of the Guhyasamāja Noble tradition)
SA_T02_bsa048_u.date_estimate: c. 9th c. CE (the project hint of c. 170-270 CE reflects traditional conflation with the early Madhyamaka Āryadeva)
SA_T02_bsa048_u.summary: The Caryāmelāpakapradīpa, the 'Lamp for Integrating/Compiling the Practices,' is a systematic prose exposition of the advanced yogas of the Guhyasamāja-tantra as taught in the Ārya (Noble Nāgārjuna) tradition. Equal in authority to Nāgārjuna's Pañcakrama, it is one of the earliest Sanskrit examples of the 'stages of the mantra path' genre and lays out the gradual Vajrayana path.
SA_T02_bsa048_u.history: Attributed to the 'tantric Āryadeva' of the Guhyasamāja Noble tradition—traditionally identified with the 3rd-century disciple of Nāgārjuna, but actually a much later figure (the work presupposes the developed Guhyasamāja system). It exerted immense influence on Indian and Tibetan Buddhism and was edited and translated by Christian K. Wedemeyer (with the 11th-c. Tibetan translation by Rinchen Zangpo).

SA_T02_dipguhsu.title: Guhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi
SA_T02_dipguhsu.author: Dīpaṃkarabhadra
SA_T02_dipguhsu.summary: An influential ritual manual setting out the rite of the Guhyasamāja maṇḍala—the procedures for constructing the maṇḍala, the arrangement of its 32 deities centered on Akṣobhya/Guhyasamāja, and the associated consecrations (abhiṣeka) and visualizations. It is a foundational liturgical text for Guhyasamāja practice.
SA_T02_dipguhsu.history: Composed by Dīpaṃkarabhadra, a 9th-century tantric preceptor at Vikramaśīla active under King Dharmapāla. The work was so authoritative that the 11th-century master Ratnākaraśānti wrote a commentary on it; its empowerment (abhiṣeka) section has been the subject of detailed modern scholarship.

SA_T02_khasrpsu.title: Khasarpaṇasādhana
SA_T02_khasrpsu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_khasrpsu.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (as transmitted in the Sādhanamālā, redacted by c. 11th-12th c.)
SA_T02_khasrpsu.summary: A sādhana (meditation-and-evocation manual) for Khasarpaṇa, a popular white form of Avalokiteśvara. The practitioner visualizes himself as Khasarpaṇa—holding a lotus, displaying the varada gesture, seated on a moon-disc over a lotus, bearing Amitābha in his crown and radiating moon-like rays—characteristically attended by the four deities Tārā, Sudhanakumāra, Bhṛkuṭī and Hayagrīva.
SA_T02_khasrpsu.history: Khasarpaṇa is described in several sādhanas within the Sādhanamālā, a large compilation of tantric evocation texts by various (often anonymous) authors; the popularity of this form is reflected by the number of its sādhanas. The collection took shape in eastern India/Nepal in the Pāla period.

SA_T02_mpvv_u.title: Mahāpratisarāvidyāvidhi
SA_T02_mpvv_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_mpvv_u.date_estimate: mid-to-late 1st millennium CE (the Mahāpratisarā dhāraṇī is attested by c. 800 CE)
SA_T02_mpvv_u.summary: A ritual procedure (vidhi) for the spell/knowledge-formula (vidyā) of Mahāpratisarā, the chief of the five Pañcarakṣā protector goddesses. The text gives the dhāraṇī and the rites for its use, invoking Mahāpratisarā's power to protect against sin, disease, evils and danger; her dhāraṇī was widely worn as an amulet.
SA_T02_mpvv_u.history: Mahāpratisarā heads the Pañcarakṣā ('Five Protections') set of protective dhāraṇī-goddesses, an early dhāraṇī-genre corpus attested in Tibetan catalogues by around 800 CE and enormously popular among Mahayana Buddhists, especially in Nepal, where Pañcarakṣā manuscripts were household texts. Many divergent recensions survive across Nepal, Tibet and India.

SA_T02_mrtvstsu.title: Mṛtyuvañcanasitatārāsādhana
SA_T02_mrtvstsu.author: anonymous/traditional (within the circle of Vāgīśvarakīrti's Mṛtyuvañcana cycle)
SA_T02_mrtvstsu.date_estimate: c. 11th c. CE or later (associated with Vāgīśvarakīrti's Vikramaśīla-period Mṛtyuvañcana cycle)
SA_T02_mrtvstsu.summary: A sādhana for 'Death-Cheating White Tārā' (Mṛtyuvañcana-Sitatārā), the long-life form of White Tārā invoked to avert untimely death and extend the lifespan. The practitioner visualizes and evokes the white goddess—associated with the wheel-on-the-breast iconography of the Mṛtyuvañcana family—performing the meditation and recitation that secure protection from life-threatening dangers.
SA_T02_mrtvstsu.history: It belongs to the 'cheating death' (mṛtyuvañcana / Kālavañcana / Mṛtyuṃjaya) White Tārā long-life practices, a tradition rooted in the works of Vāgīśvarakīrti, an 11th-century paṇḍita of Vikramaśīla whose Mṛtyuvañcana instructions were regarded as the root of the Tārā long-life commentaries. Such sādhanas circulated within Sādhanamālā-type collections.

SA_T02_mrtvts_u.title: Mṛtyuvañcanatārāsādhana
SA_T02_mrtvts_u.author: Vāgīśvarakīrti
SA_T02_mrtvts_u.summary: A sādhana for 'Death-Cheating Tārā' (Mṛtyuvañcana Tārā), a White Tārā long-life practice composed to overcome untimely death and prolong life. It provides the visualization of the goddess and the ritual/recitation for invoking her power to avert mortal danger; the deity bears the distinctive wheel emblem on her breast.
SA_T02_mrtvts_u.history: Composed by Vāgīśvarakīrti, an 11th-century paṇḍita of Vikramaśīla monastery, whose Mṛtyuvañcana ('cheating death') instructions are described as the root of all later Tārā long-life commentaries. This sādhana is the core text of his death-defeating White Tārā cycle, transmitted in Sanskrit and Tibetan.

SA_T02_mrtvutsu.title: Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśatārāsādhana
SA_T02_mrtvutsu.author: Vāgīśvarakīrti
SA_T02_mrtvutsu.summary: An instructional ('upadeśa') sādhana for 'Death-Cheating Tārā,' giving practical oral-style instructions for the White Tārā long-life practice that averts untimely death. It complements the main Mṛtyuvañcanatārāsādhana with guidance on the visualization, mantra recitation and ritual application for prolonging life.
SA_T02_mrtvutsu.history: Another component of Vāgīśvarakīrti's influential 11th-century Mṛtyuvañcana ('cheating death') White Tārā cycle composed at Vikramaśīla. His death-defeating Tārā instructions became foundational for the Tibetan White Tārā long-life tradition; the text survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan transmission.

SA_T02_msitvatu.title: Mahāśītavatī vidyārājñī
SA_T02_msitvatu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_msitvatu.date_estimate: mid-1st millennium CE (part of the Pañcarakṣā, attested by c. 800 CE)
SA_T02_msitvatu.summary: The 'Great Śītavatī, Queen of Spells' is an apotropaic dhāraṇī scripture and one of the five members of the Pañcarakṣā corpus of protector-goddess incantations. It offers protection against 'cruel' planets, dangerous animals and poisonous insects (and is associated with protection from smallpox), embodied in the goddess Mahāśītavatī, who is affiliated with the Buddha Amitābha.
SA_T02_msitvatu.history: Originally circulating under titles such as Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī or Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī-Śītavatī, the text was later incorporated into the Sanskrit Pañcarakṣā collection. The 'five great dhāraṇīs' are attested in Tibetan catalogues from around 800 CE, and over 300 Pañcarakṣā manuscripts survive, mainly from Nepal and eastern India.

SA_T02_n1397u.title: Guhyavajravilāsinīsādhana
SA_T02_n1397u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n1397u.summary: A sādhana for the 'Secret Vajravilāsinī,' a tantric form of the goddess Vajravilāsinī ('Vajra Coquette/Sporting One'), a manifestation associated with the Vajrayoginī/Vajravārāhī family. It gives the visualization, mantras and evocation rituals for realizing union with this fierce-yet-blissful female deity of the highest (yoginī) tantras.
SA_T02_n1397u.history: Such sādhanas belong to the Vajrayoginī sādhana corpus that developed in eastern India and Nepal between roughly the 10th and 12th centuries, preserved in palm-leaf Sanskrit collections like the Sādhanamālā and the Guhyasamayasādhanamālā, which gather works on the many forms of the goddess by various, often unnamed, authors.

SA_T02_n1398u.title: Guhyāvalī
SA_T02_n1398u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n1398u.date_estimate: uncertain; likely later 1st millennium to early 2nd millennium CE
SA_T02_n1398u.summary: A Buddhist tantric work whose title ('Garland of Secrets/Secret Garland') points to an esoteric ritual or doctrinal compilation in the Vajrayāna mode. It belongs to the body of Sanskrit tantric texts preserved chiefly in Nepalese manuscript collections, dealing with secret (guhya) practice, mantra and meditation.
SA_T02_n1398u.history: The text is obscure and not the subject of a major critical edition; it survives within the Sanskrit Buddhist tantric corpus transmitted by the Newar Buddhist community of the Kathmandu Valley, the principal preserver of Indian Vajrayāna manuscripts after the decline of Buddhism in India. Its precise authorship, date and affiliation remain unestablished.

SA_T02_n1399u.title: Kāṇhapādasya dohākoṣa
SA_T02_n1399u.author: Kṛṣṇacaryāpāda (Kāṇha / Kanhapa / Krishnacharya)
SA_T02_n1399u.date_estimate: c. late 8th-early 9th c. CE (author c. 775-850 CE)
SA_T02_n1399u.summary: The 'Treasury of Dohās' attributed to the mahāsiddha Kāṇha (Kṛṣṇacaryāpāda), a collection of rhymed couplets in Apabhraṃśa expressing the spontaneous (sahaja) experience of awakening and critiquing external ritual and scholasticism. Commentators read the verses as an esoteric manual on advanced yogic practices such as karmamudrā, caṇḍālī (gtum mo) and prabhāsvara (clear light).
SA_T02_n1399u.history: Kāṇha, one of the eighty-four mahāsiddhas and a disciple of Jālandhara, is also among the named poets of the Caryāpada corpus; his name is entangled with that of other 'Kṛṣṇa/Kanha' figures across different lineages. The dohās survive with Sanskrit/Apabhraṃśa commentaries (e.g. by Amṛtavajra) and were preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and in Tibetan translation.

SA_T02_n1401u.title: Mahāmāyāsādhanopāyikā
SA_T02_n1401u.author: anonymous/traditional (several sādhanas attributed to various siddhas)
SA_T02_n1401u.summary: A ritual 'means of accomplishment' (sādhanopāyikā) for the deity Mahāmāyā, prescribing the self-visualization and worship of the four-faced, four-armed blue heruka Mahāmāyā embracing the consort Buddhaḍākinī, surrounded by yoginīs. It gives the iconographic detail, mantras and meditative sequence needed to realize the deity according to the Mahāmāyā Tantra.
SA_T02_n1401u.history: The work belongs to the practice literature that grew up around the Mahāmāyā Tantra, one of the earliest Yoginī tantras, and is preserved within the Indo-Nepalese Sanskrit sādhana collections (e.g. of the Sādhanamālā type) and the Tibetan Tengyur. Authorship of individual Mahāmāyā sādhanas varies and is often uncertain.

SA_T02_n1402u.title: Mahāmāyātantra
SA_T02_n1402u.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_T02_n1402u.summary: A short Buddhist Anuttarayoga tantra of the Yoginī class, in three chapters, centered on the deity Mahāmāyā, 'queen of the ḍākinīs and yoginīs,' and especially associated with Dream Yoga. It treats siddhis, the classification of cause/result/means (hetu, phala, upāya) tantras, deity manifestation and maṇḍala, and transgressive techniques for manipulating the subtle body.
SA_T02_n1402u.history: Considered one of the earliest Yoginī tantras: it postdates the Guhyasamāja Tantra (by intertextual evidence) yet predates the more developed Hevajra Tantra. It was translated into Tibetan by the paṇḍita Jinavara with the translator Gö Lhetsé in the 11th century and remains a yidam practice in the Shangpa Kagyü tradition.

SA_T02_n1403u.title: Marmakālikā
SA_T02_n1403u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n1403u.genre: tantra / tantric commentary or ritual text
