SA_T02_n1403u.summary: An obscure Buddhist tantric text whose title ('She who concerns the vital/secret points,' marman) suggests a work on the esoteric 'vital points' of practice — the secret essentials of yogic or ritual instruction. It survives within the Sanskrit Buddhist tantric manuscript tradition.
SA_T02_n1403u.history: The work is not the subject of a major published study and its author, date and exact contents are not securely established. It belongs to the large body of Sanskrit Vajrayāna texts preserved in Nepalese manuscript repositories.

SA_T02_n1413u.title: Sarahapādasya dohākoṣa
SA_T02_n1413u.author: Saraha (Sarahapāda, 'the Great Brahman')
SA_T02_n1413u.summary: The 'Treasury of Dohās' of Saraha, a celebrated collection of Apabhraṃśa couplets (in Tibet the 'Songs for the People') voicing the spontaneous, innate (sahaja) experience of mind's nature and the Mahāmudrā realization, while satirizing brahmanical ritual, ascetic extremes and dry scholasticism. It is foundational poetry for the Mahāmudrā tradition.
SA_T02_n1413u.history: Saraha is revered as the human source of the Mahāmudrā teachings, preserved above all by the Tibetan Kagyü school. An early manuscript of his Dohākośa was copied in Nepal Saṃvat 221 (1101 CE), among the earliest witnesses, attesting wide circulation of his verses by then.

SA_T02_n1414u.title: Śrīvajravārāhīsādhana
SA_T02_n1414u.author: anonymous/traditional (numerous Vajravārāhī sādhanas by various siddhas, e.g. Umāpatideva)
SA_T02_n1414u.summary: A 'means of accomplishment' for the tantric goddess Vajravārāhī (a form of Vajrayoginī), guiding the practitioner to visualize the red, sow-headed deity at the center of her maṇḍala, recite her mantra and seed-syllable (vaṃ), and identify with her embodiment of wisdom-emptiness and great bliss. The four lotus-petal goddesses Ḍākinī, Lāmā, Khaṇḍarohā and Rūpiṇī attend her.
SA_T02_n1414u.history: Vajravārāhī sādhanas form a major strand of the Cakrasaṃvara/Vajrayoginī practice literature; a well-known example is the Vajravārāhī Sādhana of Umāpatideva (a thirty-seven-goddess maṇḍala), edited and studied by Elizabeth English. Many such sādhanas circulated in the Sanskrit sādhana collections and the Tibetan canon.

SA_T02_n1416u.title: Caryāgītikoṣa
SA_T02_n1416u.author: multiple mahāsiddhas (Kāṇha, Lui-pā, Śabara, Bhusuku and others)
SA_T02_n1416u.date_estimate: c. 8th-12th c. CE (songs composite); manuscript discovered 1907
SA_T02_n1416u.summary: An anthology of about forty-seven to forty-nine 'songs of conduct/realization' (caryāgīti) in Apabhraṃśa/Old Bengali, composed by various mahāsiddhas, sung as expressions of the enlightened state. Using vivid metaphor and coded 'twilight language,' they treat existence, delusion, the subtle body and the path to liberation, accompanied by a Sanskrit commentary.
SA_T02_n1416u.history: The collection (also linked to the Caryācaryāviniścaya) was recovered in 1907 from the Nepal Royal Library by Haraprasad Shastri and is celebrated as the earliest specimen of Bengali, Assamese, Maithili and Odia literature. The standard scholarly study is Per Kværne's An Anthology of Buddhist Tantric Songs.

SA_T02_n1424u.title: Śrīguhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi
SA_T02_n1424u.author: anonymous/traditional (such maṇḍalavidhis ascribed to authors of the Ārya school, e.g. Dīpaṃkarabhadra)
SA_T02_n1424u.genre: maṇḍalavidhi (ritual manual for maṇḍala construction and consecration)
SA_T02_n1424u.summary: A ritual manual setting out the procedures for constructing, consecrating and entering the maṇḍala of the Guhyasamāja deity (Akṣobhyavajra/Samantabhadra and his retinue), including site preparation, deity placement, initiation (abhiṣeka) and offerings. It serves the generation-stage practice of the Guhyasamāja system.
SA_T02_n1424u.history: Maṇḍalavidhi texts form an essential genre of the Guhyasamāja exegetical corpus, transmitted through the Ārya (Nāgārjuna) and Jñānapāda lineages; the Tengyur preserves a large cluster of Guhyasamāja-related maṇḍala-ritual works (Toh 1796-1825). A famous example is Dīpaṃkarabhadra's Guhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi.

SA_T02_n1426u.title: Tattvasiddhi
SA_T02_n1426u.author: attributed to Śāntarakṣita (attribution debated)
SA_T02_n1426u.date_estimate: c. 8th c. CE (if by Śāntarakṣita, 725-788 CE); disputed
SA_T02_n1426u.summary: A tantric treatise establishing 'reality/the true nature' (tattva), engaging the relationship between tantric praxis (mantra, deity yoga, the use of transgressive means) and Buddhist epistemology and Madhyamaka view. It argues for the validity and supremacy of the tantric path while grounding it in established Buddhist philosophy.
SA_T02_n1426u.history: The work is traditionally ascribed to Śāntarakṣita, the great Yogācāra-Madhyamaka master and author of the Tattvasaṃgraha, though the attribution has been questioned (e.g. by Ernst Steinkellner). It is a notable example of tantric thought engaging Dharmakīrtian epistemology and yogic perception.

SA_T02_n2873u.title: Vāṅmaṇḍalanamaskāraślokāḥ
SA_T02_n2873u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n2873u.summary: A short set of verses (ślokāḥ) of salutation/homage (namaskāra), apparently addressed to a 'circle of speech' (vāṅmaṇḍala) — likely the maṇḍala of speech-deities or the speech-aspect of the buddhas. The text is a devotional/liturgical piece for recitation.
SA_T02_n2873u.history: A minor liturgical work preserved within the Sanskrit Buddhist tantric corpus; its author, date and precise object of homage are not securely established. It belongs to the namaskāra/stotra literature copied in Nepalese manuscript collections.

SA_T02_n2877u.title: Bauddha pāribhāṣikāḥ śabdāḥ
SA_T02_n2877u.author: anonymous/traditional (lexical compilation)
SA_T02_n2877u.date_estimate: uncertain; a compiled list, possibly later
SA_T02_n2877u.summary: A glossary or list of Buddhist technical/terminological words (pāribhāṣika śabda) — that is, the specialized vocabulary of Buddhist doctrine and practice gathered for reference. It functions as a lexical aid to reading Buddhist Sanskrit texts.
SA_T02_n2877u.history: Such terminological compilations belong to the lexicographic and pedagogical apparatus of Sanskrit Buddhist literature; this particular item is minor and not associated with a famous named author. The title is generic and may denote a modern or traditional compilation of defined terms.

SA_T02_n2884u.title: Samantabhadra nāma sādhana ṭīkā
SA_T02_n2884u.author: anonymous/traditional (commentator unidentified)
SA_T02_n2884u.summary: A commentary (ṭīkā) on the 'Samantabhadra-nāma-sādhana,' an evocation manual for the deity Samantabhadra. In the Guhyasamāja system Samantabhadra is a principal buddha-form, and the commentary explicates the visualization, mantra and meditative procedure of the underlying sādhana.
SA_T02_n2884u.history: The work belongs to the dense exegetical literature surrounding Guhyasamāja-related sādhanas; the root Samantabhadra-sādhana (Tib. sgrub thabs kun tu bzang po) appears in Guhyasamāja text compilations. The commentator's identity and exact date are not securely established.

SA_T02_n2886u.title: Samājasādhanavyavastholi
SA_T02_n2886u.author: Nāgabodhi (Nāgabuddhi)
SA_T02_n2886u.date_estimate: ascribed to Nāgabodhi (traditionally c. 7th c. CE; the Ārya school flourished c. 8th-9th c. CE)
SA_T02_n2886u.summary: A 'graded arrangement of the Samāja (Guhyasamāja) sādhana' (Tib. 'dus pa'i sgrub thabs rnam par bzhag pa'i rim pa), laying out the stages of the Guhyasamāja deity-yoga practice in ordered sequence. It is a generation-stage practice manual within the Ārya (Nāgārjuna) lineage of Guhyasamāja exegesis.
SA_T02_n2886u.history: Attributed to Nāgabodhi/Nāgabuddhi, a key figure of the Ārya school traditionally counted in the Nāgārjuna lineage of the Guhyasamāja transmission. The work is part of the corpus of Guhyasamāja Highest Yoga Tantra sādhana literature analyzed by modern scholars.

SA_T02_n2906u.title: Mantroddhāra
SA_T02_n2906u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n2906u.summary: A text on the 'extraction/derivation of mantras' (mantroddhāra) — the technical procedure by which the syllables of a deity's mantra are drawn out, usually from a coded matrix or alphabet diagram (mātṛkā). Such works enable the practitioner to construct the correct mantra for a given deity in tantric ritual.
SA_T02_n2906u.history: Mantroddhāra is a standard ritual procedure across Buddhist (and Śaiva) tantric literature; many tantras and sādhanas contain mantroddhāra sections. This particular short work is preserved within the Sanskrit Buddhist tantric corpus; its author and date are not securely fixed.

SA_T02_n2908u.title: Balimālikā
SA_T02_n2908u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n2908u.summary: A 'garland (mālikā) of bali (food/torma) offerings' — a ritual manual giving the verses, mantras and procedures for presenting bali offerings to deities, protectors and spirits within tantric Buddhist liturgy. Bali rites are a standard component of sādhana and maṇḍala worship.
SA_T02_n2908u.history: Bali-offering manuals are a common practical genre in Vajrayāna ritual, especially in the living Newar Buddhist tradition of Nepal where torma/bali rites remain central. This particular text is minor and its author and date are not securely established.

SA_T02_n2910u.title: Ādikarmapradīpa [Ādikarmapradīpa Bodhicaryāvatāraṭīkā]
SA_T02_n2910u.author: Anupamavajra (attributed)
SA_T02_n2910u.genre: ritual manual (vidhi) for the beginner practitioner; partly commentarial
SA_T02_n2910u.summary: The 'Lamp for the Beginning Practitioner' (ādikarmika), a manual prescribing the daily devotional and meditative routine for one newly entering the Mahāyāna/Vajrayāna path — refuge, worship, confession, the bodhisattva vow and preliminary practices. Its title also links it to the Bodhicaryāvatāra, on whose ethos of bodhisattva training it draws.
SA_T02_n2910u.history: The Ādikarmapradīpa was published by Louis de la Vallée Poussin (1898) alongside a Bodhicaryāvatāraṭīkā in his Bouddhisme, études et matériaux; it is commonly attributed to Anupamavajra. It illustrates how Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra was absorbed into practical ritual instruction for novices.

SA_T02_n2912u.title: Prajñopāyaviniścayasiddhi
SA_T02_n2912u.author: Anaṅgavajra
SA_T02_n2912u.date_estimate: c. late 7th-early 8th c. CE (author c. 705 CE)
SA_T02_n2912u.summary: The 'Establishment of the Certainty of Wisdom (prajñā) and Means (upāya),' a verse treatise on the union of wisdom and skilful means that defines tantric realization. It describes various siddhis (attainments/powers) and, in keeping with early antinomian tantra, prescribes transgressive, including sexual, practices as path.
SA_T02_n2912u.history: Attributed to Anaṅgavajra, counted among the eighty-four mahāsiddhas and dated by Tāranātha's chronology to around 705 CE. It is one of the canonical 'Seven Siddhi Texts' (saptasiddhi) of the Oḍiyāna Mahāmudrā lineage and preserves some of the earliest articulations of tantric Buddhist doctrine.

SA_T02_n2914u.title: Śrīsahajasiddhi
SA_T02_n2914u.author: Ḍombī Heruka (Ḍombipāda)
SA_T02_n2914u.date_estimate: c. mid-8th c. CE (author listed c. 777 CE)
SA_T02_n2914u.summary: The 'Glorious Accomplishment of the Innate (sahaja),' a tantric treatise expounding the realization of the innate, spontaneous nature of mind and reality, and setting out the completion-stage (utpannakrama) meditation associated with the Hevajra Tantra. It teaches the non-dual sahaja experience as the goal of tantric praxis.
SA_T02_n2914u.history: Attributed to Ḍombī Heruka, one of the eighty-four mahāsiddhas (to be distinguished from a later Indrabhūti's like-named work). It is among the 'Seven Siddhi Texts' of the Oḍiyāna Mahāmudrā lineage and an important early statement of the sahaja and Hevajra completion-stage teachings.

SA_T02_n2918u.title: Tattvaratnāvalī
SA_T02_n2918u.author: Advayavajra (Maitrīpa / Maitreyanātha, c. 1007-1085 CE)
SA_T02_n2918u.summary: The 'Jeweled Garland of Reality,' one of the earliest and most influential works of Buddhist siddhānta (tenet) literature, surveying and ranking the Indian Buddhist philosophical schools — Vaibhāṣika, Sautrāntika, Yogācāra and Madhyamaka — and situating tantric Mantranaya within them. It records Advayavajra's view of 11th-century Buddhist philosophy.
SA_T02_n2918u.history: Composed by Advayavajra (Maitrīpa), an eleventh-century scholar-siddha whose works are gathered in the Advayavajrasaṃgraha; the text has been critically re-edited by Torsten Gerloff. It is a key source for the formation of the doxographic genre that classified Buddhist tenets.

SA_T02_n2932u.title: Adhyātmasāraśataka [Adhyātmasāraśatakasaṭīka]
SA_T02_n2932u.genre: stotra / didactic śataka (century of verses) with commentary (ṭīkā)
SA_T02_n2932u.date_estimate: uncertain; likely medieval to early-modern (no firm dating)
SA_T02_n2932u.summary: A 'śataka' (collection of roughly one hundred verses) on the 'essence of the inner self/spirit' (adhyātma-sāra), i.e. devotional and contemplative reflection on the soul (ātman) and the path to liberation. The transmitted version is accompanied by a commentary (sa-ṭīkā). The verses are didactic and meditative in character rather than narrative.
SA_T02_n2932u.history: The title is generic in the Sanskrit śataka tradition and no firmly attested single author or date could be confirmed; it is preserved in manuscript collections together with its commentary. Not to be confused with the better-known Adhyātma Rāmāyaṇa or Adhyātma Upaniṣad.

SA_T02_n2934u.title: Abhayapaddhati
SA_T02_n2934u.author: Abhayākaragupta
SA_T02_n2934u.date_estimate: c. early 12th c. CE (Abhayākaragupta fl. late 11th–early 12th c., d. 1125)
SA_T02_n2934u.summary: A commentary (paddhati) by the great Bengali master Abhayākaragupta on the Buddhakapālamahātantra, a scripture of the yoginītantra class. It explicates the tantra's maṇḍala rituals, including the 'generation of Heruka and his maṇḍala', and is one of only a small number of direct commentaries Abhayākaragupta wrote on a root tantra.
SA_T02_n2934u.history: Abhayākaragupta of Vikramaśīla was one of the last great systematizers of Indian tantric Buddhism. The Abhayapaddhati survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan; chapters 9–14 were critically edited and translated by Luo Hong (Beijing–Hamburg, 2010), and the Buddhakapālatantra with this commentary has been studied at the University of Hamburg's Centre for Tantric Studies.

SA_T02_n2946u.title: Paribhāṣāvṛtti
SA_T02_n2946u.date_estimate: disputed; the title is borne by several works (Vyāḍi's, traditionally very early; Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita's, 17th c.; and others)
SA_T02_n2946u.summary: A 'vṛtti' (gloss/commentary) on the paribhāṣās, the interpretive metarules that govern the application of Pāṇini's grammar of Sanskrit. Such works collect and explain the principles by which conflicting or hierarchically ordered rules of the Aṣṭādhyāyī are to be applied.
SA_T02_n2946u.history: Several distinct Paribhāṣāvṛttis exist (e.g. Vyāḍi's Vyāḍīyaparibhāṣāvṛtti, edited by Wujastyk; and the 17th-century work of Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita). Without an author attribution the specific text here cannot be pinned to one of these traditions, so identification is uncertain.

SA_T02_n2952u.title: Bauddhadharme tathā purāṇeṣu karuṇā
SA_T02_n2952u.date_estimate: modern (likely 20th–21st c.); the title is post-classical scholarly Sanskrit/Hindi
SA_T02_n2952u.summary: The title means 'Compassion (karuṇā) in the Buddhist dharma and in the Purāṇas', i.e. a comparative study of the concept of compassion as developed in Buddhist doctrine versus in the Hindu Purāṇic corpus. It appears to be a modern academic work rather than a classical text.
SA_T02_n2952u.history: This is almost certainly a modern monograph or thesis published within a Nepalese/Indian Buddhist Sanskrit corpus; no classical author or date applies. Identification of a specific edition could not be confirmed.

SA_T02_n2955u.title: Kriyāsaṁgrahapañjikā
SA_T02_n2955u.author: Kuladatta
SA_T02_n2955u.genre: pañjikā — ritual manual / commentary on ritual
SA_T02_n2955u.summary: Kuladatta's commentarial ritual manual (pañjikā) on the Kriyāsaṁgraha, dealing with tantric Buddhist rites including the construction and consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of monasteries and images. It instructs the ācārya in visualizing a building site as the Vajradhātumaṇḍala and prescribes the 'ten rites' (daśakriyā) of consecration.
SA_T02_n2955u.history: The work draws on and parallels Śaiva pratiṣṭhātantra material and remains foundational to contemporary Newar Buddhist ritual in Nepal. It has been studied by Tanemura and others; manuscripts survive in Nepalese and Cambridge collections.

SA_T02_n2961u.title: Tattvajñānasaṁsiddhi-mahāsukharakāśikā-ṭīkā
SA_T02_n2961u.genre: ṭīkā — commentary on a tantric siddhi-text
SA_T02_n2961u.date_estimate: uncertain; likely c. 10th–12th c. CE (mature Indian tantric period)
SA_T02_n2961u.summary: A commentary (ṭīkā) connected with the Tattvajñānasaṁsiddhi, a tantric work on the 'accomplishment of the knowledge of reality (tattva-jñāna)', here associated with the theme of 'great bliss' (mahāsukha) central to the yoginī/anuttarayoga traditions. It expounds the realization of ultimate reality understood as innate bliss.
SA_T02_n2961u.history: The Tattvajñānasaṁsiddhi belongs to the corpus of 'siddhi' texts of the mahāsiddha milieu (cf. works of Dombi-Heruka and the seven-siddhi cycle); the precise authorship of this ṭīkā could not be confirmed, hence the cautious dating.

SA_T02_n2963u.title: Āryamāyājālamahātantroddhṛtamaṇḍalagāthāṭippaṇī
SA_T02_n2963u.genre: ṭippaṇī — short gloss/annotation on maṇḍala verses
SA_T02_n2963u.date_estimate: uncertain; the root Māyājālatantra is a late tantra (c. 8th c. or later), so the gloss is later
SA_T02_n2963u.summary: A short annotation (ṭippaṇī) on the 'maṇḍala-gāthā' (verses on the maṇḍala) extracted (uddhṛta) from the noble Māyājālamahātantra, the 'Tantra of the Magical Net'. The root tantra is the source of the famous Nāmasaṃgīti and is associated with esoteric forms of Mañjuśrī; this gloss explicates the maṇḍala verses drawn from it.
SA_T02_n2963u.history: The Māyājāla cycle was influential in Tibet and especially Nepal (where multi-armed Mañjuśrī forms 'after the Māyājāla tantra' are widely depicted). The author of this particular ṭippaṇī could not be identified.

SA_T02_n2965u.title: Kālacakrāvatāra
SA_T02_n2965u.genre: introductory treatise (avatāra) to a tantric system
SA_T02_n2965u.date_estimate: c. 11th–12th c. CE (Kālacakra emerged in India early 11th c.)
SA_T02_n2965u.summary: An 'introduction/entry' (avatāra) to the Kālacakra ('Wheel of Time') tantric system, the last major Buddhist tantra to develop in India. Such a work serves as a primer to the Kālacakra's cosmology, astronomy/calendar, subtle-body physiology and soteriology, oriented toward the practitioner entering the tradition.
SA_T02_n2965u.history: The Kālacakra tradition traces itself to a teaching given at Dhānyakaṭaka and to the abridged Laghukālacakratantra attributed to king Mañjuśrī Yaśas of Śambhala. Numerous introductory and commentarial works (avatāras) cluster around it; the specific author of this Kālacakrāvatāra was not confirmed.

SA_T02_n2974u.title: Munimatālaṁkāra
SA_T02_n2974u.author: Abhayākaragupta
SA_T02_n2974u.summary: An 'Ornament of the Sage's Doctrine' — an encyclopedic exposition by Abhayākaragupta of the entire system of non-tantric Buddhist (Mahāyāna) doctrine and practice, synthesizing Prajñāpāramitā, Madhyamaka and Yogācāra. It treats topics such as the skandhas, dhātus and āyatanas, the justification of the Mahāyāna, and the path, drawing on Candrakīrti and Kamalaśīla.
SA_T02_n2974u.history: Composed around 1113 CE, it is one of the last great comprehensive treatises of Indian Buddhism; widely studied in Tibet until the 14th century. Long known only in Tibetan, Sanskrit manuscript portions reported by Li Xuezhu are now being critically edited (Kano, Li, and others).

SA_T02_n2982u.title: Mañjuvajramukhyākhyāna
SA_T02_n2982u.date_estimate: uncertain; Guhyasamāja Mañjuvajra tradition c. 9th–12th c. CE
SA_T02_n2982u.summary: A 'principal exposition' (mukhya-ākhyāna) concerning Mañjuvajra ('Gentle Vajra'), the esoteric form of Mañjuśrī who is the central deity of the Mañjuvajra maṇḍala of the Guhyasamāja tantra (an aspect of Akṣobhya, with three faces and six arms in union with his consort). The text expounds this deity and his nineteen-deity maṇḍala / its practice.
SA_T02_n2982u.history: The Guhyasamāja–Mañjuvajra cycle (Tibetan 'Jam-rdor) is one of the principal forms of Guhyasamāja practice, with its own lineages of root tantra, initiation and sādhana. The specific author/date of this particular ākhyāna could not be confirmed.

SA_T02_n2984u.title: Caryāsaṃgraha
SA_T02_n2984u.date_estimate: uncertain; mature Indian tantric period (c. 9th–12th c. CE)
SA_T02_n2984u.summary: A 'collection/compendium of practices' (caryā), i.e. a digest of tantric Buddhist conduct and yogic practice. Works of this type assemble rites, observances and yogic disciplines (caryā) of the anuttarayoga traditions for the practitioner.
SA_T02_n2984u.history: The title is generic and several caryā-collections circulate in the Indo-Tibetan tantric corpus (associated with the mahāsiddha and Six-Dharmas-of-Nāropa milieu of yogic practice). No single author/date could be confidently fixed for this entry.

SA_T02_n2988u.title: Jñānasiddhi
SA_T02_n2988u.author: Indrabhūti
SA_T02_n2988u.summary: Indrabhūti's 'Accomplishment of Gnosis' — a foundational tantric treatise on the achievement (siddhi) of jñāna, the awakened cognition (gnosis) of a Buddha. It cites and comments on key tantric scriptures, treating awakened gnosis and the great-bliss soteriology of esoteric Buddhism.
SA_T02_n2988u.history: One of the celebrated 'seven siddhi texts', traditionally composed in Uḍḍiyāna by the king-adept Indrabhūti; it acknowledges the Guhyasamāja as supreme authority and summarizes parts of it. Recently re-edited (critical Sanskrit edition with Tibetan and English translation).

SA_T02_n2998u.title: Laghutantraṭīkā
SA_T02_n2998u.author: Vajrapāṇi
SA_T02_n2998u.summary: A commentary by the Bodhisattva-master Vajrapāṇi on the first chapter of the Cakrasaṃvaratantra (the 'Laghusaṃvara' / 'Laghutantra'), interpreting the root verses through the doctrines of both the Cakrasaṃvara and the Kālacakra systems. It is notable for reading the Cakrasaṃvara through an emergent Kālacakra lens.
SA_T02_n2998u.history: Datable to around the turn of the 11th century, when the Kālacakra was assuming its earliest form, making it valuable for dating the Kālacakra's emergence. Critically edited by Claudio Cicuzza (Serie Orientale Roma).

SA_T02_n3006u.title: Boudhatantrokta Siddhauṣadhi Saṅgraha
SA_T02_n3006u.date_estimate: modern compilation (likely 20th–21st c.); draws on older tantric material
SA_T02_n3006u.summary: Title means roughly 'A compilation of efficacious/accomplished medicines (siddha-auṣadhi) as stated in the Buddhist tantras'. It appears to be a collected handbook of medicinal and ritual-pharmacological recipes (auṣadhi) drawn from tantric Buddhist sources, of the practical/medical-tantric genre.
SA_T02_n3006u.history: This looks like a modern Nepalese/Indian compilation gathering tantric pharmacological lore rather than a single classical work; no classical author or firm date could be confirmed.

SA_T02_n3011u.title: Laghukaumudīvyākaraṇa
SA_T02_n3011u.author: Varadarāja
SA_T02_n3011u.summary: The Laghu(siddhānta)kaumudī of Varadarāja, an abridged Sanskrit grammar that condenses Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī for beginners. It reorders Pāṇini's sūtras topically (saṃjñā, sandhi, declension, etc.) and reduces them to roughly a third (~1270 sūtras), serving as the standard primer of Pāṇinian grammar.
SA_T02_n3011u.history: Varadarāja, a pupil of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita, produced three abridgements (madhya, laghu, sāra) of his teacher's Siddhāntakaumudī. The Laghukaumudī became the most widely used introductory grammar; J. R. Ballantyne's 1849 translation introduced it to Western indology.

SA_T02_n691u.title: Cakrasaṃvaratantra Vol. 1
SA_T02_n691u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n691u.date_estimate: c. late 8th–early 9th c. CE (terminus ante quem late 10th c.)
SA_T02_n691u.summary: Volume 1 of the Cakrasaṃvaratantra (also Laghusaṃvara / Śrīherukābhidhāna), the 'Great King of Yoginī Tantras called the Glorious Cakrasaṃvara', centered on the wrathful deity Heruka/Cakrasaṃvara and his consort Vajravārāhī and their maṇḍala of yoginīs and ḍākinīs. It teaches the maṇḍala, mantras, deity yoga and antinomian yogic practices of one of the most important anuttarayoga traditions.
SA_T02_n691u.history: One of the earliest and most influential yoginī tantras (alongside the Hevajra), dated by David B. Gray to the late 8th/early 9th century. It generated an enormous body of Indian and Tibetan commentary and ritual; Gray produced a study and annotated translation (2007).

SA_T02_n692u.title: Cakrasaṃvaratantra Vol. 2
SA_T02_n692u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_n692u.date_estimate: c. late 8th–early 9th c. CE (terminus ante quem late 10th c.)
SA_T02_n692u.summary: The second volume of the Cakrasaṃvaratantra (Laghusaṃvara / Śrīherukābhidhāna), continuing the root scripture of the Cakrasaṃvara cycle on Heruka, Vajravārāhī and the yoginī maṇḍala, mantras and yogic practice. As with Vol. 1 it belongs to the yoginītantra class of anuttarayoga Buddhism.
SA_T02_n692u.history: A two-volume presentation of a single root tantra; see Vol. 1. The tantra is foundational to one of the major Indian tantric Buddhist traditions and spawned extensive commentarial literature.

SA_T02_n798u.title: Ḍākinījālasaṃvararahasya
SA_T02_n798u.date_estimate: uncertain; mature Indian tantric period (c. 9th–12th c. CE)
SA_T02_n798u.summary: A text whose title means roughly 'The Secret (rahasya) of the Saṃvara of the Net of Ḍākinīs (ḍākinī-jāla)', belonging to the ḍākinī/yoginī-tantra milieu of esoteric Buddhism that centers on the network ('net') of ḍākinīs and the saṃvara (union/vow) practices associated with Heruka-class deities. It treats the secret maṇḍala, mantras and yogic practices of these female deities.
SA_T02_n798u.history: Related in name and content to the Saṃvara/Cakrasaṃvara and Ḍākinī cycles; the specific author and dating could not be confirmed from available sources.

SA_T02_n800u.title: Sekoddeśapañjikā
SA_T02_n800u.genre: pañjikā — commentary on a tantric text
SA_T02_n800u.summary: A commentary (pañjikā) on the Sekoddeśa, the 'Summary Statement on Initiation/Consecration (seka)', which is the section of the Kālacakra (Paramādibuddha) tantra dealing with the abhiṣeka initiations. The pañjikā explicates the system of empowerments that form the entry into Kālacakra practice.
SA_T02_n800u.history: The Sekoddeśa is the earliest surviving text of the Kālacakra tradition and contains the nucleus of its doctrine; it attracted famous commentaries, most notably Nāḍapāda's (Nāropa's) Sekoddeśaṭīkā (ed. Carelli 1941). This 'pañjikā' is a related commentary on the same Sekoddeśa, whose exact author could not be definitively confirmed here.

SA_T02_nagbhc_u.title: Bodhicittavivaraṇa
SA_T02_nagbhc_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T02_nagbhc_u.date_estimate: attributed to Nāgārjuna; if by the Madhyamaka founder, c. 2nd–3rd c. CE (authorship/date debated)
SA_T02_nagbhc_u.summary: The 'Commentary on the Awakening Mind' — a work of ~112 verses (with a prose preface) cultivating bodhicitta in two aspects: conventional bodhicitta (compassionate aspiration) and ultimate bodhicitta (direct realization of emptiness, śūnyatā). It grounds compassion in Madhyamaka analysis, refuting Vijñānavāda idealism, the tīrthika self, and the śrāvaka view of the skandhas.
SA_T02_nagbhc_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna and transmitted in the tantra section of the Tengyur (with a frame relating it to the Guhyasamāja), though its tantric classification is debated and some scholars question the attribution. It was cited as authoritative by Bhāviveka, Asvabhāva and Śāntarakṣita and remains a key text on bodhicitta.

SA_T02_nagpancu.title: Pañcakrama
SA_T02_nagpancu.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (the tantric/Ārya Nāgārjuna)
SA_T02_nagpancu.tradition: Buddhist (Vajrayana; Ārya school of the Guhyasamāja, Mantranaya/Anuttarayoga tantra)
SA_T02_nagpancu.date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (traditionally ascribed to the 2nd-c. Nāgārjuna, but the tantric author is a much later figure)
SA_T02_nagpancu.summary: The Pañcakrama ('Five Stages') is the foundational text of the Ārya school's interpretation of the completion stage (niṣpannakrama/utpannakrama) of the Guhyasamāja Tantra. It sets out the advanced yogic stages that lead to buddhahood: speech isolation (vajrajāpa), mind isolation (cittaviśuddhi), the illusory body (māyādeha), clear light (prabhāsvara), and union (yuganaddha). It treats the manipulation of the subtle body, winds (prāṇa) and mind to realize the union of ultimate and conventional truth.
SA_T02_nagpancu.history: Traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna, who is said to have received the Guhyasamāja from Saraha; modern scholarship dates the tantric Nāgārjuna and this corpus to roughly the 9th-10th century. Together with the Piṇḍīkramasādhana it became the authoritative basis of the Ārya tradition, hugely influential through Candrakīrti, Nāropā and later Tsongkhapa, who wrote 'A Lamp to Illuminate the Five Stages'.

SA_T02_nagpindu.title: Piṇḍīkrama
SA_T02_nagpindu.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (tantric/Ārya Nāgārjuna)
SA_T02_nagpindu.tradition: Buddhist (Vajrayana; Ārya school of the Guhyasamāja, Anuttarayoga tantra)
SA_T02_nagpindu.date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna)
SA_T02_nagpindu.summary: The Piṇḍīkrama (also Piṇḍīkṛta-sādhana / Piṇḍīkramasādhana) is the Ārya school's principal manual for the generation stage (utpattikrama) of Guhyasamāja practice. It gives instructions for visualizing the Guhyasamāja maṇḍala, generating oneself as the deities, and performing their ritual activities, opening with the statement that the goal of the generation stage is to purify the mind of conceptualizations (vikalpa), the source of delusion.
SA_T02_nagpindu.history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna and transmitted as the 'first krama' of the Pañcakrama, though now generally regarded as a separate work composed around the 9th-10th century. With the Pañcakrama it forms the twin foundation of the Ārya exegesis of the Guhyasamāja; studied by R. Wright and others.

SA_T02_nitkarpu.title: Nityakarmapūjāvidhi
SA_T02_nitkarpu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_nitkarpu.genre: ritual manual (pūjāvidhi / liturgy for daily obligatory worship)
SA_T02_nitkarpu.summary: A ritual manual prescribing the procedure (vidhi) for nityakarma, the daily obligatory acts of worship (pūjā), apparently within a Tantric Buddhist (probably Newar Buddhist) liturgical setting. Such texts lay out the prescribed sequence of offerings, mantras, mudrās and visualizations for the practitioner's required daily devotions.
SA_T02_nitkarpu.history: The title is generic (nitya 'daily/obligatory' + karma 'act' + pūjāvidhi 'rite of worship'); many such liturgical handbooks circulated, especially in Nepalese Buddhist tradition, and exact authorship and date are usually unrecorded. Identity of this particular witness is not securely established.

SA_T02_psarkdhu.title: Pratisarākalpadhāraṇī
SA_T02_psarkdhu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_psarkdhu.genre: dhāraṇī (protective incantation text) with kalpa (ritual prescription)
SA_T02_psarkdhu.date_estimate: core dhāraṇī by c. 6th c. CE; ritual material developed thereafter (incorporated into the Pañcarakṣā set later)
SA_T02_psarkdhu.summary: A text of the Mahāpratisarā dhāraṇī together with its kalpa (ritual instructions). The Mahāpratisarā ('Great Amulet / She Who Grants Wishes When Worn') dhāraṇī protects against sin, disease and evils and is used to create protective, wish-fulfilling amulets by writing down the incantation and performing prescribed rites. It is the chief of the Pañcarakṣā ('Five Protections') set of apotropaic spells and goddesses.
SA_T02_psarkdhu.history: The Mahāpratisarā dhāraṇī-sūtra was composed probably no later than the 6th century and is an early work of the dhāraṇī genre, attested in Tibetan records by c. 800 CE. It was later incorporated into the Pañcarakṣā collection; the deity Mahāpratisarā is depicted surrounded by the four other protector goddesses.

SA_T02_rabhramu.title: Bhramaharanāma Hevajrasādhana
SA_T02_rabhramu.author: Ratnākaraśānti (Śāntipā)
SA_T02_rabhramu.tradition: Buddhist (Vajrayana; Hevajra cycle; the author also a Yogācāra/Prajñāpāramitā master)
SA_T02_rabhramu.summary: The Bhramahara ('Remover of Error') is a comprehensive Hevajra sādhana, surviving in its original Sanskrit. It gives the generation-stage (utpattikrama) practice of Hevajra: the visualizations, ritual procedures and verses for self-generation as the deity within the Hevajra maṇḍala. The work forms the opening section of a larger sādhana corpus by its author.
SA_T02_rabhramu.history: By Ratnākaraśānti (Śāntipā), one of the most brilliant Indian masters of Tantric Buddhism (early 11th c.), associated with Vikramaśīla. A critical edition was published in 2002 (JICABS) and the text has been studied by Harunaga Isaacson. Ratnākaraśānti also wrote major works in Yogācāra and Prajñāpāramitā (e.g. the Sāratamā).

SA_T02_sunytjsu.title: Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi
SA_T02_sunytjsu.author: attributed to Ḍombīheruka (attribution debated)
SA_T02_sunytjsu.summary: The Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi ('Establishing the Knowledge of Reality') is a Tantric Buddhist treatise on the realization of the true nature of reality (tattvajñāna), expounding the view of emptiness (śūnyatā) within a Vajrayana framework. The internal work-id stem (śūnyatā) and title indicate a focus on establishing non-conceptual gnosis of ultimate reality as the ground of tantric practice.
SA_T02_sunytjsu.history: Identity and authorship are not firmly settled; the work circulates among the tantric siddha literature and has been ascribed (with debate) to figures such as Ḍombīheruka. Dating is correspondingly uncertain within the later Indian tantric period.

SA_T02_vadhdhdu.title: Vasudhārādhāraṇī 1 [Vasudhārādhāraṇīsūtra]
SA_T02_vadhdhdu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_vadhdhdu.tradition: Buddhist (Mahayana/Vajrayana; especially Newar Buddhist; dhāraṇī literature)
SA_T02_vadhdhdu.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE; popular from at least the 11th c. CE
SA_T02_vadhdhdu.summary: A recension of the Noble Vasudhārā Dhāraṇī, teaching the dhāraṇī of Vasudhārā ('Stream of Gems'), the bodhisattva-goddess of wealth, prosperity, abundance and fertility. Reciting the dhāraṇī with pure intention and generosity (dāna) is said to grant prosperity and avert harmful spirits, demons and disease.
SA_T02_vadhdhdu.history: A canonical and very popular dhāraṇī (cf. Toh 662), preserved in multiple Sanskrit and Tibetan forms; especially venerated in the Newar Buddhist tradition of the Kathmandu Valley since at least the 11th century. The framing narrative is the inquiry of the layman Sucandra.

SA_T02_vadhdhgu.title: Vasudhārādhāraṇī 2 [Sucandragṛhapatiparipṛcchā/Sucandrāvadāna]
SA_T02_vadhdhgu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_vadhdhgu.genre: dhāraṇī sūtra in narrative (paripṛcchā / avadāna) frame
SA_T02_vadhdhgu.summary: A recension of the Vasudhārā Dhāraṇī in its narrative form, 'The Inquiry of the Householder Sucandra' (Sucandragṛhapatiparipṛcchā) / Sucandrāvadāna. An impoverished layman, Sucandra, approaches the Buddha asking how to obtain gold, grain and gems to feed his large family and practice charity; the Buddha teaches him the Vasudhārā dhāraṇī, and through faithful recitation and generosity his storehouses overflow with wealth.
SA_T02_vadhdhgu.history: This question-and-answer frame is the canonical origin story for the Vasudhārā cult; the dhāraṇī's efficacy is tied to ethical conduct and generous giving (dāna). The text is transmitted in Sanskrit and Tibetan and underlies the widespread Newar Buddhist worship of Vasudhārā.

SA_T02_vadhdhju.title: Vasudhārādhāraṇī 3 [Vasudhārādhāraṇīsūtra]
SA_T02_vadhdhju.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T02_vadhdhju.tradition: Buddhist (Mahayana/Vajrayana; especially Newar Buddhist; dhāraṇī literature)
SA_T02_vadhdhju.summary: A further recension of the Vasudhārā Dhāraṇī Sūtra, transmitting the dhāraṇī of the wealth-goddess Vasudhārā. As with the other recensions, the text teaches the incantation whose recitation, joined to ethical conduct and giving, is held to bestow prosperity and abundance while warding off disease and malevolent spirits.
SA_T02_vadhdhju.history: One of several parallel Sanskrit witnesses of the popular Vasudhārā dhāraṇī (cf. Toh 662); such multiple recensions reflect its wide ritual use, particularly in Nepalese/Newar Buddhism.

SA_T02_vamvuppu.title: Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa
SA_T02_vamvuppu.author: Vāgīśvarakīrti
SA_T02_vamvuppu.genre: tantric instructional treatise (upadeśa on cheating death / longevity)
SA_T02_vamvuppu.date_estimate: late 10th-early 11th c. CE (Tibetan translation completed c. 1042/3 CE)
SA_T02_vamvuppu.summary: The Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa ('Instruction on Cheating Death') is Vāgīśvarakīrti's major work. It gives an exhaustive description of the omens and signs of impending death, classified as external (bāhya) and internal (abhyantara), followed by a compendium of religious and ritual practices to escape or postpone death. To achieve longevity the White Tārā is especially invoked.
SA_T02_vamvuppu.history: Vāgīśvarakīrti was an Indian Tantric master of the 10th/11th century, reckoned among the 'six doorkeepers' of Vikramaśīla monastery. The Tibetan translation was completed c. 1042/3 by Atiśa (Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna) and Rin chen bzang po at Tho ling. Edited and studied by Johannes Schneider.

SA_T03_abhisamu.title: Abhisamayālaṅkāranāmaprajñāpāramitopadeśaśāstra
SA_T03_abhisamu.author: attributed to Maitreyanātha (Tibetan tradition: revealed by Maitreya to Asaṅga)
SA_T03_abhisamu.genre: śāstra (verse treatise / instructional summary of Prajñāpāramitā)
SA_T03_abhisamu.date_estimate: c. 4th c. CE (traditionally; author Maitreyanātha c. 350 CE)
SA_T03_abhisamu.summary: This is the full-titled Abhisamayālaṅkāra, 'Ornament for Clear Realization: A Treatise of Instructions on the Perfection of Wisdom.' In 273 verses across eight chapters plus a summary, it lays out the hidden path-structure (the eight abhisamayas / clear realizations and seventy topics) implicit in the Prajñāpāramitā sūtras, summarizing especially the 25,000-line Perfection of Wisdom and charting the stages, practices and result leading to omniscient buddhahood.
SA_T03_abhisamu.history: Traditionally ascribed to Maitreyanātha (in Tibet, revealed by the bodhisattva Maitreya to Asaṅga in NW India c. 4th c.). It is the central scholastic root-text of Prajñāpāramitā study and generated a vast Indian and Tibetan commentarial tradition (Ārya Vimuktisena, Haribhadra, etc.).

SA_T03_bsa011_u.title: Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha
SA_T03_bsa011_u.author: Dignāga
SA_T03_bsa011_u.date_estimate: c. early-mid 6th c. CE (Dignāga, c. 480-540 CE)
SA_T03_bsa011_u.summary: The Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha ('Condensed Meaning of the Perfection of Wisdom'), also called Prajñāpāramitāsaṃgrahakārikā, condenses the Aṣṭasāhasrikā (Eight Thousand Line) Prajñāpāramitā into a structured set of about thirty-two principal points/themes. Dignāga frames the sūtra's teaching through Yogācāra categories (e.g. the antidotes to distracted conceptions, the three natures), reading it via Madhyāntavibhāga and Mahāyānasaṃgraha.
SA_T03_bsa011_u.history: By Dignāga (c. 480-540 CE), the founder of the Buddhist epistemological (pramāṇa) tradition. The work is an early example of systematizing Prajñāpāramitā through Yogācāra doctrine and was influential in later commentarial scholasticism.

SA_T03_bsa026_u.title: Abhisamayālaṅkāra
SA_T03_bsa026_u.author: attributed to Maitreyanātha (Tibetan tradition: Maitreya, redacted by Asaṅga)
SA_T03_bsa026_u.summary: The Abhisamayālaṅkāra ('Ornament for Clear Realization'), the root verse-text (273 kārikās in eight chapters plus summary) that systematizes the path-structure latent in the Prajñāpāramitā sūtras through eight clear realizations (abhisamaya) and seventy topics. This is the standalone mūla text; entry T03abhisamu carries its full long title.
SA_T03_bsa026_u.history: One of the 'Five Treatises of Maitreya' in Tibetan tradition, ascribed to Maitreyanātha (c. 350 CE). It is the central scholastic basis of Prajñāpāramitā study in Indo-Tibetan Buddhism and spawned twenty-one classical Indian commentaries.

SA_T03_bsa031_u.title: Abhisamayālaṅkāravṛttiḥ sphuṭārthā
SA_T03_bsa031_u.author: Haribhadra
SA_T03_bsa031_u.summary: The Sphuṭārthā ('Clear Meaning') is Haribhadra's concise commentary (vṛtti) on Maitreya's Abhisamayālaṅkāra, in effect an abridgement of his own large commentary the Abhisamayālaṅkārāloka. It explains the eight subjects and seventy topics of the root text and is the most celebrated and widely studied commentary on the Abhisamayālaṅkāra.
SA_T03_bsa031_u.history: By Haribhadra (late 8th c.), the most influential of all Abhisamayālaṅkāra commentators. The Sphuṭārthā became foundational in Tibet after rngog Blo ldan shes rab's translation and summary, launching a Prajñāpāramitā exegetical tradition that spread from gSang phu monastery into all four Tibetan schools.

SA_T03_bsa033_u.title: Abhisamayālaṅkārāntaḥpātināṃ (saṃketānāṃ nāmāni)
SA_T03_bsa033_u.author: anonymous/traditional (scholastic glossary)
SA_T03_bsa033_u.genre: scholastic glossary / nomenclature list (nāmasaṅgati of technical terms)
SA_T03_bsa033_u.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval Indian Buddhist scholasticism (1st-2nd millennium CE)
SA_T03_bsa033_u.summary: A list/glossary giving the names of the technical categories (saṃketa) 'contained within' (antaḥpātin) the Abhisamayālaṅkāra, i.e. an enumeration of the standardized terms, topics and sub-points (the eight subjects and seventy topics and their components) of the root treatise. It functions as a study aid and index of the Abhisamayālaṅkāra's nomenclature rather than a discursive commentary.
SA_T03_bsa033_u.history: Such terminological lists were compiled within the Abhisamayālaṅkāra commentarial tradition as mnemonic and pedagogical tools; authorship and precise date for this particular witness are not securely established.

SA_T03_bsa050_u.title: Pāramitāsamāsa
SA_T03_bsa050_u.author: Āryaśūra
SA_T03_bsa050_u.genre: kāvya-style doctrinal poem (compendium of the perfections)
SA_T03_bsa050_u.date_estimate: uncertain; c. 4th c. CE if by the author of the Jātakamālā (debated)
SA_T03_bsa050_u.summary: The Pāramitāsamāsa ('Compendium / Summary of the Perfections') is a poem expounding the six perfections (pāramitās) cultivated by a bodhisattva on the path to buddhahood, presenting them from the standpoint of emptiness (śūnyavāda / Madhyamaka). It treats generosity, morality, patience, vigor, meditation and wisdom.
SA_T03_bsa050_u.history: Attributed to Āryaśūra (traditionally identified with the author of the Jātakamālā, c. 4th c., though the attribution and date of this work are debated). Edited, translated and analyzed by Carol Meadows (Indica et Tibetica, 1986).

SA_T03_bsa051_u.title: Sāratamā [āryāṣṭasāhasrikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ sāratamākhyā pañjikā]
SA_T03_bsa051_u.author: Ratnākaraśānti (Śāntipā)
SA_T03_bsa051_u.summary: The Sāratamā ('The Very Best / Most Essential') is Ratnākaraśānti's pañjikā on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā ('Perfection of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines'), interpreting it along Yogācāra lines as a theory of transcendental knowledge. It is one of only two commentaries on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā surviving in the original Sanskrit, the other being Haribhadra's Āloka.
SA_T03_bsa051_u.history: By Ratnākaraśānti (11th c.), a leading Vikramaśīla master. Edited by P. S. Jaini (1979, Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series, Patna) from incomplete manuscripts photographed by Rāhula Sāṃkṛtyāyana in 1937; compared with Haribhadra's Āloka in scholarship (e.g. SOAS Bulletin).

SA_T03_kamvchtu.title: Prajñāpāramitāvajracchedikāṭīkā
SA_T03_kamvchtu.author: Kamalaśīla
SA_T03_kamvchtu.genre: ṭīkā (commentary on the Vajracchedikā / Diamond Sūtra)
SA_T03_kamvchtu.summary: A ṭīkā (commentary) by Kamalaśīla on the Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra (the 'Diamond Sūtra'), the famous short Perfection-of-Wisdom scripture that teaches the cutting through of conceptual clinging to self, dharmas and signs. The commentary expounds the sūtra's dialectic of non-apprehension and emptiness.
SA_T03_kamvchtu.history: By Kamalaśīla (late 8th c.), the disciple of Śāntarakṣita known for the Bhāvanākrama and for representing the gradualist position in the Tibetan 'bSam yas debate.' His Diamond Sūtra commentary survives chiefly through the Tibetan canon.

SA_T03_knavslou.title: Navaślokī
SA_T03_knavslou.author: Kambala (Kambalapāda)
SA_T03_knavslou.summary: The Navaślokī ('[Text of] Nine Verses') is a short Prajñāpāramitā work by Kambala, comprising nine ślokas (with an accompanying auto-commentary) that condense the essential meaning of the Perfection of Wisdom. It is closely associated with the same author's Ālokamālā.
SA_T03_knavslou.history: Attributed to Kambala (Kambalapāda); his identity and date are debated, with proposals ranging from the late 8th to early 11th century. The Navaślokī was translated into Chinese by Dharmapāla in the early 11th century; a Sanskrit witness survives in a Göttingen manuscript (Cod.ms.sanscr.259).

SA_T03_n1146u.title: Abhisamayālaṃkārāloka [Abhisamayālaṅkārāloka]
SA_T03_n1146u.author: Haribhadra
SA_T03_n1146u.genre: commentary (vyākhyā) correlating the Abhisamayālaṅkāra with the Aṣṭasāhasrikā
SA_T03_n1146u.summary: The Abhisamayālaṅkārāloka ('Light for the Ornament of Clear Realization'), full title Abhisamayālaṅkārālokā Prajñāpāramitāvyākhyā, is Haribhadra's large commentary that correlates the 273 verses of Maitreya's Abhisamayālaṅkāra with the corresponding passages of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā, providing a verse-by-verse and section-by-section synthesis of root-treatise and sūtra.
SA_T03_n1146u.history: Composed by Haribhadra (late 8th c.) under Pāla patronage; it is one of the two Aṣṭasāhasrikā commentaries surviving in Sanskrit (with Ratnākaraśānti's Sāratamā). First edited by G. Tucci (1932, Gaekwad's Oriental Series 62) and by U. Wogihara (1932-35). Its abridgement is Haribhadra's own Sphuṭārthā.

SA_T03_n864u.title: Prajñāpāramitāstuti
SA_T03_n864u.author: traditionally Rāhulabhadra
SA_T03_n864u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (attribution and date uncertain)
SA_T03_n864u.summary: A short devotional hymn in praise of the Perfection of Wisdom (Prajñāpāramitā), personified and venerated as the 'mother' of all buddhas and the source of awakening. The verses extol transcendent wisdom as ineffable, beyond conceptual grasping, and the ground of the bodhisattva path.
SA_T03_n864u.history: This Prajñāpāramitā praise is traditionally ascribed to Rāhulabhadra (an early Madhyamaka figure sometimes linked to Nāgārjuna's lineage), and such stutis were frequently appended to Prajñāpāramitā sutra manuscripts such as the Aṣṭasāhasrikā. The Sanskrit attribution and exact dating remain uncertain.

SA_T03_n865u.title: Ṣaṭpāramitāstotra
SA_T03_n865u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T03_n865u.summary: A hymn in praise of the six perfections (ṣaṭpāramitā) that constitute the bodhisattva's training: generosity (dāna), ethics (śīla), patience (kṣānti), diligence (vīrya), meditative concentration (dhyāna), and wisdom (prajñā). It celebrates each perfection devotionally as a stage of the path to buddhahood.
SA_T03_n865u.history: A minor devotional Mahayana stotra preserved among the praises (stotra/stava) collections of the Buddhist canon. Authorship is uncertain and the work is transmitted chiefly through the Tibetan Tengyur and related stotra anthologies.

SA_T04_acatstvu.title: Catuḥstavasamāsārtha [Catuḥstavasamārtha]
SA_T04_acatstvu.author: Amṛtākara
SA_T04_acatstvu.date_estimate: c. 6th-9th c. CE (author Amṛtākara of uncertain date)
SA_T04_acatstvu.summary: A short exegetical work by Amṛtākara giving the condensed or summarized meaning (samāsārtha) of the Catuḥstava, the 'Four Hymns' attributed to Nāgārjuna (including the Lokātītastava, Niraupamyastava, Acintyastava, and Paramārthastava). It clarifies the Madhyamaka import of the hymns, which praise the Buddha and ultimate reality (emptiness).
SA_T04_acatstvu.history: The text was edited and made known to scholarship by Giuseppe Tucci in his 'Minor Buddhist Texts' (1956); it is an important witness for reconstructing Nāgārjuna's Catuḥstava. Amṛtākara is otherwise little documented and his dates are uncertain.

SA_T04_bsa003_u.title: Bodhicaryāvatāra [Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra]
SA_T04_bsa003_u.author: Śāntideva
SA_T04_bsa003_u.summary: A celebrated verse work, 'Entering the Way of the Bodhisattva,' guiding the practitioner through the cultivation of the awakening mind (bodhicitta) across ten chapters, covering confession, the gift of bodhicitta, vigilance, patience, diligence, meditation, and wisdom. The ninth (wisdom) chapter presents a rigorous Madhyamaka critique of inherent existence, and the work is renowned for its emotional power and ethical depth.
SA_T04_bsa003_u.history: Composed by Śāntideva, a monk of Nālandā; he must predate 763 CE since Śāntarakṣita quotes him. The text became foundational in Tibetan Buddhism, attracting numerous Indian and Tibetan commentaries, and exists in distinct recensions (a shorter Dunhuang version and the longer canonical version).

SA_T04_bsa004_u.title: Bodhipathapradīpa
SA_T04_bsa004_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa004_u.summary: Atiśa's 'Lamp for the Path to Enlightenment,' a concise verse treatise outlining the entire graded path to buddhahood. It introduces the schema of three levels of spiritual capacity (lesser, middling, superior practitioners) and integrates the doctrines of sutra and tantra into a unified progression.
SA_T04_bsa004_u.history: Written in Tibet at the request of the king Jangchub Ö (Byang chub 'od), it is Atiśa's most influential work and became the root text of the entire Lamrim ('stages of the path') genre, including Tsongkhapa's later expansions. Atiśa composed an autocommentary (Bodhimārgapradīpapañjikā).

SA_T04_bsa008_u.title: Garbhasaṅgraha
SA_T04_bsa008_u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (traditional)
SA_T04_bsa008_u.summary: A short 'compendium concerning the womb/embryo' (garbha), a minor work transmitted in the Buddhist canon dealing with conception, gestation and the stages of embryonic development, set within a Buddhist framework of rebirth and impermanence. It relates to the broader Indic-Buddhist embryological tradition (cf. the Garbhāvakrānti-sūtra).
SA_T04_bsa008_u.history: A minor work preserved chiefly in Tibetan translation in the Tengyur, sometimes ascribed to Nāgārjuna; the attribution is traditional and uncertain. It is part of the small body of Buddhist texts on embryology that informed later Tibetan medical literature.

SA_T04_bsa009_u.title: Hastavālaprakaraṇavṛtti
SA_T04_bsa009_u.author: attributed to Āryadeva (also linked to Dignāga); disputed
SA_T04_bsa009_u.summary: The prose commentary (vṛtti) accompanying the Hastavālaprakaraṇa, a short treatise that uses the famous rope-mistaken-for-a-snake analogy to argue that things lack inherent essence (asvabhāva) when analyzed into parts. The commentary develops a whole-part critique pointing toward emptiness and, in places, consciousness-only (vijñaptimātratā).
SA_T04_bsa009_u.history: Attribution is contested: the verses and commentary are variously assigned to Āryadeva or to Dignāga, and the original Sanskrit is largely lost, the work surviving in Tibetan and Chinese. It is a notable early statement of the snake-rope analysis in Buddhist epistemology.

SA_T04_bsa012_u.title: Pratītyasamutpādahṛdayakārikā
SA_T04_bsa012_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_bsa012_u.summary: The 'Verses on the Heart of Dependent Origination,' a very short text (seven stanzas) distilling the essence of pratītyasamutpāda. It analyzes the twelve links of dependent origination, reducing them to defilements, action, and suffering, and argues there is no transmigrating self, only conditioned arising.
SA_T04_bsa012_u.history: Attributed to Nāgārjuna and preserved in the Abhidharma section of the Tengyur (Toh 4101); it survives with early Dunhuang manuscript witnesses and has been edited and studied by R.C. Jamieson. It is paired with its own prose interpretation (vyākhyāna).

SA_T04_bsa013_u.title: Pratītyasamutpādahṛdayavyākhyāna
SA_T04_bsa013_u.author: Nāgārjuna (attributed)
SA_T04_bsa013_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (attribution to Nāgārjuna traditional)
SA_T04_bsa013_u.summary: The prose 'interpretation' accompanying the Pratītyasamutpādahṛdayakārikā, explaining its seven verses on the essence of dependent origination. It expounds the reduction of the twelve links to affliction, karma and suffering, and the absence of any substantial transmigrating entity.
SA_T04_bsa013_u.history: Transmitted as the autocommentary to the Heart of Dependent Origination verses and traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna, though the attribution of the commentary is debated. It survives in Tibetan and in Dunhuang manuscript material studied alongside the kārikā.

SA_T04_bsa014_u.title: Śikṣāsamuccayakārikā
SA_T04_bsa014_u.author: Śāntideva
SA_T04_bsa014_u.summary: The set of root verses (kārikā) that form the skeleton of Śāntideva's Śikṣāsamuccaya ('Compendium of Training'). These few mnemonic stanzas summarize the bodhisattva's training in safeguarding and purifying body, possessions, and merit for the benefit of beings, around which the prose anthology of sutra quotations is organized.
SA_T04_bsa014_u.history: By Śāntideva (early 8th c.), the same author as the Bodhicaryāvatāra; the kārikās are the verse armature of the larger Śikṣāsamuccaya, an anthology preserving many otherwise lost Mahayana sutra passages.

SA_T04_bsa015_u.title: Suhṛllekha
SA_T04_bsa015_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_bsa015_u.summary: The 'Letter to a Friend,' a didactic verse epistle of about 123 stanzas addressed to a South Indian king (traditionally a Śātavāhana ruler). It presents a compact summary of Buddhist ethics and doctrine, urging the recipient toward mindfulness, the six recollections, renunciation, karma, the dangers of saṃsāra, and the path to liberation.
SA_T04_bsa015_u.history: Attributed to Nāgārjuna and traditionally said to be written for Gautamīputra Śātakarṇi; the Sanskrit original is lost but it survives in three Chinese translations and one Tibetan version, and is widely commented upon in Tibetan Buddhism.

SA_T04_bsa016_u.title: Śūnyatāsaptati
SA_T04_bsa016_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_bsa016_u.summary: The 'Seventy Verses on Emptiness,' a core Madhyamaka treatise arguing that all phenomena are empty of inherent existence (svabhāva) precisely because they are dependently arisen. It defends emptiness against objections and clarifies that conventional designations (arising, ceasing, existence) hold only nominally.
SA_T04_bsa016_u.history: One of Nāgārjuna's 'collection of reasonings' (yukti corpus), transmitted with a prose autocommentary also ascribed to Nāgārjuna; the Sanskrit is lost and the text survives mainly in Tibetan. It is a key companion to the Mūlamadhyamakakārikā.

SA_T04_bsa021_u.title: Vigrahavyāvartanī
SA_T04_bsa021_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_bsa021_u.summary: The 'Dispeller of Disputes,' a work of seventy verses with a prose autocommentary in which Nāgārjuna answers objections (largely from Nyaya logicians) against his doctrine of universal emptiness. Most famously he replies to the charge that if his own thesis is empty it cannot refute anything, arguing his statements have no inherent thesis to be self-refuting.
SA_T04_bsa021_u.history: A fundamental early Madhyamaka text and an important document of early Indian dialectics; it engages the pramāṇa (means of knowledge) theory of the Nyaya. The Sanskrit survives, and it was critically edited and translated by E. H. Johnston, A. Kunst and K. Bhattacharya.

SA_T04_bsa024_u.title: Satyadvayāvatāranāma
SA_T04_bsa024_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (Dīpaṃkara)
SA_T04_bsa024_u.summary: Atiśa's 'Entry into the Two Truths,' a short verse treatise expounding the Madhyamaka doctrine of conventional (saṃvṛti) and ultimate (paramārtha) truth. It addresses how ultimate reality is known and which Madhyamaka authorities to follow, reflecting Atiśa's Prāsaṅgika-leaning understanding.
SA_T04_bsa024_u.history: Composed by Atiśa (Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna, 982-1054), likely during his studies, the work was influential in early Tibetan and even Tangut Madhyamaka; an early Tibetan commentary on it is a key witness to the beginnings of Tibetan scholasticism (studied by J. Apple).

SA_T04_bsa027_u.title: Madhyamakaśāstra
SA_T04_bsa027_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_bsa027_u.summary: An alternate title for Nāgārjuna's foundational Mūlamadhyamakakārikā ('Root Verses on the Middle Way'), the seminal Madhyamaka treatise in roughly 450 verses over 27 chapters. Through systematic reductio arguments it shows that all phenomena (causation, motion, time, self, nirvāṇa, the Buddha) lack inherent existence and are empty, established only as dependently arisen.
SA_T04_bsa027_u.history: The cornerstone text of the Madhyamaka school, it spawned a vast commentarial tradition (Buddhapālita, Bhāviveka, Candrakīrti's Prasannapadā, and the Chinese 'Zhong lun' tradition). 'Madhyamakaśāstra' is the title under which the work, often with embedded commentary, was transmitted.

SA_T04_bsa028_u.title: Catuḥśatikā
SA_T04_bsa028_u.author: Āryadeva
SA_T04_bsa028_u.summary: The Catuḥśataka ('Four Hundred Verses'), Āryadeva's principal Madhyamaka treatise in sixteen chapters of roughly four hundred verses. The first eight chapters set out bodhisattva practice and the abandonment of misconceptions about permanence, pleasure, self and purity; the latter eight refute the metaphysical positions of rival Buddhist and non-Buddhist schools, establishing emptiness.
SA_T04_bsa028_u.history: By Āryadeva, the chief disciple of Nāgārjuna; it became a core Madhyamaka authority. Candrakīrti wrote the most important Indian commentary (Catuḥśatakaṭīkā / Bodhisattvayogācāracatuḥśatakaṭīkā), and the work was widely studied in Tibet.

SA_T04_bsa035_u.title: Bodhisattvādikarmikamārgāvatāradeśanā
SA_T04_bsa035_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa035_u.summary: A teaching by Atiśa instructing the beginner bodhisattva (ādikarmika) on how to enter the path (mārgāvatāra). It lays out the preliminary practices, conduct, and graded steps appropriate to a novice setting out on the Mahayana path to awakening.
SA_T04_bsa035_u.history: One of Atiśa's many short instructional works composed for or transmitted to Tibet and collected among his complete works; it complements his Bodhipathapradīpa within the Kadam path literature.

SA_T04_bsa036_u.title: Śaraṇagamanadeśanā
SA_T04_bsa036_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa036_u.summary: A short teaching by Atiśa on going for refuge (śaraṇagamana) to the Three Jewels (Buddha, Dharma, Saṅgha). It explains the basis, motivation, and practice of taking refuge as the entry point and foundation of the Buddhist path.
SA_T04_bsa036_u.history: A minor instructional text among Atiśa's collected works composed in connection with his teaching mission in Tibet, where refuge instruction was foundational to the Kadam tradition he inspired.

SA_T04_bsa037_u.title: Mahāyānapathasādhanasaṅgraha
SA_T04_bsa037_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa037_u.summary: A 'compendium of the means of accomplishing the Mahayana path,' summarizing the methods and practices (sādhana) by which a practitioner progresses along the Great Vehicle path to buddhahood. It gathers the essential practical instructions of the bodhisattva path.
SA_T04_bsa037_u.history: Listed among Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna's major compositions that shaped Tibetan Buddhist commentarial and path literature, transmitted within his collected works and the Kadam tradition.

SA_T04_bsa038_u.title: Sūtrārthasamuccayopadeśa
SA_T04_bsa038_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa038_u.summary: An instructional digest 'gathering the meaning of the sutras,' in which Atiśa assembles and explains essential points drawn from Mahayana scriptures as practical guidance for practitioners. It functions as a condensed scriptural anthology with the master's pith instructions (upadeśa).
SA_T04_bsa038_u.history: One of Atiśa's short works of scriptural synthesis preserved in his collected writings and the Tibetan Tengyur, part of the body of texts through which he transmitted Indian Mahayana learning to Tibet.

SA_T04_bsa039_u.title: Daśākuśalakarmapathadeśanā
SA_T04_bsa039_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa039_u.tradition: Buddhist (Mahāyāna; Bengali master active in Tibet, associated with the Bka'-gdams-pa/Lamrim tradition)
SA_T04_bsa039_u.summary: A short instructional text expounding the 'ten unwholesome paths of action' (daśa-akuśala-karmapatha): the three bodily misdeeds (killing, stealing, sexual misconduct), four of speech (lying, divisive speech, harsh speech, idle talk), and three of mind (covetousness, malice, wrong view). It presents these as the basis of negative karma to be abandoned on the Buddhist path.
SA_T04_bsa039_u.history: Attributed to Atiśa, the renowned Indian master from Vikramaśīla who came to Tibet in 1042; like his other short works it survives chiefly in Tibetan translation in the Tengyur. It belongs to the cluster of ethical and practice texts that shaped the Tibetan stages-of-the-path (Lamrim) literature.

SA_T04_bsa040_u.title: Cittotpādasaṃvaravidhikrama
SA_T04_bsa040_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa040_u.summary: A liturgical manual setting out the sequence (krama) of the rite (vidhi) for generating bodhicitta (cittotpāda) and taking the bodhisattva vow/restraint (saṃvara). It provides the procedure for the aspiring bodhisattva to formally arouse the mind of awakening and undertake the bodhisattva discipline.
SA_T04_bsa040_u.history: One of several short ritual and ethical texts by Atiśa preserved in the Tibetan Tengyur; such bodhicitta-vow liturgies were foundational to the Bka'-gdams-pa transmission of Mahāyāna practice in Tibet.

SA_T04_bsa041_u.title: Āpattideśanāvidhi
SA_T04_bsa041_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa041_u.summary: A short ritual procedure (vidhi) for the confession or disclosure (deśanā) of transgressions/faults (āpatti), i.e. the formal Buddhist practice of acknowledging and purifying ethical lapses against one's vows. It guides the practitioner through the liturgy of confessing downfalls and restoring discipline.
SA_T04_bsa041_u.history: Ascribed to Atiśa and transmitted in the Tibetan canon among his short practice texts; confession-of-faults rites of this kind were integral to the monastic and bodhisattva ethics that Atiśa promoted in Tibet.

SA_T04_bsa042_u.title: Gurukriyākrama
SA_T04_bsa042_u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna
SA_T04_bsa042_u.summary: A short manual setting out the sequence (krama) of devotional acts and ritual procedure (kriyā) directed toward the spiritual teacher (guru), i.e. the proper way of relying upon and serving one's master. It belongs to the genre of guru-devotion instructions central to Mahāyāna and tantric practice.
SA_T04_bsa042_u.history: Attributed to Atiśa and preserved among his minor works in the Tibetan Tengyur; reliance on the qualified teacher was a key theme of the Lamrim/Bka'-gdams-pa tradition he founded in Tibet.

SA_T04_bsa053_u.title: Madhyamakāvatāra
SA_T04_bsa053_u.author: Candrakīrti
SA_T04_bsa053_u.genre: philosophical treatise (verse śāstra, introduction to Madhyamaka)
SA_T04_bsa053_u.summary: A versified 'Introduction to the Middle Way' that presents Madhyamaka philosophy structured around the ten bodhisattva stages (bhūmis) corresponding to the ten perfections (pāramitās). Its sixth chapter, on the perfection of wisdom, contains Candrakīrti's celebrated refutation of intrinsic existence (svabhāva), his critique of Yogācāra mind-only and of Bhāvaviveka's autonomous-inference method, and his sevenfold analysis of the self (the 'chariot' argument).
SA_T04_bsa053_u.history: Composed by Candrakīrti as a complement and entryway to Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, the work (with its autocommentary, the Madhyamakāvatārabhāṣya) became the central textbook of Prāsaṅgika Madhyamaka in Tibet, especially in the Dge-lugs curriculum following Tsong-kha-pa. The Sanskrit of the verses largely survives; the bhāṣya is preserved mainly in Tibetan.

SA_T04_bsa054_u.title: Bodhicaryāvatārapañjikā
SA_T04_bsa054_u.author: Prajñākaramati
SA_T04_bsa054_u.tradition: Buddhist (Mahāyāna; Madhyamaka, in Candrakīrti's Prāsaṅgika lineage)
SA_T04_bsa054_u.summary: A detailed word-commentary (pañjikā) on Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra, the classic poem on the bodhisattva path. It glosses the verses chapter by chapter, expounding the cultivation of bodhicitta, the perfections, and especially the difficult ninth (wisdom) chapter, which it reads through a Prāsaṅgika-Madhyamaka lens.
SA_T04_bsa054_u.history: Prajñākaramati was a Madhyamaka master associated with Vikramaśīla; his pañjikā is the most important surviving Indian commentary on the Bodhicaryāvatāra and a principal witness to the Sanskrit text. It was edited by L. de la Vallée Poussin and later P. L. Vaidya.

SA_T04_bsa058_u.title: Bhavasaṅkrāntiṭīkā
SA_T04_bsa058_u.author: Maitreyanātha (attributed; commentator on Nāgārjuna's Bhavasaṅkrānti)
SA_T04_bsa058_u.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE; precise dating uncertain (commentary on a work ascribed to Nāgārjuna)
SA_T04_bsa058_u.summary: A word-by-word commentary (ṭīkā, Toh 3841) on the Bhavasaṅkrānti, a short verse treatise attributed to Nāgārjuna that addresses 'transmission/transmigration through existences' (bhava-saṅkrānti). The root text and commentary explain how rebirth and karmic continuity occur without any self or dharma actually passing from one life to the next, harmonizing transmigration with anātman and emptiness.
SA_T04_bsa058_u.history: The Bhavasaṅkrānti is one of three similarly titled works attributed to Nāgārjuna in the Tengyur; this commentary on it is ascribed to Maitreyanātha (here per the project metadata). The texts survive primarily in Tibetan translation and relate to the Bhavasaṅkrāntisūtra.

SA_T04_bsu060_u.title: Karmavibhaṅgopadeśa
SA_T04_bsu060_u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T04_bsu060_u.genre: upadeśa (exegetical exposition) on a karma sūtra
SA_T04_bsu060_u.summary: An exposition/instruction (upadeśa) elaborating the Karmavibhaṅga ('Exposition of Karma'), a Buddhist text framed as the Buddha's answers about why beings differ in lifespan, health, beauty, status and rebirth-destiny according to past deeds. It catalogs specific wholesome and unwholesome actions together with their ripening results, treating the mechanics of karma and rebirth in detail.
SA_T04_bsu060_u.history: The Karmavibhaṅga circulated widely in Sanskrit, Tibetan and other Asian languages; its imagery is famously depicted on the base reliefs of Borobudur in Java. The upadeśa is a commentarial layer expanding the root karma-classification material.

SA_T04_canprasu1.title: Prasannapadā [Madhyamakaśāstravṛtti] Chapter 1
SA_T04_canprasu1.author: Candrakīrti
SA_T04_canprasu1.summary: The first chapter of Candrakīrti's 'Clear Words' commentary on Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, commenting on the opening 'examination of conditions' (pratyaya-parīkṣā). It is famous for Candrakīrti's methodological defense of the Prāsaṅgika reductio method against Bhāvaviveka's use of autonomous syllogisms, and his analysis of the four conditions and the denial of arising from self, other, both, or neither.
SA_T04_canprasu1.history: Chapter 1 of the Prasannapadā is the most studied and philosophically pivotal portion, as it stages the Prāsaṅgika-Svātantrika debate; it has been edited and translated repeatedly (e.g. by Stcherbatsky, May, and others). The full Prasannapadā is the only Sanskrit commentary on the MMK to survive complete.

SA_T04_canprasu.title: Prasannapadā [Madhyamakaśāstravṛtti]
SA_T04_canprasu.author: Candrakīrti
SA_T04_canprasu.summary: Candrakīrti's 'Clear Words' (Prasannapadā), a prose commentary on the twenty-seven chapters of Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā. It expounds emptiness (śūnyatā) and dependent origination chapter by chapter and articulates the Prāsaṅgika method of refuting opponents purely through reductio (prasaṅga), critiquing Bhāvaviveka's autonomous-inference (svatantra) approach.
SA_T04_canprasu.history: The Prasannapadā is the only fully extant Sanskrit commentary on the MMK and the most influential for Tibetan Buddhism; it preserves many verses of the root text and defines the Prāsaṅgika sub-school. It was edited by Louis de la Vallée Poussin (Bibliotheca Buddhica, 1903-13).

SA_T04_ksmadhlu.title: Madhyamakāloka
SA_T04_ksmadhlu.author: Kamalaśīla
SA_T04_ksmadhlu.summary: The 'Illumination of the Middle Way', the major independent treatise of Kamalaśīla, synthesizing Madhyamaka emptiness with Yogācāra epistemology and pramāṇa-method. It argues for the lack of intrinsic nature of all phenomena using logical reasoning, treats the two truths, and is reckoned among the 'three works of the eastern Svātantrikas'.
SA_T04_ksmadhlu.history: Kamalaśīla was the direct disciple of Śāntarakṣita (and the Indian protagonist of the 'Council of Lhasa/Samyé' debate). The Madhyamakāloka complements his commentaries on Śāntarakṣita's Madhyamakālaṃkāra and Tattvasaṃgraha and survives chiefly in Tibetan translation.

SA_T04_n1002u.title: Hastavālaprakaraṇavṛtti
SA_T04_n1002u.author: attributed to Āryadeva (or Dignāga; authorship disputed)
SA_T04_n1002u.date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (uncertain; depends on attribution)
SA_T04_n1002u.summary: The 'Treatise of the Hand-grasp/Handful' (also rendered Hastavālanāmaprakaraṇa), a very short verse treatise with its own commentary (vṛtti) arguing that ordinary objects, like a snake mistakenly seen in a coiled rope, are merely conceptual imputations lacking real existence when analyzed. It uses the rope-snake and part-whole analyses to teach the unreality of substantial things and thus emptiness.
SA_T04_n1002u.history: The work is variously ascribed to Āryadeva or to Dignāga in different traditions, and its attribution is debated in modern scholarship; it survives in Tibetan and Chinese with the autocommentary. It is a compact pedagogical demonstration of Madhyamaka non-substantiality.

SA_T04_n1003u.title: Madhyamakahṛdaya
SA_T04_n1003u.author: Bhāvaviveka (Bhāviveka/Bhavya)
SA_T04_n1003u.summary: The 'Heart of the Middle Way' (Madhyamakahṛdayakārikā), Bhāvaviveka's independent verse treatise expounding Madhyamaka and surveying rival systems. It defends the use of autonomous inference (svatantra-anumāna) in establishing emptiness and devotes whole chapters to critiquing the Śrāvakas, Yogācāra, Sāṃkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Vedānta, and Mīmāṃsā.
SA_T04_n1003u.history: Bhāvaviveka founded the Svātantrika branch of Madhyamaka, criticizing Buddhapālita and provoking Candrakīrti's Prāsaṅgika response. The kārikās partly survive in Sanskrit; the prose autocommentary, the Tarkajvālā ('Blaze of Reasoning'), survives only in Tibetan and is a major doxographical source for sixth-century Indian philosophy.

SA_T04_n1047u.title: Madhyamārthasaṃgraha
SA_T04_n1047u.author: attributed to Bhāvaviveka (Bhavya; attribution debated)
SA_T04_n1047u.date_estimate: c. 6th c. CE if by Bhāvaviveka; attribution and date uncertain
SA_T04_n1047u.summary: A very brief 'Compendium of the Meaning of the Middle' summarizing core Madhyamaka doctrine, notably the analysis of the two truths (conventional and ultimate) and the divisions of ultimate truth. It distills the Madhyamaka view into a compact set of verses.
SA_T04_n1047u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Bhāvaviveka/Bhavya, though scholars debate whether it is by the sixth-century Bhāvaviveka or a later author of the same name; it is preserved in Tibetan. It is studied as a concise statement of the graded two-truths schema in the Svātantrika tradition.

SA_T04_n2850u.title: Svabhāvavāda
SA_T04_n2850u.author: anonymous/traditional (collection; authorship unclear)
SA_T04_n2850u.tradition: Buddhist (Madhyamaka context) / Indian philosophy on svabhāva
SA_T04_n2850u.genre: verse collection / doctrinal treatise on 'inherent nature'
SA_T04_n2850u.summary: A text concerned with svabhāva-vāda, the doctrine of 'inherent nature/own-being'. In the broader Indian setting svabhāvavāda denotes a naturalist position that things are simply as their intrinsic nature determines (a view discussed and often rejected); in the Buddhist Madhyamaka frame the concept of svabhāva is the central target of the emptiness critique. The exact content of this particular short work is not securely identifiable from available sources.
SA_T04_n2850u.history: Verses relating to svabhāvavāda were collected and cited across philosophical literature; the precise identity and authorship of this catalogued item (Toh 2850) could not be confirmed.

SA_T04_n2980u.title: Madhyamakāvatārakārikā
SA_T04_n2980u.author: Candrakīrti
SA_T04_n2980u.summary: The root verses (kārikā) of Candrakīrti's Madhyamakāvatāra, i.e. the verse text alone of the 'Introduction to the Middle Way', extracted from the work he glossed in his own bhāṣya. It lays out Madhyamaka along the ten bodhisattva grounds and culminates in the refutation of svabhāva and the critique of Yogācāra in the sixth ground.
SA_T04_n2980u.history: This is the bare verse layer corresponding to the full Madhyamakāvatāra (with autocommentary); the verses largely survive in Sanskrit and are the basis of extensive Tibetan exegesis, especially in the Dge-lugs tradition.

SA_T04_n847u.title: Madhyamakaśāstrastuti
SA_T04_n847u.author: Candrakīrti (traditionally attributed)
SA_T04_n847u.date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE if by Candrakīrti (uncertain)
SA_T04_n847u.summary: A short hymn 'in praise of the Madhyamaka treatise', that is, a eulogy to Nāgārjuna and his Mūlamadhyamakakārikā as the foundational śāstra of the Middle Way. It venerates the author and teaching of emptiness in verse.
SA_T04_n847u.history: Such dedicatory verses to Nāgārjuna appear in and around Candrakīrti's works; this brief stuti is traditionally linked to him, though its independent transmission and authorship are not firmly established. It survives among the minor Madhyamaka texts of the canon.

SA_T04_n848u.title: Niraupamyastava
SA_T04_n848u.author: Nāgārjuna (traditionally attributed; part of the Catuḥstava)
SA_T04_n848u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE if by Nāgārjuna; authorship of the Catuḥstava disputed
SA_T04_n848u.summary: The 'Hymn to the Peerless/Incomparable One', a devotional poem praising the Buddha as without equal. While laudatory in form, it expresses Madhyamaka themes, presenting the Buddha and his awakening in terms consistent with emptiness and the absence of intrinsic marks.
SA_T04_n848u.history: It is counted among Nāgārjuna's Catuḥstava ('Four Hymns'), alongside the Lokātītastava, Acintyastava, and Paramārthastava, though more than four such hymns are transmitted and which constitute the original quartet is debated. The Sanskrit survives and the hymns were popular in Indian Mahāyāna circles.

SA_T04_n849u.title: Paramārthastava
SA_T04_n849u.author: Nāgārjuna (traditionally attributed; part of the Catuḥstava)
SA_T04_n849u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE if by Nāgārjuna; authorship of the Catuḥstava disputed
SA_T04_n849u.summary: The 'Hymn to the Ultimate', praising the Buddha in terms of ultimate truth (paramārtha). It uses a sequence of negations to indicate that the Buddha, like ultimate reality and nirvāṇa, cannot be captured by predicates, thereby voicing Madhyamaka apophasis in the form of devotional praise.
SA_T04_n849u.history: One of the four hymns of Nāgārjuna's Catuḥstava; its Sanskrit survives. As with the other staves, the secure attribution to Nāgārjuna and membership in the original 'four' are matters of scholarly discussion.

SA_T04_nagmmk_u.title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā
SA_T04_nagmmk_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_nagmmk_u.summary: The 'Root Verses on the Middle Way', the foundational text of the Madhyamaka school, comprising c. 450 verses in 27 chapters. Through dialectical analysis it argues that all phenomena are empty of intrinsic nature (svabhāva) because they arise dependently, examining causation, motion, the aggregates, time, the self, nirvāṇa, and the four noble truths, and equating dependent origination with emptiness and the middle way.
SA_T04_nagmmk_u.history: Composed by Nāgārjuna (c. 150-250 CE), the MMK is among the most influential works of Buddhist philosophy and spawned a vast commentarial tradition (Buddhapālita, Bhāvaviveka's Prajñāpradīpa, Candrakīrti's Prasannapadā, etc.) and the Svātantrika/Prāsaṅgika split. The verses are preserved in Sanskrit (chiefly via Candrakīrti's commentary), Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_T04_nagmyv_u.title: Mahāyānaviṃśikā [Mahāyānaviṃśati]
SA_T04_nagmyv_u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (disputed)
SA_T04_nagmyv_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (if by Nāgārjuna, c. 150-250 CE; authorship doubted, may be later)
SA_T04_nagmyv_u.summary: A short work of roughly twenty verses ('Twenty Verses on the Great Vehicle') expounding core Mahayana and Madhyamaka themes such as emptiness (śūnyatā), the illusory, dependently arisen nature of phenomena, and the non-substantiality of mind and objects. It compresses the standard Madhyamaka critique of inherent existence into a brief devotional-philosophical compass.
SA_T04_nagmyv_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna, the founder of the Madhyamaka school, though the attribution is contested by modern scholars and the text may belong to a later author working in his name. It survives chiefly in Tibetan (and partly Sanskrit/Chinese) and was studied alongside his Pratītyasamutpādahṛdayakārikā.

SA_T04_nagvigvu.title: Vigrahavyāvartanī
SA_T04_nagvigvu.author: Nāgārjuna (traditional; some scholarly doubt)
SA_T04_nagvigvu.summary: The 'Averting of the Disputes,' comprising seventy verses with an accompanying prose autocommentary (vṛtti), in which Nāgārjuna answers objections to the doctrine of emptiness, especially the charge that the thesis 'all things are empty' is self-refuting. It contains his famous critique of the Nyāya theory of pramāṇas (means of valid knowledge) and the assertion that emptiness is itself empty and entails no positive thesis.
SA_T04_nagvigvu.history: One of the two works (with the Mūlamadhyamakakārikā) used as the touchstone for Nāgārjuna's authentic corpus, though a few scholars have questioned the attribution. It is a foundational text for Buddhist debate with the Brahmanical Nyāya school and survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese with later commentaries.

SA_T04_nagvvy_u.title: Vigrahavyāvartanīkārikā
SA_T04_nagvvy_u.author: Nāgārjuna (traditional; some scholarly doubt)
SA_T04_nagvvy_u.summary: The bare verse portion (kārikās, ~70 stanzas) of the Vigrahavyāvartanī, presenting Nāgārjuna's reply to objections against emptiness and his critique of the Nyāya epistemology of pramāṇas, without the accompanying prose autocommentary. The verses argue that the proposition 'everything is empty' does not undermine itself because the statement too lacks intrinsic nature.
