SA_T04_nagvvy_u.history: This is the metrical core of the same work catalogued separately above (the Vigrahavyāvartanī normally circulates with Nāgārjuna's own vṛtti); the kārikā designation isolates the verses alone. It is part of Nāgārjuna's foundational Madhyamaka corpus and shares its transmission and commentarial tradition.

SA_T04_nagysk_u.title: Yuktiṣaṣṭikakārikā
SA_T04_nagysk_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_nagysk_u.summary: The 'Sixty Stanzas on Reasoning' (kārikā form), a concise Madhyamaka treatise that develops the relationship between emptiness (śūnyatā) and dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda) and argues for the middle way avoiding the extremes of existence and non-existence. It frames philosophy therapeutically as the elimination of reifying and nihilistic cognitive biases.
SA_T04_nagysk_u.history: One of Nāgārjuna's most frequently cited works among later Madhyamaka commentators and a member of the Tibetan 'Sixfold Canon of Reasoning' (rigs tshogs drug). Candrakīrti wrote an influential commentary (Yuktiṣaṣṭikāvṛtti); the verses survive in Tibetan and Chinese with Sanskrit fragments.

SA_T04_nagyuksu.title: Yuktiṣaṣṭikārikā
SA_T04_nagyuksu.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T04_nagyuksu.summary: A variant catalogue spelling of the Yuktiṣaṣṭikā ('Sixty Stanzas on Reasoning'), the same sixty-verse Madhyamaka work that correlates emptiness with dependent origination and counsels the middle way free of the extremes of being and nothingness. It is among Nāgārjuna's shortest and most-quoted reasoning treatises.
SA_T04_nagyuksu.history: Identical work to the Yuktiṣaṣṭikakārikā entry above, here listed under a slightly different title form. Part of the Tibetan 'Sixfold Canon of Reasoning,' it is traditionally read with Candrakīrti's commentary.

SA_T04_santbcpu.title: Bodhicaryāvatāra [Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra]
SA_T04_santbcpu.author: Śāntideva
SA_T04_santbcpu.genre: kavya / didactic religious poem (verse manual of the bodhisattva path)
SA_T04_santbcpu.summary: The 'Introduction to the Practice of Awakening,' a ten-chapter verse work guiding the practitioner through the cultivation of bodhicitta (the awakening mind) and the six perfections, culminating in the renowned ninth chapter on wisdom (prajñā) that expounds Madhyamaka emptiness, and a tenth chapter of dedication. It is celebrated for its poetic power and ethical depth, including the argument against the self and for compassion grounded in the absence of inherent existence.
SA_T04_santbcpu.history: Composed by Śāntideva, an 8th-century monk-scholar at Nālandā university. The most beloved practical manual of the Mahayana, especially in Tibetan Buddhism where it generated an enormous commentarial literature; it survives in Sanskrit with multiple recensions (a shorter Dūn-huáng version exists) and numerous translations.

SA_T04_vasbocpu.title: Bodhicittotpādasūtraśāstra
SA_T04_vasbocpu.author: Vasubandhu (attributed)
SA_T04_vasbocpu.summary: A treatise ('Treatise on the Sūtra of Generating the Mind of Awakening') discoursing on the arising of bodhicitta — the resolve to attain buddhahood for the sake of all beings — and on the six perfections (pāramitās) through which that vow matures into buddhahood. It treats the causes, conditions, and stages of the bodhisattva's aspiration.
SA_T04_vasbocpu.history: Attributed to Vasubandhu and preserved primarily through its Chinese translation (Fa putixin lun, T.1659). As with several works ascribed to Vasubandhu, the attribution is traditional and the underlying Sanskrit does not survive intact.

SA_T05_bsa062_u.title: Śālistambakakārikā [Āryaśālistambakakārikā]
SA_T05_bsa062_u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (disputed)
SA_T05_bsa062_u.genre: commentary in verse (kārikā on a sūtra)
SA_T05_bsa062_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (uncertain; if Nāgārjuna, c. 150-250 CE)
SA_T05_bsa062_u.summary: A versified commentary on the Śālistamba ('Rice Seedling') Sūtra, distilling the sūtra's teaching on dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda) — illustrated by the growth of a rice shoot from a seed — into mnemonic kārikās. It articulates the twelve links and the absence of a creator or self in the causal process.
SA_T05_bsa062_u.history: One of three Indian commentaries on the Rice Seedling Sūtra preserved in Tibetan; two of them (this kārikā and the Śālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā) are traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna, though the attribution is doubted by scholars and may belong to a later author of that name. Studied and translated by Jeffrey D. Schoening (1995).

SA_T05_bsu027_u.title: Pratītyasamutpādādivibhaṅganirdeśasūtra
SA_T05_bsu027_u.author: anonymous/traditional (Buddhavacana)
SA_T05_bsu027_u.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (first centuries CE; pre-5th c.)
SA_T05_bsu027_u.summary: The 'Sūtra Teaching the Fundamental Exposition and Detailed Analysis of Dependent Arising,' which expounds the doctrine of pratītyasamutpāda, first stating its basic formula and then analyzing each of the twelve links (nidānas) from ignorance to old-age-and-death. It is a canonical scriptural source treating dependent origination as the essence of the Dharma.
SA_T05_bsu027_u.history: A short canonical sūtra (Tohoku 211 in the Tibetan Kangyur) that stands beside the Rice Seedling Sūtra (Toh 210) and the Sūtra on Dependent Arising (Toh 212) as part of a cluster of Kangyur texts on dependent origination; translated into English by Annie Bien for 84000 (2020).

SA_T05_n1110u.title: Bodhyāpattideśanāvṛtti
SA_T05_n1110u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna
SA_T05_n1110u.genre: bhasya / vṛtti (running commentary on a sūtra)
SA_T05_n1110u.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (uncertain; traditionally Nāgārjuna, c. 150-250 CE)
SA_T05_n1110u.summary: A 'Running Commentary on the Confession of the Transgressions of a Bodhisattva,' explicating the practice of confessing ethical downfalls before the Thirty-Five Buddhas drawn from the Triskandhaka (Three Heaps) Sūtra. It treats the bodhisattva's purification of moral faults through prostration, confession, and dedication.
SA_T05_n1110u.history: Ascribed to Nāgārjuna, this śāstra-commentary supports one of the central purificatory practices of Mahayana and Tibetan Buddhism (the confession of downfalls to the Thirty-Five Buddhas), the root text of which is embedded in the Ratnakūṭa collection's 'Definitive Vinaya' chapter. The attribution to Nāgārjuna is traditional.

SA_T05_nagsaliu.title: Śālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā [Āryaśālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā]
SA_T05_nagsaliu.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (disputed)
SA_T05_nagsaliu.date_estimate: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (uncertain; if Nāgārjuna, c. 150-250 CE)
SA_T05_nagsaliu.summary: A prose commentary (ṭīkā) on the Śālistamba ('Rice Seedling') Mahāyāna Sūtra, expounding its teaching on dependent origination through the simile of a rice seedling arising from a seed and explaining the twelve links of conditioned arising and the impossibility of an independent agent. It elaborates the sūtra's identification of dependent arising with the Dharma itself.
SA_T05_nagsaliu.history: One of three Indian commentaries on the Rice Seedling Sūtra surviving in Tibetan; like the related Śālistambakakārikā it is traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna, an attribution modern scholarship questions. Studied and translated by Jeffrey D. Schoening (1995); a third commentary is by Kamalaśīla.

SA_T06_abhsubhu.title: Abhidharmasamuccayabhāṣya
SA_T06_abhsubhu.author: attributed to Sthiramati (some sources Jinaputra/Buddhasiṃha)
SA_T06_abhsubhu.summary: A prose commentary (bhāṣya) on Asaṅga's Abhidharmasamuccaya ('Compendium of Abhidharma'), the principal Yogācāra systematization of Buddhist phenomenology — the aggregates, elements, bases, and the path. The bhāṣya glosses and expands Asaṅga's terse compendium, integrating Abhidharma analysis with Yogācāra doctrine such as the storehouse consciousness.
SA_T06_abhsubhu.history: Surviving in Sanskrit (a unique manuscript), Tibetan, and Chinese, the bhāṣya is commonly attributed to Sthiramati, the great 6th-century Yogācāra commentator, though some traditions name Jinaputra or Buddhasiṃha; modern scholars debate the precise authorship of works ascribed to Sthiramati.

SA_T06_asmahsbu.title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṅkāra
SA_T06_asmahsbu.author: Maitreya(nātha) (kārikās); Vasubandhu (bhāṣya); transmitted via Asaṅga
SA_T06_asmahsbu.genre: philosophical treatise (verse kārikās with prose bhāṣya)
SA_T06_asmahsbu.date_estimate: c. 4th c. CE (verses 4th c.; Vasubandhu bhāṣya c. 4th-5th c.)
SA_T06_asmahsbu.summary: The 'Ornament of the Mahayana Sūtras,' a verse treatise of about 800 stanzas in 22 chapters presenting the entire Mahayana path from the Yogācāra standpoint — the greatness of the Mahayana, taking refuge, the bodhisattva lineage, generation of bodhicitta, the perfections, the stages, and buddhahood. It includes the three-nature doctrine and mind-only (cittamātra) themes, accompanied by Vasubandhu's prose commentary (bhāṣya).
SA_T06_asmahsbu.history: Attributed in the Yogācāra tradition to Maitreyanātha, who is said to have transmitted it to Asaṅga, with a prose bhāṣya by Asaṅga's brother Vasubandhu; both verse and bhāṣya survive in Sanskrit, Tibetan, Chinese, and Mongolian, with sub-commentaries by Sthiramati and Asvabhāva. It is one of the 'Five Treatises of Maitreya.'

SA_T06_asycsaru.title: Śarīrārthagāthā
SA_T06_asycsaru.author: anonymous/traditional (compiled within the Yogācārabhūmi, ascribed to Asaṅga)
SA_T06_asycsaru.genre: verse anthology with exegesis (section of a śāstra)
SA_T06_asycsaru.date_estimate: c. 4th c. CE (final redaction of the Yogācārabhūmi)
SA_T06_asycsaru.summary: The 'Verses on the Essential Meaning' (or 'Collected-Meaning Verses'), a collection of canonical gāthās embedded in the Yogācārabhūmi together with their exegesis, organizing earlier scriptural verses around fundamental Buddhist doctrinal topics. It functions as a structured anthology that interprets received verse-material within the Yogācāra framework.
SA_T06_asycsaru.history: A constituent section of the vast Yogācārabhūmi-śāstra, the foundational Yogācāra encyclopedia traditionally attributed to Asaṅga (or to Maitreya as transmitted through Asaṅga) and reaching final redaction in the 4th century CE. The title's term śarīra ('body/aggregate') is rendered in Tibetan as 'collection,' reflecting its anthological character.

SA_T06_bsa010_u.title: Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā
SA_T06_bsa010_u.author: Maitreya(nātha); transmitted via Asaṅga
SA_T06_bsa010_u.summary: The 'Verses Distinguishing the Middle from the Extremes,' a concise Yogācāra treatise expounding the three natures (trisvabhāva), a distinctly Yogācāra account of emptiness, and the middle way between the extremes of reification and denial, organized across chapters on characteristics, obscurations, the truths, cultivation of antidotes, the vehicles, and the ultimate. It is foundational for the mind-only analysis of imagined, dependent, and perfected natures.
SA_T06_bsa010_u.history: One of the 'Five Treatises of Maitreya,' transmitted to Asaṅga and equipped with a prose commentary (bhāṣya) by Vasubandhu and an influential sub-commentary (ṭīkā) by Sthiramati; the verses survive in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese. Translated and studied in English by Mario D'Amato (2012).

SA_T06_bsa017_u.title: Tattvaratnāvaloka [Tattvaratnāloka]
SA_T06_bsa017_u.author: Vāgīśvarakīrti
SA_T06_bsa017_u.summary: The 'Light on the Jewel of Reality,' a tantric Buddhist treatise (with an accompanying vṛtti) by Vāgīśvarakīrti dealing with the realization of ultimate reality (tattva) within the esoteric Vajrayana framework, including discussion of higher initiations (abhiṣeka) and the practices and coded language of tantric attainment.
SA_T06_bsa017_u.history: Composed by Vāgīśvarakīrti, an East Indian tantric scholar who flourished in the early 11th century as a colleague of Ratnākaraśānti at the monastic university of Vikramaśīla. His views were influential enough to be debated in Kashmir before 1057 CE and he is reverently alluded to on the Khmer Sab Bāk inscription of 1067 CE.

SA_T06_trimsikatika.title: Triṃśikāṭīkā
SA_T06_trimsikatika.author: Vinītadeva
SA_T06_trimsikatika.summary: A sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā ('Thirty Verses' on consciousness-only), explicating the foundational Yogācāra doctrines of the verses — the storehouse consciousness (ālayavijñāna), the afflicted mind (kliṣṭa-manas), the active consciousnesses, the three natures, and the realization of vijñaptimātratā. It glosses both Vasubandhu's verses and the established commentarial tradition (associated with Sthiramati's reading).
SA_T06_trimsikatika.history: Written by Vinītadeva (c. 645-715), a Nālandā scholar important in both the Yogācāra and Buddhist-logic (pramāṇa) traditions, who produced numerous ṭīkās preserved chiefly in Tibetan; Sanskrit fragments of this Triṃśikāṭīkā were published by P. S. Jaini (1985).

SA_T06_bsa019_u.title: Trisvabhāvanirdeśa
SA_T06_bsa019_u.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T06_bsa019_u.summary: The 'Treatise on the Three Natures,' a short verse work presenting Vasubandhu's mature exposition of the Yogācāra trisvabhāva doctrine: the imagined (parikalpita), dependent (paratantra), and consummate (pariniṣpanna) natures, and their interrelation. It explains, often via the simile of a magical illusion, how delusion arises through misconstruing the dependent and how liberation comes through realizing the perfected nature.
SA_T06_bsa019_u.history: Among the trio of short independent treatises (with the Viṃśatikā and Triṃśikā) that present Vasubandhu's own Yogācāra philosophy, and possibly his last work; it survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan and is noted for lacking an early Indian commentary. The attribution to Vasubandhu is generally accepted.

SA_T06_bsa030_u.title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra
SA_T06_bsa030_u.author: Maitreya(nātha) (kārikās); Vasubandhu (bhāṣya); via Asaṅga
SA_T06_bsa030_u.genre: philosophical treatise (verse kārikās with prose bhāṣya)
SA_T06_bsa030_u.date_estimate: c. 4th c. CE (verses; Vasubandhu bhāṣya c. 4th-5th c.)
SA_T06_bsa030_u.summary: The 'Ornament of the Mahayana Sūtras' — the same 22-chapter, ~800-verse Yogācāra treatise on the entire bodhisattva path (the greatness of the Mahayana, bodhicitta, the perfections, the bhūmis, and buddhahood) listed earlier under the variant spelling. It systematizes Mahayana doctrine with the three-nature and mind-only teachings and is accompanied by Vasubandhu's bhāṣya.
SA_T06_bsa030_u.history: Identical work to entry T06asmahsbu above, here catalogued with the anusvāra spelling Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra. It is one of the 'Five Treatises of Maitreya,' transmitted to Asaṅga with a prose commentary by Vasubandhu and later sub-commentaries by Sthiramati and Asvabhāva; it survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, Chinese, and Mongolian.

SA_T06_bsa073_u.title: Ratnagotravibhāga [Ratnagotravibhāga Mahāyānottaratantraśāstra, Ratnagotravibhāga-Mahāyāna-Uttaratantra, Uttaratantra]
SA_T06_bsa073_u.author: Maitreya (verses, per Tibetan tradition) / Sāramati (per Chinese tradition); commentary ascribed to Asaṅga — disputed
SA_T06_bsa073_u.genre: philosophical treatise (verse kārikās with prose vyākhyā)
SA_T06_bsa073_u.summary: The 'Analysis of the Jeweled Lineage,' also called the Uttaratantra, the principal Indian śāstra on buddha-nature (tathāgatagarbha) theory, expounding seven 'vajra topics' — the Three Jewels, the buddha-element/disposition present in all beings, awakening, the qualities of a buddha, and buddha-activity. It argues that all sentient beings possess the potential for buddhahood, which is merely obscured by adventitious defilements.
SA_T06_bsa073_u.history: Authorship is famously disputed: the Tibetan tradition assigns the verses to Maitreya and the prose commentary (vyākhyā/RGVV) to Asaṅga, while the Chinese tradition attributes the whole to the obscure Sāramati; modern scholarship remains divided. Composed probably in the 5th century, it is the only tathāgatagarbha treatise surviving in Sanskrit, was translated into Chinese by Ratnamati (early 6th c.), and became central to East Asian and Tibetan buddha-nature thought.

SA_T06_n1290u.title: Bāhyārthasiddhikārikā
SA_T06_n1290u.author: Śubhagupta
SA_T06_n1290u.tradition: Buddhist (epistemological/pramāṇa school; realist position between Sautrāntika and Vaibhāṣika)
SA_T06_n1290u.summary: A verse treatise (kārikā) 'Establishing [the Existence of] External Objects' that argues for the reality of external objects of cognition against Yogācāra idealism. Śubhagupta engages critically with Diṅnāga and Vasubandhu, defending the existence of mind-independent atoms/objects while holding a distinctive view that the images (ākāra) of those objects do not themselves appear in cognition. His position oscillates between Sautrāntika and Vaibhāṣika realism.
SA_T06_n1290u.history: The work survives only in Tibetan translation. Śubhagupta was a notable opponent targeted in the Bahirarthaparīkṣā section of Śāntarakṣita's Tattvasaṅgraha and Kamalaśīla's commentary, which helps fix his date to the 8th century. The text is a key source for the Buddhist realist defense of external objects in the perception debate.

SA_T06_n1333u.title: Cittatattvasaṅgraha
SA_T06_n1333u.summary: A 'Compendium of the True Nature of Mind' (citta-tattva-saṅgraha). The precise identity of this Taishō-canon item could not be confidently established; the title suggests a compendium focused on the nature of mind, and works of this name appear in the esoteric/tantric portions of the Chinese Buddhist canon, distinct from Śāntarakṣita's well-known Tattvasaṅgraha.
SA_T06_n1333u.history: This is an obscure title that could not be securely identified or distinguished from similarly named works. It should not be confused with Śāntarakṣita's encyclopedic Tattvasaṅgraha. Authorship, date, and content remain uncertain in the available scholarly sources.

SA_T06_n1394u.title: Yogācārabhūmi
SA_T06_n1394u.author: Asaṅga (traditional; East Asian tradition ascribes to Maitreya; modern scholarship sees it as a layered compilation)
SA_T06_n1394u.summary: A monumental encyclopedic foundational text of the Yogācāra school describing the spiritual states, practices, and fruits along the path to Buddhahood. Its core 'Maulī Bhūmi' division comprises seventeen stages (bhūmis), followed by four supplementary compendia (Viniścaya-, Vivaraṇa-, Paryāya-, and Vastu-saṃgrahaṇī, plus a Vinaya-saṃgrahaṇī). It expounds the full range of mental and spiritual levels and Yogācāra doctrine including the ālayavijñāna.
SA_T06_n1394u.history: Traditionally attributed to Asaṅga (4th c.), though East Asian tradition credits his teacher Maitreya; modern scholars regard it as a multi-authored, layered compilation finalized around 300-350 CE. Preserved in Sanskrit fragments, Tibetan, and Chinese (Xuanzang's translation). It is the largest and most influential foundational treatise of Yogācāra.

SA_T06_n2994u.title: Mahāyānasaṃgraha
SA_T06_n2994u.author: Asaṅga
SA_T06_n2994u.summary: The 'Summary (Compendium) of the Great Vehicle', a ten-chapter systematic exposition of the major doctrines of Yogācāra Mahāyāna. It offers the most extensive early analysis of the ālayavijñāna (storehouse consciousness), the eight consciousnesses, the three natures (trisvabhāva), 'mind-only' (cittamātra), dependent origination, the bodhisattva path, the four wisdoms, and the three bodies (kāyas) of a buddha.
SA_T06_n2994u.history: One of the two most important works attributed to Asaṅga and often regarded as a distillation of the Yogācārabhūmi. It generated major commentaries, including Vasubandhu's Mahāyānasaṃgraha-bhāṣya and Asvabhāva's Mahāyānasaṃgraha-upanibandhana (6th c.). It survives in Tibetan and several Chinese translations.

SA_T06_ybh-laukikamarga.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Laukikamārga
SA_T06_ybh-laukikamarga.author: Asaṅga (traditional; see Yogācārabhūmi)
SA_T06_ybh-laukikamarga.genre: philosophical treatise (section of an encyclopedic śāstra)
SA_T06_ybh-laukikamarga.summary: The 'Mundane Path' (laukika-mārga) portion within the Yogācārabhūmi (specifically within the Śrāvakabhūmi's treatment of the path). It describes the worldly/mundane branch of meditative practice—progressive attainment of the dhyānas and formless absorptions by suppressing grosser levels for subtler ones—as distinct from the supramundane path (lokottara-mārga) that yields liberating insight.
SA_T06_ybh-laukikamarga.history: This is a sub-section of the larger Yogācārabhūmi corpus, which treats meditation practice (especially in the Śrāvakabhūmi) by dividing the path into the mundane and supramundane. It shares the Yogācārabhūmi's composite authorship and dating.

SA_T06_sambhu.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Samāhitā Bhūmi
SA_T06_sambhu.author: Asaṅga (traditional; see Yogācārabhūmi)
SA_T06_sambhu.genre: philosophical treatise (section of an encyclopedic śāstra)
SA_T06_sambhu.summary: The 'Stage of Concentration/Meditative Equipoise' (samāhitā bhūmi), one of the seventeen bhūmis constituting the core (Maulī) division of the Yogācārabhūmi. It systematically treats states of meditative absorption (samādhi/samāpatti), including the dhyānas and formless attainments, and the conditions and characteristics of mind in equipoise.
SA_T06_sambhu.history: This is one of the seventeen foundational stages of the Yogācārabhūmi. It belongs to the same composite corpus and shares its dating and transmission in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_T06_srabhu_u.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Śrāvakabhūmi
SA_T06_srabhu_u.author: Asaṅga (traditional; see Yogācārabhūmi)
SA_T06_srabhu_u.genre: meditation manual (section of an encyclopedic śāstra)
SA_T06_srabhu_u.date_estimate: c. 300-350 CE (hints of an earlier 3rd-c. core)
SA_T06_srabhu_u.summary: The 'Stage of the Disciple/Hearer' (śrāvakabhūmi), the second-largest book of the Yogācārabhūmi and a detailed meditation manual. Divided into four yogasthānas, it gives an exhaustive presentation of meditation, defining dhyāna, vimokṣa, samāpatti and samādhi, treating prerequisites of practice, objects of meditation, and the staged progress through mundane and supramundane paths.
SA_T06_srabhu_u.history: A self-contained meditation treatise embedded in the Yogācārabhūmi, often studied independently as one of the most systematic Indian Buddhist accounts of meditative practice. Some scholars posit an early core; the project hint of c. 200-270 CE reflects this proposed earlier stratum, while the broader corpus is dated c. 300-350 CE.

SA_T06_srabhusu.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Śrāvakabhūmi
SA_T06_srabhusu.author: Asaṅga (traditional; see Yogācārabhūmi)
SA_T06_srabhusu.genre: meditation manual (section of an encyclopedic śāstra)
SA_T06_srabhusu.date_estimate: c. 300-350 CE (hints of an earlier 3rd-c. core)
SA_T06_srabhusu.summary: The 'Stage of the Disciple/Hearer' (śrāvakabhūmi) of the Yogācārabhūmi—a detailed, four-part meditation manual presenting the prerequisites, objects, and stages of meditative practice for the śrāvaka. This appears to be a second representation (e.g., a distinct recension or text-stream, perhaps the Sanskrit) of the same Śrāvakabhūmi as the preceding entry.
SA_T06_srabhusu.history: A duplicate/parallel record of the Śrāvakabhūmi within this dataset, likely reflecting a different language version or edition (e.g., Sanskrit vs. Chinese). Same corpus, authorship, and dating as the Yogācārabhūmi.

SA_T07_dhrmsk_u.title: Dharmaskandha
SA_T07_dhrmsk_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Śāriputra in Sanskrit/Tibetan sources, to Maudgalyāyana in Chinese sources)
SA_T07_dhrmsk_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-1st century BCE (one of the earliest of the seven Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma treatises)
SA_T07_dhrmsk_u.summary: The 'Aggregation of Dharmas', one of the seven canonical Abhidharma treatises of the Sarvāstivāda. It is an early, list-based ('mātṛkā') treatise systematizing core Buddhist doctrinal categories—such as the path factors, faculties, meditative attainments, and the constituents (skandhas, dhātus, āyatanas)—largely by collecting and glossing sūtra material.
SA_T07_dhrmsk_u.history: Counted among the 'six feet and one body' of Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma and regarded as one of the oldest of the seven. Authorship is disputed: Śāriputra per Sanskrit/Tibetan tradition, Maudgalyāyana per Chinese tradition. It survives in Chinese (and Sanskrit fragments) and is foundational for later Abhidharma scholasticism.

SA_T07_n772u.title: Abhidharmāmṛta
SA_T07_n772u.author: Ghoṣaka
SA_T07_n772u.summary: The Abhidharmāmṛta(-rasa)-śāstra, 'The [Taste of the] Nectar of Abhidharma', a concise systematic manual of Sarvāstivāda-Vaibhāṣika Abhidharma. It presents core Abhidharma topics—dharma categories, faculties, defilements, karma, the path, and meditative states—as an accessible compendium of the system.
SA_T07_n772u.history: Attributed to Ghoṣaka, a Sarvāstivāda master associated with the post-Mahāvibhāṣā Gandhāran tradition (sometimes called Tocharian). The work survives in Chinese translation (T 1553) and represents the more concise manual genre within Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma literature.

SA_T07_n797u.title: Subodhālaṅkāra
SA_T07_n797u.author: Saṅgharakkhita Mahāsāmi
SA_T07_n797u.tradition: Buddhist (Theravāda; Pāli literary tradition of Sri Lanka)
SA_T07_n797u.summary: The 'Lucid (Easily-Understood) Ornament', the first manual of Pāli poetics, treating figures of speech, poetic faults and merits, and rhetorical ornamentation (alaṃkāra). It adapts Sanskrit poetic theory—drawing heavily on Daṇḍin's Kāvyādarśa—into Pāli, recasting some ideas in a Buddhist light.
SA_T07_n797u.history: Composed in the 13th century by the Sri Lankan monk Saṅgharakkhita, it was a pivotal work in the development of second-millennium Pāli kāvya. The Pali Text Society edition (ed. P. S. Jaini, 2000) includes two versions of its vṛtti and documents extensive parallels with Daṇḍin's Kāvyādarśa. Note: this is a Pāli rather than Sanskrit work.

SA_T07_nagdhdbu.title: Dharmadhātugarbhavivaraṇa
SA_T07_nagdhdbu.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (attribution uncertain)
SA_T07_nagdhdbu.date_estimate: uncertain (attributed authorship would imply c. 2nd-3rd c. CE, but likely later)
SA_T07_nagdhdbu.summary: An 'Exposition of the Womb/Essence of the Dharma-Realm' (dharmadhātu-garbha), a short doctrinal work in the dharmadhātu / tathāgatagarbha sphere associated with the Nāgārjuna corpus. It elaborates the nature of the dharmadhātu as the basis temporarily obscured by adventitious stains and uncovered along the path—themes central to the Dharmadhātustava milieu.
SA_T07_nagdhdbu.history: The attribution to Nāgārjuna is part of the broad pseudepigraphic 'Nāgārjuna' devotional/tathāgatagarbha corpus and is not securely established; this particular vivaraṇa is obscure and distinct from the better-known Dharmadhātustava. Reliable details on its date and transmission are limited.

SA_K10_udanav_u.title: Udānavarga
SA_K10_udanav_u.author: anonymous/traditional (compilation traditionally ascribed to Dharmatrāta)
SA_K10_udanav_u.date_estimate: compiled in the early centuries CE (verse material from much earlier; compiler dated c. 1st c. BCE-2nd c. CE)
SA_K10_udanav_u.summary: An early Buddhist anthology of aphoristic verses ('utterances', udāna) attributed to the Buddha and his disciples, organized into topical chapters (varga). It is the Sanskrit counterpart to the Pāli Dhammapada and Gāndhārī Dharmapada, sharing 330-340 verses, sixteen chapter headings, and an underlying structure, while adding much further material.
SA_K10_udanav_u.history: Traditionally compiled by the Sarvāstivādin master Dharmatrāta. Scholars (Hinüber, Brough) debate whether it grew from an Udāna-like core augmented with Dhammapada verses or whether all three Dharmapada-type texts share a common ancestor. Preserved in Sanskrit (Central Asian manuscripts), Tibetan, and Chinese.

SA_T07_vabhdicu.title: Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti
SA_T07_vabhdicu.author: the anonymous 'Dīpakāra' (the Abhidharmadīpa's author; project hint of 'Vimalamitra' is unverified—proposals also include Vasumitra/Īśvaravarman)
SA_T07_vabhdicu.summary: The prose auto-commentary ('Light of the Vibhāṣā Commentary') accompanying the Abhidharmadīpa-kārikā. It defends orthodox Vaibhāṣika positions and is written primarily to refute Vasubandhu's Sautrāntika-leaning critiques in the Abhidharmakośabhāṣya, polemically calling Vasubandhu the 'Kośakāra'.
SA_T07_vabhdicu.history: Edited together with the Abhidharmadīpa by Padmanabh S. Jaini (1959/1977) from incomplete Sanskrit fragments. Unlike most Abhidharma works, it was not translated into Tibetan or Chinese. Its author—the 'Dīpakāra'—is anonymous; the attribution to Vimalamitra in the project metadata is not well supported in scholarship.

SA_T07_vabhdiku.title: Abhidharmadīpakārikā
SA_T07_vabhdiku.author: the anonymous 'Dīpakāra' (project hint 'Vimalamitra' unverified)
SA_T07_vabhdiku.summary: The verse (kārikā) portion of the Abhidharmadīpa, the 'Lamp of Abhidharma'. Modeled on Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośakārikā, it sets out Vaibhāṣika Abhidharma doctrine but adopts an orthodox Vaibhāṣika standpoint to refute the Sautrāntika-leaning positions Vasubandhu took in his Kośa-bhāṣya.
SA_T07_vabhdiku.history: A less-known Vaibhāṣika treatise surviving only in incomplete Sanskrit fragments, critically edited (with its Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti commentary) by Padmanabh S. Jaini. It is valuable for confirming Vasubandhu's authorship of the Abhidharmakośakārikā and for documenting late Vaibhāṣika polemics. The author is conventionally the anonymous 'Dīpakāra'.

SA_T07_vabhdilu.title: Abhidharmadīpakārikā
SA_T07_vabhdilu.author: the anonymous 'Dīpakāra' (project hint 'Vimalamitra' unverified)
SA_T07_vabhdilu.summary: The kārikā (verse) text of the Abhidharmadīpa, 'Lamp of Abhidharma', presenting Vaibhāṣika Abhidharma in verse and refuting Vasubandhu's Sautrāntika-leaning Abhidharmakośa from an orthodox Vaibhāṣika standpoint. This appears to be a second representation (distinct recension or text-stream) of the same kārikā work as the preceding entry.
SA_T07_vabhdilu.history: A duplicate/parallel record of the Abhidharmadīpa-kārikā within this dataset. The work survives in incomplete Sanskrit fragments and was edited by Padmanabh S. Jaini. The author is conventionally the anonymous 'Dīpakāra'; the metadata attribution to Vimalamitra is not well established.

SA_T07_yabhkvyu.title: Sphuṭārthā Abhidharmakośavyākhyā
SA_T07_yabhkvyu.author: Yaśomitra
SA_T07_yabhkvyu.date_estimate: c. 6th century CE (sometimes placed slightly later)
SA_T07_yabhkvyu.summary: The 'Clear-Meaning Commentary' (Sphuṭārthā), a celebrated and extensive commentary on Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośa-bhāṣya. It glosses the verses and prose of the Kośa word-by-word, clarifies technical terms, identifies opponents' positions, and preserves much information about the various Abhidharma schools.
SA_T07_yabhkvyu.history: Authored by Yaśomitra and preserved in both Sanskrit and Tibetan—making it especially important since it transmits the Kośa-bhāṣya's Sanskrit. Regarded by the Tibetan scholar Chim Jampaiyang as the best of the renowned Kośa commentaries. The standard edition is Unrai Wogihara's (Tokyo, 1932-1936).

SA_T08_jsphustu.title: Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā
SA_T08_jsphustu.author: Jayarakṣita
SA_T08_jsphustu.tradition: Buddhist (Vinaya; nikāya affiliation debated—linked to Mahāsāṃghika)
SA_T08_jsphustu.summary: The 'Clear-Meaning Commentary' on the Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha—a compendium of regulations for Buddhist novices (śrāmaṇera). It explains the rules and conduct prescribed for novices, elaborating the verses of the root saṃgraha and providing detailed vinaya context.
SA_T08_jsphustu.history: Composed by Jayarakṣita. Its nikāya affiliation has been studied by scholars, with the Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā linked to the Mahāsāṃghika vinaya tradition (comparable with the Chinese Mahāsāṃghika vinaya). It survives in Sanskrit and is an important witness to novice-discipline literature.

SA_T08_sghancsu.title: Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha
SA_T08_sghancsu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T08_sghancsu.tradition: Buddhist (Vinaya; nikāya affiliation debated—linked to Mahāsāṃghika)
SA_T08_sghancsu.date_estimate: before the 7th-8th c. CE (root text of Jayarakṣita's ṭīkā)
SA_T08_sghancsu.summary: A 'Compendium of the Conduct of Śrīghana [the Buddha]', a verse compilation of regulations governing the conduct of Buddhist novices (śrāmaṇera). It serves as the root text on which Jayarakṣita's Sphuṭārthā ṭīkā comments.
SA_T08_sghancsu.history: The root text whose nikāya affiliation has been examined alongside its commentary and connected to the Mahāsāṃghika tradition. It survives in Sanskrit; its specific author is not securely identified, and it predates Jayarakṣita's commentary.

SA_T08_suvrnavu.title: Suvarṇavarṇāvadāna
SA_T08_suvrnavu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T08_suvrnavu.summary: The 'Avadāna of [the youth] Suvarṇavarṇa (Golden-Colored)', a Buddhist narrative tale recounting the karmically significant story of a golden-hued protagonist, illustrating the workings of karma and merit. A version is embedded in the later Vrata-avadāna-mālā, where the story is adapted to highlight the importance of observing various religious vows (vratas).
SA_T08_suvrnavu.history: Long known only through its adaptation in the Vratāvadānamālā, the text was later identified through a palm-leaf Sanskrit manuscript corresponding closely to Tibetan and Chinese versions, showing the mālā version to be a secondary reworking. It was edited and studied by Sita Ram Roy (1971) and translated by Tissa Rajapatirana.

SA_T09_ajm_u.title: Jātakamālā
SA_T09_ajm_u.author: Āryaśūra
SA_T09_ajm_u.genre: kavya / jataka (campū-style birth-story collection in mixed verse and prose)
SA_T09_ajm_u.date_estimate: c. 3rd-4th c. CE (sometimes placed earlier; debated)
SA_T09_ajm_u.summary: The 'Garland of Birth-Stories' retells in polished classical Sanskrit thirty-four jatakas, edifying tales of the previous lives of the Buddha-to-be (Bodhisattva), each illustrating a moral perfection (pāramitā) such as generosity, patience, and self-sacrifice. The stories alternate ornate verse with elegant prose and conclude with a doctrinal or ethical moral.
SA_T09_ajm_u.history: A celebrated work of Buddhist kavya whose author Āryaśūra is sometimes (uncertainly) identified with Aśvaghoṣa; its scenes parallel paintings at Ajanta, indicating wide influence. The Sanskrit editio princeps appeared in 1891 as the first volume of the Harvard Oriental Series.

SA_T09_bsa032.title: Jātakamālā
SA_T09_bsa032.author: Āryaśūra
SA_T09_bsa032.genre: kavya / jataka (mixed verse and prose birth-story collection)
SA_T09_bsa032.summary: A second catalogue record of Āryaśūra's 'Garland of Birth-Stories,' the same canonical collection of thirty-four jatakas recounting the Bodhisattva's former lives, each demonstrating a perfection (pāramitā) through self-sacrifice and virtue in ornate Sanskrit prose-and-verse.
SA_T09_bsa032.history: Identical work to the other Āryaśūra Jātakamālā entry in this batch; a foundational Buddhist Sanskrit kavya, edited in the Harvard Oriental Series and illustrated in the Ajanta murals.

SA_T09_bsu078_u.title: Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā
SA_T09_bsu078_u.author: Kṣemendra (with the final avadāna added by his son Somendra)
SA_T09_bsu078_u.tradition: Buddhist (Mahāyāna), composed by a Kashmiri Hindu polymath
SA_T09_bsu078_u.summary: A large verse collection of 108 avadanas recounting the meritorious past-life deeds of the Buddha and bodhisattvas, presented as a 'wish-fulfilling creeper' (kalpalatā) of edifying legends emphasizing the bodhisattva ideal of self-sacrifice. Kṣemendra composed the first 107 tales; his son Somendra added the concluding Jīmūtavāhanāvadāna and the frame verses.
SA_T09_bsu078_u.history: Written by the prolific 11th-century Kashmiri poet and satirist Kṣemendra (c. 990-c. 1070), it became extremely influential in Tibet, where it was translated and richly illustrated. Sanskrit text survives and was edited from Nepalese and Tibetan sources.

SA_T09_gopjatmu.title: Jātakamālā [Ajātaśatrvavadāna]
SA_T09_gopjatmu.author: Gopadatta
SA_T09_gopjatmu.genre: avadana / jataka (mixed verse and prose narrative)
SA_T09_gopjatmu.summary: Gopadatta's Jātakamālā is a separate 'Garland of Birth-Stories' modeled on Āryaśūra's, retelling some thirty-four legends of the Buddha's previous lives in a mixture of verse and prose. The Ajātaśatrvāvadāna is one of its constituent legends, treating the famous story of King Ajātaśatru, the patricide of Magadha, and his ultimate redemption.
SA_T09_gopjatmu.history: Gopadatta lived in the seventh or eighth century; only about half of his Garland has been recovered from later Sanskrit story-anthologies. The Ajātaśatrvāvadāna was attributed to Gopadatta by Michael Hahn, who reconstructed and edited much of the corpus.

SA_T09_jatkstpu.title: Jātakastava
SA_T09_jatkstpu.author: Jñānayaśas
SA_T09_jatkstpu.genre: stotra (devotional hymn in praise of the Buddha's former births)
SA_T09_jatkstpu.summary: A religious poem in praise of the Buddha glorifying his self-sacrificing deeds across his previous births (jatakas). It is built on an iterative hymnic structure with introductory and concluding stanzas, allusively recalling famous jataka episodes as objects of veneration rather than narrating them in full.
SA_T09_jatkstpu.history: The Sanskrit Jātakastava of Jñānayaśas survives with an interlinear Tibetan gloss and belongs to the same hymnographic genre as the better-known Khotanese Jātakastava, though it is a distinct, more elaborate composition. It was studied and edited by D. R. Shackleton Bailey (1939, 1954).

SA_T10_bsa025_u.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā]
SA_T10_bsa025_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T10_bsa025_u.genre: philosophical treatise / didactic epistle (rajaparikatha, advice to a king)
SA_T10_bsa025_u.summary: The 'Precious Garland' (also Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā) is a verse work of advice addressed to a king that interweaves Madhyamaka philosophy (emptiness, the two truths, dependent origination) with ethical, political, and religious counsel on righteous governance and the bodhisattva path. It uniquely combines subtle metaphysics with worldly statecraft.
SA_T10_bsa025_u.history: Attributed to Nāgārjuna, founder of the Madhyamaka school, whose dates and even number remain debated (1st-3rd c. CE). It is one of his most accessible works and survives partly in Sanskrit and fully in Tibetan and Chinese; it is counted among his epistolary/advisory writings alongside the Suhṛllekha.

SA_T10_bsa067_u.title: Bhavasaṃkrāntiparikathā
SA_T10_bsa067_u.author: Nāgārjuna (attributed)
SA_T10_bsa067_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (with Nāgārjuna; attribution and date uncertain)
SA_T10_bsa067_u.summary: A short Madhyamaka treatise on 'transmigration of existence' (bhavasaṃkrānti) that addresses how rebirth and the passage from one existence to another can occur in the absence of any permanent self or transferable entity, explaining karmic continuity in terms of emptiness and dependent origination.
SA_T10_bsa067_u.history: Attributed to Nāgārjuna and associated with the corpus of Madhyamaka logical works; the Bhavasaṃkrānti (sūtra and śāstra) was edited by N. A. Sastri (Adyar Library, 1938). Its authorship and date, like several minor works ascribed to Nāgārjuna, are debated.

SA_T10_cansisy2.title: Śiṣyalekha (alternate)
SA_T10_cansisy2.author: Candragomin
SA_T10_cansisy2.date_estimate: c. 5th-7th c. CE (Candragomin variously dated; this record an alternate version of the text)
SA_T10_cansisy2.summary: An alternate/variant version of Candragomin's 'Letter to a Disciple,' an ornate verse epistle expounding Buddhist doctrine, the sufferings of saṃsāra, and the bodhisattva path. It was addressed to a disciple (traditionally Ratnakīrti) to recall him from worldly attachment back to the religious life.
SA_T10_cansisy2.history: Candragomin, a celebrated Buddhist grammarian and lay poet, composed the Śiṣyalekha, regarded as the most refined of the surviving Buddhist epistles. This entry is a second recension/version of the text; it was edited by I. P. Minaev (1889) and M. Hahn (1998).

SA_T10_cansisyu.title: Śiṣyalekha
SA_T10_cansisyu.author: Candragomin
SA_T10_cansisyu.summary: The 'Letter to a Disciple' is a short, highly ornate kavya in epistolary form that sets out Buddhist teaching on impermanence, the perils of saṃsāra, the rarity of the human birth, and the bodhisattva's compassionate path. It is framed as an exhortation to a disciple who had abandoned his vows for a love affair.
SA_T10_cansisyu.history: Composed by the lay scholar and grammarian Candragomin (dated variously 5th-7th c. CE), it is considered the most refined extant Buddhist epistle. The Sanskrit survives (Cambridge and other manuscripts) and was edited by Minaev and later Michael Hahn.

SA_T10_n2902u.title: Daṇḍikathā
SA_T10_n2902u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T10_n2902u.summary: Apparently a short Buddhist narrative tale (kathā); the title, meaning roughly 'the story of the staff/of Daṇḍin,' suggests an edifying or avadāna-type story, possibly a moral or karmic narrative in the manner of Buddhist story collections. The specific content could not be confidently identified.
SA_T10_n2902u.history: A minor and poorly attested Buddhist Sanskrit text known mainly as a catalogue/corpus entry; its author, date, and precise contents are obscure and were not verifiable from the sources consulted.

SA_T10_n3009u.title: Vimalaratnalekha
SA_T10_n3009u.author: Atiśa Dīpaṅkara Śrījñāna
SA_T10_n3009u.genre: lekha (didactic epistle of moral counsel to a king)
SA_T10_n3009u.summary: The 'Spotless Jewel Letter' is an epistle of ethical and religious guidance addressed to the Pāla king Nayapāla of Magadha, offering a collection of moral maxims rooted in bodhisattva ideals and Buddhist moral philosophy for the conduct of a devout ruler.
SA_T10_n3009u.history: Composed by Atiśa Dīpaṅkara Śrījñāna, the influential 11th-century master who later revitalized Buddhism in Tibet, at the request of King Nayapāla. The Sanskrit original is lost; it survives in Tibetan and has been reconstructed into Sanskrit with translation by modern scholars.

SA_T10_nrat_1_u.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 1
SA_T10_nrat_1_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T10_nrat_1_u.summary: Chapter/section 1 of Nāgārjuna's 'Precious Garland,' the verse advice-to-a-king that blends Madhyamaka philosophy of emptiness with ethical and political counsel. The opening portion lays out the foundations of higher status (abhyudaya) and definite goodness (niḥśreyasa) and the basics of the path.
SA_T10_nrat_1_u.history: Part of the same Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā attributed to Nāgārjuna; this record represents one division of the work as transmitted. The text survives partly in Sanskrit and fully in Tibetan and Chinese.

SA_T10_nrat_2_u.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 2
SA_T10_nrat_2_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T10_nrat_2_u.summary: Chapter/section 2 of Nāgārjuna's 'Precious Garland,' continuing the integrated exposition of Madhyamaka emptiness and the bodhisattva path together with practical counsel to the king on virtuous rule and merit.
SA_T10_nrat_2_u.history: A division of the Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā ascribed to Nāgārjuna, transmitted in Sanskrit fragments and in complete Tibetan and Chinese versions.

SA_T10_nrat_4_u.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 3
SA_T10_nrat_4_u.author: Nāgārjuna
SA_T10_nrat_4_u.summary: A further chapter/section of Nāgārjuna's 'Precious Garland,' developing the union of Madhyamaka philosophy (emptiness, the bodhisattva's accumulation of merit and wisdom) with ethical guidance addressed to a king. (Note: the work_id suffix '_4' alongside the displayed '3' suggests a numbering offset in the corpus.)
SA_T10_nrat_4_u.history: Another transmitted division of Nāgārjuna's Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā, preserved in Sanskrit fragments and in Tibetan and Chinese translation.

SA_T10_subhrk_u.title: Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā
SA_T10_subhrk_u.author: attributed to Āryaśūra (also given as Śūra/Aśvaghoṣa; uncertain)
SA_T10_subhrk_u.summary: The 'Casket of Jewel-like Well-Spoken Verses' is a Buddhist didactic verse collection setting out moral and religious instruction (well-turned aphorisms, subhāṣitas) on virtue, the disadvantages of vice, and the conduct conducive to the Buddhist path. It is a gnomic/edifying work distinct from Vidyākara's later secular anthology of similar name.
SA_T10_subhrk_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Āryaśūra (the title sometimes circulates under Śūra or Aśvaghoṣa), the attribution and date are uncertain. It survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan and belongs to the early Buddhist subhāṣita tradition.

SA_T11_arhebt_u.title: Hetubinduṭīkā
SA_T11_arhebt_u.author: Arcaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa)
SA_T11_arhebt_u.summary: A detailed commentary on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu ('Drop of Reason'), a short treatise on the logical reason/probans (hetu) in Buddhist inference. Arcaṭa expounds the theory of valid inference, the triple-character of the logical reason (trairūpya), and topics such as the inference of momentariness (kṣaṇikatvānumāna).
SA_T11_arhebt_u.history: Composed by the Buddhist logician Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa (8th c.), it is a key link in the post-Dharmakīrti pramāṇa tradition. It was transmitted with the later sub-commentary Āloka of Durveka Miśra and edited together at Baroda in 1949.

SA_T11_arhebt2u.title: Hetubinduṭīkā
SA_T11_arhebt2u.author: Arcaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa)
SA_T11_arhebt2u.summary: A second catalogue record of Arcaṭa's commentary on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu, explicating the Buddhist theory of the logical reason (hetu), the three conditions of a valid probans (trairūpya), and associated epistemological problems.
SA_T11_arhebt2u.history: Same work as the other Hetubinduṭīkā entry in this batch; a major eighth-century commentary in the Buddhist epistemological tradition, edited with Durveka Miśra's sub-commentary Āloka (Baroda, 1949).

SA_T11_bsa001_u.title: Ālambanaparīkṣā
SA_T11_bsa001_u.author: Dignāga
SA_T11_bsa001_u.summary: The 'Investigation of the Object/Percept' is a very short verse treatise (about eight kārikās) arguing that the object of perception cannot be an external atom or aggregate of atoms. Instead, what is cognized and what causes cognition is only an internal mental form, thereby supporting Yogācāra idealism.
SA_T11_bsa001_u.history: A foundational work of Dignāga, the founder of the Buddhist epistemological (pramāṇa) tradition. The Sanskrit survives only in fragments; the text is preserved in Tibetan and in several Chinese translations (e.g., by Paramārtha and Xuanzang) and generated extensive commentary.

SA_T11_bsa002_u.title: Ālambanaparīkṣāvṛtti
SA_T11_bsa002_u.author: Dignāga
SA_T11_bsa002_u.summary: Dignāga's own prose auto-commentary on his Ālambanaparīkṣā, unpacking the verses' arguments that the perceptual object cannot be external (neither atoms nor their aggregates can satisfy the requirements of an object-support, ālambana) and that the genuine object is an internal cognitive form, in support of mind-only (Yogācāra) idealism.
SA_T11_bsa002_u.history: The vṛtti is transmitted together with the kārikās; like the root text it survives only in Sanskrit fragments and is preserved in Tibetan (Derge 4206) and Chinese. It was the focus of refutations by realist opponents such as Śubhagupta.

SA_T11_bsa020_u.title: Vajrasūcī
SA_T11_bsa020_u.author: Aśvaghoṣa (attribution disputed)
SA_T11_bsa020_u.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. CE if by Aśvaghoṣa; attribution and date contested
SA_T11_bsa020_u.summary: The 'Diamond Needle' is a short polemical tract that mounts a vigorous attack on the brahminical caste system, arguing that there is in reality only one undifferentiated varṇa and that birth does not confer the qualities claimed for brahmanhood. It refutes the brahmins using the authority of their own scriptures.
SA_T11_bsa020_u.history: Traditionally ascribed to Aśvaghoṣa (c. 80-150 CE), the attribution is seriously contested (the maṅgala invokes Mañjuśrī, supporting a Buddhist origin, but scholars including Olivelle doubt Aśvaghoṣa's authorship). A closely related Brahmanical recension circulates as the Vajrasūcikā Upaniṣad.

SA_T11_bsa0551u.title: Apohasiddhi
SA_T11_bsa0551u.author: Ratnakīrti
SA_T11_bsa0551u.tradition: Buddhist (Yogācāra / Buddhist epistemology, pramāṇa school of Dignāga–Dharmakīrti)
SA_T11_bsa0551u.genre: philosophical treatise (siddhi / proof text on philosophy of language)
SA_T11_bsa0551u.date_estimate: c. first half of the 11th c. CE (Ratnakīrti fl. c. 1000-1050)
SA_T11_bsa0551u.summary: A concise treatise establishing the Buddhist apoha ('exclusion') theory of meaning, according to which words and concepts do not refer to real universals but pick out their objects negatively, by exclusion of what they are not (e.g. 'cow' = 'not non-cow'). It defends Dignāga's and Dharmakīrti's nominalist semantics against realist (Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika and Mīmāṃsā) accounts of universals and verbal reference.
SA_T11_bsa0551u.history: Ratnakīrti was a student of Jñānaśrīmitra at Vikramaśīla and one of the last great figures of the Indian Buddhist logico-epistemological tradition; his short 'siddhi' tracts survive in the Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī collection. The Apohasiddhi distills and systematizes Jñānaśrīmitra's longer Apohaprakaraṇa and is a key source for the mature Buddhist theory of exclusion.

SA_T11_bsa0552u.title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi
SA_T11_bsa0552u.author: Ratnakīrti
SA_T11_bsa0552u.date_estimate: c. first half of the 11th c. CE (Ratnakīrti fl. c. 1000-1050)
SA_T11_bsa0552u.summary: A proof of the doctrine of universal momentariness (kṣaṇabhaṅga): the thesis that whatever exists is causally efficient and therefore arises and perishes in a single instant, so that all phenomena are radically impermanent. Ratnakīrti develops the argument from existence/causal efficacy (sattvānumāna) against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika and Mīmāṃsā defenders of enduring substances and selves.
SA_T11_bsa0552u.history: Part of the Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī, the text exists in two complementary recensions, one arguing by positive correlation (anvayātmikā) and one by negative correlation (vyatirekātmikā). It is a culmination of centuries of Buddhist–Nyāya debate over momentariness initiated by Dharmakīrti and is widely studied in modern scholarship (Feldman & Phillips translation).

SA_T11_bsa0553u.title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi vyatirekātmikā
SA_T11_bsa0553u.author: Ratnakīrti
SA_T11_bsa0553u.date_estimate: c. first half of the 11th c. CE (Ratnakīrti fl. c. 1000-1050)
SA_T11_bsa0553u.summary: The 'negative-correlation' recension of Ratnakīrti's proof of momentariness, which establishes that all existents are momentary by way of contraposition/negative concomitance (vyatireka): if something were non-momentary it could not be causally efficient and hence could not exist. It complements the anvayātmikā (positive-correlation) version of the same proof.
SA_T11_bsa0553u.history: This forms a pair with the anvaya version within the Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī, reflecting the standard Buddhist logical method of proving a thesis through both positive (anvaya) and negative (vyatireka) pervasion. It belongs to the late Vikramaśīla phase of Buddhist pramāṇa scholarship and draws on Jñānaśrīmitra's work on momentariness.

SA_T11_bsa0554u.title: Avayavinirākaraṇa
SA_T11_bsa0554u.author: Paṇḍita Aśoka
SA_T11_bsa0554u.summary: A short polemical treatise ('Refutation of the Whole') denying the reality of the avayavin, the partite whole that Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika realists posit as a distinct substance inhering in and over its parts. Paṇḍita Aśoka argues that the alleged whole is unperceived and incoherent, so that only the atoms/parts (or momentary particulars) are real, supporting the Buddhist reductionist and nominalist ontology.
SA_T11_bsa0554u.history: Paṇḍita Aśoka was a Buddhist logician of the Dignāga–Dharmakīrti tradition, roughly contemporary with Jñānaśrīmitra and Ratnakīrti. The Sanskrit text survives and has been edited and translated (e.g. by Tola and Dragonetti); together with his Sāmānyadūṣaṇa it represents a sharp later-Buddhist attack on Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika realism about wholes and universals.

SA_T11_bsa0555u.title: Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā
SA_T11_bsa0555u.author: Paṇḍita Aśoka
SA_T11_bsa0555u.summary: A treatise refuting the reality of universals (sāmānya), often cited simply as the Sāmānyadūṣaṇa ('Refutation of the Universal'); the fuller title indicates it 'opens up the directions' of (i.e. lays out) the critique. Inspired by Dignāga and Dharmakīrti, Paṇḍita Aśoka ridicules the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika and Mīmāṃsā doctrine of real, permanent, omnipresent universals and upholds the Buddhist view that only unique particulars (svalakṣaṇa) are real.
SA_T11_bsa0555u.history: A companion piece to Paṇḍita Aśoka's Avayavinirākaraṇa, transmitted among the later Buddhist Nyāya tracts and edited in modern collections of Buddhist Sanskrit logical texts. It is a representative document of mature Buddhist nominalism (apoha-based) directed against realism about universals.

SA_T11_bsa0556u.title: Antarvyāptisamarthana
SA_T11_bsa0556u.author: Ratnākaraśānti
SA_T11_bsa0556u.date_estimate: c. early 11th c. CE (Ratnākaraśānti fl. c. 970-1045)
SA_T11_bsa0556u.summary: A technical logical treatise 'establishing internal pervasion' (antarvyāpti), arguing that the invariable concomitance (vyāpti) between reason and what is to be proved is grasped through the conceptual relation of the terms themselves rather than requiring an external corroborating instance (bahirvyāpti / sapakṣa). This refines Buddhist inference theory by minimizing reliance on positive examples.
SA_T11_bsa0556u.history: Ratnākaraśānti was a celebrated scholar and gatekeeper at Vikramaśīla in the early 11th century, with some forty works preserved in the Tibetan Tengyur. The Antarvyāptisamarthana (edited and studied by Y. Kajiyama) is his principal surviving Sanskrit contribution to pramāṇa logic and influenced later debates on the basis of pervasion.

SA_T11_bsa057_u.title: Nyāyabindu Prakaraṇakārikā
SA_T11_bsa057_u.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_bsa057_u.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā / aphoristic primer on logic and epistemology)
SA_T11_bsa057_u.summary: A short manual ('Drop of Logic') systematically presenting Dharmakīrti's theory of the two valid means of knowledge: perception (pratyakṣa), defined as non-conceptual and non-erroneous, and inference (anumāna), divided into inference for oneself and for others. It also treats the logical 'mark' (liṅga), its threefold conditions, and the fallacies of inference and proof statements.
SA_T11_bsa057_u.history: The Nyāyabindu is the most compact of Dharmakīrti's seven treatises on pramāṇa and became the standard introductory text of the school. It attracted major commentaries, above all Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā, and was transmitted in both Sanskrit and Tibetan; the 'prakaraṇakārikā' label marks it as a self-standing aphoristic compendium.

SA_T11_bsa059_u.title: Santānāntarasiddhi
SA_T11_bsa059_u.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_bsa059_u.summary: A treatise 'establishing the existence of other mind-streams' (santānāntara), i.e. addressing the problem of other minds from within an idealist (Vijñānavāda) framework. Dharmakīrti argues that, even if external objects are denied, one can infer the existence of other conscious continua from observed purposive behaviour and speech, which require a mental cause analogous to one's own.
SA_T11_bsa059_u.history: One of Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa works, the Santānāntarasiddhi survives chiefly in Tibetan translation (the full Sanskrit being lost or fragmentary) and was glossed by Vinītadeva. It is a classic locus for the Buddhist Yogācāra response to the solipsism objection.

SA_T11_bsa061_u.title: Santānāntarasiddhiṭīkā
SA_T11_bsa061_u.author: Vinītadeva
SA_T11_bsa061_u.summary: A commentary (ṭīkā) on Dharmakīrti's Santānāntarasiddhi, expounding and defending his proof of the existence of other minds from a Yogācāra-idealist standpoint. It elucidates the inference from purposive behaviour to other cognitive continua and answers objections to the doctrine.
SA_T11_bsa061_u.history: Vinītadeva was an eighth-century commentator at Nālandā who wrote ṭīkās on most of Dharmakīrti's treatises; his works survive mainly in Tibetan translation. This commentary is the principal early exegesis of the Santānāntarasiddhi and an important witness to its interpretation.

SA_T11_bsa063_u.title: Sambandhaparīkṣā
SA_T11_bsa063_u.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_bsa063_u.summary: An 'Examination of Relation', a short verse work with Dharmakīrti's own prose commentary (vṛtti) analysing the notion of relation (sambandha) such as cause-effect and inherence. Dharmakīrti argues that real, mind-independent relations are untenable: relations are conceptual superimpositions on momentary, discrete particulars, so what appears as connection is mentally constructed.
SA_T11_bsa063_u.history: One of the seven treatises of Dharmakīrti's pramāṇa corpus, transmitted with his autocommentary and commented on by Vinītadeva and others; it survives in Sanskrit fragments and Tibetan. It underpins the Buddhist critique of realist categories of relation and is foundational for the school's nominalist ontology.

SA_T11_bsa064_u.title: Vādanyāya
SA_T11_bsa064_u.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_bsa064_u.summary: A treatise on the rules and logic of formal debate ('The Logic of Debate'), defining what counts as a legitimate point of defeat (nigrahasthāna) for proponent and respondent. Dharmakīrti reworks and criticizes the Nyāya theory of debate, reducing the genuine grounds of defeat essentially to failing to state a correct proof or to expose a real fault.
SA_T11_bsa064_u.history: The last of Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa treatises, the Vādanyāya is preserved in Sanskrit and commented on by Śāntarakṣita (Vipañcitārthā). It is the school's mature statement on dialectics and a sustained polemic against the Nyāya account of debate procedure.

SA_T11_bsa066_u.title: Nyāyapraveśapravṛtti
SA_T11_bsa066_u.author: Haribhadrasūri
SA_T11_bsa066_u.tradition: Jain (Śvetāmbara) commentary on a Buddhist logical text
SA_T11_bsa066_u.date_estimate: 8th c. CE (Haribhadrasūri, c. 700-799 per the hint; traditionally 8th c.)
SA_T11_bsa066_u.summary: A commentary (vṛtti/pravṛtti) by the Jain scholar Haribhadrasūri on the Nyāyapraveśa, the short Buddhist primer of Dignāga-style logic. It explains the elements of inference and proof statement—thesis, reason, example, and their fallacies—and the means of knowledge, glossing the base text for students.
SA_T11_bsa066_u.history: The Nyāyapraveśa ('Introduction to Logic') is generally ascribed to Śaṅkarasvāmin, a pupil of Dignāga (sometimes attributed to Dignāga himself). Remarkably, the Jain Haribhadrasūri wrote a commentary on this Buddhist manual, attesting to its wide use as a logic textbook across sectarian lines; it was edited by A. B. Dhruva together with Pārśvadeva's gloss.

SA_T11_bsa069_u.title: Tarkaśāstra
SA_T11_bsa069_u.author: anonymous/traditional (sometimes attributed to Vasubandhu or his school)
SA_T11_bsa069_u.summary: An early Buddhist treatise on logic and debate in three chapters: the five members of the syllogism (proposition, reason, example, application, conclusion); the futile rejoinders (jāti); and the points of defeat (nigrahasthāna). It is concerned more with the rules of dialectical disputation (vāda) than with epistemology proper.
SA_T11_bsa069_u.history: The Tarkaśāstra survives only in Chinese translation; the Sanskrit is lost and was retranslated by G. Tucci in his 'Pre-Diṅnāga Buddhist Texts on Logic from Chinese Sources.' It represents the formative, pre-Dignāga phase of Buddhist logic, before Dignāga reformed the syllogism into three members and grounded inference in a developed theory of pervasion.

SA_T11_bsa070_u.title: Upāyahṛdaya
SA_T11_bsa070_u.author: anonymous/traditional (sometimes attributed to Nāgārjuna, disputed)
SA_T11_bsa070_u.summary: An early Buddhist manual on the art of debate ('Heart of the Means'), surveying the means of knowledge (it refers to the four pramāṇas: perception, inference, comparison, and testimony), the components of argument, examples, and the techniques and faults of disputation. It is a practical guide to winning and conducting philosophical debates.
SA_T11_bsa070_u.history: Like the Tarkaśāstra, the Upāyahṛdaya is preserved only in Chinese and was retranslated into Sanskrit by G. Tucci in his collection of pre-Diṅnāga Buddhist logical texts. Its attribution to Nāgārjuna is contested; it is significant as a witness to Buddhist debate theory before Dignāga's reforms.

SA_T11_bsa071_u.title: Hetubindu
SA_T11_bsa071_u.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_bsa071_u.summary: A treatise ('Drop of Reason') focused on the theory of the logical reason or evidence (hetu), classifying the valid types of inferential mark into the non-perception of something perceptible (anupalabdhi), identity/essential property (svabhāva), and effect (kārya). It sets out the conditions a reason must satisfy to yield valid inference and analyses the corresponding fallacies.
SA_T11_bsa071_u.history: One of Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa works, the Hetubindu survives in Sanskrit fragments and Tibetan and was commented on by Arcaṭa (Hetubinduṭīkā) and Vinītadeva. It is a core source for the Buddhist threefold typology of valid evidence.

SA_T11_bsa072_u.title: Tarkabhāṣā
SA_T11_bsa072_u.author: Mokṣākaragupta
SA_T11_bsa072_u.genre: philosophical treatise (manual / digest of logic and epistemology)
SA_T11_bsa072_u.date_estimate: c. late 11th-12th c. CE (Mokṣākaragupta fl. between 1050 and 1202)
SA_T11_bsa072_u.summary: A late systematic manual ('Language of Logic') of Buddhist epistemology and logic, expounding the two valid means of knowledge—perception and inference—the threefold logical reason, debate, and the apoha theory of meaning. It serves as a lucid late-period summary of the whole Dignāga–Dharmakīrti system.
SA_T11_bsa072_u.history: Mokṣākaragupta was a scholar of the Jagaddala monastery in Varendra (Bengal); his dates are fixed between c. 1050 and 1202 partly from his being cited in the Jain Syādvādamañjarī. The Bauddha-Tarkabhāṣā is one of the finest and last comprehensive Buddhist logic textbooks, translated and studied notably by Y. Kajiyama. (Not to be confused with the Naiyāyika Keśava Miśra's Tarkabhāṣā.)

SA_T11_bsa075_u.title: Nibandhāvalī
SA_T11_bsa075_u.author: Ratnakīrti
SA_T11_bsa075_u.date_estimate: c. first half of the 11th c. CE (Ratnakīrti fl. c. 1000-1050; hint 11th c.)
SA_T11_bsa075_u.summary: The Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī is the collected corpus of the short philosophical tracts (nibandhas) of Ratnakīrti, including the Apohasiddhi, the two Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi recensions, the Sthirasiddhidūṣaṇa, the Īśvarasādhanadūṣaṇa, the Citrādvaitaprakāśavāda, the Santānāntaradūṣaṇa, and others. Together they argue for momentariness, apoha semantics, idealism, and against God, enduring substances, and other minds, from the late Buddhist epistemological standpoint.
SA_T11_bsa075_u.history: These tracts, gathered as the Nibandhāvalī, were critically edited by Anantalal Thakur (Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series) and are a principal source for the final, highly refined phase of Indian Buddhist philosophy at Vikramaśīla under Jñānaśrīmitra's lineage. They have been intensively studied in modern scholarship (e.g. Parimal Patil, McAllister).

SA_T11_dhkhetuu.title: Hetubindu
SA_T11_dhkhetuu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhkhetuu.summary: A treatise ('Drop of Reason') on the theory of the valid logical reason (hetu), classifying inferential evidence into non-perception (anupalabdhi), essential identity (svabhāva), and effect (kārya), and specifying the conditions and fallacies of valid inference. (This is a duplicate record of Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu, likely from a different edition/source than T11bsa071_u.)
SA_T11_dhkhetuu.history: One of Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa works, surviving in Sanskrit fragments and Tibetan, with major commentaries by Arcaṭa and Vinītadeva. It is foundational for the Buddhist account of valid evidence.

SA_T11_dhknyayu.title: Nyāyabindu
SA_T11_dhknyayu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhknyayu.genre: philosophical treatise (aphoristic manual of logic and epistemology)
SA_T11_dhknyayu.summary: The 'Drop of Logic', Dharmakīrti's compact manual presenting the two valid means of knowledge—non-conceptual, non-erroneous perception (pratyakṣa) and inference (anumāna), for oneself and for others—together with the theory of the threefold logical mark and the fallacies of reason and proof statement. It is the most concise distillation of his epistemology.
SA_T11_dhknyayu.history: The Nyāyabindu became the standard textbook of the Buddhist logical school and the object of Dharmottara's celebrated Nyāyabinduṭīkā, transmitted in both Sanskrit and Tibetan and central to the study of Buddhist pramāṇa down to the present.

SA_T11_dhkprviu.title: Pramāṇaviniścaya
SA_T11_dhkprviu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhkprviu.summary: The 'Ascertainment of the Means of Knowledge', a major treatise in three chapters on perception, inference-for-oneself, and inference-for-others, partly restating and condensing the Pramāṇavārttika in verse with autocommentary. It gives Dharmakīrti's mature, self-revised account of valid cognition, the apoha theory, and the logic of inference.
SA_T11_dhkprviu.history: One of Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa works and second in importance only to the Pramāṇavārttika; long known mainly through Tibetan, substantial Sanskrit has been recovered and critically edited (Steinkellner and the Vienna/Beijing project). It was commented on by Dharmottara and was highly influential in both India and Tibet.

SA_T11_dhkprvku.title: Pramāṇavārttikakārikā
SA_T11_dhkprvku.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhkprvku.tradition: Buddhist (pramāṇavāda / Dignāga–Dharmakīrti epistemological school, with Yogācāra–Sautrāntika commitments)
SA_T11_dhkprvku.summary: The verse-text (kārikā) of Dharmakīrti's magnum opus on valid cognition (pramāṇa), comprising roughly 2,000 stanzas in four chapters: inference-for-oneself (svārthānumāna), establishment of the means of knowledge / the Buddha as authority (pramāṇasiddhi), perception (pratyakṣa), and inference-for-others / proof (parārthānumāna). It defines perception as non-conceptual and non-erroneous and inference as grounded in necessary (causal or identity) relations.
SA_T11_dhkprvku.history: Composed as an expansion of and commentary on Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya, the work became the foundational text of Buddhist epistemology and generated a vast commentarial tradition in India and Tibet. The kārikā verses are often transmitted and studied separately from Dharmakīrti's own and others' prose commentaries.

SA_T11_dhkpvsvu.title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti [Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti]
SA_T11_dhkpvsvu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhkpvsvu.summary: Dharmakīrti's own prose autocommentary (svavṛtti) on the first chapter of the Pramāṇavārttika, the chapter on inference-for-oneself (svārthānumāna). It expounds in detail his theories of the logical reason (hetu), necessary relations (vyāpti), the apoha (exclusion) theory of meaning and concept-formation, and the refutation of rival semantic and inferential theories.
SA_T11_dhkpvsvu.history: Dharmakīrti wrote a self-commentary only on the svārthānumāna chapter; the surviving Sanskrit was edited by Gnoli (1960). It is among the most studied Sanskrit sources for his philosophy of language and was elaborated by Śākyabuddhi and Karṇakagomin.

SA_T11_dhksparu.title: Sambandhaparīkṣā
SA_T11_dhksparu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhksparu.summary: A short treatise (about 25 verses with Dharmakīrti's own vṛtti) examining the notion of 'relation' (sambandha). Dharmakīrti argues that real relations such as conjunction or inherence cannot exist between distinct momentary entities, so relations are conceptual constructions superimposed on causally connected particulars.
SA_T11_dhksparu.history: One of Dharmakīrti's seven treatises (the 'seven works on pramāṇa'), it survives in Sanskrit fragments and Tibetan translation and was commented on by Vinītadeva and others. It is important for Buddhist nominalism about relations and the critique of Nyāya–Vaiśeṣika categories.

SA_T11_dhkvadnu.title: Vādanyāya
SA_T11_dhkvadnu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhkvadnu.genre: philosophical treatise on debate (vāda) and dialectics
SA_T11_dhkvadnu.summary: A treatise on the rules and logic of formal philosophical debate, defining the legitimate grounds for defeat (nigrahasthāna) in disputation. Dharmakīrti criticizes the Nyāya analysis of debate, reducing valid points of defeat essentially to stating a non-establishing reason or failing to state a sound one, and rejects many of the traditional Naiyāyika categories of defeat.
SA_T11_dhkvadnu.history: The last of Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa works, it survives in Sanskrit and was commented on by Vinītadeva and Śāntarakṣita (Vipañcitārthā). It is a key source for Buddhist debate theory and the polemic against the Nyāyasūtra tradition.

SA_T11_dhnyabpu.title: Nyāyabinduprakaraṇa
SA_T11_dhnyabpu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_dhnyabpu.genre: philosophical treatise / manual (prakaraṇa) on logic and epistemology
SA_T11_dhnyabpu.summary: A compact prose manual ('A Drop of Logic') in three chapters covering perception (pratyakṣa), inference-for-oneself (svārthānumāna), and inference-for-others (parārthānumāna). It distills Dharmakīrti's epistemology into an accessible introductory form, defining valid cognition, the three marks of a sound logical reason, and the types of fallacies.
SA_T11_dhnyabpu.history: One of the most widely studied of Dharmakīrti's seven works, it survives complete in Sanskrit and is famous through Dharmottara's commentary (Nyāyabinduṭīkā) and Stcherbatsky's English translation in 'Buddhist Logic'. It served as a standard introduction to Buddhist logic.

SA_T11_jithettu.title: Hetutattvopadeśa
SA_T11_jithettu.author: Jitāri (Jetāri)
SA_T11_jithettu.tradition: Buddhist (Dignāga–Dharmakīrti epistemological school; classed by Tibetans as Yogācāra-Svātantrika-Madhyamaka)
SA_T11_jithettu.date_estimate: c. 940-1000 CE (latter half of the 10th c.)
SA_T11_jithettu.summary: A short instructional treatise ('Teaching on the True Nature of the Logical Reason') on the theory of the valid inferential reason (hetu) and its threefold characteristic, together with the types of correct and fallacious reasons. It is a concise pedagogical text within the post-Dharmakīrti logical tradition.
SA_T11_jithettu.history: Jitāri was a prolific Buddhist scholar at Vikramaśīla and a teacher of Atiśa. The Hetutattvopadeśa survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan and was used as an introductory logic primer; multiple recensions/editions exist (hence the two work-IDs in this batch).

SA_T11_jithetuu.title: Hetutattvopadeśa
SA_T11_jithetuu.author: Jitāri (Jetāri)
SA_T11_jithetuu.tradition: Buddhist (Dignāga–Dharmakīrti epistemological school; classed by Tibetans as Yogācāra-Svātantrika-Madhyamaka)
SA_T11_jithetuu.date_estimate: c. 940-1000 CE (latter half of the 10th c.)
SA_T11_jithetuu.summary: A duplicate record (alternate edition/recension) of Jitāri's 'Teaching on the True Nature of the Logical Reason', a concise manual on the threefold mark of the valid inferential reason and the classification of sound and fallacious reasons in the Dharmakīrtian tradition.
SA_T11_jithetuu.history: See the companion entry (T11jithettu): Jitāri was a 10th-century Vikramaśīla scholar and teacher of Atiśa; this work is a short logic primer surviving in Sanskrit and Tibetan. The two IDs likely reflect two editions of the same text.

SA_T11_jnaniva.title: Nibandhāvalī
SA_T11_jnaniva.author: Jñānaśrīmitra
SA_T11_jnaniva.tradition: Buddhist (Yogācāra and pramāṇavāda / late Indian epistemological school)
SA_T11_jnaniva.summary: The 'Garland of Treatises' is the collected corpus of Jñānaśrīmitra's philosophical monographs, including works on momentariness (Kṣaṇabhaṅgādhyāya), exclusion/apoha (Apohaprakaraṇa), pervasion (Vyāpticarcā), non-perception (Anupalabdhirahasya), the means of valid cognition, and the reality of the external world. The essays defend Dharmakīrtian positions against Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā critics.
SA_T11_jnaniva.history: Jñānaśrīmitra was a leading scholar at Vikramaśīla and teacher of Ratnakīrti. The Nibandhāvalī was edited by Anantalal Thakur (Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series, Patna, 1959) as a single collection of his Buddhist nyāya works; it is a principal source for late Indian Buddhist epistemology.

SA_T11_kgpramv.title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti Commentary [Pramāṇavārttikavṛttiṭīkā]
SA_T11_kgpramv.author: Karṇakagomin
SA_T11_kgpramv.summary: A detailed Sanskrit sub-commentary on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti (the autocommentary on the first, svārthānumāna chapter). It closely follows and expands Śākyabuddhi's commentary, explicating Dharmakīrti's logic, the apoha theory, and his arguments against rival schools.
SA_T11_kgpramv.history: Karṇakagomin's ṭīkā survives in Sanskrit (edited by Rāhula Sāṅkṛtyāyana) and is, with Śākyabuddhi, a crucial witness for reconstructing the text of the Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti. Karṇakagomin is conventionally placed in the 8th-9th century; the '40-120 CE' figure seen in some web sources is erroneous.

SA_T11_n1226u.title: Vādanyāyaṭīkā
SA_T11_n1226u.author: Vinītadeva
SA_T11_n1226u.summary: Vinītadeva's commentary on Dharmakīrti's Vādanyāya, explicating the rules of debate and the grounds of defeat (nigrahasthāna) and clarifying Dharmakīrti's critique of the Nyāya theory of disputation.
SA_T11_n1226u.history: Vinītadeva was a prolific commentator who annotated several of Dharmakīrti's seven works as well as Vasubandhu, Sthiramati, and Dignāga. Most of his commentaries survive primarily in Tibetan translation. The work-ID number (1226) corresponds to the Bibliotheca Indica 'New Series No. 1226' provenance used by this project.

SA_T11_n1238u.title: Jātinirākṛti
SA_T11_n1238u.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in some editions to Dharmakīrti or his school)
SA_T11_n1238u.summary: A short polemical tract, 'Refutation of the Universal' (jāti), arguing against the realist (Nyāya–Vaiśeṣika and Mīmāṃsā) doctrine of real, eternal universals (sāmānya/jāti). In line with the apoha theory it holds that words and concepts do not denote positive universals but operate by exclusion of what is other.
SA_T11_n1238u.history: The text is one of the 'Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts' edited by Haraprasad Shastri (Bibliotheca Indica, 1910). Its authorship is uncertain — it is sometimes ascribed to Dharmakīrti or treated as a school product; hence it is best given as anonymous within the Dharmakīrtian tradition.
related_works: Six Buddhist Nyaya Tracts (ed. Haraprasad Shastri)

SA_T11_n1242u.title: Nyāyabinduṭīkāṭippaṇī
SA_T11_n1242u.author: anonymous
SA_T11_n1242u.date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (after Dharmottara, fl. c. 740-800)
SA_T11_n1242u.summary: An anonymous set of glosses (ṭippaṇī) on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā, which is itself a commentary on Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu. It clarifies points of Dharmottara's exposition of perception, inference, and logical reasons.
SA_T11_n1242u.history: The text was edited by Th. Stcherbatsky and published in the Bibliotheca Buddhica series (1909). It belongs to the layered commentarial tradition on the Nyāyabindu (Dharmakīrti → Dharmottara → ṭippaṇī).

SA_T11_n2848u.title: Bauddha manīṣā - Bauddheṣu Pramāṇamīmāṃsā
SA_T11_n2848u.genre: modern scholarly study / anthology on Buddhist epistemology (likely)
SA_T11_n2848u.date_estimate: modern (20th c.) — uncertain; title not securely identified
SA_T11_n2848u.summary: The title translates roughly as 'Buddhist Thought — Investigation of the Means of Knowledge among the Buddhists', i.e. a treatment of pramāṇa (epistemology) in the Buddhist tradition. It appears to be a modern Sanskrit-medium study or anthology surveying Buddhist theories of valid cognition (perception and inference) rather than a single classical work.
SA_T11_n2848u.history: Could not be securely identified with a specific classical text or named author in available sources; the phrasing ('Bauddha manīṣā') is characteristic of modern Sanskrit secondary literature on Indian philosophy. Treated as low confidence pending a positive catalogue identification.

SA_T11_n2859u.title: Anupalabdhirahasya
SA_T11_n2859u.author: Jñānaśrīmitra
SA_T11_n2859u.tradition: Buddhist (Yogācāra and pramāṇavāda / late Indian epistemological school)
SA_T11_n2859u.genre: philosophical treatise (prakaraṇa, part of the Nibandhāvalī)
SA_T11_n2859u.summary: The 'Secret of Non-Perception' is a treatise on anupalabdhi — the inference of the absence (abhāva) of a perceptible thing from its non-perception. Jñānaśrīmitra refines Dharmakīrti's theory of how non-cognition can serve as a valid means of knowing absence, against rival accounts of negation.
SA_T11_n2859u.history: One of the monographs forming Jñānaśrīmitra's Nibandhāvalī. It was critically edited and studied (with the Sarvaśabdābhāvacarcā) by Birgit Kellner, who surveyed his anupalabdhi-theory. It is a major late-Buddhist contribution to the epistemology of negation.

SA_T11_n2992u.title: Viśālāmalavatī Pramāṇasamuccayaṭīkā
SA_T11_n2992u.author: Jinendrabuddhi
SA_T11_n2992u.date_estimate: c. 2nd half of the 8th c. CE (Jinendrabuddhi, c. 710-770)
SA_T11_n2992u.summary: The 'Spotless and Extensive' commentary on Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya (with its vṛtti), explicating Dignāga's foundational treatise on perception and inference. It is the only known commentary on the Pramāṇasamuccaya and a vital aid to reconstructing Dignāga's otherwise Sanskrit-lost text.
SA_T11_n2992u.history: Jinendrabuddhi was an 8th-century scholar; his ṭīkā has been recovered in Sanskrit manuscripts and critically edited (Chapters 1-2) in the Sanskrit Texts from the Tibetan Autonomous Region series by Steinkellner, Krasser, Lasic, and others. It is indispensable for the study of Dignāga.

SA_T11_n634u.title: Nyāyapraveśakasūtra
SA_T11_n634u.author: Śaṅkarasvāmin (traditionally; sometimes ascribed to Dignāga)
SA_T11_n634u.date_estimate: c. 6th c. CE (after Dignāga, before Dharmakīrti)
SA_T11_n634u.summary: A brief 'Introduction to Logic' presenting the core elements of Dignāga's system: the members of a proof (thesis, reason, example), the three characteristics of a valid reason, the fallacies of thesis, reason, and example, perception and inference, and refutation. It is a compact primer for engaging in Buddhist logical debate.
SA_T11_n634u.history: Most commonly attributed to Dignāga's pupil Śaṅkarasvāmin, though the Tibetan colophon and some scholars assign it to Dignāga himself. Extremely influential in East Asia (as the Yinming ru zhengli lun, basis of the Chinese/Japanese yinming/inmyō logic tradition) and preserved in Sanskrit, Chinese, and Tibetan.

SA_T11_pramanvu.title: Pramāṇavārttika
SA_T11_pramanvu.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_pramanvu.genre: philosophical treatise (verse epistemology, with autocommentary on ch. 1)
SA_T11_pramanvu.summary: Dharmakīrti's magnum opus, the 'Commentary on Valid Cognition', in four chapters: inference-for-oneself, establishment of valid cognition / the Buddha's authority, perception, and inference-for-others. It is the most comprehensive statement of Buddhist epistemology, logic, philosophy of language (apoha), and the proof of Buddhist soteriological claims.
SA_T11_pramanvu.history: Conceived as an expansion of Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya, it became the central text of the Buddhist pramāṇa tradition and was commented on by Devendrabuddhi, Śākyabuddhi, Karṇakagomin, Prajñākaragupta, Manorathanandin, and many Tibetan authors. (This record likely denotes the whole work, complementing the separate kārikā and svavṛtti entries.)
