SA_T11_prpva.title: Pramāṇavārttikālaṃkāra
SA_T11_prpva.author: Prajñākaragupta
SA_T11_prpva.summary: The 'Ornament of the Pramāṇavārttika', a vast commentary (over 16,000 ślokas) on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika, beginning with the pramāṇasiddhi chapter. Prajñākaragupta develops a distinctive and sometimes innovative reading of Dharmakīrti, treating topics such as the proof of the Buddha as authority, causality, and momentariness.
SA_T11_prpva.history: Also called Pramāṇavārttikabhāṣya, it survives in a single Sanskrit manuscript and in Tibetan translation. Prajñākaragupta founded an influential interpretive lineage continued by Jayanta and Yamāri, and was studied closely by Jñānaśrīmitra and Ratnakīrti.

SA_T11_ratksanu.title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi Vyatirekātmikā
SA_T11_ratksanu.author: Ratnakīrti
SA_T11_ratksanu.tradition: Buddhist (Yogācāra and pramāṇavāda / late Indian epistemological school)
SA_T11_ratksanu.genre: philosophical treatise (prakaraṇa, part of the Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī)
SA_T11_ratksanu.summary: The 'Proof of Momentariness via the Negative Concomitance', one of two complementary proofs (the other being anvayātmikā, positive correlation) that Ratnakīrti gives for the doctrine that all existents are momentary (kṣaṇika). The vyatireka form argues that whatever is not momentary cannot have causal efficacy and hence cannot exist, defending universal momentariness against Nyāya critics.
SA_T11_ratksanu.history: Ratnakīrti, a pupil of Jñānaśrīmitra at Vikramaśīla, condensed and sharpened his teacher's arguments. The text was edited in the Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts (Haraprasad Shastri) and the Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī (A. Thakur); it was translated and studied by A. C. Senape McDermott.

SA_T11_rvadrahu.title: Vādarahasya [Udayananirākaraṇa]
SA_T11_rvadrahu.author: Ratnakīrti (attributed; uncertain)
SA_T11_rvadrahu.date_estimate: c. 11th c. CE (if by Ratnakīrti); attribution uncertain
SA_T11_rvadrahu.summary: A 'Secret of Debate', sub-titled here as a refutation of Udayana (Udayananirākaraṇa), i.e. a Buddhist rebuttal of the great Nyāya theist Udayana's arguments — particularly his proofs for God (Īśvara) and his critiques of Buddhist momentariness and idealism. The work defends Buddhist epistemological and ontological positions against Naiyāyika theism.
SA_T11_rvadrahu.history: The attribution to Ratnakīrti is marked as uncertain in the project metadata and is debated; Udayana (fl. c. 1000 CE) was a contemporary or near-contemporary of Ratnakīrti, so a Buddhist refutation of him fits the late-11th-century Vikramaśīla milieu. Could not fully confirm the work's authorship from available sources, hence reduced confidence.

SA_T11_samtasi.title: Saṃtānāntarasiddhi
SA_T11_samtasi.author: Dharmakīrti
SA_T11_samtasi.summary: A short treatise ('Proof of the Existence of Other Mindstreams/Continua') addressing the Buddhist version of the problem of other minds. Dharmakīrti argues that knowledge of other sentient beings' minds is inferred from observed bodily movements and speech that one knows, by analogy with one's own case, to be caused by intention; he further argues that the Yogācāra idealist can justify belief in other minds by the same argument from analogy that the realist uses.
SA_T11_samtasi.history: One of Dharmakīrti's two minor works (alongside the Sambandhaparīkṣā), difficult to place within the sequence of his seven epistemological treatises. The Sanskrit is largely lost; the text survives chiefly in Tibetan translation and was the subject of a commentary by Vinītadeva.

SA_T11_sbpramvu.title: Pramāṇavārttikaṭīkā
SA_T11_sbpramvu.author: Śākyabuddhi
SA_T11_sbpramvu.summary: A detailed sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika, the foundational verse-text of Buddhist epistemology and logic. Śākyabuddhi glosses Dharmakīrti's arguments on valid cognition (pramāṇa), perception, inference, the apoha theory of meaning, the unreality of universals, and debate technique against Nyāya and Brahmanical opponents.
SA_T11_sbpramvu.history: Śākyabuddhi (often dated c. 700 CE) was an important early exegete of Dharmakīrti; his ṭīkā was in turn drawn upon by Karṇakagomin. The Sanskrit survives only in fragments (e.g. National Archives Kathmandu manuscripts transcribed in 1992); the full text is preserved in Tibetan translation.

SA_T12_n2957u.title: Vṛttamālāstuti
SA_T12_n2957u.author: Jñānaśrīmitra
SA_T12_n2957u.tradition: Buddhist (Mahāyāna; author of the pramāṇa tradition)
SA_T12_n2957u.summary: A 'Garland-of-Metres Hymn': a praise-poem to the Bodhisattva Mañjuśrī composed across roughly 150 different Sanskrit metres, where each stanza simultaneously praises Mañjuśrī and exemplifies (and in effect defines) the metre in which it is cast. It thus serves both as devotional stotra and as a practical handbook of Sanskrit prosody.
SA_T12_n2957u.history: Jñānaśrīmitra was a celebrated Buddhist philosopher-poet and dvāra-paṇḍita of Vikramaśīla monastery. The work was transmitted with Śākyarakṣita's commentary (Vṛttamālā(stuti)vivṛti) and has been critically edited by Michael Hahn, S. S. Bahulkar, and others.

SA_T12_n801u.title: Chandoratnākara
SA_T12_n801u.author: Ratnākaraśānti (Śāntipa)
SA_T12_n801u.summary: An 'Ocean of Metres', a treatise on Sanskrit (and Prakrit) prosody that describes metres and sets out combinatorial algorithms (pratyaya) for enumerating metrical patterns, including binomial-type enumeration. It treats both classical metres and Prakrit/Apabhraṃśa material.
SA_T12_n801u.history: Composed by Ratnākaraśānti, an 11th-century Buddhist master of Vikramaśīla. Conceived partly as a reworking in the tradition of Piṅgala's Chandaḥsūtra, it was influential on prosody in Nepal and on the Buddhist metrical culture transmitted to Tibet.

SA_T13_bghsic_u.title: Siddhārthacaritrakāvya
SA_T13_bghsic_u.author: Buddhaghoṣa (Buddhaghoṣa Thera, modern Sri Lankan author)
SA_T13_bghsic_u.genre: mahākāvya (court epic poem) on the life of the Buddha
SA_T13_bghsic_u.summary: A Sanskrit kāvya narrating the life-story (caritra) of Siddhārtha Gautama, the future Buddha. As a 'Siddhārtha-conduct poem' it recounts in ornate verse the Buddha's birth, renunciation, awakening and career.
SA_T13_bghsic_u.history: Attributed to a Buddhaghoṣa who is not the famous 5th-century Pali commentator but apparently a later (modern Sinhalese) monk-poet; the work is preserved in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist collections. Attribution and dating are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

SA_T13_davyasou.title: Yaśodharācarita
SA_T13_davyasou.author: Davuldeṇa Jñāneśvara (Davuldena Gnanissara) Mahāsthavira
SA_T13_davyasou.summary: A modern Sanskrit narrative poem on the life of Yaśodharā, the wife of Prince Siddhārtha (the future Buddha), telling her story, marriage, separation and devotion within the broader Buddha-legend.
SA_T13_davyasou.history: Composed by the Venerable Davuldena Gnanissara Mahānāyaka Thera, an eminent Sri Lankan scholar-monk and centenarian (1915-2017) renowned for his command of Sanskrit, Pali and English. The work is an example of modern Sinhalese Sanskrit literary composition, preserved in digital Sanskrit-text collections.

SA_T13_n2940u.title: Triratnasaundaryagāthā
SA_T13_n2940u.summary: Verses (gāthā) celebrating the beauty/excellence (saundarya) of the Three Jewels (triratna): the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Saṅgha. It is a devotional praise-composition extolling the qualities of the three Buddhist refuges.
SA_T13_n2940u.history: Anonymous in the available sources; transmitted in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist text collections. It could not be confidently dated or attributed, and may be a modern composition; identification is tentative.

SA_T13_n2976u.title: Saṅgītamālikā
SA_T13_n2976u.genre: kāvya / gīti (lyrical poetry, possibly a 'garland of songs')
SA_T13_n2976u.summary: A 'Garland of Songs/Music', apparently a collection of lyrical Sanskrit verses or songs on Buddhist themes. The precise content and arrangement could not be confirmed from available sources.
SA_T13_n2976u.history: Anonymous in available catalogues and preserved in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist text collections; very likely a modern (Sinhalese) Sanskrit composition. The work could not be confidently identified, so the description is tentative.

SA_T13_n3004u.title: Paścāttāpa
SA_T13_n3004u.summary: A poem titled 'Repentance/Remorse' (paścāttāpa), treating the theme of contrition and moral self-reproach. The exact narrative content could not be confirmed; the title signals a Buddhist meditation on repentance.
SA_T13_n3004u.history: Anonymous in available sources and preserved in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist text collections; very likely a modern Sinhalese Sanskrit composition. Could not be confidently identified, so the description is tentative.

SA_T13_n929u.title: Saundarananda Mahākāvya
SA_T13_n929u.author: Aśvaghoṣa
SA_T13_n929u.summary: A mahākāvya in 18 cantos narrating the conversion of Nanda, the Buddha's handsome half-brother. The first half depicts Nanda's worldly life and his love for his wife Sundarī and his reluctant ordination; the second half expounds Buddhist doctrine, meditation and the ascetic path leading to Nanda's awakening as an arhat.
SA_T13_n929u.history: Composed by Aśvaghoṣa, a Buddhist monk-poet of c. 1st-2nd century CE associated with Sāketa/Ayodhyā. Together with his Buddhacarita it is among the earliest surviving examples of Sanskrit kāvya and a model for later poets including, arguably, Kālidāsa.

SA_T14_bsa044_u.title: Kriyāsaṃgrahakārikā
SA_T14_bsa044_u.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (uncertain; not the Madhyamaka founder)
SA_T14_bsa044_u.genre: kārikā (verse manual) on ritual (kriyā) and consecration
SA_T14_bsa044_u.date_estimate: uncertain; likely late 1st millennium / early 2nd millennium CE
SA_T14_bsa044_u.summary: A verse compendium ('Summary of Ritual Actions') setting out tantric Buddhist ritual procedures, especially the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of images and stūpas and associated construction rites. It belongs to the body of Vajrayāna ritual literature dealing with the establishment and worship of sacred objects.
SA_T14_bsa044_u.history: Attributed to a 'Nāgārjuna' (a name used by many later tantric authors, not the Madhyamaka philosopher). It is closely related to the Kriyāsaṃgrahapañjikā of Kuladatta, a fuller ritual manual whose consecration material parallels the Śaiva pratiṣṭhātantras. Authorship and date are not securely established.

SA_T14_bsu059_u.title: Samyaksambuddhabhāṣita Pratimālakṣaṇa
SA_T14_bsu059_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the Buddha's word)
SA_T14_bsu059_u.date_estimate: archetype c. Gupta period - 10th c. CE (before Atiśa, d. 1054)
SA_T14_bsu059_u.summary: The 'Characteristics of Images as Spoken by the Perfectly Awakened One', a canonical text laying down the proportional measurements (tālamāna / aṅgula units) for making images of the Buddha. It specifies the correct body-proportions for sculpted and painted Buddha figures and is foundational to Buddhist iconometry.
SA_T14_bsu059_u.history: One of the principal sources for image-proportion theory in the Tibetan Buddhist canon (where it circulates as the 'Sūtra requested by Śāriputra'), most likely belonging to the Mūlasarvāstivāda tradition. The Sanskrit archetype probably dates to the Gupta period, no later than the 10th century; it was rendered into Tibetan in the early 14th century.

SA_T14_n1279u.title: Ātreyatilaka
SA_T14_n1279u.tradition: Indian (śilpaśāstra; iconometry, used in Buddhist art context)
SA_T14_n1279u.summary: A short text on the proportional measurements (lakṣaṇa) of images, transmitted alongside the corpus of iconometric/pratimālakṣaṇa literature. It belongs to the technical tradition of tālamāna prescribing how the parts of a sacred image should be measured.
SA_T14_n1279u.history: A minor and obscure iconometric text grouped with the Pratimālakṣaṇa-family of image-proportion manuals; preserved in collections of iconometric literature. Its author, date and exact relations are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

SA_T14_n1282u.title: Pratimālakṣaṇavivaraṇa
SA_T14_n1282u.summary: A commentary (vivaraṇa) explaining the Pratimālakṣaṇa, the canonical text on the proportions of Buddha-images. It elaborates the tālamāna/aṅgula measurement system used to construct correctly proportioned images of the Buddha.
SA_T14_n1282u.history: The Vivaraṇa on the Pratimālakṣaṇa was translated from Sanskrit into Tibetan by Grags-pa-rgyal-mtshan and the Indian paṇḍita Dharmadhara; it is a recognized part of the Tibetan iconometric corpus. Its Indian author is not securely identified.

SA_T14_nagcitpu.title: Citralakṣaṇa
SA_T14_nagcitpu.author: Nagnajit (traditional attribution)
SA_T14_nagcitpu.tradition: Indian (śilpaśāstra; influential in Buddhist art traditions)
SA_T14_nagcitpu.genre: śilpaśāstra / citraśāstra (treatise on painting and image-proportions)
SA_T14_nagcitpu.date_estimate: c. Gupta period (5th-6th c. CE) or earlier
SA_T14_nagcitpu.summary: One of the oldest treatises on the canons of Indian painting and image-making, ascribed to the sage Nagnajit. It sets out the principles of portraiture, iconometry (proportional measurements), and the rules for depicting figures, framed in a dialogue/origin narrative of the art of painting.
SA_T14_nagcitpu.history: The original Sanskrit was largely lost to the Indian tradition; the work survives chiefly through a Tibetan translation (studied since Berthold Laufer's 1913 edition). It is of major importance for early Indian art history and for the painting/iconometric traditions of Tibet, Mongolia and China, and must be distinguished from the later Citralakṣaṇa chapter in Śrīkumāra's 16th-century Śilparatna.

SA_T14_stuplaku.title: Stūpalakṣaṇakārikāvivecana
SA_T14_stuplaku.genre: vivecana (explanatory commentary) on verse-rules for stūpa proportions
SA_T14_stuplaku.summary: An explanatory work ('Discussion of the Verses on the Characteristics of Stūpas') treating the prescribed proportions and construction features of the Buddhist stūpa. It expounds the kārikās governing the measurements and symbolic structure of stūpas, whose parts are mapped onto the body of the Buddha and the five elements.
SA_T14_stuplaku.history: A specialized text in the Buddhist architectural/iconometric tradition concerned with stūpa proportions (transmitted especially in Tibetan-influenced sources). Its author and precise date are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

SA_T14_tarkrplu.title: Pratyakṣalakṣaṇa
SA_T14_tarkrplu.genre: philosophical treatise / definitional text on perception
SA_T14_tarkrplu.summary: A short text on the 'Definition/Characteristics of Perception' (pratyakṣa) within Buddhist epistemology. Following the Dignāga-Dharmakīrti tradition, it would define perception as an unmistaken, non-conceptual (kalpanāpoḍha) cognition whose object is the bare particular (svalakṣaṇa), distinguishing it from inference (anumāna).
SA_T14_tarkrplu.history: Anonymous in the available sources; the work_id suggests transmission alongside Tarka (logic) literature. It treats the standard Buddhist definition of perception elaborated in works such as Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya and Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu. The specific text could not be confidently identified, so the description is tentative.

SA_T15_bsa060_u.title: Śatagāthā
SA_T15_bsa060_u.author: Vararuci (traditional attribution)
SA_T15_bsa060_u.summary: A 'Hundred Verses' (also known as the Gāthāśataka), a collection of around 100-107 moral and gnomic maxims (nīti). The verses convey ethical and worldly-wisdom teachings drawing on the Prajñādaṇḍa and Cāṇakya nīti traditions.
SA_T15_bsa060_u.history: Attributed to Vararuci and included in the Tibetan Tanjur; the work survives primarily in Tibetan translation. It belongs to the popular Buddhist didactic-aphorism literature transmitted across South and Inner Asia.

SA_T15_n1144u.title: Nītiśāstra
SA_T15_n1144u.author: attributed to Masūrākṣa / Nāgārjuna (traditional, uncertain)
SA_T15_n1144u.summary: A 'Treatise on Right Conduct/Policy', a collection of gnomic verses on ethics, prudent conduct, and worldly wisdom. It belongs to the Buddhist nīti tradition of moral aphorisms (akin to the Cāṇakya/Bhartṛhari nīti collections) preserved in the Sanskrit Buddhist corpus.
SA_T15_n1144u.history: Transmitted anonymously or under traditional ascriptions (such as to Masūrākṣa or 'Nāgārjuna') in Buddhist nīti collections, and preserved in digital Sanskrit Buddhist text canons. The specific recension and authorship are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

SA_T16_n1289u.title: Tarkabhāṣāṭīkā
SA_T16_n1289u.author: Śrīmokṣākaragupta (text) / commentary by a later ṭīkākāra
SA_T16_n1289u.date_estimate: after c. 1100 CE (base text late 11th-12th c.)
SA_T16_n1289u.summary: A commentary (ṭīkā) on the Tarkabhāṣā ('Language of Logic') of Mokṣākaragupta, one of the finest late summaries of Buddhist logic and epistemology. The base Tarkabhāṣā concisely surveys the pramāṇa system (perception, inference, the apoha theory, and debate), and this ṭīkā glosses and elaborates its arguments.
SA_T16_n1289u.history: Mokṣākaragupta was one of the great paṇḍitas of the Jagaddala monastery in Varendrī (North Bengal), founded under King Rāmapāla c. 1080 CE; his Tarkabhāṣā represents one of the last systematic works of the Dharmakīrtian tradition before the decline of Buddhism in India. The ṭīkā is a later exegetical layer on this manual.

SA_T16_n2875u.title: Śvānastavakāvya
SA_T16_n2875u.author: Davuldena Jñāneśvara Mahāsthavira
SA_T16_n2875u.summary: A short Sanskrit kāvya in praise of a dog (śvan), composed by the modern Sri Lankan Buddhist monk-poet Davuldena Jñāneśvara Mahāsthavira. The poem celebrates the virtues, loyalty and companionship of the dog in ornate classical Sanskrit verse.
SA_T16_n2875u.history: This is a piece of contemporary Sri Lankan Sanskrit poetry rather than a classical work, illustrating the continued living tradition of Sanskrit composition among Buddhist monastic scholars in Sri Lanka. It has been studied in indological scholarship on modern Sanskrit literature.

SA_T16_n2916u.title: Svastyayanagāthā
SA_T16_n2916u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T16_n2916u.summary: A set of benedictory verses (svastyayana = 'recitation of auspicious/blessing verses', gāthā = stanzas) of the Buddhist tradition, of the type recited to invoke well-being and auspiciousness, typically praising the Buddha and the Three Jewels. The title indicates a protective/auspicious liturgical poem rather than a doctrinal treatise.
SA_T16_n2916u.history: Svastyayana benedictory verses follow the standard convention of Buddhist Sanskrit works, opening with praise of the Buddha. The specific text could not be confidently identified beyond its genre and is preserved in digital Buddhist Sanskrit text collections.

SA_T16_n2959u.title: Vajrayāna darśana mīmāṁsā
SA_T16_n2959u.genre: philosophical treatise (darśana-mīmāṁsā = inquiry into the philosophical view)
SA_T16_n2959u.date_estimate: modern (likely 20th c. CE Sanskrit composition); uncertain
SA_T16_n2959u.summary: A Sanskrit treatise inquiring into the philosophical standpoint (darśana) of Vajrayāna Buddhism — the tantric/esoteric vehicle of Mahāyāna that combines Yogācāra and Madhyamaka thought with ritual practice (mantra, mudrā, maṇḍala, deity visualization). The 'mīmāṁsā' (critical investigation) framing suggests a systematic exposition or apologetic of the tantric view.
SA_T16_n2959u.history: The title appears to be a modern scholarly Sanskrit exposition of Vajrayāna philosophy rather than a classical primary tantra; it is preserved in digital Buddhist Sanskrit collections. The specific authorship and date could not be confirmed.

SA_T16_tarkrsau.title: Tarkarahasya
SA_T16_tarkrsau.author: anonymous/traditional (Buddhist Pramāṇa tradition)
SA_T16_tarkrsau.summary: A short Sanskrit manual ('Secret/Essence of Reasoning') belonging to the Buddhist pramāṇa (logic and epistemology) tradition, treating the means of valid cognition (perception and inference) and the rules of inference and debate. It is one of a small group of Buddhist Sanskrit logical primers transmitted alongside texts such as the Hetuvidyā section of the Yogācārabhūmi and the Dharmottaraṭippanaka.
SA_T16_tarkrsau.history: The Tarkarahasya (sometimes paired with a Vādarahasya) has been studied by indologists such as Gudrun Bühnemann as part of the late Indian Buddhist logical curriculum descending from Dignāga and Dharmakīrti. Its precise authorship and date remain debated.

SA_T17_bsa046_u.title: Yogāvatāra
SA_T17_bsa046_u.author: Dignāga (attributed)
SA_T17_bsa046_u.summary: A very short verse text ('Introduction/Descent into Yoga') on Buddhist meditative practice and the stages of yogic absorption, attributed to the great logician Dignāga. It sketches the path of mental cultivation by which the yogin realizes the nature of mind/reality.
SA_T17_bsa046_u.history: Dignāga, the founder of the Buddhist epistemological school and an important Yogācāra thinker, is traditionally credited with the Yogāvatāra; the attribution and the relationship to a lost commentary on the Yogācārabhūmi have been discussed by scholars such as Marek Mejor. The Sanskrit survives in only a small number of verses.

SA_T17_byogalbu.title: Buddhist Yoga Manual
SA_T17_byogalbu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T17_byogalbu.tradition: Buddhist (Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma with Yogācāra elements; Central Asian)
SA_T17_byogalbu.summary: Modern scholarly title (Schlingloff's 'Yogalehrbuch') for a fragmentary Sanskrit Buddhist meditation manual recovered from the cave sites of Qizil and Shorchuk near Kucha on the Silk Road. It guides the yogin through methods of vivid visualization and stages of meditative attainment on the bodhisattva path, drawing on Abhidharma (Vibhāṣā) metaphysics.
SA_T17_byogalbu.history: The fragments were found 1903–1906 and partially reconstructed and translated into German by Dieter Schlingloff (1964), who named the untitled text 'Ein buddhistisches Yogalehrbuch.' Written in North Turkestan Brāhmī, it is important for the history of Buddhist meditation and its links to Chinese visualization texts.

SA_T17_kukalpmu.title: Kalpanāmaṇḍitikā
SA_T17_kukalpmu.author: Kumāralāta
SA_T17_kukalpmu.summary: The Kalpanāmaṇḍitikā Dṛṣṭāntapaṅkti ('Garland of Examples Adorned by Poetic Fancy') is a Sanskrit collection of around 90 illustrative stories (dṛṣṭānta/avadāna) that teach Buddhist morality, piety toward the Three Jewels, and ethical conduct, often interwoven with concerns for social respectability and prudent worldly action. It is a major example of early ornate Buddhist narrative literature from Gandhāra.
SA_T17_kukalpmu.history: Composed by Kumāralāta, a Gandhāran monk from Taxila regarded as the first teacher (mūlācārya) of the Sautrāntikas, the work was long misattributed to Aśvaghoṣa under the title Sūtrālaṃkāra. The complete text survives only in Kumārajīva's early-5th-century Chinese translation (Dà zhuāngyán lùn, T201); Sanskrit fragments were recovered at Kizil and edited by Heinrich Lüders (1926).

SA_T17_matpra_u.title: Prasādapratibhodbhava [Śatapañcāśatka]
SA_T17_matpra_u.author: Mātṛceṭa
SA_T17_matpra_u.genre: stotra (hymn of praise to the Buddha)
SA_T17_matpra_u.summary: A celebrated hymn of praise to the Buddha in 150 (actually 152–153) verses, also titled Śatapañcāśatka ('Hymn in 150 Verses'). It extols the Buddha's wisdom, compassion, supreme qualities and teaching in elegant, deeply devotional Sanskrit, expressing the faith and inspired eloquence (prasāda-pratibhā) the Buddha awakens.
SA_T17_matpra_u.history: Composed by the poet Mātṛceṭa, the hymn was enormously popular across all Buddhist schools, was translated into several languages, attracted numerous Indian commentaries, and was reportedly recited by monks throughout India (noted by the pilgrim Yijing). It is a foundational work of Buddhist stotra literature.

SA_T17_n2936u.title: Śabdabhedaprakāśa
SA_T17_n2936u.author: Maheśvara
SA_T17_n2936u.summary: A Sanskrit lexicographical work ('Illumination of Distinctions among Words') by Maheśvara that treats words distinguished by subtle differences in form, gender, or meaning, complementing his larger homonym dictionary. It belongs to the koṣa tradition that aided poets and scholars in correct Sanskrit usage.
SA_T17_n2936u.history: Maheśvara (Maheśvara Kavīndra/Sūri), an 11th–12th-century lexicographer, is best known for the homonym lexicon Viśvaprakāśa (1111/1253 CE); the Śabdabhedaprakāśa is a companion lexical work attributed to him. The specific text identity is less well documented than the Viśvaprakāśa.

SA_T17_n2944u.title: Viśvaprakāśa
SA_T17_n2944u.author: Maheśvara (Maheśvara Sūri / Kavīndra)
SA_T17_n2944u.summary: A major Sanskrit homonym lexicon ('Illumination of All [Words]') that arranges words with multiple meanings, organized by final consonant and number of syllables. It was a standard reference for poets and commentators and a principal source for later lexicons.
SA_T17_n2944u.history: Composed by Maheśvara, son of Śrī Brahma, the Viśvaprakāśa is one of the chief medieval Sanskrit anekārtha (homonym) dictionaries and was directly drawn upon by Medinīkāra's Medinīkośa. It was edited and published (Chowkhamba, Benares, 1911) and is frequently cited in Sanskrit lexical scholarship.

SA_T17_n958u.title: Bauddhatantrakośa I
SA_T17_n958u.author: anonymous/traditional (compilation)
SA_T17_n958u.date_estimate: compilation drawing on tantric sources of the late 1st millennium CE onward; modern digital edition
SA_T17_n958u.summary: A Sanskrit glossary/encyclopedia (kośa) of Buddhist tantric terminology, systematically defining and explaining key Vajrayāna concepts — emptiness and non-duality, ritual and initiation, meditative visualization, deities, the subtle body (channels and cakras), and mantric syllables. 'I' indicates the first part/volume.
SA_T17_n958u.history: The work is preserved in romanized Sanskrit in the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon (input by Milan Shakya, 2013; Nagarjuna Institute), compiling tantric Buddhist vocabulary; no single classical author is identified in the digital edition.

SA_T17_n996u.title: Mahāvyutpatti
SA_T17_n996u.author: anonymous/traditional (committee of imperially appointed translators)
SA_T17_n996u.genre: kośa (bilingual lexicon / glossary of Buddhist terms)
SA_T17_n996u.date_estimate: early 9th c. CE (final redaction c. 814 CE)
SA_T17_n996u.summary: The Mahāvyutpatti ('Great Detailed Explanation') is the earliest substantial bilingual Sanskrit–Tibetan dictionary, a systematically organized glossary of thousands of Buddhist technical terms — names of buddhas and bodhisattvas, doctrinal categories, titles, and vocabulary. It was created to standardize the translation of Sanskrit Buddhist scriptures into Tibetan.
SA_T17_n996u.history: Compiled in Tibet under imperial sponsorship in the late 8th–early 9th centuries (final redaction sanctioned by decree, c. 814 CE, during the reign of Sad-na-legs / Ralpacan), it standardized translation terminology and became foundational for the Tibetan Buddhist canon. Later expanded into quadrilingual (Sanskrit-Tibetan-Chinese-Mongolian) versions.
related_works: Madhyavyutpatti (sGra sbyor bam po gnyis pa)

SA_T17_sabhivpu.title: Sabhikasaugataśāsanapravrajyāvratacaraṇaparivarta
SA_T17_sabhivpu.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_T17_sabhivpu.date_estimate: section of a text whose core dates to c. 2nd c. BCE–4th c. CE (composite)
SA_T17_sabhivpu.summary: A chapter ('Chapter on Sabhika's going-forth, vows and practice in the dispensation of the Sugata/Buddha') narrating how the wanderer Sabhika questions the Buddha, is converted, and takes ordination and the monastic vows. It belongs to the 'Questions of Sabhika' episode of the Mahāvastu.
SA_T17_sabhivpu.history: The episode corresponds to the Sabhika section (Chapter XXXVIII) of the Mahāvastu, the great Vinaya-related biography of the Buddha belonging to the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda school, a composite text built up over several centuries.

SA_T17_vtrksopu.title: Tarkasopāna
SA_T17_vtrksopu.author: Vidyākaraśānti
SA_T17_vtrksopu.summary: The Tarkasopāna ('Staircase of Reasoning') is a concise Sanskrit manual of Buddhist logic and epistemology by Vidyākaraśānti, treating the means of valid cognition (pramāṇa) — perception and inference — and the structure of inference and debate within the late Indian Buddhist pramāṇa tradition.
SA_T17_vtrksopu.history: Vidyākaraśānti, a Buddhist logician of the 11th–12th century, belongs to the late phase of Indian Buddhist epistemology stemming from Dignāga and Dharmakīrti, contemporaneous with figures like Mokṣākaragupta (Tarkabhāṣā). Such manuals were influential in both India and Tibet before the decline of Indian Buddhism.

SA_XX_bowermsu.title: Bower Manuscript
SA_XX_bowermsu.author: anonymous/traditional (multiple authors)
SA_XX_bowermsu.genre: composite collection: medical treatise, divination, and dhāraṇī (incantation)
SA_XX_bowermsu.summary: A birch-bark Sanskrit manuscript comprising seven fragmentary treatises: three on Āyurvedic medicine (including the Nāvanītaka, with prescriptions on garlic, recipes and medicinal formulas), two on divination by dice (pāśaka), and two on incantations (dhāraṇī) against snakebite. It is one of the oldest surviving Indian manuscripts and the earliest dated witness of Indian medical writing.
SA_XX_bowermsu.history: Found in a Buddhist memorial stupa near Kucha and acquired in 1890 by Lt. Hamilton Bower, it was studied and edited by Rudolf Hoernle. Written in early Gupta (late Brāhmī) script, it is preserved in the Bodleian Library, Oxford, and is of major importance for the history of Indian medicine, palaeography, and Silk Road Buddhism.

SA_XX_n1136u.title: Buddhamata
SA_XX_n1136u.genre: doctrinal exposition (treatise on the Buddhist view)
SA_XX_n1136u.summary: A Sanskrit text whose title means 'the doctrine/view of the Buddha' (buddha + mata), i.e., an exposition of Buddhist tenets. The specific work could not be identified with confidence; it appears to be a doctrinal summary of Buddhist teaching rather than a single canonical scripture.
SA_XX_n1136u.history: No specific author, date, or transmission could be confidently established for a work under this exact title; it is preserved in digital Sanskrit text collections and may be a modern or compilatory presentation of Buddhist doctrine.

SA_XX_n1137u.title: Nāgānanda Nāṭaka
SA_XX_n1137u.author: Harṣa (Harṣavardhana / Harṣadeva, King of Kanauj)
SA_XX_n1137u.summary: A five-act Sanskrit play ('Joy of the Serpents') dramatizing the Buddhist jātaka-derived story of the compassionate Vidyādhara prince Jīmūtavāhana, who renounces his kingdom, marries the Siddha princess Malayavatī, and offers his own body to the eagle Garuḍa to save a Nāga youth, before being miraculously revived. The benedictory verse invokes the Buddha defeating Māra, and the play is suffused with themes of compassion, impermanence, and self-sacrifice.
SA_XX_n1137u.history: Composed by the emperor Harṣa, who turned from Śaivism toward Mahāyāna Buddhism, it is his only full nāṭaka (his Ratnāvalī and Priyadarśikā are lighter nāṭikās) and a key example of Buddhist-themed classical drama. It contains an early reference to the practice of satī.

SA_XX_n1142u.title: Nālandādahana
SA_XX_n1142u.genre: kavya / historical poem (likely modern Sanskrit composition)
SA_XX_n1142u.summary: A Sanskrit poem titled 'The Burning of Nālandā,' evidently treating the destruction by fire of the great Buddhist monastic university of Nālandā (traditionally associated with the late 12th-century invasions). The specific work could not be confidently identified and appears to be a modern Sanskrit composition on this historical theme.
SA_XX_n1142u.history: Nālandā flourished from the 5th century CE until its destruction around the late 12th century; its burning became a powerful theme in later and modern literature. No classical author or firm date for a poem of this exact title could be confirmed.

SA_XX_n1143u.title: Nṛtyaprasūti
SA_XX_n1143u.summary: A Sanskrit work whose title means 'the origin/bringing-forth of dance' (nṛtya + prasūti), evidently a treatise or poem concerning dance, its source and principles within the Indian performing-arts (nāṭya) tradition rooted in the Nāṭyaśāstra. The specific text could not be confidently identified.
SA_XX_n1143u.history: No definite author, date, or transmission could be established for a work of this exact title; it belongs to the broad Indian tradition of texts on nṛtta, nṛtya, and nāṭya descending from Bharata's Nāṭyaśāstra and may be a modern composition.

SA_XX_n2856u.title: Kalpoktamārīcīsādhana
SA_XX_n2856u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_XX_n2856u.date_estimate: c. 8th–11th c. CE (within the Sādhanamālā corpus)
SA_XX_n2856u.summary: A tantric sādhana ('the rite-prescribed [kalpokta] evocation of Mārīcī') giving the visualization and ritual procedure for the goddess Mārīcī, deity of the dawn associated with protection and invincibility. It is preserved as sādhana no. 134 in the Sādhanamālā, where Mārīcī appears in numerous forms (e.g., three-faced and eight-armed).
SA_XX_n2856u.history: It is one of sixteen Mārīcī sādhanas collected in the Sādhanamālā, a Sanskrit anthology of around 300 tantric evocations composed by various Indian masters between roughly the 5th and 11th centuries. The 'kalpokta' designation marks it as following a prescribed ritual manual (kalpa).

SA_XX_n2898u.title: Svapnādhyāya
SA_XX_n2898u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_XX_n2898u.tradition: Hindu (divination/nimittaśāstra); the title also occurs in Buddhist contexts
SA_XX_n2898u.date_estimate: uncertain; the genre is ancient (1st millennium CE), individual recensions later and variable
SA_XX_n2898u.summary: A 'chapter/treatise on dreams' (svapna-adhyāya), i.e. a manual of oneiromancy classifying auspicious and inauspicious dreams and their predictive meanings. Such texts form one of the recognized divisions of the 'science of omens' (nimittaśāstra) and circulate both as independent tracts and as embedded chapters in larger works (e.g. the Agni Purāṇa's dream chapter, the Suśruta Saṃhitā's section on dreams).
SA_XX_n2898u.history: Svapnādhyāya is a generic title shared by several short anonymous dream-interpretation texts in the Sanskrit (and Buddhist) traditions rather than a single fixed work; dream divination also entered later redactions of texts such as the Divyāvadāna. Without a specific manuscript or recension it cannot be pinned to one author or date.

SA_XX_n2927u.title: Buddhakapālatantra
SA_XX_n2927u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_XX_n2927u.summary: A Buddhist Yoginītantra of the Heruka/Yoginī class centered on the deity Buddhakapāla ('Buddha-skull') and his consort, teaching maṇḍala, mantra, deity-yoga and transgressive yoginī-cult ritual and practice aimed at the attainment of mahāmudrā-siddhi. It belongs to the same milieu as the Hevajra, Cakrasaṃvara and Saṃvarodaya tantras.
SA_XX_n2927u.history: The Yoginītantras are a late stratum of Indian Buddhist tantric literature, emerging no earlier than the 8th century and prominent in the 9th-10th centuries. The Buddhakapālatantra survives in Sanskrit and in Tibetan translation with commentary, and is associated with the mahāsiddha lineages.

SA_XX_n2938u.title: Ḍākārṇava
SA_XX_n2938u.author: anonymous/traditional (Tibetan tradition attributes commentary to Padmavajra)
SA_XX_n2938u.summary: The Ḍākārṇava ('Ocean of the Ḍāka'), fully Śrīḍākārṇavamahāyoginītantrarāja, is a large, inclusive Buddhist Yoginītantra of the Cakrasaṃvara cycle that integrates deities from numerous tantric traditions and the Buddhist cosmos into the Saṃvara system, describing the Heruka maṇḍala, sacred sites (pīṭhas), and yoginī practice. It functions as one of the principal explanatory tantras of the Saṃvara corpus.
SA_XX_n2938u.history: Composed in eastern India between the late 10th and early 12th centuries, it was translated into Tibetan (with Padmavajra's Vohitā commentary) by Jayasena and Dharma yon tan in 12th-century Kathmandu. It is studied as a key witness to the late, encyclopedic phase of Indian tantric Buddhism.

SA_XX_n690u.title: Nānāsiddhopadeśa
SA_XX_n690u.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_XX_n690u.tradition: uncertain; likely tantric/siddha milieu (Buddhist or Nātha-Śaiva)
SA_XX_n690u.summary: The title means 'instruction on the various siddhis (perfections/accomplishments)', indicating a short tantric/yogic manual conveying esoteric teachings on attaining diverse magical and spiritual powers. The specific recension and tradition could not be confidently established.
SA_XX_n690u.history: An obscure text not well documented in the standard reference literature; it belongs to the broad siddha-upadeśa genre cultivated in both Buddhist Vajrayāna and Hindu Nātha/Śaiva circles. Its author, date and affiliation remain unverified.

SA_XX_n693u.title: Advayavajrasaṃgraha
SA_XX_n693u.author: Advayavajra (Maitrīpāda / Maitrīpa, c. 1007-1085)
SA_XX_n693u.summary: A modern editorial collection (first edited by H. P. Śāstrī/Bhattacharyya, 1927) of about twenty-one short works attributed to Advayavajra, treating non-conceptual realization (amanasikāra), Mahāmudrā, and a strongly anti-foundational Madhyamaka view (apratiṣṭhāna), blending Siddha tantric and Madhyamaka thought. Component texts include the Tattvaratnāvalī, Nairātmyāprakāśa, Caturmudropadeśa and others.
SA_XX_n693u.history: Advayavajra is another name for the mahāsiddha Maitrīpāda (Maitrīpa), a major figure in the transmission of Mahāmudrā. In Tibet these texts correspond largely to the 'Amanasikāra cycle'; the Sanskrit collection assembled under this title is a scholarly grouping of his short works.

SA_XX_n740u.title: Advayasiddhi
SA_XX_n740u.author: Lakṣmīṅkarā (Lakṣmī; a yoginī, traditionally sister of king Indrabhūti of Oḍḍiyāna)
SA_XX_n740u.summary: A brief tantric work of about thirty-six verses expounding a non-dual (advaya) view of Vajrayāna emancipation, critical of external ritualism and image-worship in favour of inner sahaja realization. It is notable as a rare early Buddhist tantric text authored by a woman.
SA_XX_n740u.history: Attributed to Lakṣmīṅkarā, counted among the eighty-four mahāsiddhas and traditionally a princess of Oḍḍiyāna. Long lost in Sanskrit and preserved in Tibetan translation in the Tengyur (alongside two of her other works); it is valued as an example of bold female-authored Vajrayāna doctrine.

SA_MB_saundaryalahariHK.title: Saundaryalaharī
SA_MB_saundaryalahariHK.author: traditionally attributed to Śaṅkarācārya (authorship disputed)
SA_MB_saundaryalahariHK.genre: stotra (devotional hymn) with strong tantric content
SA_MB_saundaryalahariHK.date_estimate: disputed; commonly placed in the latter 1st millennium CE, plausibly c. 7th-10th c. CE; the attribution to Ādi Śaṅkara (c. 8th c.) is questioned
SA_MB_saundaryalahariHK.summary: A celebrated hymn of (traditionally) one hundred verses in the śikhariṇī metre praising the Goddess Tripurasundarī. The first part (Ānandalaharī, 'wave of bliss') is heavily tantric, treating the Śrīcakra, kuṇḍalinī, mantra and yantra; the second part (the 'Saundaryalaharī' proper) lyrically describes the Goddess's beauty from head to foot.
SA_MB_saundaryalahariHK.history: Traditionally ascribed to Ādi Śaṅkara, but many scholars doubt this, suggesting a later Śrīvidyā-tantra author or a different 'Śaṅkara'. The text is an epitome of Śākta Tantrism, accreted with dozens of commentaries (notably Lakṣmīdhara's) and a rich ritual/yantra tradition.

SA_MB_kaalottaratantramHK.title: Kāllottara (Kālottara)
SA_MB_kaalottaratantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaalottaratantramHK.date_estimate: uncertain; early-medieval (c. mid 1st millennium CE), with recensions of varying length
SA_MB_kaalottaratantramHK.summary: The Kālottara (also spelled Kāllottara) is an influential Śaiva Siddhānta scripture circulating in multiple recensions of differing verse-length (e.g. 50, 100, 150 verses; the shortest being the Śatika-Kālajñāna). It supplies doctrine, mantra and ritual material on which many surviving Saiddhāntika ritual manuals are based.
SA_MB_kaalottaratantramHK.history: An important but long under-edited tantra, quoted by later authorities such as Kṣemarāja, Hemādri, Raghunandana and Kamalākara. This entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (work id E00012); the project records two 'kāllotara' items (this and E0011), apparently different recensions/copies.

SA_MB_cinmantramaliniHK.title: Cinmantramālinī
SA_MB_cinmantramaliniHK.author: Prakāśānanda (likely the 16th-c. author; identity uncertain for this title)
SA_MB_cinmantramaliniHK.date_estimate: uncertain; possibly c. 16th c. CE if by the known Prakāśānanda
SA_MB_cinmantramaliniHK.summary: The title ('garland [mālinī] of the mantra of consciousness [cit]') indicates a Śākta/non-dual tantric work organized as a sequence (mālā/mālinī) of mantras and meditations on consciousness as the supreme reality. The specific content could not be confirmed from available sources.
SA_MB_cinmantramaliniHK.history: Attributed to a Prakāśānanda; a well-known Prakāśānanda flourished c. 1550-1600 (author of the Advaita Vedānta Siddhāntamuktāvalī), though it is uncertain whether he authored this tantric text. The entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (id E0002).

SA_MB_dattatreyabodhaHK.title: Dattātreyabodha
SA_MB_dattatreyabodhaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Dattātreya)
SA_MB_dattatreyabodhaHK.tradition: Hindu (Advaita / Avadhūta yoga tradition of Dattātreya)
SA_MB_dattatreyabodhaHK.summary: A short text in the Dattātreya tradition whose title means 'the awakening/instruction (bodha) of Dattātreya', presumably conveying non-dual, avadhūta-style realization associated with the deity-sage Dattātreya (the combined form of Brahmā, Viṣṇu and Śiva and a paradigmatic lord of yoga). The precise content and recension could not be verified.
SA_MB_dattatreyabodhaHK.history: Belongs to the broad corpus of Dattātreya works (alongside the Avadhūta Gītā, Dattātreya Upaniṣad, Dattātreya Yogaśāstra, etc.); a specific 'Dattātreyabodha' is not well documented in standard references. The entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (id E0003).

SA_MB_cinmayadiksavidhanamHK.title: Cinmayadīkṣāvidhāna
SA_MB_cinmayadiksavidhanamHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_cinmayadiksavidhanamHK.summary: A tantric ritual manual setting out the procedure (vidhāna) for 'consciousness-pervaded' initiation (cinmaya-dīkṣā), i.e. the rite by which a guru transmits liberating consciousness to the disciple. It belongs to the Śaiva/Śākta initiatory ritual literature.
SA_MB_cinmayadiksavidhanamHK.history: An obscure ritual text known chiefly through manuscript/e-text catalogues; this entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (id E0004). Author and date are not established.

SA_MB_jnanadiksaHK.title: Jñānadīkṣā
SA_MB_jnanadiksaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_jnanadiksaHK.summary: The title 'Jñānadīkṣā' denotes the 'initiation by knowledge' (jñāna-dīkṣā), a form of higher, non-ritual or gnostic initiation in Śaiva/Śākta tantra by which the guru confers liberating knowledge directly. The text presumably expounds the doctrine and procedure of this initiation.
SA_MB_jnanadiksaHK.history: An obscure tantric initiation text documented through manuscript/e-text catalogues; this entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (id E0005). Author, date and exact affiliation are unverified.

SA_MB_jnanasiddhyagamaHK.title: Jñānasiddhyāgama
SA_MB_jnanasiddhyagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_jnanasiddhyagamaHK.summary: An āgama (revealed Śaiva scripture) whose title means 'āgama of the attainment of knowledge' (jñāna-siddhi), presumably treating the jñāna-pāda (doctrinal/gnostic section) of Śaiva revelation, alongside the usual āgamic concerns of ritual, yoga and conduct. The precise recension is unconfirmed.
SA_MB_jnanasiddhyagamaHK.history: Part of the large body of South Indian Śaiva āgamas/upāgamas, many of which survive only in manuscripts being edited by institutions such as the IFP and Muktabodha. This entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (id E0006); its author and date are not established.

SA_MB_dhyanaratnavaliHK.title: Dhyānaratnāvali
SA_MB_dhyanaratnavaliHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_dhyanaratnavaliHK.summary: The title 'Dhyānaratnāvalī' ('jewel-garland of meditations') indicates an anthology of dhyāna verses — iconographic visualization formulae describing deities for meditation and worship. Such collections are used in tantric and pūjā contexts to fix the mental image of deities before mantra recitation.
SA_MB_dhyanaratnavaliHK.history: An obscure compilatory text known through manuscript/e-text catalogues; this entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (id E0008). Author and date are not established.

SA_MB_matatrayaikyaprakasikaHK.title: Matatrayaikyaprakāśikā
SA_MB_matatrayaikyaprakasikaHK.author: Aiyyaṇācārya (Aiyyaṇa)
SA_MB_matatrayaikyaprakasikaHK.tradition: Hindu (non-dualist synthesis of Śaiva, Vaiṣṇava and Advaita Vedānta)
SA_MB_matatrayaikyaprakasikaHK.summary: A treatise 'illuminating the unity (aikya) of the three systems (matatraya)' — arguing that Śaiva, Vaiṣṇava and the non-dualist Vedānta of the Upaniṣads ultimately teach the same non-dual reality. It is a harmonizing/conciliatory work in the spirit of South Indian samarasa thought.
SA_MB_matatrayaikyaprakasikaHK.history: Attributed to Aiyyaṇācārya, an otherwise little-documented author; the text survives as a Muktabodha digital-library e-text (id E0009). Its date and precise sectarian background are not securely established.

SA_MB_vijayottaratantraHK.title: Vijayottaratantra
SA_MB_vijayottaratantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_vijayottaratantraHK.summary: A Śaiva tantra; the title 'Vijayottara(tantra)' suggests an 'uttara' (later/supplementary) recension associated with a 'Vijaya' scripture. It may be related to, or confused with, the well-known Trika Mālinīvijayottaratantra, or be a distinct Vijaya-class Śaiva tantra; the identity could not be confirmed.
SA_MB_vijayottaratantraHK.history: Known through manuscript/e-text catalogues (this entry corresponds to a Muktabodha digital-library e-text, id E0010). Whether it is independent of the famous Mālinīvijayottara (commented on by Abhinavagupta) is unclear, so the identification remains tentative.

SA_MB_kalottaratantraHK.title: Kāllottara (Kālottara)
SA_MB_kalottaratantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kalottaratantraHK.date_estimate: uncertain; early-medieval (c. mid 1st millennium CE), recensions of varying length
SA_MB_kalottaratantraHK.summary: Another copy/recension of the Kālottara, an influential Śaiva Siddhānta scripture transmitted in several recensions of differing verse-length, supplying the doctrinal, mantric and ritual basis for many surviving Saiddhāntika manuals. (Duplicate title to E00012 within this project.)
SA_MB_kalottaratantraHK.history: The Kālottara was widely quoted by later authorities (Kṣemarāja, Hemādri, Raghunandana, Kamalākara) and underlies much Śaiva Siddhānta ritual practice. This entry corresponds to a second Muktabodha digital-library e-text labelled 'kāllotara' (id E0011), apparently a distinct recension or copy from E00012.

SA_MB_aagamapraamaanyamHK.title: Āgamaprāmāṇya
SA_MB_aagamapraamaanyamHK.author: Yāmunācārya (Yāmunācārya-svāmin, c. 918-1038 CE / traditionally 10th-11th c.)
SA_MB_aagamapraamaanyamHK.summary: A polemical treatise establishing the scriptural authority (prāmāṇya) of the Pāñcarātra Āgamas, arguing they are as authoritative as the Veda and refuting Mīmāṃsaka and other objections to their validity. It defends the Vaiṣṇava ritual-theological tradition against charges of non-Vedic, heterodox origin.
SA_MB_aagamapraamaanyamHK.history: By Yāmunācārya (Ālavandār), the grand-teacher of Rāmānuja, who laid the philosophical foundations of Viśiṣṭādvaita Śrīvaiṣṇavism. Together with his Gītārthasaṃgraha it is among his most important surviving works and was foundational for the later Śrīvaiṣṇava acceptance of Pāñcarātra as orthodox.

SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaVol1HK.title: Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā, Volume 1
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaVol1HK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaVol1HK.tradition: Hindu (Vaiṣṇava; Pāñcarātra Āgama, with strong tantric and Śākta elements)
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaVol1HK.date_estimate: composite; early-to-mid 1st millennium CE (frequently placed c. 2nd-7th c. CE; disputed)
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaVol1HK.summary: Volume 1 of the Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā, a major Pāñcarātra scripture cast as a dialogue revealed by Ahirbudhnya (a form of Śiva/Rudra) to Nārada. It expounds Pāñcarātra cosmology and metaphysics (the vyūhas, śakti, the unfolding of the cosmos from Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa), as well as mantra-śāstra, the Sudarśana cakra, ritual, yoga and even statecraft/weaponry.
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaVol1HK.history: One of the most important and philosophically rich Pāñcarātra Saṃhitās; F. O. Schrader's 'Introduction to the Pāñcarātra and the Ahirbudhnya Saṃhitā' (1916) made it a key text for Western study of the tradition. Its date is much disputed and the text is likely composite, accreted over several centuries.

SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaa2HK.title: Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā, Volume 2
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaa2HK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaa2HK.tradition: Hindu (Vaiṣṇava; Pāñcarātra Āgama, with strong tantric and Śākta elements)
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaa2HK.date_estimate: composite; early-to-mid 1st millennium CE (frequently placed c. 2nd-7th c. CE; disputed)
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaa2HK.summary: Volume 2 (continuation) of the Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā, the same Pāñcarātra scripture revealed by Ahirbudhnya to Nārada. The later portions continue its treatment of mantra-śāstra, the Sudarśana cakra, ritual worship, yoga, and applied topics, completing the work begun in Volume 1.
SA_MB_ahirbudhnyasamhitaa2HK.history: Part of the same major Pāñcarātra text as M00002a, split into two volumes in this edition. Foundational for the study of Vaiṣṇava tantric theology since Schrader's work; its composite character and uncertain dating apply throughout.

SA_MB_akulaviiratantramHK.title: Matsyendranātha: Akulavīratantra
SA_MB_akulaviiratantramHK.author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (traditional)
SA_MB_akulaviiratantramHK.tradition: Hindu; Kaula/Yoginī Kaula Śaiva-Śākta tantra (later associated with the Nātha Sampradāya)
SA_MB_akulaviiratantramHK.date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (the early Kaula stratum attributed to Matsyendranātha; manuscript redaction later)
SA_MB_akulaviiratantramHK.summary: A short Kaula tantric scripture of the Yoginī Kaula stream attributed to the legendary master Matsyendranātha. It teaches a radically non-dual, antinomian path in which liberation comes through direct realization of the 'akula' (the transcendent reality beyond the kula/clan ordering of reality), declaring that the Akulavīra alone leads to liberation and that this truth cannot be confined by scriptures or philosophical schools.
SA_MB_akulaviiratantramHK.history: Transmitted within the corpus associated with Matsyendranātha, founder-figure of the Yoginī Kaula and revered ancestor of the Nātha tradition; it was edited together with the Kaulajñānanirṇaya and related texts by P. C. Bagchi in the 1930s. Its attribution is traditional rather than securely historical, and it reflects the early medieval Kaula milieu honored by Abhinavagupta.

SA_MB_anuttaraprakasapancasikaHK.title: Ādyanātha: Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā
SA_MB_anuttaraprakasapancasikaHK.author: attributed to Ādyanātha (Ādinātha, the primordial Śiva-teacher; traditional/legendary attribution)
SA_MB_anuttaraprakasapancasikaHK.genre: philosophical stotra / didactic verse treatise (pañcāśikā, 'fifty verses')
SA_MB_anuttaraprakasapancasikaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely 1st-2nd millennium CE, a non-dual Śaiva devotional-philosophical poem
SA_MB_anuttaraprakasapancasikaHK.summary: A set of about fifty verses (pañcāśikā) expounding 'anuttara', the supreme unsurpassed reality of non-dual Śaivism, identified with pure consciousness, light (prakāśa) and the supreme 'I' (aham). The work is a condensed meditative-philosophical hymn on the recognition of this absolute as one's own nature.
SA_MB_anuttaraprakasapancasikaHK.history: Listed in Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum as a Śaiva work. The ascribed author 'Ādyanātha/Ādinātha' is the mythic primordial guru (Śiva himself as first teacher), so the historical authorship and date are not securely established. It belongs to the broader non-dual Śaiva 'anuttara' literature exemplified by Abhinavagupta's Anuttarāṣṭikā.

SA_MB_bhogakarikaHK.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Bhogakārikā
SA_MB_bhogakarikaHK.author: Sadyojyoti(s)
SA_MB_bhogakarikaHK.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_bhogakarikaHK.date_estimate: c. 675-725 CE (Sadyojyoti, the first systematizing theologian of Śaiva Siddhānta)
SA_MB_bhogakarikaHK.summary: A versified treatise on 'bhoga', the soul's experience and enjoyment/suffering in bondage, analyzing how the impure self undergoes embodied experience and the karmic-material conditions of the bound soul (paśu). It belongs to the dualist metaphysics of Śaiva Siddhānta, in which Śiva (pati), souls (paśu) and bonds (pāśa) are eternally distinct realities.
SA_MB_bhogakarikaHK.history: Sadyojyoti is the earliest systematic theologian of the Śaiva Siddhānta whose works survive; the Bhogakārikā is transmitted as one of the eight foundational treatises (Aṣṭaprakaraṇa) and carries a commentary by Aghoraśiva (12th c.). It was edited and studied by W. A. Borody and others.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_moksakarikaHK.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Mokṣakārikā
SA_MB_moksakarikaHK.author: Sadyojyoti(s)
SA_MB_moksakarikaHK.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_moksakarikaHK.summary: A verse treatise on 'mokṣa', liberation, expounding the Śaiva Siddhānta doctrine of how the bound soul is freed from the three bonds (āṇava, karma, māyā) through Śiva's grace and initiation, attaining a state of equality with Śiva (śivasāmya) without merging identically into him. It defends a dualist conception of liberation against rival non-dual views.
SA_MB_moksakarikaHK.history: One of the surviving doctrinal kārikā-works of Sadyojyoti, transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with the standard commentary of Aghoraśiva. It is a core source for classical dualist Śaiva soteriology.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_nadakarikaHK.title: Rāmakaṇṭha: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Nādakārikā
SA_MB_nadakarikaHK.author: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (Rāmakaṇṭha II)
SA_MB_nadakarikaHK.tradition: Hindu; Śaiva Siddhānta (dualist Śaivism), Kashmirian lineage
SA_MB_nadakarikaHK.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_nadakarikaHK.date_estimate: c. 950-1000 CE (Rāmakaṇṭha II, Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta lineage)
SA_MB_nadakarikaHK.summary: A short verse treatise developing a Śaiva Siddhānta theory of 'nāda' (primal sound/resonance) and the metaphysics of sound, speech and meaning, treating the levels of phonic emanation that link the supreme principle to articulate language. It situates the doctrine of sound within the dualist cosmology and theology of the school.
SA_MB_nadakarikaHK.history: Attributed in the tradition to Rāmakaṇṭha (the Kashmirian Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II, son of Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha), and transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with Aghoraśiva's commentary. Rāmakaṇṭha II is among the major 10th-century systematizers of dualist Śaivism.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_paramoksanirasakarikaHK.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Paramokṣanirāsakārikā
SA_MB_paramoksanirasakarikaHK.author: Sadyojyoti(s)
SA_MB_paramoksanirasakarikaHK.genre: polemical/philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_paramoksanirasakarikaHK.summary: A polemical verse treatise that refutes rival (para) conceptions of liberation (mokṣa), surveying and rejecting the soteriologies of other schools (such as those that posit liberation as annihilation, as merger, or as a quality-bearing state) in order to establish the Śaiva Siddhānta view. It is the school's classic doxographic critique of competing theories of release.
SA_MB_paramoksanirasakarikaHK.history: One of Sadyojyoti's authentic doctrinal works, transmitted in the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa; it received a commentary by Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II and is well known through modern critical editions and Alex Watson's studies of Śaiva refutations of rival mokṣa theories.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_ratnatrayapariksaHK.title: Śrīkaṇṭha: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Ratnatrayaparīkṣā
SA_MB_ratnatrayapariksaHK.author: Śrīkaṇṭha
SA_MB_ratnatrayapariksaHK.tradition: Hindu; Śaiva Siddhānta (dualist Śaivism), Kashmirian lineage
SA_MB_ratnatrayapariksaHK.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_ratnatrayapariksaHK.date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (Śrīkaṇṭha, Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta lineage)
SA_MB_ratnatrayapariksaHK.summary: An 'examination of the three gems' (ratnatraya), i.e. the three foundational categories of Śaiva Siddhānta — pati (the Lord/Śiva), paśu (the bound soul) and pāśa (bonds) — establishing their natures and mutual relations within the dualist system. It is a compact doctrinal analysis of the school's core triadic metaphysics.
SA_MB_ratnatrayapariksaHK.history: Attributed to Śrīkaṇṭha, one of the 10th-century Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta authors; transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with the commentary of Aghoraśiva. The title and its threefold scheme were influential in later Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta theology.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_tattvaprakasaHK.title: Bhojadeva: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvaprakāśa
SA_MB_tattvaprakasaHK.author: Bhojadeva (King Bhoja of Dhārā, Paramāra dynasty)
SA_MB_tattvaprakasaHK.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_tattvaprakasaHK.date_estimate: c. 1018-1060 CE (reign of King Bhoja, early 11th c.)
SA_MB_tattvaprakasaHK.summary: The 'illumination of the principles', a concise and lucid metaphysical compendium that condenses the vast scriptural corpus of Śaiva Siddhānta into a systematic exposition of its tattvas (reality-principles) and the pati-paśu-pāśa scheme. Traditionally the first text studied in the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa, it serves as the standard introduction to dualist Śaiva metaphysics.
SA_MB_tattvaprakasaHK.history: Attributed to the famed polymath-king Bhoja of Dhārā, who is said to have distilled the Siddhānta scriptures into this single treatise; it is transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with the commentary of Aghoraśiva and remained a foundational teaching text in both Sanskritic and Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_tattvasamgrahaHK.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvasaṃgraha
SA_MB_tattvasamgrahaHK.author: Sadyojyoti(s)
SA_MB_tattvasamgrahaHK.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_tattvasamgrahaHK.summary: A 'compendium of the principles', a brief verse summary of the tattvas (reality-categories) of Śaiva Siddhānta, setting out the constituents of the cosmos and the bound soul's situation within the dualist hierarchy of pati, paśu and pāśa. It distills the school's ontology into a teaching-friendly outline.
SA_MB_tattvasamgrahaHK.history: Counted among Sadyojyoti's authentic works and transmitted in the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with Aghoraśiva's commentary. (Not to be confused with the Buddhist Tattvasaṃgraha of Śāntarakṣita, an entirely different text.)
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_tattvatrayanirnayaHK.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvatrayanirṇaya
SA_MB_tattvatrayanirnayaHK.author: Sadyojyoti(s)
SA_MB_tattvatrayanirnayaHK.genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
SA_MB_tattvatrayanirnayaHK.summary: A 'determination of the three principles', a short treatise establishing the three eternal realities of Śaiva Siddhānta — pati (Śiva), paśu (the soul) and pāśa (bonds) — and their definitions. It is one of the earliest systematic statements of the school's signature triadic dualist ontology.
SA_MB_tattvatrayanirnayaHK.history: An authentic work of Sadyojyoti, transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa and provided with a commentary (vṛtti) by Aghoraśiva; it is frequently cited in studies of early dualist Śaiva metaphysics.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

SA_MB_brhattantrasaaraHK.title: Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa: Bṛhattantrasāra
SA_MB_brhattantrasaaraHK.author: Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa Bhaṭṭācārya
SA_MB_brhattantrasaaraHK.date_estimate: late 16th c. CE (Kṛṣṇānanda active c. 1575 CE in Bengal)
SA_MB_brhattantrasaaraHK.summary: A massive and authoritative compendium ('great essence of the tantras') that systematizes Śākta-tantric ritual practice — mantras, deity worship (including Kālī pūjā), yantras, initiation and other rites — drawing on some 170 tantric scriptures. It became the standard practical handbook of Bengali tantric Brahmanical worship and is still widely used today.
SA_MB_brhattantrasaaraHK.history: Composed in late-16th-century Bengal by Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa; the work was central to the consolidation of tantric orthodoxy in early-modern Bengal and is traditionally credited with shaping the iconography and ritual of Kālī worship there.
related_works: the c. 170 tantric scriptures it compiles

SA_MB_cidgaganacandrika-HK.title: Śrīvatsa: Cidgaganacandrikā
SA_MB_cidgaganacandrika-HK.author: Śrīvatsa (also called Kālidāsa)
SA_MB_cidgaganacandrika-HK.tradition: Hindu; non-dual Śākta-Śaiva, Krama (Mahānaya/Kālīkrama) system of Kashmir Shaivism
SA_MB_cidgaganacandrika-HK.genre: philosophical stotra / commentary (in praise of Kālī)
SA_MB_cidgaganacandrika-HK.date_estimate: c. 13th-14th c. CE (disputed; sometimes placed earlier)
SA_MB_cidgaganacandrika-HK.summary: A devotional-philosophical poem of 312 verses in four sections (vimarśa), praising Kālī as the absolute consciousness and expounding the Krama doctrine of the goddess as the dynamic 'moonlight in the sky of consciousness' (cid-gagana-candrikā). It treats macrocosmic and microcosmic emanation and the Śākta path (śāktopāya) to liberation, framed as a commentary/Pañcikā on Siddhanātha's Kramastuti.
SA_MB_cidgaganacandrika-HK.history: Composed by Śrīvatsa, a pupil in the lineage at Pūrṇapīṭha, as an expansion of the Krama hymn tradition; it is among the most important poetic-theological works of the Krama school and survives with commentaries such as the Divyacakorikā and Kramaprakāśikā.

SA_MB_diiksaaprakaasaHK.title: Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla: Dīkṣāprakāśa
SA_MB_diiksaaprakaasaHK.author: Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla
SA_MB_diiksaaprakaasaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely early-modern or modern (the named compiler/editor is not securely datable)
SA_MB_diiksaaprakaasaHK.summary: An 'illumination of initiation', a manual setting out the procedures and theology of tantric/Śaiva initiation (dīkṣā) — the rites, mantras and stages by which a teacher consecrates a disciple. The work is a practical liturgical handbook focused specifically on the initiatory ceremony.
SA_MB_diiksaaprakaasaHK.history: The text is attributed to Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla, of whom little is securely known; the title 'Dīkṣāprakāśa' could not be confidently matched to a well-documented historical work, so identification and dating remain uncertain.

SA_MB_Isaanapaddhati-I-HK.title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 1
SA_MB_Isaanapaddhati-I-HK.author: Īśānaśivagurudeva (Īśānaśiva)
SA_MB_Isaanapaddhati-I-HK.tradition: Hindu; Śaiva (self-described Śaivasiddhānta, syncretic 'Kerala Tantra' combining Śaiva, Vaiṣṇava and Śākta elements)
SA_MB_Isaanapaddhati-I-HK.date_estimate: c. 11th-12th c. CE (the work as a whole; cited by texts of the 15th-16th c.)
SA_MB_Isaanapaddhati-I-HK.summary: Volume 1 of a large encyclopedic ritual manual (paddhati) of the Kerala Śaiva tradition, covering the foundations of Śaiva ritual — including initiation (dīkṣā), daily and occasional worship, mantra-śāstra and the consecration of images and temples. It presents itself as a Śaivasiddhānta work while integrating Vaiṣṇava and Śākta deities and observances.
SA_MB_Isaanapaddhati-I-HK.history: Compiled by Īśānaśivagurudeva, the work is a key witness to the syncretic 'Kerala Tantra' and was edited in four parts (Tantra, Mantra, Kriyā, Yoga pādas) in the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series; it is regularly cited in studies of South Indian Śaiva ritual. The volume divisions are modern editorial sections of one continuous text.

SA_MB_isaanagurudevapaddhati-II-HK.title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 2
SA_MB_isaanagurudevapaddhati-II-HK.author: Īśānaśivagurudeva (Īśānaśiva)
SA_MB_isaanagurudevapaddhati-II-HK.date_estimate: c. 11th-12th c. CE (the work as a whole)
SA_MB_isaanagurudevapaddhati-II-HK.summary: Volume 2 (an editorial division of the continuous Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati) continuing the detailed exposition of Śaiva ritual practice — mantra-śāstra, the worship of deities, fire-rituals and the procedures of consecration and worship in the Kerala Tantric idiom. It belongs to the same encyclopedic manual as Volumes 1 and 3.
SA_MB_isaanagurudevapaddhati-II-HK.history: Part of the multi-volume Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition of Īśānaśivagurudeva's paddhati; the volume boundary is a modern editorial convenience rather than a distinct work. The text documents the syncretic ritual world of medieval Kerala Śaivism.

SA_MB_iisaanagurudevapaddhati-III-HK.title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 3
SA_MB_iisaanagurudevapaddhati-III-HK.author: Īśānaśivagurudeva (Īśānaśiva)
SA_MB_iisaanagurudevapaddhati-III-HK.date_estimate: c. 11th-12th c. CE (the work as a whole)
SA_MB_iisaanagurudevapaddhati-III-HK.summary: Volume 3 (a modern editorial division of the Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati) concluding the manual's coverage of Śaiva ritual and including its treatment of yoga and the more advanced/esoteric portions of the system, alongside the temple- and image-related rites characteristic of Kerala Tantra. It forms the final part of the single encyclopedic work.
SA_MB_iisaanagurudevapaddhati-III-HK.history: The closing volume of the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition of Īśānaśivagurudeva's paddhati; like the other volumes its boundary is editorial. The whole remains a primary source for medieval South Indian Śaiva ritual and theology.

SA_MB_IPV_HK.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī
SA_MB_IPV_HK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_IPV_HK.summary: Abhinavagupta's 'shorter' commentary (often called the laghvī-vimarśinī) on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational verse-work of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school. It expounds the doctrine that liberation consists in recognizing one's own self as identical with Īśvara/Śiva, the one absolute consciousness whose freedom (svātantrya) projects the entire universe, and develops the school's epistemology and metaphysics of self-awareness.
SA_MB_IPV_HK.history: A central work of Pratyabhijñā philosophy, written by the polymath Abhinavagupta as a commentary on Utpaladeva (fl. c. 925-975); it survives with the sub-commentary Bhāskarī of Bhāskarakaṇṭha and was edited and translated by K. C. Pandey and K. A. Subramania Iyer. It is one of the most influential statements of non-dual Śaiva idealism.

SA_MB_IPVV_1_hk.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 1
SA_MB_IPVV_1_hk.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_IPVV_1_hk.summary: Volume 1 of Abhinavagupta's 'longer' commentary (the bṛhatī-vimarśinī), which comments not on Utpaladeva's verses alone but on Utpaladeva's own extended auto-commentary (vivṛti) on the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā. This monumental work is the fullest exposition of Pratyabhijñā epistemology and metaphysics, treating perception, inference, the structure of consciousness and the recognition of the self as Śiva in exhaustive detail.
SA_MB_IPVV_1_hk.history: Composed after the shorter Vimarśinī, this commentary is doubly precious because Utpaladeva's vivṛti is largely lost, so Abhinavagupta's text preserves much of it; recovery of fragments of the vivṛti has been a major recent area of indological research. The volume division is editorial (the standard KSTS edition is in three parts).

SA_MB_IPVV_2_hk.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 2
SA_MB_IPVV_2_hk.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_IPVV_2_hk.summary: Volume 2 (an editorial division) of Abhinavagupta's longer commentary on Utpaladeva's vivṛti to the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, continuing the detailed analysis of the Pratyabhijñā system — typically covering the central chapters on knowledge (jñāna) and action (kriyā) as the two powers of the recognizing self. It forms part of the same continuous work as Volumes 1 and 3.
SA_MB_IPVV_2_hk.history: Part of the three-volume KSTS edition of Abhinavagupta's bṛhatī-vimarśinī; the volume boundary is editorial. The work is a primary source for the mature philosophy of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism.

SA_MB_IPVV_3_hk.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 3
SA_MB_IPVV_3_hk.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_IPVV_3_hk.summary: Volume 3 (an editorial division) concluding Abhinavagupta's longer commentary on Utpaladeva's vivṛti, completing the exposition of the Pratyabhijñā 'Recognition' philosophy — typically including the final sections on the categories of reality (tattvas) and the practical/soteriological dimension of recognizing the self as Śiva. It is the closing part of the single monumental commentary.
SA_MB_IPVV_3_hk.history: The final volume of the KSTS three-part edition of the bṛhatī-vimarśinī; the division is editorial. Together with the shorter Vimarśinī it represents the philosophical summit of the Pratyabhijñā school and was deeply influential on later Indian non-dualist thought.

SA_MB_IISVARASIDDHI_hk.title: Utpaladeva: Īśvarasiddhi
SA_MB_IISVARASIDDHI_hk.author: Utpaladeva
SA_MB_IISVARASIDDHI_hk.summary: The Īśvarasiddhi ('Proof of the Lord') is one of the three short demonstrative treatises (Siddhitrayī) of Utpaladeva, the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') philosopher of nondual Kashmir Shaivism. It argues for the existence of Īśvara (Śiva) as the supreme conscious Subject (the highest 'I'), the ground of all cognition and being. Together with the Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi and Sambandhasiddhi it supplements his major work, the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā.
SA_MB_IISVARASIDDHI_hk.history: Utpaladeva was a pupil of Somānanda and the teacher of the lineage that culminated in Abhinavagupta, who built the Pratyabhijñā system into the philosophical core of Trika Shaivism. The Siddhitrayī survives with Utpaladeva's own auto-commentary (vṛtti); his works were later somewhat overshadowed by Abhinavagupta's more elaborate exegesis.

SA_MB_jnaanakaarikaaHK.title: Matsyendranātha: Jñānakārikā
SA_MB_jnaanakaarikaaHK.author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (school of Matsyendranātha)
SA_MB_jnaanakaarikaaHK.date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (disputed; transmitted within the early Kaula corpus)
SA_MB_jnaanakaarikaaHK.summary: The Jñānakārikā is a short Kaula doctrinal text in verse belonging to the tradition associated with Matsyendranātha, founder of the Yoginī Kaula school. It treats esoteric Kaula gnosis (jñāna): the nature of kula, the union of Śiva and Śakti, and the inner practice leading to liberation. It is one of the minor texts grouped with the Kaulajñānanirṇaya.
SA_MB_jnaanakaarikaaHK.history: It was edited and published by P. C. Bagchi alongside the Kaulajñānanirṇaya as part of the 'minor texts of the school of Matsyendranātha.' Attribution to the semi-legendary Matsyendranātha is traditional rather than securely historical; the texts reflect an early-medieval Kaula milieu later honored by Abhinavagupta.

SA_MB_kaamakalaavilaasaHK.title: Puṇyānanda: Kāmakalāvilāsa
SA_MB_kaamakalaavilaasaHK.author: Puṇyānanda (Puṇyānandanātha)
SA_MB_kaamakalaavilaasaHK.summary: The Kāmakalāvilāsa ('Play of the Kāmakalā') is a concise Śrīvidyā work expounding the cosmogonic emanation of the universe from the supreme Goddess through the kāmakalā—the union of consciousness (prakāśa) and reflective awareness (vimarśa), bindu, nāda, and the unfolding of the Śrīcakra. It is a foundational metaphysical text for the worship of Tripurasundarī.
SA_MB_kaamakalaavilaasaHK.history: Puṇyānanda was an adherent of the Hādimata of Śrīvidyā and is credited with a commentary on the Yoginīhṛdaya. The Kāmakalāvilāsa is traditionally read with the Cidvallī commentary of Naṭanānanda(nātha); it was edited and translated into English by Arthur Avalon (Sir John Woodroffe) in the Tantric Texts series.

SA_MB_kamkaalamaaliniitantraHK.title: Kaṅkālamālinītantra
SA_MB_kamkaalamaaliniitantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kamkaalamaaliniitantraHK.date_estimate: medieval (c. 11th-16th c. CE; precise date uncertain)
SA_MB_kamkaalamaaliniitantraHK.summary: The Kaṅkālamālinītantra ('Tantra of Her who is Garlanded with Skeletons/Bones') is a short Śākta tantra of five paṭalas focused on the worship of the goddess Kālī. Cast as a dialogue between Śiva and the Goddess, it gives mantras, ritual visualizations, mythic narratives, and allegorical teaching on consciousness and liberation, belonging to the Kālīkula (Kālī-centered) current.
SA_MB_kamkaalamaaliniitantraHK.history: Its colophons ascribe it to the Dakṣiṇāmnāya (Southern transmission). It survives in printed Sanskrit and Bengali editions and is associated with the eastern Indian (Bengal) tantric milieu; like many such tantras it is anonymous and of uncertain date.

SA_MB_kaulajnaananirnayaHK.title: Matsyendranātha: Kaulajñānanirṇaya
SA_MB_kaulajnaananirnayaHK.author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (Matsyendrapāda)
SA_MB_kaulajnaananirnayaHK.date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (manuscript assigned to 9th-11th c.; tradition dates it earlier)
SA_MB_kaulajnaananirnayaHK.summary: The Kaulajñānanirṇaya ('Ascertainment of Kaula Gnosis') is the foundational scripture of the Yoginī Kaula school attributed to Matsyendranātha. In some 24 sections (paṭalas) it expounds the meaning of kula, Śiva-Śakti nonduality, bindu and nāda, modes of worship, the acquisition of yogic powers (siddhis), and esoteric—often erotic and 'left-handed'—practices for liberation, written in deliberately veiled 'twilight language.'
SA_MB_kaulajnaananirnayaHK.history: Edited from a Nepalese manuscript by P. C. Bagchi (1934), it is a key witness to early Kaula Tantra. Although its author already knew earlier Kaula scriptures (the 'Kulāgama'), Matsyendranātha is celebrated as a founder of Kaulism; Abhinavagupta honored him as a patriarch of yoga. It is central to the Nātha and Yoginī Kaula traditions.

SA_MB_kaulopanisadsHK.title: Kaulopaniṣad
SA_MB_kaulopanisadsHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaulopanisadsHK.tradition: Hindu (Śākta/Kaula; classed as a Śākta Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_kaulopanisadsHK.summary: The Kaulopaniṣad (Kaula Upaniṣad) is a brief esoteric text affiliated with the Atharvaveda (Saubhāgyakāṇḍa) that expounds Kaula and Śākta-Vedānta doctrine in aphoristic form. It emphasizes inner spiritual realization over external ritual and is intended only for the qualified (adhikārin), treating the nondual identity of the Self with the supreme reality.
SA_MB_kaulopanisadsHK.history: Considered controversial for its Kaula leanings, it was sometimes excluded from Upaniṣadic collections. It is preserved with the Sanskrit commentary of Bhāskararāya (Bhāskara Rāi, 18th c.) and was published by Arthur Avalon in the Tantric Texts series (Kaula and Other Upanishads, 1922).

SA_MB_kramadipikaHK.title: Kramadīpikā
SA_MB_kramadipikaHK.author: Keśava Kāśmīrī Bhaṭṭa (text attributed to him; 'Rasikamohana Caṭṭopādhyāya' is the modern editor, not the author)
SA_MB_kramadipikaHK.date_estimate: text c. 15th-16th c. CE (Keśava Kāśmīrī fl. late 15th-16th c.)
SA_MB_kramadipikaHK.summary: The Kramadīpikā ('Lamp of the Sequence') is a Vaiṣṇava tantric/āgamic ritual manual centered on the worship of Kṛṣṇa-Gopāla and Rādhā-Govinda. Arranged in paṭalas, it sets out mantras (including the Gopāla and Vāsudeva 'mṛtyu' mantras), the sequence of ritual installation and worship, and instructions for devotees.
SA_MB_kramadipikaHK.history: It is traditionally ascribed to Keśava Kāśmīrī Bhaṭṭa of the Nimbārka Sampradāya and was influential in the ritual of the Jagannātha temple at Purī and quoted in the Gauḍīya Haribhaktivilāsa. The name 'Rasikamohana Caṭṭopādhyāya' in the title hint refers to Rasik Mohan Chatterji, the 19th-century editor/publisher associated with printed Tantric texts, not the original author.

SA_MB_kubjikaatantramHK.title: Kubjikātantra
SA_MB_kubjikaatantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kubjikaatantramHK.summary: Kubjikātantra denotes the scriptural corpus of the cult of the goddess Kubjikā ('the crooked/hunchbacked one'), the supreme Śakti and personification of kuṇḍalinī in the Paścimāmnāya (Western Transmission) of Kaula Shaivism. The root scripture is the Kubjikāmatatantra, which expounds the goddess, the deity Navātman, the maṇḍala, mantras, and yogic-ritual practice. Kubjikā is visualized as six-faced and twelve-armed, garlanded with skulls and serpents.
SA_MB_kubjikaatantramHK.history: The Western Transmission is one of the four 'āmnāya' currents of Kaula Shaivism; the earliest evidence for the Kubjikā cult is from the tenth century, and its scriptures were shaped by both the Trika and Krama systems. The tradition flourished especially in Nepal, where its manuscripts are best preserved.

SA_MB_kularnavaHK.title: Kulārṇavatantra
SA_MB_kularnavaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kularnavaHK.summary: The Kulārṇavatantra ('Ocean of the Kula') is a leading scripture of the Kaula school of Śāktism, comprising some seventeen chapters and around 2,000 verses cast as a dialogue between Śiva and the Goddess. It expounds Kaula worship—including the five 'M's (pañcamakāra)—the path to Self-realization through detachment, and devotes notable chapters to the guru-disciple (guru-śiṣya) relationship, mantra, and ritual discipline.
SA_MB_kularnavaHK.history: Widely regarded as one of the most authoritative and frequently quoted Kaula tantras, it is extensively cited in later tantric literature and digests. Arthur Avalon (Woodroffe) published an influential edition and English readings of the text.

SA_MB_LallesvarivakyaniHK.title: Lalleśvarī: Lalleśvarīvākyāni
SA_MB_LallesvarivakyaniHK.author: Lalleśvarī (Lal Dĕd / Lalla)
SA_MB_LallesvarivakyaniHK.tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Shaivism; mystic devotional, with Sufi resonances)
SA_MB_LallesvarivakyaniHK.summary: The Lalleśvarīvākyāni ('Sayings of Lalleśvarī') are the terse mystical verses (vākhs) of the 14th-century Kashmiri yoginī Lal Dĕd, rooted in nondual Kashmir Shaivism. They express the inward quest for the Self/Śiva, the futility of empty ritual and ascetic extremism, the control of breath and mind, and direct experiential realization, in a vivid vernacular voice.
