SA_MB_LallesvarivakyaniHK.history: Composed in early Kashmiri (with Sanskritic and emerging Persian elements), the vākhs are among the earliest attested literary works in the Kashmiri language. Transmitted orally for centuries, they were first systematically recorded in writing in the early 20th century (George Grierson's Lallā-Vākyāni, 1920). Lal Dĕd is revered by both Hindus and Muslims in Kashmir.

SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa2HK.title: Śitikaṇṭha: Mahānayaprakāśa
SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa2HK.author: Śitikaṇṭha
SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa2HK.summary: The Mahānayaprakāśa ('Illumination of the Great Way') of Śitikaṇṭha is a Krama-tradition work of about ninety-four verses in Apabhraṃśa ('Old Kashmiri') accompanied by a Sanskrit commentary. It describes the inner structure of consciousness in relation to body and world and the esoteric, antinomian ('left-handed') practice of the Krama, devoted to the fierce goddesses led by Kālī.
SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa2HK.history: It is one of three distinct Kashmiri Krama texts bearing the title Mahānayaprakāśa (others by Arṇasiṃha and an anonymous author). Notable for its language ideology and Old-Kashmiri verse, it remains an understudied but important witness to the radical nondualist Krama school.

SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa1HK.title: Mahānayaprakāśa (anonymous)
SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa1HK.author: anonymous (one of several Krama works of this title; sometimes ascribed to Arṇasiṃha)
SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa1HK.summary: This is the Sanskrit Mahānayaprakāśa ('Illumination of the Great Way'), an anonymous Krama treatise distinct from Śitikaṇṭha's Old-Kashmiri work of the same name. It systematically expounds the Krama doctrine of the sequential (krama) emanation and reabsorption of reality through the cycle of goddesses, the nature of nondual consciousness, and the esoteric soteriology of the Kālī-centered school.
SA_MB_mahanayaprakasa1HK.history: There are at least three Kashmiri Krama texts titled Mahānayaprakāśa—one by Śitikaṇṭha, one by a disciple named Arṇasiṃha, and one anonymous. The 'unknown author' designation here reflects this ambiguity in the catalogue record; all belong to the Krama lineage of Kashmir Shaivism.

SA_MB_maharthamanjariHK.title: Maheśvarānanda: Mahārthamañjarī
SA_MB_maharthamanjariHK.author: Maheśvarānanda
SA_MB_maharthamanjariHK.tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Shaivism, Krama / Mahārtha; composed in South India)
SA_MB_maharthamanjariHK.summary: The Mahārthamañjarī ('Bouquet of the Great Truth') is a compact work of about seventy verses (originally in Mahārāṣṭrī Prakrit with the author's own Sanskrit rendering) expounding the 'Great Truth' (mahārtha) of the Krama/Kālīkrama tradition. With the author's Parimala auto-commentary, it presents Krama metaphysics integrated with Pratyabhijñā recognition philosophy.
SA_MB_maharthamanjariHK.history: Maheśvarānanda, working in South India, was an exponent of the Kālīkrama who quotes earlier Krama authorities (e.g., the Kramakeli) and thereby transmits the Kashmiri Krama into the South. The text is an important late synthesis of Krama and Pratyabhijñā thought.

SA_MB_makutaagamaHK.title: Makuṭāgama
SA_MB_makutaagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_makutaagamaHK.date_estimate: early-medieval (1st millennium CE; precise date uncertain)
SA_MB_makutaagamaHK.summary: The Makuṭāgama (Makuṭa Āgama) is one of the twenty-eight Siddhānta Śaivāgamas, scriptures regarded as revealed by Śiva that prescribe temple construction, consecration, ritual, and worship. It emphasizes the 'crown/regal' (makuṭa) aspect of Śiva—his sovereign authority—and is the āgama traditionally followed in the daily worship of the Naṭarāja temple at Chidambaram.
SA_MB_makutaagamaHK.history: Counted among the canonical Siddhānta āgamas of South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta, it survives mainly in manuscript and is used liturgically in temple practice. Like other āgamas it is anonymous and of uncertain date, transmitted through priestly lineages.

SA_MB_mrgendraHK.title: Mṛgendrāgama
SA_MB_mrgendraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mrgendraHK.date_estimate: c. 6th-9th c. CE (the influential Vidyāpāda relatively early)
SA_MB_mrgendraHK.summary: The Mṛgendrāgama (Mṛgendratantra) is a Śaiva Siddhānta scripture, treated as an upāgama / supplement of the Kāmikāgama. Its four pādas cover doctrine (vidyā), yoga, ritual (kriyā), and conduct (caryā); the Vidyāpāda in particular is a key statement of Siddhānta metaphysics—the categories of pati (Lord), paśu (bound soul), and pāśa (bonds).
SA_MB_mrgendraHK.history: It is one of the four āgamas relied upon by Śaiva Siddhānta that fall outside the standard list of twenty-eight. The tenth-century Kashmiri exegete Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha wrote an authoritative commentary (vṛtti) on it, and it was quoted and emulated by twelfth-century South Indian Siddhānta authors such as Aghoraśiva.

SA_MB_netratantra_HK.title: Netratantra
SA_MB_netratantra_HK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_netratantra_HK.tradition: Hindu (Śaiva, Mantramārga; non-Saiddhāntika, later read in Kashmiri nondualism)
SA_MB_netratantra_HK.date_estimate: c. 700-850 CE (composed in or before the 9th c., Kashmir)
SA_MB_netratantra_HK.summary: The Netratantra ('Tantra of the Eye'), also called Mṛtyujit and Amṛteśavidhāna, centers on the cult of Amṛteśvara(bhairava) ('Lord of Ambrosia') and Amṛtalakṣmī. As a dialogue in which Bhairava instructs the Goddess, it teaches metaphysics, cosmology, and soteriology together with apotropaic ritual, yoga, and the netra-mantra (Mṛtyuñjaya) for conquering death and warding off ills—addressing a deliberately broad range of deities and aims.
SA_MB_netratantra_HK.history: A non-Saiddhāntika Mantramārga scripture, it was provided with an influential commentary (Netroddyota) by the 11th-century Kashmiri Pratyabhijñā master Kṣemarāja, who read it through a nondualist lens; it is an important source for early Śaiva ritual and royal religion.

SA_MB_naresvarapariiksaaHK.title: Sadyojyoti: Nareśvaraparīkṣā
SA_MB_naresvarapariiksaaHK.author: Sadyojyoti(s)
SA_MB_naresvarapariiksaaHK.date_estimate: c. late 7th-early 8th c. CE (Sadyojyoti fl. c. 675-725 CE)
SA_MB_naresvarapariiksaaHK.summary: The Nareśvaraparīkṣā ('Examination of [the relation of] Man and the Lord') is a foundational philosophical treatise of dualist Śaiva Siddhānta by Sadyojyoti, the earliest systematizer of Siddhānta theology in Sanskrit. It investigates the natures of the bound soul (nara/paśu) and the Lord (īśvara/pati) and their relation, defending the Siddhānta categories of Lord, soul, and bonds against rival positions.
SA_MB_naresvarapariiksaaHK.history: Sadyojyoti grounded his exposition in scriptures such as the Rauravāgama and Svāyambhuvasūtrasaṅgraha. The Nareśvaraparīkṣā received an important commentary (prakāśa) by the Kashmiri theologian Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha, and the work shaped the later, highly systematic Saiddhāntika exegesis of tenth-century Kashmir and twelfth-century South India.

SA_MB_pancaratraraksa1HK.title: Vedāntadeśika: Pāñcarātrarakṣā
SA_MB_pancaratraraksa1HK.author: Vedānta Deśika (Veṅkaṭanātha)
SA_MB_pancaratraraksa1HK.summary: The Pāñcarātrarakṣā ('Defense of the Pāñcarātra') is Vedānta Deśika's apologetic treatise vindicating the scriptural authority (prāmāṇya) and validity of the Pāñcarātra Āgama against critics. It argues for the self-validity of the Pāñcarātra Saṃhitās and the universality of Pāñcarātra ritual and ethics, defending Vaiṣṇava temple and domestic worship within the Śrī Vaiṣṇava tradition.
SA_MB_pancaratraraksa1HK.history: Vedānta Deśika (Veṅkaṭanātha), the great Śrī Vaiṣṇava ācārya, builds on Yāmunācārya's earlier Āgamaprāmāṇya in defending Pāñcarātra orthodoxy. The work is one of his several 'rakṣā' (defense) treatises and remains authoritative in Śrī Vaiṣṇava liturgical and theological tradition.

SA_MB_paramarthasaraHK.title: Abhinavagupta: Paramārthasāra
SA_MB_paramarthasaraHK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_paramarthasaraHK.summary: The Paramārthasāra ('Essence of the Supreme Truth') is Abhinavagupta's concise compendium of nondual Trika Shaivism in 105 verses. It presents the metaphysics of Śiva as universal consciousness, the soul's bondage and liberation, the emanation of the tattvas, and the path of recognition (pratyabhijñā) and grace leading to identity with Śiva.
SA_MB_paramarthasaraHK.history: Abhinavagupta refashioned an earlier non-Śaiva Paramārthasāra (the Ādhārakārikā attributed to Ādiśeṣa / Śeṣamuni) into a Trika scripture. The work is a popular, accessible summary of the system elaborated at length in his Tantrāloka and Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī, and was commented upon (e.g., by Yogarāja).

SA_MB_paraatriisikaalaghuvrttiHK.title: Parātrīśikālaghuvṛtti
SA_MB_paraatriisikaalaghuvrttiHK.author: Abhinavagupta (the catalogue 'unknown' author hint is incorrect)
SA_MB_paraatriisikaalaghuvrttiHK.summary: The Parātrīśikālaghuvṛtti (also Parātriṃśikālaghuvṛtti, alias Anuttaratattvavimarśinī) is Abhinavagupta's short commentary on the Parātrīśikā (Parātriṃśikā), the c. 36-verse Trika tantra esteemed as a summit of Kashmir Shaivism. It expounds the Supreme Reality (anuttara/parā) and the theory and practice of mantra and phonematic emanation as the means of realization.
SA_MB_paraatriisikaalaghuvrttiHK.history: This is the concise of Abhinavagupta's two commentaries on the Parātrīśikā; the other is the much longer Parātrīśikāvivaraṇa, and the two differ in interpretation as well as length. The text is a core source for Trika mantra-theory and was edited within the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies.

SA_MB_paraatrisikaataatparyadiipikaaHK.title: Somānanda II: Parātriśikātātparyadīpikā and Śāktavijñāna
SA_MB_paraatrisikaataatparyadiipikaaHK.author: Somānanda (II)
SA_MB_paraatrisikaataatparyadiipikaaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely 2nd millennium CE (a later Somānanda, distinct from the c. 900 CE founder of the Pratyabhijñā)
SA_MB_paraatrisikaataatparyadiipikaaHK.summary: Two works grouped under the name Somānanda: the Parātriśikātātparyadīpikā, a commentary elucidating the 'inner purport' of the short but highly esteemed Parātriśikā (Parātrīśikā), the celebrated dialogue between Bhairava and the Goddess on the supreme Power (Parā) drawn from the Rudrayāmala; and the Śāktavijñāna, a non-dual Śākta-Trika exposition of the knowledge (vijñāna) of Śakti. Together they treat the metaphysics of the highest goddess-energy, the emanation of the phonemic cosmos, and the recognition of consciousness as supreme reality.
SA_MB_paraatrisikaataatparyadiipikaaHK.history: The famous Somānanda is the early (c. 900 CE) author of the Śivadṛṣṭi and teacher of Utpaladeva; the 'Somānanda II' label here flags a later author of the same name to whom these Parātriśikā-related works are attributed, since the great Somānanda's authentic oeuvre is the Śivadṛṣṭi. The Parātriśikā itself attracted commentaries by Abhinavagupta (Vivaraṇa and Laghuvṛtti) and later Kashmirian and Śākta authors; attributions of further glosses such as this Dīpikā are sometimes uncertain.

SA_MB_ParaatrisikaavivrttiHK.title: Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma: Parātriśikāvivṛti
SA_MB_ParaatrisikaavivrttiHK.author: Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma (Lakṣmīrāma)
SA_MB_ParaatrisikaavivrttiHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely 17th-19th c. CE (a late Kashmirian Śaiva scholar)
SA_MB_ParaatrisikaavivrttiHK.summary: A vivṛti (detailed gloss) on the Parātriśikā (Parātrīśikā), the short Trika scripture in which Bhairava expounds to the Goddess the nature of the supreme Power (Parā), the anuttara state, and the deployment of phonemes (mātṛkā) underlying creation. The commentary unpacks the dense esoteric verses in the non-dual Kashmirian framework.
SA_MB_ParaatrisikaavivrttiHK.history: The Parātriśikā, regarded as the essence of Trika, was commented upon most famously by Abhinavagupta; Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma belongs to the later Kashmirian pandit lineage ('Rājānaka' being an honorific borne by Kashmirian Brahman scholars) who continued to transmit and gloss the Trika and Pratyabhijñā corpus into the early modern period.

SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-I-HK.title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 1
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-I-HK.author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-I-HK.summary: An encyclopaedic 'Ocean of Preliminary Ritual Practice' (puraścaryā = the preparatory observances qualifying a practitioner to use a mantra), compiling rules for initiation, mantra-japa, homa, and the disciplines preceding tantric worship. Volume 1 covers the opening sections on the qualifications, times, places, and procedures of puraścaryā. The work draws extensively on Tantras, Purāṇas, and dharmaśāstra.
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-I-HK.history: Composed by Pratāpasiṃha Śāha (Pratap Singh Shah), the 18th-century king of Nepal and a devout Tantric, who is named as its royal author; it became a standard reference manual for tantric ritual practice and was printed in multiple volumes. Manuscripts survive in Nepal and have been digitised in preservation programmes.

SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-2-HK.title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 2
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-2-HK.author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-2-HK.summary: The second volume of the 'Ocean of Preliminary Ritual Practice', continuing the systematic treatment of puraścaryā and tantric sadhana: rules of mantra repetition, oblation (homa), feeding, ritual offerings, and associated observances. It assembles citations from Tantras, Purāṇas, and smṛti to authorise the procedures.
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-2-HK.history: Part of the multi-volume digest authored by King Pratāpasiṃha Śāha of Nepal in the late 18th century; widely used as an authoritative compendium of tantric ritual, transmitted in manuscript and later print editions.

SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-3-HK.title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 3
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-3-HK.author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-3-HK.summary: The third volume of the 'Ocean of Preliminary Ritual Practice', concluding the digest's exposition of puraścaryā and related tantric worship, including the consummation of mantra-sadhana and the rituals that complete the preparatory cycle. Like the other volumes it is a vast anthology of authoritative scriptural citations organised topically.
SA_MB_purascaryaarnava-3-HK.history: Final portion of the encyclopaedic ritual digest composed by King Pratāpasiṃha Śāha of Nepal in the late 18th century, a landmark reference work for Hindu tantric ritual practice.

SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAAhk.title: Kṣemarāja: Parāprāveśikā
SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAAhk.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAAhk.summary: A concise prose primer of non-dual Kashmirian Śaiva (Trika) philosophy by Kṣemarāja, opening with an introductory aphorism followed by a short gloss. 'Entry into the Supreme' centres on Śakti in her aspect as Parā (the Supreme Power) and lays out the tattvas (categories of universal manifestation) within the Śaivādvaita framework of consciousness as the sole reality.
SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAAhk.history: Kṣemarāja was the foremost disciple of Abhinavagupta and one of the most prolific systematisers of Kashmir Shaivism, also author of the Pratyabhijñāhṛdayam, Spandanirṇaya, and Śivasūtravimarśinī. The Parāprāveśikā functions as an accessible doctrinal introduction to the school's metaphysics.

SA_MB_mahaanirvaanatantramHK.title: Mahānirvāṇa Tantra
SA_MB_mahaanirvaanatantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mahaanirvaanatantramHK.date_estimate: c. late 18th c. CE (disputed; some place portions earlier, but the received text is generally dated to the 1700s, likely Bengal)
SA_MB_mahaanirvaanatantramHK.summary: A celebrated Śākta Tantra cast as a dialogue between Śiva and the Goddess (Pārvatī), expounding the worship of the supreme Brahman as the Goddess in the Kali age, mantra and ritual practice, the pañcatattva, social and moral duties, and a notably reformist, dharma-conscious ethical code. It is unusual among Tantras for its strong emphasis on conventional morality and law.
SA_MB_mahaanirvaanatantramHK.history: The text rose to prominence largely through the edition with Hariharānanda Bhāratī's commentary (Hariharānanda was the guru of Rammohan Roy) and the English translation by Sir John Woodroffe ('Arthur Avalon'); first printed in the 19th century. Its date and integrity are debated, with many scholars regarding it as a relatively late composition, possibly partly shaped by 18th-19th century reformist concerns.

SA_MB_kriyaasaara2HK.title: Nīlakaṇṭha: Kriyāsāra, volume 2
SA_MB_kriyaasaara2HK.author: Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya
SA_MB_kriyaasaara2HK.genre: philosophical treatise / commentary on the Brahmasūtra
SA_MB_kriyaasaara2HK.date_estimate: uncertain; generally placed in the medieval-to-early modern period (post-Śaṅkara), perhaps c. 14th-16th c. CE
SA_MB_kriyaasaara2HK.summary: A major Vīraśaiva work that expounds the 'Essence of Practice', presenting Vīraśaiva ritual and doctrine, and read alongside Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya's interpretation of Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra from a Śivādvaita (qualified non-dualist Śaiva) standpoint. Volume 2 continues the systematic exposition of doctrine and observance.
SA_MB_kriyaasaara2HK.history: Authored by Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya, a Vīraśaiva theologian; the Sanskrit text was critically edited by R. Ramasastri and N. S. Venkatanathacharya and published by the University of Mysore (1954-1958). It is an important source for Vīraśaiva (Lingāyat) Vedānta.

SA_MB_kalpavrksaHK.title: Sāhib Kaul: Kalpavṛkṣa
SA_MB_kalpavrksaHK.author: Sāhib Kaul (Sāhib Kaula)
SA_MB_kalpavrksaHK.tradition: Hindu, Kashmir Shaivism / Śākta (Pratyabhijñā fused with Advaita Vedānta)
SA_MB_kalpavrksaHK.summary: An extraordinary 'Wish-fulfilling Tree', a multilingual crossword-like figure poem (carmen cancellatum) originally displayed on a large cloth, in which hidden inscribed texts ('intexts') are read out of a grid using roughly thirty languages. It is described as the largest carmen cancellatum known in world literature, a virtuoso devotional-poetic creation.
SA_MB_kalpavrksaHK.history: Composed in 17th-century Kashmir by Sāhib Kaul, a prolific and ambitious Śākta-Śaiva poet who also wrote the Devīnāmavilāsa, the Śyāmāpaddhati, and several ritual handbooks. Alexis Sanderson has argued Sāhib Kaul and his lineage introduced eastern-Indian Śākta elements into Kashmir and fused Advaita Vedānta with Pratyabhijñā. The Sanskrit text was edited for the first time by Jürgen Hanneder.

SA_MB_nityaasodasikaarnavaHK.title: Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇavatantra with the commentary called Setubandha
SA_MB_nityaasodasikaarnavaHK.author: Commentary (Setubandha) by Bhāskararāya Makhin; mūla tantra anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_nityaasodasikaarnavaHK.date_estimate: mūla tantra: 1st millennium / early-mid 2nd millennium CE (a portion of the Vāmakeśvaratantra); Setubandha commentary: 18th c. CE (Bhāskararāya, c. 1690-1785)
SA_MB_nityaasodasikaarnavaHK.summary: The Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava ('Ocean of the Sixteen Nityā goddesses'), a principal Śrīvidyā scripture of about four hundred verses in five chapters dealing with the external and internal worship of the goddess Tripurasundarī, forming the first half of the Vāmakeśvaratantra. The accompanying Setubandha ('Building of the Bridge') is Bhāskararāya's authoritative technical commentary on mantraśāstra and the goddess's worship.
SA_MB_nityaasodasikaarnavaHK.history: The Setubandha is Bhāskararāya Makhin's magnum opus; he was an 18th-century polymath who composed around forty works across Vedānta, Mīmāṃsā, grammar, Nyāya, and mantraśāstra, and remains the most prestigious commentator in the living Śrīvidyā tradition. Śivānanda earlier wrote the first South Indian commentary on the same tantra.

SA_MB_iisvarapratyabhijnakaumudiHK.title: Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara: Īśvarapratyabhijñākaumudī
SA_MB_iisvarapratyabhijnakaumudiHK.author: Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara
SA_MB_iisvarapratyabhijnakaumudiHK.date_estimate: uncertain; a later commentary on Utpaladeva's IPK, likely 2nd millennium CE
SA_MB_iisvarapratyabhijnakaumudiHK.summary: A commentary (the 'Moonlight on the Recognition of the Lord') on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational philosophical treatise of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism. It elucidates the doctrine that liberation comes through recognising one's own self as identical with Śiva, the supreme conscious agent, engaging the dialectic with Buddhist epistemology.
SA_MB_iisvarapratyabhijnakaumudiHK.history: The Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā (c. early 10th c. CE) is the cornerstone of the Pratyabhijñā and was most famously glossed by Utpaladeva himself (Vṛtti, Vivṛti) and by Abhinavagupta (Vimarśinī, Vivṛtivimarśinī). This Kaumudī by Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara represents a further, less widely studied commentary in that tradition.

SA_MB_saaktapramodaHK.title: Devanandanasiṃha: Śāktapramoda
SA_MB_saaktapramodaHK.author: Deva Nandan Singh (Devanandana Siṃha)
SA_MB_saaktapramodaHK.summary: A liturgical compendium ('Delight of the Śāktas') gathering sixteen independent ritual manuals. The first ten are devoted to the Ten Mahāvidyās (Kālī, Tārā, Ṣoḍaśī, Bhuvaneśvarī, Chinnamastā, Mātaṅgī, Tripurabhairavī, Dhūmāvatī, Bagalāmukhī, Kamalā); further sections cover the Kumārī Tantra and a pentad of deities (Gaṇeśa, Viṣṇu, Śiva, Durgā, Sūrya). Each provides dhyāna, mantra and yantra, ritual sequences, kavacas, and litanies of epithets.
SA_MB_saaktapramodaHK.history: Compiled and first published by Raja Deva Nandan Singh, an aristocratic zamindar of Muzaffarpur, Bihar, in the 19th century; it remains a widely used practical handbook for Śākta worship. A modern critical edition with English introduction was prepared by Madhu Khanna.

SA_MB_taaraabhaktisudhaarnavaHK.title: Narasiṃhaṭhakkura: Tārābhaktisudhārṇava
SA_MB_taaraabhaktisudhaarnavaHK.author: Narasiṃha Ṭhakkura (Narasimha, disciple of Gadādhara)
SA_MB_taaraabhaktisudhaarnavaHK.summary: An 'Ocean of the Nectar of Devotion to Tārā', a digest of tantric rituals for the worship of the goddess Tārā, one of the ten Mahāvidyās. Written mainly in lucid verse with occasional prose, it is divided into eleven tarangas ('waves') and compiles the dhyāna, mantras, yantras, and ritual procedures of Tārā's cult, treating theory only incidentally.
SA_MB_taaraabhaktisudhaarnavaHK.history: Composed by Narasiṃha Ṭhakkura, a disciple of Gadādhara, of the line of Ravikara of Mithila and fifth in descent from Govinda Ṭhakkura (author of the Kāvyapradīpa); he flourished c. 1668 CE. It draws on the Tārātantra, Brahmayāmala, Rudrayāmala, and related scriptures and is a standard source for Tārā worship.

SA_MB_saubhagyabhaskariHK.title: Lalitā Sahasranāma
SA_MB_saubhagyabhaskariHK.author: anonymous/traditional (framed as revealed by the Vāgdevatās; dialogue of Hayagrīva and Agastya)
SA_MB_saubhagyabhaskariHK.genre: stotra (sahasranāma, litany of a thousand names)
SA_MB_saubhagyabhaskariHK.date_estimate: c. 1st millennium CE (often placed mid-to-late 1st millennium; embedded in the Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa, date disputed)
SA_MB_saubhagyabhaskariHK.summary: A litany of one thousand names of the goddess Lalitā (Mahātripurasundarī), the supreme deity of the Śrīvidyā tradition, set within the Lalitopākhyāna of the Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa as a dialogue between Hayagrīva and the sage Agastya. The names encode the goddess's attributes, her form, the Śrīcakra, kuṇḍalinī, and esoteric Śrīvidyā doctrine, and the hymn is among the most widely recited Śākta stotras.
SA_MB_saubhagyabhaskariHK.history: Transmitted as part of the Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa's Lalitopākhyāna; its precise date is hard to fix and is generally regarded as later than the Purāṇa's core. It is the basis of Bhāskararāya's famous commentary Saubhāgyabhāskara and is central to living Śrīvidyā devotion.

SA_MB_sivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.title: Jñānaprakāśa (Śālivāṭipuranivāsi): Śivāgamādimāhātmya
SA_MB_sivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.author: Jñānaprakāśa (of Śālivāṭipura)
SA_MB_sivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval-to-early modern South Indian Śaiva author (likely 2nd millennium CE)
SA_MB_sivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.summary: A compilation ('Greatness of the Śivāgamas and related scriptures', also styled Śivāgamādimāhātmyasaṃgraha) of passages drawn from the Purāṇas and the Śaivāgamas assembled in order to demonstrate and establish the authority and validity of the Śaivāgamas as revealed scripture. It is an apologetic-doctrinal work defending the canonical standing of the Āgamic corpus.
SA_MB_sivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.history: Authored by Jñānaprakāśa, associated with Śālivāṭipura in the South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta milieu; the work serves the polemical purpose of vindicating Āgamic scriptural authority within the dualist Śaiva Siddhānta tradition.

SA_MB_vyomavyaapistavaHK.title: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha: Vyomavyāpistava
SA_MB_vyomavyaapistavaHK.author: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (II) (commentator); the Vyomavyāpin/Vyomavyāpistava hymn itself is a Śaiva mantra-stotra
SA_MB_vyomavyaapistavaHK.date_estimate: commentary c. late 10th c. CE (Rāmakaṇṭha II fl. c. 950-1000 CE)
SA_MB_vyomavyaapistavaHK.summary: The Vyomavyāpistava ('Hymn to Him who pervades the void/space') is a Śaiva hymn-cum-mantra (the Vyomavyāpin mantra) addressed to Śiva as the all-pervading reality, here transmitted with the learned commentary of Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha. It expounds the theology of Śiva's pervasion of the cosmos within the Śaiva Siddhānta framework.
SA_MB_vyomavyaapistavaHK.history: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (II) was a leading 10th-century Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta theologian, author of commentaries such as the Nareśvaraparīkṣāprakāśa and the Kiraṇavṛtti and glosses on Sadyojyotis's works. His commentary on the Vyomavyāpin/Vyomavyāpistava situates the hymn in the dualist Śaiva exegetical tradition.

SA_MB_Pranatosini_5_BhaktiKandaHK.title: Rāmatoṣaṇa (compiler): Prāṇatoṣiṇī, Bhaktikāṇḍa
SA_MB_Pranatosini_5_BhaktiKandaHK.author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṃkāra)
SA_MB_Pranatosini_5_BhaktiKandaHK.summary: The Prāṇatoṣiṇī ('That which satisfies the life-breath / Prāṇa') is a large tantric digest compiling extracts from numerous Tantras on worship, sādhana, and ritual. This portion, the Bhaktikāṇḍa ('section on devotion'), gathers material on devotional worship and the cultivation of bhakti within the tantric system.
SA_MB_Pranatosini_5_BhaktiKandaHK.history: Compiled in 1820 by Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṃkāra) in Bengal, it became one of the most-cited 19th-century Śākta tantric reference works, valued for preserving quotations from many otherwise scattered or lost Tantras.

SA_MB_pranotisini_6_HK.title: Rāmatoṣaṇa (compiler): Prāṇatoṣiṇī, part 6
SA_MB_pranotisini_6_HK.author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṃkāra)
SA_MB_pranotisini_6_HK.summary: Part 6 of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī, the large Bengali Śākta tantric digest, continuing its anthology of citations from many Tantras on ritual worship, mantra, initiation, and sādhana arranged topically. The digest as a whole is organised into sections (kāṇḍas/parts) covering the full range of tantric practice.
SA_MB_pranotisini_6_HK.history: Belongs to the same 1820 compilation by Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya; the work is prized by scholars as a repository of quotations from numerous Tantras, many otherwise rare.

SA_MB_paraatantraHK.title: Paratantra (Parātantra)
SA_MB_paraatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_paraatantraHK.summary: A text transmitted under the title 'Paratantra'. The bare title is ambiguous: it could denote a Tantra so named (a Śaiva/Śākta scripture in the Tantric corpus) or, less likely, a doctrinal treatise. Without a distinguishing author, edition, or incipit the precise identity and contents cannot be confidently established. (Note: 'paratantra' is also a Yogācāra philosophical term for the 'dependent nature', but here it appears as a work title.)
SA_MB_paraatantraHK.history: Could not be reliably identified from available sources. Several distinct works and a philosophical concept share the name, so attribution, date, and transmission remain undetermined.

SA_MB_sakalaagamasaarasangrahaHK.title: Sakalāgamasārasaṅgraha
SA_MB_sakalaagamasaarasangrahaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (compiler unidentified in available sources)
SA_MB_sakalaagamasaarasangrahaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely medieval-to-early modern (2nd millennium CE)
SA_MB_sakalaagamasaarasangrahaHK.summary: A 'Compendium of the Essence of All Āgamas', a digest distilling the substance of the Āgamic (Śaiva/Tantric) scriptures, presenting in summary form the doctrines and ritual prescriptions drawn from the broader Āgama corpus. It belongs to the genre of synthetic handbooks that organise Āgamic teaching for practitioners and students.
SA_MB_sakalaagamasaarasangrahaHK.history: The specific compiler, date, and recension were not confidently identifiable from available sources; the title indicates a South Indian / Tantric Āgamic milieu where such summarising saṅgraha works were produced to make the vast Āgama literature accessible.

SA_MB_yoginiitantramHK.title: Yoginītantra
SA_MB_yoginiitantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_yoginiitantramHK.summary: A Śākta tantric scripture composed in Assam (ancient Kāmarūpa), cast as a dialogue between Śiva and Pārvatī on esoteric ritual and doctrine. It deals extensively with the worship of the goddesses Kālī and Kāmākhyā and is closely associated with the rites of the Kāmākhyā temple, alongside legendary and geographical material on Kāmarūpa.
SA_MB_yoginiitantramHK.history: Its dating to the 16th century rests on internal references to the Koch (Kuvacha) dynasty, said to be born of a Mech woman. It is a major regional Śākta text and an important source for the religious history of medieval Assam and the Kāmākhyā cult.

SA_MB_pratisthalaksanasarasamuccayaHK.title: Pratiṣṭhālakṣaṇasārasamuccaya
SA_MB_pratisthalaksanasarasamuccayaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_pratisthalaksanasarasamuccayaHK.date_estimate: c. early 2nd millennium CE (uncertain); transmitted especially in Nepal
SA_MB_pratisthalaksanasarasamuccayaHK.summary: A 32-chapter compendium ('summary of the essence of the characteristics of installation') on Hindu iconography and the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of images and lingas. It treats the selection of materials, the measurements and forms of deities, the qualifications of officiants, auspicious timings, and the ritual procedures for installing icons.
SA_MB_pratisthalaksanasarasamuccayaHK.history: The text survives notably in Nepalese manuscript traditions and is a significant technical source for image-making and temple installation rites; it has been studied for what it reveals about Hindu iconographic norms. Its precise authorship and date remain uncertain.

SA_MB_AnandatantramHK.title: Ānandatantra
SA_MB_AnandatantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_AnandatantramHK.summary: A large Śākta tantra of about 1913 ślokas in twenty chapters, cast as a dialogue between the Goddess and Kāmeśvara (Śiva). It treats various aspects of Tantric worship, including Śaktipūjā in chapter 15, and devotes its final five chapters to caste, philosophical schools, and Tantric sects.
SA_MB_AnandatantramHK.history: Of South Indian origin, the text claims descent from the 'Bhagamālinīsaṃhitā' said to belong to the vast Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇavatantra corpus. Two commentaries are recorded: one by Narasiṃhācārya and an anonymous one called Pradīpa.

SA_MB_SpKaavrittivivrtiHK.title: Spandakārikā
SA_MB_SpKaavrittivivrtiHK.author: Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (or his teacher Vasugupta)
SA_MB_SpKaavrittivivrtiHK.summary: A foundational text of the Spanda branch of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism, expounding spanda, the subtle creative 'vibration' or pulsation of consciousness through which Śiva manifests the universe. The verses teach how recognizing this dynamic divine pulsation underlies perception, action, and liberation.
SA_MB_SpKaavrittivivrtiHK.history: Authorship is debated: tradition and Kallaṭa's own statements ascribe the verses to Vasugupta (author of the Śivasūtra), with Kallaṭa, his disciple under King Avantivarman (855-883 CE), composing the Spandavṛtti and transmitting them. Major commentaries include Kṣemarāja's Spandanirṇaya and Spandasandoha.

SA_MB_Kulapradiipa-HK.title: Kulapradīpa
SA_MB_Kulapradiipa-HK.author: Śivānandācārya
SA_MB_Kulapradiipa-HK.summary: A Sanskrit Kaula-tantric work ('lamp of the kula') treating the doctrine and ritual of the Kaula tradition of goddess worship. It belongs to the body of Kaula manuals concerned with esoteric Śākta practice.
SA_MB_Kulapradiipa-HK.history: Attributed to Śivānandācārya (also given as Sevānandācārya), said to be the son of the Gauḍa (Bengal) author Jagannivāsa. It is a relatively obscure text known chiefly from manuscript catalogues (e.g. Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum).

SA_MB_JnanarnavaTantraHK.title: Jñānārṇavatantra
SA_MB_JnanarnavaTantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_JnanarnavaTantraHK.summary: A relatively brief and accessible Śrīvidyā tantra ('ocean of knowledge') in 26 paṭalas, cast as a dialogue between Devī and Īśvara that opens with the Goddess asking the true nature of Śrīvidyā. It describes the cakras within the body and the power of mudrās, yantras, and bīja-mantras in worshipping Tripurā/Tripurasundarī and related forms of the supreme Goddess, with notable attention to inner (rather than external) worship.
SA_MB_JnanarnavaTantraHK.history: It is counted among the foremost textual sources for Śrīvidyā practice. No firm date can be assigned to the work.

SA_MB_Pranatosini_1_SargaKandaHK.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Sargakāṇḍa)
SA_MB_Pranatosini_1_SargaKandaHK.author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṅkāra), compiler
SA_MB_Pranatosini_1_SargaKandaHK.summary: The Sargakāṇḍa ('section on creation') is the opening division of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī, a large Bengali Tantric compendium that assembles quotations from numerous tantras, purāṇas, and other authorities. This section gathers material on cosmogony and the emanation/creation of the world within the Tantric framework.
SA_MB_Pranatosini_1_SargaKandaHK.history: The Prāṇatoṣiṇī was compiled in Bengal by Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya in 1821. As a digest it preserves passages from many tantras that are otherwise rare or lost, making it an important source for the textual history of Tantra.

SA_MB_Pranatosini_2_DharmaKandaHK.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Dharmakāṇḍa)
SA_MB_Pranatosini_2_DharmaKandaHK.author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṅkāra), compiler
SA_MB_Pranatosini_2_DharmaKandaHK.summary: The Dharmakāṇḍa ('section on dharma') is a division of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī, the early-19th-century Bengali Tantric compendium of Rāmatoṣaṇa. This part compiles authoritative passages on religious duty, conduct, and the regulative aspects of Tantric and Śākta practice.
SA_MB_Pranatosini_2_DharmaKandaHK.history: Part of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī compiled in Bengal in 1821; the work as a whole is valued for preserving citations from a wide range of tantras and purāṇas.

SA_MB_Pranatosini_3_ArthaKandaHK.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Arthakāṇḍa)
SA_MB_Pranatosini_3_ArthaKandaHK.author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṅkāra), compiler
SA_MB_Pranatosini_3_ArthaKandaHK.summary: The Arthakāṇḍa ('section on artha/meaning or purpose') is a division of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī, the Bengali Tantric compendium of Rāmatoṣaṇa. It collects Tantric and Śākta source passages organized around this thematic heading within the digest.
SA_MB_Pranatosini_3_ArthaKandaHK.history: Part of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī compiled in Bengal in 1821; the compendium preserves quotations from many tantras and purāṇas important for the study of Tantra.

SA_MB_Pranatosini_4_KamyaKandaHK.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Kāmyakāṇḍa)
SA_MB_Pranatosini_4_KamyaKandaHK.author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṅkāra), compiler
SA_MB_Pranatosini_4_KamyaKandaHK.summary: The Kāmyakāṇḍa ('section on optional/desire-prompted rites') is a division of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī, the Bengali Tantric compendium of Rāmatoṣaṇa. It assembles source passages on kāmya rituals — those performed to obtain specific desired ends — within the larger digest.
SA_MB_Pranatosini_4_KamyaKandaHK.history: Part of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī compiled in Bengal in 1821; valued for preserving Tantric and Purāṇic citations otherwise scattered or rare.

SA_MB_RudraYamalaHK.title: Rudrayāmalatantra (Uttarakāṇḍa)
SA_MB_RudraYamalaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_RudraYamalaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; the surviving Uttarakhaṇḍa is comparatively late
SA_MB_RudraYamalaHK.summary: The Uttarakhaṇḍa ('latter section') circulates as a tantric text in some 17 (or 66, by another reckoning) paṭalas, written in a relatively plain style. It centers on the goddess identified with Kuṇḍalinī and covers initiation, the cakras, the types of practitioners, and the vital breaths within the body.
SA_MB_RudraYamalaHK.history: The Rudrayāmala is one of the famed Yāmala tantras, cited by many later āgamas and tantric works, but the original is effectively lost and survives mainly as quotations; the extant Uttarakhaṇḍa is widely regarded as a later compilation under that prestigious title. Its provenance and date remain unknown.

SA_MB_saivaparibhasaHK.title: Śaivasiddhāntaparibhāṣā
SA_MB_saivaparibhasaHK.author: Śivāgrayogin (Śivāgra Yogin)
SA_MB_saivaparibhasaHK.summary: A Sanskrit doctrinal manual systematizing the philosophy of Śaiva Siddhānta — its account of the three eternal realities pati (Lord), paśu (bound soul), and pāśa (bondage), and the path to liberation through Śiva's grace. It serves as a 'definitional' summary (paribhāṣā) of Siddhānta tenets.
SA_MB_saivaparibhasaHK.history: Attributed to Śivāgrayogin, a major South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta theologian of roughly the 16th century who also wrote commentaries within the Sanskrit Saiddhāntika tradition. The work is part of the late Sanskrit systematization of the school in the Tamil country.

SA_MB_SAAMBAPA_NCAASIKAAhk.title: Sāmbapañcāśikā
SA_MB_SAAMBAPA_NCAASIKAAhk.author: attributed to Sāmba (son of Kṛṣṇa)
SA_MB_SAAMBAPA_NCAASIKAAhk.summary: A devotional hymn of fifty verses (pañcāśikā) in praise of Sūrya, the Sun God, traditionally ascribed to Sāmba, the son of Kṛṣṇa. It is associated with the legend in which Sāmba, cursed with leprosy, worshipped Sūrya and was cured.
SA_MB_SAAMBAPA_NCAASIKAAhk.history: The hymn is mentioned already in the Varāhapurāṇa. Its Kāvyamālā edition carries a commentary by the Kashmiri Śaiva writer Kṣemarāja, and the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition another anonymous commentary; this Śaiva interest in a Saura hymn reflects the syncretism of medieval sun worship.

SA_MB_saradatilakaHK.title: Śāradātilaka
SA_MB_saradatilakaHK.author: Lakṣmaṇadeśika(ndra)
SA_MB_saradatilakaHK.date_estimate: disputed: c. 800 CE to 11th c. CE
SA_MB_saradatilakaHK.summary: A voluminous and authoritative compendium of mantraśāstra (some 3,500 stanzas) covering cosmogony, the deities and their mantras, ritual worship, dīkṣā, mudrās, yantras, and the practical technology of Tantric mantra practice. It became one of the most widely used handbooks of Tantric ritual.
SA_MB_saradatilakaHK.history: Composed by Lakṣmaṇadeśika(ndra); the dating ranges in scholarship from around 800 CE to the 11th century. It was hugely influential and drew many commentaries, most famously Rāghavabhaṭṭa's Padārthādarśa, and was edited by John Woodroffe (Arthur Avalon).

SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAMhk.title: Śataratnasaṃgraha
SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAMhk.author: Umāpati Śivācārya
SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAMhk.summary: A Sanskrit Śaiva Siddhānta anthology ('collection of a hundred jewels') gathering and arranging one hundred doctrinal aphorisms/verses (ratnas), drawn largely from the Śaiva Āgamas, that set out the essentials of Siddhānta theology — the soul, bondage, Śiva, and liberation. It functions as a compact authoritative digest of the school's tenets.
SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAMhk.history: Attributed to Umāpati Śivācārya (Umāpati Śivam), one of the four chief Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta teachers (santāna-ācāryas), active c. 1290-1340 CE (his Saṅkalpanirākaraṇa is dated 1313 CE). He was a Dīkṣitar of Chidambaram who held Śaiva Siddhānta to be the essence of the Vedas and Āgamas.

SA_MB_sivaarcanacandrikaaHK.title: Śivārcanacandrikā
SA_MB_sivaarcanacandrikaaHK.author: Appayya Dīkṣita
SA_MB_sivaarcanacandrikaaHK.summary: A manual ('moonlight on the worship of Śiva') setting out the procedure for the ritual worship (arcanā/pūjā) of Śiva. It belongs to Appayya Dīkṣita's group of practical works guiding the performance of Śiva pūjā, and carries an attached commentary.
SA_MB_sivaarcanacandrikaaHK.history: Composed by the great South Indian polymath Appayya Dīkṣita (1520-1593), a prolific Advaitin and devotee of Śiva who wrote across Vedānta, poetics, and Śaiva theology. The work sits among his several writings on Śiva worship such as the Śivadhyānapaddhati and Śivapūjāvidhi.

SA_MB_SAMBANDHASIDDHIHhk.title: Sambandhasiddhi
SA_MB_SAMBANDHASIDDHIHhk.author: Utpaladeva
SA_MB_SAMBANDHASIDDHIHhk.date_estimate: c. first half of 10th c. CE (c. 900-950)
SA_MB_SAMBANDHASIDDHIHhk.summary: One of the three short proof-treatises constituting Utpaladeva's Siddhitrayī, the Sambandhasiddhi ('establishment of relation') argues, against rival schools, for the reality and nature of relation (sambandha) within the Pratyabhijñā (Recognition) philosophy. It defends how distinct entities can be genuinely related given the non-dual consciousness of Śiva.
SA_MB_SAMBANDHASIDDHIHhk.history: Utpaladeva, disciple of Somānanda and teacher in the lineage culminating in Abhinavagupta, was the great systematizer of Pratyabhijñā; the Siddhitrayī (Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi, Sambandhasiddhi) supplements his main work, the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā.

SA_MB_sivadrstiHK.title: Śivadṛṣṭi
SA_MB_sivadrstiHK.author: Somānanda
SA_MB_sivadrstiHK.summary: The foundational text of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism, the Śivadṛṣṭi ('vision of Śiva') posits Śiva as the sole ultimate reality, encompassing consciousness and its dynamic power (śakti). Engaging Advaita Vedānta, Sāṃkhya realism, and Buddhist positions, it establishes the doctrine that all reality is the self-recognition of Śiva.
SA_MB_sivadrstiHK.history: Somānanda is regarded as the founder of the Pratyabhijñā school; his pupil Utpaladeva systematized and expanded the system, and it was later brought to its peak by Abhinavagupta. Utpaladeva wrote the (partly lost) Śivadṛṣṭivṛtti commentary.

SA_MB_sivasamhita-HK.title: Śivasaṃhitā
SA_MB_sivasamhita-HK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_sivasamhita-HK.summary: One of the three classical treatises on Haṭha Yoga (with the Haṭhayogapradīpikā and Gheraṇḍasaṃhitā), framed as Śiva's teaching to Pārvatī. It expounds yogic physiology, nāḍīs and cakras, prāṇāyāma, mudrās, the awakening of kuṇḍalinī, and the siddhis and liberation attainable through practice, and notably commends yoga even for householders.
SA_MB_sivasamhita-HK.history: The author is unknown; James Mallinson argues for a date of roughly 1300-1500 CE, probably composed in or near Varanasi, while some earlier scholars placed it in the 17th century. It absorbs tantric and Śrīvidyā elements into its yogic framework.

SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKAhk.title: Śivasūtravārttika
SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKAhk.author: Bhāskara (alternatively Varadarāja)
SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKAhk.date_estimate: c. 11th c. CE (Bhāskara); Varadarāja's somewhat later
SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKAhk.summary: A verse commentary (vārttika) on the Śivasūtra, the root aphoristic scripture of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism revealed to Vasugupta. It expounds the Śivasūtra's teaching on the three 'means' (upāyas) to recognizing one's identity with Śiva. Two such vārttikas exist: Bhāskara's, an original verse exposition, and Varadarāja's, which largely versifies Kṣemarāja's Vimarśinī.
SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKAhk.history: The Śivasūtra is attributed to Vasugupta (9th c. CE). Bhāskara, in Vasugupta's lineage, wrote his Vārttika c. 11th century (translated by Mark Dyczkowski); Varadarāja's later Vārttika depends on Kṣemarāja's prose Śivasūtravimarśinī.

SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHAhk.title: Spandasandoha (commentary on the Spandakārikā)
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHAhk.author: Kṣemarāja (title misattributes to Kallaṭa; the Spandakārikā mūla is by Vasugupta/Kallaṭa)
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHAhk.date_estimate: c. early 11th c. CE (Kṣemarāja fl. c. 1000-1050); the Spandakārikā it expounds is 9th c. CE
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHAhk.summary: The Spandasandoha is an extended exposition (sandoha) by Kṣemarāja concentrating on the opening verse(s) of the Spandakārikā. It elaborates the doctrine of spanda, the subtle creative 'vibration' or pulsation of divine consciousness (Śiva) that underlies all manifestation and cognition, and shows how recognition of this dynamic awareness yields liberation. It functions as a doctrinal and devotional gateway to the full Spanda system.
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHAhk.history: The mūla Spandakārikā is ascribed either to Vasugupta or to his pupil Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (9th c.); the record's attribution of the Spandasandoha to Kallaṭa is an error, as the Spandasandoha is the well-attested work of Kṣemarāja, disciple of Abhinavagupta. Kṣemarāja also wrote the fuller Spandanirṇaya covering the whole text; Kallaṭa's own contribution is the early Spandavṛtti.

SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_1-5_HK.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 1-5
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_1-5_HK.author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_1-5_HK.date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_1-5_HK.summary: The Śrītattvacintāmaṇi is a voluminous Śākta-tantric compendium in Sanskrit verse, organized into chapters (prakāśas), covering nearly the whole field of Tantra: initiation (dīkṣā), the subtle body and its cakras, kinds of mantra, tantric goddesses, and the worship of Tripurasundarī/Ṣoḍaśī and the Śrīcakra. Chapters 1-5 cover the opening matter including initiation, mantra-śāstra, and foundational ritual. The famous Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa is its sixth chapter.
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_1-5_HK.history: Composed by Pūrṇānanda, a Bengali sādhaka, in 1577. The work runs to roughly twenty-six prakāśas; its sixth chapter (Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa) circulated independently and became internationally known through Arthur Avalon's translation 'The Serpent Power.' It is a major source for the systematized cakra-yoga of later Tantra.

SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_6-10_HK.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 6-10
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_6-10_HK.author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_6-10_HK.date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_6-10_HK.summary: This portion of Pūrṇānanda's Śākta-tantric compendium opens (chapter 6) with the celebrated Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa, the description of the six cakras of the subtle body, the kuṇḍalinī, and the ascent of Śakti to Śiva at the sahasrāra. The following chapters continue with the description of mantras, tantric female deities, and the worship of the supreme goddess Tripurasundarī. It is the most influential block of the whole work.
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_6-10_HK.history: The sixth chapter (Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa) became a standard reference text for cakra-yoga and was translated by Arthur Avalon (John Woodroffe) as 'The Serpent Power' (1919), shaping modern Western understandings of the subtle body. The parent work was composed in 1577.

SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_11-15_HK.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 11-15
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_11-15_HK.author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_11-15_HK.date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_11-15_HK.summary: These middle chapters of Pūrṇānanda's Śākta compendium continue the systematic treatment of tantric worship: the various tantric goddesses, the deity Ṣoḍaśī, the nature and worship of the Śrīcakra (the yantra after which the work is named), and hymns (stotras) to Tripurasundarī. The material treats both the metaphysics of the goddess and detailed ritual procedure (pūjā, sādhanā).
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamani_11-15_HK.history: Part of the c. 1577 Śrītattvacintāmaṇi of the Bengali sādhaka Pūrṇānanda, a comprehensive Sanskrit verse digest drawing together Śākta-tantric doctrine and practice; widely copied in eastern India and an important witness to Śrīvidyā in Bengal.

SA_MB_Sritattvacintamanii_16-End_HK.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 16 to end
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamanii_16-End_HK.author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamanii_16-End_HK.date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamanii_16-End_HK.summary: The closing chapters (16 through the final, roughly the 26th prakāśa) of Pūrṇānanda's Śākta-tantric compendium. They include the sacrifice called Jñānaḍūtī, the hymn of the thousand names (sahasranāma) of the great Tripurasundarī, and the consecration of mystical diagrams (yantra) used as protective amulets, completing the treatment of advanced Śrīvidyā ritual and theology.
SA_MB_Sritattvacintamanii_16-End_HK.history: Concludes the c. 1577 Śrītattvacintāmaṇi of Pūrṇānanda; the whole work comprises about twenty-six prakāśas and stands as one of the most comprehensive late-medieval Bengali Tantric manuals.

SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANIhk.title: Stavacintāmaṇi
SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANIhk.author: Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa
SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANIhk.summary: The Stavacintāmaṇi ('Wish-fulfilling Jewel of Praise') is a hymn of about 120 invocatory verses addressed to Śiva and his Śakti as the source of creation, maintenance, and dissolution. It uniquely fuses devotional (bhakti) feeling with the metaphysics of monistic Kashmir Śaivism, expressing the lived realization of nondual Śaiva yoga through praise.
SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANIhk.history: Composed by Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa (not to be confused with the dramatist of the Veṇīsaṃhāra). The hymn was canonized within nondual Śaivism by Kṣemarāja's learned commentary, which reads its devotional verses through the technical vocabulary of Pratyabhijñā yogic experience, integrating it into the Trika canon.
related_works: commentary of Kṣemarāja on the Stavacintāmaṇi

SA_MB_suprabhedagamaHK.title: Suprabhedāgama
SA_MB_suprabhedagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revelation ascribed to Śiva)
SA_MB_suprabhedagamaHK.date_estimate: c. 7th-11th c. CE (disputed; one of the late Siddhāntāgamas)
SA_MB_suprabhedagamaHK.summary: The Suprabhedāgama is one of the twenty-eight canonical Siddhāntāgamas of dualist Śaiva Siddhānta. Like other āgamas it treats the four traditional 'feet' (pādas)—doctrine (jñāna/vidyā), yoga, ritual (kriyā), and conduct (caryā)—prescribing temple construction, image installation, initiation, daily and occasional worship, and the path to liberation through Śiva's grace.
SA_MB_suprabhedagamaHK.history: Traditionally counted among the five āgamas proclaimed by the Vāmadeva face of Śiva. Its dating is uncertain; like most Siddhāntāgamas its received text grew over centuries and survives mainly in South Indian (especially Tamil-region) manuscript traditions where it remains authoritative for temple ritual.
related_works: the 28 Śaiva Siddhānta Āgamas (e.g. Kāmika, Kāraṇa, Ajita, Raurava)

SA_MB_SvacchandatantraHK.title: Svacchandatantra
SA_MB_SvacchandatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revelation; dialogue of Bhairava and Devī)
SA_MB_SvacchandatantraHK.tradition: Hindu, Śaiva (Mantramārga Bhairava tantra; read nondualistically in Kashmir)
SA_MB_SvacchandatantraHK.date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (text); influential nondual reading 10th-11th c.
SA_MB_SvacchandatantraHK.summary: The Svacchandatantra is a large Bhairava-tantra cast as a dialogue between the goddess Devī and Bhairava, centered on the worship of Svacchanda-bhairava ('Autonomous Bhairava') and his consort Aghoreśvarī. It expounds initiation, mantra, maṇḍala, yoga, cosmology, and ritual—including liberation- and power-oriented practices—and was a major scripture of the Mantramārga.
SA_MB_SvacchandatantraHK.history: Dated to around the seventh century, it was originally read in a more dualist (Siddhānta-leaning) key. The Kashmiri exegete Kṣemarāja (10th-11th c.), disciple of Abhinavagupta, wrote the only extant commentary, the Uddyota, deliberately reinterpreting the tantra along monistic Trika lines and refuting earlier dualist commentators.

SA_MB_Tantraloka-1-14-HK.title: Tantrāloka, chapters 1-14 (āhnikas)
SA_MB_Tantraloka-1-14-HK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_Tantraloka-1-14-HK.summary: The Tantrāloka ('Light on the Tantras') is Abhinavagupta's monumental synthesis of Trika Śaivism, presented as an exposition of the Mālinīvijayottaratantra. Its first fourteen āhnikas lay the philosophical and soteriological foundations: the nature of Śiva-consciousness and its self-manifestation, the four 'means' (upāyas) to liberation (āṇava, śākta, śāmbhava, and the no-means anupāya), the categories of bondage and grace (śaktipāta), and the metaphysics of the universe as the expansion of consciousness.
SA_MB_Tantraloka-1-14-HK.history: Composed in early-11th-century Kashmir, the Tantrāloka comprises around 5,800 verses in 37 āhnikas and is the most authoritative systematic work of nondual Kashmiri Śaivism. It was illuminated by the indispensable Viveka commentary of Abhinavagupta's pupil Jayaratha (12th-13th c.).

SA_MB_Tantraloka-15-37-HK.title: Tantrāloka, chapters 15-38 (āhnikas)
SA_MB_Tantraloka-15-37-HK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_Tantraloka-15-37-HK.summary: These later āhnikas of the Tantrāloka turn from foundational metaphysics to the detailed practice of Trika ritual: regular and occasional worship, initiation (dīkṣā) in its many forms, the construction and use of maṇḍalas, mantra and the phonematic emanation of reality, consecration, funerary and expiatory rites, sacred geography and time, and Kaula esoteric practice. The final āhnika treats the transmission of the tradition and the structure of the work itself.
SA_MB_Tantraloka-15-37-HK.history: The concluding portion of Abhinavagupta's c. 37-chapter, ~5,800-verse magnum opus, completing the integration of scripture, ritual, and nondual philosophy. Jayaratha's Viveka commentary remains essential for understanding its dense technical material.

SA_MB_taaraatantramHK.title: Tāratantra
SA_MB_taaraatantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revelation; Śiva-Devī dialogue)
SA_MB_taaraatantramHK.tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (Daśamahāvidyā; cult of Tārā)
SA_MB_taaraatantramHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely late medieval (c. 11th-16th c. CE)
SA_MB_taaraatantramHK.summary: The Tāratantra is a Śākta scripture devoted to the worship of the goddess Tārā, the second of the ten Mahāvidyās, in her forms such as Ugratārā, Ekajaṭā, and Nīlasarasvatī. It sets out her mantras, meditative visualizations (dhyāna), ritual procedure, and the distinctive Mahācīnakrama ('Tibetan/Chinese rite'), reflecting the close interface between Hindu and Buddhist Tārā cults.
SA_MB_taaraatantramHK.history: One of several tantras (alongside Nīlatantra, Tārākalpa, Rudrayāmala material, etc.) underlying the Hindu Tārā cult that flourished especially in Bengal and Assam (Kāmākhyā). The text quotes from and is bound up with the Tārārahasya tradition; precise authorship and date are not securely established, and several differing works circulate under similar titles.

SA_MB_tararahasyaHK.title: Tārārahasya
SA_MB_tararahasyaHK.author: Brahmānanda (Brahmānandagiri)
SA_MB_tararahasyaHK.tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (cult of Tārā / Mahāvidyā)
SA_MB_tararahasyaHK.date_estimate: c. 16th c. CE (disputed; Brahmānandagiri of Bengal/Kāmākhyā)
SA_MB_tararahasyaHK.summary: The Tārārahasya ('Secret of Tārā') is a Śākta compendium guiding the pūjā and sādhanā of the goddess Tārā and her aspects (Nīlasarasvatī, Ugrā, Ekajaṭā, and related Śaktis). Across its chapters it treats daily worship, initiation, mantras, dhyāna, and the Mahācīnakrama, the special code of ritual conduct peculiar to Tārā worship. It is one of the principal practical handbooks of the Tārā cult.
SA_MB_tararahasyaHK.history: Compiled by Brahmānandagiri, an ascetic associated with Kāmākhyā who drew on numerous earlier sources (Tārāsāra, Nīlatantra, Mahācīnatantra, Rudrayāmala, etc.). Estimates put the text at roughly 2,500 ślokas. Its popularity was later rivaled by the Tārārahasyavṛttikā of Śaṅkara Āgamācārya of Bengal.

SA_MB_taaraarahasyavrttikaaHK.title: Tārārahasyavṛttikā
SA_MB_taaraarahasyavrttikaaHK.author: Śaṅkara Āgamācārya (of Bengal)
SA_MB_taaraarahasyavrttikaaHK.tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (cult of Tārā / Mahāvidyā)
SA_MB_taaraarahasyavrttikaaHK.date_estimate: late medieval (after the Tārārahasya; c. 16th-17th c. CE)
SA_MB_taaraarahasyavrttikaaHK.summary: The Tārārahasyavṛttikā is a commentary/gloss expanding on Brahmānandagiri's Tārārahasya. Across its chapters it elaborates the daily worship and initiation of Tārā, the mantras and meditative descriptions of the goddess, and the Mahācīnakrama 'Tibetan method' of ritual conduct, providing detailed prose explanation (including the iconographic description of Tārā's image quoted by other tantras).
SA_MB_taaraarahasyavrttikaaHK.history: Written by Śaṅkara Āgamācārya of Bengal, this commentary came to overshadow the base text in popularity and is cited as an authority by later Tārā literature (e.g. the Tāratantra draws its prose description of Tārā's image from it). It is a key witness to the systematization of Tārā worship in eastern India.

SA_MB_VamakesvarimataHK.title: Vāmakeśvarīmata (Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava)
SA_MB_VamakesvarimataHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revelation)
SA_MB_VamakesvarimataHK.tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (Kaula Śrīvidyā; cult of Tripurasundarī)
SA_MB_VamakesvarimataHK.date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (disputed; some place it before the 9th c.)
SA_MB_VamakesvarimataHK.summary: The Vāmakeśvarīmata, better known as the Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava, is the root tantra of the Kaula Śrīvidyā tradition centered on the goddess Tripurasundarī. It expounds the Śrīcakra and its goddesses (the Nityā deities), the fifteen-syllable mantra (pañcadaśākṣarī), and the ritual and yogic worship of the supreme Śakti, drawing on Trika and Kubjikā doctrine.
SA_MB_VamakesvarimataHK.history: Counted as the earliest principal Tantra of Kaula Śrīvidyā and foundational for the entire later tradition. It received important commentaries, notably the Ṛjuvimarśinī of Śivānanda and Jayaratha's Vivaraṇa. Its companion text within the lineage is the Yoginīhṛdaya.

SA_MB_varivasyaarahasyaHK.title: Varivasyārahasya
SA_MB_varivasyaarahasyaHK.author: Bhāskararāya Makhin
SA_MB_varivasyaarahasyaHK.summary: The Varivasyārahasya ('Secret of Worship') is Bhāskararāya's treatise expounding the manifold esoteric meanings of the Śrīvidyā fifteen-syllable mantra (pañcadaśākṣarī) and its worship. Through detailed analysis of the mantra's letters, deities, and contemplative correspondences it concludes by demonstrating that Śrīvidyā ultimately teaches the Upaniṣadic identity of the individual self with the supreme Self. The author supplied his own commentary, the Prakāśa.
SA_MB_varivasyaarahasyaHK.history: Bhāskararāya Makhin was an 18th-century polymath and Śrīvidyā authority who served as a court paṇḍita at Thanjavur and authored some forty works. He bridged Vedic orthodoxy and Tantric esotericism and advocated the right-hand (dakṣiṇa) path of Śrīvidyā. He also commented on the Lalitāsahasranāma, Saundaryalaharī, and Bhāvanopaniṣad.

SA_MB_vatulanathasutraHK.title: Vātūlanāthasūtra
SA_MB_vatulanathasutraHK.author: attributed to Vātūlanātha (Siddha)
SA_MB_vatulanathasutraHK.date_estimate: uncertain; probably after the 12th c. CE
SA_MB_vatulanathasutraHK.summary: The Vātūlanāthasūtra is a short set of about thirteen terse aphorisms belonging to the Kashmiri Krama tradition. They expound the highest nondual realization—the 'great spontaneous leap' (mahāsāhasa-vṛtti) into Śiva-consciousness—and the supreme state of unmediated identity with the absolute, framed as revelation received from the Yoginīs.
SA_MB_vatulanathasutraHK.history: Authorship is ascribed to the Kaula Siddha Vātūlanātha, said to have lived after the 12th century. The sūtras survive with the Sanskrit commentary (vṛtti) of Anantaśaktipāda, who frames them as a direct revelation from enlightened Yoginīs; neither sūtras nor commentary can be securely dated. They preserve rare documentary evidence of the orally transmitted Krama teachings.
related_works: Vṛtti of Anantaśaktipāda (commentary on the Vātūlanāthasūtra)

SA_MB_visvasaaratantramHK.title: Viśvasāratantra
SA_MB_visvasaaratantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revelation)
SA_MB_visvasaaratantramHK.summary: The Viśvasāratantra ('Tantra of the Essence of the Universe') is a tantric scripture treating cosmogony, the ordering of the cosmic regions, salvation, and the stages of spiritual practice. It is noted for the macrocosm-microcosm maxim 'yad ihāsti tad anyatra, yan nehāsti na tat kvacit' ('what is here is elsewhere; what is not here is nowhere'), and it gives a fuller account of the stages of sādhanā than some related tantras such as the Kulārṇava.
SA_MB_visvasaaratantramHK.history: Listed among the recognized tantras (e.g. in the Mahāmokṣatantra's enumeration) and cited in Tantric bibliographies; it is also referenced in connection with Ayurvedic/medical material. Its authorship, date, and full text are not securely established and it survives mainly through manuscript references and quotations.

SA_MB_advayataarakopanisatHK.title: Advayatārakopaniṣad
SA_MB_advayataarakopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_advayataarakopanisatHK.date_estimate: disputed; broadly 1st millennium CE (Flood: c. 100 BCE-300 CE for Yoga Upaniṣads; possibly later, up to ~14th c.)
SA_MB_advayataarakopanisatHK.summary: The Advayatārakopaniṣad ('Upaniṣad on the Nondual Deliverer/Star') is a short Yoga Upaniṣad teaching tāraka-yoga, a meditative practice of inner light and the 'crossing-over' to the nondual Brahman. It describes the yogin's perception of inner luminous signs, the śāmbhavī mudrā (gaze), and culminates in the realization of the qualified and unqualified Brahman and the role of the guru.
SA_MB_advayataarakopanisatHK.history: One of the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads, transmitted within the Śukla-Yajurveda corpus and appearing in the Muktikā canon of 108 Upaniṣads. Like other minor Upaniṣads it is anonymous and of uncertain date; it integrates Vedāntic nondualism with Haṭha/Laya yoga practice.

SA_MB_amrtabinduupanisatHK.title: Amṛtabindūpaniṣad
SA_MB_amrtabinduupanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_amrtabinduupanisatHK.tradition: Hindu, Vedānta / Yoga (Yoga Upaniṣad; Bindu group)
SA_MB_amrtabinduupanisatHK.date_estimate: disputed; broadly 1st millennium CE (Flood: c. 100 BCE-300 CE)
SA_MB_amrtabinduupanisatHK.summary: The Amṛtabindūpaniṣad ('Upaniṣad of the Drop of Immortality'), also called Brahmabindu Upaniṣad, is a very short Yoga Upaniṣad of about twenty-two verses on the control of the mind as the means to liberation. It teaches that the mind alone causes bondage and freedom, distinguishes the pure (soundless) from the impure mind, and expounds the practice of dissolving thought into the silent (aśabda) Brahman through OM.
SA_MB_amrtabinduupanisatHK.history: One of the most widely transmitted minor Upaniṣads, attached to the Atharvaveda (or Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda in some lists) and included in the Muktikā canon. It shares a number of verses with the Amṛtanāda and other Bindu/Yoga Upaniṣads, and is anonymous and of uncertain date.

SA_MB_amrtanaadopanisatHK.title: Amṛtanādopaniṣad
SA_MB_amrtanaadopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_amrtanaadopanisatHK.date_estimate: disputed; broadly 1st millennium CE (Flood: c. 100 BCE-300 CE for Yoga Upaniṣads)
SA_MB_amrtanaadopanisatHK.summary: The Amṛtanādopaniṣad ('Upaniṣad of the Sound/Bliss of Immortality') is a short Yoga Upaniṣad describing a graded yogic path to liberation. It sets out a sixfold yoga (pratyāhāra, dhyāna, prāṇāyāma, dhāraṇā, tarka, samādhi), meditation on OM as the means of crossing beyond the perishable, and contemplation culminating in the realization of the immortal Self.
SA_MB_amrtanaadopanisatHK.history: Attached to the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda and counted among the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads in the Muktikā canon of 108 Upaniṣads. Anonymous and of uncertain date, it shares material with the Amṛtabindu and other Yoga Upaniṣads and reflects the fusion of Vedāntic and yogic teaching characteristic of this corpus.

SA_MB_brahmavidyaopanisatHK.title: Brahmavidyā Upaniṣad
SA_MB_brahmavidyaopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_brahmavidyaopanisatHK.date_estimate: c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (core; per Gavin Flood), with later layers; disputed
SA_MB_brahmavidyaopanisatHK.summary: A short minor Upaniṣad belonging to the Yoga Upaniṣad group, traditionally attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda (some recensions to the Atharvaveda). It expounds the science of Brahman (brahmavidyā) through meditation on the syllable Om (praṇava), analyzing its constituent mātrās, the haṃsa, and the subtle phonic and physiological dimensions of the sacred sound as a vehicle to liberation.
SA_MB_brahmavidyaopanisatHK.history: One of the 108 Upaniṣads of the Muktikā canon. Like the other Yoga Upaniṣads it is anonymous and regarded by tradition as śruti; modern scholars treat it as a relatively late composite text expanded over time. Gavin Flood places the Yoga Upaniṣads broadly in the 100 BCE-300 CE range.

SA_MB_dhyaanabinduupanisatHK.title: Dhyānabindu Upaniṣad
SA_MB_dhyaanabinduupanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_dhyaanabinduupanisatHK.date_estimate: c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (core); longer South-Indian recension expanded through the 2nd millennium CE
SA_MB_dhyaanabinduupanisatHK.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda concerned with meditation (dhyāna) on the 'point/seed' (bindu). It teaches contemplation of Om, the haṃsa breath, the subtle body with its nāḍīs and cakras, prāṇāyāma and the inner phonic vibration as the means to dissolve karma and attain union with Brahman, anticipating later Haṭha/Tantric yoga physiology.
SA_MB_dhyaanabinduupanisatHK.history: Counted among the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads and the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. A shorter and a longer (South Indian) recension exist; the longer one shows expansion with later yogic material. Anonymous and traditionally regarded as śruti.

SA_MB_hamsopanisadHK.title: Haṃsa Upaniṣad
SA_MB_hamsopanisadHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_hamsopanisadHK.date_estimate: Probably 2nd millennium CE but before the early 17th c.; disputed
SA_MB_hamsopanisadHK.summary: A short minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Śukla Yajurveda, framed as a discourse between the sage Gautama and Sanatkumāra on Haṃsa-vidyā as a prelude to Brahmavidyā. It describes the ajapā 'haṃsa/so'ham' mantra, the relationship of Om to the haṃsa, the nāḍīs and cakras, and meditation on inner sounds (nāda) leading toward realization of the Paramahaṃsa.
SA_MB_hamsopanisadHK.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads and the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. Anonymous; its medley-like structure and Haṭha-yoga content suggest a comparatively late date, after the main classical Upaniṣads but before its citation in the early 17th century.

SA_MB_ksurikopanisatHK.title: Kṣurikā Upaniṣad
SA_MB_ksurikopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_ksurikopanisatHK.date_estimate: c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (per Gavin Flood); disputed
SA_MB_ksurikopanisatHK.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda whose name means 'the razor/knife.' It presents yoga as a sharp instrument to cut through the bonds of the body and mind, describing postures (āsana), breath control (prāṇāyāma) and withdrawal of the senses (pratyāhāra), and the meditative 'severing' of the nāḍīs to free the soul from rebirth.
SA_MB_ksurikopanisatHK.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads and the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. Anonymous and traditionally śruti; modern dating follows Flood's general placement of the Yoga Upaniṣads.

SA_MB_mahaavaakyopanisatCSX.title: Mahāvākya Upaniṣad
SA_MB_mahaavaakyopanisatCSX.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mahaavaakyopanisatCSX.date_estimate: c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (per Gavin Flood); disputed, likely later
SA_MB_mahaavaakyopanisatCSX.summary: A short minor Upaniṣad, traditionally attached to the Atharvaveda, that discusses the nature of Ātman and Brahman, their identity, and the distinction between knowledge (vidyā) and ignorance (avidyā). It is named for the Vedic 'great sayings' (mahāvākyas) such as 'aham brahmāsmi,' which express the non-dual oneness of the self with the absolute.
SA_MB_mahaavaakyopanisatCSX.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads, often grouped with the Yoga/Sāmānya Upaniṣads. Anonymous; treated by tradition as śruti and used in Advaita contexts.

SA_MB_mandalabraahmanopanisaHK.title: Maṇḍalabrāhmaṇa Upaniṣad
SA_MB_mandalabraahmanopanisaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mandalabraahmanopanisaHK.date_estimate: Disputed: early 1st millennium CE (Flood) vs. post-10th c. (Raman); likely late
SA_MB_mandalabraahmanopanisaHK.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Śukla Yajurveda, presented as a teaching given by the sun-god/Nārāyaṇa to the sage Yājñavalkya. It expounds an eight-limbed (aṣṭāṅga) yoga along with taraka-yoga and amanaska-yoga, dwelling on inner light, the perception of the formless and 'spaces' (ākāśas), and the dissolution of the mind as the path to liberation.
SA_MB_mandalabraahmanopanisaHK.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads and the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. Anonymous; its developed Haṭha/laya-yoga content leads several scholars (e.g., Raman) to regard it as a late text composed after the 10th century, though Flood places the Yoga Upaniṣads earlier.

SA_MB_naadabinduupanisatHK.title: Nādabindu Upaniṣad
SA_MB_naadabinduupanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_naadabinduupanisatHK.tradition: Hindu (Yoga Upaniṣad; yoga of sacred sound)
SA_MB_naadabinduupanisatHK.date_estimate: Disputed: pre-Christian/early 1st millennium CE (Beck, Feuerstein) to 100 BCE-300 CE (Flood)
SA_MB_naadabinduupanisatHK.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Ṛgveda (and Atharvaveda in some lists) centered on nāda (inner sound) and bindu (the seed-point). It analyzes the syllable Om as the 'haṃsa-vidyā,' describes meditation on the unstruck inner sounds heard in the body, and teaches laya-yoga whereby the mind dissolves into the subtle nāda leading to liberation.
SA_MB_naadabinduupanisatHK.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads and twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. Often called the earliest systematic text on the yoga of sacred sound (Guy Beck). Anonymous and regarded as śruti.

SA_MB_paasupatabrahmopanisatHK.title: Pāśupatabrahma Upaniṣad
SA_MB_paasupatabrahmopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_paasupatabrahmopanisatHK.summary: A minor Upaniṣad attached to the Atharvaveda, opening with questions put to the creator Brahmā by his son Vaiśravaṇa. In two chapters it treats the soul as Haṃsa, the syllable Om and the ajapā mantra, yoga and meditation, the para- and apara-Brahman, while criticizing reliance on external ritual in favor of inner contemplation, blending Vedāntic non-dualism with Śaiva (Pāśupata-flavored) imagery.
SA_MB_paasupatabrahmopanisatHK.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads, usually grouped among the Yoga/Sāmānya Upaniṣads. Anonymous; its Pāśupata/Śaiva associations and developed yoga content suggest a comparatively late date within the 1st millennium CE.

SA_MB_saandilyopanisatHK.title: Śāṇḍilya Upaniṣad
SA_MB_saandilyopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_saandilyopanisatHK.date_estimate: c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (per Gavin Flood); later layers likely, disputed
SA_MB_saandilyopanisatHK.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Atharvaveda, framed as the teaching of Atharvan to the sage Śāṇḍilya, in three chapters. The first details an eight-limbed yoga (with yamas, niyamas, āsanas, nāḍī-purification, prāṇāyāma, etc.); the second expounds Brahma-vidyā; the third describes the forms of Brahman (sakala, niṣkala, sakala-niṣkala), making it an important early systematic yoga text.
SA_MB_saandilyopanisatHK.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads and twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. Anonymous and traditionally śruti; its detailed aṣṭāṅga-yoga has influenced later Haṭha-yoga literature.

SA_MB_tejobinduupanisatHK.title: Tejobindu Upaniṣad
SA_MB_tejobinduupanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_tejobinduupanisatHK.tradition: Hindu (Yoga/Advaita Vedānta; one of the Bindu Upaniṣads)
SA_MB_tejobinduupanisatHK.date_estimate: c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (core, per Flood); expanded later, disputed
SA_MB_tejobinduupanisatHK.summary: A minor Upaniṣad attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda (Atharvaveda in some lists), one of the five Bindu Upaniṣads. It teaches meditation on the 'point of light' (tejobindu) and a rigorous Advaita Vedānta from a yogic standpoint, dismissing mere bookish learning in favor of direct realization, and elaborating the fifteen-limbed yoga and the nature of the jīvanmukta.
SA_MB_tejobinduupanisatHK.history: One of the 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads and twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. Anonymous; the longer recension reflects later Advaita and yoga development layered onto an earlier core.

SA_MB_yoginiihrdayamHK.title: Yoginīhṛdaya
SA_MB_yoginiihrdayamHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_yoginiihrdayamHK.summary: A foundational Śākta Tantric treatise of the Śrīvidyā tradition, traditionally the second part of the Vāmakeśvaratantra (the first being the Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava). In three chapters (the Cakra-, Mantra- and Pūjā-saṃketa) it expounds the Śrīcakra/Śrīyantra of the goddess Tripurasundarī, the structure and ritual of her mantra, and the esoteric worship and meditation centered on the goddess.
SA_MB_yoginiihrdayamHK.history: A central scripture of the Śrīvidyā Tantra, the object of an important commentary (Dīpikā) by Amṛtānanda; the term 'Śrīvidyā' itself first appears in a 14th-century commentary on it. André Padoux and Roger-Orphé Jeanty produced the first full English translation (2013).

SA_MB_brhannilatantram-HK.title: Bṛhannīlatantra
SA_MB_brhannilatantram-HK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_brhannilatantram-HK.date_estimate: Late medieval, uncertain (perhaps c. 16th-18th c. CE); not firmly established
SA_MB_brhannilatantram-HK.summary: A Kaula Śākta Tantra dedicated chiefly to the worship of the goddesses Kālī and especially Tārā (Nīla-sarasvatī / Nīlā). It sets out mantras, yantras, ritual procedures, the pañcatattva (five-M) rites and esoteric sādhana directed to these fierce forms of the Mahāvidyā goddesses.
SA_MB_brhannilatantram-HK.history: A relatively rare text in the Śākta-Tantric corpus; a 1938 Srinagar edition was prepared by Ramchandra Kak and Harabhatta Shastri. Its author and precise date are unknown; like many such tantras it is anonymous and attributed to Śiva-Śakti dialogue.

SA_MB_agamakalpalataHK.title: Āgamakalpalatā
SA_MB_agamakalpalataHK.author: Yadunātha Cakravartī
SA_MB_agamakalpalataHK.date_estimate: Medieval/late-medieval (uncertain; author Yadunātha Cakravartī, likely Bengal)
SA_MB_agamakalpalataHK.summary: A Tantric ritual digest (nibandha), also called Āgamakalpalatikā, compiled by Yadunātha Cakravartī. It systematically gathers and illuminates Āgamic traditions, ritual procedures and devotional practices, drawing on and organizing material from the Āgama/Tantra scriptures into a practical handbook of worship.
SA_MB_agamakalpalataHK.history: Attributed to the scholar Yadunātha Cakravartī; the work survives in manuscripts and editions (e.g., digitized via Internet Archive). Precise dating is not firmly established in the scholarly literature consulted.

SA_MB_kriyasaraHK.title: Kriyāsāra (volume 1)
SA_MB_kriyasaraHK.author: Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya
SA_MB_kriyasaraHK.genre: ritual treatise / digest on Śaiva practice (kriyā)
SA_MB_kriyasaraHK.summary: A large Śaiva ritual treatise ('Essence of Action/Ritual') attributed to Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya, setting out the religious duties, initiation, worship, mantras and conduct of Śaiva (Vīraśaiva-oriented) devotees. Volume 1 of the edited edition covers the foundational doctrinal and ritual material.
SA_MB_kriyasaraHK.history: Edited and published in the Mysore Oriental (Government Oriental) Library series by R. Ramashastri (Vol. 1, 1954). The author Nīlakaṇṭha is associated with the Śrīkaṇṭha/Śivādvaita Śaiva tradition; his precise date is debated and not clearly fixed in the sources consulted.

SA_MB_saktisamgama_tantra_tarakhandaHK.title: Śaktisaṃgamatantra: Tārākhaṇḍa
SA_MB_saktisamgama_tantra_tarakhandaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_saktisamgama_tantra_tarakhandaHK.summary: The Tārākhaṇḍa ('Book of Tārā') is the second of the four khaṇḍas of the voluminous Śākta compendium Śaktisaṃgamatantra, the other three being devoted to Kālī, Sundarī and Chinnamastā. This section, comprising sixty-five chapters, expounds the mantras, yantras, ritual worship, pañcatattva and esoteric sādhana of the goddess Tārā.
SA_MB_saktisamgama_tantra_tarakhandaHK.history: The Śaktisaṃgamatantra is an encompassing Śākta Tantra said to have once contained 60,000 verses (extant manuscripts about 11,000). It was edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya in four volumes in the Gaekwad's Oriental Series, with the Tārākhaṇḍa published in 1941. Anonymous, framed as Śiva-Śakti dialogue.

SA_MB_sarvollaasatantraHK.title: Sarvollāsatantra
SA_MB_sarvollaasatantraHK.author: Sarvānandanātha (Sarvānanda)
SA_MB_sarvollaasatantraHK.date_estimate: c. 15th c. CE (author traditionally placed c. 1426 CE)
SA_MB_sarvollaasatantraHK.summary: A large Tantric compendium (saṃgraha) in sixty-four chapters compiled by the Bengali Kaula saint Sarvānandanātha, drawing together and quoting numerous earlier Tantras on the principal topics of Śākta-Tantric sādhana: mantras, ritual worship, the pañcatattva, the Mahāvidyā goddesses and esoteric practice.
SA_MB_sarvollaasatantraHK.history: Sarvānandanātha was one of the foremost Tāntrik saints of medieval Bengal, traditionally dated about a century before the 16th-century Tantrikas Kṛṣṇānanda, Brahmānanda and Pūrṇānanda (hence c. 1426 CE per one calculation, though this remains to be verified). Edited and published by Ram Mohan Chakravarti (1953, with a foreword by Gopinath Kaviraj) and others.

SA_MB_yogavishayaHK.title: Yogaviṣaya
SA_MB_yogavishayaHK.author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (Mīnanātha)
SA_MB_yogavishayaHK.date_estimate: Attribution to a 9th-10th c. figure; text likely later (c. 11th-13th c. CE), uncertain
SA_MB_yogavishayaHK.summary: A short yoga text traditionally attributed to Matsyendranātha (Mīna/Mīnanātha), founder-figure of the Nātha and Yoginī-Kaula traditions. It treats succinctly the guru and disciple, the yogic path, the cakras, and the ajapā 'haṃsa' mantra recited 21,600 times a day with the breath.
SA_MB_yogavishayaHK.history: Matsyendranātha is associated with the Yoginī-Kaula school and credited (often posthumously) with foundational Nātha/Haṭha-yoga works such as the Kaulajñānanirṇaya and Matsyendrasaṃhitā; he is usually placed in the 9th-10th centuries (Deccan/Bengal). The Yogaviṣaya is one of the short works ascribed to him, but such attributions are traditional and the actual text may be later.

SA_MB_tantrarajatantraHK.title: Tantrarājatantra
SA_MB_tantrarajatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_tantrarajatantraHK.date_estimate: Medieval (uncertain; commentary 'Manoramā' by Subhagānandanātha, Kashmir school)
SA_MB_tantrarajatantraHK.summary: The 'King of Tantras,' a major Śākta scripture (36/37 chapters) of the Śrīvidyā tradition devoted to the worship of Lalitā Mahā-Tripurasundarī. It expounds the principal precepts of Tantra, esoteric mantra and bīja doctrine, the Śrīcakra worship, nityā goddesses, and includes accounts of the Śākta pīṭhas.
