SA_MB_tantrarajatantraHK.history: A central Śrīvidyā Tantra accompanied by the Sanskrit commentary Manoramā of Subhagānandanātha (Kashmir school). The first eight chapters were edited by Mahāmahopādhyāya Lakshmana Shastri and published in John Woodroffe's ('Arthur Avalon') Tantrik Texts series (vol. 8, 1913-1933). Anonymous, framed as divine revelation.

SA_MB_jayakhyasamhitaHK.title: Jayākhyasaṃhitā
SA_MB_jayakhyasamhitaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_jayakhyasamhitaHK.date_estimate: c. 300-600 CE (early medieval); one of the oldest Pāñcarātra Saṃhitās
SA_MB_jayakhyasamhitaHK.summary: One of the three foundational ('ratna-traya') scriptures of the Vaiṣṇava Pāñcarātra tradition, alongside the Sātvata and Ahirbudhnya Saṃhitās. In about 4,592 anuṣṭubh verses across 33 paṭalas it treats cosmology and the emanation (vyūha) of God, the philosophy of creation and the liberation of the soul (jīva), mantra-construction, initiation, yoga and detailed ritual worship of Viṣṇu.
SA_MB_jayakhyasamhitaHK.history: Regarded as among the most ancient Pāñcarātra Āgamas. Anonymous and attributed to divine revelation; it has been an important source for the study of early Vaiṣṇava Tantric ritual and is the subject of modern critical study (e.g., Oxford Centre for Hindu Studies readings).

SA_MB_kamikapurvabhagaHK.title: Kāmikāgama: Pūrvabhāga
SA_MB_kamikapurvabhagaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kamikapurvabhagaHK.date_estimate: Core perhaps as early as c. 5th c. CE; transmitted and compiled into the 19th c.
SA_MB_kamikapurvabhagaHK.summary: The Pūrvabhāga (former/earlier part) of the Kāmikāgama, the first of the twenty-eight Siddhānta Śaivāgamas, a metrical scripture of about 12,000 verses in two parts. The Pūrvabhāga's seventy-five paṭalas treat the Śaiva ritual system in detail — daily obligatory (nitya), occasional (naimittika) and optional (kāmya) rites — together with temple architecture, consecration, the duties of priests and devotees, and elements of doctrine.
SA_MB_kamikapurvabhagaHK.history: A primary scripture of South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta; traditionally regarded as revealed by Śiva. While portions may date to at least the 5th century, the currently published recensions were compiled and printed in the 19th century, and a critical edition project is ongoing.

SA_MB_kaamikaagamauttarabhaagaHK.title: Kāmikāgama, Uttarabhāga
SA_MB_kaamikaagamauttarabhaagaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaamikaagamauttarabhaagaHK.date_estimate: early-medieval core (claimed pre-7th c. CE); transmitted and editorially fixed much later (published recension 19th c.)
SA_MB_kaamikaagamauttarabhaagaHK.summary: The Kāmikāgama is one of the principal scriptures (āgamas) of the South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta, a metrical work in two parts, the Pūrvabhāga and Uttarabhāga. The Uttarabhāga (roughly 98 chapters in anuṣṭubh meter) treats temple ritual, consecration, architecture, festivals, priestly duties, and the daily, occasional, and optional rites of Śaiva worship, with some doctrinal content. It is one of the most authoritative practical guides for Śaiva temple ritual in Tamil Nadu.
SA_MB_kaamikaagamauttarabhaagaHK.history: The Kāmika is counted among the twenty-eight mūla (root) Śaiva āgamas and is widely cited as a primary source of ritual authority in the Siddhānta tradition; like other āgamas its surviving form is the product of long transmission, and its printed Pūrva/Uttara recension was compiled and edited in the 19th century. It remains in active use by hereditary śivācārya temple priests.

SA_MB_kriyaakramadyotikaaHK.title: Kriyākramadyotikā
SA_MB_kriyaakramadyotikaaHK.author: Aghoraśiva (Aghoraśivācārya)
SA_MB_kriyaakramadyotikaaHK.summary: The Kriyākramadyotikā is a vast ritual digest (paddhati) by Aghoraśiva covering nearly all aspects of Śaiva Siddhānta ritual: daily worship (nitya) of Śiva, occasional (naimittika) and optional (kāmya) rites, initiation (dīkṣā), funerary rites, and temple festivals. It systematizes Āgamic ritual into a practical manual for officiating priests.
SA_MB_kriyaakramadyotikaaHK.history: Aghoraśiva, a teacher of the Amardaka order active around Chidambaram in the 12th century, composed this work c. 1156-1157 CE; it became the standard reference for ritual performance in South Indian Śaiva temples and is still followed by hereditary śivācārya priests in Tamil Nadu. Several sub-works (e.g. the Mahotsavavidhi) circulate as parts of it.

SA_MB_nirvanatantraHK.title: Nirvāṇatantra
SA_MB_nirvanatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_nirvanatantraHK.summary: The Nirvāṇatantra is a Śākta Tantric scripture belonging to the broad class of Kālī/Kaula tantras, presenting goddess worship, mantra, ritual, and liberation (nirvāṇa) doctrine in the usual dialogue form between Śiva and the Goddess. It is one of the many minor tantras catalogued in the Śākta corpus rather than a major systematic work.
SA_MB_nirvanatantraHK.history: Like most such tantras it is anonymous and of uncertain date, surviving in manuscript and print without secure attribution; it is sometimes mentioned alongside the larger Kālī-class tantras (e.g. the Mahānirvāṇatantra, Niruttaratantra), but its identity is not firmly fixed in scholarship, so this identification is tentative.

SA_MB_saktisa.mgamatantra_kalikhandaHK.title: Śaktisaṃgamatantra, Kālīkhaṇḍa
SA_MB_saktisa.mgamatantra_kalikhandaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_saktisa.mgamatantra_kalikhandaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; commonly dated c. 16th-17th c. CE
SA_MB_saktisa.mgamatantra_kalikhandaHK.summary: The Śaktisaṃgamatantra is a voluminous Śākta compendium divided into four khaṇḍas devoted to the goddesses Kālī, Tārā, Sundarī, and Chinnamastā. The Kālīkhaṇḍa is the section devoted to Kālī, treating her worship, mantras, ritual, cosmology, and Kaula doctrine; the work as a whole claims a vast original extent (tens of thousands of ślokas) of which far less survives.
SA_MB_saktisa.mgamatantra_kalikhandaHK.history: An encyclopedic late compilation of Śākta material, the Śaktisaṃgamatantra was edited in the 20th century (Benoytosh Bhattacharyya, Gaekwad's Oriental Series); its sections are anonymous and its dating is debated but generally placed in the late-medieval period. The Kālīkhaṇḍa is conventionally counted as the first of the four khaṇḍas.

SA_MB_saktisamgama_sundarikhandaHK.title: Śaktisaṃgamatantra, Sundarīkhaṇḍa
SA_MB_saktisamgama_sundarikhandaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_saktisamgama_sundarikhandaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; commonly dated c. 16th-17th c. CE
SA_MB_saktisamgama_sundarikhandaHK.summary: The Sundarīkhaṇḍa is the section of the Śaktisaṃgamatantra devoted to the goddess Sundarī (Tripurasundarī / Lalitā), covering her worship, mantras, ritual procedure, and associated Kaula and Śrīvidyā-flavored doctrine. It is one of four goddess-specific divisions (khaṇḍas) of the larger compendium.
SA_MB_saktisamgama_sundarikhandaHK.history: Part of the same encyclopedic late-medieval Śākta compilation as the Kālīkhaṇḍa, the Sundarīkhaṇḍa is anonymous and was edited and published (with a Hindi 'Sudhā' commentary) in modern times; it is conventionally the third khaṇḍa of the work. Dating is uncertain but generally late-medieval.

SA_MB_bhutasuddhitantra-HK.title: Bhūtaśuddhitantra
SA_MB_bhutasuddhitantra-HK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_bhutasuddhitantra-HK.summary: A Tantric text centered on bhūtaśuddhi, the ritual 'purification of the (five) elements' of the practitioner's body that precedes Tantric worship. The procedure dissolves the gross elements (earth, water, fire, air, ether) one into the next through breath, mantra, and visualization, then reconstitutes a divinized body fit for ritual.
SA_MB_bhutasuddhitantra-HK.history: Bhūtaśuddhi is a standard preliminary across Śākta and Śaiva Tantric practice and is described in many tantras and paddhatis; a freestanding 'Bhūtaśuddhitantra' is a minor, anonymous work of uncertain date and circulation, so its specific identity here is only tentatively fixed.

SA_MB_mahotsavavidhikramaHK.title: Mahotsavavidhikrama (Mahotsavavidhi)
SA_MB_mahotsavavidhikramaHK.author: Aghoraśiva (Aghoraśivācārya)
SA_MB_mahotsavavidhikramaHK.summary: A detailed step-by-step priest's manual for conducting the great temple festival (mahotsava), typically a nine-day cycle, in South Indian Śaiva temples. It prescribes the sequence of ritual, processions, adornment, music and dance, and the daily program by which collective devotion is focused on Śiva and the other temple deities.
SA_MB_mahotsavavidhikramaHK.history: The Mahotsavavidhi forms a part of Aghoraśiva's larger ritual digest, the Kriyākramadyotikā, completed c. 1157 CE; it is among the Āgamic works most used by modern South Indian temple priests. It was translated and studied by Richard H. Davis (A Priest's Guide for the Great Festival, Oxford UP).

SA_MB_kaamaakhyaatantraHK.title: Kāmākhyātantra
SA_MB_kaamaakhyaatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaamaakhyaatantraHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to late-medieval (2nd millennium CE)
SA_MB_kaamaakhyaatantraHK.summary: A Śākta Tantric text associated with the goddess Kāmākhyā, the Tantric form of the Goddess enshrined at the Nīlācala hill in Kāmarūpa (Assam), one of the most important centers of Tantric and Śākta worship. It sets out rituals, mantras, and the philosophy of her worship within the Kaula tradition.
SA_MB_kaamaakhyaatantraHK.history: Kāmarūpa/Assam has been a major Tantric center since at least the 7th century, and Kāmākhyā features prominently in works like the Kālikāpurāṇa and Yoginītantra. A discrete 'Kāmākhyātantra' is a minor, anonymous text of uncertain date tied to this regional cult; its precise identity and date are not securely established.

SA_MB_kaamadhenutantraHK.title: Kāmadhenutantra
SA_MB_kaamadhenutantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaamadhenutantraHK.summary: The Kāmadhenutantra is a Tantric work (c. 24 chapters) on the mystical significance of the letters of the Sanskrit alphabet (mātṛkā), treating the fifty/fifty-one varṇas as sound-forms of the Goddess. Framed as a dialogue in which Pārvatī asks Śiva about the letters and their relation to the tattvas, it correlates each letter with aspects of the Goddess, the body, and elements, and prescribes their worship.
SA_MB_kaamadhenutantraHK.history: It is an anonymous Śākta text of uncertain date belonging to the Tantric literature on varṇa/mātṛkā mysticism; it circulates in manuscript and print and is summarized on indological reference sites, but lacks a secure critical edition or firm dating.

SA_MB_saaktaanandataramginiiHK.title: Śāktānandataraṅgiṇī
SA_MB_saaktaanandataramginiiHK.author: Brahmānandagiri (Brahmānanda Giri)
SA_MB_saaktaanandataramginiiHK.genre: nibandha (digest / compendium of Tantric ritual and doctrine)
SA_MB_saaktaanandataramginiiHK.summary: The Śāktānandataraṅgiṇī ('the river of the bliss of the Goddess/Śakti') is a Śākta digest in eighteen chapters (ullāsas, 'splendours') ranging widely across Tantric themes: the elements and vital breaths in the body, the nāḍīs, the placement of the planets in the body, the process of birth, initiation, worship, and doctrine. It is a compilation that draws on and quotes many earlier tantras.
SA_MB_saaktaanandataramginiiHK.history: Attributed to the ascetic scholar Brahmānandagiri, active in roughly the 15th-17th centuries, it is valued partly because it preserves citations from tantras that are now lost; it was edited in the modern Yogatantra-granthamālā series (Sampurnanand Sanskrit University, Varanasi).

SA_MB_sattrimsattattvasamdohaHK.title: Ṣaṭtriṃśattattvasandoha
SA_MB_sattrimsattattvasamdohaHK.author: Amṛtānanda(nātha) (attributed; sometimes given as Rājānaka Ananda)
SA_MB_sattrimsattattvasamdohaHK.date_estimate: c. 12th c. CE (commentary by Rājānakānanda c. 17th c.)
SA_MB_sattrimsattattvasamdohaHK.summary: A short verse work (c. 21 stanzas) whose title means 'the totality of the thirty-six categories,' expounding the thirty-six tattvas of Kashmir Shaivism from Śiva (tattva 1) down to Dharaṇī/earth (tattva 36). It compactly maps the descent of universal manifestation from supreme consciousness to the material world.
SA_MB_sattrimsattattvasamdohaHK.history: Traditionally ascribed to Amṛtānanda(nātha) and commented upon by Rājānakānanda, it is a concise pedagogical summary of Trika metaphysics widely used to learn the tattva-scheme; the attribution and exact date are not entirely secure.

SA_MB_tantrasaaraHK.title: Tantrasāra
SA_MB_tantrasaaraHK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_tantrasaaraHK.date_estimate: c. late 10th-early 11th c. CE (author fl. c. 975-1025 CE)
SA_MB_tantrasaaraHK.summary: The Tantrasāra ('Essence of Tantra') is Abhinavagupta's own condensed, mostly-prose digest of his massive verse encyclopedia, the Tantrāloka. It systematically presents the doctrine, yoga, ritual, and soteriology of the non-dual Trika in a more accessible compass, structured around the means of liberation (upāyas).
SA_MB_tantrasaaraHK.history: Written by Abhinavagupta, the greatest synthesizer of the Trika and Pratyabhijñā schools of Kashmir Shaivism (pupil-lineage continued by Kṣemarāja), it serves as the abridgment of the Tantrāloka and is a standard entry point into his system; it survives in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies and numerous editions and translations.

SA_MB_paratrimsikavivaranaHK.title: Parātrīśikāvivṛti (Parātrīśikā-laghuvṛtti)
SA_MB_paratrimsikavivaranaHK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_paratrimsikavivaranaHK.summary: Abhinavagupta's short commentary (laghuvṛtti / vivṛti, also titled Anuttaratattvavimarśinī) on the Parātrīśikā, a brief 36-verse Trika scripture said to derive from the Rudrayāmala. It expounds the supreme reality (anuttara/parā) and the methods of realization, with a penetrating metaphysics of mantra, the Word (vāk), and consciousness.
SA_MB_paratrimsikavivaranaHK.history: Abhinavagupta wrote two commentaries on the Parātrīśikā, this short one and the much longer Parātrīśikāvivaraṇa; both are central to the Trika's understanding of the Goddess who transcends and embodies the triad. The title here (vivṛti) corresponds to the shorter laghuvṛtti rather than the long Vivaraṇa, though usage of the names overlaps.

SA_MB_janmamaranavicaaraHK.title: Janmamaraṇavicāra
SA_MB_janmamaranavicaaraHK.author: Bhaṭṭa Vāmadeva
SA_MB_janmamaranavicaaraHK.summary: The Janmamaraṇavicāra ('Investigation into Birth and Death') is a concise treatise examining the nature of life and death in non-dual Śaiva terms, tracing existence from the highest level of consciousness-awareness down to the physical world and the transmigrating individual who undergoes birth and death. It juxtaposes quotations from both Śaiva and medical (āyurvedic) sources to argue the unity of body and Śiva as expressions of one consciousness.
SA_MB_janmamaranavicaaraHK.history: Little is known of its author, Bhaṭṭa Vāmadeva, who was very likely a disciple of Yogarāja and thus within the post-Abhinavagupta Kashmiri Śaiva lineage (via Kṣemarāja); it is an enigmatic minor work published in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies and recently studied by John Nemec.

SA_MB_maliniijayavartikaHK.title: Mālinīvijayavārttika
SA_MB_maliniijayavartikaHK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_maliniijayavartikaHK.summary: A verse exposition (vārttika) by Abhinavagupta on the opening of the Mālinīvijayottaratantra, in which he takes the first verse(s) of that root tantra and unfolds their meaning at length, clarifying key Trika terms and laying out the doctrinal framework. It functions as a deep meditation on the scriptural basis of his system.
SA_MB_maliniijayavartikaHK.history: The Mālinīvijayottaratantra is the tantra Abhinavagupta treats as the chief scriptural authority for his Tantrāloka, and the Mālinīvijayavārttika is his focused commentarial elaboration of its beginning; it is preserved in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies.

SA_MB_maliniivijayottaraHK.title: Mālinīvijayottaratantra
SA_MB_maliniivijayottaraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_maliniivijayottaraHK.summary: The Mālinīvijayottaratantra is, per Abhinavagupta, the foremost of the sixty-four non-dual Bhairava tantras on which the Trika is based. It expounds the metaphysics, ritual, initiation, and yoga of the Trika centered on the triad of goddesses Parā, Parāparā, and Aparā, and attempts to integrate competing yoga systems into a framework of levels of awareness and Kaula immersions.
SA_MB_maliniivijayottaraHK.history: An anonymous revealed scripture composed in Kashmir, generally dated to around the 9th century (certainly before Abhinavagupta), it became the central scriptural authority for Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka, which frequently quotes it; it is foundational to the entire Trika tradition.

SA_MB_spandanirnayaHK.title: Spandanirṇaya
SA_MB_spandanirnayaHK.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_MB_spandanirnayaHK.date_estimate: c. early 11th c. CE (author fl. early 11th c.)
SA_MB_spandanirnayaHK.summary: The Spandanirṇaya is Kṣemarāja's authoritative prose commentary on the Spandakārikā, the foundational verses of the Spanda ('vibration/pulsation') school of Kashmir Shaivism. It expounds the doctrine that the dynamic creative throb of consciousness (spanda) underlies all experience, and is regarded as the most complete and authoritative of the commentaries on the kārikās.
SA_MB_spandanirnayaHK.history: The root Spandakārikās are traditionally attributed to Vasugupta (with his disciple Kallaṭa, who wrote the Vṛtti and who is sometimes named as author); the title metadata's attribution to Kallaṭa reflects this debated authorship. Kṣemarāja, a direct disciple of Abhinavagupta, composed the Spandanirṇaya at the request of his disciple Śūra. The text is preserved in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies.

SA_MB_devinamalavilasaHK.title: Devīnāmavilāsa
SA_MB_devinamalavilasaHK.author: Sāhib Kaul
SA_MB_devinamalavilasaHK.summary: The Devīnāmavilāsa is a tour de force of devotional Sanskrit poetry by Sāhib Kaul, an elaborate and stylistically complex kāvya-style elaboration of the Bhavānīsahasranāmastotra (the thousand names of the goddess Bhavānī). It treats the divine names of the Goddess in highly refined classical poetic style.
SA_MB_devinamalavilasaHK.history: Sāhib Kaul (active mid-17th century) and his lineage were important in Kashmir's religious history for introducing eastern-Indian Śākta elements into Kashmiri religious culture; he also composed works such as the Śyāmāpaddhati. The Devīnāmavilāsa exemplifies the late efflorescence of Śākta devotional poetry in Kashmir.

SA_MB_bhagavadgiitaa_ramakanthaHK.title: Bhagavadgītā
SA_MB_bhagavadgiitaa_ramakanthaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_MB_bhagavadgiitaa_ramakanthaHK.date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (composite; redaction often placed 3rd-2nd c. BCE)
SA_MB_bhagavadgiitaa_ramakanthaHK.summary: The Bhagavadgītā ('Song of the Lord') is a 700-verse dialogue in which the god Kṛṣṇa, as Arjuna's charioteer, counsels the despondent Pāṇḍava prince on the eve of the Kurukṣetra war. It synthesizes paths of action (karma), devotion (bhakti), and knowledge (jñāna) and presents a theology of God as the supreme reality, becoming the most influential single text of Hindu thought.
SA_MB_bhagavadgiitaa_ramakanthaHK.history: It forms chapters 23-40 of the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata and is traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, though scholars regard it as a composite work of multiple hands inserted into the epic. It has attracted major commentaries from Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva, Abhinavagupta, and others, and is central to every school of Vedānta.

SA_MB_uddamaresvaratantramHK.title: Uḍḍāmareśvaratantra
SA_MB_uddamaresvaratantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_uddamaresvaratantramHK.summary: The Uḍḍāmareśvaratantra (c. 15 chapters) is a Tantric text on magical rites (the six acts, ṣaṭkarma-type practices) aimed at worldly ends: subjugation, attraction, controlling enemies, and remedies for diseases. It compiles practical mantra-magic and ritual recipes rather than original metaphysics.
SA_MB_uddamaresvaratantramHK.history: An anonymous applied-Tantric work of uncertain date, it is associated with the broader Uḍḍāmara/Uḍḍīśa class of magical tantras and is cited in later Tantric-medical compilations; it has been edited (e.g. by Jagaddhar Zadoo) and survives in manuscript and print, with much medically and magically relevant material.

SA_MB_bodhapancadasikaaHK.title: Abhinavagupta: Bodhapañcadaśikā
SA_MB_bodhapancadasikaaHK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_bodhapancadasikaaHK.summary: A very short work of fifteen verses (plus a closing stanza) by Abhinavagupta distilling the essence of Trika/Kashmir Shaiva non-dualism for devotees lacking the training to follow his large treatises. It compactly presents the nature of Śiva as supreme consciousness, the role of Śakti, and the relation of consciousness to manifestation.
SA_MB_bodhapancadasikaaHK.history: Attributed with confidence to Abhinavagupta (fl. c. 975–1025 CE), the great Trika polymath. It belongs to his accessible, devotionally oriented short works and is widely studied in modern Kashmir Shaiva circles (e.g., the oral commentaries of Swami Lakshman Joo).

SA_MB_saivakaalavivekaHK.title: Śaivakalāviveka
SA_MB_saivakaalavivekaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (uncertain)
SA_MB_saivakaalavivekaHK.summary: A Shaiva work, known from the Muktabodha manuscript corpus, whose title indicates a 'discrimination (viveka) of the kalās' — the divisions/phases of cosmic emanation (the tattvas grouped under the kalās: nivṛtti, pratiṣṭhā, vidyā, śānti, śāntyatīta) within Shaiva cosmology. It would expound the structure of the Shaiva cosmos and the placement of principles within the kalā scheme.
SA_MB_saivakaalavivekaHK.history: An obscure text preserved chiefly in manuscript collections rather than in standard editions; its authorship, exact tradition (Siddhānta vs. non-Siddhānta), and date are not securely established. Identification here is provisional, based on the title's analysis of the Shaiva kalā cosmology.

SA_MB_phetkaariniitantramHK.title: Phetkāriṇītantra
SA_MB_phetkaariniitantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_phetkaariniitantramHK.summary: A Shakta tantric scripture dealing with mantras, deities, ritual procedures (pūjā, nyāsa) and magical/practical rites. It is frequently cited in later digest (nibandha) literature for its mantra prescriptions and is associated with fierce goddess worship.
SA_MB_phetkaariniitantramHK.history: An anonymous tantra transmitted in manuscript form and printed from 19th-century editions; it was digitized by the Muktabodha Indological Research Institute under Mark S.G. Dyczkowski. Its precise date and provenance are uncertain.

SA_MB_jnaanasankaliniitantramHK.title: Jñānasaṅkalinītantra
SA_MB_jnaanasankaliniitantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_jnaanasankaliniitantramHK.summary: A short tantra of about 107 verses cast as a dialogue between Śiva and Śakti, emphasizing jñāna (pure knowledge) over ritual. It treats the syllable Oṃ (a-u-m as Śiva, Śakti and their union), the three guṇas, macrocosm–microcosm correspondence, the five elements, and the identity of the individual ātman with the universal paramātman, defining yoga as the cessation of all thought.
SA_MB_jnaanasankaliniitantramHK.history: An anonymous, concise tantric text popular in modern yoga circles and translated several times (e.g., by Paramahansa Prajnanananda). Its date and original milieu are not firmly established.

SA_MB_kaalaanalatantraHK.title: Kālānalatantra
SA_MB_kaalaanalatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaalaanalatantraHK.summary: A tantra associated with the Kālī cluster of Shakta scriptures (named alongside the Nīlā, Vīra and Māyā tantras), containing mantras, kavacas and stotras such as a Pratyaṅgirā / Siddhilakṣmī protective hymn. 'Kālānala' ('the fire of time/death') signals its fierce, apotropaic and goddess-centered character.
SA_MB_kaalaanalatantraHK.history: An obscure tantra preserved in manuscripts and digital catalogues; its authorship and date are unknown. The name also occurs as a technical term (e.g., a doorkeeper in the Kubjikā tradition), but here it denotes a discrete tantric text.

SA_MB_svacchandapaddhatiHK.title: Cidānandanātha: Svacchandapaddhati
SA_MB_svacchandapaddhatiHK.author: Cidānandanātha
SA_MB_svacchandapaddhatiHK.summary: A paddhati (systematized ritual handbook) organizing the worship of Svacchanda Bhairava according to the Svacchanda Tantra, by an author bearing the initiatory name Cidānandanātha. It would lay out the mantras, nyāsa, maṇḍala and pūjā sequence for the Svacchandabhairava cult.
SA_MB_svacchandapaddhatiHK.history: The base scripture, the Svacchanda(bhairava)tantra, is a major Bhairava/Kashmir Shaiva tantra of over 3,000 verses (datable to at least the 7th century) commented on by Kṣemarāja. This paddhati is a later ritual digest; the identity and date of Cidānandanātha are not well documented.

SA_MB_merutantraHK.title: Merutantra
SA_MB_merutantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_merutantraHK.summary: A large, encyclopedic tantra covering mantra-śāstra, ritual, cosmology, deity worship and miscellaneous lore; many of its verses parallel chapters of the Vārāhī Tantra. It is wide-ranging and digest-like rather than a single focused doctrinal scripture.
SA_MB_merutantraHK.history: Generally regarded as a comparatively late composition (it references foreign peoples and is often dated to the early modern period). Anonymous and transmitted in multi-volume printed editions; its breadth made it a popular source for later ritualists.

SA_MB_pauskarvrttiHK.title: Jñānaprakāśācārya: Pauṣkaravṛtti
SA_MB_pauskarvrttiHK.author: Jñānaprakāśa (Jñānaprakāśācārya of Śālivāṭi, Jaffna)
SA_MB_pauskarvrttiHK.summary: A vṛtti (commentary) on the Pauṣkarāgama (Pauṣkara-pāramēśvara), one of the doctrinally important Shaiva Siddhānta scriptures whose chapters expound core Siddhānta tenets such as the nature of pati, paśu and pāśa and the means of liberation. The commentary develops the Pauṣkara's doctrine in its later South Indian form.
SA_MB_pauskarvrttiHK.history: Jñānaprakāśa of Śālivāṭi (Jaffna, Sri Lanka) is one of two principal commentators on the Pauṣkarāgama (the other being Umāpati). The work belongs to the later, Tamil-region phase of Sanskrit Shaiva Siddhānta scholasticism; one of the Pauṣkara commentaries remained long unpublished.

SA_MB_isvarapratyabhijnavimarsinivyakhya.title: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinīvyākhyā
SA_MB_isvarapratyabhijnavimarsinivyakhya.author: uncertain (a commentator on the Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī; possibly the Bhāskarī of Bhāskarakaṇṭha)
SA_MB_isvarapratyabhijnavimarsinivyakhya.date_estimate: uncertain (the famous Bhāskarī sub-commentary is c. 17th–18th c. CE)
SA_MB_isvarapratyabhijnavimarsinivyakhya.summary: A vyākhyā (explanatory sub-commentary) on Abhinavagupta's Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī, itself the 'short gloss' on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational text of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of Kashmir Shaivism. The sub-commentary unpacks Abhinavagupta's dense exposition of the doctrine that liberation is the recognition of one's identity with Śiva-consciousness.
SA_MB_isvarapratyabhijnavimarsinivyakhya.history: The best-known such sub-commentary is the Bhāskarī by Bhāskarakaṇṭha (edited by K. A. Subramania Iyer and K. C. Pandey, Allahabad, 1938–1954), written because the kārikās and Vimarśinī are extremely difficult without help. Without the colophon it is not certain which vyākhyā this manuscript represents, but the Bhāskarī is the most likely candidate.

SA_MB_amsumatkaasyapaamgamaHK.title: Aṃśumatkāśyapāgama
SA_MB_amsumatkaasyapaamgamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to revelation; assoc. with sage Kāśyapa)
SA_MB_amsumatkaasyapaamgamaHK.genre: āgama (Shaiva scripture; temple architecture and iconography)
SA_MB_amsumatkaasyapaamgamaHK.summary: The Kāśyapa portion/recension associated with the Aṃśumadāgama, one of the Siddhāntāgamas; it is essentially a śilpaśāstra dealing at length with temple architecture and the iconography of deities. It is divided into a part on temple construction (from soil-testing onward) and a part on the making of images.
SA_MB_amsumatkaasyapaamgamaHK.history: Aṃśumat (Aṃśumadāgama) is counted among the 28 Siddhāntāgamas; Aṃśumatkāśyapa/Aṃśumatkāśyapīya is named as one of its upāgamas in manuscript colophons. The associated Kāśyapa Śilpaśāstra is widely treated as the architectural-iconographic component of the Aṃśumat āgama and is influential in South Indian temple building (āgamic śilpa).

SA_MB_kincittantraHK.title: Kiñcitatantra
SA_MB_kincittantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kincittantraHK.summary: An obscure tantric text known essentially by title from manuscript/digital-library catalogues; no reliable description of its specific contents could be confirmed. The title would denote it a tantra ('Kiñcit-tantra'), presumably treating mantra and ritual within the Shaiva-Shakta sphere.
SA_MB_kincittantraHK.history: Not identifiable with confidence; the work appears in the Muktabodha-type corpus of digitized tantric manuscripts but lacks an established scholarly edition, secure author, or date.

SA_MB_dvisatikaalottaramHK.title: Dviśatikālottara
SA_MB_dvisatikaalottaramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_dvisatikaalottaramHK.date_estimate: mid-1st millennium CE (the Kālottara corpus is early)
SA_MB_dvisatikaalottaramHK.summary: The 200-verse (dviśatikā) recension of the Kālottara Tantra, a widely transmitted early Shaiva scripture that exists in several recensions of different lengths (50, 100, 200, 350, 700, 1300 verses). It concentrates on initiation, mantra, yoga and the practical ritual core of the Shaiva path, and underlies many later ritual manuals.
SA_MB_dvisatikaalottaramHK.history: The Kālottara (a section/recension complex of the Kālajñāna / Sārdhatriśatikālottara family) is among the more archaic Shaiva tantras; its recensions were edited and studied by scholars such as Dominic Goodall and others. The Dviśatikālottara specifically is the 200-verse version within this group.

SA_MB_candrajnaanaagamaHK.title: Candrajñānāgama (Vīraśaiva recension)
SA_MB_candrajnaanaagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to revelation by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva)
SA_MB_candrajnaanaagamaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (the Vīraśaiva recension comparatively late)
SA_MB_candrajnaanaagamaHK.summary: The Vīraśaiva recension of the Candrajñānāgama, one of the 28 Siddhāntāgamas. It is a pratiṣṭhā-tantra concerned with the installation and worship of Śaiva images and liṅgas, and its Kriyāpāda notably classifies Śaivas by conduct (ācāra), including the Vīraśaiva type.
SA_MB_candrajnaanaagamaHK.history: Candrajñāna is counted among the five āgamas revealed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Sadāśiva. The Vīraśaiva community preserved and adapted a recension of it as part of its agamic foundation, even though the philosophy of the Vīraśaivas is said to draw on the full set of 28 Śaivāgamas.

SA_MB_makutaagama_viirasaivaHK.title: Makuṭāgama (Vīraśaiva recension)
SA_MB_makutaagama_viirasaivaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to revelation by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva)
SA_MB_makutaagama_viirasaivaHK.summary: The Vīraśaiva recension of the Makuṭāgama (Makuṭa), one of the 28 Siddhāntāgamas. It provides guidelines for temple construction, image installation, ritual and worship practices, here transmitted within the Vīraśaiva community.
SA_MB_makutaagama_viirasaivaHK.history: Makuṭa is among the five āgamas revealed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Sadāśiva (proclaimed at sage Gautama). Like the Candrajñāna, it was preserved in a Vīraśaiva recension and counts among the scriptures underpinning Vīraśaiva ritual.

SA_MB_Tridasadaamaratantresiddhikaaliist.title: Tridaśaḍāmaratantre Siddhikālīstotra
SA_MB_Tridasadaamaratantresiddhikaaliist.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_Tridasadaamaratantresiddhikaaliist.summary: A hymn (stotra) to the goddess Siddhakālī / Siddhikālī found within the Tridaśaḍāmara Tantra (a Ḍāmara-class tantra; 'tridaśa' = the thirty gods). The Ḍāmara stream is a fierce, magically oriented branch of tantra centered on yoginī and goddess siddhis, and this stotra would be a praise-and-invocation hymn used for such ends.
SA_MB_Tridasadaamaratantresiddhikaaliist.history: An excerpt-text known from manuscript/digital catalogues rather than a critical edition; the Ḍāmara Tantra family is associated with apotropaic and siddhi-oriented Shakta practice. Date and authorship are not established.

SA_MB_TridasadaamaraapratyaHK.title: Tridaśaḍāmarāpratyaṅgirāviṣayakanānātantra
SA_MB_TridasadaamaraapratyaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_TridasadaamaraapratyaHK.tradition: Hindu (Shakta / Kālī- and Pratyaṅgirā-tantra, Ḍāmara tradition)
SA_MB_TridasadaamaraapratyaHK.genre: tantra (compilation of extracts on a single deity)
SA_MB_TridasadaamaraapratyaHK.summary: A composite text gathering 'various tantra' (nānātantra) materials on the subject (viṣaya) of the fierce protective goddess Pratyaṅgirā, drawn from the Tridaśaḍāmara/Ḍāmara tantric stream. It collects mantras, kavacas and ritual material for Pratyaṅgirā worship, a tradition used especially for protection and counter-sorcery.
SA_MB_TridasadaamaraapratyaHK.history: A manuscript-corpus compilation rather than a unitary classical scripture; its very title ('various tantras concerning Pratyaṅgirā from the Tridaśaḍāmara') marks it as an anthology. No secure author or date; identification is provisional.

SA_MB_MahaayaagakramaHK.title: Bhāskararāya: Mahāyāgakrama
SA_MB_MahaayaagakramaHK.author: Bhāskararāya Makhin
SA_MB_MahaayaagakramaHK.summary: A ritual manual by the great Śrīvidyā authority Bhāskararāya setting out the sequence (krama) of the 'great sacrifice/worship' (mahāyāga) — the elaborate Śrīcakra worship of the goddess Lalitā Tripurasundarī, including the prescribed offerings, mantras and order of rites.
SA_MB_MahaayaagakramaHK.history: Bhāskararāya Makhin (c. 1690–1785) was a prolific Śrīvidyā polymath who authored more than forty works (including the Varivasyārahasya, Setubandha, and the Lalitāsahasranāma-bhāṣya). The Mahāyāgakrama belongs to his practical, ritual-prescriptive output systematizing Śrīvidyā worship.

SA_MB_laksmiitantraHK.title: Lakṣmītantra
SA_MB_laksmiitantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_laksmiitantraHK.tradition: Hindu (Vaishnava / Pāñcarātra, with strong Śākta orientation)
SA_MB_laksmiitantraHK.summary: A Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇava saṃhitā of roughly 3,600 verses in about 57 chapters, dedicated to the goddess Lakṣmī as the supreme Śakti of Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa. It expounds Pāñcarātra cosmogony, the vyūha doctrine, mantra-śāstra, and the iconography of Lakṣmī-Nārāyaṇa, glorifying the feminine divine power as the dynamic cause of creation, preservation and liberation.
SA_MB_laksmiitantraHK.history: A medieval Pāñcarātra text notable for integrating Śākta theology into Vaiṣṇavism; commonly dated between the 9th and 12th centuries CE. It was edited and translated into English by Sanjukta Gupta (1972), which made it well known in scholarship.
related_works: Pāñcarātra saṃhitās (e.g., Ahirbudhnya Saṃhitā, Jayākhya Saṃhitā)

SA_MB_sidhaantasikhaamaniHK.title: Śivayogiśivācārya: Siddhāntaśikhāmaṇi
SA_MB_sidhaantasikhaamaniHK.author: Śivayogi Śivācārya (Śivayogin)
SA_MB_sidhaantasikhaamaniHK.date_estimate: disputed: traditionally early (8th–10th c.?), but text most plausibly c. 13th–15th c. CE
SA_MB_sidhaantasikhaamaniHK.summary: The foundational Sanskrit doctrinal text of the Vīraśaiva (Liṅgāyata) tradition, expounding the system of the 101 sthalas (ekottaraśatasthala) — graded stations of doctrine and practice culminating in union with Śiva. It teaches Śakti-viśiṣṭādvaita, holding Śiva to be identical with the Upaniṣadic brahman and Śaiva teaching to be in harmony with the Veda.
SA_MB_sidhaantasikhaamaniHK.history: Attributed to Śivayogi Śivācārya; its date is much debated, with traditional claims of great antiquity but scholarly opinion generally placing the work in the later medieval period (often c. 13th–15th c.). It is the key systematic Sanskrit scripture for Vīraśaiva theology and has its own commentary tradition (e.g., Maritontadārya's Tattvapradīpikā).

SA_MB_karanagamaHK.title: Karaṇāgama
SA_MB_karanagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to revelation)
SA_MB_karanagamaHK.summary: One of the 28 Siddhāntāgamas of Shaiva Siddhānta. Like its companion āgamas it concerns the worship of Śiva — temple ritual, installation and worship of the liṅga and images, mantra, dīkṣā and related kriyā — within the Siddhānta framework of pati, paśu and pāśa.
SA_MB_karanagamaHK.history: Karaṇa is enumerated among the 28 canonical Śaiva Siddhāntāgamas (revealed through the five faces of Sadāśiva). It survives chiefly in South Indian āgamic manuscript and ritual traditions; precise dating of the individual āgama is not established.
related_works: the 28 Śaiva Siddhāntāgamas (e.g., Kāmika, Kāraṇa, Ajita, Raurava)

SA_MB_sivadharmaHK.title: Śivadharma (Śivadharmaśāstra)
SA_MB_sivadharmaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_sivadharmaHK.date_estimate: c. 6th-7th c. CE (earliest layer of the Śivadharma corpus; before 12th c.)
SA_MB_sivadharmaHK.summary: The Śivadharmaśāstra is the foundational text of the Śivadharma corpus, a body of works prescribing the religious duties, devotional practices, and observances of the lay Śaiva community. It treats topics such as worship of the Śivaliṅga, gift-giving (dāna), the merit of building and maintaining temples, vows, and the conduct expected of a Śiva devotee. It is cast as a dialogue, traditionally narrated by Nandikeśvara (Nandi) to the sages.
SA_MB_sivadharmaHK.history: It is among the earliest surviving devotional Śaiva texts, immensely influential as shown by abundant manuscript evidence (especially from Nepal) and inscriptions, and it even served as a source for the Buddhist Kāraṇḍavyūhasūtra. Its narration is traditionally ascribed to Nandin, so it is also cited as 'Nandiprokta.' It circulates as the first of usually six to eight texts that make up the Śivadharma corpus.

SA_MB_sivadharma_nandiproktaHK.title: Śivadharma Nandiprokta (Śivadharmaśāstra, 'spoken by Nandin')
SA_MB_sivadharma_nandiproktaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (narrated by Nandikeśvara)
SA_MB_sivadharma_nandiproktaHK.summary: This title ('Śivadharma spoken by Nandin') refers to the Śivadharmaśāstra, the foundational text of the Śivadharma corpus, framed as the teaching delivered by Nandikeśvara (Nandin) on the religious duties of lay Śaivas. It covers Śivaliṅga worship, dāna (gift-giving), temple endowment, vows, and the merit of devotion to Śiva.
SA_MB_sivadharma_nandiproktaHK.history: The epithet 'Nandiprokta' marks the work's traditional ascription of narration to Nandin and indicates this is the same text as the Śivadharmaśāstra rather than a separate work. It is the earliest and most influential component of the Śivadharma corpus, widely transmitted in Nepalese and South Indian manuscript traditions.

SA_MB_sivadharmottaraHK.title: Śivadharmottara
SA_MB_sivadharmottaraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_sivadharmottaraHK.date_estimate: c. 7th-9th c. CE (after the Śivadharmaśāstra; before 12th c.)
SA_MB_sivadharmottaraHK.summary: The Śivadharmottara is the second principal text of the Śivadharma corpus, a sequel ('uttara') to the Śivadharmaśāstra. In roughly 1,750 verses across about twelve chapters it expounds dharma, worldly enjoyment (bhukti) and liberation (mukti), with emphasis on gift-giving, the merit of teaching and copying the Śivadharma itself, devotional discipline, and a notable chapter on yoga and meditation.
SA_MB_sivadharmottaraHK.history: Composed somewhat later than the Śivadharmaśāstra, it became one of the two most authoritative Śivadharma texts, transmitted widely in Nepal, South India and Odisha. It is sometimes classed as an upāgama of the Śarvoktāgama within the Siddhānta scheme and survives in versions of twelve or thirteen chapters in different manuscript traditions.

SA_MB_mrigendrapaddhatitiikaaHK.title: Mṛgendrapaddhatiṭīkā
SA_MB_mrigendrapaddhatitiikaaHK.author: Naṭeśaguru (alias Vaktraśambhu); on the Mṛgendrapaddhati attributed to Aghoraśivācārya
SA_MB_mrigendrapaddhatitiikaaHK.date_estimate: ṭīkā likely 13th c. or later; base Mṛgendrapaddhati mid-12th c. CE (Tamil country)
SA_MB_mrigendrapaddhatitiikaaHK.summary: This is the commentary (ṭīkā) on the Mṛgendrapaddhati, a Śaiva Siddhānta ritual manual that systematizes the practices taught in the Mṛgendrāgama (an authoritative Siddhānta scripture, an upāgama of the Kāmikāgama). The work transmits the rules of initiation, daily and occasional worship, and temple ritual, with the commentary clarifying the terse procedural verses.
SA_MB_mrigendrapaddhatitiikaaHK.history: The Mṛgendrapaddhati is associated with Aghoraśivācārya (mid-12th c.), the major systematizer of South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta; the ṭīkā is by Naṭeśaguru, known also as Vaktraśambhu, son of one Śaṃkara, per its colophon. Both text and commentary survive only in manuscript and have been studied/edited by the French Institute of Pondicherry.

SA_MB_yogasarasamgrahaHK.title: Yogasārasaṃgraha
SA_MB_yogasarasamgrahaHK.author: Vijñānabhikṣu
SA_MB_yogasarasamgrahaHK.tradition: Hindu (Pātañjala Yoga / Sāṃkhya, integrated with Bhedābheda Vedānta)
SA_MB_yogasarasamgrahaHK.summary: The Yogasārasaṃgraha is a concise prose digest of the essentials of Pātañjala Yoga compiled by Vijñānabhikṣu, distilling the substance of his larger commentary, the Yogavārttika (on the Yogasūtras and the Vyāsabhāṣya). It presents the key concepts and stages of yoga practice in an accessible, systematic form.
SA_MB_yogasarasamgrahaHK.history: Vijñānabhikṣu (15th-16th c., from Bihar) was a major commentator who wrote on Vedānta, Sāṃkhya and Yoga and sought to integrate them on a non-dualist (Bhedābheda) basis. The Yogasārasaṃgraha was edited and translated into English by Gaṅgānātha Jhā. (Note: a distinct Śaiva 'Yogasārasaṃgraha' also exists; this entry follows the best-attested identification.)

SA_MB_parasuramakalpasutraHK.title: Paraśurāmakalpasūtra
SA_MB_parasuramakalpasutraHK.author: traditionally ascribed to Paraśurāma (compiled by Sumedha); historically anonymous
SA_MB_parasuramakalpasutraHK.summary: The Paraśurāmakalpasūtra is a terse aphoristic manual of Śrīvidyā Tantric worship of the goddess Lalitā-Tripurasundarī and associated deities (including Gaṇapati), set out in sūtra form. It codifies the Kaula ritual procedures, mantras, and the construction of the Śrīcakra used in the cult of the goddess.
SA_MB_parasuramakalpasutraHK.history: Traditionally attributed to the avatāra Paraśurāma and said to have been compiled by his disciple Sumedha, it is in fact an anonymous text of roughly the 16th-17th century from South India, with roots in the Dattātreya tradition. Its aphorisms are cryptic and are typically read with Rameśvara's commentary (Saubhāgyodaya, 1831 CE), making it a central practical text of Śrīvidyā.

SA_MB_Paraatrisikaavivrtti_berlinHK.title: Parātriśikāvivṛti (Parātrīśikāvivaraṇa)
SA_MB_Paraatrisikaavivrtti_berlinHK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_Paraatrisikaavivrtti_berlinHK.summary: This is Abhinavagupta's extensive commentary on the Parātriśikā (Parātrīśikā), a short Trika scriptural text of around 36 verses (drawn from the Rudrayāmala) on the supreme goddess Parā and the metaphysics of sound, mantra, and the phonematic emanation of reality. In it Abhinavagupta develops his non-dual Trika philosophy, the doctrine of recognition (pratyabhijñā), and the highest 'anuttara' teaching.
SA_MB_Paraatrisikaavivrtti_berlinHK.history: Abhinavagupta (fl. c. 975-1025 CE), the great polymath of Kashmir, wrote two commentaries on this text: a longer Vivaraṇa (often called Parātriśikāvivṛti) and a shorter Laghuvṛtti. The Vivaraṇa is one of his most philosophically dense works and a cornerstone of Trika Śaiva metaphysics and mantra theory.

SA_MB_gurustutiratnavaliHK.title: Gurustutiratnāvalī
SA_MB_gurustutiratnavaliHK.author: Govinda Kaula
SA_MB_gurustutiratnavaliHK.genre: stotra (devotional hymn) in praise of the guru
SA_MB_gurustutiratnavaliHK.summary: The Gurustutiratnāvalī ('String of Jewels in Praise of the Guru') is a sophisticated Sanskrit hymn by Govinda Kaula of Kashmir celebrating his lineage teacher, the prolific author Sāhib Kaula, and equating the guru with the supreme deity Śiva. It is a literary expression of guru-devotion (gurubhakti) within the Kashmirian Śaiva-Śākta tradition.
SA_MB_gurustutiratnavaliHK.history: Govinda Kaula was an eighteenth-century Kashmirian author writing in the post-classical Kashmir Shaiva milieu; the hymn praises his teacher's teacher Sāhib Kaula. The work survives in several manuscripts and has been studied as a window onto the history of guru-stuti and guru-devotion in Kashmir and South Asia more broadly.

SA_MB_srividyarnavatantramHK.title: Śrīvidyārṇavatantra
SA_MB_srividyarnavatantramHK.author: Vidyāraṇya Yati (Śrīvidyāraṇya)
SA_MB_srividyarnavatantramHK.genre: tantra / comprehensive ritual and mantra compendium
SA_MB_srividyarnavatantramHK.date_estimate: disputed; commonly placed c. 16th-17th c. CE (later than the famed 14th-c. Vidyāraṇya)
SA_MB_srividyarnavatantramHK.summary: The Śrīvidyārṇava ('Ocean of Śrīvidyā') is a large, comprehensive Tantric manual of mantraśāstra and Śākta worship, especially of the goddess in the Śrīvidyā tradition. Divided into pūrva and uttara halves of eighteen chapters (śvāsa) each, it served as a textbook for mantra instruction in certain brahminical circles and even discusses Buddhist deities and mantras absorbed into the Hindu Tantras.
SA_MB_srividyarnavatantramHK.history: It is attributed to a Vidyāraṇya Yati (Śrīvidyāraṇya); the attribution to the renowned 14th-century Vidyāraṇya of Vijayanagara/Sringeri is doubtful, and the text is generally regarded as a later compilation. It is an important encyclopedic source for Śrīvidyā and tantric practice, frequently cited in studies of cross-traditional mantra borrowing.

SA_MB_paramesvariitantraHK.title: Parameśvarīmatatantra
SA_MB_paramesvariitantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_paramesvariitantraHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely 1st-2nd millennium CE Kaula tantra (medieval)
SA_MB_paramesvariitantraHK.summary: The Parameśvarīmatatantra is a Tantric scripture associated with the cult of the goddess (the 'Parameśvarī'), connected to the Kubjikā / Paścimāmnāya stream of goddess-centred Kaula Śaivism. Such texts teach the worship of the supreme Goddess, her mantras, maṇḍalas, yogic anatomy, and esoteric ritual practice.
SA_MB_paramesvariitantraHK.history: It belongs to the broad corpus of goddess-oriented Kaula tantras (the Kubjikā tradition and related Bhairava/Śākta scriptures); precise authorship and date are not securely established. It should be distinguished from the Saiddhāntika Pārameśvaratantra (a different, dated Śaiva Siddhānta scripture).

SA_MB_vatulottaraHK.title: Vātulottarāgama (Vātulottara)
SA_MB_vatulottaraHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_vatulottaraHK.summary: The Vātulottarāgama ('vatullotarāgama') is an upāgama (supplementary scripture) of the Vātulāgama, one of the twenty-eight Siddhāntāgamas that form the canonical Śaiva Siddhānta revelation. As an āgama it treats the standard four-fold matter of doctrine, yoga, ritual, and conduct (jñāna, yoga, kriyā, caryā), elaborating worship, initiation, and temple practice of Śiva.
SA_MB_vatulottaraHK.history: Within the Siddhānta classification the twenty-eight mūlāgamas each have associated upāgamas; the Vātulottara is listed among the upāgamas of the Vātulāgama. Such upāgamas were held to expound the parent scripture more fully and to add new material; they survive chiefly in South Indian manuscript traditions.

SA_MB_somasambhupaddhativyakhyaHK.title: Somaśambhupaddhativyākhyā
SA_MB_somasambhupaddhativyakhyaHK.author: uncertain (commentary on the Somaśambhupaddhati of Somaśambhu)
SA_MB_somasambhupaddhativyakhyaHK.date_estimate: commentary post-1100 CE; base text Somaśambhupaddhati 1048/49 CE
SA_MB_somasambhupaddhativyakhyaHK.summary: This is a commentary (vyākhyā) on the Somaśambhupaddhati (also called Kriyākāṇḍakramāvalī), the influential eleventh-century Śaiva Siddhānta manual of ritual composed by Somaśambhu, abbot of the Golakī monastery. The parent paddhati systematizes initiation (dīkṣā), consecration, daily and occasional rites, funerary ritual, and expiations; the vyākhyā explains its procedures and terminology.
SA_MB_somasambhupaddhativyakhyaHK.history: The Somaśambhupaddhati (1048/49 CE) was one of the most authoritative Saiddhāntika ritual digests and was revised and commented on by later Southern authors (e.g. in the lineage of Trilocanaśiva and others) in the eleventh-twelfth centuries; it was studied and translated by Hélène Brunner. The precise author of this particular vyākhyā is not securely identified here.

SA_MB_tripuraarahasyamHK.title: Tripurārahasya, Māhātmyakhaṇḍa
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyamHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyamHK.genre: purāṇa-style māhātmya (narrative glorification) within a Tantric framework
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyamHK.summary: The Māhātmyakhaṇḍa ('Section on Glorification') is one of the two extant sections of the Tripurārahasya, a Śākta work affiliated with the cult of the goddess Tripurā (later Śrīvidyā). This section narrates, in purāṇic style, the deeds and greatness of Tripurā and the goddesses regarded as her manifestations, embedding Tantric theology within mythological narrative.
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyamHK.history: The Tripurārahasya survives in a Māhātmyakhaṇḍa and a Jñānakhaṇḍa (a third Caryākhaṇḍa is lost). On the evidence of its mythic origin-story and the sources it cites, it is dated relatively to the 13th-16th centuries and located in South India. It is a key text of the Tripurā/Śrīvidyā Śākta tradition.

SA_MB_vaarunapaddhatiHK.title: Vāruṇapaddhati
SA_MB_vaarunapaddhatiHK.author: Varuṇaśivācārya
SA_MB_vaarunapaddhatiHK.date_estimate: medieval; based on classical Siddhāntāgamas (compiled before the 16th c.)
SA_MB_vaarunapaddhatiHK.summary: The Vāruṇapaddhati (also called Ātmārthapūjāpaddhati) is a detailed yet compact Śaiva Siddhānta manual on personal ('ātmārtha') worship and ritual practice. Varuṇaśivācārya compiled it from the major Śaivasiddhāntāgamas, organizing initiation, daily worship, and liturgical procedure into a systematic guide for practitioners.
SA_MB_vaarunapaddhatiHK.history: Varuṇaśiva opens the work by invoking many of the great Siddhāntācāryas and draws extensively on their writings, making the paddhati an important witness to the Saiddhāntika ritual tradition. The text was edited and published in the latter half of the twentieth century.

SA_MB_sivajnaanasiddhiHK.title: Śivajñānasiddhi
SA_MB_sivajnaanasiddhiHK.author: uncertain (a Śivajñānasiddhi is associated with Arulnandi Śivācārya; Tamil tradition)
SA_MB_sivajnaanasiddhiHK.date_estimate: uncertain; Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta canon c. 13th-14th c. CE (if = Śivajñānasiddhiyār)
SA_MB_sivajnaanasiddhiHK.summary: Śivajñānasiddhi ('Establishment of the Knowledge of Śiva') is the title of a doctrinal work of Śaiva Siddhānta expounding the system's core metaphysics: the three principles pati (Lord), paśu (bound souls) and pāśa (bondage), and the path to liberation through Śiva's grace. It defends the Saiddhāntika position against rival schools.
SA_MB_sivajnaanasiddhiHK.history: The name corresponds to the Tamil Śivajñānasiddhiyār of Aruḷnandi Śivācārya, one of the fourteen canonical Meykaṇṭa Śāstras of Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta, which seconds and elaborates Meykaṇṭār's Śivajñānabodham. As a Sanskrit title the precise text and author cannot be securely fixed here.

SA_MB_sarvajnaanottaravrittiHK.title: Sarvajñānottaravṛtti
SA_MB_sarvajnaanottaravrittiHK.author: Aghoraśivācārya
SA_MB_sarvajnaanottaravrittiHK.date_estimate: 12th c. CE (commentary); base Sarvajñānottaratantra c. 7th c. CE
SA_MB_sarvajnaanottaravrittiHK.summary: The Sarvajñānottaravṛtti is Aghoraśivācārya's commentary on the Sarvajñānottaratantra, an early Saiddhāntika Śaiva scripture especially valued for its sections on yoga and Śaiva doctrine. The commentary expounds the tantra's teaching on the soul, bondage, knowledge, and yogic practice within the Śaiva Siddhānta framework.
SA_MB_sarvajnaanottaravrittiHK.history: Aghoraśivācārya (mid-12th c., Tamil country) was the foremost systematizer of South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta and a prolific commentator. The Sarvajñānottara, a 7th-century scripture, later acquired nondualist readings and became a proof text for Saiddhāntika theologians; Aghoraśiva's vṛtti survives in manuscript (e.g. at the IFP, Pondicherry) and covers the Yogaprakaraṇa among other sections.
related_works: other works of Aghoraśivācārya (e.g. Mṛgendrapaddhati, Tattvaprakāśavṛtti)

SA_MB_saivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.title: Śaivāgamādimāhātmya (Śivāgamādimāhātmyasaṃgraha)
SA_MB_saivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.author: Jñānaprakāśa of Śālivāṭi (Jaffna)
SA_MB_saivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.genre: doctrinal treatise / compilation (saṃgraha) glorifying the Śaiva Āgamas
SA_MB_saivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.summary: The Śivāgamādimāhātmyasaṃgraha is a treatise that establishes the supreme authority and validity of the Śaiva Āgamas and the doctrines of Śaiva Siddhānta. It compiles passages glorifying Śaiva scripture and supplements them with the author's own explanatory comments, defending the primacy of Śaiva revelation.
SA_MB_saivaagamaadimaahaatmyamHK.history: Its author Śālivāṭi Jñānaprakāśa was a prolific Śaiva Siddhānta ācārya from Tirunelveli (Śālivāṭi) in Jaffna, Sri Lanka, active in the late sixteenth to early seventeenth century; he is also known for commentaries on the Pauṣkarāgama. The work reflects the late-Saiddhāntika effort to assert Śaiva scriptural supremacy.

SA_MB_mantrakaumudiiHK.title: Mantrakaumudī
SA_MB_mantrakaumudiiHK.author: Devanātha Ṭhakkura (Tarkapañcānana)
SA_MB_mantrakaumudiiHK.date_estimate: uncertain; Maithila author, likely c. 16th-17th c. CE
SA_MB_mantrakaumudiiHK.summary: The Mantrakaumudī ('Moonlight of Mantras') is a digest on mantraśāstra and Tantric ritual practice compiled by the Maithila scholar Devanātha Ṭhakkura. It systematizes the use of mantras, deity worship, and associated ritual procedures for practitioners in the Mithila Tantric milieu.
SA_MB_mantrakaumudiiHK.history: Devanātha Ṭhakkura, also styled Tarkapañcānana, was a Maithila author credited with both the Mantrakaumudī and a companion Tantrakaumudī. The works belong to the rich tradition of Maithila Tantric and ritual digests (nibandhas); the Tantrakaumudī has been printed in the Internet Archive collections.

SA_MB_kaulaavaliinirnayaHK.title: Kaulāvalīnirṇaya
SA_MB_kaulaavaliinirnayaHK.author: Jñānānanda Paramahaṃsa (Jñānānandagiri)
SA_MB_kaulaavaliinirnayaHK.summary: The Kaulāvalīnirṇaya ('Determination of the Sequence/Series of Kaula [Rites]') is a compendium of Kaula Tantric doctrine and ritual practice, treating the worship, mantras, and esoteric observances of the Kaula left-hand (vāmācāra) Śākta tradition. It serves as a practical and doctrinal handbook for Kaula initiates.
SA_MB_kaulaavaliinirnayaHK.history: Its author Jñānānanda Paramahaṃsa is known essentially only from this work; the text was edited and published by Arthur Avalon (Sir John Woodroffe) in 1928 within his Tantric Texts series. It is a frequently cited authority on Kaula ritual but its precise date of composition is undetermined.

SA_MB_kriyaasaaratattvaprakaasiniivyaakh.title: Kriyāsāratattvaprakāśinīvyākhyā
SA_MB_kriyaasaaratattvaprakaasiniivyaakh.author: uncertain (a commentary on the Kriyāsāra of Nīlakaṇṭhaśivācārya)
SA_MB_kriyaasaaratattvaprakaasiniivyaakh.date_estimate: commentary later than base text; Kriyāsāra c. 1350-1450 CE
SA_MB_kriyaasaaratattvaprakaasiniivyaakh.summary: This is a commentary (the 'Tattvaprakāśinī vyākhyā') on the Kriyāsāra ('Essence of Ritual Action'), a venerated Vīraśaiva work by Nīlakaṇṭhaśivācārya treating Śaiva ritual, worship of the Śivaliṅga set in a maṇḍala, and yogic/devotional practice. The commentary elucidates the ritual prescriptions and doctrine of the base text.
SA_MB_kriyaasaaratattvaprakaasiniivyaakh.history: The Kriyāsāra of the 14th-15th-century Vīraśaiva leader Nīlakaṇṭhaśivācārya was an influential ritual authority, cited from the late 15th century (e.g. by Rāghavabhaṭṭa, 1493 CE) onward into the 19th century, and contains some of the earliest evidence for Sūrya/Namaskāra sequencing. The specific commentator and date of this 'Tattvaprakāśinī' vyākhyā are not securely identified here.

SA_MB_iisaanasivagurudevapaddhati4HK.title: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 4
SA_MB_iisaanasivagurudevapaddhati4HK.author: Īśānaśiva Gurudeva (Īśānaśivagurudevamiśra)
SA_MB_iisaanasivagurudevapaddhati4HK.tradition: Hindu, Śaiva (Śaiva Siddhānta with Kerala-Tantra syncretism; also called Tantrapaddhati)
SA_MB_iisaanasivagurudevapaddhati4HK.date_estimate: c. 11th-12th c. CE (some scholars to 13th c.)
SA_MB_iisaanasivagurudevapaddhati4HK.summary: A large and encyclopedic Śaiva ritual compendium (paddhati) from medieval Kerala, organized into four sections (Sāmānyapāda, Mantrapāda, Kriyāpāda, Yogapāda). Volume 4 corresponds chiefly to the later portions (Yogapāda and associated material), treating yoga, meditation, and ancillary ritual and esoteric practice. The work describes Śaiva initiation (dīkṣā) in detail while also covering the worship of numerous Śaiva and Vaiṣṇava deities, mantras, yantras, and temple/iconographic procedures.
SA_MB_iisaanasivagurudevapaddhati4HK.history: Attributed to Īśānaśiva Gurudeva and edited in four volumes by T. Ganapati Sastri in the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series. Though it styles itself a work of the Śaivasiddhānta, it reflects the syncretistic 'Kerala Tantra' tradition and is a major source for the history of South Indian, especially Keralan, ritual.

SA_MB_diiksaadarsaHK.title: Dīkṣādarśa
SA_MB_diiksaadarsaHK.author: Vedajñāna (II) / Nigamajñānaguru of Cidambaram
SA_MB_diiksaadarsaHK.summary: A comprehensive Śaiva Siddhānta manual ('Mirror of Initiation') systematically setting out the rites of dīkṣā (initiation) together with related ritual procedures. It draws on and synthesizes the Saiddhāntika āgamas and earlier paddhati literature to present an authoritative ordering of initiatory practice.
SA_MB_diiksaadarsaHK.history: One of the influential ritual works of Vedajñāna II (also called Nigamajñāna II / Vedajñānaguru), a learned 16th-century Śaivasiddhānta teacher associated with Cidambaram in Tamil Nadu, who composed several paddhatis and doctrinal digests. His works became standard references and are frequently quoted in later 17th-century manuals.

SA_MB_kaalahastiisavilaasaHK.title: Kālahastīśavilāsa (Kālahastīśvaravilāsa)
SA_MB_kaalahastiisavilaasaHK.author: uncertain (attributed to a South Indian, Vijayanagara/Nāyaka-era court poet)
SA_MB_kaalahastiisavilaasaHK.genre: kāvya / campū (devotional court poem on a Śaiva shrine)
SA_MB_kaalahastiisavilaasaHK.summary: An ornate Sanskrit poem celebrating Śiva as Kālahastīśvara, lord of the famous Śaiva pilgrimage temple at Kālahasti (Śrīkālahasti, Andhra Pradesh), a vāyu-liṅga site. It glorifies the deity, the sanctity of the place, and the 'play/sport' (vilāsa) of the lord, in the panegyric māhātmya-kāvya manner.
SA_MB_kaalahastiisavilaasaHK.history: The temple at Kālahasti inspired several literary works (notably Dhūrjaṭi's Telugu Kālahastīśvara Śatakamu and Māhātmyamu in the 16th c.). The Sanskrit Kālahastīśvaravilāsa is less securely attributed; its precise authorship and date could not be confidently established from available sources.

SA_MB_karpuuraadistotramHK.title: Karpūrādistotra (Karpūrādi-stotra)
SA_MB_karpuuraadistotramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the Mahānirvāṇa/Śākta tantric tradition)
SA_MB_karpuuraadistotramHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval, latter half of the 1st millennium CE or later
SA_MB_karpuuraadistotramHK.summary: A short tantric hymn of about twenty-two verses praising the goddess Kālī (Dakṣiṇakālikā). It encodes the goddess's mantras (mantroddhāra), her visualization (dhyāna), yantra, mode of worship (sādhanā), and description of her form (svarūpa-varṇanā), and is noted for its bold, 'scandalous' tantric imagery.
SA_MB_karpuuraadistotramHK.history: Famous through Arthur Avalon's (Sir John Woodroffe's) 1922 edition and translation, which printed Vimalānanda-svāmī's esoteric commentary (Svarūpavyākhyā / Vimalānandadāyinī). The verse-text circulates within Śākta tantric anthologies such as the Śāktapramoda and Tantrasāra.

SA_MB_aasaucadiipikaaHK.title: Āśaucadīpikā
SA_MB_aasaucadiipikaaHK.author: Vedajñāna (II) / Nigamajñānaguru of Cidambaram
SA_MB_aasaucadiipikaaHK.summary: A treatise ('Lamp on Impurity') dealing with āśauca, the ritual impurity (notably sūtaka, the pollution caused by birth and death) and the rules and expiations governing it within the Śaivasiddhānta ritual framework. It clarifies how such states of impurity affect the initiate's eligibility for worship and the procedures for their removal.
SA_MB_aasaucadiipikaaHK.history: A work of Vedajñāna II, the 16th-century Śaivasiddhānta teacher of Cidambaram who authored a cluster of ritual and doctrinal manuals. Such impurity-rules were of central concern in dualist Śaiva Siddhānta, where ritual offences include states of pollution caused by life-events.

SA_MB_sriitatvanidhiHK.title: Śrītattvanidhi
SA_MB_sriitatvanidhiHK.author: Kṛṣṇarāja Wodeyar III (Mummaḍi Kṛṣṇarāja Woḍeyar) of Mysore
SA_MB_sriitatvanidhiHK.tradition: Hindu (compendium spanning Śaiva, Vaiṣṇava, Śākta and other traditions)
SA_MB_sriitatvanidhiHK.summary: An illustrated compendium ('Illustrious Treasure of Realities') on the iconography and iconometry of divine figures of South India, compiled and patronized by the Maharaja of Mysore. Arranged in nine 'nidhis' (treasures), it catalogues deities with their visual descriptions, mantras, and dhyāna; one celebrated section depicts 122 yoga postures (āsanas), the most elaborate pre-twentieth-century treatment of āsana.
SA_MB_sriitatvanidhiHK.history: Attributed to and produced under Kṛṣṇarāja Wodeyar III (1794-1868), a major patron of arts and learning who had the treatise written and illustrated by palace miniaturists. It is an important nineteenth-century source for South Indian iconography and for the history of yoga.

SA_MB_kaamaratnatantraHK.title: Kāmaratnatantra
SA_MB_kaamaratnatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaamaratnatantraHK.tradition: Hindu, tantric (Śākta/folk tantra, associated with Assam/Kāmarūpa)
SA_MB_kaamaratnatantraHK.genre: tantra (manual of magical and ritual practices)
SA_MB_kaamaratnatantraHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely later medieval (2nd millennium CE)
SA_MB_kaamaratnatantraHK.summary: A tantric manual dealing largely with practical/applied magic — mantras, the six 'acts' (ṣaṭkarma: śānti, vaśīkaraṇa, stambhana, etc.), charms, healing, and apotropaic and erotic-magical rites. It belongs to the broad current of mantraśāstra associated with the Kāmarūpa (Assam) tantric milieu.
SA_MB_kaamaratnatantraHK.history: Circulates as a popular tantra in the Kāmarūpa tradition; its authorship is anonymous and its date insecure. It is representative of the 'folk tantra' literature of eastern India rather than a major scriptural āgama.

SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaaT1034HK.title: Varuṇapaddhativyākhyā
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaaT1034HK.author: commentary by Nigamajñānadeva (Nigamajñāna II); base text Varuṇapaddhati ascribed to a Varuṇaśiva/Varuṇa
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaaT1034HK.genre: bhāṣya/vyākhyā (commentary) on a Śaiva ritual paddhati
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaaT1034HK.date_estimate: commentary 16th c. CE; base text earlier (medieval)
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaaT1034HK.summary: A commentary (vyākhyā), titled Vilocana, on the Varuṇapaddhati, a Śaivasiddhānta ritual manual. The commentary explains in detail the concepts and types of initiation (dīkṣā) and the installation (pratiṣṭhā) of the śivaliṅga in temples, topics treated only briefly in the root Varuṇapaddhati.
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaaT1034HK.history: Preserved in the manuscript collections of the French Institute of Pondicherry (e.g. IFP MS 30480) and transcribed within the Muktabodha–IFP Śaiva Siddhānta e-text project; the commentary Vilocana is attributed to Nigamajñānadeva, a 16th-century Tamil Śaivasiddhānta scholar of the Vedajñāna/Nigamajñāna lineage.

SA_MB_hathayogapradiipikaaHK.title: Haṭhayogapradīpikā
SA_MB_hathayogapradiipikaaHK.author: Svātmārāma
SA_MB_hathayogapradiipikaaHK.date_estimate: first half of the 15th c. CE (earliest manuscript 1496)
SA_MB_hathayogapradiipikaaHK.summary: The classic Sanskrit manual ('Light on Haṭha Yoga') compiling the techniques of haṭhayoga: āsana (postures), prāṇāyāma, mudrās and bandhas, ṣaṭkarma (cleansing practices), the rousing of kuṇḍalinī, and nāda/laya leading to samādhi. It presents haṭha yoga as a means to and support for rāja yoga.
SA_MB_hathayogapradiipikaaHK.history: Composed by Svātmārāma, who traces the teaching lineage to Matsyendranātha and the Nātha tradition and draws together earlier haṭha texts. It is one of the three classical haṭha-yoga manuals (with the Gheraṇḍasaṃhitā and Śivasaṃhitā) and the most influential, attracting commentaries such as Brahmānanda's Jyotsnā.

SA_MB_gitarthasamgrahaHK.title: Bhagavadgītā
SA_MB_gitarthasamgrahaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa; embedded in the Mahābhārata)
SA_MB_gitarthasamgrahaHK.tradition: Hindu (synthesizing Vedānta, Sāṃkhya, Yoga and bhakti)
SA_MB_gitarthasamgrahaHK.summary: A dialogue of 700 verses in which Kṛṣṇa instructs the warrior Arjuna on the battlefield of Kurukṣetra, expounding the paths of disinterested action (karma-yoga), knowledge (jñāna-yoga), and devotion (bhakti-yoga), the nature of the self, dharma, and the divine. It harmonizes diverse strands of Indian thought into a foundational scripture.
SA_MB_gitarthasamgrahaHK.history: Forms part of the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata and is traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. It became the most commented-upon Hindu text, with celebrated bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva, Abhinavagupta and many others, and remains central to Hindu religious and philosophical life.

SA_MB_diiksaapaddhatiHK.title: Dīkṣāpaddhati
SA_MB_diiksaapaddhatiHK.author: anonymous/traditional (various Śaiva ritualists)
SA_MB_diiksaapaddhatiHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (title used for several distinct works)
SA_MB_diiksaapaddhatiHK.summary: A ritual manual setting out the procedure of Śaiva initiation (dīkṣā): the construction of the maṇḍala, invocation, the rites by which the guru purifies and liberates the disciple's soul, and the differing grades of initiation. 'Dīkṣāpaddhati' is a generic title borne by several distinct Śaivasiddhānta manuals.
SA_MB_diiksaapaddhatiHK.history: Numerous paddhatis bearing this name survive in South Indian (especially IFP/Pondicherry) manuscript collections, generally derived from the Saiddhāntika āgamas and earlier digests such as the Somaśambhupaddhati. The specific recension intended here cannot be pinned to a single named author.

SA_MB_agnikaaryapaddhatiHK..title: Agnikāryapaddhati
SA_MB_agnikaaryapaddhatiHK..author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_agnikaaryapaddhatiHK..summary: A manual prescribing the performance of the agnikārya (fire ritual / homa) within Śaiva worship: the kindling and consecration of the sacred fire, the placement of the kuṇḍa, oblations, accompanying mantras, and the integration of the fire-rite into daily and occasional Śaiva ceremony.
SA_MB_agnikaaryapaddhatiHK..history: A generic ritual-manual title found among South Indian Śaiva (and brahmanical) manuscript traditions; preserved in paddhati collections rather than as a single attributable scripture. Its exact recension and author are not identifiable from available sources.

SA_MB_uddharakosaHK.title: Uddhārakośa
SA_MB_uddharakosaHK.author: ascribed to Dakṣiṇāmūrti
SA_MB_uddharakosaHK.genre: kośa (lexicon of the tantric syllabic code)
SA_MB_uddharakosaHK.summary: A tantric dictionary (also Mantroddhārakośa) giving the key to the secret/coded names of the bīja syllables and monosyllabic mantras used in tantric mantroddhāra ('extraction of mantras'). It enables practitioners to decode the cryptic syllabic terminology pervading tantric scriptures.
SA_MB_uddharakosaHK.history: Attributed to Dakṣiṇāmūrti and edited (with introduction and notes) notably by Raghu Vira (1938, Lahore). It was hailed as a rare aid for penetrating the deliberately obscure 'coded' language of the tantras.

SA_MB_kalottaragamaHK.title: Kālottara
SA_MB_kalottaragamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_kalottaragamaHK.date_estimate: c. 6th-9th c. CE (core layers early; multiple recensions)
SA_MB_kalottaragamaHK.summary: An important and influential Śaivasiddhānta scripture (more fully Kālottarāgama / Kālottaratantra) that circulated in many recensions of varying length, named by verse-count (e.g. Sārdhatriśatikā, Śatika, Trayodaśaśatika, and the very large ṣaṭsahasra). It treats Śaiva doctrine, mantras, yoga, initiation and ritual in compact, widely cited form.
SA_MB_kalottaragamaHK.history: One of the four āgamas (with Mataṅgapārameśvara, Mṛgendra, and Sarvajñānottara) on which the Śaiva Siddhānta especially relies, frequently quoted by Saiddhāntika authors. Its shorter recensions are among the earliest datable Mantramārga sources; the larger recensions are later expansions.

SA_MB_satsahasrakalottaragamaHK.title: Ṣaṭsahasrakālottarāgama (Ṣaṭsāhasra Kālottara)
SA_MB_satsahasrakalottaragamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_satsahasrakalottaragamaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; the longest recension is comparatively late (likely later 1st millennium - early 2nd millennium CE)
SA_MB_satsahasrakalottaragamaHK.summary: The 'six-thousand-verse' recension of the Kālottara, the most extensive form of that scriptural cycle. Like the other Kālottara recensions it expounds Śaivasiddhānta cosmology, mantra, yoga, dīkṣā and ritual, but at much greater length, incorporating expanded ritual and doctrinal material.
SA_MB_satsahasrakalottaragamaHK.history: The Kālottara was transmitted in numerous recensions distinguished by verse-count; the ṣaṭsahasra ('6000') version is the largest and represents an expanded, later stratum of the textual tradition. It survives mainly in South Indian āgama manuscript collections.

SA_MB_yogajagamaHK.title: Yogajāgama
SA_MB_yogajagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_yogajagamaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (one of the 28 Saiddhāntika āgamas)
SA_MB_yogajagamaHK.summary: One of the twenty-eight mūlāgamas of the Śaiva Siddhānta, traditionally revealed by the Sadyojāta face of Sadāśiva. As an āgama it would cover the four 'feet' (caryā, kriyā, yoga, jñāna) of Śaiva practice. Notably, the surviving manuscripts titled Yogajāgama are in fact compilations of ritual statements drawn from several scriptures (Yogaja, Suprabheda, Ajita, Mataṅga, etc.) and paddhatis (Somaśambhupaddhati, Siddhāntasārāvalī).
SA_MB_yogajagamaHK.history: Listed among the 28 Siddhāntāgamas with its upāgamas (Tāra, Vīṇāśikhottara, Santa, Santati, Ātmayoga). Manuscripts (e.g. an Āgamavacanasaṅgraha at the IFP) show that, despite the scriptural title, the extant text functions as a ritual anthology rather than an intact early scripture.

SA_MB_nisvasakarikatantramHK.title: Niśvāsakārikā
SA_MB_nisvasakarikatantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_nisvasakarikatantramHK.date_estimate: later layer of the Niśvāsa corpus, c. 7th-9th c. CE (post-dating the early core, c. 450-550 CE)
SA_MB_nisvasakarikatantramHK.summary: A constituent text of the Niśvāsa corpus, the large concluding 'kārikā' portion that expands on doctrine, ritual, mantra and ancillary topics. The Niśvāsa as a whole is recognized as probably the oldest surviving scripture of the Śaiva Mantramārga, but the Niśvāsakārikā represents a later accretion to that corpus.
SA_MB_nisvasakarikatantramHK.history: The Niśvāsatattvasaṃhitā survives in a well-preserved ninth-century Nepalese palm-leaf manuscript and comprises the Niśvāsamukha, Mūlasūtra, Uttarasūtra, Nayasūtra, Guhyasūtra and the much longer Niśvāsakārikā. The corpus has been critically edited under Dominic Goodall with Alexis Sanderson and Harunaga Isaacson.

SA_MB_lalitaagamaHK.title: Lalitāgama
SA_MB_lalitaagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_lalitaagamaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (one of the 28 Saiddhāntika āgamas)
SA_MB_lalitaagamaHK.summary: One of the twenty-eight mūlāgamas of the Śaiva Siddhānta, traditionally revealed by the Īśāna face of Sadāśiva. As a Saiddhāntika scripture it concerns Śaiva doctrine, mantra, temple ritual and worship, with its teachings elaborated in its upāgamas (Lalita, Lalitottara, Kaumāra).
SA_MB_lalitaagamaHK.history: Figures in the canonical list of 28 āgamas and survives chiefly in South Indian (Pondicherry/IFP) Śaiva manuscript collections rather than in widely available critical editions. Details of its specific contents and date are not well documented in accessible sources.

SA_MB_viiraagamaHK.title: Vīrāgama
SA_MB_viiraagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_viiraagamaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (one of the 28 Saiddhāntika āgamas)
SA_MB_viiraagamaHK.summary: One of the twenty-eight mūlāgamas of the Śaiva Siddhānta, traditionally revealed by the Aghora face of Sadāśiva (alongside Vijaya, Niśvāsa, Svayambhuva and Anala). As a Saiddhāntika scripture it treats Śaiva doctrine, mantra, initiation, temple worship and iconography across the four pādas.
SA_MB_viiraagamaHK.history: Listed in the standard enumeration of the 28 āgamas and preserved within South Indian Śaiva manuscript collections; like several of the mūlāgamas it is not fully extant in critically edited form, and its precise date is undetermined.

SA_MB_saivasiddhantaparibhasamanjariHK.title: Śaivasiddhāntaparibhāṣāmañjarī
SA_MB_saivasiddhantaparibhasamanjariHK.author: Vedajñāna (II) / Nigamajñānaguru of Cidambaram
SA_MB_saivasiddhantaparibhasamanjariHK.genre: philosophical/doctrinal digest (paribhāṣā – exposition of technical terminology)
SA_MB_saivasiddhantaparibhasamanjariHK.summary: A doctrinal manual ('Bouquet of the Technical Terminology of the Śaiva Siddhānta') that systematically defines and expounds the key concepts and technical terms of the Śaivasiddhānta system — pati (Lord), paśu (bound soul), pāśa (bonds), the malas, tattvas, dīkṣā, mukti and related doctrines. It serves as a concise reference digest of Saiddhāntika philosophy.
SA_MB_saivasiddhantaparibhasamanjariHK.history: Composed by Vedajñāna II, the influential 16th-century Śaivasiddhānta teacher of Cidambaram who produced a coordinated set of doctrinal and ritual works; his digests became standard scholastic references and are quoted in later 17th-century Śaiva manuals.

SA_MB_mrgendravrttidiipikaHK.title: Aghoraśivācārya: Mṛgendravṛttidīpikā
SA_MB_mrgendravrttidiipikaHK.author: Aghoraśivācārya (Aghoraśiva)
SA_MB_mrgendravrttidiipikaHK.date_estimate: c. 12th c. CE (Aghoraśiva fl. 1157 CE, Chidambaram)
SA_MB_mrgendravrttidiipikaHK.summary: A sub-commentary (dīpikā, 'lamp') by Aghoraśivācārya on the Vṛtti of Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha on the Mṛgendra(tantra/āgama), an authoritative scripture of the dualist Śaiva Siddhānta. It elucidates the Āgama's vidyāpāda (doctrine) and other sections, treating Śaiva metaphysics, the categories of pati (Śiva), paśu (bound souls) and pāśa (bonds), and ritual.
SA_MB_mrgendravrttidiipikaHK.history: Aghoraśiva was a leading 12th-century Śaiva Siddhānta theologian of the Amardaka lineage based at Chidambaram in the Tamil south; he authored a series of influential commentaries (including the aṣṭaprakaraṇa exegesis) that reshaped Saiddhāntika doctrine, notably reclassifying the higher tattvas as effects within the category of bonds. The Mṛgendravṛttidīpikā glosses the earlier Kashmirian Vṛtti of Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha and became a standard study aid for the Mṛgendra in the southern Siddhānta school.

SA_MB_prodgitagamaHK.title: Prodgītāgama (Prodgīta)
SA_MB_prodgitagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_prodgitagamaHK.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (undated; among the canonical 28 Śaiva Āgamas, formative c. 6th-10th c. CE)
SA_MB_prodgitagamaHK.summary: Prodgīta (also Prodgītam/Udgīta) is counted as one of the twenty-eight Mūlāgamas of the Śaiva Siddhānta canon, traditionally revealed by the Īśāna face of Śiva and taught to the sage Agastya. Like other Śaiva Āgamas it would cover the four pādas (jñāna/doctrine, yoga, kriyā/ritual, caryā/conduct) governing Śiva worship, temple ritual, and liberation, though it survives only fragmentarily.
SA_MB_prodgitagamaHK.history: It belongs to the standard enumeration of the 28 Siddhānta Āgamas (typically grouped as 10 Śivabheda and 18 Rudrabheda); the Prodgīta is generally listed among the Rudrabheda group revealed in the final cluster with Lalita, Siddha, Santāna, Kiraṇa, Vātula and others. Most of these Āgamas are lost or partially preserved in southern manuscript collections and ritual practice.

SA_MB_siddhalaksmipujapaddhatiHK.title: Siddhalakṣmīpūjāpaddhati
SA_MB_siddhalaksmipujapaddhatiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_siddhalaksmipujapaddhatiHK.tradition: Hindu, Śākta tantric (Śrīkula / Sarvāmnāya; strongly attested in Newar/Nepalese tradition)
SA_MB_siddhalaksmipujapaddhatiHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval to early-modern (c. 14th-18th c. CE, in the Nepalese Malla-period Siddhilakṣmī cult)
SA_MB_siddhalaksmipujapaddhatiHK.summary: A paddhati (procedural ritual manual) prescribing the pūjā (worship), mantras, nyāsa and visualization for the tantric goddess Siddhalakṣmī (Siddhilakṣmī). Siddhilakṣmī is an esoteric fierce form of the Goddess connected with the Krama and Kālī traditions and worshipped especially in the Newar Śrīkula of the Kathmandu Valley, where she received the highly secret ritual associated with Malla royal patronage.
SA_MB_siddhalaksmipujapaddhatiHK.history: Siddhilakṣmī (Purṇacaṇḍī/Pratyaṅgirā-related) was a primary tantric deity of the Malla kings of Nepal (e.g. the Nyatapola temple, Bhaktapur), and her cult preserves esoteric Sarvāmnāya material linking her to Kubjikā and Kālī. Such pūjāpaddhatis circulated as practical manuals within initiatory lineages; the specific manuscript here is not individually well documented.

SA_MB_timirodghataHK.title: Timirodghāṭa(tantra)
SA_MB_timirodghataHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_timirodghataHK.date_estimate: before c. 1000-1050 CE (quoted by Kṣemarāja, so pre-11th c.)
SA_MB_timirodghataHK.summary: A Śaiva tantric treatise in verse whose title means 'the dispelling of darkness' (timira 'darkness' + udghāṭa 'removal'). It is known mainly through quotation rather than as a complete surviving text; it appears to belong to the early Śaiva tantric/Bhairava-stream scriptures.
SA_MB_timirodghataHK.history: The work is cited by the great Kashmirian exegete Kṣemarāja (early 11th c.), which establishes it as an earlier Śaiva tantra, and it is registered in Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum. No author, full text, or precise date is securely established.
related_works: early Śaiva / Bhairava tantras quoted by Kṣemarāja

SA_MB_pauskarasamhitaHK.title: Pauṣkarasaṃhitā
SA_MB_pauskarasamhitaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed Pāñcarātra scripture)
SA_MB_pauskarasamhitaHK.date_estimate: disputed; early strata possibly c. 3rd-5th c. CE, with later redaction
