SA_MB_pauskarasamhitaHK.summary: One of the most revered Pāñcarātra Āgama saṃhitās, counted with the Sāttvata and Jayākhya as the 'Three Gems' (ratnatraya) of the canon. Its roughly 6,500 verses focus heavily on maṇḍala worship, temple architecture, image consecration (pratiṣṭhā) and iconography, framing detailed maṇḍala construction as the core of initiatory worship leading to liberation.
SA_MB_pauskarasamhitaHK.history: Traditionally one of the oldest and most authoritative Pāñcarātra texts, it is foundational for Vaiṣṇava temple ritual and theology; its dating is debated, with the earliest layers sometimes placed in the mid-first millennium CE. It has been edited and translated (e.g. the South Indian recension) and remains central to Śrīvaiṣṇava and Pāñcarātra ritual scholarship.

SA_MB_stutikusumaanjaliHK.title: Jagaddhara Bhaṭṭa: Stutikusumāñjali
SA_MB_stutikusumaanjaliHK.author: Jagaddhara Bhaṭṭa
SA_MB_stutikusumaanjaliHK.summary: The 'Flower-offering of Praise', a celebrated collection of Śaiva devotional hymns comprising about 1,439 verses arranged in 38-39 stotras dedicated chiefly to Śiva. Highly wrought as literary poetry, it deploys alliteration, complex puns (śleṣa) and varied metres, blending praise, petition and benediction so that prayer itself becomes ornate kāvya.
SA_MB_stutikusumaanjaliHK.history: Jagaddhara Bhaṭṭa was a 14th-century Kashmiri poet; his Stutikusumāñjali is a key monument of the Kashmiri Sanskrit stotra tradition and has attracted commentaries and modern scholarly study (e.g. Hamsa Stainton's 'Poetry as Prayer in the Sanskrit Hymns of Kashmir'). It exemplifies the fusion of high court poetry with bhakti in late-medieval Kashmir.
related_works: Kashmiri Śaiva stotra tradition (e.g. Utpaladeva's Śivastotrāvalī)

SA_MB_satcakranirnayaHK.title: Ṣaṭcakranirṇaya (Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa)
SA_MB_satcakranirnayaHK.author: Pūrṇānanda (if identified with the Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa)
SA_MB_satcakranirnayaHK.date_estimate: c. 1577 CE (if = Pūrṇānanda's Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa, 16th c. Bengal)
SA_MB_satcakranirnayaHK.summary: A treatise describing the six bodily cakras (mūlādhāra, svādhiṣṭhāna, maṇipūra, anāhata, viśuddha, ājñā) and the rise of kuṇḍalinī through the suṣumnā to union with Śiva. The title 'determination/description of the six cakras' indicates the classic subtle-body physiology of tantric yoga; it is very likely the same work as, or closely modelled on, Pūrṇānanda's famous Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa.
SA_MB_satcakranirnayaHK.history: The standard Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa forms the sixth chapter of Pūrṇānanda's Śrītattvacintāmaṇi (Bengal, 1577 CE) and became internationally known through Arthur Avalon's (Sir John Woodroffe) translation 'The Serpent Power' (1919). The slight variant in the title here ('nirṇaya' rather than 'nirūpaṇa') leaves some uncertainty about whether a distinct work is meant.

SA_MB_brahmasandhanaHK.title: Brahmasandhāna
SA_MB_brahmasandhanaHK.summary: The title means 'joining/uniting with brahman' (brahma + sandhāna, 'fixing, conjunction, contemplation'). It most plausibly designates a short ritual or contemplative text concerning the uniting of the worshipper or installed object with brahman/the supreme reality (a sandhāna being a recognized ritual act, e.g. of joining the soul with the divine in tantric and āgamic worship). The specific work could not be securely identified.
SA_MB_brahmasandhanaHK.history: No author, date, or definite textual identity could be confirmed from available sources; brahmasandhāna also occurs as a ritual term within larger paddhati/āgama literature, so this entry may name a section or minor manual rather than an independent classic.

SA_MB_candrajnanaHK.title: Candrajñānāgama (Candrajñāna)
SA_MB_candrajnanaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_candrajnanaHK.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (undated; canonical Śaiva Siddhānta Āgama, formative c. 7th-10th c. CE)
SA_MB_candrajnanaHK.summary: A Siddhānta Śaiva Āgama (also classed as an Upāgama) styled a pratiṣṭhātantra, i.e. a scripture dealing primarily with the installation (pratiṣṭhā) and worship of liṅgas and other representations of Śaiva deities, temple ritual, and related kriyā. Its name ('moon-knowledge') links it to the lunar/calming aspect of Śiva worship.
SA_MB_candrajnanaHK.history: Candrajñāna is enumerated among the 28 Śaiva Āgamas, traditionally proclaimed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva and transmitted through Ananta/Bṛhaspati, and is said to have many Upāgamas. It survives chiefly in southern manuscript and ritual traditions and is cited in studies of Śaiva temple installation ritual.

SA_MB_hamsasaraHK.title: Haṃsasāra
SA_MB_hamsasaraHK.summary: The title means 'the essence (sāra) of the haṃsa', the haṃsa ('so'ham') being the spontaneous breath-mantra central to yoga and tantra, where Ha = exhalation and Sa = inhalation and the haṃsa is identified with the inner self/Śiva. The work is therefore most likely a short tract on haṃsa-mantra yoga or prāṇa contemplation, but its specific identity, author and date could not be confirmed.
SA_MB_hamsasaraHK.history: Haṃsa speculation is widespread in tantric (e.g. Niruttara-type) and yogic literature and in Haṃsa/Haṃsopaniṣad material; a discrete text titled Haṃsasāra is not well documented in accessible sources, leaving its identification uncertain.

SA_MB_jnanasaraHK.title: Jñānasāra
SA_MB_jnanasaraHK.author: uncertain (multiple works share this title; e.g. Yaśovijaya for the Jain Jñānasāra)
SA_MB_jnanasaraHK.tradition: uncertain — title used in Jain, Vedānta, and Śaiva/tantric traditions
SA_MB_jnanasaraHK.date_estimate: unknown (varies by work; Yaśovijaya's Jain Jñānasāra is 17th c. CE)
SA_MB_jnanasaraHK.summary: 'The essence of knowledge.' Several distinct Sanskrit works bear this title. The best-known is the Jain Jñānasāra by Upādhyāya Yaśovijaya (1624-1688), a compact verse treatise on spiritual knowledge by a master of Navya-Nyāya; there are also Vedāntic/Advaita texts of the same name distilling Upaniṣadic teaching. Without further markers the intended work cannot be fixed.
SA_MB_jnanasaraHK.history: Because 'Jñānasāra' is a common title across traditions, identification depends on context not given here. Yaśovijaya's Jñānasāra is among his roughly 100 works and is a standard Jain text; other Jñānasāras circulate in Vedānta and tantric anthologies.

SA_MB_niruttaravadaHK.title: Niruttaravāda
SA_MB_niruttaravadaHK.summary: The title means 'the doctrine/discourse of the niruttara' (niruttara = 'that which has nothing higher, the unsurpassed; the unanswerable'). It appears to be a doctrinal exposition, plausibly connected with the Kula/Kālī 'Niruttara' tantric stream (cf. the Niruttaratantra) where niruttara names the supreme nondual reality, but it is distinct in title from that tantra and could not be securely identified.
SA_MB_niruttaravadaHK.history: The Niruttaratantra is a Kālī-class Kula scripture (likely 9th-12th c.) emphasizing nondual realization; a separate work styled Niruttaravāda is not clearly documented in accessible sources, so its author, date and exact affiliation remain uncertain.

SA_MB_nirvanayogottaraHK.title: Nirvāṇayogottara
SA_MB_nirvanayogottaraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_nirvanayogottaraHK.date_estimate: unknown (early-medieval Śaiva tantric range, c. 7th-11th c. CE, tentative)
SA_MB_nirvanayogottaraHK.summary: Apparently a Śaiva tantric or yogic scripture whose title combines nirvāṇa ('extinction, liberation') with yogottara ('the higher/supreme yoga'). 'Yogottara' designates an advanced class of tantric teaching; the work would treat liberation-oriented tantric yoga. Its precise identity, contents, author and date could not be confirmed.
SA_MB_nirvanayogottaraHK.history: The label 'yogottara' is used for elevated classes of both Śaiva and Buddhist tantras; a Śaiva text titled Nirvāṇayogottara is not well attested in accessible sources, leaving identification tentative.

SA_MB_pranagnihotraHK.title: Prāṇāgnihotra (Prāṇāgnihotra Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_pranagnihotraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_pranagnihotraHK.tradition: Hindu, Vedānta (minor/Sāmānya Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
SA_MB_pranagnihotraHK.date_estimate: later Vedic/post-Vedic; commonly placed in the early centuries CE (one of the minor Upaniṣads)
SA_MB_pranagnihotraHK.summary: A short Upaniṣad (about 23 verses) whose title means 'the fire-offering (agnihotra) into the fire of breath (prāṇa).' It internalizes the external Vedic agnihotra: eating is recast as an oblation to the digestive fire, the bodily prāṇas as the sacrificial fires, and the universal Self as dwelling within the body. It teaches that liberation can be attained by realizing the indwelling brahman even without external ritual or formal Sāṃkhya/Yoga.
SA_MB_pranagnihotraHK.history: It belongs to the corpus of minor (Sāmānya) Upaniṣads attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda and represents the transition from external Vedic sacrifice to internalized yogic/contemplative practice. It survives in standard Upaniṣad collections and editions.
related_works: minor Upaniṣads of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda

SA_MB_subodhamanjariHK.title: Subodhamañjarī
SA_MB_subodhamanjariHK.genre: commentary / explanatory manual (the title suggests a ṭīkā or primer)
SA_MB_subodhamanjariHK.summary: The title means 'a cluster (mañjarī, 'blossom-spray') of easy understanding (subodha)'. Such names typically belong to an accessible commentary, primer or didactic compendium on grammar, poetics, ritual or philosophy. The specific work, its subject, author and date could not be identified from available sources.
SA_MB_subodhamanjariHK.history: 'Subodha' (easy to understand) and 'mañjarī' (anthology/garland) are common in titles of pedagogical and commentarial Sanskrit works, so multiple unrelated texts could bear this name; none could be confirmed here.

SA_MB_sukavakyaHK.title: Śukavākya
SA_MB_sukavakyaHK.summary: The title means 'the words/sayings of Śuka.' This most likely refers to teachings attributed to the sage Śuka, son of Vyāsa and renowned narrator of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa and exemplar of the liberated knower, and may name a short discourse or anthology of his utterances on liberation. (It is unlikely to be the Śukasaptati 'parrot tales', which is a separate genre.) The specific work could not be securely identified.
SA_MB_sukavakyaHK.history: Śuka is a major figure in the Mahābhārata (Mokṣadharma) and Bhāgavata Purāṇa; collections of sayings or dialogues ascribed to him circulate, but a discrete text titled Śukavākya is not well documented in accessible sources.

SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaHK.title: Vijñānabhairava (Vijñānabhairavatantra)
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed tantra)
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaHK.summary: A foundational Trika scripture of Kashmir Śaivism, framed as a dialogue between Bhairava (Śiva) and the Goddess, presenting 112 dhāraṇās (centering/meditation techniques) for attaining recognition of nondual consciousness. The methods range over breath awareness, concentration on bodily centres, sound and mantra, the senses, void, and direct nondual awareness, all aimed at realizing one's identity with Bhairava.
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaHK.history: Said to be drawn from (a chapter of) the Rudrayāmala, it became a key text of the Trika and was commented on by Kṣemarāja and Śivopādhyāya; in the 20th century it was popularized in the West via Swami Lakshmanjoo and Paul Reps's 'Centering' (Zen Flesh, Zen Bones). Its exact date is uncertain, broadly placed in the 7th-9th centuries.

SA_MB_acintyavisvasadakhyaHK.title: Acintyaviśvasādākhyam (Acintyaviśvasādākhya)
SA_MB_acintyavisvasadakhyaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_acintyavisvasadakhyaHK.date_estimate: unknown (Śaiva Siddhānta range, mid/late first millennium CE, tentative)
SA_MB_acintyavisvasadakhyaHK.summary: An apparently Śaiva work whose title combines acintya ('inconceivable'), viśva ('the universe/all'), and sādākhya — a Saiddhāntika technical term for the Sadāśiva-tattva (also called Sādākhya), the highest manifest level where Śiva's powers of will, knowledge and action are in balance. It would therefore concern the Sadāśiva/Sādākhya principle and Śaiva cosmology/metaphysics. The exact text, author and date could not be confirmed; note the related Cintya/Acintya Āgama within the 28 Śaiva Āgamas.
SA_MB_acintyavisvasadakhyaHK.history: Sādākhya = Sadāśiva-tattva is a standard category in Śaiva Siddhānta Āgama theology; a discrete work under this compound title is not clearly attested in accessible sources, so identification is tentative and may relate to the Cintyāgama tradition.

SA_MB_candrajnanagamaHK.title: Candrajñānāgama (Candrajñāna)
SA_MB_candrajnanagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_candrajnanagamaHK.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (undated; canonical Śaiva Siddhānta Āgama, formative c. 7th-10th c. CE)
SA_MB_candrajnanagamaHK.summary: A duplicate-titled entry for the Candrajñāna(āgama), a Siddhānta Śaiva Āgama/Upāgama classed as a pratiṣṭhātantra dealing chiefly with the installation and worship of liṅgas and Śaiva images, temple ritual and associated kriyā; the name signals the lunar/serene ('moon-knowledge') aspect of Śiva worship. This work_id may represent a different recension, portion, or manuscript of the same Āgama listed at M00263.
SA_MB_candrajnanagamaHK.history: Candrajñāna is one of the 28 Śaiva Āgamas, traditionally revealed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva and said to possess fourteen Upāgamas; it survives mainly in South Indian manuscript and ritual traditions and is cited in studies of Śaiva pratiṣṭhā (installation) ritual.

SA_MB_kulalasastraHK.title: Kulālaśāstra
SA_MB_kulalasastraHK.tradition: uncertain (Hindu; possibly Śaiva/Śākta Kula tantric, or a śilpa/technical treatise)
SA_MB_kulalasastraHK.summary: The title is ambiguous. 'Kulāla' ordinarily means 'potter', so it could denote a śāstra on pottery/ceramics or a caste/occupational manual; alternatively the compound may relate to the Kula (kaula) tantric tradition. Given the strongly Śaiva-Śākta tantric character of this batch, a Kula(śākta) tantric reading is plausible, but the specific work, its author and date could not be securely identified.
SA_MB_kulalasastraHK.history: No definite text matching this title could be confirmed from available sources; the word kulāla ('potter') and the Kula tantric stream both make the affiliation uncertain, so this identification remains tentative.

SA_MB_unmattabhairavaHK.title: Unmattabhairava
SA_MB_unmattabhairavaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_unmattabhairavaHK.tradition: Hindu, Śaiva (Bhairava/Vidyāpīṭha tantra; associated with the Sammohana tradition)
SA_MB_unmattabhairavaHK.date_estimate: c. 8th-12th c. CE (uncertain; medieval tantric stratum)
SA_MB_unmattabhairavaHK.summary: A Bhairava-stream Śaiva tantra named after the deity Unmatta-bhairava ('the intoxicated/frenzied Bhairava'), a fierce form of Śiva. As a Bhairavatantra it would have transmitted mantras, ritual worship, yoga, and magical/coercive rites (ṣaṭkarman) centered on Bhairava and associated yoginīs.
SA_MB_unmattabhairavaHK.history: The text is known mainly from references and citations within the wider Bhairavatantra/Vidyāpīṭha literature; it is associated by some traditions with the Sammohana corpus. It is obscure, with little critical study, and exact dating and recension remain uncertain.

SA_MB_umasamhitaHK.title: Umāsaṃhitā
SA_MB_umasamhitaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_umasamhitaHK.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (composite; section of the Śivapurāṇa)
SA_MB_umasamhitaHK.summary: The Umāsaṃhitā is one of the saṃhitās (books) of the Śivapurāṇa, the great Śaiva Purāṇa traditionally said to comprise seven saṃhitās and 24,000 verses. It expounds devotion (bhakti) to Śiva through meditation, prayer, and worship, and contains numerous narratives, including accounts of the goddess Umā/Pārvatī and Śaiva cosmology and eschatology.
SA_MB_umasamhitaHK.history: As a saṃhitā of the Śivapurāṇa, it is anonymous and traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; the Śivapurāṇa is a composite work compiled and recast over centuries. The number and arrangement of saṃhitās differ across recensions, and the Umāsaṃhitā is one of the standardly listed seven.

SA_MB_bhavachudamaniHK.title: Bhāvacūḍāmaṇi
SA_MB_bhavachudamaniHK.author: Vidyākaṇṭha
SA_MB_bhavachudamaniHK.summary: A Saiddhāntika Śaiva commentary by the Kashmirian scholar Vidyākaṇṭha on the Mayasaṃgraha, a Pratiṣṭhātantra (installation/consecration scripture) of the Śaiva Siddhānta. It treats temple and image consecration ritual and related doctrine within the Mantramārga.
SA_MB_bhavachudamaniHK.history: Vidyākaṇṭha is said in the tradition to have received his teaching from Rāmakaṇṭha and to have been instructed to compose a clear, useful commentary. The Bhāvacūḍāmaṇi survives in a single manuscript and is of value for the early history of Śaiva Siddhānta consecration ritual; it has been studied by scholars of the French Institute of Pondicherry / Early Tantra circles.

SA_MB_kriyakalagunottaramHK.title: Kriyākālaguṇottara
SA_MB_kriyakalagunottaramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kriyakalagunottaramHK.summary: An early Śaiva scriptural compilation of tantric medicine in about 35 chapters, drawing heavily on the older Gāruḍa and Bhūta Tantras. It treats the cure of snakebite and poisoning, exorcism of disease-causing spirits, and the use of mantras, maṇḍalas, and ritual healing rather than species-specific antidotes.
SA_MB_kriyakalagunottaramHK.history: The text preserves much archaic Gāruḍa-tantric material and probably predates the eleventh century. It was studied and partly edited and translated by Michael Slouber as a key source for the early history of Gāruḍa (snakebite) medicine in South Asia.

SA_MB_diiksottaraHK.title: Dīkṣottara
SA_MB_diiksottaraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_diiksottaraHK.summary: An early Śaiva scripture on initiation (dīkṣā) that forms one of the later strata associated with the Niśvāsa corpus, the oldest body of Mantramārga Śaiva tantra. It treats the rites and metaphysics of tantric initiation, including the sixfold division of meditative goals (lakṣya-bheda) on which a yogin fixes attention.
SA_MB_diiksottaraHK.history: The Dīkṣottara is transmitted in connection with the Niśvāsa and survives in early Nepalese and South Indian witnesses; it is among the materials studied by the Early Tantra project (Goodall, Sanderson, Isaacson and collaborators) for the formative stage of Śaiva initiation.

SA_MB_nisvaasakaarikaatantram1HK.title: Niśvāsakārikā
SA_MB_nisvaasakaarikaatantram1HK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_nisvaasakaarikaatantram1HK.date_estimate: c. 8th-9th c. CE (latest, expanded layer of the Niśvāsa)
SA_MB_nisvaasakaarikaatantram1HK.summary: The Niśvāsakārikā is the large, comparatively late book of the Niśvāsa corpus, the earliest surviving Śaiva tantra (Mantramārga). It covers cosmology, ritual worship, initiation, mantra, and yoga, expanding upon the older sūtra-layers of the Niśvāsatattvasaṃhitā.
SA_MB_nisvaasakaarikaatantram1HK.history: Unlike the early Nepalese-manuscript layers (Mūlasūtra, Uttarasūtra, Nayasūtra, Guhyasūtra, Niśvāsamukha), the Niśvāsakārikā is preserved separately in South Indian transcripts at the French Institute of Pondicherry; the Guhyasūtra itself refers to a 'Kārikā,' presumably this work. It is a key witness for the formative period of Śaivism, studied by the Early Tantra project.

SA_MB_yogapiithakramodayaHK.title: Yogapīṭhakramodaya
SA_MB_yogapiithakramodayaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_yogapiithakramodayaHK.summary: An obscure tantric work whose title ('arising of the sequence of the yoga-seat/yogapīṭha') points to a Śaiva-Śākta text concerned with the ritual ground, the krama (sequence) of worship, and the installation/visualization of the deity's seat. Its precise content is not securely established from available scholarship.
SA_MB_yogapiithakramodayaHK.history: The text is little studied and survives chiefly as a title/manuscript in tantric collections; no critical edition or reliable dating is widely available, so its identity and tradition cannot be fixed with confidence.

SA_MB_kamakalavilasaHK.title: Kāmakalāvilāsa
SA_MB_kamakalavilasaHK.author: Puṇyānanda (Puṇyānandanātha)
SA_MB_kamakalavilasaHK.date_estimate: c. 16th c. CE (disputed; some place it somewhat earlier)
SA_MB_kamakalavilasaHK.summary: A short, dense Śrīvidyā work in verse expounding the kāmakalā, the dynamic out-pouring of the energy of consciousness (cicchakti) in the worship of the goddess Tripurasundarī. It gives a concise account, from the Hādi standpoint, of the initial unfolding of Śakti and the structure and meaning of the Śrīcakra/Śrīyantra.
SA_MB_kamakalavilasaHK.history: The work is attributed to Puṇyānandanātha and is studied with the celebrated Cidvallī commentary of his lineage successor Naṭanānanda (pupil of Nāthānanda). It is a foundational doctrinal text of the Śrīvidyā tradition, translated by Arthur Avalon (John Woodroffe); precise dating is debated but it is commonly placed around the sixteenth century.

SA_MB_haharavatantraHK.title: Hāhārāvatantra
SA_MB_haharavatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_haharavatantraHK.summary: An obscure Śaiva-Śākta tantra known mainly by title (the name evokes 'hāhā'-sound, possibly a bhairava or yoginī-related deity). On the analogy of comparable tantras it would have transmitted mantras, deity worship, and ritual/magical practice, but its specific content is not securely documented.
SA_MB_haharavatantraHK.history: The work is very poorly attested in modern scholarship, surviving as a title/manuscript reference within tantric catalogues; no critical study, secure recension, or reliable date is available.

SA_MB_kulanandadiHK.title: Kulānandādi
SA_MB_kulanandadiHK.author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (traditional)
SA_MB_kulanandadiHK.tradition: Hindu, Śaiva-Śākta (Kaula; Yoginī Kaula of Matsyendranātha)
SA_MB_kulanandadiHK.summary: The title ('Kulānanda etc.') refers to the Kulānandatantra and associated short Kaula works. The Kulānandatantra is a brief Kaula scripture (about 60 verses) ascribed in its colophon to Matsyendra-pāda (Matsyendranātha), treating the cakras, granthis, and the piercing/ascent of the cakras in the Yoginī Kaula idiom.
SA_MB_kulanandadiHK.history: The Kulānanda was published by P. C. Bagchi alongside the Kaulajñānanirṇaya of Matsyendranātha's school, from a Newari-script manuscript in the Darbar Library, Nepal. As with much of the Matsyendranātha corpus, authorship is traditional/ascriptive and dating is uncertain.

SA_MB_KulasaaraHK.title: Kulasāra
SA_MB_KulasaaraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_KulasaaraHK.date_estimate: c. 8th-10th c. CE (early Kaula stratum; uncertain)
SA_MB_KulasaaraHK.summary: An early Kaula Śaiva scripture transmitting Kaula doctrine and ritual—worship of Śiva-Śakti, kuṇḍalinī/cakra schemes, mantra, and transgressive (left-hand) practice. It belongs to the formative body of Kaula tantras cited by later exegetes of the Trika/Kaula tradition.
SA_MB_KulasaaraHK.history: The Kulasāra survives in early Nepalese manuscripts and is among the old Kaula sources studied in connection with the work of Alexis Sanderson on the early Śaiva-Śākta scriptural corpus; it is anonymous and difficult to date precisely.

SA_MB_ascaryayogamalaHK.title: Āścaryayogamālā
SA_MB_ascaryayogamalaHK.author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (the alchemist)
SA_MB_ascaryayogamalaHK.summary: An alchemical-medical work ('garland of wondrous applications/yogas') ascribed to the alchemist Nāgārjuna, offering rasa- and herbal recipes, mercurial and metallic preparations, and 'wondrous' efficacious formulae (yoga = prescription/combination). It belongs to the Sanskrit rasaśāstra and tantric-medical genre concerned with transmutation, rejuvenation, and cures.
SA_MB_ascaryayogamalaHK.history: The name Nāgārjuna is a traditional, multiply-applied attribution attached to numerous alchemical and medical works (e.g. Rasaratnākara); such ascriptions are legendary and do not point to a single historical author, so the text's true date and authorship are uncertain.

SA_MB_kakacandesvarimataHK.title: Kākacaṇḍeśvarīmata
SA_MB_kakacandesvarimataHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kakacandesvarimataHK.date_estimate: c. 10th-13th c. CE (relatively early in the rasa-tantra sequence)
SA_MB_kakacandesvarimataHK.summary: A tantric alchemical-medical tract (also called Mahārasāyanavidhi) revealed in the voice of the goddess Kākacaṇḍeśvarī. It treats rasāyana (rejuvenation/elixir) methods, mercurial and herbal preparations, and tantric ritual-medical lore.
SA_MB_kakacandesvarimataHK.history: It is regarded as a comparatively early rasaśāstra source: the Rasaratnākara and Kakṣapuṭatantra are said to postdate the Dattātreyatantra and the Kākacaṇḍeśvarīmata. It circulates anonymously within the Sanskrit alchemical/tantric-medical corpus.

SA_MB_GoraksayogasastraHK.title: Gorakṣayogaśāstram
SA_MB_GoraksayogasastraHK.author: attributed to Gorakṣanātha (Gorakhnāth)
SA_MB_GoraksayogasastraHK.date_estimate: c. 12th-15th c. CE (attribution traditional; core Gorakṣa material c. 1200 CE)
SA_MB_GoraksayogasastraHK.summary: A yoga treatise of the Nātha tradition ascribed to Gorakṣanātha, teaching the methods of haṭha/laya yoga—posture, breath control (prāṇāyāma), the bandhas and mudrās, cakra meditation, and the raising of kuṇḍalinī—as the path to liberation. It belongs to the cluster of Gorakṣanātha-attributed yoga works (Gorakṣaśataka, Gorakṣasaṃhitā, etc.).
SA_MB_GoraksayogasastraHK.history: Authorship is traditional/ascriptive: a large body of Sanskrit yoga texts is attributed to Gorakṣanātha, founder-figure of the Nātha Sampradāya (c. 11th-12th c.), but many took their present form later and circulate in multiple recensions. The Gorakṣayogaśāstra is one such recension within this fluid corpus.

SA_MB_TattvaratnaavaliiHK.title: Tattvaratnāvalī
SA_MB_TattvaratnaavaliiHK.author: Vimalaśiva
SA_MB_TattvaratnaavaliiHK.summary: A Saiddhāntika Śaiva work ('garland of jewels of the tattvas/principles') by Vimalaśiva, treating the categories (tattvas) and doctrine of the Śaiva Siddhānta and their ritual application. It belongs to the South-Asian Saiddhāntika exegetical and paddhati literature.
SA_MB_TattvaratnaavaliiHK.history: The text survives in a fragmentary, disordered palm-leaf manuscript (NAK 1-1697 / NGMPP B 26/16) and is little studied; Vimalaśiva is a Saiddhāntika author known chiefly from such manuscript witnesses, so dating remains tentative.

SA_MB_goraksagutikaHK.title: Gorakṣaguṭikā
SA_MB_goraksagutikaHK.author: attributed to Gorakṣanātha (Gorakhnāth)
SA_MB_goraksagutikaHK.date_estimate: c. 12th-15th c. CE (attribution traditional; related to the Gorakṣaśataka tradition)
SA_MB_goraksagutikaHK.summary: A short Nātha yoga text ascribed to Gorakṣanātha, closely related to (and sometimes overlapping with) the Gorakṣaśataka recensions. It expounds the practice of haṭha/laya yoga—prāṇāyāma, bandhas, mudrās, cakra meditation, and kuṇḍalinī—as the means to liberation.
SA_MB_goraksagutikaHK.history: Like the other Gorakṣa-attributed works, its authorship is traditional and its text fluid across manuscripts and titles (Gorakṣaśataka, Gorakṣasaṃhitā, Gorakṣaguṭikā). It forms part of the foundational Nātha yoga corpus.

SA_MB_mantrakosaHK.title: Mantrakoṣa
SA_MB_mantrakosaHK.author: Āśāditya Tripāṭhī (Tripāṭhin)
SA_MB_mantrakosaHK.summary: A 'treasury of mantras' (mantra-kośa): a compiled reference work that collects and explains tantric mantras and their applications, of the kind used by practitioners and ritual specialists. It belongs to the nibandha/kośa genre of mantraśāstra rather than to a single revealed tantra.
SA_MB_mantrakosaHK.history: The work is attributed to Āśāditya Tripāṭhin and is catalogued in manuscript collections (e.g. Ulwar 2263). It is a compilation rather than a primary scripture; the author's dates are not securely established.

SA_MB_kalikalpalataHK.title: Kālīkalpalatā
SA_MB_kalikalpalataHK.author: Vimarśānandanātha
SA_MB_kalikalpalataHK.summary: A Śākta ritual-compendium ('wish-granting creeper of Kālī') by Vimarśānandanātha, dealing with the worship of the goddess Kālī—her mantras, visualization, rites, and associated Kaula practices. It draws on and cites earlier tantric authorities (e.g. the Yāmala literature).
SA_MB_kalikalpalataHK.history: The work is cited in later scholarship as a source on Kālī worship and Śākta sacrificial practice; it belongs to the digest/paddhati literature of the Kālīkula. Its author Vimarśānandanātha and exact date are not firmly fixed.

SA_MB_kulasaraHK.title: Kulasāra
SA_MB_kulasaraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kulasaraHK.date_estimate: c. 8th-10th c. CE (early Kaula stratum; uncertain)
SA_MB_kulasaraHK.summary: An early Kaula Śaiva scripture (a second record of the same title as M00285) transmitting Kaula doctrine, the worship of Śiva-Śakti, kuṇḍalinī/cakra schemes, mantra, and transgressive Kaula ritual. It is part of the formative Kaula scriptural corpus drawn on by later Trika/Kaula exegetes.
SA_MB_kulasaraHK.history: Preserved in early Nepalese manuscripts, the Kulasāra is among the old Kaula sources studied in connection with Alexis Sanderson's work on the early Śaiva-Śākta corpus; it is anonymous and hard to date precisely. This entry duplicates the title of work M00285.

SA_MB_vimalavatiitantraHK.title: Vimalāvatītantra
SA_MB_vimalavatiitantraHK.author: attributed to Vimalaśiva (per project hint; uncertain)
SA_MB_vimalavatiitantraHK.summary: A Śaiva tantra/āgama; the title is connected with the 'Vimala' āgamic line (Vimalāgama is counted among the twenty-eight Siddhāntāgamas of the Śaiva canon proclaimed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva). As a Siddhānta āgama it would treat doctrine, ritual worship, consecration, and yoga.
SA_MB_vimalavatiitantraHK.history: The text is obscure and poorly documented; the project's attribution to Vimalaśiva is uncertain, and the title may relate to the Vimalāgama tradition rather than to a securely identified single work. No critical edition or firm dating is available.

SA_MB_darsanopanisatHK.title: Darśanopaniṣat (Darśana Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_darsanopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_darsanopanisatHK.date_estimate: uncertain; classed among the Yoga Upaniṣads, variously dated c. 100 BCE–300 CE (Flood) but likely a comparatively late composition reflecting developed yoga and haṭha ideas
SA_MB_darsanopanisatHK.summary: One of the twenty minor 'Yoga Upaniṣads', cast as a teaching of the sage Dattātreya to Sāṃkṛti. It expounds an eight-limbed (aṣṭāṅga) yoga, the nāḍīs and cakras of the subtle body, prāṇāyāma, purificatory practices and meditation aimed at realizing the non-dual Self (Brahman/Ātman).
SA_MB_darsanopanisatHK.history: It is a sectarian Yoga Upaniṣad usually affiliated to the Sāmaveda and absorbed into late anthologies of the 108 Upaniṣads; its precise authorship and date are unknown, and scholars debate whether it predates or postdates Patañjali's Yogasūtra. It is preserved within the South Indian Muktikā canon and printed in collections of the minor Upaniṣads.

SA_MB_trisikhibrahmanopanisatHK.title: Triśikhibrāhmaṇopaniṣat (Triśikhibrāhmaṇa Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_trisikhibrahmanopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_trisikhibrahmanopanisatHK.date_estimate: disputed; Flood places the Yoga Upaniṣads in the early 1st millennium CE, but Raman and others argue this text is post-10th c. CE because it reflects haṭha-yoga traditions
SA_MB_trisikhibrahmanopanisatHK.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Śukla Yajurveda in which a brāhmaṇa named Triśikhin questions the sun-deity. It teaches non-dualist Vedānta through yoga, treating the unity of Brahman, Ātman and the world, and presenting an eight-limbed yoga with prāṇāyāma, āsana and meditative absorption as the means to liberating self-knowledge.
SA_MB_trisikhibrahmanopanisatHK.history: It belongs to the corpus of twenty Yoga Upaniṣads incorporated into the late Muktikā canon; its authorship is unknown and its dating is contested, with much of its content overlapping later haṭha-yoga literature. It survives in South Indian Upaniṣad anthologies and printed editions of the minor Upaniṣads.

SA_MB_varahopanisatHK.title: Vārāhopaniṣat (Varāha Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_varahopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_varahopanisatHK.date_estimate: late; commonly dated c. 13th–16th c. CE
SA_MB_varahopanisatHK.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda in five chapters (c. 251 verses), framed as a dialogue between Viṣṇu as the boar-avatāra Varāha and the sage Ṛbhu. It surveys the tattvas (lists of 24, 36 and 96), the path to liberation, realization of the Self, jīvanmukti, and incorporates tantric and yogic material on cakras, nāḍīs, mantra, laya and haṭha yoga.
SA_MB_varahopanisatHK.history: A demonstrably late Upaniṣad, it is absent from the 17th-c. Dārā Shikoh and early-19th-c. Colebrooke anthologies, and its authorship and source are uncertain. It is preserved in the South Indian Muktikā canon and printed among the thirty minor Upaniṣads.

SA_MB_yogacudamanyupanisatHK.title: Yogacūḍāmaṇyupaniṣat (Yogacūḍāmaṇi Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_yogacudamanyupanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_yogacudamanyupanisatHK.date_estimate: late; c. 14th–15th c. CE (Rosen), among the latest of the Yoga Upaniṣads
SA_MB_yogacudamanyupanisatHK.summary: A short 'Crest-Jewel of Yoga' treatise for advanced practitioners, treating the subtle body of nāḍīs (72,000 issuing from the kanda below the navel), cakras, prāṇāyāma, the bandhas and mudrās, kuṇḍalinī and the syllable Om. It is one of the principal Upaniṣadic sources for kuṇḍalinī and haṭha-yoga theory.
SA_MB_yogacudamanyupanisatHK.history: Counted among the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads and closely tied to the Nātha and haṭha-yoga traditions, it overlaps with texts such as the Gorakṣa corpus. Its anonymous composition is placed in the late medieval period, and it survives in South Indian Upaniṣad collections.

SA_MB_yogakundalyupanisatHK.title: Yogakuṇḍalyupaniṣat (Yogakuṇḍalinī / Yogakuṇḍalī Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_yogakundalyupanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_yogakundalyupanisatHK.date_estimate: late; among the late medieval Yoga Upaniṣads (broad range c. 100 BCE–1100 CE proposed for the corpus, but this text is reckoned late)
SA_MB_yogakundalyupanisatHK.summary: One of the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads, attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, devoted chiefly to kuṇḍalinī yoga. In three chapters it describes the rousing of kuṇḍalinī through prāṇa, food and posture, haṭha and lambikā techniques (khecarī mudrā), and culminates in the quest for the non-dual Ātman/Brahman and living liberation.
SA_MB_yogakundalyupanisatHK.history: A late, anonymous Yoga Upaniṣad incorporated into the Muktikā canon; it is one of the most important Upaniṣadic expositions of kuṇḍalinī and haṭha practice and draws on the same milieu as the Goraksa and Haṭhayoga literature.

SA_MB_yogasikhopanisatHK.title: Yogaśikhopaniṣat (Yogaśikhā Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_yogasikhopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_yogasikhopanisatHK.date_estimate: disputed; Flood/Eliade place it in the early 1st millennium CE alongside the early Yoga Upaniṣads, though it shows later expansion
SA_MB_yogasikhopanisatHK.summary: Called by Feuerstein the 'Crest of Yoga' and the most comprehensive of the Yoga Upaniṣads, it presents an integral yoga combining mantra, laya, haṭha and rāja yoga to realize the unity of Self and Brahman, with discussion of prāṇa, the subtle body and the dissolution of mind.
SA_MB_yogasikhopanisatHK.history: An anonymous Yoga Upaniṣad transmitted in the Muktikā canon; Eliade grouped it with the earliest Yoga Upaniṣads (Brahmabindu, Tejobindu, Dhyānabindu, etc.), though its received text shows later accretions. Printed among the minor Upaniṣads.

SA_MB_yogatattvopanisatHK.title: Yogatattvopaniṣat (Yogatattva Upaniṣad)
SA_MB_yogatattvopanisatHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_yogatattvopanisatHK.date_estimate: disputed; reckoned among the early Yoga Upaniṣads (Eliade/Flood, early 1st millennium CE) but with later haṭha expansions (proposed 9th–13th c. and beyond)
SA_MB_yogatattvopanisatHK.summary: A Yoga Upaniṣad that expounds four kinds of yoga—mantra, laya, haṭha and rāja—and elaborates the meaning of the Ātman through yogic discipline beginning from the syllable Om. It treats prāṇāyāma, the subtle physiology and the stages of meditative attainment within an Advaita-Vedānta framework.
SA_MB_yogatattvopanisatHK.history: Grouped by Eliade with the early Yoga Upaniṣads, its received form also reflects later haṭha-yoga developments; anonymous and transmitted in the Muktikā canon, it exists in shorter and longer recensions.

SA_MB_prayascittasamuccayaHK.title: Prāyaścittasamuccaya
SA_MB_prayascittasamuccayaHK.author: Trilocanaśiva (a similar, slightly earlier work of the same name is by Hṛdayaśiva)
SA_MB_prayascittasamuccayaHK.genre: nibandha / ritual digest (dharmaśāstra-style compilation on expiation)
SA_MB_prayascittasamuccayaHK.date_estimate: 12th c. CE (Trilocanaśiva); Hṛdayaśiva's version slightly earlier
SA_MB_prayascittasamuccayaHK.summary: An authoritative Śaiva-Siddhānta manual wholly devoted to rites of expiation (prāyaścitta). It compiles and systematizes verses from a range of Siddhānta scriptures prescribing atonements for ritual lapses and transgressions, paralleling the dharmaśāstra digests of the same era.
SA_MB_prayascittasamuccayaHK.history: Trilocanaśiva, a 12th-century Siddhānta theologian also known for the Siddhāntasārāvalī, composed this nibandha; an earlier and closely related Prāyaścittasamuccaya by Hṛdayaśiva attributes many of the same verses to source tantras reaching back perhaps to the 6th century. It was critically edited and translated by R. Sathyanarayanan with an introduction by Dominic Goodall (IFP/EFEO).

SA_MB_mohacudottaramHK.title: Mohacūḍottara (Mohacūḍottaratantra)
SA_MB_mohacudottaramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (scripture)
SA_MB_mohacudottaramHK.date_estimate: early medieval; pre-12th c. CE (classed as an upabheda of the Siddhānta canon)
SA_MB_mohacudottaramHK.summary: A Śaiva-Siddhānta scripture counted as one of the two principal pratiṣṭhā-tantras (with the Mayasaṃgraha) that the tradition relies on for the rites of installation (pratiṣṭhā) of liṅgas, images and temple structures. It is largely concerned with ritual consecration and the construction and establishment of sacred objects.
SA_MB_mohacudottaramHK.history: Classified as an upabheda—a derivative scripture not among the twenty-eight 'original' Siddhānta tantras but claiming descent from them—it is cited as authoritative for pratiṣṭhā in later Siddhānta ritual and is preserved in South Indian and Nepalese manuscript traditions.

SA_MB_pingalamataHK.title: Piṅgalāmata
SA_MB_pingalamataHK.author: anonymous/traditional (a Nepalese manuscript names Śrīkaṇṭhanātha as promulgator)
SA_MB_pingalamataHK.genre: tantra / āgama (with strong pratiṣṭhā and ritual content)
SA_MB_pingalamataHK.summary: A Śaiva tantra of the Yāmala/Vidyāpīṭha stream, transmitted as a subsidiary of the Jayadrathayāmala (itself a subsidiary of the Brahmayāmala). It treats tantric ritual including the installation (pratiṣṭhā) of images and temples, iconography and related observances, and is an important source for early medieval Śaiva temple ritual.
SA_MB_pingalamataHK.history: Known chiefly from early Nepalese palm-leaf manuscripts, it is cited in scholarship (e.g. Sanderson) as an early witness to Śaiva pratiṣṭhā and iconographic norms; its precise authorship is traditional/anonymous, one old manuscript attributing it to Śrīkaṇṭhanātha.

SA_MB_laghustavaHK.title: Laghustava
SA_MB_laghustavaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (one of the five hymns of the Pañcastavī)
SA_MB_laghustavaHK.date_estimate: older than a millennium (the Pañcastavī as a whole is reckoned over 1000 years old); precise date uncertain, c. 1st millennium CE
SA_MB_laghustavaHK.summary: The first of the five hymns constituting the Pañcastavī, a pentad of laudations to the supreme Goddess Mahātripurasundarī. In 21 verses it celebrates the dynamic power (śakti) of the Goddess and encodes Śākta-tantric doctrine through bīja-mantras, treating kuṇḍalinī and the inner ascent of consciousness.
SA_MB_laghustavaHK.history: Highly popular among the Śaivas of Kashmir, the Pañcastavī is a monistic Śākta work corresponding to the Krama system of Kashmir Śaivism; the Laghustava bears several Sanskrit commentaries, one of which titles it Tripurabhairavīstava. Its individual authorship is unknown.

SA_MB_carcastavaHK.title: Carcāstava
SA_MB_carcastavaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (one of the five hymns of the Pañcastavī)
SA_MB_carcastavaHK.date_estimate: c. 1st millennium CE (with the rest of the Pañcastavī)
SA_MB_carcastavaHK.summary: The second hymn of the Pañcastavī, addressed to Mahātripurasundarī. In 31 reflective verses it meditates on the nature of the supreme Goddess and her cosmic power, expressing the non-dual Śākta-tantric vision of Kashmir in devotional form.
SA_MB_carcastavaHK.history: Part of the Pañcastavī pentad popular among Kashmiri Śaivas and aligned with the Krama tradition; like its companion hymns it is anonymous and survives with Sanskrit commentaries within the Śākta hymn literature.

SA_MB_bhuvanesvarimahastotramHK.title: Bhuvaneśvarīmahāstotra
SA_MB_bhuvanesvarimahastotramHK.author: Pṛthvīdharācārya (Pṛthvīdhara Ācārya)
SA_MB_bhuvanesvarimahastotramHK.date_estimate: uncertain; traditionally placed early (a tradition makes the author a disciple of Śaṅkara, hence c. 8th c. CE), but the dating is unverified
SA_MB_bhuvanesvarimahastotramHK.summary: A devotional Śākta hymn in praise of the goddess Bhuvaneśvarī, one of the ten Mahāvidyās. It extols the Goddess as supreme cosmic sovereign and encodes mantric/tantric elements of her worship.
SA_MB_bhuvanesvarimahastotramHK.history: Attributed to Pṛthvīdharācārya, styled a scholar of mantra and tantra; a traditional account makes him a disciple of Śaṅkara, but this attribution and dating are not securely established. The work survives in manuscripts and printed Śākta stotra collections.

SA_MB_DeviirahasyaHK.title: Devīrahasya (Devīrahasyatantra)
SA_MB_DeviirahasyaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (scripture, framed as Śiva–Pārvatī dialogue)
SA_MB_DeviirahasyaHK.date_estimate: medieval; uncertain (commonly regarded as a comparatively late Śākta compilation)
SA_MB_DeviirahasyaHK.summary: A large tantric digest in roughly 60 chapters dealing comprehensively with the mantra, yantra, pūjā and sādhanā of many gods and especially goddesses—Bālā, Jvālāmukhī, Śārikā, Mahārājñī, Tārā, Tripurasundarī and others. It covers bīja-mantras, their purification and empowerment, puraścaraṇa and initiation, and includes appendices such as the Uddhārakośa.
SA_MB_DeviirahasyaHK.history: Transmitted especially in the Kashmiri/Himalayan Śākta milieu (with prominence of regional goddesses such as Śārikā and Jvālāmukhī), it functions as a practical handbook for the sādhaka; anonymous and scripturally framed, it survives in manuscripts and printed tantric editions.

SA_MB_asesakulavallariHK.title: Aśeṣakulavallarī
SA_MB_asesakulavallariHK.author: Gaṇananāthabhaṭṭa (Gaṇanāthabhaṭṭa)
SA_MB_asesakulavallariHK.summary: A Śākta-Kaula tantric work composed by Gaṇananāthabhaṭṭa, transmitted in a Nepalese manuscript. Its title ('the creeper of the entire kula') points to Kaula ritual and worship; little is published about its specific contents beyond the manuscript record.
SA_MB_asesakulavallariHK.history: Known from a National Archives of Nepal / NGMCP manuscript (5-4656) digitized by the Muktabodha Indological Research Institute; its author Gaṇananāthabhaṭṭa and its date are otherwise poorly documented in scholarship.

SA_MB_saubhagyataranginiHK.title: Saubhāgyataraṅgiṇī
SA_MB_saubhagyataranginiHK.author: Mukunda
SA_MB_saubhagyataranginiHK.date_estimate: medieval; uncertain (use of late tantric sources suggests a medieval composition)
SA_MB_saubhagyataranginiHK.summary: A Śrīvidyā ritual manual (paddhati) for the worship of the goddess Tripurasundarī, composed by Mukunda and organized in 'waves' (laharī/taraṅga). It lays out the theology of the Goddess, daily worship and meditation, the structure of the Śrīcakra and advanced practice, with frequent appeal to Vedic and Purāṇic authority (Kūrmapurāṇa, Devīmāhātmya and various tantras).
SA_MB_saubhagyataranginiHK.history: The author Mukunda treats Veda and Vedic yajña with marked respect as authority for goddess worship, situating his Śrīvidyā within an orthodox frame. The text is preserved in a Nepalese manuscript (breaking off in the fifth chapter) digitized by Muktabodha.

SA_MB_trotalamahatantramHK.title: Troṭalātantra (Troṭala/Troṭalātantra)
SA_MB_trotalamahatantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (scripture)
SA_MB_trotalamahatantramHK.date_estimate: early medieval; uncertain (cited in classical tantric lists, so likely 1st-millennium-CE in core)
SA_MB_trotalamahatantramHK.summary: A tantric scripture named in classical lists of the sixty-four (or thrice-sixty-four) tantras and associated with the Śākta worship tradition reflected in the Saundaryalaharī and Vāmakeśvaratantra. It opens with an enumeration of the tantras and treats the fundamentals of tantric doctrine and ritual.
SA_MB_trotalamahatantramHK.history: Recognized as a Tantra already in lists embedded in works such as the Ānandalaharī/Saundaryalaharī tradition; it survives in Nepalese manuscript form (NGMCP 5-4852) digitized by Muktabodha, but its specific contents are little studied.

SA_MB_TantracintamaniHK.title: Tantracintāmaṇi
SA_MB_TantracintamaniHK.author: Navamīsiṃha (also called Ādyānanda)
SA_MB_TantracintamaniHK.date_estimate: late 17th c. CE (author flourished under the Malla kings of Kathmandu, c. 1689–1694)
SA_MB_TantracintamaniHK.summary: A vast tantric compendium (40 chapters; first 30 surviving with a pañjikā commentary) compiling passages from a wide range of tantras. It treats the creation of the cosmos and of the tantras, the mantras and ritual procedures for the worship of the Ten Mahāvidyās, and the worship of Śiva and Śakti, with extensive treatment of the goddess Śyāmā.
SA_MB_TantracintamaniHK.history: Its author Navamīsiṃha/Ādyānanda was a minister of a Malla king of Kathmandu (likely Bhūpālendra Malla) and also composed the Kulamuktikallolinī. A complete 40-chapter manuscript is held in the Asiatic Society of Mumbai; the partial text with commentary survives in a Nepalese manuscript digitized by Muktabodha.

SA_MB_moksasopanaHK.title: Mokṣasopāna
SA_MB_moksasopanaHK.author: Kāṇḍadvayātītayogī
SA_MB_moksasopanaHK.summary: A 'Staircase to Liberation' (mokṣasopāna), a treatise charting graded steps toward liberation through yogic and contemplative discipline, transmitted together with a commentary. Its author bears the ascetic title Kāṇḍadvayātītayogī ('the yogī who has transcended the two sections').
SA_MB_moksasopanaHK.history: Known from a Nepalese (NGMCP) manuscript digitized by the Muktabodha Indological Research Institute; the work and its author are otherwise little documented in published scholarship, leaving its precise affiliation and date uncertain.

SA_MB_NityatantraHK.title: Nityākaulatantra (Nityākaula)
SA_MB_NityatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional (scripture)
SA_MB_NityatantraHK.tradition: Hindu (Śākta/Kaula; Nityā cult, antecedent of Śrīvidyā)
SA_MB_NityatantraHK.date_estimate: early medieval; c. 9th–11th c. CE (an early witness of the Nityā tradition)
SA_MB_NityatantraHK.summary: The sole surviving scripture of the lost Kaula 'Nityā' tradition, the precursor cult to the worship of Tripurasundarī. Its principal goddess is Kāmeśvarī with her consort Kāmadeva and eleven subordinate Nityā goddesses; it expounds the mantras, deities and ritual of this Kaula cult.
SA_MB_NityatantraHK.history: Recognized as authoritative scripture in the Manthānabhairavatantra and important to scholars (Sanderson and others) for reconstructing the origins of Śrīvidyā; it survives in a Nepalese manuscript (NGMCP 2-226) digitized by Muktabodha and has been the object of recent critical editing.

SA_MB_varahitantramHK.title: Vārāhītantra
SA_MB_varahitantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_varahitantramHK.tradition: Hindu, Shakta (Tantra; Kaula-influenced, associated with Dakṣiṇāmnāya/Southern transmission)
SA_MB_varahitantramHK.date_estimate: uncertain; surviving recensions likely medieval (c. 11th-16th c. CE), drawing on older Shakta material
SA_MB_varahitantramHK.summary: A Śākta Tantra ('Doctrine of the Boar-Goddess') centered on the fierce goddess Vārāhī, revealed as a dialogue between Varāha and Vārāhī. It treats the worship of the Goddess and her śaktis, mantras and yantras, ritual, and esoteric Kaula doctrine, with one recension of fifty chapters and roughly 2545 verses praised as 'highest among the great Tantras.'
SA_MB_varahitantramHK.history: Counted among the sixty-four nondual Tantras and frequently quoted in later Tantric compendia such as the Bṛhattantrasāra, Puraścaryārṇava, Śyāmārahasya and Śāktānandataraṅginī. At least four different works bear the title Vārāhī Tantra, so it is uncertain which (if any) is the one cited in the classical lists of sixty-four; the surviving manuscripts represent overlapping but distinct recensions.

SA_MB_kulamuktikalloliniHK.title: Kulamuktikallolinī
SA_MB_kulamuktikalloliniHK.author: Navamīsiṃha (also called Ādyānanda)
SA_MB_kulamuktikalloliniHK.summary: A large Kaula Tantric compendium (nibandha) that gathers and arranges quoted passages from a wide range of Tantras on Kula doctrine and ritual practice aimed at liberation (mukti). It functions as a digest of authoritative scriptural citations rather than an original revelation.
SA_MB_kulamuktikalloliniHK.history: Composed by Navamīsiṃha (Ādyānanda), a minister at the Malla court of Kathmandu (Nepal) under King Bhūpālendra/Mahendra Malla, ruling in the late 17th century. The same author compiled the related compendium Tantracintāmaṇi; both works are valued for preserving citations from otherwise lost or fragmentary Tantras.

SA_MB_BrahmayamalaHK.title: Brahmayāmala (Picumata)
SA_MB_BrahmayamalaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_BrahmayamalaHK.tradition: Hindu, Shaiva (Vidyāpīṭha / Bhairava-Yāmala tantra; Kāpālika ascetic milieu)
SA_MB_BrahmayamalaHK.date_estimate: c. 650-750 CE, with later additions into the early 9th c.
SA_MB_BrahmayamalaHK.summary: A monumental early Śaiva Bhairava-tantra of the Vidyāpīṭha, also titled Picumata, comprising over 12,000 verses in 104 chapters. It expounds the cult of Kapālīśabhairava and the goddess Caṇḍā Kāpālinī, detailing transgressive ascetic observances (vratas), mantra systems, sexual and cremation-ground ritual, yoginī worship, and the construction of ritual diagrams.
SA_MB_BrahmayamalaHK.history: One of the most important sources for the early Śākta-Śaiva 'left-current' (vāma) traditions and Kāpālika practice. Critically edited and studied by Shaman Hatley and others; transmitted in an early-11th-century Nepalese palm-leaf manuscript. Its dating (late 7th-early 8th c. core) makes it a key witness to the formation of esoteric Śaivism.

SA_MB_KalasamkarsinitippaniHK.title: Kālasaṃkarṣiṇīmatatantraṭippaṇī
SA_MB_KalasamkarsinitippaniHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_KalasamkarsinitippaniHK.date_estimate: uncertain; the underlying tradition is c. 9th-11th c. CE Kashmir, the gloss likely later medieval
SA_MB_KalasamkarsinitippaniHK.summary: A short annotative gloss (ṭippaṇī) on a tantra of the Kālasaṃkarṣiṇīmata, the doctrine focused on the goddess Kālasaṃkarṣiṇī, the supreme 'devourer of time' worshipped in the Kālī-centered Krama system. It explains the esoteric Krama worship in which Kālasaṃkarṣiṇī is identified with undifferentiated pure consciousness (the void within Parā Saṃvit).
SA_MB_KalasamkarsinitippaniHK.history: Kālasaṃkarṣiṇī appears as a Mata and Tantra in the Jayadrathayāmala, an early and extensive source of the emergent Kālīkrama. The Krama received its most sophisticated theoretical articulation in the works of Abhinavagupta. The ṭippaṇī is a secondary explanatory text within this Kashmirian esoteric lineage.

SA_MB_NigamasaranirnayaHK.title: Nigamasāranirṇaya
SA_MB_NigamasaranirnayaHK.author: Rāmāramaṇadeva (attributed)
SA_MB_NigamasaranirnayaHK.date_estimate: unknown; likely medieval or early modern
SA_MB_NigamasaranirnayaHK.summary: A Tantric treatise whose title means roughly 'ascertainment of the essence of the nigama (Tantric scripture).' It appears to be a digest or doctrinal exposition that distills the essential teachings of the Tantric ('nigama/āgama') scriptures, attributed to an author named Rāmāramaṇadeva.
SA_MB_NigamasaranirnayaHK.history: An obscure work known chiefly through the Muktabodha digital catalogue; little independent scholarly literature identifies its author, date, or precise affiliation. The identification here rests mainly on the title and the author hint supplied in the project metadata.

SA_MB_saktisutramHK.title: Śaktisūtra
SA_MB_saktisutramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_saktisutramHK.summary: A brief aphoristic Tantric text comprising about twenty sūtras concerning Śakti, the divine feminine power and active principle of the universe. It is conceived as a Shakti counterpart to the Śaiva Śivasūtra, treating the nature and operation of cosmic Power in concise mnemonic formulas.
SA_MB_saktisutramHK.history: Listed in Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum as a tantric text of twenty sūtras. It is little studied and exists in several short manuscript versions; the term śaktisūtra also occurs metaphorically (as 'cord of power') in early Śaiva tantras such as the Brahmayāmala, so identification of the specific work can be ambiguous.

SA_MB_kriyakramadyotikavyakhyaHK.title: Kriyākramadyotikāvyākhyā
SA_MB_kriyakramadyotikavyakhyaHK.author: Kacchapeśvaraśivācārya
SA_MB_kriyakramadyotikavyakhyaHK.date_estimate: after 1156 CE; commentary likely later medieval (c. 13th-16th c. CE)
SA_MB_kriyakramadyotikavyakhyaHK.summary: A commentary (vyākhyā) on Aghoraśiva's Kriyākramadyotikā, the standard Śaiva Siddhānta ritual handbook. The base text covers nearly all Siddhānta ritual—daily worship of Śiva, occasional rites, initiation, funerary rites, and festivals—and this gloss explains its ritual procedures for practicing temple priests.
SA_MB_kriyakramadyotikavyakhyaHK.history: The root Kriyākramadyotikā was composed in 1156 CE by Aghoraśiva of the Amardaka order at Chidambaram and became the most authoritative ritual guide for the hereditary Śivācārya priests of Tamil Nadu. Kacchapeśvaraśivācārya's commentary belongs to the South Indian Siddhānta exegetical tradition that grew up around Aghoraśiva's works.

SA_MB_KaularahasyaHK.title: Kaularahasya
SA_MB_KaularahasyaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_KaularahasyaHK.summary: A Kaula Tantric text ('Secret of the Kaula') expounding the esoteric doctrine and ritual of the Kula/Kaula path. As a kaularahasya it would treat the inner, transgressive worship of the Goddess and the lineage's secret practices reserved for initiates.
SA_MB_KaularahasyaHK.history: Known principally from manuscript witnesses—including a Nepalese codex (NGMCP 1-257, with many missing folios) digitized by the Muktabodha Indological Research Institute (catalogue M00326) and a related manuscript in the Cambridge collection. It belongs to the corpus of North Indian/Bengali Śākta-Kaula Tantras; detailed scholarly study is limited.

SA_MB_T-334-sarvajnanottaramHK.title: Sarvajñānottara
SA_MB_T-334-sarvajnanottaramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_T-334-sarvajnanottaramHK.summary: An early Śaiva tantra of the Siddhānta corpus, notable for its sections on yoga and on the nature of the self and Śiva. Together with the Kālottara, Mataṅgapārameśvara and Mṛgendra it is one of the older scriptures relied upon by the Śaiva Siddhānta, transmitted in both Northern and Southern recensions.
SA_MB_T-334-sarvajnanottaramHK.history: Though influential, it does not appear in the canonical list of twenty-eight Siddhāntāgamas, instead circulating as an authoritative supplementary scripture cited across Śaiva communities. Its yoga chapter and jñānapāda were drawn on by later Siddhānta theologians; portions have been critically edited in recent indological scholarship.

SA_MB_siddhantarthasamuccayaHK.title: Siddhāntārthasamuccaya
SA_MB_siddhantarthasamuccayaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_siddhantarthasamuccayaHK.summary: A doctrinal compendium whose title means 'collection of the meanings/aims of the Siddhānta,' apparently summarizing the tenets of the Śaiva Siddhānta—its theology of Śiva (pati), bound souls (paśu) and the bonds (pāśa), and its ritual-soteriological framework. It is a synthesizing treatise rather than a primary scripture.
SA_MB_siddhantarthasamuccayaHK.history: An obscure text known mainly through manuscript catalogues (Muktabodha M00328); independent scholarship securely identifying its author, date and precise contents is lacking, so the affiliation is inferred chiefly from the title.

SA_MB_suksmagamaHK.title: Sūkṣmāgama
SA_MB_suksmagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_suksmagamaHK.tradition: Hindu, Shaiva Siddhanta (one of the 28 Āgamas)
SA_MB_suksmagamaHK.date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (composite; core perhaps c. 7th-10th c.), with later ritual additions
SA_MB_suksmagamaHK.summary: One of the twenty-eight canonical Śaiva Siddhānta Āgamas, classed among the ten Śivabheda group and traditionally said to have been revealed by the Vāmadeva face of Śiva. As an Āgama it prescribes temple construction and consecration, iconography, initiation (dīkṣā), daily and occasional worship, and the fourfold path of caryā, kriyā, yoga and jñāna.
SA_MB_suksmagamaHK.history: Counted seventh in the standard list of twenty-eight Āgamas and reputed to be of enormous (hyperbolically 'trillions of verses') extent. Like other Āgamas it survives in South Indian manuscript recensions used by Śivācārya priests, and its ritual portions have been edited and translated by the French Institute (IFP) and allied scholars.

SA_MB_bhagavadgitavirashaivabhashyamHK.title: Bhagavadgītā
SA_MB_bhagavadgitavirashaivabhashyamHK.author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa; composite)
SA_MB_bhagavadgitavirashaivabhashyamHK.tradition: Hindu (synthesizes Vedānta, Sāṃkhya, Yoga and bhakti)
SA_MB_bhagavadgitavirashaivabhashyamHK.date_estimate: c. 2nd-1st c. BCE (disputed; some place core c. 3rd c. BCE-2nd c. CE)
SA_MB_bhagavadgitavirashaivabhashyamHK.summary: A 700-verse dialogue in which Kṛṣṇa instructs the warrior Arjuna on the battlefield of Kurukṣetra, reconciling duty (dharma), disciplined action without attachment (karmayoga), knowledge (jñāna) and loving devotion (bhakti) as paths to liberation. It teaches the imperishable self (ātman), the supreme deity, and selfless performance of one's prescribed duty.
SA_MB_bhagavadgitavirashaivabhashyamHK.history: Embedded in the Bhīṣmaparvan (Book 6) of the Mahābhārata and traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, it is regarded by most scholars as a composite work by multiple hands, possibly inserted into or developed within the growing epic. It became one of Hinduism's most authoritative texts, drawing classical commentaries from Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva, Abhinavagupta and others.

SA_MB_KulavrttiHK.title: Kulavṛtti
SA_MB_KulavrttiHK.author: Pūrṇānanda (Pūrṇānanda(giri))
SA_MB_KulavrttiHK.summary: A Kaula Tantric exposition (vṛtti) on the Kula doctrine and ritual, expounding the esoteric worship of the Goddess in the Śākta-Kaula tradition. It belongs to the explanatory/commentarial genre that systematizes Kula teaching for initiates.
SA_MB_KulavrttiHK.history: Attributed to Pūrṇānanda(giri), the prolific Bengali Śākta author who compiled the Śyāmārahasya, Śrītattvacintāmaṇi (1577), Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa, Śāktakrama and Tattvānandataraṅgiṇī. His works became foundational for later Bengali Tantric practice; the Kulavṛtti is among his lesser-studied compositions.

SA_MB_laksmiitantramHK.title: Lakṣmītantra
SA_MB_laksmiitantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_laksmiitantramHK.date_estimate: c. 9th-12th c. CE (often placed c. 9th-10th c.)
SA_MB_laksmiitantramHK.summary: A Pāñcarātra Āgama devoted almost exclusively to the goddess Lakṣmī (Śrī) as the active power of Nārāyaṇa/Viṣṇu, cast as her own self-revelation. It expounds Pāñcarātra cosmogony and philosophy, the emanation of the vyūhas, mantraśāstra, and a yoga grounded in śaktivāda (the doctrine of divine power).
SA_MB_laksmiitantramHK.history: Among the large Pāñcarātra corpus it is distinctive for its goddess focus and its sophisticated theology of Śakti, influential on later Śrīvaiṣṇava thought. It was edited and translated into English by Sanjukta Gupta (1972), which made it widely accessible to scholars.

SA_MB_LingadhaaranachandrikaHK.title: Liṅgadhāraṇacandrikā
SA_MB_LingadhaaranachandrikaHK.author: Nandikeśvara
SA_MB_LingadhaaranachandrikaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; post-12th c. CE (Vīraśaiva movement; treatise likely later medieval)
SA_MB_LingadhaaranachandrikaHK.summary: A Vīraśaiva treatise establishing the scriptural validity and efficacy of wearing the iṣṭaliṅga (liṅgadhāraṇa), the defining practice of the Liṅgāyats. Nandikeśvara expounds the liṅga in both its external form and its internal/psychological and cosmological dimensions, arguing for the religious necessity of carrying it always on the body.
SA_MB_LingadhaaranachandrikaHK.history: The work belongs to the apologetic-doctrinal literature of Vīraśaivism that justifies the iṣṭaliṅga cult promoted in the tradition associated with Basavaṇṇa from the 12th century. It is cited in modern expositions of Liṅgāyat theology as the authoritative treatment of liṅgadhāraṇa.

SA_MB_samnyaasaupanisadHK.title: Saṃnyāsa Upaniṣads
SA_MB_samnyaasaupanisadHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_samnyaasaupanisadHK.date_estimate: composite; earliest c. last centuries BCE-early CE, several as late as c. 14th-15th c. CE
SA_MB_samnyaasaupanisadHK.summary: A group of about nineteen-twenty minor Upaniṣads (within the Muktikā list of 108) devoted to renunciation (saṃnyāsa)—the rules, stages, and inner attitude of the ascetic who abandons worldly life to realize Brahman. They cover the saṃnyāsin's vows, conduct, and types, and include such texts as the Jābāla, Āruṇi, Kuṇḍika, Paramahaṃsa, Bhikṣuka and Avadhūta Upaniṣads.
SA_MB_samnyaasaupanisadHK.history: Composed across a long span, from early ascetic texts to late medieval works, they were collected and studied as a corpus; Patrick Olivelle produced the standard critical edition and translation (The Saṃnyāsa Upaniṣads, OUP). Several were transmitted with the commentary of Upaniṣad-brahmayogin.

SA_MB_Yvaasistha1vairaagyaprakaranamHK.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 1, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
SA_MB_Yvaasistha1vairaagyaprakaranamHK.author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
SA_MB_Yvaasistha1vairaagyaprakaranamHK.date_estimate: base text c. 9th-13th c. CE (composite); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary later (traditionally placed c. 17th c. CE or after)
SA_MB_Yvaasistha1vairaagyaprakaranamHK.summary: Part 1 of the Yogavāsiṣṭha (Vāsiṣṭha-Rāmāyaṇa/Mahārāmāyaṇa), a vast philosophical poem of over 29,000 verses framed as sage Vasiṣṭha's instruction to prince Rāma on liberation through self-effort, dispassion, and knowledge. This opening portion (covering the Vairāgya and early sections) sets out Rāma's disenchantment with the world and the non-dual teaching that reality is mind-projected, accompanied by Ānandabodhendra's Tātparyaprakāśa gloss.
SA_MB_Yvaasistha1vairaagyaprakaranamHK.history: Traditionally ascribed to Vālmīki but actually anonymous and composite, philosophically akin to Advaita Vedānta with idealist (dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi) tendencies. The Tātparyaprakāśa, the most widely used commentary, was written by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī (pupil of Gaṅgādharendra Sarasvatī) and is printed with the standard Sanskrit editions of the text.

SA_MB_YvaasisthaBmumuksuvyavaharaprakara.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 2, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
SA_MB_YvaasisthaBmumuksuvyavaharaprakara.author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
SA_MB_YvaasisthaBmumuksuvyavaharaprakara.date_estimate: base text c. 9th-13th c. CE (composite); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary later (c. 17th c. CE or after)
SA_MB_YvaasisthaBmumuksuvyavaharaprakara.summary: Part 2 of the Yogavāsiṣṭha with Ānandabodhendra's Tātparyaprakāśa commentary, continuing the dialogue between Vasiṣṭha and Rāma. This portion (around the Mumukṣuvyavahāra and Utpatti books) elaborates the longing for liberation and the doctrine of the origination of the world as a mental appearance, illustrated through philosophical narratives.
SA_MB_YvaasisthaBmumuksuvyavaharaprakara.history: Part of the multi-volume Sanskrit edition of the Yogavāsiṣṭha printed with the Tātparyaprakāśa of Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī. The work as a whole is an anonymous composite text conventionally attributed to Vālmīki and central to later Advaita-flavored devotional Vedānta.

SA_MB_vyasishtaCutpattiprakarnamHK.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 3, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
SA_MB_vyasishtaCutpattiprakarnamHK.author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
SA_MB_vyasishtaCutpattiprakarnamHK.date_estimate: base text c. 9th-13th c. CE (composite); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary later (c. 17th c. CE or after)
SA_MB_vyasishtaCutpattiprakarnamHK.summary: Part 3 of the Yogavāsiṣṭha with the Tātparyaprakāśa commentary, advancing into the larger middle books (notably the Sthiti and Upaśama sections). It develops the teaching of the world's persistence as mind-stuff and the quieting of mental agitation, using extended allegorical tales to convey the non-dual metaphysics.
SA_MB_vyasishtaCutpattiprakarnamHK.history: One volume of the standard Sanskrit edition of the Yogavāsiṣṭha printed with Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī's Tātparyaprakāśa. The text is anonymous and composite, traditionally credited to Vālmīki and influential in syncretic Advaita-devotional circles.

SA_MB_vyasishthaDsthitiprakaranamHK.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 4, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
SA_MB_vyasishthaDsthitiprakaranamHK.author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
SA_MB_vyasishthaDsthitiprakaranamHK.date_estimate: base text c. 9th-13th c. CE (composite); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary later (c. 17th c. CE or after)
SA_MB_vyasishthaDsthitiprakaranamHK.summary: Part 4 of the Yogavāsiṣṭha with the Tātparyaprakāśa commentary, completing the work in the large concluding book (Nirvāṇa-prakaraṇa). It culminates the teaching with the realization of liberation-in-life (jīvanmukti), the dissolution of the apparent world in pure consciousness, and Rāma's attainment of equanimity.
SA_MB_vyasishthaDsthitiprakaranamHK.history: The final volume of the standard multi-volume Sanskrit edition printed with Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī's Tātparyaprakāśa. The Nirvāṇa book is itself the longest of the six and is sometimes transmitted in two halves; the commentary remains the most authoritative traditional exegesis of the whole composite, Vālmīki-attributed text.

SA_MB_yogavyasishthaEupasamaprakaranamHK.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 5, with commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
SA_MB_yogavyasishthaEupasamaprakaranamHK.author: attributed to Vālmīki (text); commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
SA_MB_yogavyasishthaEupasamaprakaranamHK.tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta, with strong influence of idealist/Yogācāra-style monism)
SA_MB_yogavyasishthaEupasamaprakaranamHK.genre: philosophical poem / didactic epic with commentary (bhāṣya/ṭīkā)
SA_MB_yogavyasishthaEupasamaprakaranamHK.date_estimate: core text c. 9th-10th c. CE (some strata 6th-14th c.); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary much later (Ānandabodhendra, traditionally placed c. 16th-19th c.)
SA_MB_yogavyasishthaEupasamaprakaranamHK.summary: The Yogavāsiṣṭha is a vast Advaitic verse dialogue (c. 23,000-32,000 verses) framed as the sage Vasiṣṭha instructing the young Rāma on the unreality of the phenomenal world, the nature of mind and consciousness, liberation while living (jīvanmukti), and the path of knowledge, illustrated by elaborate philosophical fables. Part 5 corresponds to a portion of the work (likely the Upaśama or Nirvāṇa prakaraṇa) treating the quieting of the mind and dissolution into the Absolute. This edition presents the Sanskrit verses together with the Tātparyaprakāśa, the standard learned gloss.
SA_MB_yogavyasishthaEupasamaprakaranamHK.history: The work is pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki, author of the Rāmāyaṇa, but is a later composite Advaita text also called Mahārāmāyaṇa or Jñānavāsiṣṭha. The Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī became the most widely printed commentary, notably in the Nirṇaya-sāgara Press edition of Wāsudeva Laxman Śāstrī Paṇaśīkar.

SA_MB_durgarcanasrtiHK.title: Durgārcanasmṛti
SA_MB_durgarcanasrtiHK.author: Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Goswāmī
SA_MB_durgarcanasrtiHK.date_estimate: uncertain; likely late medieval / early modern (post-16th c. CE)
SA_MB_durgarcanasrtiHK.summary: A ritual-digest (smṛti) work setting out the procedures, rules, and liturgy for the worship (arcana) of the goddess Durgā, drawing on Purāṇic and Tantric-smārta sources. Such texts codify the sequence of pūjā, mantras, offerings, and observances for Durgā worship, especially around Navarātri/Durgā Pūjā.
SA_MB_durgarcanasrtiHK.history: An obscure ritual compendium attributed to one Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Goswāmī; little firm scholarly information about the author or precise date is available, and the work survives mainly in manuscript/printed Śākta ritual collections.

SA_MB_matrkacakravivekaHK.title: Mātṛkācakraviveka
SA_MB_matrkacakravivekaHK.author: Svatantrānandanātha (Svatantrānanda)
SA_MB_matrkacakravivekaHK.tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism / Trika-Śākta; tantric phonematic mysticism)
SA_MB_matrkacakravivekaHK.date_estimate: medieval; uncertain, likely c. 13th-16th c. CE
SA_MB_matrkacakravivekaHK.summary: A treatise expounding the mātṛkācakra, the 'wheel of the mother-phonemes', i.e. the doctrine that the Sanskrit alphabet (the varṇamālā / mātṛkā) embodies the cosmic emanation of Śiva and Śakti, with vowels representing Śiva (prakāśa) and consonants representing Śakti (vimarśa). It analyzes how the phonemes, their groupings, and the kuṇḍalinī energy generate speech, mantra, and the manifested universe.
SA_MB_matrkacakravivekaHK.history: A specialized work in the non-dual Śaiva/Śākta phonematic-mysticism tradition associated with concepts elaborated by Abhinavagupta and later Krama-Trika authors; the author Svatantrānandanātha and the precise date are poorly attested in standard scholarship.

SA_MB_sadhanamala1IHK.title: Sādhanamālā, Vol. 1
SA_MB_sadhanamala1IHK.author: anonymous/traditional (compilation of many authors); critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
SA_MB_sadhanamala1IHK.date_estimate: individual sādhanas c. 7th-12th c. CE; this critical edition published 1925 (Gaekwad's Oriental Series No. 26)
SA_MB_sadhanamala1IHK.summary: The Sādhanamālā ('garland of sādhanas') is a large anthology of several hundred Buddhist tantric sādhanas — visualization and ritual-worship texts for evoking Vajrayāna deities such as Avalokiteśvara, Tārā, Mañjuśrī, Hevajra, and many forms of Buddhas and Bodhisattvas. It is a primary source for Buddhist iconography and tantric liturgy. Volume 1 contains the first portion of the collected sādhanas.
SA_MB_sadhanamala1IHK.history: Compiled from numerous authors over centuries, the anthology was critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya from eight manuscripts and published in the Gaekwad's Oriental Series (Vol. I, 1925). It is foundational to the modern study of Buddhist tantric art and ritual.

SA_MB_saadhanamaalaa-IIHK.title: Sādhanamālā, Vol. 2
SA_MB_saadhanamaalaa-IIHK.author: anonymous/traditional (compilation of many authors); critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
SA_MB_saadhanamaalaa-IIHK.date_estimate: individual sādhanas c. 7th-12th c. CE; this critical edition (Vol. II) published 1928 (Gaekwad's Oriental Series No. 41)
SA_MB_saadhanamaalaa-IIHK.summary: The second volume of the Sādhanamālā, continuing the anthology of Buddhist tantric sādhanas for the visualization and worship of Vajrayāna deities, and including the editor's substantial introduction and apparatus. It remains a key reference for Buddhist tantric iconography and ritual practice.
SA_MB_saadhanamaalaa-IIHK.history: Edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya and published as Gaekwad's Oriental Series Vol. 41 (1928); together with Vol. 1 it constitutes the standard edition of the collection.

SA_MB_tripuraarahasyaHK.title: Tripurārahasya, Jñānakhaṇḍa
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyaHK.author: attributed to the sage Hāritāyana (pseudepigraphic; 'Hāritāyana Saṃhitā')
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyaHK.tradition: Hindu (Śākta — Śrīvidyā / Tripurasundarī cult, with Advaita coloring)
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyaHK.summary: The Tripurārahasya ('secret of Tripurā/Tripurasundarī') is a Śākta work in three sections; this is the Jñānakhaṇḍa, the 'section on knowledge', comprising c. 2,000 verses on non-dual metaphysics, the nature of consciousness, and liberation, presented through the worship and philosophy of the goddess Tripurasundarī. It teaches an Advaitic-tantric path and is praised in the Ramana Maharshi tradition.
SA_MB_tripuraarahasyaHK.history: Ascribed to the legendary sage Hāritāyana (hence 'Hāritāyana Saṃhitā'); the standard learned commentary is the Tātparyadīpikā of Śrīnivāsa (1831 CE). The Jñānakhaṇḍa was edited and studied (English translation by A. U. Vasavada).

SA_MB_YogavaasisthaSampurnamIIORGHK.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 6, with commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
SA_MB_YogavaasisthaSampurnamIIORGHK.author: attributed to Vālmīki (text); commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
SA_MB_YogavaasisthaSampurnamIIORGHK.genre: philosophical poem / didactic epic with commentary
SA_MB_YogavaasisthaSampurnamIIORGHK.date_estimate: core text c. 9th-10th c. CE (composite); Tātparyaprakāśa commentary much later
SA_MB_YogavaasisthaSampurnamIIORGHK.summary: The sixth and final portion of the Yogavāsiṣṭha, corresponding to the Nirvāṇa-prakaraṇa, the longest section, which culminates the teaching with extended expositions of liberation, the dissolution of the mind into pure consciousness, and the state of the jīvanmukta, again richly illustrated with philosophical narratives. This volume gives the Sanskrit text with the Tātparyaprakāśa commentary.
SA_MB_YogavaasisthaSampurnamIIORGHK.history: Same as Part 5: the work is pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki, and the Tātparyaprakāśa of Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī is its principal commentary, preserved in the Nirṇaya-sāgara Press / Paṇaśīkar edition.

SA_MB_SriikarabhaashyacombinedHK.title: Śrīkarabhāṣya
SA_MB_SriikarabhaashyacombinedHK.author: Śrīpati Paṇḍitācārya (Śrīpati)
SA_MB_SriikarabhaashyacombinedHK.summary: The Śrīkarabhāṣya is a commentary on the Brahmasūtras (Vedāntasūtras) of Bādarāyaṇa expounding the Vīraśaiva theological standpoint, often termed Śakti-Viśiṣṭādvaita or Vīraśaiva-Viśiṣṭādvaita. It argues a bhedābheda (difference-and-non-difference) relation between the soul/world and Śiva as supreme Brahman, and explicitly contests rival Vedānta readings, especially Rāmānuja's, while engaging Śaṅkara and Madhva.
SA_MB_SriikarabhaashyacombinedHK.history: Its author Śrīpati Paṇḍitācārya styled himself a Vīraśaiva ascetic; the work is the principal Vedāntic foundation for Vīraśaiva (Liṅgāyat) philosophy. It was discussed at length by historians of Indian philosophy such as S. N. Dasgupta.

SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaanandabhattaHK.title: Vijñānabhairava, with commentary Kaumadī (Kaumudī)
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaanandabhattaHK.author: root text anonymous (revealed tantra); commentary Kaumudī by Bhaṭṭa Ānanda (Ānanda Bhaṭṭa)
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaanandabhattaHK.tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism — Trika; commentary by an Advaita-Vedāntin)
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaanandabhattaHK.date_estimate: root text c. 7th-8th c. CE; Kaumudī commentary 17th-18th c. CE
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaanandabhattaHK.summary: The Vijñānabhairava is a celebrated Trika tantra cast as a dialogue between Bhairava and the Goddess, presenting 112 dhāraṇās — concise contemplative/yogic techniques for realizing the non-dual consciousness (Bhairava) at the heart of all experience. This edition pairs the text with the Kaumudī commentary of Bhaṭṭa Ānanda, which reads it through a Śaṅkara-Advaita lens.
SA_MB_vijnanabhairavaanandabhattaHK.history: The Vijñānabhairava is a core scripture of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism, also commented on by Kṣemarāja and Śivopādhyāya. The Kaumudī of Bhaṭṭa Ānanda (whose colophon dates him to the 17th-18th c.) was published as Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies No. IX (1918).

SA_MB_sivastotraavaliiHK.title: Śivastotrāvalī
SA_MB_sivastotraavaliiHK.author: Utpaladeva (Utpalācārya)
SA_MB_sivastotraavaliiHK.summary: The Śivastotrāvalī ('series of hymns to Śiva') is a collection of devotional verses by Utpaladeva expressing intense bhakti toward Śiva while embodying the non-dual Pratyabhijñā philosophy of the recognition of one's own identity with Śiva. The hymns blend ecstatic devotion with the metaphysics of consciousness, and are valued both as poetry and as spiritual practice.
SA_MB_sivastotraavaliiHK.history: Utpaladeva, pupil of Somānanda and teacher in the lineage of Abhinavagupta, was the chief systematizer of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school. The Śivastotrāvalī was arranged and transmitted with a commentary by Kṣemarāja.

SA_MB_sivasuutravimarsiniiHK.title: Śivasūtravimarśinī (text version) [commentary on the Śivasūtra revealed to Vasugupta]
SA_MB_sivasuutravimarsiniiHK.author: Śivasūtra revealed to/by Vasugupta; Vimarśinī commentary by Kṣemarāja
SA_MB_sivasuutravimarsiniiHK.date_estimate: Śivasūtra c. early 9th c. CE; Śivasūtravimarśinī c. early 11th c. CE
SA_MB_sivasuutravimarsiniiHK.summary: The Śivasūtra is the foundational aphoristic scripture of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism, traditionally revealed to the sage Vasugupta, setting out the nature of universal Consciousness (Caitanya) as the Self, the means (upāyas) to liberation, and the powers of the realized yogin in three sections. The Śivasūtravimarśinī is Kṣemarāja's authoritative prose commentary; this is the text-only version.
SA_MB_sivasuutravimarsiniiHK.history: Tradition holds that Vasugupta found the sūtras inscribed on a rock (Mahādeva mountain) by Śiva's grace. Kṣemarāja, a disciple of Abhinavagupta, produced the Vimarśinī, the standard commentary that frames the Śivasūtra within Trika-Pratyabhijñā doctrine.

SA_MB_pratyabhijnahrdayaHK.title: Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya (text version)
SA_MB_pratyabhijnahrdayaHK.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_MB_pratyabhijnahrdayaHK.summary: The Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya ('the heart of recognition') is Kṣemarāja's concise primer of the Pratyabhijñā philosophy, consisting of twenty short sūtras with his own auto-commentary. It distills the doctrine that the entire universe is the free self-manifestation of one absolute Consciousness (Citi/Śiva), and that liberation is the recognition (pratyabhijñā) of one's true nature as that Consciousness.
SA_MB_pratyabhijnahrdayaHK.history: Written by Kṣemarāja as an accessible introduction to the more technical Pratyabhijñā works of Utpaladeva and Abhinavagupta; it remains the most popular entry-point text to non-dual Kashmir Śaivism and has been widely translated (e.g. by Jaideva Singh).

SA_MB_LaghuYV-combinedHK.title: Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha, with commentary Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā
