SA_MB_LaghuYV-combinedHK.author: abridgement traditionally ascribed to Abhinanda (of Kashmir); commentary Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā by Ātmasukha (Mummaḍideva)
SA_MB_LaghuYV-combinedHK.date_estimate: Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha c. 10th-11th c. CE; Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā commentary medieval
SA_MB_LaghuYV-combinedHK.summary: The Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha ('shorter Yogavāsiṣṭha') is a condensed recension of the large Yogavāsiṣṭha, retaining its Advaitic teaching on the unreality of the world, the mind, and liberation-in-life through the Vasiṣṭha-Rāma dialogue, in a more manageable compass. This edition adds the Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā commentary.
SA_MB_LaghuYV-combinedHK.history: The abridgement is traditionally credited to the Kashmiri Abhinanda; the Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā is the well-known commentary (by Ātmasukha / Mummaḍideva) printed with it. The Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha was historically more widely read and translated than the full text.

SA_MB_SpandakarikaRamakanthaHK.title: Spandakārikā, with Vivṛti by Rāmakaṇṭha
SA_MB_SpandakarikaRamakanthaHK.author: Spandakārikā by Kallaṭa Bhaṭṭa (or Vasugupta); Vivṛti commentary by Rāmakaṇṭha
SA_MB_SpandakarikaRamakanthaHK.tradition: Hindu (Kashmir Śaivism — Spanda school of non-dual Trika)
SA_MB_SpandakarikaRamakanthaHK.date_estimate: Spandakārikā c. 9th c. CE; Rāmakaṇṭha's Vivṛti c. late 9th-10th c. CE
SA_MB_SpandakarikaRamakanthaHK.summary: The Spandakārikā ('verses on vibration') is the root text of the Spanda branch of Kashmir Śaivism, teaching that ultimate reality is a dynamic, throbbing Consciousness (spanda) — the creative pulsation of Śiva — and that recognizing this vibration in all states of experience is the means to liberation. This edition includes the Vivṛti, the commentary by Rāmakaṇṭha (Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha).
SA_MB_SpandakarikaRamakanthaHK.history: The kārikās are traditionally attributed either to Vasugupta or to his pupil Kallaṭa Bhaṭṭa, and are closely linked to the Śivasūtra. Several commentaries exist (Kallaṭa's Vṛtti, Rāmakaṇṭha's Vivṛti, and Kṣemarāja's Spandanirṇaya); Rāmakaṇṭha's Vivṛti is one of the earliest.

SA_MB_aniruddhasamhitaaHK.title: Aniruddhasaṃhitā
SA_MB_aniruddhasamhitaaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed āgama)
SA_MB_aniruddhasamhitaaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (c. 8th-13th c. CE range typical for Pāñcarātra saṃhitās)
SA_MB_aniruddhasamhitaaHK.summary: The Aniruddhasaṃhitā is a Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇava āgama of thirty-four chapters (c. 1800 granthas) prescribing temple ritual: modes of worship, initiation (dīkṣā), expiations (prāyaścitta), image-making and installation, and especially the annual temple festival cycle, to which it gives unusual attention, along with topics such as royal duty (rājadharma) and town planning.
SA_MB_aniruddhasamhitaaHK.history: One of the many (200+) Pāñcarātra saṃhitās, most preserved only in manuscript; named after Aniruddha, one of the vyūha (emanation) forms of Viṣṇu. It is a source for South Indian Vaiṣṇava temple liturgy.
related_works: Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā literature (e.g. Pādma, Pārameśvara, Sāttvata, Jayākhya saṃhitās)

SA_MB_laghustutiHK.title: Laghustuti, with commentary by Rāghavānanda
SA_MB_laghustutiHK.author: Laghubhaṭṭāraka (also a name used in the Krama/Śākta tradition); commentary by Rāghavānanda
SA_MB_laghustutiHK.date_estimate: medieval; stotra possibly c. 12th-15th c. CE, commentary later
SA_MB_laghustutiHK.summary: The Laghustuti ('short hymn') is a concise Śākta hymn praising the goddess (Tripurasundarī / Devī), dense with Śrīvidyā mantra-doctrine and the symbolism of the Śrīcakra, here accompanied by the explanatory commentary of Rāghavānanda. Such stutis function simultaneously as devotion and as encoded tantric teaching.
SA_MB_laghustutiHK.history: Attributed to Laghubhaṭṭāraka and published with Rāghavānanda's commentary in the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series; it belongs to the corpus of esoteric Śrīvidyā hymns whose precise authorship and dating are uncertain.

SA_MB_paraanandasutraHK.title: Pārānandasūtra
SA_MB_paraanandasutraHK.author: attributed to Pārānanda Muni (Parānanda)
SA_MB_paraanandasutraHK.date_estimate: early, c. 900 CE (one of the earlier surviving tantric sūtra works)
SA_MB_paraanandasutraHK.summary: The Pārānandasūtra is an aphoristic Tantric work expounding Kuladharma — described as the 'cream of the Vedas' — and teaching that the Supreme Being is one, while accommodating seven sectarian deities (Brahmā, Viṣṇu, Śiva, Gaṇeśa, Sūrya, Śakti, Bhairava). It treats the three paths of sādhana (Dakṣiṇa, Vāma, Uttara mārga) and stresses initiation (dīkṣā) from a qualified, ideally jīvanmukta, guru.
SA_MB_paraanandasutraHK.history: Regarded as one of the earlier extant Tantric sūtra texts (c. 900 CE), it is cited in surveys of Hindu Tantric/Śākta literature as an important witness to Kaula doctrine and its relation to Vedic authority.

SA_MB_parasambhumahimnastavaHK.title: Paraśambhumahimnastava (Paraśambhumahimnaḥstava)
SA_MB_parasambhumahimnastavaHK.author: attributed to one styled 'Krodhabhaṭṭāraka' (alias; the epithet is traditionally associated with the sage Durvāsas in the Krama tradition)
SA_MB_parasambhumahimnastavaHK.summary: A hymn (mahimnastava) glorifying the 'greatness of the supreme Śambhu (Śiva)', composed in the non-dual Śaiva idiom in which praise of Śiva simultaneously articulates the recognition of one's own consciousness as Śiva. It belongs to the devotional-philosophical stotra literature of the Krama/Trika tradition.
SA_MB_parasambhumahimnastavaHK.history: Ascribed to an author bearing the epithet Krodhabhaṭṭāraka ('the wrathful lord'), a title traditionally linked to the sage Durvāsas, regarded in the Krama lineage as a transmitter of Bhairava Tantra. The work is obscure and not well documented in standard scholarship.

SA_MB_natyasatrav1HK.title: Nāṭyaśāstra, Vol. 1
SA_MB_natyasatrav1HK.author: attributed to Bharata Muni
SA_MB_natyasatrav1HK.tradition: Hindu (secular/scientific — śāstra on dramaturgy and the performing arts)
SA_MB_natyasatrav1HK.genre: śāstra (treatise on drama, dance, music, and aesthetics)
SA_MB_natyasatrav1HK.date_estimate: composite, c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (often c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE)
SA_MB_natyasatrav1HK.summary: The Nāṭyaśāstra is the foundational Sanskrit treatise on the performing arts, encyclopedically covering drama (nāṭya), dance, music, stagecraft, gesture, costume, the structure of plays, and above all the theory of aesthetic experience (rasa and bhāva). Volume 1 contains the opening chapters, including the mythic origin of theatre, the construction of the playhouse, and the foundational rasa/bhāva doctrine.
SA_MB_natyasatrav1HK.history: Ascribed to the legendary sage Bharata, it is in fact a composite work compiled over centuries. It is the single most influential text on Indian aesthetics, drama, and music, and generated a long commentarial tradition culminating in Abhinavagupta's Abhinavabhāratī.

SA_MB_guhyasamaajatantraHK.title: Guhyasamājatantra
SA_MB_guhyasamaajatantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha/Vajradhara; sometimes associated with Asaṅga or with the mahāsiddha tradition)
SA_MB_guhyasamaajatantraHK.date_estimate: c. 4th-8th c. CE; core often dated c. 4th-6th c. CE (disputed; commentarial Ārya school from c. 8th-9th c.)
SA_MB_guhyasamaajatantraHK.summary: The Guhyasamājatantra ('tantra of the secret assembly/union') is one of the most important Buddhist Anuttarayoga (father) tantras, centered on the deity Akṣobhya/Guhyasamāja and the maṇḍala of the Five Buddhas. It systematizes the generation and completion stages of tantric yoga, deity visualization, mantra, and subtle-body practices, and is famous for its deliberately transgressive, antinomian symbolic language.
SA_MB_guhyasamaajatantraHK.history: Considered the foundational text of its class, it is traditionally placed at the head of the Anuttarayoga corpus. It generated two major exegetical lineages — the Ārya (Nāgārjuna/Āryadeva attribution) and the Jñānapāda schools — and is central to later Indo-Tibetan Vajrayāna.

SA_MB_natyrasatrav2HK.title: Nāṭyaśāstra, Vol. 2
SA_MB_natyrasatrav2HK.author: attributed to Bharata Muni (anonymous/composite)
SA_MB_natyrasatrav2HK.genre: śāstra (technical treatise on drama, dance, music and poetics)
SA_MB_natyrasatrav2HK.date_estimate: core c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (composite, with later accretions); estimates range 500 BCE - 500 CE
SA_MB_natyrasatrav2HK.summary: The Nāṭyaśāstra is the foundational Sanskrit treatise on the performing arts, covering drama (nāṭya), acting (abhinaya), dance, music, prosody, stagecraft, and the theory of aesthetic experience (rasa and bhāva). 'Vol. 2' is a volume of a multi-volume printed edition (most likely the Gaekwad's Oriental Series edition with Abhinavagupta's Abhinavabhāratī commentary), covering a middle block of the text's roughly 36-37 chapters (in that edition Vol. II corresponds approximately to chapters 8-18, dealing with gesture, bodily acting, and dramatic representation).
SA_MB_natyrasatrav2HK.history: Traditionally ascribed to the mythical sage Bharata, the work is in fact anonymous and composite, expanded over centuries. It became the authoritative source for all later Indian poetics and performance theory; its most celebrated commentary is the Abhinavabhāratī of Abhinavagupta (c. 1000 CE), and the standard critical edition is the four-volume Gaekwad's Oriental Series edition (vols. 36, 68, 124, 145).

SA_MB_natyasatrav3HK.title: Nāṭyaśāstra, Vol. 3
SA_MB_natyasatrav3HK.author: attributed to Bharata Muni (anonymous/composite)
SA_MB_natyasatrav3HK.genre: śāstra (technical treatise on drama, dance, music and poetics)
SA_MB_natyasatrav3HK.date_estimate: core c. 200 BCE - 200 CE (composite, with later accretions); estimates range 500 BCE - 500 CE
SA_MB_natyasatrav3HK.summary: The Nāṭyaśāstra is the foundational Sanskrit treatise on theatre, dance, music and poetics, expounding the theory of rasa and the practical conventions of Sanskrit drama. 'Vol. 3' is a further volume of a multi-volume printed edition (most plausibly the Gaekwad's Oriental Series edition with the Abhinavabhāratī commentary), covering later chapters that treat such topics as music (svara, tāla, instruments), language, metre, the parts and types of drama, and stage construction.
SA_MB_natyasatrav3HK.history: An anonymous composite work conventionally attributed to Bharata, it is the single most influential text in the Indian aesthetic tradition. Its transmission is complex, with a 'longer' and 'shorter' recension; the critical Gaekwad's Oriental Series edition incorporates Abhinavagupta's tenth-century Abhinavabhāratī commentary across its four volumes.

SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa1HK.title: Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa, Part 1
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa1HK.author: anonymous (Mahāyāna/Mantrayāna compilation)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa1HK.genre: tantra / kalpa (esoteric ritual manual, also classed as a Mahāyāna sūtra)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa1HK.date_estimate: core c. 6th c. CE with accretions; often dated as a whole to c. 8th c. CE (c. 775 CE per Matsunaga/Sanderson)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa1HK.summary: The Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa ('Root Ordinance/Manual of the Rites of the Noble Mañjuśrī') is a large esoteric Buddhist compendium centred on the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī, embodiment of wisdom. It prescribes maṇḍalas, mantras, mudrās, image-making, fire rites, and astrological and apotropaic rituals, and famously includes a long quasi-historical 'prophetic' chapter (rājavyākaraṇa) listing Indian kings. 'Part 1' is the first of the three volumes of T. Ganapati Sastri's Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition.
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa1HK.history: Often cited as the earliest surviving Indian Buddhist tantra, it is an anonymous, composite text whose core may be sixth-century with substantial later additions; the whole is frequently dated to the later eighth century. Its 'historical' chapter has been mined (controversially) by historians for the chronology of the Guptas and other dynasties. The standard edition is the three-part Trivandrum Sanskrit Series text edited by T. Ganapati Sastri (1920-1925).

SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa2HK.title: Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa, Part 2
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa2HK.author: anonymous (Mahāyāna/Mantrayāna compilation)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa2HK.genre: tantra / kalpa (esoteric ritual manual, also classed as a Mahāyāna sūtra)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa2HK.date_estimate: core c. 6th c. CE with accretions; often dated as a whole to c. 8th c. CE (c. 775 CE per Matsunaga/Sanderson)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa2HK.summary: Second volume of the Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa, the foundational Buddhist esoteric manual of the rites of Mañjuśrī. This part continues the text's ritual and doctrinal exposition: mantra-recitation procedures (mantroddhāra), maṇḍala worship, homa (fire offerings), divination, and the mixing of Mahāyāna ethics and philosophy with Mantrayāna practice. The division into 'Part 2' reflects the volumes of T. Ganapati Sastri's Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition, not separate works.
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa2HK.history: Part of a single anonymous composite tantra, generally regarded as among the earliest extant Indian Buddhist tantras. The text uniquely glosses its rituals with general Buddhist doctrine. Edited in three parts in the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series by T. Ganapati Sastri.

SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa3HK.title: Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa, Part 3
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa3HK.author: anonymous (Mahāyāna/Mantrayāna compilation)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa3HK.genre: tantra / kalpa (esoteric ritual manual, also classed as a Mahāyāna sūtra)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa3HK.date_estimate: core c. 6th c. CE with accretions; often dated as a whole to c. 8th c. CE (c. 775 CE per Matsunaga/Sanderson)
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa3HK.summary: Third and final volume of the Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa, completing the great esoteric manual of Mañjuśrī's rites. This part contains the concluding chapters (paṭalas), including astrological, prognosticatory and the celebrated 'prophetic' (rājavyākaraṇa) material listing rulers, alongside further mantra and ritual prescriptions. The 'Part 3' designation again reflects T. Ganapati Sastri's three-volume Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition.
SA_MB_aryamanjusrimulakalpa3HK.history: Concludes the anonymous composite text widely treated as the earliest extant Indian Buddhist tantra; its dynastic 'prophecies' have been heavily used and debated by historians of early-medieval India. Edited as the third part of the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition by T. Ganapati Sastri.

SA_MB_tantrabhidanaHK.title: Tantrābhidhāna
SA_MB_tantrabhidanaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (title also used for a modern reference lexicon)
SA_MB_tantrabhidanaHK.genre: abhidhāna (lexicon / glossary of technical Tantric terms and seed-syllables)
SA_MB_tantrabhidanaHK.date_estimate: uncertain; traditional Tantric nighaṇṭu material is medieval (later 1st - early 2nd millennium CE)
SA_MB_tantrabhidanaHK.summary: Tantrābhidhāna ('Tantric Lexicon') denotes a glossary of the coded technical vocabulary of the Tantras, especially the bīja (seed-syllable) and mudrā nomenclatures used to conceal mantras from the uninitiated (e.g. a Bījanighaṇṭu and Mudrānighaṇṭu). Such works explain the secret 'code-names' for letters, deities, ritual substances and gestures. The title is also borne by a modern scholarly Tantric encyclopaedia/lexicon by N. N. Bhattacharyya (Manohar, 2002).
SA_MB_tantrabhidanaHK.history: Tantric lexica (nighaṇṭu/abhidhāna) circulated as appendices and aids within the wider Tantric corpus and were edited in the Tantric Texts series. Because 'Tantrābhidhāna' is a generic title attached both to traditional bīja/mudrā glossaries and to a 20th-century reference work, the precise text intended here cannot be fixed with certainty.

SA_MB_kamalanamniHK.title: Kāmyakarmakamalā (by Prāṇamañjarī)
SA_MB_kamalanamniHK.author: Prāṇamañjarī (as given in the metadata; possibly a name/colophon attribution)
SA_MB_kamalanamniHK.genre: paddhati/vidhi (manual of optional/desire-driven ritual acts, kāmya-karman)
SA_MB_kamalanamniHK.date_estimate: unknown (likely medieval or early-modern Tantric ritual manual)
SA_MB_kamalanamniHK.summary: The title Kāmyakarmakamalā ('Lotus of Desire-Prompted Rites') indicates a ritual manual dealing with kāmya-karman, i.e. optional rites performed to obtain specific worldly aims (prosperity, protection, subjugation, success). 'Prāṇamañjarī' is given as the author/source, though it may be a textual name or colophon designation rather than a securely attested historical author. No standard scholarly edition or study was located.
SA_MB_kamalanamniHK.history: Could not be confidently identified in indological literature; the work appears to be a relatively obscure ritual paddhati known mainly from manuscripts. The attribution to 'Prāṇamañjarī' and the precise tradition (Vaidika or Tāntrika) remain unverified.

SA_MB_kulacuu.daama.nitantraHK.title: Kulacūḍāmaṇi Nigama
SA_MB_kulacuu.daama.nitantraHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kulacuu.daama.nitantraHK.genre: tantra (nigama, with the Goddess as teacher)
SA_MB_kulacuu.daama.nitantraHK.date_estimate: uncertain; commonly placed in the medieval period (1st - early 2nd millennium CE)
SA_MB_kulacuu.daama.nitantraHK.summary: The Kulacūḍāmaṇi Tantra is a short Kaula/Śākta scripture that, unusually, takes the form of a nigama: instead of the goddess questioning Śiva, here Śiva (Bhairava) questions and the Goddess (Bhairavī) expounds. In seven brief but vivid chapters she reveals the essence of her worship (kulācāra), the cult of the goddess Mahiṣamardinī/Kālī, and secret left-hand practices aimed at siddhi.
SA_MB_kulacuu.daama.nitantraHK.history: A core Kaula text whose secret doctrine was said to be transmitted orally and veiled in technical 'code' language intelligible only through a guru. It was edited and published in the Tantric Texts series associated with Arthur Avalon (John Woodroffe), which brought it to wider scholarly attention.

SA_MB_mahaalak.smiipuujaavidhiHK.title: Mahālakṣmīpūjāvidhiḥ
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiipuujaavidhiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiipuujaavidhiHK.date_estimate: unknown (ritual paddhati, likely medieval to early-modern)
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiipuujaavidhiHK.summary: A 'procedure for the worship of Mahālakṣmī' (Mahālakṣmīpūjāvidhi) is a liturgical manual setting out the step-by-step ritual for worshipping the great goddess Mahālakṣmī: invocation (āvāhana), seat (āsana), offerings (upacāra), mantras, and concluding rites. Such vidhi texts are practical priestly handbooks rather than independent scriptures.
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiipuujaavidhiHK.history: A generic ritual-manual title shared by many manuscripts and printed pūjā-pamphlets; no single author or critical edition could be identified. In a Nepalese/Newar context such Lakṣmī worship manuals overlap with the Śākta cult and may be related to the regional Siddhilakṣmī/Mahālakṣmī traditions.

SA_MB_mantradaanavidhiHK.title: Mahādānavidhiḥ
SA_MB_mantradaanavidhiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mantradaanavidhiHK.date_estimate: unknown; underlying mahādāna tradition is Purāṇic (mid-1st millennium CE onward)
SA_MB_mantradaanavidhiHK.summary: Mahādānavidhi ('procedure of the great gifts') is a ritual manual prescribing the performance of the ṣoḍaśa-mahādāna, the sixteen 'great gifts' such as the tulāpuruṣa (gifting one's weight in gold) and hiraṇyagarbha (golden-womb rebirth donation). These elaborate, costly royal/elite donations are described in the Purāṇas (notably the Matsya and Agni Purāṇas) and conferred religious merit and legitimacy.
SA_MB_mantradaanavidhiHK.history: The sixteen great gifts are attested in inscriptions from about the 8th century CE and in earlier Purāṇic and Atharvaveda-pariśiṣṭa material; numerous nibandha/vidhi compilations codify their performance. The specific Mahādānavidhi here is a generic procedural digest and could not be tied to a single named author or edition.

SA_MB_kalaagniyajnavidhiHK.title: Kālāgniyajñavidhi
SA_MB_kalaagniyajnavidhiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kalaagniyajnavidhiHK.date_estimate: unknown (Tantric/ritual manual, likely medieval to early-modern)
SA_MB_kalaagniyajnavidhiHK.summary: Kālāgniyajñavidhi denotes a manual for a 'Kālāgni' sacrifice/ritual. Kālāgni ('fire of time/world-destruction', associated with Kālāgnirudra/Kālāgni-Bhairava) is a fierce Śaiva form; the vidhi would prescribe the homa, mantras and offerings for propitiating this deity, typically for protective or apotropaic ends. The text is a procedural liturgy rather than an independent scripture.
SA_MB_kalaagniyajnavidhiHK.history: Could not be matched to a specific edited work or named author; it appears to be a regional/manuscript ritual manual within the broader Śaiva-Śākta fire-rite (homa) literature.

SA_MB_kalasaarcanavidhiHK.title: Kalaśārcanavidhi of Siddhalakṣmīdevī
SA_MB_kalasaarcanavidhiHK.author: anonymous/traditional (Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu; some such manuals copied/composed under Malla royal patronage)
SA_MB_kalasaarcanavidhiHK.date_estimate: manuscript tradition flourishing in the Malla period, esp. 17th-18th c. CE
SA_MB_kalasaarcanavidhiHK.summary: A 'procedure for the worship of the water-vase (kalaśa) of the goddess Siddhalakṣmī/Siddhilakṣmī'. Siddhilakṣmī is a fierce Kālī-form goddess (revealed in the Jayadrathayāmala) who in Nepal is publicly worshipped aniconically in a water-vase; this vidhi prescribes the consecration and worship of that kalaśa with mantras, yantra and offerings. It belongs to the esoteric Sanskritic ritual layer of her Newar cult.
SA_MB_kalasaarcanavidhiHK.history: Part of the rich Nepalese Sarvāmnāya/Krama liturgical literature surrounding Siddhilakṣmī, the patron goddess of Malla kings and clan deity of many Newar families. Numerous such paddhatis survive in Nepalese archives; the 17th-18th-century Bhaktapur kings (e.g. Jitāmitramalla) actively composed and copied Siddhilakṣmī liturgies.

SA_MB_madhyapii.thapujavidhiHK.title: Madhyapīṭhapūjāvidhi
SA_MB_madhyapii.thapujavidhiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_madhyapii.thapujavidhiHK.date_estimate: unknown (Tantric ritual manual; if Newar, Malla period 16th-18th c. CE)
SA_MB_madhyapii.thapujavidhiHK.summary: Madhyapīṭhapūjāvidhi ('procedure for the worship of the central seat/pīṭha') is a ritual manual for the worship of the madhyapīṭha, the central 'seat' of a deity within a yantra or maṇḍala (the focal point where the principal goddess/deity is installed). It would prescribe the installation and worship of the central deity together with the surrounding āvaraṇa (retinue) deities.
SA_MB_madhyapii.thapujavidhiHK.history: A specialized procedural manual that could not be tied to a single named author or edition; the 'pīṭha' framework and the company it keeps in this batch suggest a Śākta Tantric, likely Nepalese, ritual context.

SA_MB_m.rtyunjayakalpaHK.title: Mṛtyuñjayakalpa
SA_MB_m.rtyunjayakalpaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_m.rtyunjayakalpaHK.date_estimate: unknown; cult of Amṛteśa-Mṛtyuñjaya is attested from c. 9th c. CE (Netratantra) onward
SA_MB_m.rtyunjayakalpaHK.summary: Mṛtyuñjayakalpa is a ritual manual centred on Mṛtyuñjaya ('Conqueror of Death'), i.e. Amṛteśvara/Amṛteśa-Bhairava, the formless Śaiva deity who bestows health, longevity and freedom from untimely death. It would set out the mantra (the Mahāmṛtyuñjaya mantra), japa, homa and meditative procedures for averting illness and death, in the manner of the Netratantra's amṛtīkaraṇa rites.
SA_MB_m.rtyunjayakalpaHK.history: Belongs to the broad Śaiva literature on the conquest of death organized around Amṛteśa/Mṛtyuñjaya, whose locus classicus is the ninth-century Netratantra. The specific Mṛtyuñjayakalpa here is a kalpa-type extract or manual that could not be matched to a single critical edition or named author.

SA_MB_phe.takapratis.thacandikagnividhiH.title: Pheṭakapratiṣṭhācaṇḍikāgnividhi
SA_MB_phe.takapratis.thacandikagnividhiH.author: anonymous/traditional (Nepalese/Newar ritual milieu)
SA_MB_phe.takapratis.thacandikagnividhiH.genre: pratiṣṭhā- / agnividhi (manual of installation and fire-rite procedure)
SA_MB_phe.takapratis.thacandikagnividhiH.date_estimate: unknown (Tantric installation/fire-ritual manual; if Newar, likely Malla period)
SA_MB_phe.takapratis.thacandikagnividhiH.summary: A composite ritual manual combining a pratiṣṭhā (consecration/installation) rite with a Caṇḍikā fire-ritual (caṇḍikāgnividhi), apparently concerning a 'pheṭaka' (a ritual object/vessel or possibly relating to the fierce 'pheṭkāriṇī' goddess). It would prescribe the installation/consecration of the deity or object and the accompanying homa to the fierce goddess Caṇḍikā, with mantras and offerings.
SA_MB_phe.takapratis.thacandikagnividhiH.history: A highly specialized procedural text that could not be located in scholarly editions; its terminology (pratiṣṭhā, Caṇḍikā, agni-vidhi) places it within Śākta Tantric installation-and-fire ritual, most plausibly in the Nepalese/Newar manuscript tradition that produced many such hybrid liturgies.

SA_MB_siddhilak.smii-aramikaHK.title: Siddhilakṣmī Ārāmika
SA_MB_siddhilak.smii-aramikaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu)
SA_MB_siddhilak.smii-aramikaHK.genre: ritual/liturgical text (paddhati) of the Siddhilakṣmī cult
SA_MB_siddhilak.smii-aramikaHK.date_estimate: Malla-period Newar tradition, esp. 17th-18th c. CE
SA_MB_siddhilak.smii-aramikaHK.summary: A liturgical/ritual text belonging to the Nepalese cult of the goddess Siddhilakṣmī, the fierce Kālī-form (riding Bhairava, of Kāpālika character) worshipped as patron deity of the Malla kings and clan deity of Newar families. The qualifier 'ārāmika' (lit. relating to a garden/grove or attendant) is obscure; the work is one of the many Siddhilakṣmī ritual manuals preserved in Nepalese archives.
SA_MB_siddhilak.smii-aramikaHK.history: Part of the esoteric Sanskritic ritual corpus of Siddhilakṣmī within the Nepalese Sarvāmnāya/Krama tradition, which preserves the most secret aspects of her cult (mantroddhāra and yantroddhāra). The exact content and the sense of 'ārāmika' could not be confirmed from accessible scholarship.

SA_MB_siddhilaksmiidevyarcanaHK.title: Siddhilakṣmīdevyārcana
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiidevyarcanaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu)
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiidevyarcanaHK.genre: arcana / pūjā-paddhati (manual of worship of a deity)
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiidevyarcanaHK.date_estimate: Malla-period Newar tradition, esp. 17th-18th c. CE
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiidevyarcanaHK.summary: Siddhilakṣmīdevyārcana ('worship of the goddess Siddhilakṣmī') is a ritual manual prescribing the arcana (worship) of the fierce Newar goddess Siddhilakṣmī/Siddhalakṣmī, a Kālī-form revealed in the Jayadrathayāmala. It would detail her visualization (riding Bhairava), mantras, yantra, offerings and the sequence of her worship within the esoteric Sarvāmnāya/Krama framework.
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiidevyarcanaHK.history: One of numerous Siddhilakṣmī liturgical paddhatis preserved in Nepal, reflecting the goddess's central place in Malla royal religion and Newar kinship; the 17th-18th centuries saw intense royal patronage (temples in Bhaktapur and Kathmandu, royal compositions). No single named author or critical edition was identified for this particular arcana.

SA_MB_siddhilasmiikotyahutidinakrtyaHK.title: Siddhilakṣmīkoṭyāhutidinakṛtya
SA_MB_siddhilasmiikotyahutidinakrtyaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu)
SA_MB_siddhilasmiikotyahutidinakrtyaHK.genre: vidhi / dinakṛtya (manual of daily/sequential ritual procedure)
SA_MB_siddhilasmiikotyahutidinakrtyaHK.date_estimate: Malla-period Newar tradition, esp. 17th-18th c. CE
SA_MB_siddhilasmiikotyahutidinakrtyaHK.summary: A manual setting out the day-by-day procedure (dinakṛtya) for a koṭyāhuti, a 'ten-million-oblation' fire sacrifice, dedicated to the goddess Siddhilakṣmī. It would prescribe the schedule, mantras and offerings for this large-scale homa performed over multiple days to propitiate the fierce goddess for great ends (protection, prosperity, royal welfare).
SA_MB_siddhilasmiikotyahutidinakrtyaHK.history: Belongs to the Nepalese Siddhilakṣmī ritual corpus associated with Malla royal cult; large koṭi-homa rites were elite/royal undertakings. The specific text could not be matched to a named author or scholarly edition, but its terminology firmly locates it in the Newar Śākta tradition.

SA_MB_siddhilaksmiyutaksaramalamamtraHK.title: Siddhilakṣmī-akṣaramālāmantra (siddhilakṣmīkyutākṣaramālāmantra)
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiyutaksaramalamamtraHK.author: anonymous/traditional (Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu)
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiyutaksaramalamamtraHK.genre: mantra text (akṣaramālā / mālāmantra of a deity)
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiyutaksaramalamamtraHK.date_estimate: Malla-period Newar tradition, esp. 17th-18th c. CE
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiyutaksaramalamamtraHK.summary: An akṣaramālā ('garland of syllables') or mālāmantra of the goddess Siddhilakṣmī: a long 'rosary' mantra of the deity used in recitation and worship, here presented with its uddhāra (extraction/spelling-out of the syllables). The garbled element 'kyutā' in the transmitted title is likely a corruption (perhaps for an uddhāra/uddhṛta form). It belongs to the mantra-literature of the Newar Siddhilakṣmī cult.
SA_MB_siddhilaksmiyutaksaramalamamtraHK.history: Mantra-garland and mantroddhāra texts were central to Siddhilakṣmī practice, by which practitioners demonstrated expertise; King Jitāmitramalla of Bhaktapur himself composed a Siddhilakṣmīmantrayantroddhārastotra. The exact text and its corrupt title could not be precisely fixed, but it clearly belongs to this Newar Śākta corpus.
related_works: other Siddhilakṣmī ritual and mantra texts

SA_MB_ucchistacandaaliniikalpaHK.title: Ucchiṣṭacāṇḍālinīkalpa
SA_MB_ucchistacandaaliniikalpaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_ucchistacandaaliniikalpaHK.tradition: Hindu (Śākta Tantra; Mahāvidyā cult of Mātaṅgī)
SA_MB_ucchistacandaaliniikalpaHK.date_estimate: unknown (Tantric ritual manual, medieval to early-modern)
SA_MB_ucchistacandaaliniikalpaHK.summary: Ucchiṣṭacāṇḍālinīkalpa is a ritual manual for the fierce goddess Ucchiṣṭa-Cāṇḍālinī, the outcaste 'leftover-food' form of Mātaṅgī, one of the ten Mahāvidyās and the Tantric counterpart of Sarasvatī (governing speech, music and learning). It prescribes her mantras, visualization and worship, the latter deliberately employing impure substances (ucchiṣṭa); such practice is undertaken to gain control over enemies, attraction (vaśīkaraṇa), mastery of the arts, and esoteric knowledge.
SA_MB_ucchistacandaaliniikalpaHK.history: Ucchiṣṭa-Cāṇḍālinī/Mātaṅgī worship, with its transgressive use of polluted offerings, is attested in Śākta Tantric sources (including forms linked to the Kubjikā tradition as Juṣṭacaṇḍālinī). The specific kalpa here is a procedural text within this Mahāvidyā/Mātaṅgī corpus; no single named author or critical edition was identified.

SA_MB_ugracandapujapaddhatiHK.title: ugracaṇḍāpūjāpaddhati
SA_MB_ugracandapujapaddhatiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_ugracandapujapaddhatiHK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern (post-12th c. CE)
SA_MB_ugracandapujapaddhatiHK.summary: A ritual handbook (paddhati) prescribing the worship (pūjā) of the goddess Ugracaṇḍā, a fierce multi-armed form of Durgā/Caṇḍikā. Such manuals lay out step-by-step the invocation, mantras, offerings, and meditative visualization (dhyāna) of the deity. Ugracaṇḍā figures among the Ashtashakti/Navadurgā forms worshipped in Śākta and tantric Durgā cults.
SA_MB_ugracandapujapaddhatiHK.history: An anonymous practitioner's compilation of the type produced widely in eastern Indian (Bengal/Assam) and broader Śākta tantric milieus; no fixed author, date, or critical edition is established. It draws on the standard Durgā/Caṇḍī ritual repertoire attested in works such as the Durgāpūjātattva.

SA_MB_urdhvamnayadamanarohanapaddhatiHK.title: ūrdhvāmnāya damanārohaṇapaddhati
SA_MB_urdhvamnayadamanarohanapaddhatiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_urdhvamnayadamanarohanapaddhatiHK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern
SA_MB_urdhvamnayadamanarohanapaddhatiHK.summary: A ritual manual for the damanārohaṇa (also damanotsava/damanakāropaṇa), a springtime rite in which the fragrant damanaka plant is consecrated and offered to the deity, here within the Ūrdhvāmnāya ('upward transmission') current of Śākta-tantric tradition. The damana rite is a seasonal festival of reparation and renewal associated with the god of love and with offering blossoming herbs.
SA_MB_urdhvamnayadamanarohanapaddhatiHK.history: Belongs to the Ūrdhvāmnāya stream of the āmnāya (transmission-current) scheme of Śākta/Śrīvidyā Tantra. The damana spring rite is also documented in Śaiva sources (e.g. Jñānaśambhu's Jñānaratnāvalī, studied by D. Goodall); this particular paddhati is an anonymous practitioner's manual with no critical edition.

SA_MB_urddhvamnayapujavidhiHK.title: ūrdhvāmnāyapūjāvidhi
SA_MB_urddhvamnayapujavidhiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_urddhvamnayapujavidhiHK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern
SA_MB_urddhvamnayapujavidhiHK.summary: A prescriptive ritual text (vidhi) setting out the procedure of worship (pūjā) according to the Ūrdhvāmnāya, the 'upward' transmission-current of Śākta-tantric ritual. It would specify the sequence of nyāsa, mantra, mudrā, and offerings proper to that current's deities.
SA_MB_urddhvamnayapujavidhiHK.history: An anonymous tantric pūjā manual within the āmnāya classificatory scheme of Śākta Tantra, especially Śrīvidyā; no established author, date, or critical edition.

SA_MB_T0274makutagamaHK.title: mukuṭāgama
SA_MB_T0274makutagamaHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)
SA_MB_T0274makutagamaHK.summary: The Mukuṭāgama (= Makuṭāgama) is one of the twenty-eight Siddhāntāgamas, the foundational revealed scriptures of Śaiva Siddhānta. Like other Śaivāgamas it treats the four traditional pādas — doctrine (jñāna), yoga, ritual (kriyā), and conduct (caryā) — including temple construction, image installation, initiation, and daily worship of Śiva.
SA_MB_T0274makutagamaHK.history: Counted among the five Āgamas said to have been revealed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva, with the upāgamas Makuṭa and Makuṭottara. Transmitted as anonymous divine revelation within the Śaiva Siddhānta pratisaṃhitā framework; survives largely in South Indian manuscript and ritual usage.
related_works: the 28 Śaiva Siddhānta Āgamas (e.g. Kāmika, Kāraṇa, Rauravāgama)

SA_MB_jnaanaratnaavaliiHK.title: jñānaratnāvalī
SA_MB_jnaanaratnaavaliiHK.author: Jñānaśiva (Jñānaśambhu)
SA_MB_jnaanaratnaavaliiHK.summary: A comprehensive Saiddhāntika ritual manual (paddhati) systematizing Śaiva Siddhānta ritual practice — initiation, daily and occasional rites, and festivals, including the damanotsava spring rite of reparation. It is a major source for the reconstruction of medieval Śaiva ritual.
SA_MB_jnaanaratnaavaliiHK.history: Composed in Varanasi by a guru from the Cōḻa country known by the initiatic names Jñānaśiva / Jñānaśambhu, a contemporary of the influential exegete Aghoraśiva (12th c.). The text has been studied by Dominic Goodall and others, partly from a Nepalese palm-leaf manuscript.

SA_MB_kuladiipikaaHK.title: kuladīpikā
SA_MB_kuladiipikaaHK.author: Tryambakabhaṭṭāraka (attributed)
SA_MB_kuladiipikaaHK.summary: The Kuladīpikā ('Lamp of the Kula') is a Kaula tantric work dealing with the doctrines and ritual of the Kula tradition — the esoteric Goddess- and Bhairava-centred current associated with secret worship, mantra, and initiatic transmission. As a 'dīpikā' it functions as an illuminating exposition of Kula practice.
SA_MB_kuladiipikaaHK.history: Attributed to Tryambakabhaṭṭāraka; the name Tryambaka is also that of the legendary founder-teacher of the monistic (Kaula/Trika) lineage of Kashmir Śaivism, so authorship and date are uncertain. No critical edition or secure dating is established.

SA_MB_parakramapujaHK.title: parākramapūjā
SA_MB_parakramapujaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_parakramapujaHK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern
SA_MB_parakramapujaHK.summary: A ritual worship text (pūjā). The title is most plausibly read as the worship associated with Parā (the supreme Goddess) — i.e. a Parā-krama-pūjā setting out the sequence (krama) of her tantric worship — though the exact deity and affiliation cannot be confidently fixed.
SA_MB_parakramapujaHK.history: An anonymous tantric pūjā manual; no established author, date, or critical edition, and the precise text behind the title is uncertain.

SA_MB_hayasirsapancaraatramHK.title: hayaśīrṣapañcarātra
SA_MB_hayasirsapancaraatramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed)
SA_MB_hayasirsapancaraatramHK.summary: The Hayaśīrṣa Pañcarātra (Hayaśīrṣasaṃhitā) is a large Pāñcarātra Āgama in roughly 6,500 verses across four kāṇḍas (Ādi, Saṃkarṣaṇa, Liṅga, Saura) totalling 144 chapters. It is concerned almost entirely with temple architecture, iconography, and the rites of image installation and consecration in Vaiṣṇava worship.
SA_MB_hayasirsapancaraatramHK.history: Attributed to the horse-headed (Hayaśīrṣa/Hayagrīva) form of Viṣṇu, regarded as the original revealer of the Pāñcarātra. It is among the Pāñcarātra texts cited in the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition; transmitted anonymously as revealed scripture.
related_works: Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā corpus (Ahirbudhnya, Jayākhya, Pādma, etc.)

SA_MB_kalikapuranaHK.title: kālikāpurāṇam
SA_MB_kalikapuranaHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kalikapuranaHK.date_estimate: c. late 9th–11th c. CE (sections range 7th–12th c.)
SA_MB_kalikapuranaHK.summary: The Kālikāpurāṇa (also Kālī/Sati Purāṇa, sometimes Kālikātantra) is one of the eighteen Upapurāṇas of the Śākta tradition. It glorifies the Goddess (Kālī, Kāmākhyā, Durgā), narrates the myth of Satī, and gives extensive treatment of Goddess worship, pilgrimage, and ritual — including the noted chapter on blood sacrifice (rudhirādhyāya).
SA_MB_kalikapuranaHK.history: Most likely composed in or near medieval Kāmarūpa (Assam), as shown by its detailed treatment of the Kāmākhyā temple and local geography; associated with the Pāla rulers of Kāmarūpa. Scholarly dating ranges from the late Gupta/early-medieval period, with the core c. 9th–11th c.

SA_MB_mahaaguhyakaaliividhaanamHK.title: mahāguhyakālīvidhānam
SA_MB_mahaaguhyakaaliividhaanamHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mahaaguhyakaaliividhaanamHK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern
SA_MB_mahaaguhyakaaliividhaanamHK.summary: A ritual-prescription text (vidhāna) for the worship of Guhyakālī ('Secret Kālī'), an esoteric and fierce form of the goddess Kālī prominent in Śākta tantra (especially Nepal). The 'mahā-' (great) marks it as an elaborated rite. It would lay out the mantras, nyāsa, visualization, and offerings of her secret worship.
SA_MB_mahaaguhyakaaliividhaanamHK.history: An anonymous tantric ritual manual within the Guhyakālī cult of Śākta Tantra; no established author, date, or critical edition.

SA_MB_mahaalak.smiidiipadaanavidhiHK.title: mahālakṣmīdīpadānavidhiḥ
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiidiipadaanavidhiHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiidiipadaanavidhiHK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiidiipadaanavidhiHK.summary: A ritual-procedure text (vidhi) for the dīpadāna — the offering/donation of lamps — in honour of the goddess Mahālakṣmī. Lamp offering is a meritorious votive rite, especially associated with autumn/Dīpāvalī worship of Lakṣmī; the text would specify the manner, materials, mantras, and rewards of the rite.
SA_MB_mahaalak.smiidiipadaanavidhiHK.history: An anonymous votive/ritual manual of the nibandha/vidhi type; no established author, date, or critical edition.
related_works: dharmaśāstra/nibandha literature on vrata and dāna

SA_MB_ParaatrimsikavivaranaHK.title: parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa
SA_MB_ParaatrimsikavivaranaHK.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_MB_ParaatrimsikavivaranaHK.summary: Abhinavagupta's monumental commentary (vivaraṇa) on the Parātriṃśikā (Parātrīśikā), the short tantric text traditionally drawn from the Rudrayāmala and central to Trika. Going far beyond its 30-odd verses, Abhinavagupta expounds the highest reality, the philosophy of language and mantra, and the means of realizing supreme consciousness.
SA_MB_ParaatrimsikavivaranaHK.history: One of Abhinavagupta's most difficult and revered works, composed by the great Trika master of Kashmir (fl. c. 975–1025 CE). It is a cornerstone of the exegesis of the Parātrīśikā/Trikasūtra teachings and has attracted extensive modern translation and study.

SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaanamT0143HK.title: vāruṇapaddhativyākhyāna
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaanamT0143HK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaanamT0143HK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaanamT0143HK.summary: An explanatory commentary (vyākhyāna) on a Vāruṇapaddhati — a ritual manual connected with Varuṇa (the water deity) or with water-related (vāruṇa) rites/mantras. It would gloss and expand the underlying paddhati's ritual procedures.
SA_MB_varunapaddhativyaakhyaanamT0143HK.history: An anonymous commentarial text on a now poorly identified ritual paddhati; no established author, date, or critical edition.

SA_MB_kaalikaarahasyamHK.title: kālikārahasya
SA_MB_kaalikaarahasyamHK.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_MB_kaalikaarahasyamHK.date_estimate: undated; likely later medieval / early modern
SA_MB_kaalikaarahasyamHK.summary: A 'secret' (rahasya) text concerning the worship of the goddess Kālikā/Kālī, presenting her esoteric mantras, meditative forms, and ritual practice. Texts of this 'rahasya' genre typically compile from multiple tantras a guide to the pūjā and sādhanā of a particular Mahāvidyā.
SA_MB_kaalikaarahasyamHK.history: An anonymous Śākta-tantric compilation on Kālī worship; not securely identified with a single edited text and easily confused with related titles (e.g. Tārārahasya, Kālīvilāsatantra). No established author, date, or critical edition.

SA_MB_maahesvaratantramHK.title: māheśvaratantra
SA_MB_maahesvaratantramHK.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed)
SA_MB_maahesvaratantramHK.tradition: Hindu; tantric (presented as a Śiva–Pārvatī dialogue; classed by some as Vaiṣṇava tantra)
SA_MB_maahesvaratantramHK.date_estimate: undated; likely late medieval / early modern
SA_MB_maahesvaratantramHK.summary: The Māheśvaratantra is a tantra cast as a dialogue between Śiva (Maheśvara) and Pārvatī. Though framed in Śaiva form, it is often classed as a Vaiṣṇava tantra; it treats mantras, deities, prophecy, and ritual and is quoted in later Śākta digests.
SA_MB_maahesvaratantramHK.history: Quoted in the Śāktānandataraṅgiṇī, indicating circulation within later Sanskrit tantric/Śākta scholarship; transmitted anonymously as revealed scripture. Published in modern Sanskrit–Hindi editions (e.g. ed. Sudhakar Malaviya).

SA_MB_Tararahasyavrtti2HK.title: tārārahasyavṛtti 2
SA_MB_Tararahasyavrtti2HK.author: Brahmānanda (Brahmānandagiri)
SA_MB_Tararahasyavrtti2HK.summary: Part 2 of the Tārārahasya ('Secret of Tārā'), a compendium and guide to the pūjā and sādhanā of the goddess Tārā, the second Mahāvidyā, including her aspects Nīlasarasvatī, Ugratārā, Ekajaṭā, and related śaktis. Arranged in four paṭalas (c. 2,500 ślokas), it sets out her mantras, meditations, and ritual worship.
SA_MB_Tararahasyavrtti2HK.history: Compiled by the celebrated tantric author Brahmānandagiri, who is said to have worshipped Tārā at Kāmākhyā before composing his compendia; he draws on numerous tantras (Tārāsāra, Nīlatantra, Mahācīna, Rudrayāmala, etc.). Editions include one with a Hindi commentary by Sarayu Prasad Śāstrī.

SA_K10_sf140_sc.title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśa (Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra)
SA_K10_sf140_sc.author: anonymous/traditional
SA_K10_sf140_sc.date_estimate: c. 1st–2nd c. CE (no later than 3rd c. CE)
SA_K10_sf140_sc.summary: A celebrated Mahāyāna sūtra centred on Vimalakīrti, an enlightened lay householder whose wisdom matches that of the great bodhisattvas. Through his dialogues — culminating in his famous 'thunderous silence' on nonduality — the sūtra teaches emptiness, skillful means, the bodhisattva path, and the Pure Land as accessible to laypeople.
SA_K10_sf140_sc.history: An early Mahāyāna text (composed c. 100 CE), enormously influential in East Asian Buddhism (Chan/Zen). Long thought lost in the original, a Sanskrit manuscript was recovered in 1999 at the Potala Palace, Lhasa, and critically edited by the Taishō University study group (2004/2006).

SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAA_hk.title: parāprāveśikā
SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAA_hk.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAA_hk.summary: A short prose primer of nondual Kashmir Śaiva (Śaivādvaita) philosophy, consisting of an introductory aphorism followed by a concise gloss. It expounds the tattvas (categories of cosmic manifestation) and centres on Śakti in her supreme (Parā) aspect, serving as an accessible introduction to Trika metaphysics.
SA_MB_PARAAPRAAVESIKAA_hk.history: Composed by Kṣemarāja, the foremost disciple of Abhinavagupta (early 11th c.), author of the Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya, Spandanirṇaya, and Śivasūtravimarśinī. The Parāprāveśikā is among his shorter introductory works on Pratyabhijñā doctrine.

SA_GSP29_nysvbh_u.title: Nyāyasūtra
SA_GSP29_nysvbh_u.author: Gautama (Akṣapāda Gautama)
SA_GSP29_nysvbh_u.date_estimate: core c. 2nd c. CE (with later interpolations; range 6th c. BCE–2nd c. CE debated)
SA_GSP29_nysvbh_u.summary: The foundational text of the Nyāya school, comprising about 528 aphorisms (sūtras) in five books. It systematizes logic, epistemology (the pramāṇas — perception, inference, comparison, testimony), debate theory, and metaphysics, framing the sixteen categories whose right knowledge leads to liberation.
SA_GSP29_nysvbh_u.history: Attributed to Gautama (also called Akṣapāda); its date is uncertain, with the core usually placed around 150 CE alongside significant later interpolation. It became the basis of a long commentarial tradition, most importantly Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya. (The work_id suggests this is the bhāṣya recension.)

SA_GV01_rvh1-10u.title: Ṛgveda
SA_GV01_rvh1-10u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to seer-families, ṛṣis)
SA_GV01_rvh1-10u.summary: The oldest of the four Vedas and the foundational text of Indian religion: a collection of 1,028 hymns (sūktas) in 10 maṇḍalas, composed in archaic Vedic Sanskrit. The hymns invoke and praise deities such as Agni, Indra, Soma, Varuṇa, and Uṣas, and contain cosmogonic, ritual, and speculative poetry.
SA_GV01_rvh1-10u.history: Composed orally in the Punjab region by families of seers (the 'family books' II–VII being the oldest core, with books I and X later) and preserved through an extraordinarily precise oral tradition before being written down. It is the cornerstone of Vedic religion and Indo-European studies, exhibiting the most archaic Sanskrit.

SA_GSP29_antarkpu.title: Tarkasaṃgraha
SA_GSP29_antarkpu.author: Annambhaṭṭa
SA_GSP29_antarkpu.genre: philosophical treatise (prakaraṇa / introductory manual of logic)
SA_GSP29_antarkpu.date_estimate: second half of the 17th c. CE
SA_GSP29_antarkpu.summary: A concise introductory manual of the syncretic Navya-Nyāya/Vaiśeṣika system, expounding the seven categories (padārthas) of Vaiśeṣika ontology together with Nyāya epistemology, including the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇas) and especially the theory of inference (anumāna). It is famous for its brevity and pedagogical clarity, intended to teach beginners the basic principles of logic and reasoning.
SA_GSP29_antarkpu.history: Annambhaṭṭa, a scholar associated with Banaras and originally from Andhra, composed both the root text and his own commentary, the Tarkasaṃgraha-dīpikā. The work became one of the most widely used elementary textbooks of Indian logic, attracting numerous later commentaries and remaining standard in traditional pāṭhaśālā education to this day.

SA_GP12_garup1_u.title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_garup1_u.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_garup1_u.date_estimate: core c. 850-1000 CE with earlier material and later accretions (composite)
SA_GP12_garup1_u.summary: A Vaiṣṇava Mahāpurāṇa framed as a dialogue between Viṣṇu and his eagle mount Garuḍa. Its first, encyclopedic part (Pūrva-khaṇḍa / Ācāra-kāṇḍa) covers cosmology, worship, ethics, gemmology, medicine (Āyurveda) and astrology, while its celebrated later portion, the Preta-kāṇḍa, deals with death, the fate of the soul, karmic punishments in the hells, and the rites for the departed (śrāddha).
SA_GP12_garup1_u.history: One of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, the text is composite and grew over centuries, with the eschatological Preta-kāṇḍa becoming its best-known section, traditionally recited at funerals. Its dating is disputed, with scholars placing the layered core in the early medieval period.

SA_GK23_jonart_pu.title: Rājataraṅginī
SA_GK23_jonart_pu.author: Jonarāja (and Pseudo-Jonarāja continuator)
SA_GK23_jonart_pu.summary: Jonarāja's Dvitīyā Rājataraṅginī is a verse chronicle of the kings of Kashmir that continues Kalhaṇa's original Rājataraṅginī, picking up the narrative around 1148/1150 CE and carrying it down to the reign of his patron Sultan Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn (mid-15th c.). It vividly records the decline of the Hindu dynasties and the rise of Muslim rule and conversion in Kashmir.
SA_GK23_jonart_pu.history: Jonarāja died before completing the chronicle; his pupil Śrīvara continued it as the Tṛtīyā Rājataraṅginī. An interpolated recension known as Pseudo-Jonarāja contains additional verses. The project metadata's date hint (1100-1200) conflates Jonarāja with Kalhaṇa and is incorrect; Jonarāja belongs to the 15th century.

SA_GK21_sokss_pu.title: Kathāsaritsāgara
SA_GK21_sokss_pu.author: Somadeva
SA_GK21_sokss_pu.summary: An enormous Sanskrit collection of tales, fables, romances and legends ('Ocean of the Streams of Story'), arranged in 124 chapters within a frame narrative centering on Naravāhanadatta. It interweaves popular folklore, vidyādhara fantasy, and didactic and erotic narratives into a vast embedded story-within-story structure.
SA_GK21_sokss_pu.history: Composed by the Kashmiri Brahmin poet Somadeva for the entertainment of Queen Sūryavatī, wife of King Anantadeva of Kashmir. It is a Sanskrit recension of the lost Bṛhatkathā of Guṇāḍhya (originally in Paiśācī), parallel to Kṣemendra's Bṛhatkathāmañjarī, and is a major source for comparative folklore and the migration of tale-motifs.

SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHA_hk.title: Spandasandoha
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHA_hk.author: Kṣemarāja
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHA_hk.genre: bhāṣya / commentary (vivṛti on a single verse)
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHA_hk.summary: An extensive exegetical exposition (sandoha) by Kṣemarāja on the first verse of the Spandakārikā, the foundational text of the Spanda branch of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism. It elaborates the doctrine of spanda, the dynamic creative 'vibration' or pulsation of Śiva's consciousness by which the universe is manifested, sustained and reabsorbed.
SA_MB_SPANDASAMDOHA_hk.history: The title given ('Kallaṭa attributed author spandakārikā: spandasandoha') signals that this is a commentary on the Spandakārikā, which is variously attributed to Vasugupta or his disciple Kallaṭa. The Spandasandoha itself, however, was written by Kṣemarāja, chief disciple of Abhinavagupta, who also wrote the fuller Spandanirṇaya on the whole text.

SA_T07_vakobha9.title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya Chapter 9
SA_T07_vakobha9.author: Vasubandhu
SA_T07_vakobha9.summary: The ninth and final chapter of Vasubandhu's prose auto-commentary on the Abhidharmakośa, known as the Pudgalaviniścaya ('Determination concerning the Person') or Ātmavādapratiṣedha ('Refutation of the Doctrine of Self'). It argues that liberation is impossible without abandoning belief in a self, and systematically refutes self/soul theories: chiefly the pudgala doctrine of the Vātsīputrīya/Sāṃmitīya Buddhists, and also the ātman of the Brahmanical Vaiśeṣikas.
SA_T07_vakobha9.history: Unlike the preceding eight chapters, this section is composed entirely in prose without root kārikās and is generally regarded as an independent treatise appended to the Kośa. It is a landmark statement of the Buddhist no-self (anātman) doctrine and a key source for understanding inter-Buddhist controversy on personhood.

SA_GSD36_visnu_pu.title: Viṣṇusmṛti
SA_GSD36_visnu_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (revealed by Viṣṇu in the frame)
SA_GSD36_visnu_pu.date_estimate: c. 7th-9th c. CE (one of the latest Dharmaśāstras; disputed, 700-1000 CE)
SA_GSD36_visnu_pu.summary: A treatise on dharma, also styled the Vaiṣṇava Dharmaśāstra, largely a collection of older legal aphorisms set within a Vaiṣṇava devotional frame in which the god Viṣṇu instructs the Earth-goddess. It covers the duties of castes and life-stages, ritual, penance, inheritance, and law, distinguished by its strong bhakti orientation and the requirement of daily pūjā to Viṣṇu.
SA_GSD36_visnu_pu.history: Though it preserves an archaic aphoristic core (whence its older designation as the Viṣṇu Dharmasūtra), the surviving text is comparatively late. Markers such as the centrality of written documents, mention of satī, and Kashmiri Vaiṣṇava iconographic correlations point to composition in the early medieval period, making it among the latest works of the Dharmaśāstra tradition.

SA_GSP30_tantralu.title: Tantrāloka
SA_GSP30_tantralu.author: Abhinavagupta
SA_GSP30_tantralu.summary: Abhinavagupta's monumental encyclopedic synthesis of non-dual Trika Shaivism, treating in great detail the metaphysics, ritual, initiation, mantra, cosmology, yoga and means to liberation (the four upāyas) of the tradition. He presents it as an exposition of the teachings of the Mālinīvijayottara Tantra, organizing diverse Śaiva doctrine through the categories of Pratyabhijñā philosophy.
SA_GSP30_tantralu.history: The Tantrāloka is the central work of mature Kashmir Shaivism and was extensively commented upon by Jayaratha in his Tantrālokaviveka. It integrates the Spanda, Pratyabhijñā, Kaula and Krama streams into a single critical philosophical-ritual structure and remains the foundational reference for the tradition.

SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKA_hk.title: Śivasūtravārttika
SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKA_hk.author: Bhāskara (alternatively Varadarāja/Kṛṣṇadāsa; ambiguous)
SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKA_hk.summary: A versified commentary (vārttika) on the Śivasūtra of Vasugupta, the foundational aphoristic scripture of the non-dual Trika system of Kashmir Shaivism that expounds the nature of bondage and the yogic means to liberation. The title given ('Vasugupta author śivasūtra: śivasūtravārttika') indicates the work is a vārttika on Vasugupta's Śivasūtra.
SA_MB_SIVASUUTRAVARTIKA_hk.history: Two distinct vārttikas on the Śivasūtra are known: one by Bhāskara (an original work in verse, in Vasugupta's lineage, regarded as somewhat idiosyncratic) and one by Varadarāja (also called Kṛṣṇadāsa). Without a disambiguating author the record most plausibly denotes Bhāskara's Vārttika, edited with the Śivasūtra in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies; the identification is therefore not certain.

SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAM_hk.title: Śataratnasaṃgraha
SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAM_hk.author: Umāpatiśivācārya
SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAM_hk.summary: A compendium of one hundred Āgamic aphorisms/gems (ratna) collected from the Śaiva Āgamas, with an explanatory commentary, systematizing the doctrines of Śaiva Siddhānta. It presents the tradition's core categories of pati (Lord), paśu (soul) and pāśa (bondage) and the path to liberation.
SA_MB_SATARATNASAMGRAHAM_hk.history: Umāpatiśivācārya (Umāpati Civācāriyar) was a 14th-century theologian of the Dīkṣitar family attached to the Naṭarāja temple at Cidambaram and one of the four chief preceptors (santāna-ācāryas) of Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta. He is noted as the author who fixed the term 'Śaiva Siddhānta' in his Śivaprakāśam; the Śataratnasaṃgraha is among his Sanskrit works.

SA_GV00_avs___u.title: Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā
SA_GV00_avs___u.author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic ṛṣis; ascribed to Atharvan and Aṅgiras)
SA_GV00_avs___u.date_estimate: c. 1200-900 BCE (composite; later than the Ṛgveda)
SA_GV00_avs___u.summary: The fourth Veda, a collection of hymns, spells, charms and incantations addressing everyday life: healing of diseases, protection from demons and rivals, love and marriage, prosperity, royal consecration, and also speculative cosmological and philosophical hymns. It is the principal source for early Indian magico-religious practice and 'domestic' ritual.
SA_GV00_avs___u.history: Transmitted in two main recensions, the Śaunaka and the Paippalāda. Once marginal to the orthodox three-Veda (trayī) canon, it was later fully accepted as the fourth Veda and is associated with the priestly role of the brahman and with the Āyurvedic and Atharvanic ritual traditions.

SA_GSP30_utipk_pu.title: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvṛtti
SA_GSP30_utipk_pu.author: Utpaladeva
SA_GSP30_utipk_pu.date_estimate: c. early-mid 10th c. CE (Utpaladeva fl. c. 925-975)
SA_GSP30_utipk_pu.summary: Utpaladeva's own concise commentary (vṛtti) on his Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational verse-text of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism. The work argues that liberation comes through the recognition that one's own self is identical with Śiva, the absolute self-luminous and free consciousness, developing a sophisticated philosophy of cognition, agency and the Lord.
SA_GSP30_utipk_pu.history: Utpaladeva, a disciple of Somānanda, composed the kārikās and the vṛtti together (the vṛtti being indispensable for construing the terse verses), and also a longer ṭīkā/vivṛti now only partly extant. Abhinavagupta later wrote the Vimarśinī and Vivṛtivimarśinī commentaries, making this the theoretical core of the entire Trika.

SA_GK19_ragh1-6u.title: Raghuvaṃśa
SA_GK19_ragh1-6u.author: Kālidāsa
SA_GK19_ragh1-6u.summary: A mahākāvya in nineteen cantos narrating the dynasty (vaṃśa) of the solar kings of Raghu, from Dilīpa and Raghu through Aja and Daśaratha to Rāma and his less illustrious successors. It is celebrated as a model of the ideal king and dynasty and as a showcase of Kālidāsa's refined poetic style.
SA_GK19_ragh1-6u.history: Composed by Kālidāsa, the foremost classical Sanskrit poet, generally placed in the Gupta era. The poem became a standard of kāvya pedagogy and attracted many commentaries, most famously Mallinātha's Sañjīvinī.

SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANI_hk.title: Stavacintāmaṇi
SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANI_hk.author: Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇa
SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANI_hk.summary: A devotional hymn of around 120 verses in praise of Lord Śiva as Supreme Reality, together with his power (śakti) responsible for creation, maintenance and dissolution. At once devotional and philosophical, its sustained theme is that loving devotion (bhakti) to Śiva is everything, expressed within the metaphysical framework of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism.
SA_MB_STAVACINTAAMANI_hk.history: Authored by Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇa (not the dramatist of the Veṇīsaṃhāra), the hymn was transmitted with the influential commentary of Kṣemarāja and first published in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies (vol. X, 1918). It is a revered text of the Kashmiri Śaiva devotional tradition.

SA_GP12_vipce_pu.title: Viṣṇu-Purāṇa
SA_GP12_vipce_pu.author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Parāśara, related to Vyāsa)
SA_GP12_vipce_pu.summary: One of the principal Mahāpurāṇas and a foundational text of Vaiṣṇavism, structured as the teaching of the sage Parāśara to his pupil Maitreya. It treats cosmogony, cosmic geography, the genealogies of gods and kings, the four yugas and manvantaras, the duties of caste and life-stage, and devotion to Viṣṇu, including an account of Kṛṣṇa's life.
SA_GP12_vipce_pu.history: The Viṣṇu Purāṇa is regarded as one of the more systematically organized and earlier-redacted of the Mahāpurāṇas, well representing the classical 'pañcalakṣaṇa' (five-topic) purāṇic scheme. It received a notable commentary by Śrīdhara and was translated early into English by H.H. Wilson (1840).

MByogakundalyupanisatHK.docx.title: Yogakuṇḍalyupaniṣat (Yoga-kuṇḍalinī Upaniṣad)
MByogakundalyupanisatHK.docx.author: anonymous/traditional
MByogakundalyupanisatHK.docx.date_estimate: later medieval (c. 12th-16th c. CE; one of the late Yoga Upaniṣads)
MByogakundalyupanisatHK.docx.summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, in three chapters totaling about 171 verses. The first chapter expounds the rousing of kuṇḍalinī through prāṇāyāma, bandhas, āsana and related haṭha/lambika techniques; the second treats khecarī-mudrā; and the third turns to the ātman, Brahman, meditation and living liberation (jīvanmukti).
MByogakundalyupanisatHK.docx.history: Listed as number 86 among the 108 Upaniṣads of the Muktikā canon, narrated by Rāma to Hanumān. It belongs to the corpus of late Yoga Upaniṣads that systematize haṭha- and laya-yoga practice within a Vedāntic soteriological frame.

SA_GSP33_gaukarpu.title: Āgamaśāstra (Gauḍapādakārikā / Gauḍapādīyakārikā / Māṇḍūkyakārikā)
SA_GSP33_gaukarpu.author: Gauḍapāda
SA_GSP33_gaukarpu.summary: A foundational verse treatise of Advaita Vedānta in four chapters (prakaraṇas): the first comments on the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad and the three states of consciousness plus turīya; the later chapters (Vaitathya, Advaita, Alātaśānti) argue for the unreality of the phenomenal world, the doctrine of non-origination (ajātivāda), and non-duality, drawing on dialectical methods with notable parallels to Buddhist (Madhyamaka/Yogācāra) thought.
SA_GSP33_gaukarpu.history: Attributed to Gauḍapāda, traditionally the teacher of Śaṅkara's teacher Govinda. It is the earliest systematic exposition of Advaita and is famous for its engagement with Buddhist philosophy; Śaṅkara (or a follower) wrote a commentary on it. The fourth chapter's relation to the rest is debated by scholars.

SA_GS40_caraka_u.title: Carakasaṃhitā
SA_GS40_caraka_u.author: Agniveśa (redacted by Caraka; later supplemented by Dṛḍhabala)
SA_GS40_caraka_u.date_estimate: core c. 2nd c. BCE-2nd c. CE, with redaction by Caraka and later additions by Dṛḍhabala (c. 4th-5th c. CE); composite
SA_GS40_caraka_u.summary: One of the two foundational classics of Āyurveda, focused chiefly on internal medicine (kāyacikitsā). It treats physiology, the three doṣas, diagnosis, pathology, therapeutics, pharmacology, diet, ethics of the physician, and medical methodology, organized into eight sections (sthānas) and structured around the teaching lineage of Ātreya-Agniveśa.
SA_GS40_caraka_u.history: Traditionally the Agniveśatantra composed by Agniveśa (pupil of Ātreya Punarvasu), redacted by Caraka, with the lost portions later restored by Dṛḍhabala. It is a layered text foundational to classical Indian medicine and was commented upon by Cakrapāṇidatta among others; it was transmitted in Arabic and influenced wider medical traditions.

SA_GV05_chup___u.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad
SA_GV05_chup___u.author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic ṛṣis)
SA_GV05_chup___u.date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (one of the oldest, pre-Buddhist Upaniṣads)
SA_GV05_chup___u.summary: One of the oldest and largest principal Upaniṣads, belonging to the Sāmaveda and embedded in the Chāndogya Brāhmaṇa. In eight chapters it moves from the symbolism of the udgītha and the syllable Oṃ to profound metaphysical teachings, including the doctrine of Brahman as the Self, the dialogue of Śvetaketu and the great saying 'tat tvam asi' ('that thou art'), and Sanatkumāra's instruction on bhūman.
SA_GV05_chup___u.history: A foundational source for Vedānta, it is among the most frequently cited Upaniṣads in the Brahmasūtra and the commentaries of Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja and others. Its prose dialogues are key documents of early Indian philosophical and religious thought.

SA_GSD36_manu2p_u.title: Mānava-Dharmaśāstra [Manusmṛti]
SA_GSD36_manu2p_u.author: anonymous (ascribed to Manu)
SA_GSD36_manu2p_u.date_estimate: ~2nd–3rd c. CE (Olivelle, Manu’s Code of Law 2005)
SA_GSD36_manu2p_u.summary: The Laws of Manu — the first Dharmaśāstra composed entirely in verse and the watershed text of the genre. In Olivelle’s chronology it follows the four Dharmasūtras and precedes the Yājñavalkya Dharmaśāstra.
